The work was the practice, performed in community, across generations, with sufficient labour invested to leave the deep archaeological record § I documented and the textual and ethnographic record § II named.

The captured institutional apparatus the rest of this volume traces inherited this work. Some of the work the apparatus continues to do, in the documented continuing operation of Catholic hospitals, Jewish mutual-aid societies, Sikh langars, Islamic *zakat* institutions, Buddhist monastic education programmes, named Indigenous-faith cultural-transmission projects. Some of the work the structural apparatus has dramatically reduced, while continuing to claim the vocabulary of the work it has reduced. Some of the work the apparatus has, in documented and named instances, inverted — turned against the populations it claimed to serve, in coordination with the four-century mechanism the first volume documented and the political class the second volume documented. These distinctions are what the later chapters trace.

The architectural payoff of § III is the same payoff § II prepared. The believer's faith, in every tradition this volume treats, was operating on documented work — work the pre-institutional community had developed and was sustaining at the believer's scale, work that mattered to her life and her community. That work is the believer's faith in its documented operating reality. That work is what the governing apparatus was built on top of, and what the apparatus has been capturing across the four centuries the volume documents. The believer is not the apparatus. The believer's faith is, on the empirical record, doing work that matters. The volume honours what the work has been and what the work, in many places, still is.

The apparatus is what the volume documents.

---


### IV.

The movement becomes the institution.

This is the universal pattern, in each tradition the volume treats. Some part of the way through the founding generation, or in the generation immediately following, or in the centuries after, the foundational community of the named original figures begins to acquire the features that distinguish a movement from an apparatus. The features are documented and recurring. A clergy emerges, a class of professional religious specialists distinguished from the general body of believers, with specific powers reserved to them. An institutional hierarchy emerges, a graded structure of offices with documented chains of authority. A canonical scripture is fixed, a bounded set of authoritative texts, with a defined process for distinguishing canon from non-canon, with named gatekeepers of orthodox interpretation. A doctrinal apparatus is built, a set of authoritative formulations of belief produced at named councils, distinguishing the orthodox from the heretical. An economic-institutional structure is established, with tithes, levies, endowments, lands, treasuries, and the offices that manage them. And, in the documented majority of cases, the structure acquires state or imperial alignment, either as the patron's church or as the church the state submits to or, eventually, as both.

The pattern was named in 1912 by Max Weber as the *Veralltäglichung des Charisma* — the routinisation of charisma — the documented process by which the charismatic religious movement, after the founder's death, transforms into a bureaucratic institution. Ernst Troeltsch in the same period made the church-sect distinction. The broader sociology of religion has refined and tested these foundational analyses across more than a century. The pattern holds across the traditions with considerable variation in timing and intensity, and the variations are interesting; but the pattern itself is documented as recurring.

What this section documents is the *dated transitions*. The volume's later chapters trace the co-options that came after the transitions. The transitions are themselves their own moments — frequently named, frequently visible in the historical record, frequently understood at the time as moments of consequence. Part II of the volume is about what happened to the structural church after it had become an institutional church. § IV names how each institutional church became one.

For Christianity the dates are precise and the transitions are sharp. The Christian movement before 313 CE existed as a documented persecuted minority across the Roman Empire. A network of local communities, with house churches and named bishops in named cities, with the theological tradition of Ignatius and Justin and Irenaeus and Tertullian and Clement and Origen and Cyprian, with the diversity of Ebionite, Marcionite, Gnostic, and Montanist movements alongside the proto-orthodox party that would later define itself as the catholic — the universal — church. In 313 the Edict of Milan, issued jointly by Constantine in the western empire and Licinius in the east, granted Christians religious toleration and restored confiscated church properties. In 314 the Council of Arles was convened by Constantine personally to address the Donatist controversy, the first imperially-sponsored Christian council. In 325 the Council of Nicaea was convened, also by Constantine, who attended in person; the Nicene Creed was produced as the first authoritative formulation of orthodox Christological doctrine, against the Arian position. In 380, on 27 February of that year, the Edict of Thessalonica was issued by Theodosius I, Gratian, and Valentinian II, making Nicene Christianity the official state religion of the Roman Empire. With the text of the *Codex Theodosianus* 16.1.2 declaring all subjects of the empire required to follow the faith of Damasus of Rome and Peter of Alexandria. In 381 the First Council of Constantinople finalised the Nicene Creed in the form still used today. In 391 and 392 Theodosius's edicts closed the pagan temples and banned pagan sacrifice. In sixty-seven years the Christian church had been built. The Donatist schismatics, the named pagan philosophical schools, the Arian Goths, the named eastern Christological dissidents — all became, by the legal-institutional definitions of the post-Theodosian church, heretics or pagans subject to the apparatus the empire and the church now ran together. Peter Brown's *Through the Eye of a Needle*, published in 2012 by Princeton, is the standard contemporary scholarly account of the wealth, the land, and the governing power the Christian church accumulated across this transition. The Catholic capture of Chapter 2 will trace what came next.

For Islam the dates are precise and the transition is also sharp, occurring within thirty years of the founder's death. The Prophet Muhammad died on 8 June 632 CE at Medina. The four caliphs of the Rashidun period. Abu Bakr from 632 to 634, Umar from 634 to 644, Uthman from 644 to 656, Ali from 656 to 661 — ruled in continuity with the Medinan ummah, though the institutional development across those three decades was already considerable: the compilation of the Quran under Abu Bakr with the documented work of Zayd ibn Thabit. The finalisation of the Quranic text under Uthman around 650, the establishment of the *diwan* state register under Umar, the first Arab conquests carrying the early Muslim armies into Syria and Egypt and Sasanian Persia. But the structural rupture came in 661. Ali was assassinated in or around January of that year by a Kharijite named Abd al-Rahman ibn Muljam during prayers at the Great Mosque of Kufa. Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan — long-time governor of Syria, who had refused to recognise Ali's caliphate during the First Fitna — was proclaimed caliph at Damascus. He founded the Umayyad Caliphate, the first hereditary dynasty in Islamic history. The capital moved from Medina, the Prophet's city, to Damascus, the imperial Syrian centre. The consultative *shura* model of caliphal succession was replaced by hereditary monarchy. The bureaucracy was centralised on Greek and Persian imperial-administrative models. Imperial titles and courtly practices were adopted. The contemporary Islamic historiographical tradition, from al-Tabari forward, treats the Umayyad turn as the moment at which the religious community became a dynastic empire. The Sunni and Shia captures of Chapters 5 and 6 trace the divergent institutional developments from this point.

For Buddhism the central transition is dated to the third century BCE under the patronage of Emperor Ashoka. The first two Buddhist councils — at Rajgriha around 483 BCE under King Ajatashatru and Mahakasyapa, at Vesali around 383 BCE on the ten disciplinary points — were internal to the sangha and concerned with the preservation and consistency of the Buddha's teachings. But Ashoka's accession to the Mauryan throne around 268 BCE, his conquest of Kalinga around 260 BCE (the war he documents in his Thirteenth Rock Edict as the moment of his turn toward *dhamma* after the documented hundred thousand killed and hundred fifty thousand wounded). And the Third Buddhist Council convened at Asokarama in Pataliputra around 250 BCE under the presidency of Moggaliputta Tissa with around a thousand monks attending. These established the state-sangha relationship that would define institutional Buddhism for the next two and a half millennia. The Pali Canon was finalised. The sangha was purged of imposters who had joined for the state patronage. The Dhamma Mahamatras, state officers charged with promoting Buddhist *dhamma*, were established as imperial institutional infrastructure. Ashoka's son Mahinda was sent on the mission to Sri Lanka around 247 BCE, founding what would become the Theravada tradition's institutional centre at the Mahavihara monastery in Anuradhapura, with his sister Sanghamitta following with a sapling of the Bodhi tree itself. The sangha was transformed from a movement of itinerant mendicants into a settled, landed, state-supported monastic institution. There is, in fairness to the scholarly debate, a documented contention about the historicity of the Third Council specifically: Ashoka's own edicts do not mention it, and the surviving Sri Lankan chronicles (the *Dipavamsa* of the 4th century and the *Mahavamsa* of the 5th–6th centuries) are several centuries later than the events they record. The volume documents the council as the tradition transmits it and names the dispute. The broader institutional transformation under Ashoka — the state patronage, the stupa construction, the missions, the Pali Canon, the Mahinda mission to Sri Lanka — is established. The Buddhist capture of Chapter 9 will trace the state-sangha relationship forward through the contemporary captured hierarchies.

For Hinduism the transition was gradual, not sharp, and the volume names the gradualness honestly. The post-Vedic Brahmana texts of around 900 to 700 BCE codified the elaborate sacrificial system administered by the Brahmin priestly caste. The Upanishadic and Sramana movements of the 8th through 4th centuries BCE — the Upanishads, the Buddha, the Jains, the Ajivikas — emerged in documented response to theisation of the Brahmanical system, challenging the necessity of sacrificial mediation; the response across the subsequent centuries was Brahmanical reabsorption rather than displacement. The Dharmashastra literature, including the *Manusmriti* compiled across roughly 200 BCE to 200 CE, codified caste, the four ashramas, the four varnas, and the religious law that would govern institutional Hinduism through the medieval period. The Gupta dynasty of 320 to 550 CE consolidated Hindu temple construction, the major Puranas, and the Vaishnava and Shaiva sectarian traditions; the Gupta period is often called the classical age of Hinduism. The medieval South Indian temple-economy under the Pallavas, Pandyas, Cholas, and Vijayanagara from the 6th to the 16th centuries produced the large landed temple complexes with state-managed treasuries and named priestly lineages holding hereditary office that the colonial encounter would later inherit. The Hindu capture of Chapter 8 picks up at the colonial encounter in 1828, where the modern institutional consolidation begins. The point for § IV is that the structuralisation of Hinduism, while gradual, was the same documented process — clergy, hierarchy, canon, doctrinal apparatus, economic-institutional structure, state patronage — executed across a longer historical timescale.

For Judaism the transitions are three, named and dated. After the return from the Babylonian exile (beginning around 538 BCE under Cyrus the Great's decree), Ezra and Nehemiah are documented in the biblical texts bearing their names as the architects of the post-exilic Jewish institutional reformation. The codification of the Torah around 458 to 398 BCE, the institutionalisation of the priestly and Levitical orders, the centralisation of worship around the rebuilt Second Temple dedicated 516 BCE. The Roman destruction of the Second Temple on the 9th of Av in 70 CE during the First Jewish-Roman War ended the priestly-Temple institutional order. The rabbinic Judaism that emerged across the first three Christian centuries was a documented new institutional form, centred on Torah study and rabbinic interpretation, codified in the Mishnah around 200 CE and subsequently the Talmud. The Jerusalem Talmud around 400 CE, the Babylonian Talmud around 500 CE. The medieval European rabbinical academies, the consolidation of *halakha* through Maimonides's *Mishneh Torah* in 1180 and Joseph Karo's *Shulchan Aruch* in 1565, established the governing rabbinical Judaism that the captures of Chapter 7 will trace from Jewish emancipation in 1789 forward.

For Sikhism the consolidation occurred under the later Gurus across the period from Guru Nanak's death in 1539 to the death of Guru Gobind Singh in 1708. Goindwal was founded as a Sikh centre under Guru Amar Das around 1552, with the *manji* system of missionary territories. Amritsar was founded by Guru Ram Das in 1577. The Adi Granth was compiled by Guru Arjan in 1604 as the first canonical Sikh scripture. The execution of Guru Arjan by the Mughal emperor Jahangir in 1606 was the first martyrdom of a Sikh Guru and the catalyst for the militarisation under Guru Hargobind, who introduced the *miri-piri* doctrine of dual temporal-spiritual authority and wore two swords representing the two kinds of power. Guru Tegh Bahadur was executed by Aurangzeb in 1675. Guru Gobind Singh founded the Khalsa order on 13 April 1699 at Anandpur Sahib, on Vaisakhi day, with the five Beloved Ones and the *amrit* initiation ritual that would define orthodox Sikh institutional membership. Guru Gobind Singh's death in 1708 closed the line of human Gurus; authority passed to the Guru Granth Sahib as scripture and to the Guru Khalsa Panth as the collective body of initiated Sikhs. The Sikh capture of Chapter 10 picks up at the 18th-century Misl period.

The hermetic tradition's relationship to institutional consolidation is structurally different from the other named traditions. Hermetic institutions have formed and dissolved repeatedly across nineteen centuries, often in deliberate resistance to the co-opted institutional churches surrounding them. The late-antique compilation of the *Corpus Hermeticum* in Greco-Roman Alexandria, the Islamic preservation through Jabir ibn Hayyan and the Sabians of Harran. The Renaissance recovery under Ficino at the Medici court in 1463 to 1471, the Rosicrucian manifestos of 1614 to 1616, the Freemasonic Grand Lodge of 1717, the Theosophical Society of 1875, the Golden Dawn of 1887, the Anthroposophical Society of 1912. None of these became state religions. None controlled empires. The section on the hermetic tradition will document the persecutions hermeticism suffered at the hands of the apparatuses that did. The wider occult and esoteric traditions present the same structural feature: the modern named occult orders of the 20th century — Wicca from 1954, the Alexandrian tradition from the 1960s, the Reclaiming tradition from 1980, the Asatru and Heathen revivals — are recent institutional formations, post-dating captured-apparatus chapters of Part II by roughly two millennia in the central cases.

The Indigenous-faith traditions present a complex case worth naming. Some had institutional structures: the Iroquois Great Law of Peace as a formal constitutional arrangement; the Yoruba Ifa as a structured priestly lineage with the Awo training network. Others operated through elder-led and community-led practices without a formal clerical caste. The institutional development of the Indigenous-faith traditions in their documented modern form occurred largely in interaction with — and under suppression by — the colonial-state-missionary apparatuses that Chapter 11 will document. The institutionalising agents in many cases were the colonising churches, not the Indigenous communities themselves; what survived the colonial encounter is what Chapter 11 documents.

The structural pattern repeats. A clergy. A hierarchy. A canon. A doctrinal apparatus. An economic-institutional structure. State or imperial alignment. The pattern is documented across the named traditions, at different timings, with different sharpness, with different specific institutional forms. Without the infrastructure built across these documented transitions, the captures the rest of this volume traces could not have occurred. A movement cannot be captured by external interests at the scale the volume documents until it has the structural machinery that can be captured. The captures of Part II are operating on the machinery the transitions of § IV produced.

This is the architectural pivot. § II named the founders. § III named the work the foundational communities were doing. § IV names the moment at which each tradition acquired the governing apparatus that would be captured. § V will name what the believers across these institutionalised traditions have. On the documentary record, continued to share at the level of lived faith beneath captured apparatuses. Part II will document the seizures.

The institutions came. The captures came after.

---


### V.

Across the foundational records of the named traditions, there are documented commonalities.

This is the comparative-religion record. It is not the metaphysical claim. The traditions documented in § II — Hindu, Buddhist, Jewish, Christian, Islamic, Sikh, Indigenous-faith, hermetic, and the wider occult and esoteric — share, at the level of their foundational texts, their foundational practices, their foundational ethical teachings, and their foundational contemplative reports, a set of themes that the academic comparative-religion literature has documented across more than a century of scholarly work. What the documented sharing *means* is contested, and the volume will name the contesting interpretations without endorsing any of them. What the sharing is, as a documentary record, is established. The volume names it before Part II opens.

The themes are these.

The transcendent is repeatedly named, across the traditions, as something the language of the tradition cannot fully capture. The Upanishads called it *neti neti*, "not this, not this" — the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad in 2.3.6 and 4.5.15 articulating the negation as the only honest approach to the most fundamental reality. Adi Shankara in the eighth and ninth centuries developed the doctrine of *nirguna Brahman*, Brahman without attributes. The Buddha, when pressed in the *Cūla-Mālunkya Sutta* and the *Aggi-Vacchagotta Sutta* on the metaphysical questions about the nature of the cosmos and what survives after death, refused the questions as wrongly framed, and Nagarjuna in the second or third century developed the Mahayana doctrine of *śūnyatā*, emptiness, as the articulation of what conceptual language cannot reach. The Hebrew tradition prohibited the pronunciation of the divine name and gave the world Maimonides's *via negativa* in the *Guide for the Perplexed* of around 1190. Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite in the early sixth century wrote of "the divine darkness as the unapproachable light," and the Christian mystical tradition through Meister Eckhart and the anonymous *Cloud of Unknowing* of the late fourteenth century developed the apophatic theology in continuity with the Areopagite. The Sufi tradition under al-Junayd of Baghdad in the ninth and tenth centuries developed the doctrine of *fanā'*, the annihilation of the self in God, alongside the *tanzīh* theology of transcendence beyond all attributes. The *Corpus Hermeticum* articulated repeatedly that the One is beyond all naming. The Sikh Mool Mantar opens by naming the divine first as One — *Ik Onkar* — and then by negation: *Akal Murat*, without form; *Ajuni*, beyond birth; *Saibhang*, self-existent. The Lakota *Wakan Tanka* and the named Indigenous Australian *Tjukurpa* refuse final translation in the same structural manner. William James named ineffability as one of the four marks of mystical experience in *The Varieties of Religious Experience* of 1902. Rudolf Otto in *Das Heilige* of 1917 named the *numinous* as the structural category across traditions. The documented record is consistent across more than a century of comparative scholarship.

The ethical reciprocity principle — the Golden Rule in its various positive and negative formulations — has been documented in some form in every major tradition § II treated. The detailed textual citations are in § III: Confucius's *Analects* 15:23, Hillel's reformulation recorded in Talmud Shabbat 31a, Matthew 7:12, the Buddhist *Udāna-Varga* 5:18, the *Mahabharata* *Anushasana Parva* 113.8, Nawawi's Forty Hadiths 13. The cross-tradition convergence on this principle is one of the most heavily documented findings of comparative religion, surveyed in Jeffrey Wattles's *The Golden Rule* of 1996 and John Hick's *An Interpretation of Religion* of 1989.

The recognition of impermanence runs across the record. The Hindu *anitya*, articulated in the Bhagavad Gita 2.13–2.17. The Buddhist *anicca*, foundational to the tradition as one of the three marks of existence alongside *dukkha* and *anattā*. The Hebrew Ecclesiastes — *hevel havalim* — vanity of vanities, vapour of vapours. The Pauline "the form of this world is passing away" in 1 Corinthians 7:31. The Quranic 28:88, "all things shall perish save His Face." The hermetic doctrine of the soul's passage through impermanent material forms in the *Poimandres*. The Lakota *mitakuye oyasin* naming the interdependence of all impermanent forms within the sacred whole. The recognition is the same recognition, in different idioms, across traditions that did not share borders, languages, or institutions.

Compassion and care for the vulnerable are documented across every named tradition as a foundational ethical commitment, in formulations whose detailed citations are in § III. Contemplative practice — quietness, attention, the cultivation of receptive awareness, the practice of meditation in some form — is documented across every named tradition, in formulations whose detailed citations are also in § III, and whose neuroscientific verification is anchored to Brewer and the Davidson laboratory and the broader literature. The recognition that the human stands in a more-than-human field of meaning — whether monotheistic, polytheistic, monistic, non-theistic, ancestral, or impersonally hermetic — is documented across every named tradition. The transmission of the moral framework through ritual and story is documented across every named tradition. The cognitive-science-of-religion research of Whitehouse and his colleagues, anchored to § III, has documented the empirical mechanisms by which the transmission operates.

This is what the comparative-religion record shows. It is documented. It is the work of more than a century of academic study across many institutions, many methodologies, and many positions in the underlying philosophical debate. The shared themes are not the discovery and not the claim. They are the established record.

What the shared themes *mean* is contested.

One named interpretation, going back to the Italian Renaissance, is the perennialist position. The Latin phrase *philosophia perennis* was coined by Agostino Steuco — 1497 to 1548 — in his treatise *De perenni philosophia libri X*, published at Lyon in 1540 on the eve of the Council of Trent. Steuco drew on the earlier Renaissance Hermetic-Platonic synthesis of Marsilio Ficino and Giovanni Pico della Mirandola, and proposed that "there is one principle of all things, of which there has always been one and the same knowledge among all peoples." The position was developed by Symphorien Champier. Francesco Giorgio, Nicholas of Cusa, Guillaume Postel, and in the seventeenth century by Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, who used the term in his correspondence. The standard scholarly history of the early period is Charles B. Schmitt's "Perennial Philosophy: From Agostino Steuco to Leibniz" in the *Journal of the History of Ideas* in 1966, and Wilhelm Schmidt-Biggemann's *Philosophia perennis* of 2004 is the contemporary comprehensive monograph.

In the nineteenth century the position was taken up by the New England Transcendentalists, and by the Theosophical Society — founded in New York in 1875 by Helena Blavatsky, Henry Steel Olcott, and William Quan Judge — which popularised it under the Theosophical label of *Wisdom-Religion*. In the twentieth century the Traditionalist School — René Guénon, 1886 to 1951. Ananda Coomaraswamy, 1877 to 1947; Frithjof Schuon, 1907 to 1998; Titus Burckhardt, 1908 to 1984; Martin Lings, 1909 to 2005; Seyyed Hossein Nasr, born 1933 — articulated the position in metaphysically strong terms, with Schuon's *De l'unité transcendante des religions* of 1948 (English: *The Transcendent Unity of Religions*, 1953) as the central text. Aldous Huxley's *The Perennial Philosophy* of 1945 became the most widely-read modern statement of the position, summarising the core perennialist claim in four points: an underlying Divine Ground of being. A human capacity for direct knowing of that Ground; a spark within the human continuous with that Ground; and the realisation of that unity as the purpose of religious practice. Huston Smith — 1919 to 2016 — articulated the position with extraordinary clarity in *The Religions of Man* of 1958 (revised as *The World's Religions* in 1991), in *Forgotten Truth: The Common Vision of the World's Religions* of 1976. And in his 1987 *Journal of the American Academy of Religion* essay "Is There a Perennial Philosophy?" Ken Wilber, born 1949, has developed the position within his "Integral Theory" programme from the 1977 *Spectrum of Consciousness* forward. Robert K.C. Forman has defended a version of the position in *The Problem of Pure Consciousness* of 1990 and *Mysticism, Mind, Consciousness* of 1999.

The perennialist interpretation is a contested position. The volume names it for what it is: one interpretation of the documented commonalities, articulated by a documented intellectual tradition with named historical and contemporary proponents, with internal variation across the proponents on the precise metaphysical claims, and with a considerable body of contemporary academic critique.

The contesting positions are also documented and named.

The constructivist or contextualist school in the academic study of mysticism, articulated foundationally by Steven T. Katz of Boston University in his 1978 edited volume *Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis* and developed in three subsequent edited volumes — *Mysticism and Religious Traditions* of 1983, *Mysticism and Language* of 1992, *Mysticism and Sacred Scripture* of 2000 — holds that mystical experiences are not pre-experientially "pure" but are constructed by the linguistic, cultural, and religious framework of the mystic. There are no unmediated experiences. The apparent cross-tradition commonalities are produced by similar conceptual structures and similar contemplative-formation programmes, not by a common underlying reality. Cross-tradition comparison must therefore proceed with attention to difference, not assumed similarity. Robert Gimello at Notre Dame has been the prominent Buddhist-studies constructivist. The position is now the mainstream academic position in comparative mysticism studies. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry on Mysticism documents the debate and treats the constructivist position as the dominant contemporary view.

The exclusivist position within each tradition — that the holder's own tradition is the truth and the others are at best partial — is the documented institutional position of most clergy across most traditions, including the Catholic magisterium, conservative Protestant theology, Salafi and Wahhabi Sunni Islam, certain Shia institutional positions, certain Orthodox-Jewish positions, and the Hindu-nationalist position. The exclusivist position is not a single argument but a recurring institutional posture across the apparatuses the rest of the volume will document.

The pluralist position, articulated most influentially by John Hick — 1922 to 2012 — in *An Interpretation of Religion: Human Responses to the Transcendent* of 1989, distinguishes itself from both perennialism and exclusivism: the major traditions are different culturally-conditioned responses to a single transcendent reality, the nature of which is beyond direct human knowing. Paul Knitter and Wilfred Cantwell Smith have developed the position in different directions. Hick held that perennialism makes too strong a metaphysical claim and that exclusivism is untenable on the comparative-religion evidence.

The post-modern emphasis on difference, articulated by Talal Asad in *Genealogies of Religion* of 1993, by Russell McCutcheon in *Manufacturing Religion* of 1997, and by Tomoko Masuzawa in *The Invention of World Religions* of 2005. Argues that the cross-tradition commonalities are over-stated, that the differences are constitutive rather than incidental, and that the perennialist project (and to some extent the comparative-religion project itself) is a Western intellectual construction imposed on traditions that did not see themselves through this lens.

This volume does not adjudicate among these positions. The volume is not a work in philosophy of religion. The volume documents what is documented and attributes what is interpretation. The reader who holds the perennialist position can read what the comparative-religion record shows. The reader who holds the constructivist position can read the same record. The reader who holds the exclusivist position of her own tradition can read the same record. The volume is silent on which interpretation is correct, and the volume insists, against any reader's impulse to require it to take a side, on remaining silent. The volume is about the compromised institutional apparatus and what to do about it. The volume is not about which faith is true.

What the volume does claim, architecturally, is this: the unity that the co-opted institutional apparatuses of Part II will be documented as exhibiting — the cross-tradition coordination of method, of financial flows, of political alignments, of operational practice that cuts across the official theological differences — is one kind of cross-tradition unity. The cross-tradition commonality documented in this section, at the level of the believers' lived faith and the foundational records of the traditions, is a different kind of cross-tradition unity. The captured apparatuses have, in the record the volume will trace, been operating in a kind of coordination over the heads of believers who, on the comparative-religion record, share more with each other than the apparatus has wanted them to know.

The unity that matters here is the believer's. The capture is the apparatus's. The volume documents both, and refuses to confuse them. Chapter 31, near the end of the volume, will return to this material at length under the title "The Believers, Across the Traditions". As the positive-space counterpoint to the Devil and the Demons and the Mask That Smiles which follow it. § V at the foundational level of Chapter 1 names what Chapter 31 will return to at the architectural level: the believer's faith, across the traditions, shares a documented common ground beneath the apparatus that has been speaking in its name.

What the believers share is documented in the comparative-religion record. What that sharing means is contested. The volume names the documentation and the contest both, and proceeds.

Part I closes here. Part II opens with the Catholic capture, Constantine to Trent, three hundred and thirteen to fifteen-sixty-three of the common era. The institutions came. The captures came after. The captures begin.

---


# Part II — The Capture of the Church

## Chapter 2. The Catholic Capture, Constantine to Trent (313–1563)

### I. The Constantinian Turn (313–476 CE)

On 13 February 313 of the common era, two emperors of Rome met in the northern Italian city of Mediolanum, modern Milan. Constantine ruled the western empire. Licinius ruled the Balkans and the east. They had previously consolidated their joint position by defeating the rival emperor Maximinus. At Mediolanum they issued a joint statement, preserved in two slightly divergent transmissions — one in the Christian apologist Lactantius's *De mortibus persecutorum*, composed within a year or two of the event, and one in Eusebius of Caesarea's *Historia Ecclesiastica*, completed in revisions through about 325. The statement is now known as the Edict of Milan.

The Edict ended three centuries of intermittent and sometimes intense Roman persecution of Christians. It granted Christianity full legal status within the empire. It returned to the Christian communities — explicitly to the corporate communities and not merely to individual Christians — all the property the Roman state had confiscated during the Diocletianic persecution that had begun in 303. It compensated the current holders of that property from the imperial treasury. It included the formal declaration that "no diminution must be made from the honor of any religion," presenting the policy in a pluralist frame. The Christian church received, within months of the Edict, the documented infrastructure of a legally protected, propertied, and increasingly imperially-supported institution.

What had been built before the Edict and what was built after across the fourth and fifth centuries is what the rest of this section documents. The captures of the next twelve hundred years built on this foundation. The foundation was considerable.

Constantine had already, before the Edict itself, donated the imperial property of the Lateran to the bishop of Rome. The Lateran was an extensive imperial estate in the southeastern quarter of the city; it had previously belonged to the Laterani family and had passed through Constantine's wife Fausta's family to the imperial treasury. Constantine handed it over to Bishop Miltiades. On the property the Basilica Constantiniana was constructed, dedicated probably around 318 to 324; it became, and is to this day, the cathedral of the bishop of Rome — now known as the Archbasilica of Saint John Lateran. Within five years of the Edict, the bishop of Rome had moved from being the leader of an underground community holding services in private homes and rented spaces to being the resident of an imperial palace, presiding over services in an imperial basilica, with the documented property base of a Roman senator several times over.

The construction continued. The first St Peter's Basilica was built over the traditional site of Peter's burial on the Vatican Hill from about 318 to 333. The Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem was begun around 326, with the documented role of Constantine's mother Helena in the identification of the site during her pilgrimage of 326 to 328 and in the discovery of what the tradition would call the True Cross. The Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem was built around 326. Hagia Eirene in the new imperial capital of Constantinople was completed around 330. The Anastasis Basilica in Jerusalem was dedicated in 335. Across two decades, the imperial purse funded the construction of the foundational architectural infrastructure of institutional Christianity at every site that would matter to the tradition for the next millennium and a half.

In a letter from 313 to his proconsul of Africa, Anulinus, Constantine articulated the working logic of the new arrangement. The Christian clergy were to be freed from secular obligations, he wrote, because "when they are free to render supreme service to the Divinity, it is evident that they confer great benefit upon the affairs of state." The benefit was institutional. The clergy were exempted from the *curiales* — the local-council obligations that bankrupted Roman provincial gentry across the late empire. They were exempted from civic taxation. The bishops were given documented jurisdiction in civil disputes through the institution of the *episcopalis audientia*, in which any party could refer a civil case to the bishop's court even against the wishes of the other party, by Constantine's 318 expansion of the original 315 provision. The bishops became, within a generation, magistrates of an institutional system parallel to and increasingly integrated with the imperial one. The church received the legal right to receive testamentary inheritances in 321, the documented mechanism by which the wealth of the Roman senatorial class would flow into institutional Christian hands across the next century. In 321 Constantine also declared Sunday, *dies Solis*, an official day of rest for the urban population of the empire. In 333 he gave bishops authority to confirm or override the verdicts of secular judges in certain categories of cases.

The legal-institutional infrastructure was built within twenty years of the Edict. The accumulation of wealth across that infrastructure would take another century, and would be the foundation of everything the medieval church became.

Then Nicaea.

In May of 325 Constantine convened, at Nicaea in Bithynia — modern İznik in northwest Turkey — what would be called the first ecumenical council of the Christian church. Around 250 to 318 bishops attended; the figure 318 is the traditional one given in *Acts* 7:9 as the number of the patriarch Abraham's household, and the number was almost certainly idealised, but the assembly was the largest gathering of Christian bishops the tradition had ever seen. The emperor opened the council in person, in the imperial palace at Nicaea, in a speech preserved by Eusebius in the *Vita Constantini*, and remained involved in its proceedings across two months of meetings.

The council produced the Nicene Creed in its 325 form, which would be revised at the First Council of Constantinople in 381 into the form still recited every Sunday in Catholic, Orthodox, and most Protestant churches today. The council produced twenty disciplinary canons covering the organisation of the clergy. It condemned the position of Arius, an Alexandrian presbyter who had taught that the Son was a creature subordinate to the Father, as heresy, exiling him along with the bishops who refused to sign the new creed. It standardised the dating of Easter on a calculation reconcilable with the Roman calendar — a documented detail of considerable institutional consequence, since the date of Easter governed the liturgical year of every Christian community in the empire. And it recognised the metropolitan authority structure: the bishops of Rome, Alexandria, and Antioch were named with special jurisdiction over the surrounding territories, in a structure that prefigured what became the system of patriarchates.

What Nicaea established institutionally, beyond any specific doctrinal position, was the mechanism. Doctrinal orthodoxy was now produced by ecumenical councils convened by the emperor, with imperial backing for the enforcement of conciliar decisions, with the legal mechanism for the suppression of dissenting positions, and with the documented partnership between imperial and ecclesiastical authority in the production of binding doctrine for all Christians under Roman jurisdiction. This mechanism — the imperial-conciliar mechanism, as the contemporary scholarship of Henry Chadwick, Richard Hanson, Lewis Ayres, and Harold Drake has documented at length — was the foundational institutional structure of Catholic and Orthodox Christianity for the next eight centuries, and in modified forms it continues to operate to this day.

Constantine died on 22 May 337, baptised on his deathbed by Eusebius of Nicomedia — an Arian-leaning bishop, in a detail the orthodox tradition would find awkward and the historical record establishes. The empire passed to his three sons across a documented succession marked by murders and civil wars. The Christian church inherited the Constantinian apparatus and entered, for the rest of the fourth century, a sustained internal struggle between Nicene and Arian factions, with imperial favour shifting between the parties as one emperor after another took the throne.

Athanasius, bishop of Alexandria, was exiled five times across the period 335 to 366 in this conflict — the documented pattern of imperial-ecclesiastical politics now operating against bishops on the losing side of the doctrinal disputes. Councils were called by emperors to enforce their preferred positions: Tyre in 335, Antioch in 341, Serdica in 343, Milan in 355, the parallel councils of Rimini and Seleucia in 359, Constantinople in 360, Alexandria in 362, Constantinople again in 381. Each produced documented winners and losers. Each empowered the imperial-conciliar mechanism to identify orthodoxy and heresy. Each accustomed the church and the imperial state to operating as a single ecclesiastical-political body.

There was one documented attempt to reverse the trajectory. The emperor Julian, called the Apostate by the tradition he opposed, reigned from late 361 to June of 363. He had been raised Christian — his mother was a Christian, his cousin Constantine was the emperor who had built the Christian church — but he had documented in his surviving writings his abandonment of Christianity in favour of a philosophical paganism rooted in Neoplatonism. As emperor he restored the pagan temples, removed the clerical exemptions, withdrew imperial financial support from Christian institutions, and attempted to organise a pagan parallel infrastructure to the Christian one — a documented effort to establish pagan hospitals and charitable institutions modelled on what the Christian institutions had become. He was killed in battle against the Sasanian Persians on 26 June 363. The policies died with him. The institutional Christianisation resumed under his Christian successors.

By the second half of the fourth century the Cappadocian Fathers. Basil of Caesarea, Gregory of Nazianzus, and Gregory of Nyssa — were producing the developed Trinitarian theology that consolidated Nicene orthodoxy on the doctrinal side, and Basil was building, at Caesarea in Cappadocia in the 370s, the *Basileias*: a complex of hospitals, hospices, leper colonies, and poor relief that documented the first institutional Christian charitable infrastructure at large scale. The Basilian *Asketikon* — the Greater and Lesser Rules — became the foundational document of Eastern Christian monasticism, transmitting a documented institutional framework that governed monastic life in the Greek-speaking world for the next millennium and a half.

Then Theodosius.

On 27 February 380 the emperors Theodosius I, Gratian, and Valentinian II issued the Edict of Thessalonica, preserved in the *Codex Theodosianus* compiled in 438 at 16.1.2. The edict declared that all subjects of the empire should follow the religion "which the divine apostle Peter delivered to the Romans" — that is, Nicene Christianity in the form held by Damasus of Rome and Peter of Alexandria. Non-Nicene Christians were declared heretics. Their assemblies were declared not to be churches. They were liable, in the edict's documented language, to "divine vengeance and . . . punishment of our authority." Within a year the First Council of Constantinople, the second ecumenical council. Finalised the Nicene Creed in the form still recited today, condemned the remaining non-Nicene positions, and named the see of Constantinople as second only to Rome in the church's developing structure of patriarchates.

Within ten years of Thessalonica, the Theodosian campaign against the visible pagan infrastructure of the empire was in full operation. *Codex Theodosianus* 16.10.10 of 24 February 391 prohibited entry into pagan temples and any form of sacrifice. *Codex Theodosianus* 16.10.11 of June 391 extended the prohibition to Egypt; within months Theophilus, the bishop of Alexandria, led the documented destruction of the Serapeum. One of the great temples of the Roman East, the cult centre of Serapis with its renowned library, levelled to its foundations in 391, with the Christian sources celebrating the destruction and the surviving pagan sources documenting the loss. *Codex Theodosianus* 16.10.12 of 8 November 392 issued the comprehensive prohibition: no sacrifice, no entry into temples, no domestic shrines, no private rituals. The legal infrastructure for the systematic dismantling of pagan religious practice was in place. The Eleusinian Mysteries, the oldest continuous religious institution of the Greek-speaking world, documented as operating without interruption from the second millennium BCE, were terminated around 396, with Alaric's Goths under the documented encouragement of Archbishop Theophilus carrying out the final closure.

The Catholic church had, within eighty years of the Edict of Milan, gone from being a persecuted minority within the empire to being the only legal religion of the empire, with the legal-institutional infrastructure to suppress all rival religious practice, and with the imperial-conciliar mechanism for producing binding orthodoxy for all subjects. The structural consequences for the inheritance of late-Roman wealth were considerable.

Peter Brown's *Through the Eye of a Needle*, published in 2012 by Princeton University Press, is the standard contemporary scholarly account of the wealth transfer that occurred across the period 350 to 550. The named patterns Brown documents: the Roman senatorial families — the Anicii, the Symmachi, the Caecinae, the Petronii — converted to Christianity across the late fourth and fifth centuries, in many cases under pressure from their Christian wives and Christian daughters, and on conversion they transferred or bequeathed considerable portions of their estates to the church. The documented examples are extraordinary in their scale. The Sicilian senator Pinianus and his wife Melania the Younger, around 408, freed around eight thousand slaves and gave away their estates across Sicily, North Africa, Spain, and Britain. Paulinus of Nola, on his conversion around 394, gave away an estate the ancient sources value at around two million solidi — the largest documented single act of Christian aristocratic philanthropy in the historical record. The family of Olybrius and Demetrias gave heavily across the early fifth century. The Anicii family — the most prominent Roman senatorial dynasty of the period — were major patrons of the structural church across multiple generations.

The mechanism by which the wealth was received and managed produced its own institutional infrastructure. The bishop's *matricula* — the documented list of registered poor within the diocese who received institutional support — became the operating administrative unit of charitable distribution. The *xenodochia* — hospices for travellers, pilgrims, and the sick — were built across the empire with the bequest funds. The institutional church operated as creditor and lender to its own poor, with the documented mechanism that Brown analyses at length. By 450 the Catholic church was the largest single landholder in the western empire, with the Roman church alone holding documented estates across Italy, North Africa, Sicily, Gaul, Spain, and the Eastern Mediterranean. The *Patrimonium Petri* — the estates of the see of Rome itself — was the foundation of what would become, across the medieval period, the temporal power of the papacy.

The patristic theological tradition continued to produce work of permanent significance across this period. Augustine of Hippo, born 354, baptised by Ambrose of Milan in 387, bishop of Hippo Regius in North Africa from 395 until his death in 430, wrote in his *Confessions* and his *City of God* and his many shorter works the foundational theology of Western Christianity for the next millennium and a half. Jerome, from around 382 to his death in 420, produced the Latin Vulgate translation of the Bible that became the standard biblical text of the Latin West until the Reformation. Pope Leo I, bishop of Rome from 440 to 461, wrote the Tome of Leo that proved central to the Christological definition at the Council of Chalcedon in 451 and articulated the developed doctrine of papal primacy the medieval popes inherited. Pope Gelasius I, bishop of Rome from 492 to 496, formulated the documented "two swords" doctrine in his letter to the Byzantine emperor Anastasius — the position that the spiritual and temporal authorities are distinct and parallel, with the spiritual standing above the temporal — which became the foundational claim of medieval papal political theory.

The documented work of these figures honours the early Christian intellectual tradition. The volume names them as part of what was being built. The volume also documents that what was being built was an institution — a propertied, legally privileged, doctrinally orthodox, imperially aligned, hierarchically organised institutional church — and that this institution became, in the centuries the next sections trace, the central religious-political-financial apparatus of the European world.

By 476 the Western Roman Empire was gone. Romulus Augustulus, the last Western emperor, was deposed by the Germanic general Odoacer on 4 September of that year. The empire in the East continued for another thousand years from Constantinople. In the West, the imperial structure fragmented into the named successor kingdoms: the Visigoths in Spain, the Franks in Gaul, the Ostrogoths in Italy under Theoderic from 493, the Vandals in North Africa. The Catholic church remained. The bishop of Rome remained in the Lateran. The estates remained. The hierarchy remained. The legal privileges, which the new kingdoms in most cases inherited from Roman law, remained. The Roman bishop, now increasingly styled *Papa* — "Father" — and eventually *Pontifex Maximus* in a title transferred from the pagan imperial cult of Rome, had begun the documented process of taking over the administrative functions of the failed imperial structure in the West. The barbarians of the successor kingdoms found the governing church as a fact on the ground, with property, hierarchy, legal protection, and the documented imperial-conciliar mechanism for producing orthodoxy now operating without an empire to convene the councils.

The institution had been built in 163 years. The capture, in the sense Volume One uses the word, was not yet what it would become — the four-century mechanism would not exist for another twelve hundred years. But the *institution* was now built. The clergy, the hierarchy, the canon, the doctrinal apparatus, the economic-institutional structure, the documented partnership with state power, the legal mechanisms for the suppression of rival positions. All were in place. § II will trace the medieval consolidation of what had been built. § III will trace the late-medieval crisis and the financial machinery that grew up around the institution. § IV will trace the Reformation crisis and the response that would define the Catholic church through the next four centuries.

The institution was built. The captures the volume will document operated, across the next twelve hundred years, on the foundation that the Constantinian turn had produced.

---


### II. The Medieval Consolidation (500–1300 CE)

The institution that survived the fall of Rome built itself, across eight hundred years, into the dominant religious-political-financial apparatus of western Europe.

It built itself through documented administrative innovations, documented territorial acquisitions, documented forged legal instruments, documented theological consolidations of supreme authority, documented military campaigns financed and indulged by the apparatus itself, and documented mechanisms for the suppression of any community or movement that threatened its claim. The volume names the dated turning-points and the named figures who presided over them. The medieval Catholic apparatus that the captures of Part III will operate upon was constructed across the period this section documents. The construction was not accidental, and it was not a side-effect of belief. It was institutional work, carried out by named operators with named instruments across named decades.

The work begins with Gregory.

Gregory I, born around 540 to the Roman senatorial family of the Anicii — the same family the Brown 2012 scholarship documented in § I as among the major aristocratic donors to the post-Constantinian church — served as prefect of the city of Rome around 573 before withdrawing to monastic life. Elected pope on 3 September 590, he died in office on 12 March 604. In his fourteen-year papacy he established the administrative model that subsequent medieval popes inherited. The *Patrimonium Petri* — the estates of the see of Rome, accumulated across the two and a half centuries since the Constantinian donations — was reorganised under a documented system of *rectores* and *defensores* governing the regional holdings across Italy, Sicily, Sardinia, North Africa, Dalmatia, and southern Gaul. The *Registrum epistolarum*, the surviving collection of around 850 of Gregory's letters from his papacy, documents the detail of the administration: rent collection, agricultural management, the legal-financial defence of church property against the Lombard kingdoms encroaching on Roman territory, the documented economic and political management of the Roman church as a major landed institution. The Roman liturgy was reformed; the Gregorian chant tradition, though the precise attribution of specific musical material to Gregory himself is contested in contemporary scholarship, takes its name from his work. The Augustine of Canterbury mission, dispatched in 596 and arriving in Kent in 597, began the documented conversion of Anglo-Saxon England that produced the English church operating in formal communion with Rome from then forward. Gregory the Great is one of the four traditional Latin Doctors of the Church and is by general consent the foundational figure of the medieval Latin Christian institutional tradition. The volume names him for what he was: the architect of the administrative church that would dominate western Europe for the next thousand years.

What Gregory administered was bounded by the fact of the Lombard kingdom occupying much of the former Roman Italy. The political-territorial transformation that gave the papacy its independent temporal base came in the eighth century, through the documented alliance with the Frankish Carolingian dynasty.

In 751 Pippin the Short — known by the Latin tradition as Pepin — Mayor of the Palace under the failing Merovingian dynasty, deposed the last Merovingian king Childeric III with the documented support of Pope Zachary. Boniface of Mainz anointed Pepin king of the Franks. The papacy had explicitly authorised the dynastic transfer. In 754 Pope Stephen II crossed the Alps in person, the first reigning pope ever to do so, and on 28 July at the Basilica of Saint-Denis personally anointed Pepin and his sons as kings of the Franks — securing the papal-Carolingian alliance with the most public possible religious legitimation. In 755 and 756 Pepin invaded Italy against the Lombards under King Aistulf, defeated them, and transferred the conquered territories — the former Exarchate of Ravenna and the Pentapolis — to the papacy in the documented Donation of Pepin of 756. This was the foundational legal-political instrument establishing the Papal States, the temporal sovereign territory the popes would rule, with various boundaries and interruptions, from 756 until the Italian unification of 1870. On Christmas Day 800, Pope Leo III crowned Pepin's son Charlemagne — Charles the Great, around 747 to 814 — as Roman Emperor in St Peter's Basilica. The papal-imperial alliance had been formalised at the foundational level: the Pope crowned the Emperor, and the Emperor protected the Pope.

In the same decade as the Donation of Pepin, almost certainly between 751 and 756, an unknown author within or close to the papal curia composed the document that would become the most consequential forgery in the documented history of European religious-political institutional life.

The Donation of Constantine — *Constitutum domini Constantini imperatoris* in its Latin title — purported to be a fourth-century decree by the emperor Constantine I, recording that on or around the year 315 Constantine had been cured of leprosy by Pope Sylvester I and. In gratitude, had granted to the Pope supreme spiritual authority over all Christendom and supreme temporal authority over the entire western Roman empire — Rome, Italy, and all the western provinces. The Pope, in his magnanimity, had returned temporal authority over the East to the imperial successors, retaining for himself sovereign rule over the western half. The forgery's text dated itself to 30 March of an unspecified year and contained an internal chronological error — naming the consul of 315 as Gallicanus, who was actually consul of 317 — which the linguistic-philological analysis of Lorenzo Valla in 1440 would identify as one of many evidences of inauthenticity.

The composition timing aligns with precision to the political moment. The papacy was negotiating with Pepin the Short for the territorial donation that would become the Papal States. The forgery served to provide a documentary basis for the papal claim to the territories — to give the Donation of Pepin the appearance not of a new grant but of a restoration of long-standing papal rights established four centuries earlier by Constantine himself. The probable authorial location is the papal chancery under Stephen II or his immediate successor Paul I. Some contemporary scholarship has argued the forgery might also have been intended to support the broader papal political-territorial position against the Byzantine East. The intended audience and the precise authorial location remain debated in current scholarship; the dating of composition to the period 751 to 756 is the contemporary scholarly consensus.

The oldest surviving manuscript copy of the Donation, datable to the ninth century, is preserved at the Bibliothèque nationale de France in Paris as MS Latin 2777. The document was included in the Pseudo-Isidorian Decretals — themselves another major medieval forgery, composed around 847 to 853 — and through that inclusion entered the developing corpus of medieval canon law. It was subsequently incorporated into Anselm of Lucca's canonical collection in the eleventh century, into Deusdedit's collection in the same period, and most famously into Gratian's *Decretum* of around 1140, the foundational text of medieval canon law that would govern legal practice within the Western Church for the next four hundred years. The Donation was cited as authoritative by at least ten popes for whom we have documented records, including Pope Leo IX in his correspondence with the Patriarch Michael Cerularius of Constantinople in 1054, in the disputes that culminated in the Great Schism between Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Christianity.

Doubts emerged. Pope Otto III declared the document a fraud around 1001; the declaration was not implemented institutionally. Nicholas of Cusa, the German humanist and cardinal, cast doubts in his *De concordantia catholica* around 1433. The decisive exposure came in 1440 from Lorenzo Valla — Italian priest, humanist, classical scholar, and then secretary to King Alfonso of Aragon, who was at that moment in territorial conflict with Pope Eugene IV. Valla composed *De falso credita et ementita Constantini Donatione declamatio*, "Discourse on the False Claim and Forged Donation of Constantine." His method was philological: the Latin of the document contained vocabulary unknown in the fourth century, employed grammatical constructions that had developed only in the eighth, and made factual errors no contemporary of Constantine could have made. The document was a forgery. Valla suspected, as he wrote in the *Declamatio*, that the Church knew the document was inauthentic and had continued to use it anyway. The text was circulated in manuscript across the next seventy years; it was first printed by the German humanist Ulrich von Hutten in 1517 — the year, by historical coincidence or not, of Martin Luther's Ninety-Five Theses. The text became popular among Protestant reformers; an English translation was published for Thomas Cromwell in 1534. In 1559 Valla's work was placed on the Catholic Church's *Index Librorum Prohibitorum*. The Catholic Church did not formally admit the forgery until the nineteenth century.

Dante Alighieri, writing the *Inferno* around 1308 to 1314 — three hundred years before the forgery was exposed — accepted the document as genuine but condemned the spiritual consequences of the papal acquisition of temporal wealth. *Inferno* canto 19, lines 115 to 117:

> *Ahi, Costantin, di quanto mal fu matre,
> non la tua conversion, ma quella dote
> che da te prese il primo ricco patre!*

"Ah, Constantine, how great an evil was mother, not your conversion, but that dowry which the first rich father received from you." Dante placed the simoniac popes — those who had bought or sold spiritual office, head downward in burning rock — in the third pouch of the eighth circle of hell. The institutional church Dante denounced was operating, at the highest level, on a forged legal instrument that the church had known was problematic at least since Otto III three centuries earlier, and would not formally admit was fraudulent for another five centuries after Dante. The volume names this for what it was: an institution that had built its territorial temporal power on a documented forgery, knew or had ample reason to know the forgery was a forgery, and continued to operate on the forged basis for nine centuries until the documentary evidence became impossible to deny.

The Donation of Constantine was one of the operating legal-political instruments of the medieval papacy. The Gregorian Reform was the second.

Through the mid-eleventh century the Catholic Church faced the documented crisis of investiture: bishops and abbots, holding considerable wealth and political influence as feudal magnates within the Holy Roman Empire and the various medieval kingdoms, were being appointed by secular rulers. Emperors, kings, dukes — who took for granted that the senior clergy of their realms were instruments of their political authority. The reform party within the church — beginning under Pope Leo IX, intensifying under Nicholas II, and reaching its fullest articulation under Gregory VII — argued that this state of affairs subordinated the church to the state and corrupted its independence. They proposed a structural correction.

In 1059 the bull *In Nomine Domini* of Pope Nicholas II restricted papal election to the College of Cardinals, removing the practical influence of the Holy Roman Emperor and the Roman nobility on papal succession. In 1073 the cardinal Hildebrand of Sovana, born around 1015, was elected pope, taking the regnal name Gregory VII. He was the documented central figure of the reform that bears his name. In 1075 he issued the *Dictatus Papae* — a documented twenty-seven-point papal declaration of supreme authority preserved in his *Registrum*. The claims included: that the Pope alone may be called universal; that he may depose emperors; that he may absolve subjects from their oaths of fealty to wicked rulers; that all princes shall kiss only the feet of the pope; that the Roman church has never erred, nor will it err to all eternity. These were maximal claims. They produced the predictable response from the imperial side. In 1076 Gregory VII excommunicated the German Emperor Henry IV. The Emperor was facing serious internal challenges from his own nobility; the excommunication threatened to absolve his subjects of their oaths of loyalty. On 25 to 28 January 1077 Henry IV stood barefoot in the snow outside the castle of Matilda of Tuscany at Canossa, where Gregory VII was residing, for three days, until the Pope absolved him. The image of the Holy Roman Emperor performing public penance to the Pope is one of the documented founding images of medieval papal political theory.

The reconciliation did not hold. Henry IV recovered his position, marched on Rome, and in 1084 drove Gregory VII into exile at Salerno, where Gregory died the following year. But the structural transformation had been achieved. The papacy had asserted, in the *Dictatus Papae*, claims of universal supreme authority — spiritual and temporal — that the structural Church would maintain for the next eight hundred years. The dispute was formally settled by the Concordat of Worms, signed on 23 September 1122 by Pope Callixtus II and Emperor Henry V, on a compromise: bishops elected by the cathedral chapters, with temporal investiture by the emperor for the German bishops, spiritual investiture by the pope. The Gregorian Reform's broader transformation — the structural consolidation of the medieval papal monarchy — was permanent. From this point onward, the papal claim to supremacy over both spiritual and temporal affairs is the operating doctrine of the Western Church.

Then the Crusades.

On 27 November 1095, Pope Urban II — Odo of Châtillon, a French Cluniac monk elected pope in 1088 — preached at the Council of Clermont a sermon calling for armed expedition to recover the Christian holy places of the East from the Muslim powers that had held them since the seventh century. The documented version of the speech survives in five variant accounts written by chroniclers either present at Clermont or working from those who were, including Fulcher of Chartres, Robert the Monk, Baldric of Dol, and Guibert of Nogent. Urban offered to those who took the cross a plenary indulgence — full remission of the temporal penalties for sin — and the documented assurance of immediate salvation for those who died on the expedition. The First Crusade was launched. It captured Jerusalem on 15 July 1099 in a documented massacre of the city's Muslim and Jewish populations, the numbers cited by medieval chroniclers in maximalist terms (Raymond of Aguilers's "horses wading in blood up to their knees") but estimated by contemporary scholarship in the thousands. The Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem was established, surviving in various forms until the fall of Acre in 1291.

What the Crusades produced institutionally, beyond the temporal kingdoms in the Levant that did not survive, was a set of documented financial-political instruments. The plenary indulgence, initially granted only to crusaders taking the cross, was by the thirteenth century being granted in exchange for monetary payment or specific charitable works funding the Crusades. The expansion of the indulgence system in this period is the foundation of the late-medieval indulgence trade that § III will document at length. The military-religious orders — the Knights Templar founded 1119, the Knights Hospitaller founded around 1099, the Teutonic Knights founded 1190 — became major financial institutions in their own right. The Templars in particular developed documented banking operations across Europe: transfer of funds between distant cities by promissory note, deposit-taking, the issuing of credit. The Templars financed kings. They held the documented treasury of the French crown across much of the thirteenth century. They held estates from Scotland to Cyprus. In 1307 Philip IV of France, deep in debt to the Templars and seeking access to their wealth, arrested the entire French Templar leadership on 13 October 1307, charging them with heresy, sodomy, and idolatry under torture. Pope Clement V — the French pope who had moved the papal court to Avignon — formally suppressed the order at the Council of Vienne in 1312. The Templar wealth was transferred largely to the Hospitallers and to the French crown.

The Fourth Crusade, launched in 1202, never reached its declared destination of Egypt. Indebted to Venice for transport, the crusaders agreed to sack the Catholic Dalmatian city of Zara at the request of Venice in November 1202, for which they were excommunicated by Pope Innocent III in a documented papal letter — the excommunication subsequently quietly withdrawn. In April 1204 they sacked Constantinople, the capital of Eastern Orthodox Christianity, in a documented three-day pillage during which they looted relics, gold, sacred objects, manuscripts, and the bronze horses now displayed on the façade of St Mark's Basilica in Venice. They established the Latin Empire of Constantinople, which lasted from 1204 until the Byzantine recovery in 1261. The Eastern Orthodox tradition has documented the Fourth Crusade as the foundational injury of the Catholic-Orthodox schism, deeper than the formal break of 1054. Pope John Paul II issued formal apologies for the Fourth Crusade in 2001 to Archbishop Christodoulos of Athens and in 2004 to Patriarch Bartholomew of Constantinople. The Vatican Library and Vatican Museums hold documented items taken from Constantinople in 1204. They have never been returned.

In 1209 Pope Innocent III — Lotario dei Conti di Segni, the documented theoretician of the most fully developed medieval papal monarchy, pope from 1198 to 1216 — proclaimed the Albigensian Crusade against the Cathar communities of Languedoc in southern France. The Cathars were a dualist Christian movement documented from around 1145 onward, with a particularly developed presence in the prosperous semi-independent regions under the protection of the Counts of Toulouse and the Trencavel viscounts. The contemporary scholarly debate, particularly R.I. Moore's *The War on Heresy* of 2012, has argued that the systematic theological coherence of the Cathar movement as the medieval inquisitors described it may have been largely an inquisitorial construction; the volume notes the debate without resolving it. What is not contested is that a sizeable regional Christian community in southern France was, in March 1208, proclaimed by the Pope to be subject to a crusade of extermination, in the documented papal-bull language of *extermination* and *expurgation*. The murder of the papal legate Pierre de Castelnau on 14 January 1208 by an agent of Raymond VI of Toulouse provided the immediate casus belli; the structural causes had been developing for decades.

The crusader army assembled in June 1209 under the military command of Simon de Montfort and the papal-legate authority of Arnaud Amaury, abbot of Cîteaux. On 22 July 1209 the city of Béziers was taken by storm and its inhabitants slaughtered. The Cistercian chronicler Caesarius of Heisterbach, writing about a decade after the events in his *Dialogus miraculorum*, attributes to Arnaud Amaury — when asked by a crusader how to distinguish the Cathar inhabitants of the city from the orthodox Catholic inhabitants — the documented reply: *"Caedite eos! Novit enim Dominus qui sunt eius."* "Kill them all. The Lord knows who are his." Arnaud Amaury's own letter to Pope Innocent III, written in August 1209, gives a different account, in which the killing occurred before the leaders could organise a process for separating the orthodox from the heretics. The contemporary historiographical debate about which version represents what was said is documented in the modern scholarship; the volume names both versions honestly. What is not in dispute: Béziers was taken by storm, its inhabitants — Cathar, Catholic, Jewish, and otherwise — were slaughtered, the city was burned, and Arnaud Amaury's own letter to the Pope claimed around twenty thousand killed. The Britannica entry for the Massacre at Béziers gives the figure of twenty thousand Albigensian and citizen losses. The crusade continued for twenty years.

Carcassonne was taken in August 1209. Minerve in 1210. Lavaur in 1211. The Battle of Muret in 1213, in which Peter II of Aragon was killed attempting to defend his Cathar-sympathising vassals against the crusader forces. Simon de Montfort was killed at the siege of Toulouse in 1218 by a stone projectile. The crusade entered a second phase under King Louis VIII of France in 1226. It ended with the Treaty of Meaux-Paris of April 1229, by which the County of Toulouse was absorbed into the French crown. The documented political consequence: Languedoc passed from the semi-independent southern political-cultural sphere into the French royal domain; the Mediterranean coast of France acquired by the French crown. The cultural consequence: the documented distinct Occitan civilisation of southern France, with its troubadour tradition, its regional language, its religious diversity, was permanently subordinated to the northern French royal-religious institutional structure. The mass burning of around two hundred Cathars at Montségur on 16 March 1244 was the closing event of the documented Cathar resistance.

The Albigensian Crusade produced two documented institutional consequences for the Catholic apparatus. First, in 1216 Pope Honorius III confirmed the Order of Preachers — the Dominicans, founded by Dominic of Caleruega out of his preaching campaigns in Languedoc against the Cathars — in the bull *Religiosam vitam*. Second, in 1231 Pope Gregory IX, nephew of Innocent III, issued the bull *Excommunicamus*, formally establishing the *Inquisitio Haereticae Pravitatis* — the Inquisition into Heretical Depravity — and in 1233, in the bull *Licet ad capiendos*, assigned the operation of the new Inquisition to the Dominican Order. In 1252 Pope Innocent IV's bull *Ad extirpanda* authorised the use of torture by inquisitors. The Medieval Inquisition was now operating as an institutional structure: secret denunciations, the protection of accusers' names from the accused, the documented confiscation of convicted heretics' property with the financial flows running to the Church and to cooperating secular rulers. The legal fiction by which the Church transferred unrepentant convicted heretics to the secular arm for execution and thus avoided formal responsibility for the executions it had directed. The inquisitorial manuals of Bernard Gui (*Practica Inquisitionis Heretice Pravitatis*, around 1323) and Nicolau Eymerich (*Directorium Inquisitorum*, 1376) document the operating procedures.

The apparatus was now complete at the medieval scale.

The high-water mark was reached in 1302. Pope Boniface VIII — Benedetto Caetani, pope from 1294 to 1303 — was engaged in a documented conflict with King Philip IV of France over the taxation of the French clergy. On 18 November of that year he issued the bull *Unam sanctam*. The bull's documented core claim, in the strongest medieval formulation: *"We declare, say, define, and pronounce that for every human creature it is altogether necessary for salvation to be subject to the Roman Pontiff."* The doctrine of the two swords. Articulated by Gelasius I eight hundred years earlier in the form documented in § I, was now stated in its strongest medieval terms: the spiritual sword belongs directly to the Church. The temporal sword is to be wielded by secular rulers but under the direction of the Church; the spiritual ranks above the temporal in all things. Every human creature, on this claim, must be subject to the Roman Pontiff for salvation. The claim was made in the year 1302 of the common era by an institution that controlled territorial empires, financed crusades and indulgences. Operated an Inquisition with the documented power to torture and confiscate, drew rents from estates spanning every country of western Europe, and rested its claims on a forged document the institution had used for five and a half centuries.

The political consequence of *Unam sanctam* was swift. Boniface VIII's conflict with Philip IV led to the documented Affair of Anagni on 7 September 1303: Philip's agent Guillaume de Nogaret, acting with the support of Sciarra Colonna of the Roman noble house that had been at war with Boniface's Caetani family, seized the elderly Pope at Anagni in central Italy. Boniface was held captive for three days, struck and humiliated, and released by local townspeople. He died at Rome on 11 October 1303, reportedly of the shock. Two years later, in 1305, Philip IV secured the election of a French pope, Clement V, who moved the papal court from Rome to Avignon in 1309, beginning what would become known as the Avignon Papacy.

The maximum medieval claim had been made. The consequences of having made it would unfold across the next two centuries. § III will document the late-medieval crisis the maximum claim produced: the Avignon Papacy, the Western Schism, the Council of Constance. The developed indulgence trade, the documented integration of the papacy with the leading banking houses of Italy. § IV will document the Reformation crisis and the Catholic institutional response that produced the modern Catholic apparatus through the Council of Trent.

The institution had been consolidated. The institution had reached its medieval maximum. The capture, in the sense Volume One uses the word, was still six and a half centuries away. But the apparatus on which the four-century mechanism would operate had been built — built with documented administrative innovations, documented forged legal instruments, documented military-financial campaigns, and documented machinery of suppression — across the eight hundred years from Gregory the Great to Boniface VIII. The next two sections document what happened to the institution when its own maximum claim collided with the historical forces of the late-medieval and early-modern world.

---


### III. The Late-Medieval Crisis (1300–1500)

The maximum claim collapses on its own arithmetic.

*Unam sanctam* of 1302 had declared that every human creature must be subject to the Roman Pontiff for salvation. Within three years the response from the European political-economic powers had begun: Philip IV of France secured the election of a French pope; the papal court relocated from Rome to Avignon; the governing Catholic Church entered the seventy-year residency on the Rhône that contemporary observers, beginning with Petrarch, would call the Babylonian Captivity. The maximum spiritual claim of *Unam sanctam* was now being made by an institution physically resident on French territory under French royal influence. The contradiction between the universal claim and the dependence on a single European monarchy was, across the next two centuries, the central institutional problem the medieval Catholic Church could not solve.
