The 10 June 1560 death of Mary of Guise, regent for Mary Queen of Scots, removed the French Catholic obstacle to the Scottish Reformation. The Treaty of Edinburgh of 6 July 1560 secured the withdrawal of French and English forces. The Scottish Protestant nobility took control of the governing church reorganisation.

The Reformation Parliament met on 17 August 1560. The Scottish Parliament adopted the documented *Scots Confession of Faith* — drafted within four days by the "six Johns": Knox, John Winram (former Augustinian prior), John Spottiswoode of Mid Calder, John Willock (former Dominican friar), John Douglas (provost of St Mary's College at St Andrews), and John Row (formerly procurator at the papal court in Rome). The same Parliament abolished the jurisdiction of the Bishop of Rome over Scotland; abolished the Mass; and declared all previous acts contrary to the Confession to be of no force.

The first *Book of Discipline* of 1560, drafted by the same six Johns, was the institutional charter of the new Reformed Church of Scotland — the Kirk. provisions for parish-level discipline through kirk sessions, a national educational system at the Scottish parish level, and poor relief. Knox died at Edinburgh on 24 November 1572. The second *Book of Discipline*, drafted by Andrew Melville in 1578, established the full Presbyterian polity — sessions, presbyteries, synods, General Assembly — that defines the Church of Scotland through to the present moment.

The Covenanting period from 1638 to 1660 saw the integration of the Scottish Kirk with the English Parliamentary cause. The National Covenant of 1638 opposed the Laudian liturgical impositions of Charles I. The Solemn League and Covenant — ratified by the Scottish Convention of Estates and the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland on 17 August 1643 and subsequently signed by the English Parliament on 25 September 1643 — brought the Scottish army into the English Civil War on the Parliamentary side. Committed the Church of England to "the nearest conjunction and uniformity in religion, confession of faith, form of church government, directory for worship, and catechizing" with the Church of Scotland. The Westminster Assembly that produced the Westminster Confession of Faith and the Larger and Shorter Catechisms across the period 1643 to 1647 was the outcome of this English-Scottish Reformed alliance.

The Restoration of 1660 produced the suppression of the Scottish Presbyterian settlement. The Act Rescissory of 1661 restored episcopacy in Scotland by royal decree. The persecution of the Presbyterian Covenanters across the "Killing Time" of 1680 to 1688 — the military operations by the named senior royal officers including James Graham of Claverhouse, Viscount Dundee — produced Presbyterian martyrs and a foundational mythology of Scottish Presbyterian persecution that defined the structural memory of the Kirk into the modern period. The Glorious Revolution of 1688 to 1689 restored William of Orange to the thrones of Scotland and England. On 7 June 1690 the Scottish Parliament re-established Presbyterian government in the Church of Scotland and ratified the Westminster Confession. The institutional charter of the modern Church of Scotland.

The English Reformation produced its institutional settlement across one and a half centuries of monarchical oscillation between Protestant and Catholic policies, two civil wars, the Interregnum, the Restoration, and the Glorious Revolution. The final settlement of 1689 produced the modern Church of England as the established Protestant national church with toleration for Trinitarian Protestant Dissenters.

The Henrician break of November 1534, with the Act of Supremacy that declared Henry VIII "Supreme Head on Earth of the Church of England," was documented in Chapter 2 § IV. The consequence across the period 1536 to 1541 was the Dissolution of the Monasteries by Henry VIII via Thomas Cromwell — the largest single transfer of property in English history, with the confiscation of monastic lands and assets across England and Wales producing the financial-political foundation of the post-Reformation English propertied class. The Six Articles of 1539 Henry's theologically Catholic confession — transubstantiation, clerical celibacy, private masses, auricular confession, communion under one kind, monastic vows — alongside the break with Rome. The Henrician settlement was schismatic-Catholic rather than Protestant in doctrine.

The Edwardian Reformation across the period 1547 to 1553 produced the Protestant turn under the Regency of Edward Seymour, Duke of Somerset, and the archbishopric of Thomas Cranmer. The 1549 First Book of Common Prayer, drafted by Cranmer, was followed by the more thoroughly Reformed 1552 Second Book of Common Prayer. The Marian restoration of 1553 to 1558 under Mary I produced the restoration of papal jurisdiction over England via Cardinal Reginald Pole — the same reform cardinal documented in Chapter 2 § IV. The Marian persecutions of 1555 to 1558 produced around two hundred and eighty Protestant executions by burning, including Thomas Cranmer at Oxford on 21 March 1556, Hugh Latimer and Nicholas Ridley together at Oxford on 16 October 1555, and the others recorded in John Foxe's *Acts and Monuments* of 1563. Commonly called "Foxe's Book of Martyrs," the foundational text of post-Reformation English anti-Catholic mythology.

The Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559 produced the governing architecture that defined the Church of England across the next four and a half centuries. Elizabeth I succeeded Mary on 17 November 1558. The Act of Supremacy 1558, passed by Parliament in April 1559, restored royal supremacy with Elizabeth's title as "Supreme Governor" of the Church of England — changed from Henry VIII's "Supreme Head" to accommodate Protestant scruples about a lay person, and a woman in particular, claiming spiritual headship. The Act of Uniformity 1558, also passed in April 1559 and given the operative date of 28 April 1559, mandated the 1559 *Book of Common Prayer* — largely derived from the 1552 Edwardian prayer book with modifications softening the eucharistic theology — for use in all worship services in the Church of England. The Act required Sunday church attendance with fines for non-attendance.

The Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion of 1563, adopted by Convocation under Archbishop Matthew Parker, established the doctrinal standard of the Church of England — Reformed in core doctrine, with Lutheran and patristic elements. The Articles were finalised at Convocation in 1571 and given statutory force by the Subscription Act of 1571, requiring all new ministers to affirm their agreement.

The institutional outcome was the Anglican settlement: Protestant in doctrine, with retention of episcopal church government, retention of much of the medieval liturgical structure in modified Protestant form, and royal supremacy as the operative constitutional principle. The historian David Starkey's summary applies: "a Church that was Protestant in doctrine, Catholic in appearance." The contemporary French ambassador's 1597 report confirmed the character of the settlement: the Church of England as a Protestant-doctrine church that preserved much of the Catholic liturgical inheritance.

The Stuart period from 1603 to 1649 produced the crisis that culminated in the English Civil War. The accession of James VI of Scotland as James I of England in 1603 produced the Hampton Court Conference of 1604 between the King and the Puritan party — and the commission for the King James Bible, published in 1611 as the Authorized Version that became the standard English Protestant Bible for the next three centuries. The accession of Charles I in 1625 produced the Laudian liturgical reforms under William Laud, Archbishop of Canterbury from 1633, which produced Puritan opposition and the confrontation that led to the Long Parliament of 1640, the impeachment of Laud (1640, executed 1645), and the Parliamentary control of religious policy from 1641 onward.

The English Civil War broke out in 1642. The Long Parliament's ordinance of 12 June 1643 summoned the Westminster Assembly of Divines: one hundred and twenty-one ministers, ten Lords, twenty Commons. The Assembly opened on 1 July 1643 at Westminster Abbey, subsequently moving to the smaller Jerusalem Chamber when cold weather came. Typical attendance was around sixty to seventy of the one hundred and twenty-one named members.

The Solemn League and Covenant signed by the English Parliament on 25 September 1643 — the alliance with the Scottish Estates that had ratified the same document at Edinburgh on 17 August — brought the Scottish army into the English Civil War on the Parliamentary side. The commitment: to bring the Church of England into "the nearest conjunction and uniformity in religion, confession of faith, form of church government, directory for worship, and catechizing" with the Church of Scotland. The Westminster Assembly's work across the next four years produced the structural Reformed-Protestant settlement that would define the Reformed tradition across the Anglophone world.

The *Directory for Public Worship* was completed in 1644. The *Westminster Confession of Faith* was completed at Westminster on 29 April 1647 with Scripture proof-texts. The Confession was adopted entire by the General Assembly of the Church of Scotland on 27 August 1647. The Westminster Shorter Catechism was completed in November 1647. The Westminster Larger Catechism was completed across 1647. In April 1648 the Westminster Confession was partly adopted by the English Parliament as the Articles of Christian Religion, omitting sections on Christian Liberty, Marriage and Divorce, Church Censures, and Synods and Councils. Charles I was executed on 30 January 1649. The Interregnum followed.

The Cromwellian Protectorate religious policy across the years 1653 to 1658 produced toleration for most Protestant traditions: Independents, Baptists, Quakers; and the Cromwellian readmission of Jews to England in 1656, after their expulsion in 1290. The religious institutional pluralism of the Interregnum was the opposite of the Anglican-Presbyterian uniformity that the Westminster Assembly had been intended to produce.

The Restoration of 1660 produced the re-establishment of the Anglican episcopacy and the suppression of the Interregnum institutional pluralism. The Act of Uniformity 1662 required clerical assent to the revised Book of Common Prayer of 1662 — still the standard liturgy of the Church of England. The documented "Great Ejection" of 24 August 1662, St Bartholomew's Day, ejected around two thousand Puritan-leaning clergy from Church of England livings for refusing assent — the foundational moment of English Nonconformity. The Conventicle Act of 1664 prohibited religious meetings of five or more persons not according to the Book of Common Prayer. The Five Mile Act of 1665 prohibited Nonconformist ministers from coming within five miles of any place where they had previously ministered. The Test Act of 1673 excluded Catholics and Dissenters from public office by requiring receipt of the Anglican sacrament — the governing mechanism of Anglican political-religious monopoly that would continue in force until the Catholic Emancipation Act of 1829.

The reign of James II from 1685 to 1688 — the Catholic king of Protestant England — produced the constitutional crisis. The Declaration of Indulgence of 1687, extending toleration to Catholics and Dissenters by royal prerogative, produced the political confrontation with the Anglican establishment that, combined with the birth of a Catholic heir to James II in June 1688, triggered the Glorious Revolution. William of Orange landed at Brixham on 5 November 1688. James II fled to France on 23 December 1688. William and Mary were jointly proclaimed sovereigns on 13 February 1689. The Bill of Rights of December 1689 excluded Catholics from the English throne.

The **Toleration Act of 24 May 1689** — formally the Act for Exempting their Majestyes Protestant Subjects dissenting from the Church of England from the Penalties of certaine Lawes — granted religious toleration to Trinitarian Protestant Dissenters: Presbyterians, Independents (Congregationalists), Baptists, Quakers. exclusions: Catholics, Unitarians, and the residual penal apparatus continued to exclude these from full civic life. The Test Acts continued to exclude Dissenters from public office until the Sacramental Test Act repeals of 1828; Catholic Emancipation came in 1829.

The apparatus of post-Reformation Protestantism in Europe took, by the toleration Act of 1689, the forms it would carry into the modern period. Six forms.

The German Lutheran *Landeskirchen* — the territorial state churches under princely sovereignty, tax-funded, with clerical apparatuses trained in confessional Lutheran universities. The Scandinavian Lutheran national state churches — Sweden 1593, Denmark-Norway 1536 to 1540, Finland under Sweden, Iceland 1550 — operating under royal sovereignty across the next four centuries. The Reformed Calvinist confessional formations — the Genevan model with its Consistory discipline from 1541, the French Reformed Huguenot churches until 1685, the Dutch Reformed Church as the state church of the Dutch Republic from 1568 (formalised at Dort 1618-1619). The Reformed Palatinate from 1559, the Reformed cantons of Switzerland, the Reformed Church of Hungary, the German Reformed *Landeskirchen* in Hesse, Brandenburg-Prussia after 1613, and other territories. The Scottish Presbyterian Kirk — the Reformed state church of Scotland from 1560, re-established 1690 after the Restoration interruption. The Church of England — the Anglican settlement under royal supremacy, established 1559 in the Elizabethan settlement, restored 1660 to 1662 after the Interregnum, defined by the Thirty-Nine Articles of 1563/1571 and the Book of Common Prayer of 1662. The Dissenting Protestant traditions — the post-1689 English Nonconformist communities, the Continental Anabaptist communities including the Mennonites after the Schleitheim Confession of 1527, and the persecuted Hussite-descended communities.

The institutional Protestant architecture surveyed across § I was, in institutional terms, a state-religious architecture. The confessional state churches of Lutheran Scandinavia and the German territories; the Reformed-Calvinist confessional formations of Geneva, the Netherlands, the Palatinate, and Scotland; the Anglican state church of England — all operated as institutional arms of the named European sovereign apparatuses. The clerical apparatus was tax-funded. The doctrinal apparatus was state-enforced. The educational apparatus was state-confessional. The persecutions of religious dissenters — Anabaptists across the German territories, Catholics under Protestant regimes, the Servetus execution at Geneva, the Remonstrants expelled at Dort, the Covenanters under the Scottish Restoration, the Nonconformists under the English Restoration — were carried out by state-religious authority operating under the confessional state-church model.

The thing the Reformation had identified as the corruption of the medieval Catholic apparatus — the integration of the church with the political-territorial sovereignty of the named European princes — was the form into which the Lutheran and Reformed Reformations consolidated. The believers of the Lutheran, Reformed, Anglican, Presbyterian, and Anabaptist traditions across the period 1525 to 1700 carried, on the record, the faith of their traditions — the Reformation rediscovery of justification by grace through faith, the Reformation recovery of biblical preaching and lay literacy, the Reformation simplification of the church to its scriptural core. The institutional apparatuses that operated above the believers across the same period operated, on the record, as confessional state churches integrated with the named European sovereign apparatuses, with persecution of their own dissenters and coordination with the political-territorial sovereignties of the named European states.

§ II of this chapter documents the American religious capture across the period 1620 to 1865. The Atlantic migration of the European Protestant traditions across the seventeenth century; the structural consolidation of the American Protestant churches across the colonial period; the Great Awakenings; the antebellum Protestant slavery defence; and the capture across the period 1620 to 1865. The architecture surveyed in § I sets the governing context. § II documents what the Protestant churches became in their American formation.

---


### II. The American Religious Capture (1620–1865)

The Protestant traditions crossed the Atlantic. They came as established state churches in some places, as separatist congregations in others, as proprietary religious settlements in a third class of cases. They consolidated across the colonial period as established colonial churches under royal or proprietary charter. They were contested by the Awakenings of the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. They were disestablished by the Revolutionary and early-Republic statutes between 1776 and 1833. They reconstituted across the early nineteenth century as the voluntary denominational architecture that defined American Protestantism through to the contemporary moment.

By the antebellum period the architecture had taken its operative form. And by the 1840s the major Protestant denominations of the United States — Methodist, Baptist, Presbyterian, Congregational — had split, on the evidence, along sectional lines, with the Southern branches operating as the religious arm of the slaveholding political-economic order. The institutional pattern this volume has been tracing from Constantine onward — the Christian church operating as the religious legitimation apparatus of the dominant political-economic order — recurred, in denominational form, across the American antebellum period. § II documents the period 1620 to 1865.

The Pilgrims landed at Plymouth on 21 December 1620 (11 December Old Style). One hundred and two passengers aboard the *Mayflower* — Separatist Congregationalists from the Scrooby congregation of Nottinghamshire, who had been at Leiden in the Netherlands since 1608, and who had contracted with the Virginia Company of London for passage to the Hudson River area but had been blown off course to Cape Cod. Forty-one of the male passengers signed the Mayflower Compact on 11 November 1620, off the coast of Cape Cod, establishing a civil-religious covenant for the colony they would found at Plymouth.

The Massachusetts Bay Colony followed in 1630 under the Winthrop Fleet. Around seven hundred passengers in eleven ships, the flagship the *Arbella*, sailed under the Massachusetts Bay Company charter of 4 March 1629. John Winthrop, elected governor of the Massachusetts Bay Company before sailing, delivered the sermon *A Model of Christian Charity* — either just before or after the *Arbella*'s departure — containing the passage that became the foundational text of American religious-political self-understanding: *we shall be as a city upon a hill, the eyes of all people are upon us*. The biblical reference is to Matthew 5:14, in Jesus's Sermon on the Mount.

The Great Migration followed across the decade 1630 to 1640: around twenty thousand Puritans crossed to New England, founding the Congregational towns of Massachusetts Bay (Boston, founded 17 September 1630. Salem already established from 1626) and the offshoots that would become Connecticut (1636 under Thomas Hooker), New Haven (1638), and Rhode Island (Providence, 1636, under Roger Williams after his banishment from Massachusetts Bay for religious dissent). The Pilgrim Plymouth Colony was absorbed by Massachusetts Bay in 1691 under the Province of Massachusetts Bay charter.

The New England Congregational churches operated as established state churches of the New England colonies across the entire colonial period. Tax-funded parishes. Mandatory church attendance. The Cambridge Platform of 1648 establishing Congregational polity. The Half-Way Covenant of 1662, adopted by the Synod of Boston, extending baptism to the children of unconverted but baptised parents — the response to the declining-piety problem of the second-generation New England Congregationalists, the evidence that the founding Puritan religious intensity was not transmitting to the children of the founding generation.

The institutional Congregational discipline of the colonial New England period produced the persecutions of the colonial dissenters. Roger Williams, banished from Massachusetts Bay in 1636 for his theological positions on church-state separation and on the Massachusetts charter's invalidity, founded Providence Plantation at Narragansett Bay as the first colonial polity with full religious toleration. Anne Hutchinson was tried in 1637 for theological dissent — the Antinomian Controversy — banished from Massachusetts Bay, and killed by Mohawks in 1643 at the Hutchinson Settlement in what is now the Bronx. The Quaker persecutions across Massachusetts Bay between 1659 and 1661 produced the Boston Martyrs: Mary Dyer, William Robinson, Marmaduke Stephenson, and William Leddra, executed at Boston Common for the offence of returning to Massachusetts as Quakers after banishment. And the Salem Witch Trials of 1692 — nineteen hanged at Gallows Hill across the period 10 June to 22 September 1692, with Giles Corey pressed to death on 19 September; around one hundred and fifty imprisoned — were carried out by the Massachusetts Bay civil authorities under the religious-juridical framework of the Puritan colonial church. The trials closed in May 1693 under intervening Governor William Phips; Massachusetts colony formally apologised in 1702.

The Anglican establishment operated in Virginia, the Carolinas, Maryland after 1692, and Georgia after 1732 — established Church of England in each colony, tithes funding the parish clergy, vestries administering the parish system, the Bishop of London exercising nominal ecclesiastical jurisdiction across the colony though no Anglican bishop was ever appointed for North America during the colonial period.

The middle colonies operated under a religious pluralism that was the structural exception in the colonial American religious landscape. Pennsylvania was founded in 1681 by William Penn under royal charter as a Quaker colony with religious toleration for all Trinitarian Christians. Maryland was founded in 1632 by Cecil Calvert, Second Lord Baltimore, as a refuge for English Catholics, with the Maryland Toleration Act of 21 April 1649 — the first colonial statute granting religious toleration to Trinitarian Christians. New York was founded as Dutch New Netherland in 1624 under the Dutch Reformed Church establishment; after the English conquest of 1664 the Anglican Church was nominally established under the Duke of York's charter, with the religious pluralism of the former Dutch colony continuing under nominal Anglican institutional supervision.

The colonial American religious landscape by 1700 was, in institutional terms, a transplantation of the European confessional state-church model. New England under the Congregational establishment; Virginia and the Carolinas and Maryland under the Anglican establishment; Pennsylvania and Maryland under the Quaker and Catholic proprietary settlements; New York under the nominal Anglican-over-Dutch-Reformed settlement. The believers carried the religious traditions of the European Protestant Reformations across the Atlantic. The institutional apparatuses operated, on the record, as colonial established churches with persecutions of their own dissenters.

The First Great Awakening across the 1730s and 1740s was the eighteenth-century rupture of this colonial established-church architecture. The institutional consequence: denominational fragmentation across the colonies, the founding of new institutional centres (Princeton in 1746, Dartmouth in 1769), the Baptist and Methodist expansion, and the eighteenth-century religious-cultural shift that contributed to the Revolutionary disestablishments.

Theodorus Jacobus Frelinghuysen — the Dutch Reformed Pietist who led the revivals in the Raritan Valley of New Jersey across the late 1720s — is identified by Sydney Ahlstrom as the herald of the Great Awakening. William Tennent Sr. established the documented "Log College" at Neshaminy, Pennsylvania, across the 1720s — the seminary for Presbyterian revivalist preachers including his sons Gilbert Tennent, William Tennent Jr., John Tennent, and Charles Tennent. The Log College was the ancestor of Princeton University, chartered as the College of New Jersey on 22 October 1746.

The New England Awakening centred on Jonathan Edwards, Congregational pastor at Northampton, Massachusetts, from 1729. Edwards began preaching series on justification by faith alone in late 1733. In December 1734 the town-wide revival broke out at Northampton — around three hundred of Northampton's one thousand one hundred population converted across six months. Edwards published his account of the revival in London in 1737 — *A Faithful Narrative of the Surprising Work of God in the Conversion of Many Hundred Souls in Northampton*, edited by Isaac Watts and John Guyse — and the *Faithful Narrative* became the model for the subsequent revival accounts on both sides of the Atlantic.

George Whitefield — Anglican priest, born at Gloucester in 1714, ordained 1736 at the age of twenty-one — first visited Georgia in 1738 and returned to the colonies in November 1739 for the preaching tour that defined the transatlantic Awakening. itinerary across the years 1739 to 1741: Philadelphia (Christ Church and the open-air preaching from the courthouse steps). New York, Northampton at Edwards's invitation in October 1740 (Whitefield preached four sermons at Northampton with mass conversions. Edwards wept under Whitefield's preaching), Yale, the New England Congregational churches, the southern colonies. In one year Whitefield covered around five thousand miles and preached more than three hundred and fifty sermons. He drew crowds of thousands; Benjamin Franklin, the religious sceptic, became one of Whitefield's friends and business associates.

Jonathan Edwards delivered his sermon *Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God* at Enfield, Connecticut, on 8 July 1741. The foundational text of the New England Awakening preaching tradition. Documented passage: *the wrath of God burns against them, their damnation don't slumber, the pit is prepared, the fire is made ready, the furnace is now hot, ready to receive them, the flames do now rage and glow. The glittering sword is whet, and held over them, and the pit hath opened her mouth under them.* The theological-rhetorical force of the sermon. And of Edwards's broader Awakening preaching corpus, including the documented *Religious Affections* of 1746 and the documented *Freedom of the Will* of 1754 — produced the New England theological tradition that defined Calvinist Congregationalism through to the nineteenth century.

The Awakening produced the New Light/Old Light split among New England Congregationalists across 1742 and 1743. Charles Chauncy, pastor of First Church Boston, became the leading Old Light critic of the Awakening with his *Seasonable Thoughts on the State of Religion in New England* of 1743. Edwards defended the Awakening in *Some Thoughts Concerning the Present Revival of Religion in New England* of 1742 and the *Religious Affections* of 1746. The consequence: denominational fragmentation across the Congregational and Presbyterian churches, the founding of New Light institutional centres including Princeton (1746) and Dartmouth (1769), the Baptist and Methodist expansion across the colonies that produced. By the time of the American Revolution thirty-five years after Whitefield's first colonial tour — a religious-cultural landscape that the colonial established churches could no longer dominate.

The Awakening spread to the southern colonies through the Presbyterian preaching tour of Samuel Davies across Virginia in the years 1748 to 1759. The Southern Baptist expansion followed across the 1760s; the Methodist expansion in the South followed in the 1770s under Francis Asbury and the circuit-rider system. By the opening of the American Revolution, the religious-cultural ground had been prepared for the disestablishment of the colonial established churches that would follow under the Revolutionary state legislatures and the federal First Amendment.

The Revolutionary and early-Republic disestablishments ran across the period 1776 to 1833. The Virginia Declaration of Rights of 12 June 1776, drafted by George Mason, established the principle: *all men are equally entitled to the free exercise of religion, according to the dictates of conscience*. Virginia disestablished the Anglican Church in 1776; North Carolina the same year; New York in 1777. Thomas Jefferson drafted the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom in 1779, which was enacted under James Madison's leadership in the Virginia General Assembly on 16 January 1786 with the text: *no man shall be compelled to frequent or support any religious worship. Place, or ministry whatsoever ... but ... all men shall be free to profess, and by argument to maintain, their opinions in matters of religion*.

The First Amendment to the United States Constitution was ratified on 15 December 1791 as part of the Bill of Rights. The Establishment Clause and Free Exercise Clause: *Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof*. The New England Congregational establishments survived into the early nineteenth century at the state level — Connecticut disestablished in 1818, New Hampshire in 1819, and Massachusetts last of all on 11 November 1833 under the Eleventh Amendment to the Massachusetts Constitution. By 1833 no state in the United States retained a documented established religion. The American religious-voluntary architecture had been established.

The Second Great Awakening ran across the period 1790s to the 1840s. revival began in New England under Timothy Dwight — Jonathan Edwards's grandson, president of Yale from 1795 — with the Yale revival of 1801 in which around half the student body was converted. The Kentucky and Tennessee frontier revivals followed under James McGready, Presbyterian, at Logan County and Gasper River, Kentucky, across the years 1797 to 1801.

The foundational event of the American camp-meeting revival tradition was the Cane Ridge Revival, held at Cane Ridge in Bourbon County, Kentucky, from 6 to 12 (or 13) August 1801, hosted by the Cane Ridge Presbyterian Church under Barton W. Stone. Estimated attendance ten thousand to twenty thousand — around ten percent of Kentucky's 1801 population of around two hundred thousand. Presbyterian, Methodist, and Baptist ministers shared the platform. The physical manifestations: participants falling, jerking, dancing, shouting; the contemporary description, *the noise was like the roar of Niagara*. Contemporary accounts described the scene as *awful beyond description*.

The Cane Ridge model spread across Kentucky, Tennessee, Ohio, and the western frontier across the next quarter-century. institutional consequence: explosive Methodist and Baptist expansion across the South and West. By 1850 the Methodist Episcopal Church was the largest religious denomination in the United States, second only to Catholics — with around one million three hundred thousand members across the circuit-rider apparatus that Francis Asbury and his successors had built across the half-century — and the Baptists were the second-largest Protestant denomination.

Charles Grandison Finney — born 29 August 1792 in Connecticut, raised in upstate New York, lawyer by profession before his conversion at Adams, New York, on 10 October 1821 — became the dominant figure of the second phase of the Awakening. Finney developed the documented "New Measures" across his preaching career across the 1820s and 1830s: the documented "anxious bench" at the front of the meeting for those under conviction; the protracted meeting (nightly preaching for weeks); women praying aloud in mixed assemblies; named-prayer for unconverted individuals. The Rochester Revival of 1830 to 1831 was the urban-revival breakthrough — Finney preached in Rochester for six months across the winter of 1830-1831, with conversions across the city's professional and merchant classes.

The "Burned-Over District" — western New York state across the period 1825 to 1850 — produced religious-cultural intensity that the "Burned-Over District" term (coined by Finney himself in his 1876 *Memoirs*) was meant to capture. Mormonism was founded by Joseph Smith at Palmyra, New York, in 1830 with the publication of the *Book of Mormon* and the founding of the Church of Jesus Christ on 6 April 1830. The Millerite Adventist movement was founded by William Miller across the 1830s and produced the Great Disappointment of 22 October 1844 when Christ's predicted return did not occur. The spiritualist movement was founded at Hydesville, New York, in 1848 by the Fox Sisters with their reported communications with the dead — an account this volume's Chapter 13 will document in its religious-historical setting.

The consequence of the Second Great Awakening was the American Protestant denominational landscape that defined the nineteenth century. The Methodist Episcopal Church, the Baptist conventions, the Presbyterian Church USA, the Disciples of Christ founded by Barton Stone and Alexander Campbell, the African Methodist Episcopal Church founded by Richard Allen at Philadelphia and formally incorporated on 16 April 1816 — the denominations that operated, by the 1830s and 1840s, as the dominant religious institutional formations of antebellum America.

The voluntary-society apparatus of the Awakening built the infrastructure of American Protestant institutional life. The American Bible Society (1816), the American Sunday School Union (1824), the American Tract Society (1825), the American Home Missionary Society (1826), the American Temperance Society (1826), the American Anti-Slavery Society founded by William Lloyd Garrison and others on 4 December 1833. The Protestant voluntary societies that operated across the antebellum period as the governing infrastructure of the reformist Northern evangelical tradition.

And this is the point at which the Northern and Southern Protestant traditions began to diverge,  and doctrinally, across the matter of chattel slavery.

The antebellum Southern Protestant denominations produced, across the period 1830 to 1865, a theological defence of chattel slavery that was the religious legitimation apparatus of the slaveholding political-economic order. The scholarly count, in Larry Tise's *Proslavery* of 1987, identifies two hundred and seventy-five clergymen who published defences of slavery; around half of all published proslavery defences originated from Protestant ministers; in one hundred and fifty-four cases the proslavery defence appeared in official denominational publications.

The named senior proslavery theologians of the antebellum South was the theological architects of the apparatus.

**James Henley Thornwell** — born 9 December 1812, South Carolina Presbyterian, professor at South Carolina College from 1840, president of the College from 1851, founder of the *Southern Presbyterian Review* — produced the sermon *The Rights and the Duties of Masters*, preached on 26 May 1850 at Charleston at the dedication of a church *erected for the religious instruction of the Negroes*. The theological architecture of the sermon: slavery was not in itself a sin under biblical law; the New Testament did not condemn slavery; the master's duty was to provide religious instruction and humane treatment; the slave's duty was labour; the institution itself was within the divine ordering of human society. Thornwell's sermon *Our National Sins*, preached on South Carolina's day of prayer and fasting in November 1860 — three weeks after Lincoln's election — called the South to repentance for the abuses of slavery rather than for slavery itself, and articulated the sectional theological-political position that would carry the Southern Presbyterian Church into secession. Thornwell was the architect of the "spirituality of the church" doctrine — the doctrine that the church should not address political-social questions including slavery, which was used in practice to inoculate Southern Protestant institutions against abolitionist critique.

Thornwell's 1850 description of the sectional theological conflict, in *The Rights and the Duties of Masters*: *these are the mighty questions which are shaking thrones to their centres ... the parties in this conflict are not merely abolitionists and slaveholders. They are atheists, socialists, communists, red republicans, jacobins, on the one side, and the friends of order and regulated freedom on the other*.

**Robert Lewis Dabney** — born 5 March 1820, Virginia Presbyterian, professor at Union Theological Seminary in Virginia, chief of staff to Confederate General Thomas "Stonewall" Jackson during the Civil War — produced the *A Defence of Virginia, and Through Her, of the South*, written across the period 1862 to 1865 and published in 1867 after the war. Dabney's argument: the Old Testament explicitly recognised and sanctioned slavery in the examples of the Curse on Canaan, Abraham, Hagar, the Mosaic Law, and the Decalogue; in the New Testament, slavery was never condemned by Christ and was approved by Paul. The apparatus of proof-texts that the Southern Protestant clerical apparatus deployed across the antebellum period.

Dabney's documented 1867 speech before the Synod of Virginia, against the ordination of African Americans as pastors in white churches in the Presbyterian Church, set the postwar pattern. Dabney's argument: *an insuperable difference of race, made by God and not by man, and of character and social condition, makes it plainly impossible for a black man to teach and rule white Christians to edification*. The theological apparatus of antebellum proslavery hardened, after the Confederate defeat, into the theological apparatus of the postwar Southern Protestant racial-segregation institutional order.

**Benjamin Morgan Palmer** — born 25 January 1818, pastor of the First Presbyterian Church of New Orleans from 1856 — preached the Thanksgiving Day sermon *The South: Her Peril, Her Duty* on 29 November 1860, three weeks after Lincoln's election. Palmer's declaration: slavery was *the central trust* of the South, and the South must defend the institution *even if God tested it with the sword*. The sermon was published as proslavery-secessionist religious propaganda across the South in December 1860 and contributed to the South Carolina secession on 20 December 1860.

**Charles Hodge** — born 27 December 1797, Princeton Theological Seminary professor from 1822, principal Old School Presbyterian theologian — was based at Princeton in the North but produced the theological accommodation of slavery as an institution that was not in itself sinful under biblical law, though he opposed the abuses of the slave trade and supported gradual emancipation. Hodge represents the Northern Old School Presbyterian position that contributed to the 1837 Old School/New School Presbyterian split.

The denominational splits along North-South lines defined the Protestant response to the slavery question across the period 1837 to 1861.

The Old School/New School Presbyterian split of 1837. At the May 1837 General Assembly the Old School faction — led by Charles Hodge and the Princeton-Old School theological apparatus — carried a vote to exscind the four New School synods (Utica, Geneva, Genesee, and the Western Reserve, all in New York and Ohio; anti-slavery position). The Presbyterian Church divided into Old School (Southern-aligned, accommodating-of-slavery) and New School (Northern-aligned, anti-slavery). The Old School/New School split was officially over the Plan of Union of 1801 with the Congregationalists and over theological liberalism in the New School. The slavery question was the underlying division.

The Wesleyan Methodist Connection split of 1843. Orange Scott and other abolitionist Methodists separated from the Methodist Episcopal Church at the Utica Convention of 31 May 1843 over the parent church's refusal to discipline slaveholding bishops. The Wesleyan Methodist Connection was the abolitionist Methodist denomination across the antebellum period.

The Methodist Episcopal Church split of 1844. The 1844 General Conference at New York. The test case: Bishop James Andrew of Georgia, who had become a slaveholder through his second marriage. The 1844 General Conference voted, after six weeks of debate, to require Bishop Andrew to *desist from the exercise of his functions* while he remained a slaveholder. The Plan of Separation was approved. In May 1845 the southern delegates met at Louisville, Kentucky, and founded the **Methodist Episcopal Church, South** (MECS). The Methodist split would continue until reunification in 1939.

The Baptist split of 1845. The test case: the Alabama Baptists in 1844 asked the Foreign Mission Board whether a slaveholder could be appointed as a missionary; the Board (controlled by Northern Baptists) said no. The Georgia Baptists separately nominated a slaveholder named James Reeve for appointment as a home missionary; the Home Mission Society declined. In May 1845 the Southern Baptist delegates met at Augusta, Georgia, and founded the **Southern Baptist Convention** (SBC). The founding president of the SBC was William B. Johnson. The SBC operated, by the late twentieth century, as the largest Protestant denomination in the United States and continues as the largest Protestant denomination in the United States today.

The Presbyterian Old School split of 1861. The May 1861 General Assembly of the Old School Presbyterian Church. The Southern Old School delegates withdrew. In December 1861 the southern delegates met at Augusta, Georgia, and founded the Presbyterian Church in the Confederate States of America (PCCSA), renamed the Presbyterian Church in the United States (PCUS) after the war. The Northern Old School became the Presbyterian Church in the USA (PCUSA). The New School Presbyterians had already split themselves along North-South lines in 1857.

By the outbreak of the Civil War in April 1861 the major American Protestant denominations had divided, formally and institutionally, along North-South sectional lines, with the Southern branches operating as the religious arm of the slaveholding political-economic order. The 1844 Methodist split, the 1845 Baptist split, and the 1857-1861 Presbyterian split were the structural precursors of the secession — Henry Clay had warned in the late 1840s that the church divisions were *the greatest source of danger to our country*. The Christian History Magazine's chronological summary: *1837: Old School and New School Presbyterians split. 1844: the Methodist Church breaks apart. 1845: the Baptist Triennial Convention divides. By 1861: the Christian South and Christian North faced each other across sectional denominational lines.*

The religious mobilisation across the Civil War operated on both sides. Northern clergy preached the war as a holy war for Union and against slavery; Southern clergy preached the war as a holy war for the Christian South against Northern infidelity and abolitionist fanaticism. The chaplaincies. The mass-revival movements in both armies — the documented *Great Revival in the Confederate Army* across the winter of 1863-1864, with conversions of tens of thousands of Confederate soldiers under field-preaching by the Southern Protestant clergy. The denominational fundraising for the war effort on both sides. James Henley Thornwell drafted the founding *Address to All the Churches of Jesus Christ Throughout the Earth* of the PCCSA at Augusta, Georgia, in December 1861 — the PCCSA's theological-political justification of secession on religious grounds.

The war ended at Appomattox on 9 April 1865 with Lee's surrender. Lincoln was assassinated five days later on 14 April 1865. The Confederate defeat produced the formal emancipation of around four million enslaved Americans under the Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution, ratified on 6 December 1865.

The Southern Protestant denominations that had defended slavery did not, in institutional practice, repent of their proslavery positions. The 1874 Methodist Episcopal Church, South General Conference reaffirmed its *attitudes and actions in the antebellum period*. Dabney's documented 1867 Synod of Virginia speech against the ordination of African Americans set the pattern. The Southern Protestant denominational apparatus was the religious arm of the post-Reconstruction Jim Crow apparatus across the period 1877 to the contemporary moment — an institutional continuity that §§ III and IV of this chapter document in its successive twentieth-century forms.

The American Protestant capture of the period 1620 to 1865 produced the governing architecture that defined the United States across the next two centuries.

The colonial Protestant establishments of the seventeenth century — Congregationalist in New England, Anglican in the Chesapeake and Carolinas, Quaker and Catholic and Reformed in the middle colonies — replicated, on record, the European confessional state-church model in transplanted colonial form. The First Great Awakening of the 1730s and 1740s ruptured the colonial established-church architecture and produced the denominational fragmentation that would, across the next century, become the operative form of American Protestant institutional life. The Revolutionary and early-Republic disestablishments between 1776 and 1833 produced the American religious-voluntary architecture — no tax-funded clerical apparatus, no state-enforced doctrinal apparatus, no state-confessional educational apparatus — within which the antebellum denominational landscape took its form.

The Second Great Awakening of the 1790s to the 1840s produced the denominational expansion across the new western frontier, the voluntary-society apparatus, and the integration of the major Protestant denominations with the sectional political-economic interests of the antebellum United States. By the 1840s the Protestant churches of America had divided, in the record, along North-South sectional lines, with the Southern branches operating as the religious legitimation apparatus of chattel slavery. The Civil War produced the military defeat of the Southern Protestant apparatus but did not produce — in institutional practice — its theological or institutional repudiation of the proslavery position.

The pattern documented in Chapter 2 and Chapter 3 § I recurred, on the documented record, in its American Protestant form. The institutional Christian church operating as the religious arm of the dominant political-economic apparatus. Where the medieval and early-modern Catholic apparatus had integrated with the European feudal-monarchical sovereignty, and where the Lutheran and Reformed apparatuses had integrated with the European territorial-monarchical sovereignty, the antebellum Southern Protestant apparatus integrated with the slaveholding political-economic sovereignty of the American South. The 1837/1844/1845/1857/1861 denominational splits documented the choice the Southern Protestant denominations made: continued operation as the religious legitimation apparatus of the slaveholding political-economic order, in formal denominational separation from the Northern co-denominations that had begun, however hesitantly and incompletely, to refuse that integration.

§ III of this chapter documents the American evangelical-political apparatus across the period 1865 to 1979. The institutional reorganisation of post-Civil War American Protestantism, the fundamentalist-modernist controversy of the early twentieth century, the post-Second World War evangelical-political mobilisation, and the Cold War alliance between the American evangelical-political apparatus and the operators dossiered in Volumes One and Two of this series.

---


### III. The American Evangelical-Political Apparatus (1865–1979)

Appomattox closed the Civil War. It did not close the Southern Protestant theological position. The denominations that had defended chattel slavery from their pulpits across the antebellum period operated, after the Confederate defeat, with their theological apparatus intact and their institutional infrastructure unrepented. The Methodist Episcopal Church South, the Southern Baptist Convention, and the Presbyterian Church in the United States carried their antebellum theological framework into the postwar period and reorganised it as the religious arm of the post-Reconstruction Jim Crow order. The 1874 MECS General Conference reaffirmed its antebellum positions. Robert Lewis Dabney's 1867 Synod of Virginia speech against the ordination of African Americans set the template. The Southern Baptist Convention did not formally apologise for its racism until 20 June 1995, at its Atlanta annual meeting — a hundred and thirty years after Appomattox.

Black Protestants built their own architecture across the same decades. The African Methodist Episcopal Church, incorporated by Richard Allen at Philadelphia on 16 April 1816, expanded rapidly across the South after Emancipation. The African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church, founded at New York City in 1821, expanded alongside. The National Baptist Convention USA was founded in 1895 and became the largest historically Black Protestant denomination — operating across the next century as the principal religious-institutional infrastructure of Black America, independent of the white Southern denominations and frequently in open opposition to them. The civil rights movement of the 1950s and 1960s would emerge from this institutional architecture; the Volume Two political-class dossiers document the contemporary residue.

The Northern Protestant trajectory after Emancipation moved in a different direction. The Social Gospel emerged in the late 1880s and 1890s as a Northern liberal Protestant response to the late-nineteenth-century industrial capitalism that Volume One Chapter 2 dossiers. Walter Rauschenbusch — German Baptist pastor at the Second German Baptist Church in Hell's Kitchen from 1886, professor at Rochester Theological Seminary from 1897 — produced the foundational texts: *Christianity and the Social Crisis* in 1907, *Christianizing the Social Order* in 1912, *A Theology for the Social Gospel* in 1917. Washington Gladden, Congregational minister at Columbus, Ohio, published *Applied Christianity* in 1886. The Social Gospel position: the Christian gospel required not only individual salvation but the transformation of social and economic structures — wages, housing, labour conditions, the regulation of industrial capitalism. The Social Gospel became the dominant theological position of mainline Protestantism — the Federal Council of Churches, founded 1908, would carry it into national Protestant leadership across the next half-century.

The Holiness and Pentecostal traditions emerged in the same period along a third trajectory. The Holiness movement organised from the 1860s onward as Methodist offshoots emphasising sanctification and Christian perfection — the National Camp Meeting Association for the Promotion of Holiness was founded in 1867 by John Inskip and others; the Church of the Nazarene was founded in 1908 at Pilot Point, Texas. Pentecostalism produced its founding event at the **Azusa Street Revival** in Los Angeles, from 9 April 1906 through 1915, with peak years 1906 to 1909. Led by William J. Seymour, son of formerly enslaved parents, at 312 Azusa Street — a converted livery stable on the edge of the Black neighbourhood of Los Angeles. The Azusa Street meeting was multiracial across colour lines that were everywhere else in 1906 America rigidly enforced. Speaking in tongues, faith healing, prophetic utterance, and ecstatic worship were the named markers. The Assemblies of God denomination consolidated the early Pentecostal movement at its founding convention in Hot Springs, Arkansas, on 2 April 1914. Pentecostalism became, across the twentieth century, the fastest-growing major Christian tradition in the world; the contemporary global Pentecostal population is around 644 million people, the majority of them outside the United States and the majority of them in the Global South.

The Northern Protestant landscape by the early twentieth century divided along three poles: the mainline-modernist Social Gospel position; the fundamentalist-traditional position that crystallised against it; and the Holiness-Pentecostal renewal traditions that operated alongside both. The fundamentalist-modernist controversy of the 1910s, 1920s, and 1930s was the central institutional battle of American Protestantism in the early twentieth century — and its outcome shaped the conditions under which the postwar evangelical-political apparatus emerged.

The first foundational text of fundamentalism was the **Scofield Reference Bible** of 1909. Cyrus Ingerson Scofield (1843–1921), American minister and Bible-study author, produced the annotated edition through Oxford University Press containing the King James Version text with extensive commentary footnotes that popularised John Nelson Darby's premillennial dispensationalism. The Darbyite system: history is divided into seven dispensations; the Second Coming will be preceded by a "rapture" of true believers, followed by a seven-year Tribulation, the return of Christ in glory, a thousand-year reign, the final judgement. The Scofield Bible sold over two million copies by the end of the Second World War and was the "Bible of fundamentalism" — the laity-accessible commentary that carried dispensationalist eschatology into millions of American Protestant homes, churches, Sunday schools, and Bible colleges across the next century.

The second foundational text was *The Fundamentals: A Testimony to the Truth*. Twelve volumes published in quarterly instalments between 1910 and 1915 by the Testimony Publishing Company. Funded by **Lyman Stewart** (1840–1923), Union Oil Company co-founder, and his brother Milton Stewart — the founding instance of the Volume One financial-industrial-religious integration pattern operating in the new American context. Edited successively by A.C. Dixon, Louis Meyer, and Reuben A. Torrey. Around 300,000 dollars total budget. 90 articles by 64 writers including B.B. Warfield and J. Gresham Machen of Princeton. Distributed free to around three million Protestant clergy, missionaries, theological students, and Christian workers across the English-speaking world.

*The Fundamentals* defined what became the canonical "five fundamentals" of the movement: biblical inerrancy; the virgin birth of Christ; the substitutionary atonement; the bodily resurrection of Christ; and either the historical reality of Christ's miracles or the imminent Second Coming, depending on the list. The movement called itself fundamentalist for the first time after 1920 — the term was coined by Curtis Lee Laws, editor of the Baptist *Watchman-Examiner*, in his 1 July 1920 editorial calling for fundamentalists "to do battle royal for the fundamentals of the faith."

The institutional infrastructure of the movement consolidated across the same period. The Bible Institute of Los Angeles — BIOLA — was founded in 1908 by Lyman Stewart with T.C. Horton, the West Coast anchor. The Moody Bible Institute, founded 1886 in Chicago by Dwight L. Moody, was the Midwestern anchor. Dallas Theological Seminary, founded in 1924 by Lewis Sperry Chafer (originally Evangelical Theological College, renamed 1936), became the academic anchor of dispensationalism. By 1925 the structural fundamentalist infrastructure existed — Bible colleges, radio programmes, the Stewart-funded publishing apparatus, the Scofield Bible distribution network, and the parachurch organisations that operated outside the increasingly modernist mainline denominations.

The cultural confrontation came at Dayton, Tennessee, in July 1925.

The **Butler Act**, signed into law by Tennessee Governor Austin Peay on 21 March 1925, prohibited the teaching of human evolution in any Tennessee public school. The American Civil Liberties Union, based in New York City, offered to defend any teacher prosecuted under the Act. A group of Dayton businessmen identified a test case as an opportunity for publicity: they recruited **John Thomas Scopes**, a high-school science teacher and football coach who had used the standard Tennessee biology textbook (which included evolution), to admit to teaching it. Scopes was charged on 7 May 1925.

The trial — *State of Tennessee v. John Thomas Scopes* — ran from 10 to 21 July 1925, in the Rhea County Courthouse at Dayton. Eight trial days. **William Jennings Bryan** led the prosecution. Bryan: three-time Democratic presidential nominee (1896, 1900, 1908), former U.S. Secretary of State under Wilson from 1913 to 1915, Presbyterian elder, leading figure of the World's Christian Fundamentals Association — the most prominent fundamentalist political figure in America in 1925. **Clarence Darrow** led the defence. Darrow: nationally renowned criminal-defence attorney, avowed agnostic, working pro bono in the first such case of his career.

More than 200 reporters and magazine writers descended on Dayton. WGN Chicago broadcast the trial live by radio — the first national radio broadcast of an American trial. H.L. Mencken filed daily dispatches for the *Baltimore Evening Sun* that became the mythology of the trial in the American popular memory. The trial's climax came on 20 July 1925, the seventh day, when Darrow called Bryan to testify as an expert witness on the Bible. The courtroom was moved to the courthouse lawn for heat and crowd-safety reasons. Bryan testified under Darrow's cross-examination for around two hours.

The exchange is in the trial transcript. Darrow asked Bryan whether the days of Genesis 1 were literal twenty-four-hour days; Bryan answered that he did not think they were necessarily. Darrow asked Bryan about the age of the earth, the date of the flood, the Tower of Babel; Bryan answered that he had not investigated, that he was too busy with matters he thought more important. Bryan's statement at the trial: *"These gentlemen have not had much chance — they did not come here to try this case. They came here to try revealed religion. I am here to defend it and they can ask me any question they please."*

Scopes was found guilty on 21 July 1925 and fined 100 dollars. The Tennessee Supreme Court overturned the conviction in January 1927 on the technicality that the judge rather than the jury had set the fine; the Butler Act itself was upheld and remained Tennessee law until its 1967 repeal. Bryan died at Dayton on 26 July 1925, five days after the trial closed.

The legal outcome favoured the prosecution. The cultural outcome, in the American mainstream press, was the defeat of fundamentalism. Mencken's vituperative dispatches — calling Bryan a "buffoon," describing his speeches as "theologic bilge," portraying Dayton as a town of "yokels" and "morons" — set the tone that would define the American intellectual class's relationship to evangelical Christianity across the next half-century. *Inherit the Wind*, the 1955 play by Jerome Lawrence and Robert E. Lee, and the 1960 Stanley Kramer film adaptation with Spencer Tracy and Fredric March, would carry Mencken's framing into American popular culture as the canonical narrative of the trial.

The Scopes trial did not, however, defeat the institutional fundamentalist infrastructure. Bible colleges, radio ministries, parachurch organisations, and dispensationalist publishing continued to expand across the 1920s, 1930s, and 1940s — operating outside the mainline denominations that the fundamentalists had largely lost. Joel Carpenter's *Revive Us Again* (1997) documents the period from 1925 to 1950 as the "underground" decades during which the fundamentalist apparatus was rebuilt for the postwar mobilisation that would follow.

The denominational battles of the fundamentalist-modernist period closed out in the late 1920s and 1930s. Harry Emerson Fosdick, a Baptist serving as guest preacher at First Presbyterian Church in New York City, preached *Shall the Fundamentalists Win?* on 21 May 1922 — opening the public Presbyterian phase of the controversy. J. Gresham Machen, Princeton Seminary professor, published *Christianity and Liberalism* in 1923, arguing that liberal Protestantism was not a variant of Christianity but a different religion. Machen and conservative Princeton faculty withdrew in 1929 to found Westminster Theological Seminary in Philadelphia. Machen was expelled from the Presbyterian Church USA in 1936 and founded the Presbyterian Church of America that year (renamed the Orthodox Presbyterian Church in 1939). Machen died on 1 January 1937. Carl McIntire, a Machen disciple, founded the more separatist Bible Presbyterian Church in 1937. The institutional Presbyterian-fundamentalist position fragmented into separatist denominations.

By the late 1930s the governing fundamentalist position in American Protestantism was: theologically intact in the Bible institutes, the radio ministries, and the new separatist denominations; politically and culturally marginalised in the post-Scopes American mainstream; and, in its 1942 institutional response, ready for a new mobilisation that would carry it into the postwar political-religious apparatus.

The new mobilisation was the **National Association of Evangelicals**.

Will Houghton, president of Moody Bible Institute, called an exploratory meeting in Chicago in October 1941. A temporary committee for United Action Among Evangelicals was formed under J. Elwin Wright of the New England Fellowship as chairman. The national conference was held in St. Louis, Missouri, from 7 to 9 April 1942 — 147 attendees from across the American evangelical Protestant traditions. The **National Association of Evangelicals** was founded at that conference. **Harold John Ockenga** (1905–1985), pastor of Park Street Church in Boston, was elected first president and served from 1942 to 1944.

Ockenga's founding address at the St. Louis conference, *"The Unvoiced Multitudes"*, lamented that the cause of evangelical Christianity in America had been reduced to individuals and individual congregations and called for a consolidated witness. The NAE was structured as an alternative to two existing alignments: the Federal Council of Churches on the mainline-modernist side, founded 1908, reorganised as the National Council of Churches in 1950; and the American Council of Christian Churches on the separatist-fundamentalist side, founded by Carl McIntire and Harvey Springer in September 1941. The NAE motto was "cooperation without compromise" — Ockenga's position that evangelicals could be theologically conservative without separatist isolation. The Constitutional Convention in Chicago in May 1943 brought around 1,000 attendees representing around 50 denominations with a constituency of around 15 million Christians.

The NAE's 1944 convention in Columbus, Ohio, founded the **National Religious Broadcasters**. The founding occasion: the Mutual Radio Network had announced it would no longer sell time for paid religious programmes and had turned the Protestant broadcasting slot over to the Federal Council of Churches — meaning evangelical and fundamentalist broadcasters were locked out of national radio. The NRB organised to challenge this policy and eventually persuaded the networks to reverse it. By the late twentieth century the NRB was the largest religious broadcasting organisation in the world, with around 1,400 member organisations.

The 1944 NAE convention also founded the NAE Chaplains Commission, to assist evangelical chaplains in the U.S. military, and the War Relief Commission, later renamed **World Relief** — the humanitarian arm of the NAE, which by the late twentieth century operated worldwide and received continuing U.S. government funding through USAID. The Evangelical Foreign Missions Association was founded in 1945. By the end of the war the NAE had built the infrastructure of postwar evangelical Protestant national presence: a Washington office, broadcasting access, military chaplaincy, foreign missions, relief operations, and a denominational federation representing fifteen million believers.

The neo-evangelical academic and publishing infrastructure followed across the next decade.

**Fuller Theological Seminary** was founded at Pasadena, California, in 1947 by Charles E. Fuller (radio evangelist of *The Old Fashioned Revival Hour*), Harold Ockenga, Carl F.H. Henry, and Harold Lindsell. Fuller became the academic anchor of post-fundamentalist neo-evangelicalism. In a 1948 convocation address at Pasadena, Ockenga formally announced "Neo-evangelicalism" — repudiating fundamentalist separatism while retaining doctrinal conservatism. The World Evangelical Fellowship was founded by the NAE in 1951 as a counter to the World Council of Churches.

*Christianity Today* magazine was founded in 1956 by Billy Graham, Carl F.H. Henry (founding editor), L. Nelson Bell (Graham's father-in-law), and Harold Ockenga (chairman of the board for the next 25 years). The flagship publication of American neo-evangelicalism. **J. Howard Pew** of Sun Oil Company was the principal financial backer of the founding — the continuation of the Stewart-brothers founding pattern of oil-industry financing of American evangelical institutional infrastructure, now operating at higher institutional scale.

The most consequential evangelical figure to emerge from the postwar mobilisation was Billy Graham.

**William Franklin "Billy" Graham Jr.** (7 November 1918 – 21 February 2018). Born at Charlotte, North Carolina, to a dairy-farmer family. Graduated Wheaton College in 1943 with a degree in anthropology. Ordained by the Southern Baptist Convention. Early career with Youth for Christ International, founded 1944 by Torrey Johnson — Graham was YFC's first full-time evangelist, preaching in 47 of the 48 states across his first YFC year. The 1944 Memorial Day rally at Soldier Field in Chicago drew 70,000 attendees; Graham was one of the main speakers.

In 1948 Graham and his core team (Cliff Barrows, George Beverly Shea, Grady Wilson, T.W. Wilson) met at a Modesto, California, motel and drafted what became known as the **Modesto Manifesto** — a code of ethics for the Graham evangelistic team addressing the failure modes of earlier American evangelism: financial irregularity, sexual scandal, exaggerated reporting of conversions, and competition with local churches. The Modesto Manifesto operated across the next sixty years as the discipline that distinguished Graham from the failure cases of postwar American evangelism — Jim Bakker, Jimmy Swaggart, and the prosperity-gospel cases that § IV will document.

The 1949 Greater Los Angeles Crusade made Graham a national figure. Organised by Christ for Greater Los Angeles, a coalition of around 1,000 prayer groups across Southern California. Held in a Ringling Brothers circus tent — originally 6,000 seats, expanded to 9,000 — at the corner of Washington Boulevard and Hill Street in downtown Los Angeles. Opened 25 September 1949. Scheduled for three weeks; ran eight. Graham preached against "common vices," critiqued atheistic communism, and called for individual repentance and conversion to Christ as personal Lord and saviour.

Attendance was modest in the first three weeks — around 3,000 to 4,000 a night, well under the tent's capacity. The breakthrough came in mid-October with the public conversion of cowboy radio host Stuart Hamblen. Other celebrity conversions followed: former Olympian and Japanese-POW-camp survivor Louis Zamperini; Jim Vaus, an associate of Los Angeles mobster Mickey Cohen; the actor Harvey Fritz.

**William Randolph Hearst** (29 April 1863 – 14 August 1951) took notice. Hearst, the media magnate of around 28 newspapers and 18 magazines at his peak, sent a directive to his editors. The traditional account, originating in Stanley High's 1956 biography *Billy Graham: The Personal Story of the Man, His Message, and His Mission*, gives the directive as the two words "Puff Graham." Patricia Cornwell's 1983 biography of Ruth Bell Graham raised doubts about the literal phrasing — no hard copy of the directive has been produced; Hearst's son denied his father had said it in those exact words; William Martin's authorised 1991 Graham biography offered further qualifications. What is on the record: Hearst's papers (the Los Angeles Examiner and the Los Angeles Herald Express in Los Angeles, the Hearst chain nationally) ran prominent Graham coverage from mid-October 1949 onward. The Associated Press, United Press International, and International News Service picked up the story. *Time* (14 November 1949) and *Life* (21 November 1949) followed.

Hearst's motive was political. He had been running an anti-communist campaign across his media properties for years. In a 1945 memo to his editors, Hearst had already instructed them to give continuing coverage to Youth for Christ. Describing it as something his papers should "be the promoters of." Hearst's 1949 calculus: Graham's anti-communism, his patriotism, and his appeal to American youth aligned with what Hearst wanted his newspapers to be promoting in the Cold War context. The Hearst-Graham media alignment was therefore a Cold War political-religious convergence — the first major instance of an American media operator using mass-circulation press to amplify an evangelical preacher's political-religious message at scale. § IV will document the contemporary equivalents.

The Los Angeles Crusade ran for eight weeks rather than three. Total attendance around 350,000; around 3,000 recorded conversions. Graham left Los Angeles a national figure. The Billy Graham Evangelistic Association was formally incorporated at Minneapolis in 1950. President Truman invited Graham to the White House — the first of Graham's relationships with every American president from Truman through Obama, a 64-year span across thirteen presidencies.

Graham's preaching in the Cold War period combined revivalism with explicit anti-communism. The Graham's statement at Los Angeles in 1949: *"Communism is not only an economic interpretation of life. Communism is a religion that is inspired, directed, and motivated by the Devil himself, who has declared war against Almighty God."* The framing — communism as not merely a political or economic system but as a religious system inspired by the Devil — would become the template for evangelical Cold War political alignment across the next four decades.

The Cold War political-religious convergence produced its institutional artefacts at the national level. The U.S. Congress voted to add "under God" to the Pledge of Allegiance; Eisenhower signed the bill on Flag Day, 14 June 1954. "In God We Trust" was adopted as the official motto of the United States — Eisenhower signed the bill on 30 July 1956 — replacing the unofficial *E Pluribus Unum* that had served as the national motto since 1782. Both initiatives were Cold War political-religious operations coordinated by the NAE, the National Council of Churches, the Knights of Columbus, and the Eisenhower White House. The Cold War American religious-political identity took its operative form: a Judeo-Christian America defined against a atheist Soviet adversary.

Graham's 1957 New York Crusade at Madison Square Garden, from 15 May to 1 September 1957, ran 16 weeks and drew total attendance of around two million. By 1957 Graham was the dominant figure of American evangelical Protestantism, a regular presence at the White House and in the national media, and the personal evangelist of the post-Eisenhower-era American political-religious establishment.

The 1960s produced tensions in the postwar evangelical settlement.

John F. Kennedy's election in November 1960 as the first Roman Catholic president of the United States produced opposition from significant sections of the American evangelical leadership — Norman Vincent Peale led a September 1960 statement against Kennedy on confessional grounds; Graham publicly supported Kennedy but the behind-the-scenes evangelical opposition was significant. The Kennedy administration's 1961 to 1963 civil rights, anti-poverty, and Catholic-public-policy initiatives sharpened the Northern-mainline / Southern-evangelical political divide.

The 1962 *Engel v. Vitale* decision (370 U.S. 421, 25 June 1962) ruled official school-sponsored prayer in public schools unconstitutional under the Establishment Clause. The 1963 *Abington School District v. Schempp* decision (374 U.S. 203, 17 June 1963) ruled mandatory Bible-reading in public schools unconstitutional. The two rulings became, in the evangelical political memory of the next half-century, the defining federal-judicial assault on Christian America — the mobilising events around which the religious-right political consciousness would consolidate.

The 1964 Civil Rights Act (Public Law 88-352, signed 2 July 1964) produced opposition from the SBC and other Southern white Protestant denominations on the desegregation provisions. The SBC did not formally apologise for its racism until 20 June 1995, at its Atlanta annual meeting — 31 years after the Civil Rights Act and 130 years after Appomattox.

The Selma to Montgomery marches of March 1965 produced a divided Protestant response. Northern mainline Protestants participated prominently, including from the National Council of Churches. Southern white Protestants largely opposed. Catholic and Jewish religious participation joined Black Protestant clergy in the front ranks — the inter-religious civil-rights coalition that operated across the 1960s, with the Southern Christian Leadership Conference under Martin Luther King Jr. as its principal organising apparatus. King's assassination on 4 April 1968 at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis marked the closure of the civil-rights phase of American Protestantism's twentieth-century history.

The 1971 *Lemon v. Kurtzman* decision (403 U.S. 602, 28 June 1971) established the Lemon Test for Establishment Clause cases — three-part test that struck down state aid to religious schools under most circumstances. The 1973 *Roe v. Wade* decision (410 U.S. 113, 22 January 1973) struck down state abortion restrictions under the Fourteenth Amendment due process clause.

*Roe* is the foundational reference point of the contemporary religious-right political mobilisation in the popular religious-right narrative. The historical record is more complex. The Southern Baptist Convention had, in a June 1971 resolution, supported abortion access in cases of rape, incest, severe foetal deformity, or maternal health. The SBC reaffirmed this position in 1974, after *Roe*, and again in 1976. The evangelical Protestant turn against abortion accelerated only after 1976, was decisive by 1979, and was driven — on record assembled by Randall Balmer and others — in large measure by a different political issue: the 1978 Internal Revenue Service proposed revocation of tax-exempt status for racially segregated Christian schools.

The IRS in January 1978 proposed revoking the tax-exempt status of "segregation academies" — the private Christian schools that had been founded across the South after *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954) and the federal desegregation orders of the 1960s and 1970s to allow white Southern Protestant families to avoid the desegregated public schools. The Bob Jones University case (the IRS had revoked Bob Jones University's tax-exempt status in 1976 over its prohibition of interracial dating) was the test case. Paul Weyrich — co-founder of the Heritage Foundation, founder of the Free Congress Foundation — has himself that the segregation-academy tax-exempt issue, not *Roe*, was the immediate political catalyst that produced the religious-right political mobilisation of 1979.

The 1976 election — *Newsweek* magazine declared 1976 "the Year of the Evangelical" — brought Jimmy Carter, born-again Southern Baptist, to the White House. The breakthrough of evangelical political consciousness into the American mainstream. Carter's presidency, however, produced conflicts with the conservative evangelical leadership across his single term: on the segregation-academy issue, on the Equal Rights Amendment, on homosexuality policy, on Israel-Palestine, on the Panama Canal. By 1979 the conservative evangelical leadership had decided that the Carter administration was a adversary rather than a ally.

The institutional infrastructure of the religious right had been assembled across the previous decade. **The Christian Broadcasting Network**, founded 1960 by Pat Robertson at Portsmouth, Virginia, with *The 700 Club* launched in 1966, was by 1979 the largest religious broadcasting network in the United States. **Trinity Broadcasting Network**, founded 1973 by Paul and Jan Crouch at Tustin, California, was the Pentecostal-charismatic broadcasting platform. **Focus on the Family**, founded 1977 by James Dobson at Arcadia, California, around Dobson's 1970 book *Dare to Discipline*, was the family-policy apparatus. **The Heritage Foundation** had been founded on 17 February 1973 by Paul Weyrich, Edwin Feulner, and Joseph Coors with funding from Coors. **The Eagle Forum**, founded 1972 by Phyllis Schlafly, was the anti-ERA and anti-feminist organising apparatus. **Liberty Baptist College** (renamed Liberty University in 1985) had been founded in 1971 by Jerry Falwell at Lynchburg, Virginia.

By 1979, the apparatus was in place. NAE, NRB, Fuller, Wheaton, Dallas, BIOLA, Moody, *Christianity Today*, the Billy Graham Evangelistic Association — the postwar evangelical institutional infrastructure. CBN, TBN, Focus on the Family, the Heritage Foundation, the Eagle Forum, Liberty — the religious-right political infrastructure. The 1973 *Roe* decision and the 1978 IRS segregation-academy proposal had produced the political conditions. Paul Weyrich had coordinated the structural meetings across early 1979 that would produce a new political-religious operation. Weyrich's documented question to Jerry Falwell in the 1979 meeting that produced the Moral Majority's founding name: *"Out there is what one might call a moral majority — that is, people who would agree on principles based on the Bible — but they have been separated by geographical and denominational differences. The next move is to get them together."*

The Moral Majority was incorporated by Jerry Falwell in June 1979 at Lynchburg, Virginia. § IV documents what happened next.

The American Protestant capture across the period 1865 to 1979 produced the contemporary American evangelical-political apparatus. The post-Civil War Southern Protestant denominational continuation of the antebellum proslavery theology, integrated with the post-Reconstruction Jim Crow order. The Social Gospel as the Northern mainline alternative, integrated with progressive politics.
