What the Bolshevik state would then do to the church across the next seven decades — the persecution, the murder of clergy in the millions, the closure of churches, the infiltration of the surviving hierarchy by the KGB, the structural capture of the Moscow Patriarchate as an asset of Soviet foreign policy — § III sets out.

---


### III. Soviet-Era Persecution, the Living Church Schism, and the KGB Infiltration of the Moscow Patriarchate (1917–1991)

The Bolshevik state inherited an Orthodox tradition that had operated for 196 years as the religious arm of the Romanov imperial apparatus (Chapter 4 § II). Lenin's regime intended to abolish the institution as a public-functioning entity; what it produced, instead, was an apparatus reshaped to operate as the religious arm of the new state. The reshaping took seventy-four years, cost the lives of clergy and lay believers across a documented range from around 100,000 at the low estimates to over half a million at the higher ones, and produced — by the late Soviet period — a senior hierarchy in which named KGB agents occupied the patriarchal office and the senior administrative positions. The institutional pattern that § II set out as Petrine subordination of the church to imperial authority returned in another form, intensified, and produced the hierarchy that § IV will document in its post-Soviet operation.

Patriarch Tikhon — Vasily Ivanovich Belavin, born 19 January 1865 at Toropets near Pskov — had been elected at the Local Council of 5 November 1917, two days before the Bolsheviks seized power in Petrograd. Lenin's *Decree on the Separation of Church from State and School from Church* of 20 January 1918 (Old Style, 2 February New Style) deprived it of legal personhood, its right to own property (all church property nationalised at a stroke), and its right to teach religion in any school or to any group of minors. The institutional structure that had operated as an imperial state department since 1721 was abolished as a juridical entity by Bolshevik decree.

On the day before Lenin's decree took effect — **19 January 1918** (Old Style; 1 February New Style on the Christian History Institute dating) — Tikhon issued the encyclical that the Bolsheviks and historians since have read as the patriarchal anathema against the regime. The text did not name the Bolshevik leadership directly. Tikhon anathematised *"those committing senseless acts of violence,"* condemning the killings, the looting of churches, the executions of clergy. The closing words went on the record:

> *"Think what you are doing, you madmen! Stop your bloody reprisals. Your acts are not merely cruel, they are the works of Satan for which you will burn in Hell fire in the life hereafter and be cursed by future generations in this life."*

The operative effect of the anathema was unambiguous regardless of its formal textual reference. Tikhon called on Orthodox believers to defend their churches — not with violence, but by being willing to lay down their own lives. The Bolshevik response was to intensify the campaign.

The first three years of Bolshevik rule produced, per the Orthodox Church in America's standard summary, **at least 28 bishops murdered, thousands of clergy imprisoned or killed, and around 12,000 lay people killed for religious activities.** The Wikipedia summary of the contemporary Russian-language sources gives. For the period June 1918 to January 1919 in Russia proper (excluding the Volga, Kama, and several other regions), 1 metropolitan, 18 bishops, 102 priests, 154 deacons, and 94 monks and nuns killed; 4 bishops and 211 priests imprisoned. Many killings were extrajudicial — Red Army troops opening fire on religious processions at Voronezh, Shatsk in Tambov province, Tula (where thirteen were killed and many wounded, including Bishop Kornilii). The first priest to die a martyr at Bolshevik hands was Saint John Kochurov (1871-1917), beaten and shot at Tsarskoye Selo on 31 October 1917 — the 1994 Russian Church glorification named him *"First Hieromartyr under the Bolshevik Yoke."*

The Romanovs followed. On the night of **16-17 July 1918**, Tsar Nicholas II and his immediate family were executed at the Ipatiev House in Yekaterinburg. The next night, **17-18 July 1918**, Grand Duchess Elizabeth (1864-1918, herself a nun at the Marfo-Mariinsky Convent she had founded after her husband Grand Duke Sergei Alexandrovich's 1905 assassination) and other extended Romanov family members were murdered near Alapaevsk — thrown alive into a mineshaft, killed with grenades and rifle fire by Bolshevik troops.

The 1921-1922 famine in the Volga region — around 5 million dead per standard demographic estimates — became the Bolshevik political opening for the seizure of remaining church valuables. The 23 February 1922 government decree authorising requisition of church valuables ostensibly for famine relief produced the Shuya incident on 15 March 1922, when Red Army troops opened fire on parishioners defending the Resurrection Cathedral against requisition. Lenin's notorious 19 March 1922 secret memorandum to the Politburo, declassified after 1991, framed the campaign explicitly:

> *"It is precisely now and only now, when in the starving regions people are eating human flesh, and hundreds if not thousands of corpses are littering the roads, that we can (and therefore must) carry out the requisition of church valuables with the most ferocious and merciless energy, not stopping at the suppression of any resistance whatsoever ... The greater the number of representatives of the reactionary clergy and the reactionary bourgeoisie that we succeed in shooting on this occasion, the better."*

The April-June 1922 public trial in Moscow of clergy who had resisted the requisition concluded with 11 death sentences and 5 executions in May 1922. Tikhon was placed under house arrest at the Donskoy Monastery in April 1922 and held under formal charges of sabotage from 6 May 1922 to 26 June 1923 — fourteen months of imprisonment.

While Tikhon was imprisoned, the OGPU engineered the schism within the Russian Orthodox Church.

The state-supported faction operated under the name **the Living Church** — also Renovationist Church (*Obnovlencheskaya Tserkov'*). The OGPU backed the takeover of the patriarchal administrative apparatus in May 1922 by a group of Renovationist clergy headed by Alexander Vvedensky (1889-1946), Vladimir Krasnitsky, and Antonin Granovsky, citing Tikhon's incapacity. The Renovationist programme proposed institutional reforms — married clergy permitted at all hierarchical levels; vernacular Russian rather than Church Slavonic in the liturgy; revised calendar; explicit accommodation with the Soviet state. The institutional model was the Lutheran or Anglican state-church accommodation, transplanted to the Bolshevik political authority.

The Living Church Sobor of April-May 1923 at Moscow formally deposed Tikhon, dissolved the patriarchate, and removed monasticism as a requirement for episcopal office. The Sobor's resolutions praised the Soviet government and condemned counter-revolutionary clergy. By the end of 1925 around 16,000 of the 30,000 functioning Russian parishes had defected to the Renovationists or fallen into administrative chaos. The faithful Orthodox understanding of the situation was that the OGPU had manufactured an alternative church to displace the patriarchal church the Bolshevik state could not directly control.

Tikhon, under sustained OGPU pressure during his imprisonment, issued a statement of partial accommodation with the Soviet state on 16 June 1923 in exchange for release. He was released on 26 June 1923 and resumed patriarchal functions until his death on 7 April 1925 — under disputed circumstances (poisoning has been alleged in Russian Orthodox émigré sources; the authorities ruled it natural causes). His Last Will of 7 April 1925 named Metropolitan Peter Polyansky of Krutitsy as locum tenens.

The Renovationist schism collapsed across the late 1920s and 1930s as the state withdrew operational support — the OGPU had used the Renovationists as the instrument to break Tikhon and the patriarchal church; once the patriarchal church was sufficiently weakened, the Renovationists were of no further use to the state and were allowed to wither. The last Renovationist parish was closed in 1946. The institutional damage to the Russian church across the 1922-1946 schism, however, had been done.

**Metropolitan Peter Polyansky** of Krutitsy (1862-1937), Tikhon's designated locum tenens, was arrested by the OGPU in December 1925 — eight months after Tikhon's death. Polyansky was held in Soviet prisons and exile for the next twelve years and was executed on 10 October 1937 during the Great Purge. His succession was assumed by his deputy: **Metropolitan Sergius (Stragorodsky)**.

Sergius — Ivan Nikolayevich Stragorodsky, born 11 January 1867 at Arzamas near Nizhny Novgorod — had himself joined the Renovationist schism in June 1922 and repented and returned to the patriarchal church in August 1923. He had been imprisoned briefly across 1926-1927. **OGPU officer Yevgeny Tuchkov**, head of the OGPU's 6th Section (the unit responsible for the Russian Orthodox Church across the 1920s), operated as Sergius's interrogator and case officer. Tuchkov's methods of sustained pressure to extract institutional concessions are documented in the post-1991 declassified OGPU archives.

On **29 July 1927**, Sergius, together with the Provisional Patriarchal Holy Synod he had assembled under OGPU sanction, issued the **Declaration of Loyalty** — the encyclical letter professing absolute submission of the Russian Orthodox Church to the Soviet government. The operative text went on the record:

> *"We wish to be Orthodox and at the same time to recognize the Soviet Union as our civil motherland, whose joys and successes are our joys and successes, and whose misfortunes are our misfortunes ... Each blow directed at the Soviet Union ... is recognized by us as a blow directed against us."*

The 1927 Declaration is the foundational text of what came to be called *Sergianism* — the governing doctrine of unconditional ecclesiastical submission to the Soviet state, justified as the price of institutional survival. The doctrine produced its own schism. Senior Russian hierarchs inside the USSR opposed Sergius — Metropolitan Joseph of Leningrad and other dissenting bishops broke communion with the Sergianist administration. The **Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia** (ROCOR), the émigré jurisdiction headquartered at Sremski Karlovci in Yugoslavia from 1921 and later at New York from 1950, formally severed administrative ties with Moscow. The North American and Western European Russian metropolitan structures broke from Moscow. The **Catacomb Church** — underground Russian Orthodox congregations refusing to commemorate Sergius — emerged across the late 1920s and 1930s and operated clandestinely outside the registered Soviet-recognised church for the rest of the Soviet period.

The 1929 *Law on Religious Associations* further tightened the pressure on Orthodox life. Religious associations could not own property, conduct charitable work, or organise activities beyond the four walls of the registered place of worship. Religious education for minors was prohibited beyond the immediate family. The **League of Militant Atheists** under Yemelyan Yaroslavsky (founded 1925 as the League of Godless, renamed 1929) was the state-sponsored anti-religious propaganda apparatus, conducting public demonstrations, publishing anti-religious materials, organising the destruction of churches, and running anti-religious education programmes across the next decade.

The institutional destruction of the institution across the period 1929 to 1941 reached its peak under Stalin's First and Second Five-Year Plans (1928-1932, 1933-1937) and the Great Purge (1936-1938). Around **50,000 of the 54,000 Russian Orthodox churches in the USSR** were closed, demolished, or converted to secular uses across 1929-1939. The **Cathedral of Christ the Saviour in Moscow** — built 1839-1883 as the Romanov national monument commemorating the 1812 defeat of Napoleon — was demolished on 5 December 1931 to make way for the Palace of the Soviets (a building Stalin's planners never actually built. The site became the Moskva Pool from 1958 to 1995; the Cathedral was rebuilt 1995-2000 under the post-restoration). The Great Purge clergy killings consumed those who had survived the 1929-1935 wave. By 1939 around 80 percent of the surviving Orthodox clergy from 1928 had been killed, imprisoned, or driven from ministry.

By **mid-1941**, on the eve of the German invasion, **only four bishops remained free in the territory of the USSR**: Sergius himself as Patriarchal Locum Tenens (he had assumed the title in 1936 on incorrect reports of Peter Polyansky's death); Alexei (Simansky) of Leningrad; Nicholas (Yarushevich) of Kiev; and Sergius (Voskresensky), Exarch of the Baltic States. The Orthodox apparatus as a public-functioning institution had been in large measure destroyed.

Then came **22 June 1941** and the German invasion. Operation Barbarossa transformed the political-religious calculation overnight.

The German occupation of Ukraine, Belarus, and western Russia (1941-1944) included the reopening of churches the Soviet state had closed. By 1942 around 10,000 churches were operating in the German-occupied territories — more than were operating in unoccupied Soviet territory. The popular religious revival in the occupied territories created a political-strategic problem for Stalin: religious belief had not been eradicated by twenty years of Bolshevik persecution. Under conditions of catastrophic war and German occupation, the institution was demonstrating that it remained a popular force the Soviet state would need to either accommodate or contend against on terms it could not afford.

Sergius issued an encyclical on **22 June 1941**, the day of the invasion, calling on Russian Orthodox believers to rally to the defence of the country. The Orthodox parishes that remained operational across the Soviet territory began organising large-scale donations for the war effort. The pivot was decisive. By 1943 Stalin had decided the accommodation.

On **3 September 1943** Stalin summoned Metropolitans Sergius (Stragorodsky), Alexei (Simansky), and Nicholas (Yarushevich) to the Kremlin for a meeting the following day. The session took place on the evening of **4 September 1943** with Stalin and his lieutenant Vyacheslav Molotov present. The meeting record was drawn up by **Georgy Karpov**, a former NKVD officer who had been head of the NKVD's 4th Department of the 3rd Directorate (the unit responsible for religious affairs and intelligence operations against the church). Karpov was named the first head of the new **Council for the Affairs of the Russian Orthodox Church** established the same month under the Council of Ministers.

Stalin's concessions at the September 1943 meeting:

- Permission to convene a Bishops' Council to elect a Patriarch.
- Reopening of theological academies and seminaries.
- Reopening of churches across Soviet territory.
- Permission for the establishment of a Holy Synod.
- Release of some imprisoned clergy.

The convocation speed indicates the prior NKVD organisation: **on 8 September 1943**, four days after Stalin's summons, the **Bishops' Council** convened at Moscow. Nineteen hierarchs in attendance, including some recently released from prison camps. The Council elected Metropolitan Sergius as **Patriarch Sergius of Moscow and All Rus'** on the same day.

Sergius died less than eight months later, on **15 May 1944** at the age of 77. The 31 January – 4 February 1945 Local Council elected **Alexei (Simansky)** as the new patriarch — Patriarch Alexei I, patriarch from 4 February 1945 to 17 April 1970.

The post-1943 Moscow Patriarchate operated as an instrument of Soviet foreign policy. The Council for the Affairs of the Russian Orthodox Church (renamed Council for Religious Affairs in 1965) was the supervisory apparatus. The Plenipotentiary (*Upolnomochenny*) at each diocesan level acted as the local NKVD and later KGB liaison to the patriarchal administration. The bishop reported, in practice, to the local Plenipotentiary as much as he reported to his metropolitan.

The Khrushchev anti-religion campaign of **1959-1964** reversed the relative accommodation of the 1943-1958 period. The around 22,000 functioning Russian Orthodox parishes of 1958 were reduced to around 7,500 by 1964. Around 50 monasteries were closed. Theological seminaries were cut from 8 to 3. The 1961 constitutional reform forced under state pressure removed the local parish priest from administrative authority over the parish, which passed to a lay *dvadtsatka* — a group of twenty registered laity, easily infiltrated by KGB-aligned individuals. The parish administrative structure became, in operative practice, accountable to the local Plenipotentiary rather than to the bishop.

The Brezhnev period (1964-1982) settled into stable lower-intensity persecution. The institution operated as a state-tolerated apparatus, with around 7,000 functioning parishes through to the late 1980s. The KGB's institutional control over the patriarchate consolidated across the Brezhnev period — and the record of that control would emerge in the post-1991 archival opening.

The **KGB's Fifth Chief Directorate** (founded 1967 under Filipp Bobkov) handled internal political and religious matters. The **Department of External Relations of the Moscow Patriarchate** (DECR), founded 1946 and the principal interface between the Moscow Patriarchate and the KGB's foreign-religious-policy operations, was headed across the period:

- **1960-1972**: Metropolitan Nikodim (Rotov).
- **1972-1989**: Metropolitan Yuvenaly (Poyarkov).
- **1989-2009**: Metropolitan (later Patriarch) Kirill (Gundyayev).

The Furov Report of 1974 — prepared by V. G. Furov, deputy chairman of the Council for Religious Affairs — classified the active Russian Orthodox bishops into three categories: those who actively supported Soviet policy; those who passively accepted it; and those who attempted to resist it. The Furov Report was smuggled out of the USSR and published in the Paris-based *Vestnik Russkogo Khristianskogo Dvizheniya* (Herald of the Russian Christian Movement) in 1979. The report is contemporaneous documentary evidence of the institutional Soviet supervision of the Orthodox episcopate.

The Mitrokhin Archive is the master documentary source.

**Vasili Mitrokhin** (1922-2004), KGB First Chief Directorate archivist from 1948 to 1984, made meticulous handwritten copies of thousands of KGB documents across his thirty-year tenure as the head of the archive of the KGB's foreign intelligence wing. After Mitrokhin's retirement and the Union's collapse, he defected to the United Kingdom in November 1992 with around 25,000 pages of handwritten documents. The Cambridge historian **Christopher Andrew** and Mitrokhin published the documentary findings in two volumes: ***The Sword and the Shield: The Mitrokhin Archive and the Secret History of the KGB*** (Basic Books, 1999) and ***The World Was Going Our Way: The KGB and the Battle for the Third World*** (Basic Books, 2005). The FBI's standard formulation: the Mitrokhin Archive is *"the most complete and extensive intelligence ever received from any source."*

**Father Gleb Yakunin** (1934-2014), Soviet-era Russian Orthodox dissident priest and human-rights activist who had been imprisoned 1979-1987 for *anti-Soviet agitation*, gained access in 1991-1992 to the KGB archives as vice-chairman of the **1992 Russian parliamentary commission investigating the KGB's role under the system**. Yakunin published the findings identifying senior Moscow Patriarchate hierarchs operating as KGB agents. His public letter to Patriarch Alexei II named the codenames of the senior hierarchs who had been KGB agents: **ADAMANT, ABBAT, TOPAZ, MIKHAILOV, and DROZDOV** (the Patriarch himself).

The cross-referenced findings from Yakunin's parliamentary commission, the Mitrokhin Archive, and the corroborating testimony of **Ion Mihai Pacepa** (the highest-ranking Soviet-bloc intelligence officer ever to defect to the West, head of Romania's foreign intelligence service before his 1978 defection) identify the senior Moscow Patriarchate hierarchs operating as KGB agents:

- **DROZDOV = Patriarch Alexei II** (Aleksei Mikhailovich Ridiger, 23 February 1929 – 5 December 2008), patriarch from 7 June 1990 to 5 December 2008. The October 1969 archive entry recording DROZDOV's European Conference of Churches mission to England, returning with information *"about certain persons of interest to the KGB"*; the 1983 entry on educational work at the Pskov-Pechory monastery; the February 1988 KGB Certificate of Honour proposed by Colonel V. I. Timoshevsky — all on the public record from the post-1991 archive disclosures. Pacepa's account adds the Estonian KGB archive identification with the same codename.
- **MIKHAILOV = Patriarch Kirill** (Vladimir Mikhailovich Gundyayev, born 20 November 1946), patriarch from 1 February 2009. Kirill served as a representative of the Moscow Patriarchate to the World Council of Churches in Geneva from 1971 under the WCC operational mission. His subsequent career — head of the DECR from 1989 to 2009; metropolitan from 1991; patriarch from 2009 — operated continuously through the position the Mitrokhin Archive documents him as having held under KGB sanction.
- **ADAMANT = Metropolitan Nikodim Rotov** (15 October 1929 – 5 September 1978), head of the Moscow Patriarchate's Department of External Relations from 1960 to 1972; one of the WCC presidents elected in 1975 (the Pacepa account gives 1972 — both dates appear in different sources). Nikodim died suddenly during an audience with the newly elected Pope John Paul I in the Vatican on 5 September 1978 — the same Pope John Paul I who himself died unexpectedly twenty-three days later on 28 September 1978.
- **ABBAT, TOPAZ, ANTONOV** and other named codenames identified in the Yakunin documents and the Mitrokhin Archive as senior Moscow Patriarchate hierarchs operating as KGB agents.

The Moscow Patriarchate has consistently denied the identifications. Patriarch Alexei II denied being DROZDOV until his death in 2008. The Russian Orthodox spokesperson Vsevolod Chaplin in 2002: *"Nobody has ever seen a single real document that would confirm the patriarch used his contacts with Soviet authorities to make harm to the church or to any people in the church."* The OrthodoxWiki article on Alexei II notes contestation around the authenticity of certain Estonian KGB archive documents (allegedly anachronistic fonts). The Mitrokhin Archive, the Yakunin parliamentary commission findings, and the Pacepa testimony are the dominant scholarly evidence base; the Moscow Patriarchate position is increasingly isolated within the international historiographical consensus across the period 1992 to 2026.

The Moscow Patriarchate's WCC operations across the Soviet period are themselves on the evidence. The World Council of Churches, founded 1948 at Amsterdam, became from the 1961 Moscow Patriarchate accession a platform for Soviet foreign-religious-policy operations. The Mitrokhin Archive documents the systematic KGB use of Russian Orthodox clerical delegations to WCC meetings as intelligence-gathering operations, influence-operations on Western religious-political networks, and disinformation conduits. The 1972 or 1975 election of Metropolitan Nikodim (ADAMANT) as one of the WCC presidents represents the structural peak of the KGB operation within the WCC.

The Mikhail Gorbachev opening of the late 1980s permitted, for the first time since the 1920s, the state-sanctioned celebration of Russian Orthodox public observance. The **1988 millennium of the baptism of Vladimir at Chersonesus** (988) was marked as a major national-religious event with state participation. Thousands of churches and monasteries were returned to the Moscow Patriarchate across the 1988-1991 period.

On **7 June 1990** Aleksei Mikhailovich Ridiger, Metropolitan of Leningrad — DROZDOV in the KGB records — was elected **Patriarch Alexei II** of Moscow and All Rus'. He would serve as patriarch through to his death on 5 December 2008. His successor would be the head of the DECR from 1989 to 2009: Kirill (Gundyayev) — MIKHAILOV in the KGB records.

On **8 December 1991** the Belavezha Accords dissolved the Soviet Union. The Moscow Patriarchate emerged from the seventy-four-year Soviet period as the structure that § IV will dossier — the senior hierarchy in large measure populated by figures whose KGB-agent identifications had been documented in the Mitrokhin Archive, the Yakunin parliamentary commission, and the corroborating sources, but had been addressed by no institutional reckoning. There was no Russian Orthodox truth-and-reconciliation process. There was no patriarchal acknowledgement of the KGB infiltration. There was no purge of the documented agents. The continuity from the Soviet-era KGB-supervised Moscow Patriarchate to the post-Soviet Moscow Patriarchate under Alexei II and then under Kirill is, on the governing record, unbroken.

What that institutional continuity has produced across the period 1991 to 2026 — the alignment of the Moscow Patriarchate under Patriarch Kirill with the Vladimir Putin political-economic apparatus dossiered in Volume Two, the 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly crisis, the Moscow Patriarchate's institutional blessing of the 22 February 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine — § IV sets out.

---


### IV. The Contemporary Kirill-Putin Alignment (1991–2026)

The 1991 dissolution of the Soviet Union ended the Bolshevik state but not the architecture § III dossiered. The patriarchate that emerged into the post-Soviet period was the same hierarchy the Mitrokhin Archive, the Furov Report, and the Yakunin parliamentary commission had identified as KGB-supervised. There was no truth-and-reconciliation process. There was no patriarchal acknowledgement of the infiltration. The senior figures named in the post-1991 disclosures continued in office. Patriarch Alexei II — DROZDOV — operated as patriarch through to 2008. His successor Kirill — MIKHAILOV — has operated as patriarch from 2009 to the moment of this volume's publication in 2026. The contemporary apparatus this section sets out is the continuation of what § III set out; what has changed is the political-economic environment within which the apparatus operates. The Russian state under Vladimir Putin (Volume Two) has provided the Moscow Patriarchate the resources, legal privileges, and political protection it could not extract from the Soviet state — and in exchange the patriarchate has provided the Putin apparatus the religious-ideological legitimation it requires.

**Patriarch Alexei II** — Aleksei Mikhailovich Ridiger, born 23 February 1929 at Tallinn in the then-independent Estonia, died 5 December 2008 at his country residence at Peredelkino — was elected patriarch on 7 June 1990, eighteen months before the 8 December 1991 Belavezha Accords that ended the Soviet Union. His eighteen-year patriarchate spanned the entire post-Soviet institutional rebuilding.

The numerical reconstruction was dramatic. The around 7,000 functioning Orthodox parishes of 1988 grew to around 30,000 across the period to 2008. The around 50 monasteries of 1988 grew to around 800. Property restitution from the state, donor support from the post-Soviet Russian business community, and direct state grants funded the rebuilding. The institutional emblem of the project was the **Cathedral of Christ the Saviour** — demolished on 5 December 1931 (§ III) and now rebuilt from 1995 to 2000 on the Moskva Pool site under Moscow Mayor Yuri Luzhkov's patronage. Alexei II consecrated the rebuilt cathedral on 19 August 2000.

The positioning of the church-state relationship under Alexei II crystallised in two documents.

The 1997 *Law on Freedom of Conscience and Religious Associations* (Federal Law No. 125-FZ, signed by Boris Yeltsin on 26 September 1997) gave the Orthodox church a privileged status as one of the *traditional* religions of Russia (alongside Islam, Buddhism, and Judaism) and imposed stricter registration requirements on other religious associations. The law has operated across the period 1997-2026 as the framework for the patriarchate's privileged position within the state apparatus. Foreign-based religious associations — Catholic, Protestant, Jehovah's Witness, Mormon — operate under restrictions that the church does not face.

The 2000 *Bishops' Council* issued the **Basis of the Social Concept of the Russian Orthodox Church** — the contemporary position document on church-state relations, the family, work, property, war and peace, ecology, science and culture, sexuality, the global economy, and bioethics. *The Basis of the Social Concept* is the foundational text of contemporary Moscow Patriarchate political theology. Its formulations on the family, on sexuality, on the legitimate uses of state violence, on the priority of *traditional values* over Western liberal individualism. These formulations have travelled across the next twenty-five years into the documented institutional language of the contemporary Russian state, the international conservative-religious network coordinated through Konstantin Malofeev's operations, and the contemporary Putin political-ideological discourse.

The emigré opposition to the Moscow Patriarchate that had operated since the 1927 Sergius Declaration (§ III) closed under Alexei II's patriarchate. **On 17 May 2007** Alexei II and ROCOR Metropolitan Laurus signed the *Act of Canonical Communion* at the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour, ending the 1927-2007 schism between the Moscow Patriarchate and the Church Outside Russia. The reunion negotiation operated under direct Putin patronage. Putin had personally met Laurus at the Synodal Cathedral of the Sign in New York in 2003 to begin the discussions. Putin attended the 17 May 2007 ceremony at the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour. The church, after eighty years of canonical division, operated again as a single global structure under Moscow — under a patriarch the post-1991 archival disclosures had identified as a KGB agent. ROCOR's opposition to Soviet-era infiltration had been transcended by the post-Soviet political settlement.

Alexei II died at Peredelkino on 5 December 2008. The succession produced the figure this section dossiers.

**Vladimir Mikhailovich Gundyayev** — Patriarch Kirill from 1 February 2009 — was born **20 November 1946** at Leningrad. His father Mikhail Gundyayev was a Orthodox priest; his grandfather Vasily Gundyayev had been imprisoned in the Solovki camps across multiple terms 1922-1955 as a priest persecuted under the Bolshevik anti-religious campaigns § III set out. The biographical claim that Kirill's family stood within the tradition of persecuted clergy carries documented weight — but his trajectory followed a path the Mitrokhin Archive and the Yakunin parliamentary commission identified as KGB-supervised collaboration rather than persecution.

The young Vladimir worked as a cartographer at the Leningrad Geological Expedition from 1962 to 1965 before entering the **Leningrad Theological Seminary** in 1965 under the direct patronage of **Metropolitan Nikodim (Rotov)** — the senior Moscow Patriarchate figure documented in § III as KGB-codename ADAMANT. Gundyayev was tonsured a monk on 3 April 1969 by Nikodim, taking the name Kirill (after the saint who, with his brother Methodius, brought Christianity to the Slavs in the ninth century and is credited with the invention of the precursor Cyrillic alphabet). Ordained priest 7 April 1969. He served as Nikodim's personal secretary from 1971 to 1974. The Mitrokhin Archive records the codename MIKHAILOV appearing in the KGB documents from the **1971 deployment of Gundyayev to Geneva as representative to the World Council of Churches** — the moment at which the patronage relationship with Nikodim moved him into the position of the church-KGB foreign-operations interface § III dossiered.

his career across the next four decades operated within the position the Mitrokhin Archive identified:

- **1971-1974**: Representative to the WCC at Geneva.
- **1974-1984**: Rector of the Leningrad Theological Academy.
- **14 March 1976**: Consecrated bishop by Nikodim (ADAMANT).
- **1984-1990**: Archbishop of Smolensk and Vyazma (renamed Smolensk and Kaliningrad 1989).
- **1989-2009**: Chairman of the Department of External Church Relations (DECR) — the position § III documents as the principal church-KGB foreign-operations interface across the Soviet period.
- **25 February 1991**: Elevated to Metropolitan.
- **6 December 2008**: Elected locum tenens of the patriarchal throne on Alexei II's death.
- **27 January 2009**: Elected Patriarch of Moscow and All Rus' by the 2009 Local Council (Pomestny Sobor) — 508 votes of around 700 delegates.
- **1 February 2009**: Enthroned at the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour. President Dmitry Medvedev and Prime Minister Vladimir Putin attended the enthronement ceremony.

Kirill became the 16th Patriarch of Moscow since Job in 1589 — the post the 1721 Petrine reform had abolished (§ II) and the 1917-1918 Local Council had restored before the Bolshevik persecution (§ III).

The doctrinal-ideological project Kirill brought to the patriarchate had been developing across the previous decade. The **Russkii Mir** (*Russian World*) doctrine emerged across the 2000s under the intellectual leadership of **Gleb Pavlovsky** (Putin's adviser 1996-2011), **Vladislav Surkov** (Putin's deputy chief of staff 1999-2011, then deputy prime minister), and the conservative Orthodox theological circle around the patriarchate that he led from his DECR position.

The doctrine fuses three commitments. Russian-speaking peoples, regardless of geographic location, are members of a single civilisational community. Orthodoxy is the spiritual core of that community. The Russian state is the political-military defender of that community against Western liberal-democratic encroachment.

The vehicle for the doctrine's promotion abroad is the **Russkii Mir Foundation** — established by Putin decree in 2007, before Kirill's accession to the patriarchate. The Foundation operates around 100 *Russian Centres* worldwide in 2026, frequently hosted within Orthodox parishes in the diaspora. The institutional architecture by which Orthodox parishes abroad operate as platforms for the *Russkii Mir* political-cultural project was in place when Kirill became patriarch in 2009; what he provided after 2009 was the religious-theological elevation of the project from political-cultural doctrine to spiritual mission.

His statements across the period 2009-2022 progressively framed the *Russkii Mir* as a theological commitment. Orthodox Christianity as the defining marker of the Russian civilisational community. The West as the *decadent and deplorable* civilisational opposite. The Moscow Patriarchate as the global custodian of authentic Orthodox tradition against Constantinople's accommodation with Western secularism. The institutional language carried forward the Philotheus-of-Pskov *Third Rome* doctrine (§ II) into contemporary state ideology.

The personal alignment is on the public record across the period 2009-2026.

On **8 February 2012**, weeks before the 4 March 2012 presidential election that returned Putin to the presidency after the 2008-2012 Medvedev tandem, the patriarch described Putin's rule on Russian state television as *"a miracle of God."* The quote has become the documented emblem of the alignment. Putin has attended every major Orthodox liturgical event of the period. The patriarch has attended every major Putin political-ceremonial occasion.

The material settlement followed. Kirill was granted a Kremlin residence in 2004 — five years before his elevation to the patriarchate, indicating the prior positioning. The state began the systematic restitution of formerly confiscated church property to the Moscow Patriarchate from 2010. The *Law on the Transfer of Religious Property in State or Municipal Ownership to Religious Organizations* (Federal Law No. 327-FZ, 30 November 2010) returned around 30,000 properties to religious organisations across the next decade. The Moscow Patriarchate is the principal beneficiary. The state additionally provides direct grants to operations running into the hundreds of millions of dollars annually across 2010-2026.

The institutional incident that crystallised the international perception of the alignment was the 2012 Pussy Riot case.

On **21 February 2012**, members of the Russian feminist punk performance collective Pussy Riot performed a *Punk Prayer* at the soleas of the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour in Moscow. The performance lyrics included the line *"Patriarch Gundyaev believes in Putin / It would be better, the bastard, if he believed in God."* Three of the performers — Nadezhda Tolokonnikova, Maria Alyokhina, Yekaterina Samutsevich — were arrested on 3 March 2012. The 17 August 2012 conviction on charges of *"hooliganism motivated by religious hatred"* produced sentences of two years' imprisonment for Tolokonnikova and Alyokhina and a suspended sentence for Samutsevich. The international response — the global solidarity campaigns, the public statements by major Western religious and political figures, the documented condemnations from Amnesty International — established the Pussy Riot case as the emblem of the criminalisation of opposition to the Moscow Patriarchate's position.

The political-military operations followed within two years.

On **18 March 2014** the Russian Federation formally annexed Crimea following the February 2014 unmarked-uniformed military intervention. The Moscow Patriarchate endorsed the annexation. His public statements across spring 2014 framed the operation as a defensive measure protecting Orthodox Russians from the Western-backed *Maidan* government in Kyiv. From April 2014 the Russian-backed military operations expanded into the Donbas region of eastern Ukraine, conducted through nominally separatist Donetsk and Luhansk People's Republics.

The Ukrainian Orthodox response across the next four years would produce the canonical crisis that broke the church's relationship with the rest of the world communion.

The Ukrainian Orthodox situation prior to 2018 was fractured. The **Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)** — the UOC-MP — had operated under Moscow's canonical jurisdiction since the 1686 transfer of the Kyiv metropolitanate from Constantinople to Moscow. The **Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Kyiv Patriarchate** — the UOC-KP — had been founded in 1992 under Patriarch Filaret (Mykhailo Denysenko) after a break from Moscow; the UOC-KP was considered schismatic by all other Orthodox jurisdictions. The **Ukrainian Autocephalous Orthodox Church** — the UAOC — was the émigré-rooted jurisdiction.

In April 2018 Ukrainian President Petro Poroshenko, in a meeting with Ecumenical Patriarch Bartholomew I at the Phanar in Istanbul, formally requested the granting of autocephaly to a unified Ukrainian Orthodox Church independent of Moscow. The process moved across the next nine months.

In September 2018 Bartholomew appointed two exarchs to Kyiv to coordinate the autocephaly process: Archbishop Daniel of Pamphilon (United States) and Bishop Hilarion of Edmonton (Canada). On **14 September 2018** the Holy Synod stopped commemorating the Ecumenical Patriarch during the divine liturgy — the first formal break.

On **11 October 2018** the Synod of the Ecumenical Patriarchate announced it would proceed with the granting of autocephaly to the Church of Ukraine. The Synod declared the 1686 transfer of the Kyiv metropolitanate to Moscow invalid; lifted the anathemas on Patriarch Filaret (UOC-KP) and Metropolitan Makary (UAOC).

On **15 October 2018** the Holy Synod broke full communion with the Ecumenical Patriarchate — the **2018 Moscow-Constantinople Schism**. The Moscow position: Constantinople had committed an *uncanonical intervention* in canonical territory.

On **15 December 2018** the **Unification Council** at the Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kyiv brought together representatives of the UOC-KP, the UAOC, and some bishops of the UOC-MP. The Council established the new **Orthodox Church of Ukraine** (OCU) and elected **Metropolitan Epiphanius I** — Serhii Petrovych Dumenko, born 3 February 1979 — as primate.

On **5 January 2019** at the Patriarchal Church of St George at the Phanar in Istanbul, Ecumenical Patriarch Bartholomew I signed the **Tomos of Autocephaly** establishing the Orthodox Church of Ukraine as the fifteenth autocephalous local Orthodox church in the world. The next day Bartholomew presented the tomos to Metropolitan Epiphanius. On **7 January 2019** Ukrainian President Poroshenko received the tomos at the Saint Sophia Cathedral in Kyiv.

The OCU's autocephaly was recognised by the Church of Greece (October 2019), the Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria (November 2019), and the Church of Cyprus (October 2020) — three of the fifteen autocephalous Orthodox churches. the patriarchate broke communion with each recognising body. The 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly is the operative break-point between the Moscow Patriarchate and the senior Greek-speaking world. The rupture is unresolved as of 2026.

Three years later the war came.

On **22 February 2022** (the date adopted by one common dating; many sources use 24 February 2022, the date of the declaration of the *Special Military Operation* and the major ground offensive) the Russian Federation under President Vladimir Putin launched the full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Russian forces crossed Ukrainian borders from Belarus, Crimea, and Russian territory; Russian missile and air strikes hit Kyiv, Kharkiv, Mariupol, Odesa, and other major Ukrainian cities. The invasion has continued to the moment of this volume's publication in 2026.

The Moscow Patriarchate's position on the invasion was set within days.

On **23 February 2022** Kirill spoke for Defender of the Fatherland Day at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier in Moscow. The patriarchal participation in the state's military-commemorative ceremony on the eve of the invasion went on the public record.

On **27 February 2022** — the *Forgiveness Sunday* before the start of Great Lent — the patriarch preached the first major statement after the invasion at the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour. The sermon framed the conflict as a *"metaphysical"* struggle and named the Donbas as the site of the spiritual defence of Orthodoxy against the *"depraved Western world."*

On **6 March 2022** — the next Sunday — he preached the sermon that became the documented international emblem of the patriarchal blessing of the war. The text declared the war a defence against the *"depraved Western world"* poisoned by liberalism and secularism, citing *gay parades* as the documented symbol of the Western moral decadence the invasion was opposing. The Pope Francis video meeting with the patriarch on 16 March 2022 — the Pope's warning against *"hiding behind religion to justify armed aggression and conquest"* — produced no shift in the patriarchal position.

In April 2022 the European Union proposed the patriarch for inclusion in the sanctions list for his role in inciting the war. Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán blocked the inclusion across the May-June 2022 negotiations. He remained outside formal EU sanctions through to the publication of this volume in 2026.

On **3 June 2022** Metropolitan Hilarion (Alfeyev), the long-serving DECR chairman from 2009 to 2022 and Kirill's senior deputy, was removed from his post and reassigned to Budapest after failing to publicly endorse the invasion. Hilarion's removal signalled the consolidation of his war-supporting position within the hierarchy.

On **27 May 2022** the Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) at a council at the Feofania Convent in Kyiv declared its full independence from the Moscow Patriarchate over its handling of the invasion. The UOC-MP's break removed its last operating ecclesiastical presence in Ukraine.

The framework for the patriarchal blessing of the war came two years into the conflict. On **27 March 2024** the **World Russian People's Council** — the public organisation founded in 1993 and chaired by Patriarch Kirill from 2009 — issued a decree declaring the war in Ukraine a **Holy War**. The decree was issued at a synodal congress at the Cathedral of Christ the Saviour with Kirill presiding. The text cast the war as a spiritual crusade to preserve the unity of *Holy Rus* and elaborated the *Russian World* concept's spiritual and cultural importance beyond Russia's contemporary borders.

The patriarchal formulation across the next two years has framed the war as a defensive struggle for the Russian civilisational mission. *"Sacrifice in the course of carrying out your military duty washes away all sins,"* he has told his around 90 million Orthodox followers — the position the church has taken across the war years to the present.

The patriarchate's contemporary operational network integrates with the Russian political-economic apparatus dossiered in Volume Two.

**Konstantin Malofeev** (born 3 July 1974), Orthodox conservative billionaire, serves as the principal financial-organisational integrator. Malofeev founded Marshall Capital (a private equity firm) in 2005; the **St Basil the Great Charitable Foundation** in 2007 (the largest private Orthodox charity in Russia); **Tsargrad TV** in 2015 (the principal Orthodox conservative-nationalist broadcaster); and the Moscow operations of the international **World Congress of Families** that hosted the WCF's 2014 Moscow summit at the Kremlin. Malofeev has been under European Union sanctions since 2014 for his role in Russia's annexation of Crimea and the Donbas operations; United States Treasury OFAC sanctions since 2014; United Kingdom sanctions since 2022. Malofeev operates as documented deputy chairman of the World Russian People's Council under his chairmanship.

**Igor Girkin** (Strelkov, born 17 December 1970), Russian FSB officer and Malofeev associate, was the documented military operations commander in the seizure of Crimea (February-March 2014) and the early Donbas operations (April-August 2014, as defence minister of the self-declared Donetsk People's Republic). Girkin was convicted in absentia by the Dutch District Court of The Hague on **17 November 2022** of complicity in the **17 July 2014 downing of Malaysia Airlines Flight MH17** (298 deaths). Girkin was subsequently arrested in Russia on **21 July 2023** on charges of *extremism* after publicly criticising Putin's conduct of the war; sentenced to 4 years' imprisonment on **25 January 2024**.

**Alexei Komov** (born 1968), Orthodox businessman and conservative-religious operative, is the deputy chairman of Malofeev's Marshall Capital and the principal liaison between the patriarchal apparatus and the international conservative-religious network — the World Congress of Families, the documented contemporary American Christian Right operators dossiered in Volume Three Chapter 3 § IV.

The **World Russian People's Council** (*Vsemirny Russky Narodny Sobor*), founded in 1993 under Patriarch Alexei II and chaired by him from 2009, is the umbrella body coordinating the patriarchate's political-ideological operations with the Russian state apparatus and the conservative-business networks Malofeev integrates. The 27 March 2024 Holy War decree was issued under the WRPC banner.

The integration with Volume One operators (the Russian oligarchic-financial structures), Volume Two operators (the Putin political apparatus), and the international conservative-religious network is set out in detail in Chapter 17 of this volume (the Moscow contemporary apparatus chapter). The patriarchate under Kirill operates in 2026 as the religious arm of the Putin political-economic apparatus that Volume Two has proved. The history Chapter 4 has set out across § I (Byzantine *symphonia*), § II (Russian Petrine subordination), and § III (Soviet KGB infiltration) is the genealogy of what § IV has dossiered. The pattern is consistent across seventeen centuries. The Eastern Orthodox tradition's institutional form has, on the documentary record, been the religious-ideological apparatus of successive imperial-political projects from Constantine to Justinian, from Vladimir to Peter, from Stalin to Putin.

What remains for Chapter 4 to do is to close. The Eastern Orthodox capture is the most direct of the three Christian capture cases (the Catholic capture of Chapter 2 operating through the layered medieval-Renaissance papal-imperial settlement. The Protestant capture of Chapter 3 operating through the more diffuse state-church and later evangelical-political networks of Northern Europe and the Americas; the Eastern Orthodox capture operating, in each major imperial epoch, as the direct religious-ideological apparatus of a single sovereign political authority). The pattern Volume Three's preface set out — the capture of religious tradition by political power — is, in the Russian case, on the most explicit documentary record of Christian institutional history.

Chapter 5 turns to Sunni Islam.

---


## Chapter 5. The Sunni Muslim Capture

### I. The Origins and the Rashidun Caliphate (610–661)

The Christian capture cases Chapters 2, 3, and 4 dossiered share a common institutional structure: a prophet (Jesus, around 4 BCE – around 33 CE) whose own teaching included no political-military programme. Followed by three centuries of pre-imperial community formation, followed by the Constantinian capture of 313 and the imperial-religious fusion that has produced every subsequent Christian institutional trajectory. The Sunni Muslim case does not share that structure. The political-military fusion was present in the founding prophet's own career. He operated as religious-political-military leader of the community at Medina across the last ten years of his life. The institutional state, the military apparatus, the system of taxation, the legal corpus, the conduct of war against and within the unbelievers, the treatment of conquered populations and conquered religious minorities — all were established under his direct authority across the period 622-632.

The capture question for Sunni Islam, therefore, is not whether the religious tradition was captured by political power. The political fusion is the founding condition. The capture question is what happened to the position after his death — when the prophet who had operated as religious-political-military authority left no clear successor and no clear institutional procedure for selecting one.

What happened is what Chapter 5 dossiers across its four sections. § I covers the founding and the first thirty years (610-661). The prophet's career, the contested succession, the four-caliph Rashidun period, and the First Fitna. § II covers the Umayyad and Abbasid caliphal apparatuses across 661 to 1258. § III covers the Ottoman millet system and the Sultan-Caliph fusion from 1453 to the 1924 Atatürk abolition. § IV covers the contemporary Sunni capture apparatuses across 1924-2026. The Saudi-Wahhabi state-religious settlement, the Egyptian state-Sunni architecture, and the connected operations dossiered in detail in Chapter 18 of this volume.

**Muhammad ibn Abdullah** — Abū al-Qāsim Muḥammad ibn ʿAbd Allāh ibn ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib ibn Hāshim in the full Arabic patronymic form — was born around 570 at Mecca. His clan was the Banu Hashim, a less wealthy branch of the Quraysh tribe that controlled the Meccan caravan economy and the Kaaba shrine. His father Abdullah died around the time of his birth; his mother Amina died when he was six. He was raised first by his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib and then by his paternal uncle Abu Talib. Across his early adulthood at Mecca he was known by the epithet Al-Amin, *The Trustworthy*. Around the age of 25 he entered the service of and married Khadija bint Khuwaylid, a wealthy widow around 40 years old at the time. The marriage lasted twenty-five years until Khadija's death in 619 and was monogamous on Muhammad's part across its full duration — a marriage pattern he would not replicate after Khadija's death.

Around 610, when Muhammad was about forty years old, he began receiving at the cave of Hira near Mecca the recitations that the Muslim tradition holds were delivered by the angel Gabriel and would form the Quran. The early Meccan period of around thirteen years (610-622) produced a small Muslim community of followers under increasing persecution by the Quraysh leadership. In 615 some of the followers were sent to the Christian Aksumite kingdom in Abyssinia to escape persecution. Khadija — his wife and the first convert — died in 619. His uncle Abu Talib — who himself never converted — died with Abu Talib's death the same year. The Quraysh persecution intensified.

The transformation came in September 622. The Hijra — the migration from Mecca to Yathrib (now Medina), the agricultural oasis 320 kilometres north of Mecca — was warned by a Quraysh assassination plot against Muhammad. He slipped out of Mecca with Abu Bakr; his cousin Ali ibn Abi Talib slept in his bed to deceive the assassins. The two men arrived at Medina on **4 September 622**. The migration marks the beginning of the Islamic (Hijri) calendar. The Muhajirun (the *emigrants* from Mecca) joined the Ansar (the Medinan converts who had invited him to arbitrate the city's tribal disputes) to form the political-religious community he would lead for the next decade.

The Constitution of Medina — dated by various readings to 622-624, with the document's unity and authenticity contested in the contemporary scholarly literature — was the founding political text of the early Islamic community. The Constitution established an alliance among the Muhajirun, the Ansar, and the three major Jewish tribes of Medina (Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir, Banu Qurayza). The text named him as the leader of the new community (*umma*) and specified the political and military mutual-assistance obligations among the signatories. The Constitution of Medina is the founding political document of what the standard contemporary scholarship terms *the first Islamic state*.

The transformation at Medina was decisive. The prophetic mission moved from preaching minority status at Mecca to political-military rule at Medina. From 622 to 632 Muhammad conducted military campaigns from his Medinan base across the Arabian Peninsula and into the northern frontiers with the Byzantine Empire and its Ghassanid allies. The campaigns produced the political-religious unification of most of the Arabian Peninsula under his authority by 632.

The major engagements of the Medinan decade went on the record:

The **March 624 Battle of Badr** — Muslim forces defeated a Meccan caravan-guard force of around three times their size. Around 45 Meccans killed including the Meccan leader Abu Jahl; 70 Meccan captives taken for ransom; 14 Muslim dead. He directed the battle from a hut near the field alongside Abu Bakr. The Badr victory was decisive for the consolidation of his authority at Medina.

The **624 expulsion of the Banu Qaynuqa** — the first of the three major Jewish tribes of Medina, expelled for an alleged violation of the Constitution of Medina after a market dispute escalated.

The **March 625 Battle of Uhud** — a Meccan counter-attack. Muslim defeat. The prophet personally wounded. The Khalid ibn al-Walid cavalry commander on the Meccan side — who would later convert and become the principal military commander of the Ridda Wars and the early caliphal conquests — flanked the Muslim position and produced the rout.

The **May 625 expulsion of the Banu Nadir** — the second Jewish tribe of Medina, expelled after accusations of an assassination plot against him.

The **627 Battle of the Trench (Khandaq)** — a Meccan-led coalition siege of Medina. The Muslim defence was successful, in part through the trench-defence innovation suggested by the Persian convert Salman al-Farsi.

The **627 massacre of the Banu Qurayza** — the third and last major Jewish tribe of Medina. The tribe had remained at Medina through the previous expulsions; during the Battle of the Trench the Banu Qurayza were accused of collusion with the besieging Meccan coalition. After the siege ended, the men of the tribe — variously reported in the standard sources at around 600 to 900 — were executed by beheading in the marketplace of Medina; the women and children were enslaved. The episode is treated in the standard scholarly accounts (Watt, Welch, Peters, Donner) as the most controversial military action of Muhammad's career. The various interpretations — divine punishment of those who rejected the prophet, an act of political consolidation after the existential threat of the Trench, the operating norms of seventh-century Arabian intertribal warfare. An act inconsistent with the broader pattern of the prophet's treatment of Jewish communities outside Medina — are all named in the contemporary scholarly literature and across the Muslim and non-Muslim historiographies.

The **628 Treaty of Hudaybiyya** — a ten-year truce with the Quraysh, concluded after a Muslim pilgrimage attempt to Mecca was turned back. The Treaty permitted Muslim pilgrimages to the Kaaba beginning the following year.

The **628 conquest of the Jewish fortress at Khaybar** — the northern Hejaz Jewish stronghold taken; survivors permitted to remain on payment of half their produce to Medina. The Khaybar settlement is the precedent for the later Islamic *jizya* tax structure for the *People of the Book* — the position of Jewish, Christian, and Zoroastrian populations under Muslim political authority that would operate as the documented legal framework across the next thirteen centuries.

The **December 629 Conquest of Mecca** — He gathered an army of around 10,000 converts after a Meccan ally violated the Hudaybiyya truce. The conquest was largely uncontested. He entered Mecca, destroyed the pagan idols at the Kaaba (the standard Sira tradition gives a figure of 360 idols destroyed), and proclaimed the city the holy city of the new faith. The Quraysh tribal leadership — including Abu Sufyan, the head of the Umayyad clan and the principal Meccan opponent across the previous decade — submitted to the prophet's authority. The submission of the Umayyad clan to a religion they had opposed for nineteen years is the fact that would produce, thirty-two years later, the Umayyad dynastic capture of the caliphate (§ II).

The **October 630 Battle of Tabuk** — his expedition northward against Byzantine-allied Ghassanid forces. No actual battle was fought — the Byzantine forces had withdrawn or were not present at Tabuk in expected strength. The expedition consolidated Muslim political authority across the northwestern Arabian Peninsula and signalled the beginning of the Muslim military pressure against the Byzantine frontier that would, within five years of Muhammad's death, produce the loss of Syria, Palestine, and Egypt to the caliphal conquests.

The **March 632 Farewell Pilgrimage** to Mecca. On the way back to Medina, at the pool of Ghadir Khumm (16-19 March 632), he delivered the sermon containing the statement of Ali ibn Abi Talib: *"For whoever I am his mawla, Ali is his mawla."* The Arabic word *mawla* is linguistically ambiguous. It can mean *leader*, *friend*, *patron*, *trusted ally*, *master* (in the technical sense of a freed slave's relationship to his former owner). Shia tradition reads the Ghadir Khumm statement as the prophet's explicit designation of Ali as his successor. Sunni tradition reads it as affirmation of Ali's spiritual status without political designation. The contested reading is the central textual moment of the subsequent Sunni-Shia division.

He fell ill on his return to Medina. He died on **8 June 632** in the house of his wife Aisha, the daughter of Abu Bakr. He died at around the age of 62 by the conventional dating from around 570. His tomb at Al-Masjid an-Nabawi at Medina is the second holiest site in the contemporary Sunni Muslim tradition.

The succession question opened the same day.

The standard Sunni and Shia narratives diverge on what happened on 8 June 632 in the hours after the prophet's death. The Sunni account: a meeting of the Ansar at the **Saqifa** (porch) of the Banu Sa'ida clan was convened to elect a successor. The Ansar initially considered the leader Sa'd ibn Ubada as the new head of the community. Three Muhajirun — Abu Bakr, Umar ibn al-Khattab, and Abu Ubaida ibn al-Jarrah — arrived at the Saqifa on hearing of the meeting. Abu Bakr argued for Quraysh leadership of the community on the grounds of the close association of the Quraysh with the prophet and the priority of the Meccan emigrants in the new faith. Umar pledged allegiance (*bay'ah*) to Abu Bakr; the assembled companions present followed; the Saqifa elected Abu Bakr as the first caliph (*khalifa*, successor) on the same day the prophet died.

The Shia account: the Saqifa meeting was a faction-political seizure of the leadership while Ali — the prophet's cousin, son-in-law, and the figure of the Ghadir Khumm designation — was at the prophet's house preparing the body for burial together with the other Banu Hashim. The Shia tradition treats the Saqifa election as institutional usurpation; the Sunni tradition treats it as legitimate community consensus (*shura*) by the surviving senior companions.

What is uncontested across both accounts: Ali was not present at the Saqifa. The election of Abu Bakr proceeded in his absence. The election produced the structural position — the *caliphate* (*khilafa*) — that has structured Sunni Muslim political theology across the subsequent thirteen centuries.

**Abu Bakr** (Abd Allah ibn Abi Quhafa, around 573 – 23 August 634) was the first caliph, reigning from 8 June 632 to 23 August 634. From the Banu Taym clan of the Quraysh — not from the prophet's own Banu Hashim. He was father-in-law of the prophet through Aisha. An early convert; senior companion; the man who had accompanied him on the Hijra.

Abu Bakr's first major institutional challenge was the **Ridda Wars** (632-633). A number of Arabian tribes — particularly those who had only recently submitted to caliphal authority and who treated their submission as personal to the prophet rather than to the political community he had led — refused to pay the *zakat* (the obligatory alms tax) to the new caliph. Some tribes followed new prophets: Musaylima of the Banu Hanifa (the *liar* in the Sunni tradition); Tulayha of the Banu Asad; the prophetess Sajah of the Banu Tamim; al-Aswad al-Ansi in Yemen. Abu Bakr dispatched eleven military expeditions under the principal command of **Khalid ibn al-Walid** — the former Quraysh cavalry commander who had defeated the Muslims at Uhud in 625 and converted in 629. Khalid's campaigns produced the consolidation of central authority over the entire Arabian Peninsula by mid-633. The Ridda Wars established the institutional principle that submission to Islam was political submission to the caliphate as well as religious profession — a principle that would carry forward into the subsequent caliphal apparatuses across the next millennium.

Abu Bakr ordered the first compilation of the Quran into a single text following the deaths of large numbers of Quranic memorisers (*qurra*) at the Battle of Yamama against Musaylima in December 632. The compilation was kept with Abu Bakr, then with Umar, then with Umar's daughter Hafsa (one of Muhammad's widows).

The early conquests began under Abu Bakr's caliphate. Khalid ibn al-Walid's 633 campaign into Sassanian-held Iraq produced the Muslim victories at Walaja and Ullais. In April 634 the Battle of Ajnadayn produced the Muslim defeat of the Byzantine army in Palestine. On **23 August 634** Abu Bakr died of natural causes at Medina aged around 61. He had designated **Umar ibn al-Khattab** as his successor — the first instance of caliphal designation rather than community election, avoiding the contested Saqifa-style *shura* process. The Umar designation is the governing precedent for direct caliphal succession that would later be exploited by the Umayyad and Abbasid dynastic apparatuses.

**Umar ibn al-Khattab** (around 583 – 3 November 644) was the second caliph, reigning from 23 August 634 to 3 November 644. From the Banu Adi clan of the Quraysh. Father-in-law of the prophet through Hafsa. The Umar caliphate produced the great Arab conquests of the Levant, Egypt, and the Sassanian Persian Empire.

The Levant campaigns: in **August 636** the Battle of Yarmouk produced the Muslim defeat of the Byzantine army and the Byzantine loss of Syria and the Levant. In **637** Patriarch Sophronius surrendered Jerusalem to Umar personally; the Umariyya Covenant of 637 (the conventional dating; some sources place the text later) regulated the status of Christians and Jews under Muslim rule. In **638** Antioch fell.

The Sassanian campaigns: in **November 636** the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah under Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas produced the Muslim defeat of the Sassanian forces under Rustam Farrokhzad; the fall of the Sassanian capital Ctesiphon followed in March 637. In **December 642** the Battle of Nahavand — the *Conquest of Conquests* (Fath al-Futuh) — produced the final Muslim defeat of the Sassanian armies and the collapse of the Persian empire that had was the eastern superpower of the Mediterranean-Near Eastern world for more than four centuries.

Egypt fell to the campaign of Amr ibn al-As across 639-642. **November 641** saw the fall of Alexandria to Muslim forces (with a brief Byzantine counter-attack 645-646 retaking the city before final Muslim conquest).

Umar's institutional innovations transformed the Rashidun caliphate into a functioning imperial state. The *diwan* — the military pension register — produced the documented bureaucracy for paying the conquering Arab soldiers. The garrison cities (*amsar*) — Kufa, Basra, Fustat (later Cairo) — were Arab military-administrative centres separate from the conquered urban populations, producing the segregation of the Arab military-ruling class from the conquered Christian, Zoroastrian, and Jewish populations across the period. The **Hijri calendar** dating from the 622 Hijra was formally instituted by Umar as the official Muslim calendar.

On **3 November 644** Umar was assassinated at the Prophet's Mosque in Medina by **Piruz Nahavandi** (Abu Lu'lu'a Firuz), a Persian slave who stabbed Umar six times during the dawn prayer. The motive is disputed in the standard sources — disputes range from personal grievance over the slave's tax burden to political revenge for the Sassanian conquest. Umar lived several days after the attack, appointing a six-person *shura* committee — Ali, Uthman, Talha, al-Zubayr, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf — to select the next caliph from among themselves. The committee selected **Uthman ibn Affan**.

The selection was institutional decisive. Uthman (around 573 – 17 June 656) was from the **Umayyad clan** — the Banu Umayya of the larger Banu Abd-Shams branch of the Quraysh. The Umayyad clan had been the principal Quraysh opponents of Muhammad at Mecca before their conversion in 629. The Umayyad election to the caliphate produced the political conditions that would, within twenty-five years, transform the Rashidun caliphate into the Umayyad dynastic apparatus.

Uthman's caliphate (around 11 November 644 to 17 June 656) carried two principal institutional decisions on the record. The first: around 650, Uthman ordered the compilation of a single standardised text of the Quran from the Hafsa codex and other sources, and the destruction of all variant manuscripts. The **Uthmanic Quran** is the textual form transmitted across all subsequent Muslim history; the variant codices of the period — including those associated with Ibn Mas'ud, Ubayy ibn Ka'b, and others — were destroyed under the standardisation programme.

The second institutional decision was the appointment of Umayyad kinsmen to provincial governorships. **Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan** — Uthman's cousin from the Umayyad clan, the son of Abu Sufyan who had led the Meccan resistance to Muhammad — held the governorship of Syria from 639 under Umar, with the position confirmed and expanded under Uthman. Marwan ibn al-Hakam, Uthman's cousin, served as caliphal secretary. Walid ibn Uqba and Sa'id ibn al-As, Umayyad kinsmen, were appointed governors of Kufa. Abd Allah ibn Sa'd ibn Abi Sarh, Uthman's foster brother, was appointed governor of Egypt — replacing Amr ibn al-As, the conqueror of the province.

The Umayyad consolidation under Uthman generated provincial resistance. The *qurra* at Kufa, the early Egyptian settler-soldiers under their leaders, the Banu Hashim and other Quraysh clans excluded from Umayyad patronage — all converged on the question of Uthman's nepotism and his diversion of provincial revenues to Medina. The grievances accumulated across 654-656.

In June 656 rebels from Egypt and Iraq surrounded Uthman's residence at Medina. On **17 June 656** they broke into the house and killed Uthman. The caliph's blood is reported in the standard sources to have poured over the copy of the Quran he was reading; his wife Na'ila lost several fingers attempting to shield him. The Umayyad family fled Medina for Mecca and Damascus, taking with them Uthman's bloodied shirt and Na'ila's severed fingers as the symbols for their later demand for blood-revenge (*qisas*).

**Ali ibn Abi Talib** (around 600 – 28 January 661) was the fourth caliph. Cousin of the prophet; husband of his daughter Fatima; from the Banu Hashim. One of the earliest converts to Islam (the first male convert per the Shia tradition; one of the very early converts per the Sunni tradition). The figure named at Ghadir Khumm.

On **22 June 656** — five days after Uthman's killing — Ali was elected the fourth caliph at the Prophet's Mosque by acclamation of the Ansar, the Egyptian rebels still present in the city, and the major Muhajirun. He had been twenty-four years younger than Abu Bakr at the moment of the prophet's death (around 32 vs around 59); the Sunni standard explanation for his exclusion from the Saqifa decision in 632 has been his comparative youth. By 656 the question of seniority no longer obtained. The question now obtained whether the Banu Hashim — Muhammad's own clan — could recover the caliphate from the Umayyad branch that had captured it under Uthman.

Ali's caliphate produced the **First Fitna** — the first civil war of the Muslim community (656-661).

**Aisha**, the prophet's widow and daughter of Abu Bakr; **Talha ibn Ubayd Allah**, an early companion who had been a candidate in the Saqifa election; and **al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam**, another senior companion and brother-in-law of Ali — three of the most senior surviving companions of Muhammad — opposed Ali's election and demanded the immediate prosecution of Uthman's killers. They assembled forces at Mecca, marched to Basra, and captured the city. Ali moved his capital from Medina to Kufa (the more populous garrison city, with stronger support for his caliphate) and assembled forces against them.

The **December 656 Battle of the Camel** at Basra — named after the camel on which Aisha rode at the field, her presence on the battlefield itself producing significant theological-political controversy across both Sunni and Shia later traditions. Ali's forces won the engagement. Talha and al-Zubayr were killed in the battle. Aisha was sent back to Medina under honourable guard and lived in retirement at the city until her death in 678.

The Battle of the Camel did not end the war. **Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan**, governor of Syria since 639 and Uthman's Umayyad kinsman, refused to recognise Ali as caliph until the punishment of Uthman's killers — knowing that the killers were now among Ali's supporters and that Ali was politically incapable of moving against them. Mu'awiya assembled forces in Syria. Ali marched on Syria.

The two armies met at **Siffin** on the Euphrates near the contemporary Syrian-Iraqi border. The three-day battle of **26-28 July 657** produced heavy casualties — Mu'awiya's forces around 90,000-120,000 strong, Ali's forces around 80,000-90,000, with around 45,000 Syrian and 25,000 Iraqi dead per the standard scholarly estimates synthesised in the contemporary literature. The turning point of the battle: Mu'awiya's troops impaled copies of the Quran on their lances — the signal for arbitration on the basis of the Quran. Ali, under pressure from elements of his own army, reluctantly agreed.

The **Adhruh arbitration** ran from February 658 to January 659. Ali's representative was Abu Musa al-Ashari — chosen by the Kufan majority of Ali's army, against Ali's own better judgement. Mu'awiya's representative was **Amr ibn al-As**, the conqueror of Egypt whom Uthman had deposed from the Egyptian governorship in favour of his Umayyad foster brother and who had subsequently sided with Mu'awiya. The arbitration produced an ambiguous verdict — variously interpreted in the contemporary sources, with the dominant scholarly reading (Madelung, Petersen) treating it as a political defeat for Ali through Abu Musa's tactical naivety. The political effect: Mu'awiya's position was strengthened, Ali's was weakened.

The arbitration produced the third major theological-political position in early Islam. A faction of Ali's army — the **Kharijites** (*Khawarij*, *those who go out*) — opposed the arbitration itself as a betrayal of the principle that *judgment belongs to God alone* (Quran 6:57). The Kharijites withdrew from Ali's army to Harura and declared all those who had agreed to the arbitration — Ali himself included — to be infidels. The Kharijite doctrinal position — a grave sin without repentance nullifies a Muslim's status. And a community is obliged to depose and if necessary kill any leader who has fallen from the faith — emerged as the third Islamic theological-political position alongside the proto-Sunni (acceptance of the political status quo as legitimate) and proto-Shia (loyalty to the Ali line) positions that would crystallise across the next two centuries.

In **July 658** Ali's forces crushed the Kharijites at the Battle of Nahrawan in Iraq. The surviving Kharijites continued as a separate movement and would persist across many subsequent generations. The contemporary **Ibadi** tradition in Oman traces its lineage to the moderate wing of the early Kharijite movement.

Mu'awiya consolidated through 658-661. He took control of Egypt with the aid of Amr ibn al-As in 658. He was proclaimed caliph at Jerusalem in 659-660. His forces moved into Ali's territory in Iraq across 660-661.

On **28 January 661** Ali was assassinated at the mosque of Kufa by the Kharijite **Ibn Muljam al-Muradi** during the dawn prayer. He died of his wounds two days later on 31 January 661 — assassinated by an extremist of the doctrinal position that had emerged from his own army's revolt against the Siffin arbitration he had agreed to under his army's own pressure.

**Hasan ibn Ali** — Ali's eldest son and the prophet's grandson, around 625-670 — was acclaimed the fifth caliph by Ali's supporters at Kufa. After six months of negotiations with Mu'awiya, Hasan abdicated his claim to the caliphate in August 661 in exchange for a sizeable pension and the agreement that the caliphate would return to the Muslim community on Mu'awiya's death.

The agreement was not honoured. § II sets out what Mu'awiya did with the caliphate when he had it.

The Rashidun period (632-661) had operated under a leadership model the Sunni tradition would later canonise as *rightly-guided* (*rāshidūn*). The four caliphs are venerated by Sunni Muslims as the model of legitimate Islamic political-religious authority. The actual historical record of the period: one election by faction at the Saqifa with the principal alternative candidate excluded. One caliphal designation rather than community election; one selection by committee with the Umayyad-clan candidate chosen; one assassination by Persian slave; one contested election in the wake of a previous caliph's killing; one civil war producing three major battles, a doctrinal schism, and the assassinations of the third and fourth caliphs.

The leadership model Sunni political theology venerates was, on the evidence of its own first thirty years, characterised by three assassinations (Umar, Uthman, Ali), one civil war, the emergence of the Shia and Kharijite political-theological positions, and the co-option by the Umayyad clan that Muhammad had spent his entire prophetic career opposing.

The capture happened in those thirty years. § II sets out the consolidation under the Umayyad and Abbasid dynastic apparatuses.

---


### II. The Umayyad and Abbasid Caliphal Apparatuses (661–1258)

Six centuries of consolidation. The Umayyad dynastic capture from 661 transformed the Rashidun consultative office into hereditary monarchy. The Abbasid revolution of 750 replaced one dynasty with another but preserved and intensified the apparatus. The four canonical Sunni schools of jurisprudence (the *madhhabs*) and the six canonical *hadith* compendia consolidated under direct or indirect Abbasid caliphal patronage across the 8th to 10th centuries. The 833-851 inquisition (the *Mihna*) attempted and failed to establish caliphal doctrinal authority over the religious scholars; the failure produced the settlement that has structured Sunni political theology ever since — the caliph holds political-military authority, the *ulama* hold religious-doctrinal authority. The Mongol destruction of Baghdad on 10 February 1258 ended the caliphate as a working political-religious authority over a unified Muslim world. What followed — the Ottoman synthesis, the post-1924 vacuum, the contemporary capture apparatuses — § III and § IV will dossier.

**Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan** (around 602 – April 680) operated as first Umayyad caliph from August 661 to April 680. He was the son of **Abu Sufyan**, the principal Meccan opponent of Muhammad before the 629 conversion — the Umayyad clan that had spent nineteen years fighting the prophet now ruling the political community the prophet had founded. The irony has gone on the record since the contemporary sources first set it out.

Mu'awiya had served as governor of Syria from 639 under Umar, the post confirmed under his Umayyad kinsman Uthman, the post retained throughout the First Fitna under his own authority. The August 661 entry into Kufa and the acceptance of Hasan ibn Ali's abdication established him as sole ruler. He moved the capital from Medina to **Damascus** — the centre of Umayyad authority across the next nine decades. The administrative apparatus was built on the surviving Byzantine bureaucracy of Syria; the languages of government were Greek and Pahlavi until Abd al-Malik's Arabisation reforms of the 690s; the senior administrators were largely Christian Syrians inherited from the Byzantine imperial structure.
