Mu'awiya's restraint on Umayyad-clan patronage — restricting Umayyad influence to the Medina governorship rather than the broad Umayyad provincial network that had triggered the 656 revolt against Uthman — was the lesson learned. The Umayyad clan would rule the empire, but the Umayyad clan would not be allowed to occupy every senior provincial post and visibly enrich itself at provincial expense.

The poisoning of Hasan ibn Ali in around 670 is on the contested historical record. Hasan, Ali's elder son and Muhammad's grandson, had abdicated the caliphate to Mu'awiya in August 661 in exchange for a pension and the agreement that the caliphate would return to the community on Mu'awiya's death. Hasan died nine years after the abdication. The dominant Shia tradition names Mu'awiya as having ordered the poisoning, with Hasan's wife Ja'da bint al-Ash'ath as the operational agent under Mu'awiya's instigation. The Sunni tradition has variously accepted, hedged, or rejected the attribution. The contemporary scholarly consensus (Madelung 1997; Hawting 2000) treats Mu'awiya as the most likely responsible party. With Hasan dead, the principal obstacle to Mu'awiya's dynastic project — the agreement that the caliphate would return to the community on his own death — was removed.

The decisive Umayyad move came in 676. Mu'awiya nominated his own son **Yazid ibn Mu'awiya** (around 647 – November 683) as his successor. The historians Fitzpatrick and Walker, in the standard scholarship the *Battle of Karbala* Wikipedia synthesises, treat the Yazid designation as the transformation of the Muslim political community *"from a consultative form to a monarchy."* The Abu Bakr designation of Umar (634) and the Uthman *shura* committee (644) had already eroded the principle of community election. The Yazid designation completed the transformation. The office Muhammad had left undetermined at his death in 632 had become a hereditary throne in forty-four years.

The Yazid nomination was contested by the sons of the senior companions. **Husayn ibn Ali** (Ali's younger son, Muhammad's grandson), **Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr** (son of al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, killed at the Battle of the Camel 656), and **Abd Allah ibn Umar** (son of the second caliph) refused allegiance. Most other opposition was bribed or coerced into acceptance.

When Mu'awiya died at Damascus in April 680, Yazid succeeded. He demanded allegiance from the three principal contesting figures. Both Husayn and Ibn al-Zubayr travelled to Mecca for sanctuary at the Kaaba — the precedent that the holy sanctuary precluded armed seizure of the persons taking refuge there.

The Kufans then made their move.

The population of Kufa — the Iraqi garrison city that had been Ali's capital during the First Fitna — sent letters to Husayn at Mecca inviting him to come to Kufa and lead a revolt against the new Umayyad caliph. Husayn left Mecca for Kufa with a small party of around 70 male companions and accompanying women and children. The expectation: the Kufans who had supported his father against Mu'awiya twenty-three years earlier would now support the son against Mu'awiya's heir.

The expectation did not materialise. Yazid's appointed governor of Kufa, **Ubayd Allah ibn Ziyad**, suppressed the Kufan revolt in September 680 before Husayn arrived. The Kufan supporters were intimidated, imprisoned, or killed. Husayn continued northward on his journey, unaware of the change in conditions, expecting popular support that no longer existed. Ibn Ziyad's forces intercepted Husayn's small party at **Karbala** — a desert plain north of Kufa near the Euphrates.

Across **2-10 October 680** (the 1st through the 10th of the month of Muharram in the year 61 AH of the Hijri calendar) the standoff continued. Husayn refused to submit to Yazid. The Umayyad forces denied his small party access to the Euphrates water supply. The siege was the small-scale operation of a small force against a smaller one. Around 4,000 Umayyad troops faced around 70 of Husayn's companions.

On **10 October 680 (10 Muharram 61 AH)** the Battle of Karbala was fought. Husayn and around 70 of his male companions were killed in the engagement by the Umayyad troops under the field command of Umar ibn Sa'd. Husayn's body was decapitated and his head sent to Yazid at Damascus.

The consequence: the 10 October 680 killing of Muhammad's grandson by the Umayyad caliphal apparatus is the founding moment of contemporary Shia Muslim identity. The annual Shia commemoration of *Ashura* — the 10th of Muharram — has was the central liturgical observance of Shia Islam across the 1,346 years from 680 to the publication of this volume. Chapter 6 will dossier the Shia tradition that consolidated around the Karbala martyrdom; for the Sunni capture argument of Chapter 5, the Karbala killing is the moment at which Umayyad political authority confirmed its willingness to kill the Prophet's own family rather than recognise legitimate succession alternatives.

The Second Fitna (680-692) followed. Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr — the remaining contesting figure of the Yazid designation — established himself at Mecca as a rival caliph. Most provinces of the empire recognised Ibn al-Zubayr's authority across 683-685. The **August 683 Battle of al-Harra** produced Yazid's army's defeat of a Medinese rebellion and the sacking of Medina by Umayyad forces. The Umayyad forces besieged Mecca next; the Kaaba was set on fire during the siege. Yazid died at Damascus in November 683. The siege was abandoned. Marwan ibn al-Hakam — the Umayyad secretary who had served Uthman — emerged as the dominant Umayyad figure across 684; he died in April 685.

**Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan** (around 646 – 8 October 705) reigned as Umayyad caliph from 685 to 705. He reconquered Iraq from Ibn al-Zubayr by 691; sent his commander **al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf** to besiege Mecca in 692; Ibn al-Zubayr was killed at the Kaaba in October 692; the Second Fitna ended.

Abd al-Malik's consolidation produced the architectural and administrative form of the mature Umayyad state. The **Dome of the Rock** (Qubbat al-Sakhrah) was built at Jerusalem in 691-692 on the site of the Jewish Temple Mount — the earliest extant Islamic monumental architecture, an explicit declaration of the Umayyad caliphate's universal claim under God against the Christian and Jewish predecessors. The administrative apparatus was Arabised — Greek and Pahlavi were replaced by Arabic as administrative languages across the empire. The Arab-Islamic gold dinar and silver dirham coinage replaced the Byzantine and Sassanian currency.

The Umayyad expansion reached its geographic peak under al-Walid I (reigned 705-715). Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed into Spain in 711; Muhammad ibn al-Qasim conquered Sindh (modern Pakistan) in 711-712; Qutayba ibn Muslim conquered Transoxiana (modern Central Asia) across 705-715; Muslim forces raided into Anatolia and southern France. The 732 Battle of Tours/Poitiers — where Charles Martel defeated an Umayyad raiding force in central France — marks the conventional historiographical western limit of Umayyad expansion.

The Umayyad caliphate by the mid-8th century extended from the Atlantic coast of Morocco and Spain across North Africa and the entire former Sassanian Persian Empire to the Indus Valley and Central Asia. The territory was the largest empire in the world at the time — larger than the contemporary Tang dynasty in China and the Byzantine Empire combined. The Umayyad apparatus operated this empire as an Arab-imperial structure with a privileged Arab military-political class ruling over the conquered Persian, Coptic, Berber, Syrian, and other populations. The non-Arab Muslim converts — *mawali*, the clients — were taxed, politically excluded from senior offices, and treated as second-class members of the Muslim community. The grievances that the *mawali* of Persia accumulated across the period 710-747 produced the Abbasid Revolution.

The **Banu Abbas** were descendants of **al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib** — Muhammad's paternal uncle, of the same Banu Hashim clan as the prophet himself. The appeal of the Banu Abbas as alternative dynastic candidates: legitimate members of the prophet's own clan, in contrast with the Umayyad branch the Quraysh had originally fought Muhammad through. The Banu Abbas operated as a clandestine revolutionary movement based at al-Humayma in modern Jordan from the early 8th century. Their recruitment base across the eastern provinces was the disaffected *mawali* of Persia and Khurasan, supplemented by Arab tribesmen alienated from the Umayyad regime and remnants of Shia and Kharijite traditions seeking alternatives to Umayyad rule.

**Abu Muslim al-Khurasani** (around 718-755) — a Persian *mawali* convert appointed Abbasid chief propagandist in Khurasan around 745 — was the principal revolutionary commander. In **June 747** (Ramadan 129 AH) Abu Muslim raised the black banners of revolt at Merv in Khurasan. The Umayyad governor Nasr ibn Sayyar's forces were unable to suppress the rising; Nasr fled westward in February 748 and died en route.

The Abbasid military campaign moved westward under **Qahtaba ibn Shabib al-Ta'i**. Victories at Gurgan (Jurjan), Nishapur, and Nahavand across 748-749 cleared the eastern provinces. In August 749 the Abbasid armies reached the Euphrates near Kufa. The Banu Abbas leadership emerged from underground. On **28 November 749** (Friday), at the mosque of Kufa, **Abu al-Abbas Abd Allah ibn Muhammad ibn Ali** — born around 721 at al-Humayma, the great-grandson of Muhammad's uncle al-Abbas — was proclaimed the first Abbasid caliph. He took the regnal name **al-Saffah** (the translation has produced *The Blood-Shedder* and *The Slaughterer* alongside other readings; the name signalled the programme against the Umayyad apparatus).

The decisive engagement came two months later. On **25 January 750** at the **Battle of the Great Zab** (a tributary of the Tigris in northern Iraq) the Umayyad caliph **Marwan II** with around 120,000 Syrian troops faced the Abbasid army of around 100,000 under the command of **Abd Allah ibn Ali**, al-Saffah's uncle. The Abbasid victory was decisive. Marwan fled toward Egypt. He was captured and killed at Abusir in the Egyptian Nile Delta in August 750 by an Abbasid detachment under Abu Awn. His head was sent to Salih ibn Ali (another of al-Saffah's uncles, the Abbasid commander in the Syrian campaign).

The Umayyad family was then systematically eliminated. The **June 750 Abu Futrus banquet** (the conventional date; some accounts give variant dates and locations) was held by Abbasid forces who invited around 80-90 surviving members of the Umayyad family to a meal at the river Abu Futrus near Ramla in Palestine under guarantee of safe passage. The Umayyads were killed at the banquet. Senior princes; minor princes; their associates. The intent was the elimination of the dynasty. Survivors were hunted across the empire.

One Umayyad survived. **Abd al-Rahman ibn Mu'awiya** (around 731-788) — grandson of caliph Hisham — escaped the Abbasid purge and after five years of flight reached al-Andalus. In May 756 he entered Cordoba and was proclaimed *emir* of al-Andalus. The **Umayyad Emirate of Cordoba** he founded would operate as a separate Sunni political-religious authority through to 1031, becoming the Umayyad Caliphate of Cordoba under Abd al-Rahman III from 929. The consequence: from 929 to 1031 three caliphates operated simultaneously — Abbasid Baghdad, Umayyad Cordoba, and the Shia Fatimid caliphate in Egypt from 909.

The Abbasid consolidation across 750-833 produced the canonical Sunni apparatus.

Al-Saffah died of smallpox in June 754 at Anbar. His successor **al-Mansur** (Abu Ja'far Abd Allah, around 714 – October 775), reigning 754-775, was the founder of the Abbasid state. In **762** al-Mansur founded **Baghdad** (*Madinat al-Salam*, the *City of Peace*) on the Tigris around 30 kilometres north of the Sassanian capital Ctesiphon. The move shifted the centre of the Muslim world from Damascus to Mesopotamia, and from the Arab-Mediterranean political-cultural environment to the Persian-influenced eastern administrative culture. The rebalancing was decisive — the Persian *mawali* who had carried the Abbasid revolution were now politically integrated into the new caliphal apparatus; the Persian administrative tradition that had operated under the Sassanians now operated at Baghdad under the Abbasids.

Al-Mansur arranged the assassination of Abu Muslim in 755 — the Persian general whose Khurasan revolt had founded the Abbasid revolution was eliminated once his usefulness to the new dynasty had ended. The killing consolidated the Abbasid dynastic position over the revolutionary base; the pattern (the founding revolutionary kills the founding revolutionary commander) has been a documented feature of subsequent revolutionary regimes across history.

The Abbasid Golden Age operated across the late 8th and 9th centuries under **al-Mahdi** (775-785), **al-Hadi** (785-786), **Harun al-Rashid** (786-809), and **al-Ma'mun** (813-833). Harun's reign produced the conventional emblem of the period — diplomatic exchanges with Charlemagne, the patronage of the translation movement, the founding of the **House of Wisdom** at Baghdad as the central translation and research apparatus that would operate from the late 8th century to the 1258 Mongol destruction.

The Sunni jurisprudential consolidation occurred across the same period. The four canonical Sunni schools of jurisprudence — the *madhhabs* — emerged under direct or indirect Abbasid caliphal patronage:

**Abu Hanifa** (around 699-767, executed under al-Mansur for refusing a judicial appointment) was the founder of the **Hanafi** school. His jurisprudence emphasised juristic reasoning and analogical method. Hanafi law would become the dominant position of the later Ottoman Empire (§ III) and continues as the dominant school across the contemporary Turkish, South Asian, and Central Asian Sunni Muslim populations.

**Malik ibn Anas** (around 711-795) was the founder of the **Maliki** school. He operated at Medina under Abbasid caliphal patronage. His *Muwatta'* — compiled around 765 — is the founding text. Maliki jurisprudence emphasised the inherited Medinan practice as the carrier of the prophet's *sunna*. The school is dominant across the contemporary North African and West African Sunni Muslim populations.

**Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i** (767-820) was the founder of the **Shafi'i** school and the principal architect of classical Sunni jurisprudential method. Al-Shafi'i's *Risala* established the position that Sunni law derived from four sources in a fixed hierarchy: the Quran; the prophetic *sunna* (recorded in *hadith*); juristic consensus (*ijma*); and analogical reasoning (*qiyas*). The Shafi'i framework was the foundational method of classical Sunni jurisprudence and is dominant across the contemporary Egyptian, Levantine, East African, and Southeast Asian Sunni populations.

**Ahmad ibn Hanbal** (780-855) was the founder of the **Hanbali** school — the most theologically conservative of the four. Hanbali jurisprudence emphasised the direct authority of *hadith* over juristic reasoning. The school is dominant across the contemporary Arabian Peninsula Sunni population — and is the foundation of the contemporary Saudi-Wahhabi state-religious settlement that § IV will dossier.

The six canonical *hadith* compendia (*Kutub al-Sittah*, the Six Books) consolidated across the next half-century under continuing caliphal patronage: **al-Bukhari** (810-870) and his *Sahih al-Bukhari*; **Muslim ibn al-Hajjaj** (around 815-875) and his *Sahih Muslim*; **Abu Dawud** (817-889); **al-Tirmidhi** (824-892); **al-Nasa'i** (around 830-915); **Ibn Majah** (around 824-887). The consolidation of Sunni orthodoxy through Abbasid caliphal patronage is the documentary fact of the 8th-10th-century period — the religious tradition Sunni Muslims venerate in 2026 as the authoritative inheritance of the prophet's teaching took its shape under the direct patronage of the dynasty that had captured the caliphate through revolutionary violence in 750.

The relationship between caliphal political authority and *ulama* religious authority was not stable across the period. The decisive crisis came under al-Ma'mun.

**al-Ma'mun** (Abu Ja'far Abd Allah ibn Harun al-Rashid, 786 – 9 August 833) reigned as seventh Abbasid caliph from 813 to 833. He was the son of Harun al-Rashid and an intellectual caliph who patronised the translation movement at the House of Wisdom. He operated with theological sympathies for the **Mu'tazila** — the school of rationalist Islamic theology that held the Quran was *created* in time by God rather than co-eternal with God. The doctrinal position carried significant implications for the relationship between revealed scripture and rational philosophy. If the Quran was created, then it could be subject to rational interpretation alongside other texts of human transmission. If the Quran was uncreated and co-eternal with God, then it stood above rational interpretation and could only be received in its received textual form.

In **827** al-Ma'mun publicly adopted the Mu'tazila doctrine of the created Quran. Six years later, in **833**, he wrote from Raqqa in northern Mesopotamia to his governor at Baghdad ordering the inquisition. The **Mihna** (Arabic *miḥna*, *ordeal* or *trial*) required judges, religious scholars, and state officials throughout the empire to publicly affirm the Mu'tazila doctrine. Those who refused faced dismissal, imprisonment, flogging, and in some cases execution.

The institutional intent was the establishment of caliphal doctrinal authority over the *ulama*. The Sunni religious scholars had operated as carriers of religious authority independent of the political-military caliphate across the 8th century; the Mihna was the caliphal attempt to subordinate the *ulama* to direct doctrinal control. The case for the contested doctrinal proposition was the pretext; the actual question was who held authority within the Sunni community — the caliph or the scholars.

Al-Ma'mun died at Tarsus on 9 August 833, less than four months after instituting the Mihna. The inquisition continued under his brother **al-Mu'tasim** (reigned 833-842), who pursued the campaign with greater intensity. The most famous confrontation came under al-Mu'tasim. **Ahmad ibn Hanbal** — the Hanbali founder, who had refused to affirm the created-Quran doctrine — was interrogated, scourged until unconscious, and imprisoned. His refusal became the documented emblem of Sunni scholarly resistance to caliphal doctrinal authority.

Al-Mu'tasim's son **al-Wathiq** (reigned 842-847) maintained the Mihna at lower intensity. **al-Mutawakkil** (reigned 847-861), al-Wathiq's brother and successor, **reversed the Mihna in 851**. Al-Mutawakkil was the first Abbasid caliph to embrace the Sunni orthodox traditionalist position. He restored Ahmad ibn Hanbal to honour; suppressed the Mu'tazila; instituted persecution of Shia, Christian, and other non-orthodox populations.

The institutional settlement that followed the Mihna failure is the foundation of subsequent Sunni political theology. The standard contemporary scholarship (Hodgson, Crone and Hinds, Lapidus) treats the Mihna's failure as the moment the caliphal-doctrinal authority project failed and the *ulama* emerged as the carriers of religious authority independent of caliphal political authority. The post-Mihna Sunni settlement: the caliph holds political-military authority; the *ulama* hold religious-doctrinal authority; the balance between them is the framework of subsequent Sunni political theology across the next 1,175 years. The contemporary Saudi-Wahhabi settlement, the Egyptian al-Azhar settlement, the Pakistani state-Islam settlement — all of them are variations of the basic Mihna-failure settlement that emerged in 851.

The post-Mihna theological consolidation continued under **al-Ash'ari** (around 874-936), a former Mu'tazila who reverted to traditionalist positions and articulated the **Ash'ari** theological school as the dominant Sunni theological framework. Ash'ari theology accepted the uncreated Quran and the principle that revealed scripture's anthropomorphic descriptions of God should be accepted *bila kayfa* — *without asking how*. The framework was the dominant Sunni theological position across the subsequent millennium.

The Abbasid central authority declined across the 9th-13th centuries. The provincial losses were continuous: the Tulunid dynasty over Egypt 868-905. The Samanid dynasty over Khurasan and Transoxiana 874-999. The **Fatimid Caliphate** (a Shia Isma'ili dynasty claiming descent from Fatima and Ali) established at Ifriqiya in 909, taking Egypt in 969 and founding Cairo as their capital, operating as the rival Shia caliphal authority through to 1171; the **Umayyad Caliphate of Cordoba** under Abd al-Rahman III from 929 to 1031.

By the mid-10th century the Abbasid caliph at Baghdad had become a religious-ceremonial figurehead. The actual political-military authority passed to a succession of regional dynasties that ruled in the caliph's name. The **Buyid dynasty** (a Shia Twelver dynasty from the Caspian region) took Baghdad in 945 and was the controlling authority over the caliph through 1055. The **Seljuk Turks** under Tughril Beg took Baghdad from the Buyids in 1055 — the Seljuks were Sunni and restored the Abbasid caliph's nominal religious authority while themselves controlling the actual political-military apparatus through 1194.

The Crusades intervened across 1095-1291. The First Crusade took Jerusalem on 15 July 1099. The Kurdish military commander **Saladin** (Salah ad-Din, around 1137-1193) recaptured Jerusalem on 2 October 1187 after the Battle of Hattin three months earlier (4 July 1187). Saladin's Ayyubid dynasty operated 1171-1260 across Egypt and Syria, replacing the Fatimid caliphate.

The Mongol invasion came across the 13th century. Genghis Khan's armies destroyed the Khwarazmian Empire in eastern Persia across 1219-1221. The Mongol-Islamic frontier shifted westward. **Möngke Khan** (the Great Khan from 1251) commissioned his brother **Hulagu Khan** in 1253 to subjugate the remaining Muslim states of Persia, Mesopotamia, and Syria.

Across 1253-1256 Hulagu's campaign destroyed the Nizari Isma'ili strongholds in northern Persia (the *Assassins*); the principal Assassin fortress of Alamut surrendered on 15 December 1256.

In 1257 Hulagu demanded the submission of the Abbasid caliph **al-Musta'sim Billah** (Abu Ahmad Abd Allah, reigning 1242-1258, born around 1213). Al-Musta'sim refused. The belief — that the prestige of the Abbasid caliphate would deter Mongol attack — produced the catastrophe that followed.

At the end of January 1258 Hulagu's army — around 150,000 Mongol troops with Chinese siege engineers, Christian Armenian and Georgian contingents, and a Frankish contingent from the Principality of Antioch — encircled Baghdad. The Abbasid garrison was around 30,000. On 17 January 1258 an Abbasid sortie of around 20,000 forced a Mongol vanguard retreat; the Abbasid army celebrated; that night the Mongol commander Baiju broke the dykes of the Dujayl Canal and flooded the Abbasid camp.

The Mongol siege of Baghdad ran from 29 January to 5 February 1258. The siege engines breached the walls within several days. The eastern wall fell on 4 February. On **10 February 1258** al-Musta'sim surrendered Baghdad to Hulagu.

The Mongol sack ran across **13-20 February 1258**. Mass killings of the population. The contemporary estimates range from 90,000 at the low end to over 800,000 or one million at the high end; the standard scholarly view places the death toll at several hundred thousand. The **House of Wisdom** — the Abbasid translation and research apparatus that had operated since the late 8th century, with the largest book collection in the contemporary world — was destroyed. The collection was ripped apart and thrown into the Tigris. The contemporary accounts describe the river running black with the ink of the destroyed books and red with the blood of the slaughtered.

On **20 February 1258** Hulagu executed al-Musta'sim. The Mongol custom on killing royal blood was the avoidance of direct shedding of blood on the earth. Al-Musta'sim was wrapped in a carpet and trampled to death by Mongol horses. All but one of his sons were killed; the surviving son was sent to Mongolia.

The dynasty al-Saffah had established in 750 ended 508 years later. The caliphate of effective Sunni political-religious authority over a unified Muslim world ended on 20 February 1258.

The Abbasid name continued symbolically. In 1261 the Mamluks of Egypt — the Turkish slave-soldier dynasty that had emerged from the Ayyubid apparatus to rule Egypt from 1250 — installed a refugee Abbasid prince at Cairo as a ceremonial caliph. The **Cairo Abbasid line** operated as a religious-ceremonial figurehead under Mamluk political authority from 1261 to 1517. When the Ottomans conquered Egypt in 1517, the last Cairo Abbasid was deported to Istanbul. The position of caliphal religious authority — separated from effective political-military rule, separated from any unified Muslim political community, reduced to a ceremonial appointment under whichever Mamluk or Ottoman sultan held the actual power — is the condition § III will set out as the Ottoman millet system and the Sultan-Caliph synthesis took shape from 1453 onward.

The co-optation argument of Chapter 5 has now established its first six centuries. The Sunni political-religious apparatus consolidated under Umayyad and Abbasid dynastic patronage. The canonical *madhhabs* and *hadith* compendia emerged under the patronage of caliphs who had taken the office through revolutionary violence (al-Saffah 750) or hereditary succession (the Umayyads 661, the Abbasids after 750). The doctrine of Sunni orthodoxy was settled through the 833-851 Mihna failure and the al-Ash'ari theological synthesis. The caliphate ended in February 1258. The apparatus the caliphate had produced — the *madhhabs*, the *hadith* corpus, the *ulama* class, the political-theology framework — operated under successor regimes from 1258 forward.

§ III sets out what the Ottoman dynasty did with the office from 1453 to 1924.

---


### III. The Ottoman Millet System and the Sultan-Caliph Synthesis (1453–1924)

The 471-year period of the Ottoman dynasty as the principal Sunni political-religious authority of the world. The dynasty's position after the 29 May 1453 capture of Constantinople, its conventional Sunni narrative of the 1517 caliphal-title acquisition from the last Cairo Abbasid (which the contemporary scholarship treats as an 18th-century retroactive construction). The consolidation under Süleyman the Magnificent, the Tanzimat reform period of 1839-1876, the late-Ottoman pan-Islamic policy under Abdülhamid II, the Young Turk Revolution of 1908, the 1914-1918 collapse, and the **3 March 1924** abolition of the caliphate by Mustafa Kemal Atatürk. The period whose closure produced the post-1924 vacuum that § IV will dossier as the void the contemporary Sunni capture apparatuses emerged to fill.

Chapter 4 § I has already dossiered the 29 May 1453 fall of Constantinople from the Eastern Orthodox side — the moment at which 1,123 years of Eastern Roman imperial-religious authority ended and the eastern Christian centre passed from imperial protection to subaltern status under Muslim political authority. For the Sunni capture argument of Chapter 5, the same event has the corresponding significance from the other direction. **Mehmed II** (born 30 March 1432, died 3 May 1481) acquired through the conquest the political-religious position of inheritor to the Roman imperial tradition. The title *Kayser-i Rum* — *Roman Caesar* — was added to his titular collection. The conversion of Hagia Sophia to a mosque immediately after the conquest was the emblem of the relationship that would structure the empire across the next 469 years.

The non-Muslim community arrangements under the early dispensation followed the classical Islamic legal framework consolidated under the Abbasid Sunni *madhhabs* of §  II. Christians and Jews — the *People of the Book* — were *dhimmi*, protected populations under Muslim political authority. The position carried the jizya tax, restrictions on construction of new churches and synagogues, restrictions on dress and on riding horses, and the legal subordination of non-Muslim testimony to Muslim testimony in the courts. The framework was inherited from the Hanafi jurisprudence the dynasty had taken as its official school from the 14th century onward.

Mehmed II appointed Gennadius Scholarius as Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople on **6 January 1454**. The standard 18th- and 19th-century historiography treated this appointment as the founding of the *Rum millet* (Orthodox Christian community) and the founding of the *millet system* itself. The contemporary scholarship — Benjamin Braude's foundational chapter in Braude and Lewis 1982; Bruce Masters's *The Arabs of the Ottoman Empire 1516-1918*; the Wikipedia synthesis of the contemporary historiography — treats the 1454 arrangement as an *ad hoc* settlement rather than the founding of a system. The systematic *millet* organisation of the empire's non-Muslim populations under three or more officially recognised confessional communities was a product of the early 19th century under Sultan Mahmud II (reigned 1808-1839) and the subsequent Tanzimat reforms. The retrospective projection of the 19th-century system back to Mehmed II was a Tanzimat-period historiographical move, not a description of 15th-century arrangements.

What did materially develop across the late 15th and 16th centuries was a working set of arrangements. The Greek Orthodox Ecumenical Patriarch at Constantinople was the head of the Orthodox community across the empire's territories; his authority was extended over the Slavonic patriarchates of Ochrida, Trnovo, and Ipek by the Conqueror's decision (cross-reference Chapter 4). The **Armenian Patriarch of Constantinople** was established in 1461 under Mehmed II. The **Hakham Bashi** (Chief Rabbi) at Constantinople was the head of the Jewish community. Jewish immigration to imperial territory from late-15th-century Europe — the expulsions from Spain (1492), Portugal (1497), Bavaria (1470), and other European territories — produced a sizeable Sephardic Jewish presence at Salonika, Istanbul, Izmir, and other imperial cities across the 16th-17th centuries. The Sephardic communities found in the empire a refuge from the European Christian persecutions that had expelled them.

**Selim I** (born 10 October 1470, died 22 September 1520), reigning 1512-1520, brought the dynasty into possession of the Sunni heartlands. The 1516-1517 conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate ran through the **24 August 1516 Battle of Marj Dabiq** north of Aleppo (where the Mamluk Sultan Qansuh al-Ghuri died of a stroke during the rout); Damascus fell by early October 1516; the **22 January 1517 Battle of Ridaniya** near Cairo defeated the new Mamluk Sultan Tuman bay II; Cairo fell on 26 January 1517; Tuman bay was captured 26 March 1517 and executed on 13 April 1517 at the Cairo gate of Bab Zuwayla. The dynasty now controlled Egypt, Syria, Palestine, and the Hejaz. The Sharif of Mecca presented the keys to the holy cities to Selim and was confirmed as their hereditary ruler under Ottoman suzerainty.

The conventional Sunni narrative of what happened next is on the record but contested. The narrative: the last Cairo Abbasid caliph **al-Mutawakkil III** (who had held the ceremonial position under Mamluk protection since 1508) was transported to Constantinople with the Mamluk notables, where he formally surrendered the caliphal title to Selim I — along with the prophet Muhammad's mantle and sword — in a ceremony at Hagia Sophia. The narrative was the standard account from the 18th century onward and has been reproduced in countless Sunni historiographical and popular treatments to the present day.

The contemporary scholarly position is otherwise. The standard scholarship — Caroline Finkel's *Osman's Dream*, the Wikipedia synthesis, and the consensus of the contemporary Ottomanist historiography — treats the 1517 caliphal-transfer narrative as **an 18th-century retroactive construction**, not a 16th-century event. Selim I did not in 1517 systematically claim the caliphal title; the contemporary 16th-century documents do not describe a formal investiture. Sultans had used caliphal titles among their long list of honorifics from the 14th century onward without claiming universal religious authority outside their territorial borders. The transformation of the dynasty's position into a claim to universal Sunni caliphal authority developed gradually across the 17th and 18th centuries and was consolidated only under the pan-Islamic policy of Sultan Abdülhamid II in the late 19th century.

What did materially happen in 1517: the relics of the prophet Muhammad held at Mamluk Cairo — the prophet's mantle (the *Hırka-ı Şerif*), his sword, his bow, and other items — were transported to Constantinople and installed at the Topkapı Palace. They remain there in the 2026 collection of the contemporary Topkapı Palace Museum. The control of Mecca, Medina, and Jerusalem passed to the sultan. The annual Hajj pilgrimage caravan was organised and protected under sultanic authority from 1517 onward; the keys to the Kaaba were held by the Sharif of Mecca under sultanic suzerainty. The sultan acquired the title *Khadim al-Haramayn al-Sharifayn* — *Servant of the Two Holy Sanctuaries* — the title that has been claimed by the Saudi kings since the 1986 adoption by Fahd bin Abd al-Aziz (anticipating Chapter 18 of this volume).

The institutional consolidation of the dynasty's Sunni position peaked under Selim's son.

**Süleyman I** (Süleyman the Magnificent, born 6 November 1494, died 7 September 1566 at the siege of Szigetvár), reigning 1520-1566, was the tenth sultan and the conventional emblem of the dynasty's peak. His territorial achievements — Belgrade 1521, Rhodes 1522, the defeat of the Hungarian Kingdom at Mohács 1526, the unsuccessful siege of Vienna in 1529, the conquest of Baghdad 1534-1535 from the Safavids — extended the empire from Algiers to the Caucasus and from the Yemen to the gates of Vienna. By Süleyman's death the empire was the world's most extensive Muslim polity and one of the world's three largest empires alongside Ming China and the Spanish Habsburg territories.

The institutional Sunni-state consolidation under Süleyman ran through the office of the **Sheikh ul-Islam** (*Şeyhülislam*), the chief Islamic legal-religious officer of the state. The architect was **Ebussuud Efendi** (1490-1574), Sheikh ul-Islam from 1545 to his death in 1574. Ebussuud's achievement was the harmonisation of sultanic decree-law (*kanun*) with classical Hanafi Sunni jurisprudence (*sharia*) into a dual legal architecture. The Hanafi *sharia* operated for matters classical Islamic jurisprudence had codified (family law, inheritance, religious obligations); the *kanun* of the sultan operated for matters of state administration, taxation, criminal law, and military discipline that classical jurisprudence had left to the political authority. The dual *sharia-and-kanun* architecture was the working model of state-Islam relations under the dynasty and has continued as the conventional template for subsequent Sunni political authorities.

The *kanun-name* of Süleyman codified the state administrative-criminal law. The epithet Süleyman acquired in Turkish — *Kanuni*, *the Lawgiver* — refers to this codification. The Hanafi *madhhab* was confirmed as the official school of the state; the position carried forward as the continuing dominance of Hanafi jurisprudence across the Turkish, Balkan, and former-Ottoman territories.

The **devşirme** system was the structural recruitment apparatus for the imperial elite across the 14th to 17th centuries. The system was the periodic forced levy of Christian boys from the Balkan provinces, converted to Islam and trained for state and military service — the Janissaries (the elite infantry) and the senior bureaucracy of the Sublime Porte. The contradiction was visible. Classical jurisprudence prohibited forced conversion of *dhimmi* populations; the *devşirme* operated as a forced conversion programme on the Christian Balkan populations on a systematic scale across three centuries. Bernard Lewis's *Race and Slavery in the Middle East* — the classical scholarly study — treats the *devşirme* as a uniquely state innovation that operated against classical Islamic legal norms. The position was justified by the empire's jurists as a sultanic prerogative under *kanun* rather than a *sharia* category, which is to say that the dual legal architecture Ebussuud Efendi had codified produced a working method for legitimating practices the classical Sunni *sharia* would have prohibited.

The dynasty's position began its slow decline from the late 16th century. The 1571 Battle of Lepanto produced the imperial naval defeat by the Holy League of Christian powers. The 1683 second Siege of Vienna ended in the imperial defeat that began the long retreat from central Europe. The 1699 Treaty of Karlowitz was the first major territorial concession to Christian powers. The 18th century continued the territorial losses to Habsburg Austria and Tsarist Russia. The 1768-1774 Russo-Turkish War produced the 1774 Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca, which conceded Russia a protectorate over the empire's Orthodox Christian populations (cross-reference Chapter 4 § II). The early 19th century saw the Greek War of Independence (1821-1832) and the rise of Muhammad Ali in Egypt as the de facto independent ruler of an Ottoman province from 1805 to 1848.

The institutional crisis produced the Tanzimat.

**3 November 1839**: under Sultan Abdülmecid I (reigned 1839-1861), the **Hatt-i Şerif of Gülhane** — the *Noble Edict of the Rose Chamber* — inaugurated the **Tanzimat** (*Reorganisation*) reform period. The edict declared security of life, honour, and property for all subjects of the empire regardless of religion; equal taxation regardless of religion; the abolition of the traditional *iltizam* tax-farming land-tenure regime; regular conscription rather than arbitrary military levies. The intent on the record was the modernisation and centralisation of the state apparatus under European secular models — the argument that the dynasty would only survive the 19th-century pressure from European powers by adopting European administrative and legal architectures.

The Tanzimat reform programme across 1839-1876 ran through a series of codifications:
- The 1840 Penal Code (modelled on French law).
- The 1850 Commercial Code (modelled on French commercial law).
- The 1854 Code of Maritime Laws.
- **18 February 1856 Hatt-i Hümayun** (*Imperial Edict*) — issued at the end of the Crimean War (1853-1856) under direct European pressure. Reinforced the 1839 reforms with explicit protections for the Christian populations and the formal codification of equality regardless of religion or ethnicity.
- The 1858 Land Code.
- The 1864 Vilayet Law (provincial administration with elected councils).
- The 1869 Ottoman citizenship law.
- The 1869-1876 Mecelle (the Ottoman civil code, the Hanafi codification of Islamic civil law as state law).
- **The 1876 Ottoman Constitution** (*Kanun-ı Esasi*) under the new Sultan Abdülhamid II — the first written constitution of a Muslim-majority state.

The consequences of the Tanzimat were two and contradictory. The first: the systematic codification of the *millet* system under Sultan Mahmud II (1808-1839) and the subsequent Tanzimat formalised the previously *ad hoc* arrangements for the non-Muslim populations as three official *millets* — Greek Orthodox, Armenian Apostolic, and Jewish — with additional millets recognised across the 19th century for Catholic, Protestant, Bulgarian Orthodox, and other communities. The *millet* system has been remembered in much of the subsequent historiography as the Ottoman generosity to non-Muslim populations; the evidence shows it as a 19th-century codification under European pressure, not as a 15th-century original.

The second institutional consequence: the simultaneous secularisation of the state apparatus. Religious law was confined to family law (marriage, divorce, inheritance); criminal, commercial, and administrative law was reorganised on European secular models. The dual *sharia*-state-law architecture of the classical system was disrupted. The tension between the traditional Sunni *ulama*-state settlement (the post-Mihna framework dossiered in § II) and the modernising bureaucratic class produced the political fissures that would dominate imperial politics across the next half-century and produce the 1908 Young Turk Revolution.

**Abdülhamid II** (born 21 September 1842, died 10 February 1918), reigning 31 August 1876 – 27 April 1909, ran the empire as autocratic sultan for thirty years after suspending the 1876 Constitution in 1878. His response to the empire's territorial losses, internal nationalist movements, and European pressure was the pan-Islamic policy.

The pan-Islamic policy operated as the reactivation and systematic deployment of the caliphal title that previous sultans had held quietly among their honorifics since the 14th century. Under Abdülhamid II the title was positioned as the unifying authority of global Sunni Islam — the claim of the sultan as the supreme religious-political leader of the world's Sunni Muslim populations, including those under British, French, Dutch, and Russian colonial rule. The apparatus of pan-Islam under Abdülhamid II included the Hejaz Railway (constructed 1900-1908, a strategic line from Damascus to Medina financed in part by donations from Muslims worldwide as pan-Islamic Hajj-pilgrimage infrastructure). Diplomatic outreach to Muslim populations under European colonial rule; state patronage of conservative Sunni religious institutions; and the alliance with traditionalist *ulema* against the modernising Young Ottomans.

Abdülhamid's repression of internal opposition operated through the Hamidian secret police (*Yıldız İstihbarat Teşkilatı*) and through episodes of mass violence against the empire's Christian populations. The **1894-1896 Hamidian Massacres** killed around 100,000-300,000 Armenian Christians in eastern Anatolia — the precedent for the larger Armenian Genocide of 1915 under the Young Turk regime.

**3 July 1908 Young Turk Revolution**: military officers of the **Committee of Union and Progress** (CUP, *İttihat ve Terakki Cemiyeti*) compelled Abdülhamid II to restore the 1876 Constitution. The Second Constitutional Era began. **27 April 1909**: Abdülhamid II was deposed after the failed counter-revolution of 13 April 1909. His brother Mehmed V (reigned 1909-1918) was installed as a constitutional figurehead under CUP rule.

The CUP regime (1908-1918) was Turkist-nationalist rather than pan-Islamist. The Young Turks pursued centralisation of the state, modernisation, and Turkification of the empire's diverse populations. The shift from Abdülhamid's pan-Islam to the CUP's Turkish nationalism was decisive for the subsequent collapse of the empire — it was not the rejection of the caliphate as such (the CUP regime continued to operate the office) but the subordination of the caliphal claim to a Turkish nationalist political programme.

**November 1914**: the empire entered the First World War on the side of the Central Powers (Germany and Austria-Hungary). **14 November 1914**: Sultan Mehmed V declared a *jihad* against the Entente powers (Britain, France, Russia) — the last formal caliphal *jihad* declaration. The declaration was significant in colonial Britain, which ruled around 100 million Muslim subjects in India and which had taken the caliphal authority seriously enough to anticipate revolt in its Muslim territories. The military effect of the *jihad* declaration was limited.

The **1915 Armenian Genocide** ran under CUP authority across 1915-1923, with the principal phase from **24 April 1915** (Armenian Genocide Remembrance Day). Around 1 to 1.5 million Armenian Christians were killed and deported. The Assyrian and Greek Orthodox populations of Anatolia were subject to mass killings in the same period.

The **Hashemite Arab Revolt** (June 1916 – October 1918) was the revolt by the Hashemite Sharif Hussein bin Ali of Mecca against Ottoman rule, conducted with British financial and military support — T.E. Lawrence's role in the British liaison apparatus. The revolt produced the Hashemite control of the Hejaz, Transjordan, and Iraq under the postwar British and French mandate arrangements.

**May 1916 Sykes-Picot Agreement**: the secret British-French agreement for the postwar partition of the empire's Arab territories. The agreement was revealed to the world by the Bolshevik regime in November 1917 from the Tsarist diplomatic archives. **2 November 1917 Balfour Declaration**: the British government's statement of support for *a national home for the Jewish people* in Palestine — anticipating Chapter 20 of this volume on the post-1948 Israeli capture apparatus.

**30 October 1918 Armistice of Mudros**: imperial military surrender to the Entente. Allied occupation of Constantinople followed. The 1920 Treaty of Sèvres imposed a partition that would have reduced the state to a small Anatolian rump.

The Turkish nationalist resistance produced the abolition.

**Mustafa Kemal Pasha** (later Atatürk, *Father of the Turks*, born 19 May 1881 at Salonika, died 10 November 1938 at Istanbul) was the Gallipoli commander of 1915 who had defeated the British landing at Çanakkale. From May 1919 he organised the Turkish nationalist resistance to the Allied partition. The **Turkish War of Independence** (1919-1923) defeated the Greek invasion of western Anatolia, the Armenian Republic in the east, and forced renegotiation of the Treaty of Sèvres.

**23 April 1920**: the **Grand National Assembly of Turkey** convened at Ankara as the alternative government to the administration under Allied occupation in Constantinople. **1 November 1922**: the Grand National Assembly abolished the Ottoman sultanate. Sultan Mehmed VI (reigned 1918-1922) was deposed; he was taken from Constantinople aboard a British warship into exile.

**19 November 1922**: Crown Prince **Abdülmecid II** (born 29 May 1868, died 23 August 1944) was elected by the Grand National Assembly as caliph — separated from the sultanate, the caliphate continuing as a religious office without political authority. Abdülmecid was the 116th caliph by the traditional Sunni count from Abu Bakr's 632 election at the Saqifa (§ I).

**24 July 1923 Treaty of Lausanne**: the peace settlement replacing the Treaty of Sèvres and establishing the international borders of the modern Turkish Republic. **29 October 1923**: the **Republic of Turkey** was proclaimed by the Grand National Assembly. Ankara was the capital. Mustafa Kemal was elected first President.

The **Khilafat Movement** in British India ran from 1919 to 1924 as the pan-Islamic political movement led by Mohammad Ali Jauhar and Maulana Shaukat Ali to defend the Ottoman caliphate against Allied dismemberment. **24 November 1923**: Syed Ameer Ali and Aga Khan III sent a letter to Prime Minister İsmet İnönü on behalf of the Khilafat Movement urging the preservation of the caliphate. The Turkish nationalist regime treated the Indian intervention as foreign interference and used it as the pretext for the abolition.

**3 March 1924** (28 Rajab 1342 AH): the Grand National Assembly passed **Law 431** abolishing the caliphate. Urfa Deputy Sheikh Saffet Efendi and 53 co-sponsors submitted the motion during the budget negotiations. The text of Law 431 read: *"The office of the Caliphate is abolished, since it is incorporated in the meaning and significance of the Government and the Republic."* The same law expelled all members of the dynasty from Turkey and confiscated their properties.

Abdülmecid II was given only hours to pack. The next dawn — 4 March 1924 — he was placed on the Orient Express out of Istanbul, escorted by police, into permanent exile in Switzerland (then in Nice, France until his death in 1944). More than 230 members of the house followed Abdülmecid into exile.

Atatürk's justification for the abolition, on the documentary record from his letter to Abdülmecid in response to a petition for an increase in allowance: *"Your office, the Caliphate, is nothing more than a historic relic. It has no justification for existence."*

The same day as the abolition, the **Unification of Education Law** (*Tevhid-i Tedrisat Kanunu*) closed the traditional *madrasas* and centralised all education under the Ministry of National Education. The *sharia* courts were dissolved. The Ministry of Sharia was replaced by the **Diyanet** (Directorate of Religious Affairs) — the model of state-administered, state-subordinated official Islam that has operated in the Turkish Republic across the subsequent century.

The institutional aftermath of the 3 March 1924 abolition.

**4-7 March 1924**: King Hussein bin Ali al-Hashimi of the Hejaz — the leader of the 1916 Arab Revolt — proclaimed himself the new caliph. The Sharifian Caliphate proclamation failed to gain wide recognition. In October 1924 Ibn Saud's Wahhabi forces conquered the Hejaz from Hussein; Hussein went into exile in Cyprus, then to Amman where he died in 1931.

**May 1926 Cairo Caliphate Conference**: convened at al-Azhar University by Rector Muhammad al-Jizawi to address the vacuum. The conference produced no consensus candidate.

**December 1931 Jerusalem Caliphate Conference**: convened under Hajj Amin al-Husseini, the Grand Mufti of Jerusalem (anticipating Chapter 20 of this volume). Produced no consensus candidate.

The institutional caliphate has remained dormant from 1924 to the publication of this volume in 2026. The 102 years without a caliph is the longest gap in the office's history since the 8 June 632 election at the Saqifa Bani Sa'ida that founded the position (§ I).

The post-1924 vacuum produced the contemporary Sunni political-religious movements that § IV will dossier:

**1928**: Hassan al-Banna founded the **Muslim Brotherhood** at Ismailia, Egypt, partly in response to the caliphate abolition.

**1932**: Abd al-Aziz ibn Saud founded the **Kingdom of Saudi Arabia** as a unified Wahhabi state on the territories that had been the empire's Hejaz and Najd. The state-religious settlement Saud established with the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab is the contemporary Saudi-Wahhabi state-religious apparatus that § IV and Chapter 18 will set out in detail.

The institutional positions of subsequent Sunni political-religious movements all operate in the post-1924 vacuum where no widely recognised Sunni caliphal authority exists. The Pakistani Jamaat-e-Islami under Mawdudi (founded 1941). The Egyptian Sayyid Qutb wing of the Brotherhood (1950s). The Saudi-Wahhabi state-religious settlement under Abd al-Aziz and his successors. The Iranian Islamic Republic (1979, dossiered in Chapter 19); the Afghan Mujahideen and the Pakistani Taliban (Taliban founded 1994); al-Qaeda (founded 1988); the Islamic State and its 2014 declaration of a *caliphate*; the Erdoğan AKP regime in Turkey (governing 2002-2026) and its reactivation of selected Ottoman-caliphate symbolism. All operate in the space the Atatürk abolition opened on 3 March 1924.

The 471 years of the dynasty's position as the principal Sunni political-religious authority of the world had operated as a working synthesis of dynastic political-military rule, classical Hanafi Sunni jurisprudence under the Sheikh ul-Islam, sultanic *kanun* legal codification, and the ad hoc and then codified *millet* arrangements for the empire's non-Muslim populations. The consolidation under Süleyman the Magnificent in the 16th century, the Tanzimat modernisation under European pressure in the 19th, and the pan-Islamic activation under Abdülhamid II at the close of the same century were all operating modifications of the same fundamental architecture — the Sunni political-religious authority of a single dynasty over a multireligious empire.

The 1924 abolition ended that architecture. § IV sets out what the contemporary apparatuses built in the space the abolition opened.

---


### IV. The Contemporary Sunni Capture Apparatuses (1924–2026)

The 102 years since the 3 March 1924 Atatürk abolition. The post-1924 vacuum has not been closed. No single contemporary Sunni political-religious authority commands the recognition the Ottoman caliphate held before the abolition. What has emerged in the vacuum is a set of parallel state-religious settlements operating across the world's principal Sunni-majority states, alongside a transnational network of non-state religious-political movements and a series of armed jihadist organisations operating in the gaps between and against the state apparatuses.

The co-optation argument of Chapter 5 culminates here. The pattern Chapters 2-4 dossiered for the Christian institutional traditions — political authority producing legitimacy through claimed religious authority, religious obedience produced through state coercion, the religious tradition serving as the apparatus by which political power perpetuates itself — operates in the contemporary Sunni Muslim case under different historical conditions but with the same fundamental capture structure. The Saudi-Wahhabi state, the Egyptian al-Azhar settlement, the Pakistani state-Islam apparatus, the Turkish AKP-Diyanet operation, the Brotherhood transnational network, and the jihadist movements — each is a capture apparatus. Each fits the same institutional template Volume Three has dossiered across the Christian and the previous Sunni cases.

The Saudi-Wahhabi settlement is the foundation.

The story begins in 1744 in the small Najdi town of **Diriyah** near present-day Riyadh. **Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab** (1703-1792) was a Hanbali jurist trained in the 14th-century theological tradition of Ibn Taymiyya, who had spent the previous fifteen years preaching a *muwahhid* (*unitarian*) reform programme — the call to reject the popular Sunni veneration of saints, tombs, and shrines as innovations (*bid'a*) outside the prophetic *sunna*. He had been expelled from successive towns whose Shia or Sufi-influenced populations rejected his programme. **Muhammad ibn Saud** (around 1687-1765), the emir of Diriyah who had ruled the town since 1727, gave him refuge.

The **1744 Diriyah Pact** between the two men was a mutual oath of loyalty (*bay'ah*). The terms: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab would be the religious authority of the new community; Ibn Saud would be the political-military leader. The agreement established the Al Saud / Al ash-Sheikh power-sharing arrangement — the royal family as political authority, the descendants of Ibn Abd al-Wahhab as religious authority, the two families bound by intermarriage across the subsequent ten generations. The arrangement has remained in place across the 282 years from the Pact to the publication of this volume.

Three Saudi states have operated on the foundation of the Pact. The **First Saudi State** (Emirate of Diriyah, 1727-1818) expanded across the Arabian Peninsula and in 1802-1803 conducted the Wahhabi sack of Karbala — the destruction of the shrine of Husayn ibn Ali (cross-reference § II) and the massacre of the Shia population. The Ottoman response came through Muhammad Ali of Egypt; the **1811-1818 Ottoman-Saudi War** ended with Ibrahim Pasha's Egyptian forces destroying Diriyah, executing the Saudi emir, and exiling the leadership. The **Second Saudi State** (Emirate of Nejd, 1824-1891) operated under Faysal I before falling to the Rashidi dynasty in 1891.

The **Third Saudi State** was founded by **Abd al-Aziz ibn Saud** (1875-1953, conventionally known as Ibn Saud) on his return from exile in Kuwait in 1902. The retaking of Riyadh in 1902 began three decades of conquests across the Arabian Peninsula. Abd al-Aziz married the daughter of Abd Allah bin Abd al-Latif, the senior Wahhabi *alim* — restoring the family-level connection that had been disrupted by the 1891 collapse.

The 1925-1926 conquest of the Hejaz removed the Hashemite Sharif Hussein from the holy cities. Hussein had been the post-1924 Sharifian Caliphate claimant (cross-reference § III); his removal by Ibn Saud meant the Hejaz, Mecca, and Medina passed to the Saudi-Wahhabi position. On **23 September 1932** Ibn Saud proclaimed the **Kingdom of Saudi Arabia**. The state-religious settlement Abd al-Aziz established with the Al ash-Sheikh has was the foundation of the contemporary Saudi state across the subsequent 94 years.

The transformation came through oil. In 1933 the Standard Oil Company of California received the concession that became the foundation of the Saudi oil industry. In 1938 oil was discovered at Dammam. In 1944 the Arabian American Oil Company (Aramco) was founded. The **14 February 1945 Quincy Pact** — the meeting between Abd al-Aziz and President Franklin D. Roosevelt aboard the USS *Quincy* in the Suez Canal — produced the founding institutional moment of the modern US-Saudi alliance.

The 1973 oil embargo following the Yom Kippur War produced the capital base for what came next. Saudi-led OPEC's quadrupling of the oil price quadrupled the Saudi government revenue base across the next decade. The capacity for the global Wahhabi-Salafi export programme of the 1980s-2000s was a function of the post-1973 oil-wealth surplus.

The decisive year was 1979.

In November 1979 a band of around 200 Wahhabi-extremist insurgents under **Juhayman al-Otaybi** seized the **Grand Mosque at Mecca** — the holiest site in the Sunni Muslim world, the Kaaba complex at the centre of the Hajj pilgrimage. Juhayman's brother-in-law Mohammed Abdullah al-Qahtani was proclaimed the Mahdi (the eschatological figure of Muslim apocalyptic). The siege ran from 20 November to 4 December 1979. The Saudi regime obtained the Wahhabi clerical authorisation for the use of force inside the sanctuary; French GIGN advisors provided technical support; the siege was ended after around 250 deaths and the execution of captured insurgents in January 1980. The consequence: the Saudi regime conceded greater public influence to the *ulama* in exchange for clerical support against further insurgency. The clerical empowerment under the regime's post-1979 concession would carry forward across the next four decades.

The February 1979 Iranian Revolution (Chapter 19) produced the second institutional shock of the year. The Shia Islamic Republic at Tehran was the regional ideological-political competitor to the Saudi-Wahhabi position. The response was the global Wahhabi-Salafi export programme. The Saudi government and royal-family-affiliated foundations across the 1980s-2010s funded the construction of mosques, madrasas, and Islamic centres across Asia, Africa, Europe, and North America. The 9/11 Commission Report of 2004 documented that 15 of the 19 hijackers of 11 September 2001 were Saudi nationals. The pattern is on the record: the Saudi-Wahhabi export programme produced the ideological-personnel base from which al-Qaeda and the subsequent Sunni jihadist movements recruited.

The contemporary Saudi position under **Mohammed bin Salman** (MBS, born 31 August 1985, Crown Prince from 21 June 2017, Prime Minister from 27 September 2022) has been read in the contemporary scholarship as a shift toward Saudi-nationalist legitimation that de-emphasises the religious-clerical leg of the traditional settlement. The November 2017 Ritz-Carlton "anti-corruption" purge that consolidated MBS's power; the 2 October 2018 murder of journalist Jamal Khashoggi at the Saudi consulate in Istanbul; the Vision 2030 reform programme; the 22 February 2022 royal decree designating Founding Day as the 1727 ascension of Muhammad ibn Saud rather than the 1744 Pact with Ibn Abd al-Wahhab. The contemporary scholarship (AGSI; the Wikipedia synthesis) treats the shift as a "post-Wahhabi era" — the Saudi regime moving toward nationalist rather than religious legitimation, while the Hanbali-Wahhabi clerical establishment remains in place as a subordinated apparatus.

The title **Khadim al-Haramayn al-Sharifayn** — *Servant of the Two Holy Sanctuaries* — was adopted by King Fahd in 1986 (reactivating the title held by the Ottoman sultans since 1517, cross-reference § III). The Saudi monarchy's claim to be the protector of Mecca and Medina is the contemporary functional equivalent of the caliphal claim, while not using the caliphal title outright.

The Egyptian al-Azhar settlement is the second principal apparatus.

**Al-Azhar Mosque** at Cairo was founded in 970-972 under the Shia Isma'ili Fatimid caliphate as a Shia institution. After Saladin's 1171 overthrow of the Fatimid caliphate (§ II), al-Azhar was reorganised as a Sunni institution. From the 13th century onward it has was the most prestigious Sunni jurisprudential-theological centre in the world. The Grand Imam of al-Azhar (*al-Imam al-Akbar*, also *Shaykh al-Azhar*) has been described in much contemporary commentary as the closest equivalent of a unified Sunni religious authority — although the office has never claimed the caliphal position itself.

The contemporary state capture of al-Azhar consolidated under Nasser. **Gamal Abdel Nasser** (President of Egypt 1956-1970) issued **Law 103 of 1961** that nationalised al-Azhar in full. The Law placed the institution under the Ministry of Religious Endowments; transferred all financial resources to state control; replaced the traditional Council of Senior Scholars (*Hay'at Kibar al-'Ulama*) with a state-appointed body; gave the President of Egypt the authority to appoint the Grand Imam directly. The 1961 Law remains the foundation of the Egyptian state-religious settlement.

Anwar Sadat (President 1970-1981) used the captured al-Azhar institution to legitimate the 1979 Egypt-Israel peace treaty. Hosni Mubarak (President 1981-2011) operated al-Azhar as a state apparatus across thirty years. The position of al-Azhar across the period was the standard pattern of a captured religious authority producing legitimating output for the regime that had captured it — the same pattern Chapter 3 § IV dossiered for the American Evangelical institutional position under the Republican Party, the same pattern Chapter 4 § IV dossiered for the Moscow Patriarchate under Putin.

The 2011 Egyptian Revolution opened a brief window. In January 2012 the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces (SCAF) amended Law 103 — restoring some autonomy to al-Azhar, requiring the Grand Imam to be elected by the Council of Senior Scholars through secret ballot, giving the Grand Imam life tenure, removing the presidential power of dismissal. Article 4 of the 2012 Constitution under President Mohamed Morsi formally declared al-Azhar autonomous. **3 July 2013**: Defence Minister Abdel Fattah el-Sisi overthrew Morsi. Grand Imam **Ahmed el-Tayeb** (Grand Imam since 10 March 2010) appeared alongside Sisi at the announcement, providing the Sunni endorsement for the military takeover.

The Sisi-Tayeb tension has been documented in extensive contemporary reporting across 2014-2026. Sisi has sought greater state control over al-Azhar; el-Tayeb has resisted within the constraints of the post-2012 framework. Sisi has shifted institutional emphasis to **Dar al-Ifta** (the office of the Grand Mufti), where the more compliant Grand Mufti Shawki Allam has issued favourable fatwas across the regime's policy programme. The 2026 position of al-Azhar: nominally autonomous; in large measure under state pressure; operating as the most prestigious Sunni voice in the world while reproducing — for all the post-2012 institutional adjustments — the standard pattern of captured religious authority.

The Pakistani state-Islam settlement is the third apparatus.

**Muhammad Ali Jinnah** (1876-1948) had founded Pakistan on 14 August 1947 as a homeland for Muslims with — on his 11 August 1947 speech to the Constituent Assembly — religious freedom for all citizens. The Jinnah vision was Muslim-majority but not theocratic; the new state was a homeland not a sharia state. Jinnah died on 11 September 1948, before the Pakistani secular-Muslim position could be consolidated. The subsequent eleven years (1948-1958) operated under continuing tension between secularising and Islamising elements.

The decisive transformation came under General **Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq** (12 August 1924 – 17 August 1988). On **5 July 1977** Zia led the military coup overthrowing Prime Minister Zulfikar Ali Bhutto. Bhutto was tried and hanged on 4 April 1979. Zia ruled Pakistan from 1977 to his death in an air crash on 17 August 1988.

The Zia Islamisation programme (1977-1988) transformed Pakistan from Jinnah's secular-Muslim-state to a Sharia-based political order:

The **1979 Hudood Ordinances** introduced Quranic *hudud* punishments — whipping, amputation, stoning — for offences of theft, robbery, adultery (*zina*), false accusation of adultery (*qazf*), and consumption of alcohol. The consequence: rape victims unable to produce four male Muslim witnesses (the classical Hanbali evidentiary standard) were prosecuted for adultery; thousands of women were imprisoned across the subsequent decades on *zina* charges.

The **Federal Shariat Court** (1980) was the court for Islamic-law adjudication. The **1980 Zakat and Ushr Ordinance** instituted state collection of the Sunni *zakat* alms tax through banks; the Shia community was eventually exempted after violent protests, producing the institutional Sunni-Shia legal differentiation in Pakistani fiscal law.

The **Blasphemy Law** was constructed through amendments to the Pakistan Penal Code sections 295-A through 295-C across 1980-1986. The penalties: life imprisonment from 1982, death from 1986, for blasphemy against the prophet Muhammad. From 1986 to 2026 the Pakistani blasphemy laws have been used in around 4,000-plus prosecutions, with around 80-100 extrajudicial killings of accused persons (including by mob violence at court premises). The Ahmadiyya community has been particularly targeted; Pakistani Christians and Hindus have also been prosecuted on blasphemy charges. The 1984 Law of Evidence gave women's testimony half the value of men's testimony in *hudud* cases.

The **1985 Eighth Amendment** to the 1973 Constitution: "*the Injunctions of Islam as laid down in the Holy Quran and Sunnah shall be the supreme law and source of guidance for legislation*." The June 1988 Sharia Ordinance declared sharia the supreme law of the country.

The **madrasa expansion** under Zia ran in parallel. Between 1979 and 1988 the number of registered madrasas in Pakistan grew from around 900 to over 8,000 under Zia's patronage and Saudi-Wahhabi external funding. Many of the expanded madrasas operated as recruitment infrastructure for the 1979-1989 Afghan jihad. The Pakistani Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) directorate served as the principal channel for Saudi and CIA funding of the Afghan mujahideen against the Soviet occupation of Afghanistan. The infrastructure produced — the network of madrasas, recruitment camps, training facilities, and ideological networks — was the foundation for the subsequent jihadist movements (Afghan Taliban, al-Qaeda, the Pakistani Taliban).

Zia's architecture has remained in large measure intact under all subsequent Pakistani governments — Benazir Bhutto, Nawaz Sharif, Pervez Musharraf, the PPP / PML / PTI governments to the contemporary 2026 Shehbaz Sharif administration. The 2006 Women's Protection Bill modified some of the most egregious Hudood Ordinance provisions; the broader Islamisation framework remains in place. The blasphemy laws have not been repealed and have been actively used across all subsequent governments. The **Pakistani Taliban** (Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan, TTP), founded 14 December 2007 under Baitullah Mehsud, is the spillover of the post-1979 jihadist infrastructure into anti-Pakistani-state insurgency. The 16 December 2014 Peshawar Army Public School attack killed 149 people, 132 of them schoolchildren.

The Turkish AKP and the partial reactivation of Ottoman-caliphate symbolism is the fourth apparatus.

The post-1924 Turkish Republic operated as a secular state across 1924-2002 with religion subordinated to the **Diyanet İşleri Başkanlığı** — the Directorate of Religious Affairs established 3 March 1924 (cross-reference § III). The **Adalet ve Kalkınma Partisi** (AKP, Justice and Development Party) was founded on 14 August 2001 by **Recep Tayyip Erdoğan** (born 26 February 1954) and Abdullah Gül. The AKP won the November 2002 general election. Erdoğan became Prime Minister 14 March 2003 (after a brief Gül premiership). He has been President of Turkey since 28 August 2014.

The AKP programme across 2002-2026 has transformed the Diyanet from a Kemalist secularising apparatus to a pan-Islamic outreach instrument. The Diyanet's budget has expanded by a factor of around 10 in real terms across the period. The Diyanet has funded mosque construction across former Ottoman territories (the Balkans, the Caucasus, Africa) and across the Turkish diaspora in Western Europe. The AKP has provided regional support for the Muslim Brotherhood network after the 2011 Arab Spring — Egypt, Tunisia, Libya, Syria — and has operated as Hamas's principal regional patron.

The **15-16 July 2016 attempted coup** produced the consolidation. A faction of the Turkish military attempted to overthrow Erdoğan; the coup failed. The Erdoğan regime attributed the coup to the Hizmet movement of Fethullah Gülen (the Turkish Islamic scholar in exile in Pennsylvania). The subsequent crackdown across 2016-2018: around 150,000 government employees dismissed or suspended; around 50,000 arrested; the purge of the judiciary, military, education, media, and civil service.

On **10 July 2020** Erdoğan re-converted **Hagia Sophia** to a mosque — reversing the 24 November 1934 Atatürk decree that had converted the building from a mosque to a museum. The symbolism — the dynasty's 1453 conversion of the building from cathedral to mosque (cross-reference Chapter 4 § I and § III) reactivated 567 years later — was the unmistakable signal of the AKP position. On 24 August 2020 Erdoğan re-converted the Chora Church (Kariye Camii) similarly. The contemporary Turkey under the AKP has positioned itself as an alternative regional Sunni leadership — without claiming the caliphal title, but with selective reactivation of Ottoman-imperial symbolism, the Diyanet's outreach to the global Sunni Muslim diaspora, and political support for the Brotherhood network and for Hamas.

The Muslim Brotherhood transnational network is the fifth apparatus.

**Hassan al-Banna** (14 October 1906 – 12 February 1949) founded the **Society of Muslim Brothers** (*al-Ikhwan al-Muslimun*) at Ismailia, Egypt in **March 1928**. The founding was partly in response to the 1924 Atatürk abolition — al-Banna's analysis named the abolition as the founding catastrophe and the recovery of Islamic political order as the programme. Al-Banna was assassinated by Egyptian security services on 12 February 1949.

**Sayyid Qutb** (9 October 1906 – 29 August 1966) was the principal ideological theorist of the militant wing of the Brotherhood. His 1964 book *Ma'alim fi-l-Tariq* (*Milestones*) is the foundational text of the contemporary Sunni jihadist movement. Qutb was executed by the Nasser regime on 29 August 1966 — the martyrdom that produced the subsequent Qutbist movements. The repression cycles of the Brotherhood ran 1948-1949 (Banna's killing), 1954 (after the failed Brotherhood assassination attempt on Nasser), 1966 (Qutb's execution), and 2013 (Sisi's overthrow of Morsi and the subsequent designation of the Brotherhood as a terrorist organisation by Egypt and several Gulf states).

**Hamas** (Harakat al-Muqawama al-Islamiyya, *Islamic Resistance Movement*) was founded on 10 December 1987 at the start of the First Intifada as the Palestinian wing of the Brotherhood. Hamas won the Palestinian legislative election of 25 January 2006; took control of Gaza in June 2007 from Fatah. The October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the subsequent Israeli military campaign in Gaza are anticipated in Chapter 20 of this volume.

The transnational Brotherhood network across 2026 includes the Tunisian Ennahda Party (founded 1981 under Rached Ghannouchi), the Syrian Brotherhood (founded 1945, suppressed in the 1982 Hama Massacre under Hafez al-Assad), the Jordanian Islamic Action Front (founded 1992), and similar formations across the region. The Qatari government (under the Al Thani dynasty) and the Turkish AKP have was the principal patrons of the network since around 2010-2011.

The jihadist movements operate in the space between and against the state apparatuses.

**Osama bin Laden** (10 March 1957 – 2 May 2011) was a Saudi national who participated in the 1979-1989 Afghan mujahideen mobilisation and founded **al-Qaeda** at Peshawar around August 1988 alongside the Egyptian Brotherhood militant Ayman al-Zawahiri. The 1996 declaration of jihad against the United States; the 1998 East African embassy bombings; the **11 September 2001 attacks** (around 2,977 killed across the World Trade Center, the Pentagon, and United Airlines Flight 93). Bin Laden was killed by US Navy SEALs at Abbottabad, Pakistan on 2 May 2011.

The **Islamic State** territorial caliphate was the most direct contemporary attempt at reactivation of the dormant office. **Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi** (Ibrahim Awad Ibrahim al-Badri al-Samarrai, 1 July 1971 – 27 October 2019) led the Islamic State of Iraq (from 2010), Islamic State of Iraq and Syria (ISIS, from April 2013), and the Islamic State caliphate (declared **29 June 2014** at the Great Mosque of al-Nuri at Mosul; al-Baghdadi proclaimed himself caliph). ISIS held a territory of around 90,000 square kilometres across Syria and Iraq at the 2014-2015 peak. Raqqa (the de facto capital) fell to the US-backed Syrian Democratic Forces on 17 October 2017. Mosul was retaken by Iraqi forces on 9 July 2017; ISIS had destroyed the Great Mosque of al-Nuri on 21 June 2017 to deny the recovered symbolism to the regime. The territorial caliphate ended with the fall of Baghouz, Syria, on 23 March 2019. Al-Baghdadi was killed by US Special Forces at Barisha, Idlib province, Syria on 27 October 2019.

The **Afghan Taliban** was founded in 1994 by Mullah Mohammed Omar at Kandahar. The Taliban regime governed Afghanistan from 27 September 1996 to 13 November 2001 (overthrown by the US-led intervention after 9/11). The Taliban returned to power on 15 August 2021 after the US withdrawal under the February 2020 Doha Agreement. The Taliban regime under Hibatullah Akhundzada (Supreme Leader since 25 May 2016) and Mullah Hassan Akhund (acting Prime Minister) operates the contemporary Afghan state as a Sunni-Deobandi political-religious settlement. The closest contemporary regime to a comprehensive sharia-based state, with women excluded from secondary and university education, public dress strictly regulated, public flogging and execution for moral offences, and the standard machinery of sharia-state coercion.

**Boko Haram** was founded in 2002 at Maiduguri, Nigeria, by Mohammed Yusuf; its militant phase began in 2009 under Abubakar Shekau. The West African Sunni jihadist movement has been responsible for the April 2014 Chibok schoolgirl abductions and around 350,000 deaths across the 2009-2026 Nigerian-Cameroonian conflict. **al-Shabab** was founded in 2006 in Somalia and became an al-Qaeda affiliate in 2012.

The 2026 condition of the global Sunni Muslim population is set out by the evidence.

The post-1924 vacuum has not been closed. The major contemporary state-religious settlements — Saudi Arabia, Egypt-al-Azhar, Pakistan, Turkey-AKP — operate in parallel, with mutually competing claims, mutually overlapping patronage networks (the Saudi-Egyptian-UAE alignment against the Qatar-Turkey-Brotherhood alignment), and mutually contradictory programmatic visions for the Sunni Muslim future. The jihadist movements operate in the gaps between and against the state settlements. The 2014-2019 Islamic State territorial caliphate was the most direct attempt at reactivation of the dormant caliphal office; its failure does not preclude further attempts.

Across all the contemporary settlements, the same pattern operates. Political authority claims religious legitimation; the religious tradition is the means by which political power perpetuates itself; the consequences for the population governed under each settlement are the standard consequences of religious-political capture. The privileged class around the regime is enriched and protected; the unprivileged classes are subordinated, surveilled, and where necessary repressed; the women, the religious minorities, the dissident believers, and the apostates carry the heaviest costs.

The argument of Chapter 5 across its four sections is complete. The Sunni Muslim tradition that consolidated under the Abbasid caliphal apparatus (§ II) and was captured by the Ottoman dynasty (§ III) fragmented in 1924 (closing § III) into the contemporary apparatuses dossiered in § IV. Each of the contemporary apparatuses operates as a capture mechanism producing political legitimacy through claimed religious authority and religious obedience through state coercion. The pattern is the same pattern Chapters 2-4 dossiered for the Christian traditions — the Catholic Church under Constantine and the medieval consolidation; the Protestant state-churches and the American evangelical-political networks; the Eastern Orthodox imperial-Soviet-Putin trajectory. The Islamic case is not the Christian case but the same fundamental structure of capture under different historical conditions.

Chapter 6 takes up the Shia Muslim case. The Shia tradition emerged out of the 656-661 First Fitna (§ I) and consolidated its institutional position around the 680 Karbala martyrdom (§ II). The Shia case has its own distinct history across the subsequent thirteen centuries. The Buyid period 945-1055, the Fatimid caliphate at Cairo 909-1171, the Safavid establishment of Twelver Shi'ism as the Iranian state religion from 1501, the 1979 Iranian Islamic Republic. And its own distinct contemporary capture mechanisms, principally the Islamic Republic of Iran (dossiered in detail in Chapter 19) and the contemporary Lebanese Hezbollah and Iraqi Shia political-military formations.

The co-optation pattern, Volume Three has now dossiered across its first five chapters, is not specific to Christianity or to Islam. It is the governing pattern by which political power across cultures and centuries has captured religious traditions and operated through them. The remaining chapters of Part II will dossier the same pattern across the Shia (Chapter 6), Jewish (Chapter 7), Hindu (Chapter 8), Buddhist (Chapter 9), Sikh (Chapter 10), Indigenous (Chapter 11), Hermetic (Chapter 12), Occult (Chapter 13), and Gender-and-Sexuality (Chapter 14) traditions.
