The founding spectaculars came in 1983. **18 April 1983**: US embassy in Beirut bombing (63 killed). **23 October 1983**: US Marine barracks suicide bombing at Beirut (241 US Marines killed) and the parallel attack on French paratroopers at the same location (58 killed). The attacks were attributed to the proto-Hezbollah Islamic Jihad Organisation; Hezbollah did not claim responsibility under its own name at the time. The consequence: the 1984 withdrawal of US forces from Lebanon. The pattern that has carried through the next four decades was established — asymmetric IRGC-trained operational violence producing political-strategic outcomes against the Western military presence in the broader region.

In **1985** Hezbollah announced its existence with the publication of its Open Letter — the founding manifesto pledging allegiance to Khomeini and the doctrine of *velayat-e faqih* (§ III, the direct theological connection to Iran), calling for the expulsion of Western forces from Lebanon, and committing to the destruction of Israel. The architecture: a Lebanese Twelver political-military organisation operating under the religious authority of the Iranian *Rahbar*.

The consolidation operated across the next decades. In **February 1992** Hezbollah secretary-general Abbas al-Musawi was killed by Israeli helicopter strike. **Hassan Nasrallah** (born 31 August 1960) became secretary-general at age 31 and held the position for 32 years. In **1992** Hezbollah entered Lebanese parliamentary politics, winning seats in the first post-Civil-War election — the transition from pure militia to combined militia-and-political-party. **24 May 2000**: Israel ended its 22-year occupation of southern Lebanon; Hezbollah claimed the credit. **2006 Lebanon War (12 July to 14 August 2006)**: 34-day inconclusive Israel-Hezbollah war following Hezbollah's 12 July cross-border raid that captured two Israeli soldiers. The 2006 war established Hezbollah's reputation as the most capable non-state military force in the world.

The Syrian Civil War (2011-2024) extended the Hezbollah operational role beyond Lebanon. Hezbollah was the principal Tehran-aligned external military intervention force in support of the Assad government across 2012-2018. The commitment produced significant Hezbollah casualties. The Gaza war from October 2023 produced the Hezbollah opening of a northern front against Israel in solidarity with Hamas. The October-November 2023 to late-2024 Israeli-Hezbollah escalation produced the most significant military confrontation between the two sides since 2006.

The Israeli decapitation strike came in late September 2024. **27 September 2024**: Hassan Nasrallah was killed by an Israeli air strike on the Hezbollah underground headquarters at the Dahiyeh suburb of Beirut. The strike was part of the broader Israeli operation that killed most of the senior Hezbollah leadership across September-October 2024. **29 October 2024**: **Naim Qassem** (born 1953), Hezbollah's deputy secretary-general for over 30 years, was appointed secretary-general as Nasrallah's successor.

As of 2024 the Hezbollah claimed strength was around 100,000 fighters including the Radwan Force special operations unit. Its position is significantly weakened relative to the pre-2024 status — the loss of senior leadership across September-October 2024; the loss of the Syrian supply corridor following the December 2024 fall of the Assad government; the November 2024 ceasefire with Israel. The contemporary Hezbollah at the publication of this volume operates under conditions of significant pressure that the pre-2024 apparatus did not face.

The Iraqi Twelver formations and the Popular Mobilisation Forces extend the architecture into Iraq.

The background: the 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq removed the Sunni Ba'athist Saddam Hussein government and produced the dominance of the Iraqi Twelver majority (around 60-65% of the Iraqi population). The post-2003 Iraqi government has been Twelver-led across all subsequent administrations. The Tehran-aligned Iraqi Twelver militias developed across the 2003-2014 period under the IRGC Quds Force coordination. The principal formations: the **Badr Organisation** (the oldest and largest of the militias, founded as the military wing of the Supreme Council for the Islamic Revolution in Iraq in Iran in 1982. Brought into Iraq after 2003 under Hadi al-Amiri); **Asa'ib Ahl al-Haq** (League of the Righteous, founded 2006 by Qais al-Khazali as a Sadrist breakaway under IRGC patronage); **Kata'ib Hezbollah** (founded 2003 under Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis and IRGC Quds Force coordination. With the closest organisational ties to the IRGC); and smaller formations including Kata'ib al-Imam Ali, Kata'ib Sayyid al-Shuhada, and Saraya Khurasani. Standing  apart: the **Peace Companies** (Saraya al-Salam, formerly the Mahdi Army) under Muqtada al-Sadr (born 1973) — the distinct Iraqi-nationalist Twelver formation that has positioned itself in periodic opposition to the Iranian-aligned militias.

The Popular Mobilisation Forces emerged from the 2014 ISIS crisis. **10 June 2014**: ISIS captured Mosul from the Iraqi army, threatening the broader Iraqi state collapse. **13 June 2014**: Grand Ayatollah **Ali al-Sistani** (born 4 August 1930, the Najaf-based senior *marja* of the contemporary Twelver Shia tradition, holding the position from 1992 to the publication of this volume) issued the *jihad kifa'i* (collective-obligation jihad) fatwa calling on all able-bodied Iraqis to join the Iraqi armed forces to fight ISIS. **15 June 2014**: the Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki established the **Popular Mobilisation Forces** (PMF, *al-Hashd al-Sha'abi*) as the umbrella for the volunteer formations responding to Sistani's fatwa.

The reality of the PMF formation diverged from the Sistani fatwa's intent within months. Sistani's fatwa called on Iraqis to join the Iraqi armed forces (the regular army); the Maliki government instead institutionalised the pre-existing pro-Iran militias under the PMF umbrella. Transforming Badr, Asa'ib Ahl al-Haq, Kata'ib Hezbollah, and the smaller formations from non-state pro-Iran militias into state-funded Iraqi armed forces while retaining their pre-existing organisational links to the IRGC Quds Force. The PMF role in the 2014-2017 anti-ISIS campaign was significant; the structural consolidation followed.

**November 2016**: the Iraqi parliament passed the Popular Mobilisation Commission Law, formally institutionalising the PMF as an official armed force under the supervision of the Commander General of the Iraqi Armed Forces (the Prime Minister). The effect: the pro-Iran militias acquired the status of official Iraqi armed forces while continuing to operate under IRGC operational coordination. The hybrid status — Iraqi armed forces by Iraqi law, IRGC operational extensions in practice — is the contemporary architectural position of the PMF.

**3 January 2020**: the US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport killed both **Qassem Soleimani** and **Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis** (Kata'ib Hezbollah leader and PMF deputy chairman) and eight others. The Iraqi parliament voted a non-binding resolution on 5 January 2020 calling for US troop withdrawal.

**March 2020**: the pro-Sistani PMF factions withdrew from the PMF in protest against its capture by the pro-Iran factions. The split confirmed the Iranian Quds Force operational control of the PMF apparatus. From 2020 onward the PMF has operated as a distinctly pro-Iran formation, with the pro-Sistani Iraqi-nationalist forces operating outside the PMF structure.

**December 2023**: the PMF political wing (the Fatah Alliance) won 101 of 285 provincial council seats in the Iraqi provincial elections. The position of the PMF as the dominant non-state armed force inside Iraq has continued into 2026. The Badr Organisation holds the Interior Ministry; the PMF maintains hybrid armed-political control across multiple Iraqi institutions; the PMF operates as an IRGC appendage inside the Iraqi state. The contemporary Iraq has been described in the contemporary scholarship (the Brookings Institution analysis, the AEI analysis) as an Iranian client state at the level of armed-political control.

The Yemeni Houthi movement extends the regional architecture into Yemen as the Zaydi variant.

The background: the Zaydi tradition (Chapter 6 § I, branch separations from the 740 Zayd ibn Ali revolt against the Umayyad caliphate) at northern Yemen had ruled the Yemeni imamate from the 9th century to the 1962 republican revolution. The Zaydi community comprises around 35% of the Yemeni population. The decline of Zaydi influence under the republican period (1962-2014) — and the parallel Saudi-funded Salafi-Wahhabi missionary penetration into Yemen under the post-1979 Saudi regional sponsorship architecture (Chapter 5 § IV) — produced the preconditions for the Houthi revival.

In **1992** the **Believing Youth** (*al-Shabab al-Mu'min*) movement was founded at Saada Governorate in northern Yemen by Hussein al-Houthi (born 1959) and his family — initially a Zaydi revivalist religious-cultural movement operating against the Saudi-funded Salafi-Wahhabi presence. In **2003** under Hussein al-Houthi, the movement adopted the slogan "*God is great, death to America, death to Israel, curse the Jews, victory for Islam*" — the moment that marked the transition from religious-cultural revivalism to political-military opposition movement. In **September 2004** Hussein al-Houthi was killed by Yemeni military forces in fighting at Saada. **Abdul-Malik al-Houthi** (born 22 May 1979, younger brother of Hussein) became the leader from 2004 to the publication of this volume.

The 2004-2010 Saada wars between the Houthis and the Yemeni government of Ali Abdullah Saleh produced six rounds of fighting and Houthi consolidation of control over northern Yemen. The Yemeni Revolution of 2011 (the Arab Spring chapter) produced the resignation of Saleh and the transition to the Abd Rabbuh Mansur Hadi government. The Houthi position consolidated across the next three years under Hadi.

The Yemeni state collapse came in 2014-2015. **September 2014**: Houthi forces stormed the Yemeni capital Sanaa, forcing the resignation of Prime Minister Mohammed Basindawa. **22 January 2015**: President Hadi and the Yemeni cabinet resigned under Houthi pressure. **6 February 2015**: the Houthi Supreme Revolutionary Committee was instated as the interim authority. **26 March 2015**: a Saudi-led coalition of nine Sunni states began the military intervention against the Houthis on behalf of the Hadi government.

The Saudi-led intervention has produced the world's worst humanitarian disaster across the subsequent eleven years (UN designation), with around 27 million Yemenis dependent on humanitarian aid and over a million cases of cholera. The pattern: Saudi-funded Sunni-state intervention against the Tehran-aligned Zaydi movement, operating as the proxy theatre for the broader Saudi-Tehran regional contest.

The Iranian operational connection to the Houthis is well-documented but contested in scale. The US, UK, France, Israel, and Saudi Arabia have repeatedly documented Iranian arms transfers including ballistic missiles and drones; Iran has denied direct operational control. The position the Tehran government takes is that the Houthis are part of the Tehran-led Axis of Resistance alongside Hezbollah, the Iraqi PMF, and the Syrian-Assad-aligned formations.

The October 2023 Gaza war produced the Houthi declaration of solidarity with Hamas and the Red Sea shipping attacks campaign from November 2023 onward. The Houthi attacks on Red Sea shipping have produced major disruption of global shipping. The US-UK Operation Prosperity Guardian (from December 2023) and the Israeli airstrike campaign against Yemen (from 2024) have failed to suppress the Houthi capabilities. **16 May 2025**: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Defence Minister Israel Katz publicly warned Abdul-Malik al-Houthi that if Houthi attacks on Israel continued, he would be targeted directly and "hunted down and eliminated," explicitly citing the Israeli precedent of the Nasrallah and Sinwar killings. The Houthi leadership has continued to operate as of the publication of this volume. The Houthi-claimed fighter strength as of October 2024 is around 100,000.

The Bahraini case is the inversion of the broader pattern.

Bahrain is the governingly unique case in the contemporary Gulf — a small island state with a Twelver-majority population (around 70%) ruled by a Sunni minority royal house (the al-Khalifa dynasty in continuous rule since 1783) and the broader Sunni Arab tribal-political class. The architecture: the Sunni royal house holds the political authority; the Twelver majority is excluded from senior military and security positions and from significant parts of the political apparatus. The pattern is the inverse of the Iraqi case (where the Twelver majority holds the government with pro-Iran militia support) and the inverse of the Iranian case (where the Twelver clerical class holds the state).

**February 2011**: the **Pearl Roundabout protests** at Manama, the Bahraini chapter of the broader Arab Spring uprising. The demands were political — constitutional reform, the end of sectarian discrimination, a transition to constitutional monarchy. The Bahraini Twelver population was the principal participating community. **14 March 2011**: a Saudi-led Gulf Cooperation Council military intervention (around 1,000 Saudi troops, 500 UAE troops) entered Bahrain at the al-Khalifa government's invitation. The intervention crushed the protest movement; the Pearl Monument at the Roundabout was demolished; the suppression continued across the subsequent years.

**2016**: the Bahraini government revoked the citizenship of the senior Bahraini Twelver cleric Sheikh Isa Qassim and dissolved the Wifaq political society (the principal Bahraini Twelver political organisation). The consolidation of the post-2011 Sunni-monarchical control over the Twelver majority.

The Bahraini case is the demonstration that Sunni governance over Twelver majorities can be sustained under Gulf-Sunni patronage. The Saudi-aligned Sunni regional architecture has  invested in preserving the Bahraini case as the counter-example to the Iraqi case — Twelver majority under Twelver government with pro-Iran militias versus Twelver majority under Sunni minority rule with GCC backing.

The Saudi-Iranian sectarian competition is the organising axis of the post-1979 regional architecture.

The founding of the contemporary Saudi-Iranian sectarian competition runs from the dual 1979 events. The **Iranian Islamic Republic** founding in February-December 1979 (§ III) installed a Twelver clerical state at Tehran calling for the export of the Islamic revolution to the broader region. The **Grand Mosque seizure at Mecca on 20 November 1979** (Chapter 5 § IV, the shock that consolidated the Saudi-Wahhabi conservative empowerment) produced the parallel Saudi consolidation under the religious-conservative direction. The two events produced the preconditions for the next 44 years of regional sectarian competition.

The pattern: the post-1979 competition operated as competing institutional sponsorships across the broader region. Saudi Arabia sponsored Sunni-Salafi-Wahhabi institutions across the Muslim world (Chapter 5 § IV); Iran sponsored Twelver institutions across the same regional space. The arena: Lebanon (Hezbollah vs Sunni-Lebanese forces), Iraq (post-2003 PMF vs Sunni resistance), Yemen (Houthi vs Saudi-aligned Hadi government), Syria (Assad-Alawi-Iran vs Sunni opposition), Bahrain (Sunni-Khalifa rule vs Twelver-majority dissent).

The diplomatic ruptures came in two phases. **31 July 1987 Mecca incident**: Saudi security forces killed around 400 Iranian pilgrims (and several hundred others) during clashes at the Hajj. The first diplomatic rupture between Iran and Saudi Arabia followed (1988-1991). **2 January 2016**: Saudi Arabia executed the prominent Twelver cleric **Sheikh Nimr al-Nimr** (born 1959) along with 46 others. The execution triggered the storming of the Saudi embassy at Tehran and the Saudi consulate at Mashhad by Iranian protesters; Saudi Arabia broke off diplomatic relations the following day. The second rupture ran 2016-2023.

The restoration came under Chinese diplomatic mediation. **10 March 2023**: the **Beijing Agreement**. After three days of secret talks at Beijing brokered by Chinese diplomacy (under General Secretary Xi Jinping's coordination), Iran and Saudi Arabia signed the trilateral joint statement restoring diplomatic relations. The agreement was signed by Ali Shamkhani (Secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council) and Musaed al-Aiban (Saudi National Security Adviser). The Tehran embassy at Riyadh reopened on 6 June 2023; the Saudi embassy at Tehran reopened on 9 August 2023. The moment that ended the seven-year diplomatic rupture and the founding moment of the contemporary Gulf realignment under Chinese diplomatic patronage. The contestation: the 2023 settlement marks either a durable de-escalation of the Saudi-Iranian competition or a temporary diplomatic pause; the answer is not yet visible at the publication of this volume in May 2026.

Chapter 6 closes here. Six hundred years from the Hidden Imam doctrine of 941 (§ I) through the medieval Fatimid-Buyid-Nizari settlements of 909-1256 (§ II), the Safavid-Pahlavi-Khomeini trajectory of 1501-1979 (§ III), and the contemporary post-1979 architecture (§ IV).

The tradition across the entire span has operated through the same fundamental pattern the volume has documented across the other religious traditions of Part II. Political authorities have captured the tradition as their legitimating apparatus — the Fatimids in Egypt, the Buyids in Baghdad, the Nizari at Alamut, the Safavid Shahanshahs in Iran, the Iranian Islamic Republic under *velayat-e faqih*. The contemporary Iranian state operates the most institutionally elaborate version of capture pattern — a clerical class holding direct political authority under the Khomeini doctrinal extension of the Hidden Imam tradition. The regional extensions (Hezbollah, the Iraqi PMF, the Houthis) operate as the projection of the Iran-clerical-state architecture across the broader region under IRGC Quds Force coordination.

The contestation is named honestly. The Sistani-quietist position at Najaf, the older traditional Twelver position that the Hidden Imam's absence precludes legitimate political-religious authority during the Major Occultation, remains the position of the majority of the contemporary *maraji al-taqlid*. The Khomeini *velayat-e faqih* position that operates the Iranian state is a minority position within the broader contemporary Twelver tradition. The contemporary religious-political settlement of the Iranian Islamic Republic is specifically an Iran-state position rather than universally Twelver. Believers within the Twelver tradition who hold the Sistani-quietist position are not believers in the Iranian Islamic Republic; the gap is real and has run across the entire post-1979 period.

The Sunni-Twelver competition that the volume has dossiered across Chapters 5 and 6 is the contemporary version of a 14-century dispute that has produced — across every generation of the disputed period — political-religious settlements of the same fundamental kind. The contemporary apparatuses on both sides are continuous with the pre-modern apparatuses dossiered earlier in this volume. The pattern is the pattern the volume has been documenting from Chapter 1 onward: religious authority captured by political authority, political authority legitimated by religious authority, the population producing the obedience the apparatus requires.

Chapter 7 takes up the Jewish tradition. The architecture of contemporary Israeli state Judaism (operational dossier deferred to Chapter 20); the Haredi religious-political settlement (operational dossier deferred to Chapter 21); the broader Jewish diasporic architecture and the contested relationship between Jewish religious identity and the contemporary Israeli state. The capture pattern continues.

---


## Chapter 7. The Jewish Capture

### I. The Emergence of Judaism and the Rabbinic Transition (c. 1200 BCE – 200 CE)

Chapter 7 opens the Jewish section of Part II. The structure parallels Chapters 2-6 of the volume: § I covers emergence and early formation; § II the medieval architecture; § III the modern transition; § IV the contemporary apparatuses (with operational dossiers for the contemporary Israeli state and the Haredi political-religious settlement deferred to Chapters 20 and 21).

A note before the analysis begins. The volume documents the the captured religious-institutional apparatuses across every major tradition. Chapter 7 applies the same analytic framework to the Jewish tradition that Chapters 2-6 applied to the Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, and Shia Muslim traditions. The analysis names apparatuss and named figures who hold institutional positions; the analysis does not generalise from institutional capture to ethno-religious community; the analysis treats Jewish believers, Jewish thinkers, and Jewish communities as not equivalent to captured apparatuss the volume documents. Conspiracy-theoretic patterns — Rothschilds-as-puppet-masters, "Jewish control" tropes, blood libel echoes, Holocaust minimisation — are excluded entirely from the analysis and treated as the historical antisemitic propaganda apparatus they are. The co-optation argument is institutional; the seizure argument is not ethnic. The symmetry across the six traditions of Chapters 2-7 is the operational guardrail.

The roughly 1,400-year period § I covers runs from the formation of the ancient Israelite religious-political settlement through to the codification of the Mishnah around 200 CE. The institutional architecture across this period operates through three major transitions. The First Temple period (c. 1000-586 BCE) under the priestly-Temple-Davidic settlement. The Second Temple period (516 BCE - 70 CE) under the Aaronid priesthood with the developing parallel synagogue and scribal-Pharisaic architecture. The post-70-CE rabbinic transition that produced the foundation continuing to the contemporary period.

The co-optation pattern documented across Chapters 2-6 operates within the Jewish tradition across this period in three identifiable cases. The Hasmonean dynasty (140-63 BCE) combined the high priesthood with the temporal monarchy — a political dynasty captured the religious establishment as its legitimating apparatus, the same pattern dossiered for the Fatimid Caliphate (Chapter 6 § II), the Safavid Empire (Chapter 6 § III), the Catholic Church under the Constantinian settlement (Chapter 2 § I). The Herodian client kingdom (37 BCE - 6 CE) operated as a Roman-installed political authority claiming Jewish religious legitimacy. The post-70-CE rabbinic class consolidated the religious authority outside the destroyed Temple architecture; the rabbinic case is distinct from the captured-by-state apparatuses of the earlier period and operates closer to the medieval Christian and Islamic clerical-scholarly traditions.

The biblical period and First Temple settlement is the starting point.

The biblical-narrative account of the Israelite formation runs from the Patriarchal traditions through the Exodus narrative, the Joshua-Judges settlement period, and the United Monarchy under Saul (c. 1047-1010 BCE per biblical chronology), David (c. 1010-970 BCE), and Solomon (c. 970-931 BCE). The historicity of the United Monarchy account is contested across the contemporary scholarship. The maximalist position (Dever 2003, Halpern 2001) defends the broad historical reality of the biblical narrative — a 10th-century BCE political consolidation centred at Jerusalem under the Davidic line. The minimalist position (Finkelstein and Silberman 2001, Davies 1992) treats the United Monarchy narrative as a 7th-6th century BCE retrojection from the period of the Deuteronomistic History composition, with the actual Israelite political consolidation dating to the 9th-century BCE Omride dynasty of the Northern Kingdom. The contestation is real and unresolved at the publication of this volume.

The architecture the biblical narrative describes — the Davidic monarchy, the Solomonic First Temple (c. 957 BCE construction per biblical chronology), the Aaronid priesthood — is the prototype of the compromised religious-political settlement that Chapters 2-6 have dossiered across other traditions. The Davidic king claimed divine investiture; the Aaronid priesthood operated the Temple cult; the priesthood and the monarchy mutually legitimated each other. The prophetic-critical tradition was the internal critique of the co-opted settlement — Amos (c. 760-750 BCE), Hosea (c. 760-720 BCE), Isaiah of Jerusalem (c. 740-700 BCE), Micah (c. 735-700 BCE), Jeremiah (c. 626-587 BCE), Ezekiel (c. 593-571 BCE). The prophets named the corruption of the priestly-monarchical apparatus in language that has carried forward as the canonical model of religious-political critique. The co-optation pattern is named within the biblical canon itself — the prophetic literature is the record of the indigenous Israelite critique of the co-opted religious-political settlement of their own period. The prophetic critique is its own analytic genealogy, traceable to the 8th century BCE.

The Divided Kingdom (922-586 BCE) split into the Northern Kingdom of Israel and the Southern Kingdom of Judah after Solomon's death. The Northern Kingdom under successive dynasties (the Jeroboam line, the Omride dynasty of Omri-Ahab, the Jehu dynasty) was conquered by the Neo-Assyrian Empire under Shalmaneser V and Sargon II in 722 BCE; the "Ten Lost Tribes" deportation produced the disappearance of the Northern Kingdom from subsequent history. The Southern Kingdom of Judah under the Davidic line continued as an Assyrian vassal until the rise of the Neo-Babylonian Empire.

The Babylonian Exile is the foundational displacement.

597 BCE: Nebuchadnezzar II's first siege of Jerusalem; King Jehoiachin and the first wave of Judean elite were deported to Babylon. **587 / 586 BCE**: the second siege under Nebuchadnezzar II. The standard contemporary chronology dates the destruction either to 587 BCE (Albright; Rodger Young) or to 586 BCE (Thiele), with the precise dating depending on the reconstruction of Babylonian regnal years. The fall came after 18-30 months of siege. The Solomon-era sanctuary was destroyed. King Zedekiah was captured, blinded, and deported to Babylon. The Judean elite was deported to Babylon; many remained in the land under the Babylonian-appointed governor Gedaliah; significant Judean populations also fled to Egypt.

The Babylonian Exile produced the central institutional development that would shape every subsequent period of the Jewish tradition. The destroyed Temple was no longer available as the centre of communal practice. The exiled population developed an architecture of practice that operated without a Temple — synagogue-based prayer; Torah study; Sabbath observance; the dietary laws; circumcision as the boundary marker. The institutional foundation of what would become rabbinic Judaism was laid under exilic conditions, six centuries before the 70 CE Roman destruction made the Temple-less architecture the permanent institutional reality. The exilic period is the founding moment of the displaced-religious-community tradition that has organised the broader Jewish diaspora architecture across the subsequent 2,600 years.

The Persian period and the Second Temple founding came under Achaemenid suzerainty.

539 BCE: Cyrus the Great conquered Babylon at the Battle of Opis. **538 BCE**: the **Edict of Cyrus** (Ezra 1:1-4) permitted the Judean exiles to return to their homeland. 538/537 BCE: the first return under Sheshbazzar (a Davidic-line figure appointed as governor of the new Persian province of **Yehud Medinata**). 520 BCE: the prophets Haggai and Zechariah encouraged the resumption of Temple construction under Zerubbabel (Davidic line) and Joshua the Priest (Aaronid line). **520-516 BCE**: the construction of the Second Temple, completed in 516 BCE (the sixth year of Darius I per Ezra 6:15). The post-exilic settlement under Persian suzerainty produced a new apparatus — the Persian-appointed governor (the political authority); the Aaronid high priesthood operating the Temple; the developing Torah-canonical religious-textual architecture.

The Ezra and Nehemiah missions consolidated the post-exilic settlement. **458 BCE**: the priest-scribe **Ezra** arrived at Jerusalem under Artaxerxes I's authorisation, with the brief to enforce Torah observance in the post-exilic community. **445 BCE**: **Nehemiah** arrived as the Persian-appointed governor of Yehud Medinata; rebuilt the Jerusalem walls; consolidated the Ezra religious settlement. The Ezra-Nehemiah programme produced the pattern that has carried forward — Torah as the foundational text; the priesthood at the Temple; the scribal-interpretive class developing in parallel as the Torah-reading authority; the prohibition of intermarriage with non-Judean populations as the community-boundary maintenance mechanism. The Hebrew Bible canon was in the process of consolidation across the 6th-2nd centuries BCE; the Torah was already canonical by the late Persian period.

The Hellenistic and Hasmonean period produced the first explicit capture moment.

332 BCE: Alexander the Great conquered the region from the Achaemenid Persians. After Alexander's 323 BCE death the region passed to the Ptolemaic dynasty of Egypt (301-200 BCE) and subsequently to the Seleucid Empire (200-167 BCE). The Hellenistic period produced the broader cultural-religious tension between the Hellenising programme of the regional successor states and the Jewish religious tradition that resisted assimilation. The Septuagint Greek translation of the Hebrew Bible was produced at Alexandria across the 3rd-2nd centuries BCE; the Hellenistic Jewish diaspora at Alexandria, Antioch, and Asia Minor developed as a major demographic and cultural reality.

167 BCE: the Seleucid king **Antiochus IV Epiphanes** (reigned 175-164 BCE) attempted to suppress Jewish practice — banning Sabbath observance, Torah study, and circumcision; desecrating the Temple by installing a statue of Zeus and sacrificing pigs on the altar. The suppression produced the **Maccabean Revolt** under Mattathias and his sons (the Hasmonean family). Judas Maccabeus led the military resistance; the December 164 BCE rededication of the Temple is commemorated as Hanukkah.

**140-63 BCE**: the **Hasmonean dynasty** operated as an independent Jewish kingdom. Simon Maccabeus (ruled 142-135 BCE), John Hyrcanus I (134-104 BCE), Aristobulus I (104-103 BCE), Alexander Jannaeus (103-76 BCE), Salome Alexandra (76-67 BCE), Hyrcanus II and Aristobulus II (67-63 BCE). The Hasmonean architecture combined the high priesthood (the cultic authority) and the temporal monarchy in the same family. The capture pattern operates here in its first explicit Jewish version: the Maccabean revolt that began as a religious-defence movement produced a political dynasty that captured the religious establishment for its own legitimation. The high priesthood, which under the Persian and early Hellenistic periods had been held by the legitimately descended Aaronid-Zadokite line, became a Hasmonean dynastic position. The non-Hasmonean priestly families and the broader religious-textual scholarly community (the proto-Pharisaic tradition) opposed the Hasmonean priestly-monarchical settlement. The Essene community at Qumran (the producers of the Dead Sea Scrolls) appears to have been founded by Zadokite priests who refused recognition of the Hasmonean high priests as legitimate. The dispute is the founding institutional split between a state-captured religious establishment and a dissenting religious-scholarly community — the pattern that has continued across the entire subsequent Jewish tradition.

The Roman period (63 BCE - 70 CE) opened the Second Temple Judaism that produced the rabbinic transition.

63 BCE: Pompey's Roman conquest of Jerusalem ended Hasmonean independence. **37 BCE - 4 BCE**: **Herod the Great** as Roman client king of Judea. Herod was an Idumean (Edomite) convert to Judaism — the novelty was that the Jewish king was not himself ethnically Jewish in the standard Davidic-line sense. The Herodian Temple expansion (from 20 BCE) produced the architectural complex that operated until 70 CE; the surviving Western Wall is the retaining wall of the Herodian Temple Mount.

6 CE: Roman direct administration began under procurators after Herod Archelaus's exile. The Roman-procuratorial period (6-66 CE, with brief Herodian interludes under Herod Agrippa I 41-44 CE) produced the structural crisis that culminated in the 66-73 CE Jewish-Roman War.

The late Second Temple sectarian formations operated across this period.

The **Pharisees** were the largest of the named formations. They emphasised Torah study and the doctrine of the Oral Torah alongside the Written Torah; included members from both priestly and lay populations; were politically quiescent under Roman rule. The Pharisaic tradition produced the post-70-CE rabbinic establishment that has continued to the contemporary period.

The **Sadducees** were the high-priestly aristocracy. They controlled the Temple administration; rejected the Pharisaic Oral Torah; rejected the resurrection of the dead. The Sadducees disappeared with the 70 CE destruction.

The **Essenes** were an ascetic separatist community that rejected the post-Hasmonean Temple establishment as  compromised. The Essenes are associated with the Qumran community that produced the Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered 1947-1956 at Khirbet Qumran by the Dead Sea.

The **Zealots** and **the Sicarii** were the militant anti-Roman factions. They produced the 66-73 CE revolt and were destroyed by the Roman response.

The Jesus movement (the first-century Jewish movement around Jesus of Nazareth) operated within the broader Second Temple Judaism context. The full dossier of the Christian emergence is in Chapter 2 § I; here the relevant point is that the Jesus movement and the Pharisaic-rabbinic tradition emerged from the same Second Temple matrix and divided across the late 1st and 2nd centuries CE.

**66-73 CE**: the **First Jewish-Roman War**. The Jewish revolt against the Roman procurator Gessius Florus produced initial Jewish military victories; the Roman counter-attack under Vespasian and his son Titus reversed the position. The **70 CE Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem and the Second Temple** under Titus took place on the 9th of Av (the Jewish liturgical calendar fast date that commemorates the destruction). The 73 CE fall of the last stronghold at Masada under the Sicarii closed the war. The Second Temple was destroyed; the Sadducee high-priestly aristocracy that depended on cultic operations disappeared; the Zealot-Sicarii militant tradition was crushed; the landscape was reduced to the Pharisaic-rabbinic tradition that had developed under exilic conditions across the previous six centuries.

The rabbinic transition (70-200 CE) produced the governing foundation of the subsequent Jewish tradition.

**Yohanan ben Zakkai** (c. 1-80 CE) was the tanna (early rabbinic sage) credited with the institutional founding of post-70-CE rabbinic Judaism. The rabbinic tradition reports that Yohanan escaped the besieged Jerusalem hidden in a coffin, met Vespasian (the Roman general who would become emperor on 1 July 69 CE), and requested permission to establish an academy at Yavneh (Jamnia, on the Mediterranean coast around 30 km south of contemporary Tel Aviv). Vespasian's reported response — "Ask what I shall give you" — produced the famous request: "*Give me Yavneh and its sages*." The historicity of the specific narrative is contested across the contemporary scholarship (Neusner 1975 treats the coffin narrative as later rabbinic legend); the reality of the Yavneh academy's founding is well-established.

The Yavneh architecture: the Sanhedrin (the Jewish governing council) was reconstituted at Yavneh; the Pharisaic-rabbinic tradition was consolidated as the foundation of subsequent Judaism; the Aaronid priesthood and the Sadducee aristocracy disappeared with the destroyed Temple; the rabbinic class was the scholarly authority in place of the priestly class. The Yavneh consolidation is the founding moment of post-Temple Judaism.

The architecture the Yavneh consolidation produced has carried forward across the subsequent 1,956 years to the publication of this volume in 2026. Rabbinic legal authority operating through textual-interpretive scholarship. Torah and Mishnah and Talmud study as the central religious-institutional practice. The synagogue as the centre of community life. The rabbi as the community leader operating in place of the Aaronid priest. The pattern is distinctive among the traditions Chapters 2-6 have dossiered: the rabbinic class does not operate as a co-opted apparatus in the 1st-3rd century period; the post-70-CE rabbinic tradition is a religious-scholarly community operating without an associated political state, in dispersed communities across the Roman, Parthian, and Sassanid empires. The state-religion capture pattern that has run across the broader Chapter 2-6 dossier resumes for the Jewish tradition only in the modern period — specifically with the founding of the contemporary State of Israel in 1948 (§ III) and the consolidation of state-rabbinical architecture in subsequent decades (§ IV).

**132-135 CE**: the **Bar Kokhba revolt** under Simon bar Kokhba (Simeon ben Koseba) against the Roman emperor Hadrian. The revolt was crushed; Hadrian renamed the province *Syria Palaestina* (the geographic origin of the contemporary name *Palestine*); renamed Jerusalem *Aelia Capitolina*; the Jews were banned from entering Jerusalem; the centre of Jewish life moved from Yavneh to Usha and subsequently to Galilee (Sepphoris, Tiberias).

**c. 200 CE**: the **Mishnah** was codified by **Judah ha-Nasi** (c. 135-217 CE; also Yehuda HaNasi, "Judah the Prince"). The Mishnah is the first canonical compilation of the rabbinic Oral Torah — a six-order systematic codification of Pharisaic-rabbinic legal practice. The Mishnah became the foundation of the subsequent rabbinic tradition; the two Talmuds (the Jerusalem Talmud c. 350-400 CE and the Babylonian Talmud c. 500-600 CE) are commentaries on the Mishnah. The Mishnah codification under Judah ha-Nasi closes the founding period of rabbinic Judaism and opens the medieval period that § II dossiers.

The summary across § I. The roughly 1,400-year period from the formation of the ancient Israelite religious-political settlement through to the Mishnah codification produced the foundational architecture of the subsequent Jewish tradition. The co-option pattern operates within the period in three identifiable cases — the Davidic-Aaronid First Temple settlement; the Hasmonean dynastic capture of the high priesthood; the Herodian client-king regime. The post-70-CE rabbinic tradition operates as a religious-scholarly community without a state-captured apparatus, the architecture that has carried across the subsequent two millennia. The prophetic-critical tradition that operated within the biblical-canonical literature provides the indigenous Jewish critique of religious-political capture — the analytic genealogy of its own framework traces back to the 8th-century BCE prophets.

§ II takes up the medieval period — the Babylonian and Palestinian rabbinic centres, the Geonic settlement, the Sephardi-Ashkenazi divergence across the medieval European and Mediterranean world, the Spanish expulsion of 1492, and the early modern eastern European settlement that would shape the contemporary Ashkenazi-rabbinic tradition.

---


### II. The Medieval Jewish Architecture (c. 200 CE – c. 1700)

The roughly 1,500-year period from the Mishnah codification c. 200 CE under Judah ha-Nasi (§ I) through to the early modern eastern European Ashkenazi settlement around 1700. The period covers five anchor developments — the post-Mishnaic rabbinic consolidation across the Palestinian and Babylonian academies. The Babylonian Geonic period at Sura and Pumbedita (c. 589-1038 CE). The Karaite emergence from the 8th century onward as the dissenting non-rabbinic tradition; the medieval Sephardi-Ashkenazi divergence across the European-Mediterranean world and the medieval European expulsions chronology; the Shulchan Aruch codification of 1563 under Joseph Karo at Safed and the consolidation of the eastern European Ashkenazi settlement under the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth.

The capture argument of the volume operates here in a distinctive register. Across the 1,500-year span § II covers, the tradition does not operate through a state-captured apparatus of the kind dossiered for the Catholic Church under the Constantinian settlement (Chapter 2 § I), the Sunni caliphates (Chapter 5), the Safavid Iranian state (Chapter 6 § III). The tradition operates instead as a religious-scholarly community organised through legal-textual authority in dispersed communities across the Christian and Islamic worlds. The apparatus of the period is the rabbinic class operating as the religious authority within communities under the political authority of non-Jewish states. The legal codes, the responsa networks, the academies, and the eastern European self-governance institutions operated within the broader political constraints set by the Caliphates and the European Christian monarchies.

The co-optation pattern operates within this period at two registers. The Geonic-Exilarchate settlement under the Abbasid Caliphate (c. 750-1038 CE) operated as a state-recognised religious-political authority over the Babylonian rabbinic community — a co-opted apparatus of the same kind dossiered for the Christian and Islamic cases. The Karaite emergence was the dissenting religious-scholarly community against the Geonic-Exilarchate establishment. The pattern is familiar: an established religious authority captured by a political settlement; a dissenting religious-scholarly community operating against captured apparatus. The second register operates externally — the medieval European Christian states that produced the persecutions and expulsions of the 1290-1501 chronology were state-captured Christian religious apparatuses of the kind dossiered in Chapter 2. The persecutions and expulsions are not the documentation of an internal Jewish capture; they are the documentation of how captured-by-state European Christian apparatus operated against the dispersed rabbinic community. The tradition appears in § II as the target of Christian-state capture rather than as the carrier of its own internal capture pattern.

The post-Mishnaic rabbinic consolidation across the 3rd-5th centuries CE produced the foundational textual corpus.

After the Mishnah codification c. 200 CE under Judah ha-Nasi (§ I), the rabbinic tradition developed across two parallel scholarly centres. The Palestinian academies in the Galilee (Tiberias, Sepphoris) operated under the Patriarchate continuing the Sanhedrin-line institutional authority until its abolition around 425 CE under the Byzantine emperor Theodosius II. The Babylonian academies operated under successive Persian and Sasanian rule outside the Roman Empire. The major Babylonian academies were **Nehardea** (the oldest, founded in the early 3rd century CE), **Sura** (founded 225 CE by Abba Arikha, also known as Rav), **Pumbedita** (founded c. 260 CE by Judah bar Ezekiel after the destruction of Nehardea), and **Mahuza** (the academies' temporary location under Rava in the 4th century CE). The two centres produced the two Talmuds. The **Jerusalem Talmud** (Talmud Yerushalmi) was completed c. 350-400 CE at the Galilean academies. The **Babylonian Talmud** (Talmud Bavli) was completed c. 500-600 CE at Sura and Pumbedita under the Amoraim, the Savoraim, and the early Geonim. The Babylonian Talmud became the dominant legal-scholarly text of subsequent Judaism.

The Babylonian Geonic period (c. 589-1038 CE) consolidated the Sura and Pumbedita Academies as the worldwide rabbinic legal authority.

The Geonic period began around the end of the 6th century CE with the establishment of the title *gaon* (Hebrew "excellency"; plural *Geonim*) for the heads of the Sura and Pumbedita Academies. The institutional dating: c. 589 CE start per the New World Encyclopedia; "end of the 6th century or somewhat later" per the Jewish Encyclopedia and Britannica. The end date of 1038 CE is well-established (the death of Hai Gaon, the last major Geonic figure).

The Geonic Academies operated under the Abbasid Caliphate from 750 CE onward (Chapter 5 § II, the Abbasid foundation). In the early 9th century the two academies relocated from their original sites to Baghdad — the Abbasid capital — while continuing to operate under the Sura and Pumbedita names. The architecture of the Geonim echoed the pre-70-CE Jerusalem Sanhedrin: the *gaon* as the head of each academy plus 70 members in seven rows of ten — the "great sanhedrin" architecture (§ I). The academies was the worldwide rabbinic legal authority through the responsa system; Jewish communities across the Islamic and broader Mediterranean world submitted legal questions; the Geonim issued written rulings.

The **Resh Galuta** (Exilarch) was the parallel institution operating as the secular authority over the Jewish community under the Caliphate. The Exilarch position was a hereditary office held within the claimed Davidic line; the Exilarch and the Geonim operated as the dual apparatus — secular vs religious-scholarly authority. The arrangement parallels the dual architecture of the Persian-period high priesthood vs Davidic-line governor (§ I), the Hasmonean combined-authority arrangement, the Herodian client-king vs high priest pattern. The Geonic-Exilarchate settlement is the medieval rabbinic version of the compromised-by-state religious authority architecture — the Caliphate recognised the Exilarch and the Geonim as the official religious-political leadership of the Jewish community; the co-opted apparatus was the legitimating mechanism for the Caliphate's *dhimmi* regime over its Jewish subjects.

Sura held the higher standing through to the end of the 8th century; Pumbedita ascended thereafter. The senior figures: **Yehudai Gaon** at Sura (c. 757-761) was the first major recorded gaon. **Saadia Gaon** (Saadia ben Joseph of Faym / al-Fayyumi) at Sura (928-942) was the most notable Geonic author — produced *Emunoth ve-Deoth* (the principal philosophical work of the medieval rabbinic tradition before Maimonides), biblical commentaries, anti-Karaite polemics. **Sherira Gaon** at Pumbedita (d. 1006) authored the *Iggeret Rav Sherira Gaon* (the foundational historical document of the post-Talmudic rabbinic tradition). **Hai Gaon** at Pumbedita (d. 1038), Sherira's son, was the last major Geonic figure; his death closed the period.

The decline of the Geonic centres ran with the broader decline of the Abbasid Caliphate across the 10th-11th centuries (Chapter 5 § II) and the migration of Babylonian Jewish scholarly resources to the Mediterranean centres (North Africa, Spain, Italy). The 12th-century Maimonidean period operates in the post-Geonic intellectual space. The Geonim produced two major handbooks of Jewish law: **Halakhot Pesukot** (attributed to Yehudai Gaon, 8th century) and **Halakhot Gedolot** by Simeon Kayyara (9th century). The handbooks consolidated Talmudic legal material into systematic codes — the genre that would culminate in Maimonides' Mishneh Torah (12th century) and Karo's Shulchan Aruch (1563).

The Karaite emergence from the 8th century was the dissenting tradition against the Geonic-Exilarchate establishment.

**Anan ben David** (c. 715 – c. 795) was the founding figure of the Karaite movement. The institutional biography: Anan was a Babylonian sage of Exilarchic family. Around 760 CE, when the Exilarchate became vacant, the Geonim passed over Anan in favour of his younger brother Hananiah. The standard scholarly accounts give the structural reason as Hananiah's greater piety and adherence to Talmudic law; the Karaite tradition holds that Anan was rejected for his anti-Talmudic tendencies; the Rabbanite tradition holds that Anan was rejected for character reasons and founded the sect in revenge. The foundational narrative is hostile-source legend rather than documented history; the contemporary scholarship treats the standard biographical account with appropriate scepticism.

Anan founded the Ananite sect in opposition to the Geonic establishment. His foundational text, *Sefer ha-Mitzvot* (Book of Precepts), established the principle that the Tanakh alone is the source of religious authority; the Talmud and the broader Oral Torah corpus are not authoritative. The Ananites rejected the rabbinic legal tradition built on the Mishnah and the Talmuds.

Across the 9th and 10th centuries the Ananites developed into the broader Karaite movement — from Hebrew *qara*, "to read", the "readers" of Scripture. **Benjamin al-Nahawandi** (9th century) is regarded by some scholars as the proper originator of Karaism in its developed form; the movement extended beyond Anan's specific positions. The Karaite communities spread from Babylonia to Persia, Egypt, Syria, Spain, Constantinople, and (from the 13th century) the Crimea and subsequently Lithuania and Galicia. Karaite intellectual centres operated at Jerusalem (10th-11th centuries), Cairo, Constantinople, and the eastern European Karaite settlements.

**Saadia Gaon** (above, Sura 928-942) was the principal medieval opponent of Karaism — wrote multiple anti-Karaite works in Arabic (largely lost, surviving only in fragments). Saadia's achievement was to consolidate the Rabbanite (Talmudic) position against the Karaite challenge. The gap between the Rabbanite and Karaite traditions has continued to the contemporary period; small Karaite communities operate at Israel (the Crimean and Egyptian Karaite communities relocated to Israel in the 20th century) and at the historical centres.

The Karaite case is the dissenting religious-scholarly community against a state-captured apparatus. The pattern parallels the broader dossier — the Pharisees against the Hasmonean priesthood (§ I); the Qumran Essenes against the same; the Buyid-era Twelver community against the Abbasid Sunni establishment (Chapter 6 § II); the Sistani-quietist Twelver position against the Khomeini-Tehran political position (Chapter 6 § IV). The dissenting position recurs across the dossier; the pattern is consistent.

The medieval Sephardi-Ashkenazi divergence produced the two major branches of the medieval rabbinic tradition.

The Sephardi tradition developed under Muslim al-Andalus and the subsequent Christian Reconquista. The Sephardi communities — the Jewish populations of the Iberian Peninsula (*Sefarad* in medieval Hebrew) — developed under the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba (756-1031) and the Andalusian successor states. The *dhimmi* status under Islamic rule (Chapter 5 § II) granted protected-subordinate civil status with restricted religious practice rights. The "Spanish Golden Age" (10th-12th centuries) produced major Jewish intellectual figures including **Hasdai ibn Shaprut**, **Samuel ibn Naghrillah** (vizier of Granada), **Solomon ibn Gabirol**, **Judah Halevi**, and the foremost of the medieval rabbinic authorities, **Moses Maimonides** (Rambam, 1138-1204). Maimonides was born at Córdoba, fled to Fez and then Egypt under the Almohad persecutions of the 12th century, and produced the *Mishneh Torah* (completed 1180) and the *Guide for the Perplexed* (completed c. 1190) — the major intellectual landmarks of the medieval rabbinic tradition.

The Almohad Caliphate's 12th-century reconquest of al-Andalus produced significant Jewish persecution; the Sephardi communities migrated north into the Christian Iberian kingdoms (Castile, Aragon, Portugal) and to North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean. The Christian Reconquista across the 11th-15th centuries produced increasing institutional pressure on the Sephardi communities. The mid-14th century marked the turning point: higher taxation, closer Church-state alliance, popular anti-Jewish sentiment. The 1391 Seville riots spread across Castile and Aragon, producing widespread massacres; an estimated 200,000 Spanish Jews converted to Catholicism across the subsequent decades (the *conversos*), with another 50,000 conversions by 1415.

The Ashkenazi tradition in Rhineland-Northern France-Germany developed across the 9th-11th centuries. The architecture: small communities organised around the synagogue and the local rabbi; community courts under rabbinic jurisdiction; the *kehillah* (community council) as the governing body. The **First Crusade Rhineland massacres** of 1096 produced the shock that shaped the subsequent Ashkenazi tradition. The Crusader forces — preached by Pope Urban II at Clermont in November 1095 — passed through the Rhineland in the spring of 1096; massacres of the communities of Mainz, Worms, Speyer, Cologne, and Trier produced thousands of deaths. The Ashkenazi response was the doctrine of *kiddush HaShem* (sanctification of the Name) — voluntary martyrdom rather than forced conversion. The Tosafist tradition that followed across the 12th-13th centuries consolidated the hardline martyrdom position as the Ashkenazi standard.

**Rashi** (Rabbi Solomon ben Isaac, 1040-1105, Troyes, Northern France) produced the foundational Ashkenazi commentary on the Talmud and the Hebrew Bible. The Rashi commentary became the standard reading-apparatus for the subsequent rabbinic tradition across both Sephardi and Ashkenazi communities. The **Tosafists** (12th-13th centuries, Northern France and Germany) — Rashi's students and successors — developed the Tosafot ("additions") commentary on the Talmud. The Tosafist tradition produced the Ashkenazi legal-scholarly architecture that would diverge from the Sephardi Maimonidean tradition.

The **Black Death** of 1348-49 produced widespread anti-Jewish massacres across Europe on the conspiracy-theoretic accusation of well-poisoning. The Strasbourg massacre of 14 February 1349 (the Valentine's Day massacre, an estimated 2,000 Jews burned alive) is the benchmark of the period. The **Shepherds' Crusades** of 1251 and 1320 produced anti-Jewish massacres across Provence and southern France.

The medieval European expulsions chronology (1182-1501) is the documentation of the co-opted-by-state Christian European apparatuses operating against the Jewish communities.

The pattern across the 13th-16th centuries was the territorial expulsion of populations from European political units, typically following the model: royal financial extraction from the local community; community indebtedness or fiscal exhaustion; popular anti-Jewish agitation; royal expulsion decree with confiscation of remaining property. The chronology of the major expulsions:

**1182**: King Philip Augustus expelled the Jews from the French royal domain (the area around Paris). Readmission followed sixteen years later. The first major European royal expulsion. **1254**: France (national expulsion under Louis IX, partial readmission followed). **18 July 1290** (the 9th of Av, Tisha B'Av): the **Edict of Expulsion of the Jews from England** by King Edward I; implementation by 1 November 1290. The first complete national expulsion that was permanent — the Jews did not return to England until invited by Oliver Cromwell in the 1650s.

**1306**: France (Philip IV expulsion). **1322**: France (Charles IV expulsion). **1394**: France (Charles VI final expulsion, the permanent end of medieval French Jewry). **1421**: Vienna. **1424**: Cologne and Linz. **1439**: Augsburg. **1442**: Bavaria. **1485-1494**: a sequence of Italian and German expulsions (Perugia 1485, Vicenza 1486, Parma 1488, Milan and Lucca 1489, Sicily 1493, Florence 1494).

**31 March 1492**: the **Alhambra Decree** of Ferdinand II of Aragon and Isabella I of Castile, urged by Grand Inquisitor **Tomás de Torquemada**. The Decree gave the Jews of Castile and Aragon four months to convert or leave by 31 July 1492. Estimates: of around 300,000 Spanish Jews at the time, around 200,000 had converted to Catholicism under earlier persecutions; between 40,000 and 100,000 chose expulsion per modern estimates (with significant scholarly contestation of the precise figures). The Ottoman Sultan **Bayezid II** dispatched the Ottoman Navy to bring the expelled Sephardim to Ottoman territories, mainly Thessaloniki and İzmir, where they established the Sephardi communities that have continued across the subsequent five centuries. The Alhambra Decree was formally revoked by the Franco regime on 16 December 1968. **1497**: **Portugal**. King Manuel I, under pressure from his planned marriage to the daughter of Ferdinand and Isabella, ordered the mass forced conversion of the Portuguese community rather than expulsion. The *converso* / *cristão-novo* population would be subject to the Portuguese Inquisition (established 1536) across the subsequent centuries. **1498**: Provence and Navarre. **1501**: the final French expulsion (Provence, after incorporation into the kingdom of France).

The Shulchan Aruch codification of 1563 and the eastern European settlement closed the medieval period.

**Joseph Karo** (1488-1575) was born in Spain or Portugal in the years immediately preceding the 1492 Alhambra Decree. The Karo family fled the Iberian Peninsula in the expulsion period; Karo grew up across the Ottoman Empire, eventually settling at **Safed** (Tzfat) in the Galilee. Safed became the major Sephardi rabbinic-mystical centre of the 16th century under Ottoman patronage; the Lurianic Kabbalah of Isaac Luria (1534-1572) developed at Safed during the same period.

Karo's institutional achievement was the consolidation of medieval rabbinic law into a single accessible code. The **Beit Yosef** (completed 1542) was his massive commentary on the *Arba'ah Turim* (the 14th-century legal compendium of Jacob ben Asher, c. 1269-1343). The Beit Yosef synthesised the post-Talmudic legal tradition. The **Shulchan Aruch** ("Set Table") was authored at Safed in 1563, published Venice in 1565 — a condensed practical legal handbook based on the Beit Yosef. Karo's stated method: legal rulings based on the majority position of three medieval authorities — **Maimonides** (Rambam, 1138-1204, Sephardi-Egyptian), **Asher ben Jehiel** (Rosh, c. 1250-1328, German-Spanish), and **Isaac Alfasi** (Rif, 1013-1103, North African) — "the three pillars on which the halakha rests."

Karo's stated vision was unified Judaism under Sephardic halakhic traditions. The Ashkenazi community resisted the assumption: the Tosafist-Ashkenazi tradition diverged from the Sephardi-Maimonidean tradition on major legal-customary questions. **Moses Isserles** (Rema, 1525/30-1572, Kraków, Poland) added the Ashkenazi corrective. The Isserles **Mappah** ("Tablecloth") glosses on the Shulchan Aruch — published as a commentary integrated into the text — incorporated the Ashkenazi positions where they differed from the Sephardi rulings, drawing on the Tosafist tradition and the later Ashkenazi authorities (Yaakov Moelin, Israel Isserlein, Israel Bruna). The combined Shulchan Aruch + Mappah became the foundational halakhic code for both communities, with Sephardim following Karo's rulings and Ashkenazim following Isserles's glosses.

The 17th-century Polish-Lithuanian commentaries consolidated the Shulchan Aruch's authority for the Ashkenazi community: the **Turei Zahav** ("Taz") by David ben Shmuel haLevi (Poland, 1586-1667); the **Siftei Kohen** ("Shakh") by Shabbetai ben Meir haKohen (Lithuania, 1621-1662). The 19th-century **Mishneh B'rurah** by Yisrael Meir Kogan (the Chofetz Chaim, 1839-1933) consolidated the contemporary Ashkenazi halakhic position on the *Orah Hayim* section of the Shulchan Aruch; the Mishneh B'rurah remains the standard contemporary Ashkenazi practical legal reference.

The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth Jewish settlement (1500-1764) consolidated the eastern European Ashkenazi-rabbinic tradition.

The post-1492 and post-medieval-expulsion eastern European Ashkenazi consolidation operated under the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (formed by the 1569 Union of Lublin). The Commonwealth offered relative legal protection and economic opportunity to the Ashkenazi population at a moment when most of western and central Europe had expelled or persecuted its Jewish communities. The **Council of Four Lands** (*Va'ad Arba Aratzot*, c. 1580-1764) was the central Jewish self-governance body across the Commonwealth. The Council was the governing architecture of the Ashkenazi-rabbinic tradition that would produce the contemporary Ashkenazi-rabbinic settlement covered fully in § III (Hasidism, the Misnagdim opposition, the Vilna Gaon, the 18th-19th century Haskalah Jewish Enlightenment, the Reform-Conservative-Orthodox differentiation, the Zionist movement, the Holocaust).

The summary across § II. The 1,500-year medieval period from the Mishnah codification c. 200 CE to the Polish-Lithuanian settlement around 1700 produced the architecture of the medieval Jewish tradition. The Babylonian Talmud (c. 500-600 CE) and the Shulchan Aruch (1563) are the foundational textual landmarks. The Geonic-Exilarchate settlement under the Abbasid Caliphate was the medieval version of the seized-by-state religious authority architecture; the Karaite emergence was the dissenting religious-scholarly community against captured Geonic-Exilarchate apparatus. The medieval European Christian states produced the persecutions and expulsions of the 1290-1501 chronology — the state-captured Christian apparatuses operating against the dispersed communities. The Sephardi-Ashkenazi divergence produced the two major branches of medieval rabbinic tradition that the Shulchan Aruch and Mappah architecture would unify in the post-1563 period. The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth settlement consolidated the eastern European Ashkenazi-rabbinic tradition that would carry the tradition into the modern period covered in § III.

§ III takes up the modern transition — the 18th-19th century Haskalah Jewish Enlightenment; the Hasidic-Misnagdim opposition under the Vilna Gaon; the Reform-Conservative-Orthodox differentiation across the 19th century; the Zionist movement from the late 19th century; the 20th-century Holocaust; the 1948 founding of the State of Israel.

---


### III. The Modern Jewish Transition (c. 1700 – 1948)

The roughly 250-year period from the early 18th-century Hasidic emergence in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth through the 14 May 1948 founding of the State of Israel. Six anchor developments organise the period: the 18th-century Hasidic emergence under the Ba'al Shem Tov and the Misnagdim opposition under the Vilna Gaon. The Haskalah from Moses Mendelssohn at Berlin; the 19th-century denominational differentiation into Reform, Conservative. And Modern Orthodox movements; the European emancipation and political-religious developments of the 1791-1914 period; the Theodor Herzl political Zionist movement from 1896 onward through the 1917 Balfour Declaration and the British Mandate; the Holocaust under the Nazi regime (1933-1945) and the 1948 founding of the State of Israel.

The co-optation argument operates here at a distinctive register relative to the preceding chapters. The 18th-19th century internal religious developments (Hasidism, Misnagdim, Haskalah, Reform-Conservative-Orthodox) operate as institutional differentiation within the religious-scholarly community without a state-captured apparatus — the tradition continues to operate as the religious-scholarly community organised in dispersed communities under non-Hebrew political authority, as it has across the entire post-70-CE period. The 19th-century European emancipation and the 20th-century Holocaust are operations of European Christian-state apparatuses on the dispersed communities — the European Christian states either granting or revoking civil status; in the German Nazi case attempting the operational elimination of the Jewish populations of Europe. The Zionist movement from 1896 to 1948 produced the vehicle for the construction of a new captured-by-state apparatus — the State of Israel — that has organised the contemporary religious-political settlement (§ IV) for the first time in 1,878 years. The co-optation pattern resumes for the tradition with the founding of the State; the dossier of the contemporary captured State of Israel apparatus is taken up in § IV and Chapter 20.

The Hasidic emergence opened the modern period.

**Hasidism** (Hebrew *Hasidut*, "piety") emerged in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in the early-to-mid 18th century. The founding figure was **Rabbi Israel ben Eliezer** (c. 1698-1760), known as the **Ba'al Shem Tov** ("Master of the Good Name," or by acronym "Besht"). The Ba'al Shem Tov's doctrine emphasised direct emotional-mystical religious experience, joy in prayer, and the spiritual value of the common worshipper alongside the Talmudic scholar. The architecture: the Hasidic *rebbe* (the *tzadik*, "righteous one") as the charismatic spiritual leader of a community of followers; the rebbe-court (the *hoyf*) as the centre; dynastic succession of the rebbe position across generations.

The movement grew rapidly across the second half of the 18th century, particularly in Poland, Galicia, and Ukraine. The appeal was populist — a religiously valid spiritual path that did not require advanced Talmudic scholarship; the emotional warmth of the rebbe community; the ritual innovations including specific changes to the prayer liturgy and the *kashrut* (kosher) standards.

The **Misnagdim** ("opponents," Hebrew *Mitnagdim*) was the structural response from the established Lithuanian rabbinic establishment. The principal figure was **Elijah ben Solomon Zalman** (1720-1797), the **Vilna Gaon** (sometimes "the Gra"). The Vilna Gaon was the leading Talmudic scholar of the period and the head of the Vilna rabbinic community. He issued multiple bans of excommunication (*herem*) against the Hasidic movement across the 1770s and 1780s. The Misnagdim position: the Hasidic movement was a threat to traditional Talmudic scholarship; the *rebbe* veneration was idolatrous; the ritual innovations were a violation of established halakhic practice.

The Hasidic-Misnagdim split produced the bifurcation of eastern European Ashkenazi religion across the late 18th and 19th centuries. The reconciliation between the two streams operated across the 19th century as the rise of the secular Haskalah and the Russian Imperial state pressures on traditional religious life produced a shared opponent for both Hasidim and Misnagdim. Hasidism was legalised by the Russian Imperial government in 1804.

The contemporary Hasidic movement (operational dossier deferred to § IV and Chapter 21) operates through the major dynastic courts that traced back to the late 18th- and 19th-century founders. Chabad-Lubavitch (founded by Shneur Zalman of Liadi, 1745-1812); Belz (founded by Shalom Rokeach, 1779-1855); Satmar (founded by Joel Teitelbaum, 1887-1979); Ger (founded by Yitzchak Meir Alter, 1799-1866); and others. The contemporary Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) political-religious settlement in Israel and the diaspora is the governing successor of these 18th-19th century foundations.

The Haskalah operated in parallel.

The **Haskalah** (Hebrew, "enlightenment"; followers called *Maskilim*) emerged at Berlin in the mid-18th century. The principal figure was **Moses Mendelssohn** (1729-1786), the Berlin philosopher who became the principal spokesman for emancipation in 18th-century Germany. Mendelssohn produced *Phaedon* (1767, a philosophical work on immortality of the soul), the German translation of the Hebrew Pentateuch with Hebrew commentary (the *Biur*, completed 1783), and *Jerusalem* (1783, his principal work on religious-political philosophy).

The Haskalah programme: the integration of religious practice with European philosophical, scientific, and cultural traditions; the modernisation of religious education; the learning of European languages alongside Hebrew and Yiddish; the development of a secular Hebrew literature. The Maskilim sought to harmonise traditional religious life with European Enlightenment culture.

The Haskalah spread eastward across the late 18th and 19th centuries — from Germany to Galicia, then to the Russian Empire. The Russian Haskalah produced figures including Isaac Baer Levinsohn (1788-1860) and the broader 19th-century Hebrew literary revival.

The Vilna Gaon was opposed to the Haskalah as well as to Hasidism — supporting the burning of Haskalah books — although the later Maskilim (sometimes inaccurately) claimed him as a precursor.

The consequence of the Haskalah was the preparation for the 19th-century Reform-Conservative-Orthodox denominational differentiation. The Maskilim's children and grandchildren entered the German university system; many converted to Christianity for civil-status reasons; many produced the Reform movement.

The 19th-century denominational differentiation produced the contemporary denominational landscape.

The **Reform** movement was the first of the three to consolidate. The **Hamburg Temple** (Israelitischer Tempel) was inaugurated on 18 October 1818 at Hamburg — the first permanent Reform synagogue. The architecturally novel features that would characterise Reform synagogues across the subsequent two centuries: an organ; choral music; sermons in German rather than Hebrew; abbreviated Hebrew prayer liturgy with German prayers added; mixed-gender seating. The Hamburg Temple Disputes (1818-1821 and 1841-1842) — the conflict between the Reform community and the Hamburg Orthodox rabbinate — produced the definition of Reform as a distinct religious movement.

The Reform movement consolidated across the 1840s through the Frankfurt Reform Synods (1844-1846). **Abraham Geiger** (1810-1874) was the principal Reform theologian — produced the academic-historical reinterpretation of Judaism that became the Reform theological framework.

Reform was brought to the United States by the post-1840 German Jewish immigrants. The principal American institutional figures: **Isaac Mayer Wise** (1819-1900) at Cincinnati produced the Union of American Hebrew Congregations (1873, the principal Reform congregational organisation), the Hebrew Union College (1875, the principal Reform rabbinical seminary), and the Central Conference of American Rabbis (1889, the Reform rabbinic organisation). The major Reform temples — Temple Emanu-El at New York, Har Sinai at Baltimore, B'ne Yeshurun at Cincinnati — were founded in the 1840s-1850s.

The **1885 Pittsburgh Platform** consolidated the Classical Reform position: Judaism as an evolving ethical-monotheistic religion; rejection of the binding authority of the rabbinic legal tradition; rejection of the messianic restoration to Zion; the Jewish people as a religious community rather than a nation. The **1937 Columbus Platform** moderated the Classical Reform position — under the influence of the eastern European immigrants and the rise of Zionism — restoring elements of traditional observance and reintroducing positive affirmation of the Jewish national-cultural identity.

**Conservative Judaism** (called Positive-Historical Judaism in 19th-century Germany) emerged in 1845. **Zacharias Frankel** (1801-1875) of Dresden seceded from the Frankfurt Reform Synod of 1845 over the question of Hebrew in the liturgy. Frankel's "positive-historical Judaism" position — historical-developmental scholarship on the tradition combined with conservative practice — was the founding moment of what would become Conservative Judaism. Frankel directed the **Breslau Seminary** (the Jewish Theological Seminary of Breslau, founded 1854) — the centre of the Positive-Historical school.

Conservative Judaism developed in the United States across the late 19th and early 20th centuries — the **Jewish Theological Seminary** at New York was founded in 1887; the Rabbinical Assembly organised in 1901; the United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism federation organised in 1913 under **Solomon Schechter** (1847-1915), the architect of American Conservative Judaism.

**Modern Orthodox Judaism** consolidated under **Samson Raphael Hirsch** (1808-1888). Hirsch served as Chief Rabbi at Nikolsburg, Moravia (1847-1851) and then led the *Israelitische Religionsgesellschaft* (Israelite Religion Society) at Frankfurt am Main from 1851 until his death. Hirsch's *Torah im Derech Eretz* doctrine — combining strict religious observance with engagement with European cultural and intellectual life — was the foundation of Modern Orthodoxy. Hirsch promoted institutional separation from the Reform community ("secession orthodoxy") — Orthodox communities should refuse cooperation with the Reform and organise their religious life in separate institutional communities. The Modern Orthodox architecture developed across the late 19th and 20th centuries in Germany, the United States, and Israel.

The traditional Orthodox community of eastern Europe — the descendants of the Misnagdim and the Hasidic communities — operated through the established yeshivot (Volozhin, Mir, Slabodka, Telz, the major Lithuanian centres) and the Hasidic courts (Chabad, Belz, Ger, Satmar, and others in Poland-Galicia-Ukraine).

The European emancipation and political-religious developments (1791-1914) shaped the broader Jewish communal trajectory.

The **French Revolution** (1789-1799) produced the structural foundation of European Jewish emancipation. The 27 September 1791 decree of the National Assembly granted French Jews full citizenship — the first European emancipation of the community. The Napoleonic conquests of 1796-1815 extended Jewish emancipation across western and central Europe. The post-Napoleonic restoration of 1815 partially reversed the emancipation across the German states and Italy; civil status was restored across the 19th century through the 1848 revolutions and subsequent reforms.

The **Russian Imperial** Jewish settlement under the **Pale of Settlement** (1791-1917) restricted residence to the western provinces of the Russian Empire — the former Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth territory that the Imperial Russia had annexed in the partitions of 1772, 1793, and 1795. The Imperial Jewish policy under the 19th-century Tsars (Alexander I, Nicholas I, Alexander II, Alexander III, Nicholas II) oscillated between assimilation programmes and persecution. The **May Laws** of 1882 under Alexander III restricted Jewish residence and economic activity; the late 19th- and early 20th-century **pogroms** — particularly the 1881-1884 wave following Alexander II's 1881 assassination, the 1903 Kishinev pogrom, the 1905-1906 pogroms during the Russian Revolution of 1905 — produced massive emigration from the Russian Empire. Around 2 million Jews emigrated to the United States across 1881-1924, producing the contemporary American community.

The **Dreyfus Affair** in France (1894-1906) — the wrongful conviction and subsequent exoneration of Captain Alfred Dreyfus on charges of treason, the role of antisemitism in the prosecution — produced major shock to the western European emancipation narrative. The Dreyfus Affair was the immediate institutional catalyst for Theodor Herzl's political Zionism.

Political Zionism opened the trajectory toward the State.

**Theodor Herzl** (2 May 1860 – 3 July 1904) was the founding figure of modern political Zionism. Herzl was a Hungarian journalist working as the Paris correspondent for the Viennese newspaper *Neue Freie Presse*. The impact of the Dreyfus Affair on Herzl — he covered the 1894 Dreyfus trial — produced his conviction that European emancipation was unsustainable in the face of antisemitism.

**February 1896**: Herzl published ***Der Judenstaat*** ("The State of the Jews") at Leipzig and Vienna. The pamphlet argued that the only solution to European antisemitism was the establishment of a Jewish state. The pamphlet spread rapidly through the communities of Europe and the broader diaspora.

**29-31 August 1897**: Herzl convened the **First Zionist Congress** at Basel, Switzerland (the Stadtcasino Basel). The Congress established the Zionist Organization with Herzl as president and adopted the Basel Programme: "Zionism aims at establishing for the Jewish people a publicly and legally assured home in Palestine." Herzl wrote in his diary after the Congress: "At Basel I have founded the Jewish state." He died on 3 July 1904 at age 44, 44 years before the establishment of the State of Israel.

The Zionist movement was  diverse from its founding — Political Zionism (Herzl); Cultural Zionism (Ahad Ha'am, 1856-1927); Labour / Socialist Zionism (Ber Borochov, A. D. Gordon, the kibbutz movement from 1909); Religious Zionism (Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook, 1865-1935, the first Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Mandate Palestine from 1921); Revisionist Zionism (Ze'ev Jabotinsky, 1880-1940, founded 1925).

The pre-1948 migration to Ottoman/Mandate Palestine operated through five major waves (*aliyot*). The **First Aliyah** (1881-1903) brought around 25,000-35,000 immigrants, mainly from the Russian Empire following the 1881 pogroms. The **Second Aliyah** (1904-1914) brought around 35,000-40,000 immigrants, including the founders of the Labour Zionist movement. **Tel Aviv** was founded in 1909 as the first modern Jewish city of the period.

**2 November 1917**: the **Balfour Declaration**. British Foreign Secretary Arthur James Balfour issued a letter to Lionel Walter Rothschild stating that "His Majesty's Government view with favour the establishment in Palestine of a national home for the Jewish people." The Declaration was the first official endorsement of Zionist territorial aspirations by a major political power. The Declaration was issued during the First World War as the British position toward the future of the Middle East was being constructed; the parallel promises to the Arab populations (the 1915-1916 McMahon-Hussein Correspondence) and to the French (the 1916 Sykes-Picot Agreement) produced the contradictions that have organised the subsequent century of Middle Eastern politics.

1922: the **British Mandate for Palestine** under the League of Nations. The Mandate incorporated the Balfour Declaration as part of its architecture. The British Mandate ran from 1922 to 14 May 1948. The Mandate period produced the architecture of the proto-Israeli state: the **Jewish Agency for Palestine** (the representation of the Yishuv and the World Zionist Organization to the British authorities). The **Haganah** (the principal Yishuv paramilitary organisation, founded 1920); the *Histadrut* (the Jewish labour federation, founded 1920); the kibbutz and moshav agricultural settlements; the major figures (Chaim Weizmann, David Ben-Gurion).

The 1936-1939 **Arab Revolt** against the British Mandate produced the governing crisis that led to the 1939 White Paper restricting immigration to Palestine — at the moment of the German Nazi persecution producing the greatest demand for refuge.
