The Holocaust under the Nazi regime is the benchmark of the 20th-century experience for the tradition.

**30 January 1933**: Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany. The regime began the persecution of German Jews immediately. The 1933-1939 period: economic boycotts; the Nuremberg Race Laws (15 September 1935); the exclusion of Jews from German civil and economic life; **Kristallnacht** (9-10 November 1938, the coordinated pogrom across Germany producing the destruction of around 7,500 businesses, 1,400 synagogues, and the murder of around 100 people with around 30,000 arrested and deported to concentration camps).

1 September 1939: the German invasion of Poland began the Second World War. The German occupation of Poland produced the ghettoisation of the Polish community — the largest concentration in Europe before the war. The Warsaw Ghetto, established in October 1940, was the largest of the German-Polish ghettos, holding around 400,000 people at its peak.

**22 June 1941**: Operation Barbarossa, the German invasion of the Soviet Union. The **Einsatzgruppen** mobile killing units accompanied the invading German forces; the Einsatzgruppen killed around 1.4 million Jews (and significant numbers of non-Jewish victims) across the occupied Soviet territories by the end of the war. The 29-30 September 1941 **Babi Yar massacre** at Kyiv (33,771 Jews killed in two days) is the benchmark of the Einsatzgruppen campaign.

**20 January 1942**: the **Wannsee Conference** at Berlin. **Reinhard Heydrich**, head of the Reich Security Main Office (RSHA), convened fifteen high-ranking Nazi officials at a villa at Wannsee to coordinate the **Final Solution to the Jewish Question** — the systematic murder of the European Jewish population across German-occupied territory. The Wannsee Conference Protocol — the evidence of the meeting — was recovered after the war and submitted at Nuremberg as the foundational documentary evidence of the institutional decision-making for the Final Solution.

The 1942-1945 architecture of the Final Solution: the killing centres at **Chełmno** (operating from 8 December 1941); **Belzec** (March 1942); **Sobibor** (May 1942); **Treblinka** (July 1942). These four operated under Operation Reinhard, the murder programme for the Polish community; and **Auschwitz-Birkenau** (the largest of the killing centres, operating mass murder from 1942), where over 1.1 million people were murdered between May 1940 and January 1945 — Jews constituted around 85% of the deportees and around 90% of the victims.

The total: around **6 million Jews** murdered across 1933-1945 — around two-thirds of the European prewar Jewish population. The institutional reality is documented by around 3,000 tons of original German records captured by Allied armies, including the Wannsee Conference Protocol and the Einsatzgruppen reports submitted at Nuremberg.

The consequence of the Holocaust for the broader Jewish trajectory was the collapse of European Jewry as the demographic and cultural centre of the worldwide community. The pre-1939 European population of around 9.5 million was reduced to around 3.5 million by 1945. The centre of the contemporary world of the tradition shifted to the United States (around 5 million people) and to Mandate Palestine / the soon-to-be State of Israel.

The 1948 founding of the State of Israel closed the modern transition.

14 February 1947: the British government announced its decision to terminate the Mandate for Palestine and to refer the question to the United Nations. The UN Special Committee on Palestine (UNSCOP) examined the question across 1947 and produced the partition plan recommendation. **29 November 1947**: the United Nations General Assembly adopted **Resolution 181 (II)** — the **Partition Plan for Palestine** — calling for the division of Mandate Palestine into a Jewish state, an Arab state, and a Special International Regime for Jerusalem under UN administration. The vote was 33 in favour, 13 against, 10 abstentions. The Yishuv leadership accepted the Plan; the Arab leadership rejected it.

The partition vote triggered the first phase of the conflict (29 November 1947 - 14 May 1948), the inter-communal phase of what would become the 1948 Arab-Israeli War. **Plan Dalet** — adopted on 10 March 1948 by the Zionist leadership under David Ben-Gurion — produced the framework that would result in the displacement of around 700,000 Palestinian Arabs across 1947-1949 (the *Nakba*).

**14 May 1948** (5 Iyar 5708 in the Hebrew calendar): **David Ben-Gurion** (1886-1973), Executive Head of the World Zionist Organization, Chairman of the Jewish Agency for Palestine, read the **Declaration of the Establishment of the State of Israel** at the Tel Aviv Museum. The Declaration came into effect at midnight that day on the termination of the British Mandate. Ben-Gurion became the first Prime Minister of Israel.

The first Arab-Israeli War (15 May 1948 - 20 July 1949) followed. Egypt, Transjordan, Syria, Iraq, and Lebanon (with smaller contingents from other Arab states) attacked the new state. The 1949 Armistice Agreements produced the Green Line — Israel controlling around 78% of Mandate Palestine (vs the 56% allocated under the UN Partition Plan); Jordan controlling the West Bank and East Jerusalem; Egypt controlling the Gaza Strip.

The summary across § III. The 250-year modern transition produced the contemporary institutional landscape of the tradition. The 18th-century Hasidic-Misnagdim division produced the eastern European traditional rabbinic architecture that survives in the contemporary Haredi communities. The Haskalah produced the foundation for the secular modernity of the tradition. The 19th-century Reform-Conservative-Orthodox differentiation produced the contemporary American denominational landscape of the tradition. The 1894-1948 trajectory from the Dreyfus Affair through Herzl, the Balfour Declaration, the British Mandate, the Holocaust, and the Israeli founding produced the contemporary State of Israel apparatus.

The institutional resumption of the compromised-by-state pattern for the Jewish tradition operates from 14 May 1948. The State of Israel apparatus is the inheritor of the co-opted-by-state pattern that has organised every other major religious tradition the volume has dossiered, after a 1,878-year gap during which the Jewish tradition operated as a religious-scholarly community without an associated state apparatus. The contemporary State of Israel religious-political settlement is the subject of § IV and Chapter 20; the contemporary Haredi political-religious settlement that emerged from the 18th-century Hasidic-Misnagdim foundations and consolidated as the political ally of the Israeli state since the post-1948 period is the subject of Chapter 21.

§ IV takes up the contemporary period — the State of Israel as the co-opted religious-political apparatus of the contemporary tradition; the Haredi political-religious settlement; the diasporic architecture; the broader contemporary religious-institutional landscape from 1948 to 2026.

---


### IV. The Contemporary Jewish Apparatuses (1948 – 2026)

The 78-year period from the 14 May 1948 founding of the State of Israel through to the May 2026 moment. Five anchor architectures organise the period: the Israeli state apparatus, centred on the Chief Rabbinate inherited from the British Mandate (1921) and the 1947 Ben-Gurion / Agudat Israel "status quo" settlement. The Haredi political-religious settlement, organised through the United Torah Judaism alliance (Agudat Israel + Degel HaTorah, formed 1992) and the Shas party (founded 1984 under Rabbi Ovadia Yosef). The religious-Zionist apparatus, distinct from both the Haredi and the secular-Zionist branches, that has organised the post-1967 settler movement and the contemporary Religious Zionist Party / Otzma Yehudit configuration. The diasporic architecture, principally American (around 7.5 million Jews per the Pew Research Center 2020 survey) with smaller communities across France, Canada, the United Kingdom, and the broader worldwide Jewish landscape; the 2024-2026 moment of crisis around the Haredi draft-exemption legislation and the dissolution of the Netanyahu coalition.

The co-optation argument operates in this section at the present-day register. The State of Israel apparatus from 14 May 1948 is the resumption of the co-option pattern that organised every other major tradition the volume has dossiered — after the 1,878-year gap during which the Jewish tradition operated as a scholarly community without an associated state apparatus (§ III closing). The contemporary captured apparatus operates through three primary mechanisms: the Chief Rabbinate's state-monopoly jurisdiction over the personal status of Jews in Israel (marriage, divorce, conversion, burial). The state recognition of one branch of the rabbinic tradition (Orthodox) as the official state authority and the exclusion of all others (Reform, Conservative, Reconstructionist); the state-funded yeshiva and educational apparatus that is the infrastructure of the Haredi political-religious settlement. The operational dossier of the Israeli state captured apparatus is deferred to Chapter 20; the operational dossier of the Haredi settlement is deferred to Chapter 21. § IV maps the architecture.

The Israeli state apparatus is the inheritor of the British Mandate Chief Rabbinate.

The **Chief Rabbinate of Israel** (*Ha-Rabbanut Ha-Rashit Li-Yisra'el*) was established in 1921 under the British Mandate. The architecture was the British colonial inheritance of the Ottoman *millet* system — the Ottoman Empire's organisation of communities as legally distinct corporate entities with jurisdiction over the personal status of their members (marriage, divorce, inheritance, family law). The British Mandate retained the millet structure for Mandate Palestine's eleven recognised confessional communities; the Chief Rabbinate was the successor to the Ottoman-recognised Jewish authority of the late Ottoman period.

The dual Ashkenazi-Sephardi Chief Rabbis structure was established at the 1921 founding: one Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi representing the European-derived Jewish populations; one Sephardi Chief Rabbi (the *Rishon LeZion*, "First of Zion") representing the Sephardi and Middle Eastern Jewish populations. The dual structure has continued through to today. The Mandate-era Chief Rabbis: the first Ashkenazi was **Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook** (1865-1935, the founding religious-Zionist figure dossiered in § III), serving 1921-1935. The first Sephardi was Rabbi Yaakov Meir (1856-1939), serving 1921-1939; **Rabbi Yitzhak HaLevi Herzog** (1888-1959) served as Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi 1936-1959 and continued in office at the 1948 transition; **Rabbi Ben-Zion Meir Hai Uziel** (1880-1953) served as Sephardi Chief Rabbi 1939-1953 and continued in office at the 1948 transition.

The 14 May 1948 founding produced a seamless transition for the Chief Rabbinate from the British Mandate structure to the State of Israel apparatus. The Provisional Government recognised Herzog and Uziel as the official religious authorities of the new state; the Mandate-era ordinances governing rabbinic functions were adopted into the new state's legal order. The **Chief Rabbinate of Israel Law (1980)** formalised the structure. The current Chief Rabbis as of May 2026: **Kalman Ber** as Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi (in office 2024-present) and **David Yosef** as Sephardi Chief Rabbi (in office 2024-present, the son of Ovadia Yosef).

The foundation of the contemporary Israeli settlement is the **"status quo" letter** of 19 June 1947 from David Ben-Gurion (then chairman of the Jewish Agency) to the Agudat Israel leadership. The letter committed the future state to upholding four key halakhic positions: Saturday as the official rest day; *kashrut* compliance in state institutions; the exclusive Orthodox rabbinic jurisdiction over marriage and divorce of Jews; the continued operation of the existing religious educational system as autonomous within the state.

The letter was issued to secure Agudat Israel's political support for the Zionist project at the partition vote moment; the position was incorporated into the 14 May 1948 founding settlement and has continued through to today. The consequence: from 14 May 1948 to the present, the Israeli state recognises Orthodox rabbinic jurisdiction over the personal status of its Jewish citizens. Reform and Conservative rabbinic acts (marriages, conversions) are not recognised by the state. The state's exclusive recognition of Orthodox rabbinic authority is the contemporary co-opted apparatus of the argument — the State of Israel apparatus recognising one branch of the rabbinic tradition as the official state religious authority and excluding all others. Critics across the Israeli political spectrum have described the configuration as a state Orthodox theocracy; the contestation is itself part of the present political landscape.

The Haredi political-religious settlement organises the ultra-Orthodox engagement with captured apparatus.

**Agudat Israel** ("Union of Israel") was founded in 1912 at Katowice as the political organisation representing the eastern European ultra-Orthodox community opposed to the Zionist movement. The position: the legitimate restoration of the Jewish state could only come through divine intervention, not through human political action; the secular Zionist project violated the traditional theological constraint on collective return to Zion before the messianic age.

The Holocaust shifted the position of much of Agudat Israel toward pragmatic engagement with the Zionist project. The 1947 Ben-Gurion / Agudat Israel "status quo" letter was the manifestation. Since the early 1950s, as a matter of ideological position, Agudat Israel / Degel HaTorah / United Torah Judaism representatives have refused to take cabinet minister positions — participating in the Knesset and serving as Deputy Ministers and Knesset Committee Chairs, but not as full ministers. The position maintained the formal refusal of full participation in a Zionist government while permitting the operational engagement with the state apparatus.

**United Torah Judaism** (UTJ, *Yahadut HaTorah*) was formed in 1992 as an electoral alliance between **Agudat Israel** (the Hasidic faction) and **Degel HaTorah** (the Lithuanian / Litvak / non-Hasidic Misnagdim-tradition faction, § III). The alliance was formed to maximise Ashkenazi Haredi representation in the Knesset under Israel's proportional electoral system. The alliance has continued (with one split in 2004-2006) through to the contemporary period. UTJ is non-Zionist — the only major Knesset party that does not affirm the Zionist character of the state, although it operates within the state's political institutions.

UTJ's institutional priorities: state funding for the Haredi yeshiva and educational system; state-funded family allowances supporting the high Haredi birth rate; the legal exemption of Haredi yeshiva students from Israel Defense Forces conscription; the preservation of the Orthodox rabbinic jurisdiction over personal status; the character of the public Sabbath observance. The apparatus: UTJ MKs operate under the authority of the **Council of Torah Sages** (*Mo'etzet Gedolei HaTorah*), the rabbinic body that determines the political positions of the Agudat Israel and Degel HaTorah factions. The current senior figures as of May 2026: **Rabbi Dov Lando** as the senior spiritual leader of Degel HaTorah; **Rabbi Moshe Hillel Hirsch** as the second senior Lithuanian figure; the various Hasidic *rebbes* (the Gur, Belz, Vizhnitz, Sanz dynastic leaders) as the senior figures of Agudat Israel.

The Haredi communal organisation operates through the major Hasidic courts (Chabad-Lubavitch, Belz, Satmar, Ger, Vizhnitz, Bobov, Sanz, Skver, dossiered in § III) and the Lithuanian yeshiva network. The principal Lithuanian yeshivot in Israel: Ponevezh (Bnei Brak), Mir (Jerusalem), Hebron-Knesset Yisrael, and others.

**Shas** (the Hebrew acronym derived from *Shomrei Sefarad*, "Sephardi Guardians") was founded in 1984 by **Rabbi Ovadia Yosef** (1920-2013), the former Sephardi Chief Rabbi of Israel (1973-1983). Shas represents the Sephardi and Mizrahi Haredi community, which had been underrepresented under the Ashkenazi-dominated Agudat Israel. Shas became one of the major Israeli political parties across the late 1980s and 1990s — peaking at 17 Knesset seats in the 1999 election, and operating as the third-largest party in the current Knesset (11 seats in the 25th Knesset, formed November 2022). The party joined the World Zionist Organization in 2010 and has shifted toward the political right — although it has historically operated as a flexible coalition partner across both Likud and Labor governments.

Current institutional leadership: **Aryeh Deri** as party chairman. The party's spiritual-rabbinic leadership operates through the Council of Torah Sages (the Shas equivalent of the UTJ body) with the senior Sephardi rabbinic figures.

The 2024-2026 draft-exemption crisis produced the contemporary rupture.

The foundation of the crisis: the Israeli **Tal Law** (2002) and subsequent legislation established the formal arrangements for Haredi exemption from IDF conscription. Each year, around 80,000 Haredi men of conscription age were exempted from IDF service on the basis of yeshiva enrolment ("Torah is their craft," *Torato Umanuto*).

The **High Court of Justice ruling of 28 March 2024** ruled that the existing exemption framework was no longer legally valid; yeshiva funds would no longer be available for students "eligible for enlistment." Attorney General Gali Baharav-Miara indicated on 31 March 2024 that the conscription process must begin on 1 April. The 25 June 2024 ruling ordered the IDF to begin drafting yeshiva students.

The consequence across 2024-2026: the Netanyahu government coalition (the 37th Government of Israel, formed November 2022) has been dependent on UTJ and Shas as coalition partners. The coalition's failure to pass legislation formally codifying the draft exemption produced the 2025-2026 crisis. UTJ formally withdrew from the government and coalition in July 2025; Shas resigned from government positions in 2025 while remaining in the coalition. The May 2026 moment: on 13 May 2026 Rabbi Dov Lando issued a written declaration that the government had lost the Lithuanian Haredi community's confidence; UTJ moved to support the dissolution of the Knesset and early elections. The general elections must be held no later than 27 October 2026 per the Israeli electoral cycle; the coalition crisis is producing the possibility of earlier elections (September or October 2026). As of 20 May 2026, the political configuration is in flux.

The operational dossier of the contemporary Haredi political-religious settlement — the mechanics of the draft-exemption crisis, the yeshiva funding architecture, the political alliances across Likud-Haredi configurations, the consequences for the Israeli state — is deferred to Chapter 21 in Part III.

The religious-Zionist apparatus is the third institutional branch.

The religious-Zionist tradition operates as distinct from both the Haredi (non-Zionist or anti-Zionist) and the secular-Zionist branches. The foundation: Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook (1865-1935, § III) — the founding religious-Zionist theological figure — affirmed the legitimacy of the secular Zionist project as the structural vehicle of the messianic process. The religious-Zionist community has been organised around the **Bnei Akiva** youth movement (founded 1929) and the **Mizrachi** / **Hapoel HaMizrachi** political parties (subsequently the National Religious Party, NRP).

The post-1967 War period saw the development of the religious-Zionist settler movement — the **Gush Emunim** ("Bloc of the Faithful") movement, founded 1974, that organised the Jewish settlement of the post-1967 occupied territories (the West Bank, the Gaza Strip until 2005, the Golan Heights, East Jerusalem). The settler movement is the intersection of the religious-Zionist theological position and the territorial-expansionist Israeli political project.

The religious-Zionist political configuration in the 25th Knesset: the **Religious Zionist Party** (formed 2019, the successor of the National Religious Party tradition); the **Otzma Yehudit** ("Jewish Power") party under Itamar Ben-Gvir (aligned with the Meir Kahane tradition that was banned from the Knesset in 1988 as racist); the **Noam** party (a smaller conservative faction). The Religious Zionist Party, Otzma Yehudit, and Noam are core coalition partners in the 37th Netanyahu Government.

The diasporic architecture organises the worldwide community outside Israel.

The contemporary worldwide Jewish population is estimated at around 15.7 million (as of 2024 estimates). The two major centres: the **State of Israel** (around 7.2 million Jews, around 46% of the worldwide population); the **United States** (around 7.5 million Jews, around 48% of the worldwide population per the Pew Research Center 2020 estimate). The remaining around 1 million Jews are distributed across France (around 440,000), Canada (around 400,000), the United Kingdom (around 290,000), Argentina (around 175,000), Russia (around 145,000), Germany (around 120,000), Australia (around 120,000), Brazil (around 90,000), South Africa (around 50,000), and smaller communities elsewhere.

The Pew Research Center 2020 study produced the most recent comprehensive demographic survey of the American Jewish community. The denominational breakdown: **Reform** 37%; **Conservative** 17%; **Orthodox** 9%; **other / Reconstructionist / Renewal / Humanistic** 4%; **no particular branch / unaffiliated** 32%.

The demographic shifts across the generations: among American Jews 65 and older, around 70% identify with Reform or Conservative Judaism; among American Jews under 30, around 37% identify with Reform or Conservative — with significantly higher percentages identifying as Orthodox (around 17%, including around 11% ultra-Orthodox) and as unaffiliated. The Orthodox community's institutional dynamics: much higher fertility rates (around 3.3 children per Orthodox adult vs around 1.4 per non-Orthodox adult) and higher retention rates (67% of those raised Orthodox remain Orthodox as adults, vs 41% Conservative retention). The projection: the Orthodox share of the American Jewish population will grow across the coming decades.

The Reform architecture: the **Union for Reform Judaism** (URJ) operates around 900 member congregations and around 1.5 million adherents; the **Hebrew Union College-Jewish Institute of Religion** (HUC-JIR, founded 1875) is the Reform rabbinical seminary; the **Central Conference of American Rabbis** (CCAR) operates as the Reform rabbinic organisation. The Conservative architecture: the **United Synagogue of Conservative Judaism** operates as the congregational organisation; the **Jewish Theological Seminary** at New York operates as the rabbinical seminary; the **Rabbinical Assembly** operates as the rabbinic organisation. The Conservative movement has experienced the decline of the contemporary American Jewish denominational landscape — high attrition rates of those raised Conservative to either Reform or unaffiliated positions.

The Orthodox architecture is subdivided into multiple institutional configurations. **Modern Orthodox** (the governing descendant of the Hirsch tradition, § III) operates through Yeshiva University (founded 1886, the Modern Orthodox flagship institution at New York), the Orthodox Union (OU, the principal kashrut certification body), the Rabbinical Council of America (RCA, the Modern Orthodox rabbinic organisation). **Haredi / Yeshivish** operates through the major American yeshivot (Beth Medrash Govoha at Lakewood, NJ — the largest yeshiva in the United States, founded 1943 by Aharon Kotler; Telshe Yeshiva at Cleveland; Mir Yeshiva branches) and the Haredi communities (Lakewood, Brooklyn Borough Park, Williamsburg, Crown Heights, Monsey, NY). **Hasidic** operates through the Brooklyn-based Satmar (Williamsburg, Kiryas Joel), Bobov, Skver (New Square, NY), and other dynastic communities.

The **Chabad-Lubavitch** outreach apparatus is the outlier of the contemporary Jewish landscape. Chabad operates around 5,000 institutional centres across around 100 countries — the largest contemporary Jewish outreach infrastructure. Under the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe **Menachem Mendel Schneerson** (1902-1994), the Hasidic dynasty (founded by Shneur Zalman of Liadi, 1745-1812, § III) consolidated the global outreach apparatus targeting non-Orthodox and unaffiliated Jews. The architecture: Chabad rabbis (*shluchim*, "emissaries") are dispatched to remote and underserved communities worldwide; the Chabad House model operates as a combined synagogue, educational centre, community centre, and outreach facility.

The Pew 2020 survey found that 16% of American Jewish adults participate in Chabad programmes or services at least sometimes — making Chabad the contemporary American institution with the broadest cross-denominational reach (24% of Orthodox, 26% of Conservative, 27% of Reform, 16% of unaffiliated participants). The contested institutional question after Schneerson's 1994 death: whether the seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe is the Messiah (the *messianist* faction within Chabad) or whether the leadership succession should continue under a new Rebbe (the position that has prevailed but without an actual successor appointed throughout the post-1994 period). Chabad operates as a Rebbe-less Hasidic dynasty, with the deceased Schneerson's recorded teachings and written rulings continuing as the authority.

The European architecture post-1945 was devastated by the Holocaust — the pre-1939 European Jewish population of around 9.5 million reduced to around 3.5 million by 1945 (§ III). The post-1945 European recovery produced smaller communities organised around the surviving urban centres (Paris, London, Berlin, Antwerp, Amsterdam, Rome). The **Conference of European Rabbis** (CER, founded 1956) is the principal Orthodox rabbinic organisation across the contemporary European landscape. The post-1989 Soviet collapse produced the recovery of the post-Soviet communities; the **Federation of Jewish Communities of the CIS** (FJC, founded 1998 under Chabad sponsorship) serves as the principal post-Soviet institutional architecture. The contemporary French community (around 440,000) is the largest European community and the third-largest worldwide; around 60,000 French Jews have emigrated to Israel across 2000-2024 in response to the rising antisemitism in France.

The contestations remain operationally active across the contemporary period.

The institutional characterisation of the State of Israel as a "Jewish state" vs as a "state of the Jewish people" vs as a "democratic state of all its citizens" remains contested across the Israeli political and legal landscape. The 2018 **Basic Law: Israel as the Nation-State of the Jewish People** formally codified the state's Jewish character; the law has been contested by the Israeli Arab community, the political left, and the Reform/Conservative diaspora as  exclusionary.

The religious-Zionist vs anti-Zionist Haredi positions remain contested today. The mainstream Haredi position (UTJ, Shas, mainstream Hasidic courts including Belz, Ger, Vizhnitz) operates pragmatic engagement with the state while maintaining theological reservations. The **Satmar** position (under the late Rabbi Joel Teitelbaum 1887-1979 and his successors) maintains an explicit anti-Zionist theological position; the small **Neturei Karta** ("Guardians of the City") faction operates an even more explicit anti-Zionist position including public participation in anti-Israel demonstrations.

The Israeli vs Palestinian historiography of the post-1948 settlement (the New Historians' contestation, § III) extends to the present. The contemporary institutional positions: the Israeli government's position on the West Bank settlements as legitimate Jewish presence in the historic territory of Judea and Samaria. The international consensus (UN Security Council Resolution 2334 of 2016) that the settlements operate in violation of international law; the Palestinian positions on the right of return for the 1948-displaced population and their descendants.

The Reform / Conservative vs Orthodox positions on the legitimacy of non-Orthodox rabbinic acts in Israel remain contested. The non-recognition of Reform / Conservative marriages, conversions, and rabbinic ordinations by the State of Israel apparatus is a continuing source of institutional tension between the Israeli state and the American diaspora.

The summary across § IV. The contemporary Jewish architecture from 1948 to 2026 operates through three interlocking apparatuses: the State of Israel as captured apparatus (the Chief Rabbinate inheritance from the British Mandate, the "status quo" settlement, the exclusive Orthodox rabbinic jurisdiction). The Haredi political-religious settlement (UTJ and Shas as the contemporary political organisation, the rabbinic authority via the Council of Torah Sages, the contested draft-exemption crisis of 2024-2026); the religious-Zionist apparatus (the Kook theological foundation, the post-1967 settler movement, the Religious Zionist Party / Otzma Yehudit / Noam configuration). The diasporic architecture — principally American (the Reform-Conservative-Orthodox-unaffiliated landscape per the Pew 2020 demographic profile), with smaller communities across Europe (devastated by the Holocaust, recovered around Paris-London-Berlin-Amsterdam), the post-Soviet space (recovered through the Chabad-sponsored FJC), and the broader worldwide Jewish landscape — is the counterweight to the Israeli captured apparatus.

The co-option pattern of the broader dossier applies in its contemporary form. The State of Israel apparatus from 14 May 1948 is the inheritor of the compromised-by-state pattern that organised the volume's Catholic (Chapter 2), Protestant (Chapter 3), Eastern Orthodox (Chapter 4), Sunni Muslim (Chapter 5), and Shia Muslim (Chapter 6) cases. The state apparatus recognising one institutional branch of the religious tradition as the official state religious authority, granting it monopoly jurisdiction over specific functions (personal status, education, the regulation of religious life), and  excluding or subordinating dissenting branches. The volume's analytic frame applies symmetrically; the dossier of Chapter 7 is the dossier of the same pattern in its present-day Jewish form.

Chapter 7 closes here. The dossier of operational mechanics — the specific institutional operations of the contemporary State of Israel and the contemporary Haredi political-religious settlement — is deferred to Part III, Chapters 20 and 21. Chapter 7 has mapped the architecture across the full historical arc: § I the emergence and the rabbinic transition (c. 1200 BCE – 200 CE); § II the medieval architecture (c. 200 CE – c. 1700); § III the modern transition (c. 1700 – 1948); § IV the contemporary apparatuses (1948 – 2026). The six-tradition Part II foundational dossier (Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish) is now complete.

Part II continues with Chapter 8 — the Hindu tradition.

---


## Chapter 8. The Hindu Capture

### I. The Emergence and the Vedic-Brahmanical Foundation (c. 3300 BCE – c. 600 CE)

The roughly 4,000-year period from the Indus Valley Civilisation (c. 3300-1300 BCE) through the Vedic period (c. 1500-600 BCE), the Mauryan Empire (c. 322-185 BCE), the post-Mauryan classical period, and the consolidation of classical Hinduism through the Gupta period (c. 320-550 CE). Six anchor developments organise the section: the Indus Valley Civilisation as the indigenous Bronze Age foundation. The Vedic period and the four Vedas with their internal Samhita-Brahmana-Aranyaka-Upanishad architecture. The varna-jati system articulated in the Purusha Sukta and codified in the Dharmashastras. The 6th-5th century BCE sramana movements (Jain and Buddhist) operating as the dissenting tradition against Brahmanical orthodoxy. The Mauryan Empire under Chandragupta, Kautilya's Arthashastra, and Ashoka's Buddhist patronage; the classical Hindu consolidation through the post-Mauryan and Gupta periods producing the Bhagavad Gita, the Mahabharata and Ramayana epics, the Puranas, the Dharmashastra codification, and the theistic Vaishnava-Shaiva-Shakta architecture.

The term "Hinduism" requires a brief note. The word "Hindu" derives from the Persian rendering of the Sanskrit "Sindhu" (the Indus River); the term entered European usage through Portuguese, English, and Persian sources from the 15th-16th centuries onward. The contemporary academic position: "Hinduism" refers to a complex of religious traditions, philosophical positions, ritual practices, social institutions, and textual corpora that developed across the subcontinent over the 4,000-year period the section covers. The internal Sanskrit term most closely corresponding to "Hinduism" is *Sanatana Dharma* ("eternal order"), although that term in its present sense is also a modern construction. The section uses "Brahmanical" for the priestly tradition operating around the Vedic ritual sacrificial system; "Vedic" for the texts and the period; "classical Hindu" for the post-Vedic synthesis that produced the contemporary religious-philosophical architecture.

The capture argument of the volume operates here in a distinctive register. Across the 4,000-year span § I covers, the Hindu tradition operates as a complex multi-textual, multi-tradition, multi-school religious-philosophical-social architecture that has been entangled with the political authority of successive states. The Mauryan apparatus (c. 322-185 BCE) is the first identifiable case of state-tradition entanglement on the subcontinent — Ashoka's Buddhist patronage establishing the model of Buddhist kingship (*chakravartin*) that would organise the subsequent two millennia of Buddhist-state relations across South and Southeast Asia. The post-Mauryan Sunga reversal restored Brahmanical orthodoxy as the state religious settlement. The Gupta period (c. 320-550 CE) consolidated the classical Hindu cultural-political settlement that has continued as the structural reference of the subsequent two millennia. The full operational dossier of the contemporary Hindu state-captured apparatus — the Bharatiya Janata Party / Sangh Parivar / Indian state architecture from the 1920s through to the contemporary moment — is the subject of § III, § IV, and Chapter 22 in Part III.

The Indus Valley Civilisation is the indigenous Bronze Age foundation.

The **Indus Valley Civilisation** (IVC, also Harappan Civilisation, Indus Civilisation) was a Bronze Age civilisation in the northwestern regions of South Asia, running from around 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE per the contemporary scholarly synthesis; the mature phase ran from around 2600 BCE to 1900 BCE. The major urban sites: **Harappa** (the type site, on the Ravi River, in modern Pakistan); **Mohenjo-daro** (on the Indus, in Sindh, modern Pakistan); **Ganweriwala**, **Dholavira**, **Rakhigarhi**. At its peak the civilisation extended across over 1,000 settlements; the major cities had populations of 40,000-50,000.

The IVC is significant for the Hindu tradition for two reasons. First, the IVC predates the Vedic period and is the indigenous Bronze Age civilisation of the subcontinent — the cultural-material foundation upon which the subsequent Vedic-Brahmanical and classical Hindu traditions were built. Second, the contested question of whether the IVC religious-cultural complex was assimilated into the subsequent Vedic-Brahmanical synthesis (the standard scholarly position) or whether the IVC was itself an early Vedic-Indo-Aryan civilisation (the "Out of India" position, contested by the mainstream scholarship) is the central anchor of the contemporary Hindu nationalist historical-political project. The contestation is dossiered fully in § III.

The Indus Script — the writing system used at the major IVC sites — has not been deciphered to the contemporary moment. The texts remain unreadable; the religious-cultural content of the IVC is reconstructed through archaeological evidence (seals, figurines, the urban architecture) rather than through deciphered texts. The IVC declined across the c. 1900-1300 BCE period. The scholarly positions on the decline: climate change (the drying of the Ghaggar-Hakra / Sarasvati river system). Tectonic shifts and flooding; ecological degradation; trade collapse with Mesopotamia and Egypt; the earlier Mortimer Wheeler "Aryan invasion" thesis (Wheeler as Director-General of the Archaeological Survey of India 1944-1948) that has been largely abandoned by the contemporary scholarship in favour of the gradual-decline position.

The Vedic period produced the foundational textual corpus.

The **Vedic period** ran from around 1500 BCE to around 600 BCE per the standard scholarly chronology. The period is subdivided: the **Early Vedic / Rigvedic period** (c. 1500-1000 BCE), during which the Rigveda was composed; and the **Later Vedic period** (c. 1000-600 BCE), during which the other three Vedas and the Brahmana, Aranyaka, and Upanishad corpora were composed.

The **Indo-Aryan migration**: the contemporary mainstream scholarly position (the Indo-Aryan Migration Theory) holds that Indo-Aryan-speaking populations migrated into the subcontinent from the Central Asian / Iranian Plateau region across the second millennium BCE, bringing the Vedic religious complex with them. The migration was the foundation of the subsequent Vedic-Brahmanical tradition. The Sanskrit term "Aryan" (*arya*) was originally a linguistic and cultural self-designation; the 19th-century European racial usage (which produced the Nazi-era misappropriation of "Aryan") is a separate question. The contested "Out of India Theory" (OIT) holds that the Vedic-Indo-Aryan tradition originated within the subcontinent and was exported from India to Central Asia; the OIT is the  Hindu-nationalist position contested by the mainstream scholarship (deferred dossier to § III).

The four Vedas. The **Rigveda** (*Ṛg-Veda*) is the oldest, composed c. 1500-1200 BCE per the contemporary mainstream scholarly position. 1,028 hymns (*suktas*) arranged in 10 books (*mandalas*). The hymns are addressed to the major Vedic deities — Indra, Agni, Soma, Varuna, Mitra, Surya, Usha. The **Samaveda** is the chant and melodic version of the Rigvedic hymns for ritual use. The **Yajurveda** contains the ritual prose for the Adhvaryu priest, with formulae for the sacrifice (*yajna*). The **Atharvaveda** contains hymns, magical formulae, and household ritual material; it is the latest of the Samhitas.

Each Veda has four internal text-types corresponding to the developmental stages of the Vedic textual tradition. The **Samhitas** are the original hymn collections (c. 1500-900 BCE). The **Brahmanas** are the prose ritual commentaries on the Samhitas (c. 1000-700 BCE); the **Shatapatha Brahmana** is the most extensive. The **Aranyakas** ("forest texts") are the transitional symbolic-philosophical texts between the Brahmana ritual focus and the Upanishadic philosophical focus. The **Upanishads** are the philosophical core, composed c. 800-500 BCE per the standard scholarly position. The **principal Upanishads** — around 10-13 texts including the Brihadaranyaka, Chandogya, Katha, Isha, Kena, Mundaka, Mandukya, Taittiriya, Aitareya, Prasna, and Svetashvatara — operate as the foundational philosophical corpus of the subsequent Hindu philosophical tradition. The Upanishads introduce the central concepts of *atman* (the individual self), *brahman* (the ultimate reality), and the various positions on their relationship that would organise the subsequent Vedanta tradition.

The **Vedic-Brahmanical ritual system** was the governing architecture of the Vedic religion: organised around the *yajna* (sacrificial ritual) performed by specialist Brahmin priests. The major rituals: the *agnihotra* (daily fire-offering); the *soma* sacrifice (involving the soma plant); the *ashvamedha* (royal horse-sacrifice). The ritual system was the apparatus by which the Brahmin class established religious authority over the broader Vedic community.

The varna-jati system was articulated in the Purusha Sukta and codified in the Dharmashastras.

The **varna** system (Sanskrit *varna*, "colour"/"class"/"category") is the fourfold social classification articulated in the **Purusha Sukta** hymn of the Rigveda (10.90). The Purusha Sukta presents the four varnas as emerging from the cosmic primordial being (*Purusha*) at the moment of cosmic creation: **Brahmins** (Vedic priests, scholars, teachers — emerging from Purusha's mouth); **Kshatriyas** (rulers, administrators, warriors — emerging from Purusha's arms); **Vaishyas** (agriculturalists, merchants, traders — emerging from Purusha's thighs); **Shudras** (labourers, servants — emerging from Purusha's feet). The Purusha Sukta is the foundational textual moment establishing the hierarchical four-varna social architecture as the cosmic-religious order.

The varna system is distinct from the broader **jati** system (Sanskrit *jati*, "birth-group" — the term that became "caste" through Portuguese *casta*). The varnas are the theoretical four classes; the jatis are the thousands of endogamous birth-groups that organise the actual social architecture of the subcontinent. The jati system developed across the post-Vedic period and was consolidated through the medieval and early modern periods.

The **Dharmashastra** tradition — the textual corpus codifying the religious-legal architecture of classical Hindu society — codified the varna-jati system across the c. 200 BCE - 1000 CE period. The most significant text: the **Manusmriti** (*Manava-Dharmasastra*, c. 200 BCE - 200 CE), which codified the four-varna duties, the inter-varna relations, the jati-based hereditary occupation system, and the patriarchal household architecture. The Manusmriti has continued as the principal institutional reference for classical Hindu social-legal theory across the subsequent two millennia; it has been contested by reform movements (the Buddha and the Buddhist tradition; the medieval bhakti saints; the 19th-century reform movements; the 20th-century Ambedkar-led Dalit movement) and continues to be the object of contemporary political-legal contestation.

The institutional **Untouchable** / **Dalit** category developed outside the four-varna system as a fifth, excluded category — populations engaged in ritually-polluting occupations (corpse-handling, manual scavenging, leather-working, butchering). The institutional exclusion of the Dalits is the principal contemporary social-political contestation of the Hindu apparatus (deferred dossier to § III and Chapter 22).

The sramana movements of the 6th-5th century BCE produced the emergence of the dissenting religious-scholarly tradition.

The 6th-5th century BCE produced the **sramana** (Sanskrit *sramana*, "striver"/"ascetic") movements — heterodox religious-philosophical traditions challenging the Vedic-Brahmanical institutional authority. The two major sramana traditions that produced contemporary world religious traditions: **Jainism**, consolidated under **Mahavira** (Vardhamana Mahavira, c. 599-527 BCE per the traditional Jain dating, or c. 540-468 BCE per some modern scholarly datings), the 24th and last Tirthankara of the present cosmic cycle per Jain tradition. **Buddhism**, consolidated under **Siddhartha Gautama** (the Buddha, c. 563-483 BCE per the traditional dating, or c. 480-400 BCE per some modern scholarly datings). The Jain and Buddhist institutional positions rejected the Vedic ritual authority, the varna-jati hierarchy, and the ritual sacrifice of animals; both founders were institutionally born into the Kshatriya varna and their teachings explicitly rejected birth-based hierarchical social position.

The other contemporary sramana traditions — Ajivika under Makkhali Gosala, Carvaka materialism, the various sceptical schools — operated as parallel heterodox positions; the Jain and Buddhist traditions are the only sramana traditions that have continued to the present period.

The sramana movements operate as the analytic pattern of dissenting religious-scholarly traditions against a state-captured religious apparatus — the Brahmanical orthodoxy of the late Vedic and Mauryan periods. The pattern parallels the Pharisaic-Qumran dissent against the Hasmonean priesthood (Ch 7 § I); the Karaite dissent against the Geonic-Exilarchate (Ch 7 § II); the Sistani-quietist Twelver position against the Khomeini-Tehran captured Shia apparatus (Ch 6 § IV); and the broader pattern across the volume.

The Mauryan Empire was the first state-tradition entanglement on the subcontinent.

The **Mauryan Empire** was founded by **Chandragupta Maurya** in around 322 BCE through the conquest of the Nanda dynasty's Magadha kingdom. The apparatus: Chandragupta's prime minister and political-philosophical advisor was **Kautilya** (also Chanakya, Vishnugupta, c. 350-275 BCE), whose ***Arthashastra*** is the foundational text of classical Indian political theory. The Arthashastra prescribes a centralised state architecture organised around military power, taxation, bureaucratic administration, an extensive espionage network, and the management of the priestly, scholarly, and commercial classes.

Chandragupta abdicated around 297 BCE in favour of his son **Bindusara** (r. c. 297-272 BCE). Bindusara was succeeded by his son **Ashoka** (r. c. 268-232 BCE) following a four-year succession war. Ashoka's reign was the apex of the Mauryan apparatus. At its peak under Ashoka, the empire extended across most of the subcontinent — from modern Afghanistan in the northwest to Karnataka in the south — operating as the first pan-Indian imperial apparatus.

The **Kalinga War** (c. 262-261 BCE) was the turning point. Ashoka conquered the Kalinga kingdom (the eastern coastal region, modern Odisha); the war produced massive casualties — the traditional figures from Ashoka's 13th Major Rock Edict are around 100,000 killed in combat, around 150,000 deported, and many thousands more dying of disease and famine (the contemporary scholarly position on the exact figures is contested). The Kalinga War produced Ashoka's institutional turn toward Buddhism and the policy of *dhamma* (Pali; Sanskrit *dharma*) — ethical rule, non-violence, religious tolerance.

The **Edicts of Ashoka** — inscriptions on rock surfaces and pillars across the Mauryan territory (around 33 surviving major edicts) — operate as the foundational primary-source documentation of the Mauryan architecture. The edicts, written in Prakrit and (in the northwestern edicts) Greek and Aramaic, articulate Ashoka's policy of *dhamma* and document the Mauryan administrative apparatus.

The Mauryan apparatus is the first identifiable case in the section of state-tradition entanglement on the subcontinent. The architecture: Chandragupta as the imperial founder operating under the Kautilyan Arthashastra political theory; Ashoka as the imperial patron of Buddhism, establishing the model of Buddhist kingship (*chakravartin*, the "wheel-turning" universal monarch) that would organise the subsequent two millennia of Buddhist-state relations across South and Southeast Asia. The Mauryan case is the precursor of the patterns the broader dossier has examined.

The Mauryan apparatus collapsed across the c. 232-185 BCE period. The last Mauryan emperor, Brihadratha, was assassinated in 185 BCE by his commander **Pushyamitra Sunga**, who founded the **Sunga dynasty** (c. 185-73 BCE). The Sunga dynasty reversed the Mauryan Buddhist patronage and reasserted Brahmanical orthodoxy as the state religious settlement.

The classical Hindu consolidation since the post-Mauryan and Gupta periods produced the contemporary architecture.

The post-Mauryan period saw the structural consolidation of classical Hinduism through the post-Mauryan dynasties: the **Sunga** (185-73 BCE), the **Kanva** (73-26 BCE), the **Satavahana** (c. 100 BCE - 200 CE, the southern Deccan dynasty), the **Kushan** (c. 30-375 CE, the northwestern dynasty), and the **Gupta Empire** (c. 320-550 CE).

The Gupta period is the classical Hindu cultural and political settlement. The major Gupta emperors: **Chandragupta I** (r. c. 320-335 CE, the founder); **Samudragupta** (r. c. 335-375 CE, the major military expansionist); **Chandragupta II** (Vikramaditya, r. c. 375-415 CE, the cultural-political apex). The Gupta period produced the foundations of the contemporary classical Hindu architecture: the consolidation of the major Hindu deities (Vishnu, Shiva, the Goddess); the construction of the major temple architecture; the codification of the major Sanskrit literary, philosophical, mathematical, astronomical, and scientific texts.

The institutional textual consolidation operated through several major corpora. The **Bhagavad Gita** (c. 200 BCE - 200 CE, embedded in the Mahabharata's Book 6) is the foundational synthesis text — organising the Vedic-Upanishadic philosophical tradition into a coherent theological-ethical framework that has continued as the principal devotional-philosophical reference of the subsequent Hindu tradition. Krishna's teaching to Arjuna establishes the *karma yoga* (path of action), *bhakti yoga* (path of devotion), and *jnana yoga* (path of knowledge) framework that has organised the subsequent two millennia of Hindu religious-philosophical practice.

The **Mahabharata** and the **Ramayana** (c. 400 BCE - 400 CE compilation period) are the two great Sanskrit epics that operate as the foundational narrative-mythological corpus of the Hindu tradition. The Mahabharata is the longer text (around 100,000 verses, the world's longest known epic) and incorporates the Bhagavad Gita; the Ramayana (around 24,000 verses, attributed to the sage Valmiki) is the more devotional-narrative text centred on the figure of Rama. Both epics have continued as the principal narrative-religious reference of the contemporary tradition.

The **Puranas** (c. 300-1000 CE compilation period) are the corpus of mythological-genealogical-religious texts (18 *Mahapuranas* and a parallel set of *Upapuranas*) that organise the major theistic Hindu traditions. The Vaishnava Puranas (Vishnu Purana, Bhagavata Purana, Padma Purana) organise the Vishnu-centred tradition; the Shaiva Puranas (Shiva Purana, Linga Purana, Kurma Purana) organise the Shiva-centred tradition; the Devi-centred Puranas (Devi Bhagavata Purana, Markandeya Purana) organise the Goddess-centred tradition.

The **Dharmashastra** corpus — the governing legal-religious code-texts — includes the Manusmriti (the principal text, c. 200 BCE - 200 CE), the **Yajnavalkya Smriti** (c. 100-400 CE), the **Narada Smriti**, the **Vishnu Smriti**, and others.

The institutional theistic traditions consolidated throughout the post-Mauryan and Gupta periods. **Vaishnavism** (Vishnu-centred devotion); **Shaivism** (Shiva-centred devotion); **Shaktism** (Devi/Goddess-centred devotion); and various synthesising traditions. The major theistic devotional tradition that would dominate the subsequent medieval architecture — the **bhakti** ("devotional") tradition — emerged in the late Gupta and post-Gupta periods, particularly in southern India (the **Tamil Alvar** Vaishnava poet-saints and the **Tamil Nayanar** Shaiva poet-saints, c. 600-900 CE).

The major Hindu **philosophical schools** (*darshanas*, "viewpoints") were consolidated across the late Vedic and classical periods. The traditional six orthodox schools — the *astika* schools that accept Vedic authority: **Nyaya** (logic); **Vaisheshika** (atomistic naturalism); **Samkhya** (dualistic enumeration); **Yoga** (the Patanjali tradition, c. 200 BCE - 200 CE *Yoga Sutras*); **Purva Mimamsa** (Vedic ritual exegesis); **Vedanta** (Upanishadic philosophy, with the major sub-traditions developing in the medieval period).

The summary across § I. The roughly 4,000-year period from the Indus Valley Civilisation through to the Gupta consolidation produced the foundational apparatus of the Hindu tradition. The IVC (c. 3300-1300 BCE) is the indigenous Bronze Age foundation. The Vedic period (c. 1500-600 BCE) produced the foundational textual corpus through the four Vedas and the Brahmana-Aranyaka-Upanishad architecture. The Purusha Sukta articulated the varna social architecture that the Dharmashastras (especially the Manusmriti c. 200 BCE - 200 CE) codified. The 6th-5th century BCE sramana movements produced the Jain and Buddhist traditions as the dissent against the Brahmanical orthodoxy; the Mauryan apparatus (c. 322-185 BCE) under Chandragupta, Kautilya, and Ashoka was the first identifiable case of state-tradition entanglement on the subcontinent. With Ashoka's Buddhist patronage establishing the chakravartin model that would organise Buddhist-state relations across South and Southeast Asia for the subsequent two millennia. The Sunga reversal restored Brahmanical orthodoxy as the post-Mauryan state religious settlement; the Gupta period (c. 320-550 CE) consolidated the classical Hindu cultural and political architecture through the Bhagavad Gita, the Mahabharata and Ramayana epics, the Puranas, the Dharmashastra codification, and the theistic Vaishnava-Shaiva-Shakta architecture.

§ II takes up the medieval architecture — the post-Gupta period, the bhakti devotional movements, the medieval Hindu philosophical schools (the Vedanta sub-traditions under Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva), the medieval temple architecture and dynastic patronage, the encounter with Islamic political power from the 8th century onward, and the late medieval Vijayanagara consolidation.

---


### II. The Medieval Hindu Architecture (c. 600 – c. 1700 CE)

The roughly 1,100-year period from the late Gupta moment (c. 600 CE) through the late Mughal period and the early modern Maratha consolidation (c. 1700 CE). Six anchor developments organise the section: the bhakti devotional movements emerging in southern India and spreading northward. The three medieval Vedanta sub-traditions under Shankara, Ramanuja, and Madhva; the medieval Hindu dynastic patronage and temple architecture across the regional kingdoms (Chola, Pallava, Hoysala, Khajuraho-era Chandela, Rashtrakuta Ellora, others); the encounter with Islamic political power across the Arab Sind conquest of 711-712 CE. The Ghaznavid raids, the Ghurid-Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526), and the Mughal Empire (1526-1857); the Vijayanagara Empire (1336-1646) as the principal medieval Hindu state-captured apparatus; the early modern Maratha consolidation under Shivaji and the Maratha Confederacy.

The co-optation argument of the volume operates here at three registers. The Islamic political-military apparatuses (the Delhi Sultanate, the Mughal Empire) operate as state-captured Islamic apparatuses (Chapter 5) operating against the dispersed Hindu religious-scholarly community. The Vijayanagara Empire is the construction of a Hindu co-opted apparatus as the medieval response — the consolidation of a centralised Hindu state apparatus organised around the Brahmanical-Vedic ritual settlement, the patronage of the Vaishnava-Shaiva-Shakta-Madhva traditions, and the construction of the major southern Indian temple complexes. The bhakti movements operate as the dissenting religious-scholarly tradition pattern — the devotional movements operating outside the Brahmanical ritual-textual establishment, frequently rejecting the varna-jati hierarchy, the Sanskrit-language monopoly, and the priestly mediation of religious practice. The volume's analytic frame applies symmetrically across the three registers.

The bhakti devotional movements emerged in southern India and spread across the subcontinent.

The bhakti tradition emerged in southern India in the late Gupta and post-Gupta period. The **Tamil Alvars** (12 Vaishnava poet-saints, c. 600-900 CE) produced the Tamil-language devotional corpus known as the *Divya Prabandham* ("Sacred Compilation") — the foundational Vaishnava bhakti texts that organised the subsequent Tamil Vaishnava tradition. The major Alvar figures: **Nammalvar** (the most authoritative figure of the Alvar tradition); **Andal** (the woman saint); **Periyalvar**; **Tirumangai Alvar**.

The **Tamil Nayanars** (63 Shaiva poet-saints, c. 600-900 CE) produced the parallel Tamil-language Shaiva devotional corpus — the *Tevaram* — that organised the subsequent Tamil Shaiva tradition. The major Nayanar figures: **Appar**, **Sambandar**, **Sundarar**, **Manikkavacakar**.

The bhakti tradition spread northward across the medieval period. The **Sant tradition** of medieval North India produced the major non-Brahmanical bhakti figures — **Kabir** (c. 1440-1518, the weaver-poet whose verses challenged both Hindu and Islamic orthodoxy). **Ravidas** (c. 1450-1520, the leather-worker poet-saint of the Dalit tradition). **Nanak** (1469-1539, the founder of the Sikh tradition, dossiered in Chapter 10); **Surdas** (c. 1478-1583, the Krishna devotional poet); **Mirabai** (c. 1498-1547, the Rajput princess Krishna devotional poet); **Tulsidas** (c. 1532-1623, author of the *Ramcharitmanas*, the Hindi-language Ramayana retelling that became the foundational Hindi-language religious text).

The bhakti movements was the dissenting religious-scholarly tradition pattern — the devotional movements operating outside the Brahmanical ritual-textual establishment, frequently rejecting the varna-jati hierarchy, the Sanskrit-language monopoly, and the priestly mediation of religious practice. The pattern aligns with the broader analytic frame: the Pharisaic-Qumran dissent against the Hasmonean priesthood (Ch 7 § I); the Karaite dissent against the Geonic-Exilarchate (Ch 7 § II); the sramana dissent against the Brahmanical orthodoxy (§ I).

The medieval Vedanta sub-traditions produced the foundational positions of medieval Hindu philosophical theology.

The three foundational philosophical positions operated as systematic interpretations of the Upanishadic *atman*-*brahman* relationship.

**Adi Shankara** (c. 788-820 CE, born in Kerala) developed **Advaita Vedanta** ("non-dual Vedanta") — the position that the individual self (*atman*) is wholly identical with the ultimate reality (*brahman*); the apparent plurality of the world is the product of *maya* (illusion). Shankara's achievement: he traveled across the subcontinent debating scholars and establishing monastic settlements (*mathas*); the four principal mathas at **Sringeri** (south), **Dwaraka** (west), **Puri** (east), and **Badrinath** (north) operate to the contemporary period as the centres of the Advaita Vedanta tradition. Shankara's principal commentaries: on the Brahma Sutras, the principal Upanishads, and the Bhagavad Gita. The architecture established by Shankara is the model of the monastic centre as the spiritual settlement, a characteristic feature of medieval Hindu life. His *guru* Gaudapada (7th century, author of the *Mandukya Karika*) is the immediate philosophical predecessor. The dating of Shankara is contested — the standard scholarly position (c. 788-820 CE) is challenged by some scholars who argue for an earlier dating.

**Ramanuja** (c. 1017-1137 CE, born in Tamil Nadu) developed **Vishishtadvaita** ("qualified non-dualism") — the position that the individual self and the ultimate reality are related as the body to the soul; the world is the divine body of God (Vishnu). Ramanuja's position synthesises the Vaishnava devotional tradition with the Vedantic philosophical apparatus; the consequence was the consolidation of the **Srivaishnava** tradition (the principal South Indian Vaishnava sectarian tradition) with its centre at **Srirangam** (Tamil Nadu). Ramanuja's principal works: the *Sri Bhashya* (commentary on the Brahma Sutras), the *Vedartha Samgraha*, and three prose prayers (*Saranagati-gadya*, *Srirangagadya*, *Vaikuthagadya*).

**Madhva** (Madhvacharya, c. 1238-1317 CE, born in Karnataka) developed **Dvaita** ("dualism") — the position that the individual self and the ultimate reality are wholly distinct unchanging realities; the individual self is dependent on the ultimate reality but not identical with it. Madhva's position is the strongest dualist position in medieval Hindu philosophy; the consequence was the consolidation of the **Madhva-Vaishnava** tradition with its centre at **Udipi** (Karnataka). Madhva's principal works: 37 *Dvaita* texts including commentaries on the principal Upanishads, the Brahma Sutras, the Bhagavad Gita, the Rigveda, and the Bhagavata Purana. Madhva polemically described Advaita Vedanta as "deceitful demons teaching Buddhism under the name of Vedanta" — the dispute between the Advaita and Dvaita positions has continued across the subsequent eight centuries.

The medieval Vedanta tradition continued with later figures including **Vallabha** (1479-1531, the founder of the *Shuddhadvaita* "pure non-dualism" position and the *Pushtimarg* devotional tradition centred on Krishna); **Chaitanya Mahaprabhu** (1486-1534, the founder of the Bengali Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition, a *bhedabheda* "difference-and-non-difference" position); **Nimbarka** (12th-13th century, the founder of the *Dvaitadvaita* position).

The medieval Hindu dynastic patronage and temple architecture produced the infrastructure.

The medieval Hindu dynasties was the patrons of the temple-construction, Brahmanical-ritual, and Sanskrit-textual apparatuses across the subcontinent.

In **South India**, the **Pallava dynasty** (c. 275-897 CE) patronised the early Dravidian temple architecture at Mamallapuram (the Shore Temple, the Pancha Rathas) and Kanchipuram. The **Chola dynasty** (c. 850-1279 CE) under **Rajaraja Chola I** (r. 985-1014 CE) and **Rajendra Chola I** (r. 1014-1044 CE) extended Chola authority across South India, Sri Lanka, and into Southeast Asia. The Chola period produced the major Dravidian temple architecture — the **Brihadeeswara Temple** at Thanjavur (completed 1010 CE under Rajaraja Chola), the **Gangaikonda Cholapuram** temple, the **Airavatesvara Temple** at Darasuram. The **Hoysala dynasty** (c. 1026-1343 CE) produced the distinctive Hoysala temple architecture at Belur, Halebidu, and Somanathapura. The **Kakatiya dynasty** (c. 1163-1323 CE) at Warangal patronised the Telugu Shaiva tradition. The **Pandyan dynasty** patronised the Madurai Meenakshi temple complex.

In **North India**, the **Pala dynasty** (c. 750-1161 CE, Bengal-Bihar) patronised both Buddhism (Nalanda, Vikramashila monastic universities) and Hindu traditions. The **Sena dynasty** (c. 1070-1230 CE) succeeded the Pala in Bengal. The **Pratihara dynasty** (c. 730-1036 CE) was the western imperial dynasty. The **Chandela dynasty** (c. 831-1308 CE) produced the **Khajuraho temple complex** (c. 950-1050 CE) — the most  famous medieval North Indian temple complex, with around 25 surviving temples of the original 85. The **Solanki/Chalukya** dynasty (c. 940-1244 CE) of Gujarat produced the Modhera Sun Temple and the Somnath Temple (the latter destroyed by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1025-26, repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt across the subsequent eight centuries).

In **Western India and the Deccan**, the **Chalukya dynasty** (c. 543-753 CE, Badami Chalukyas) and the **Western Chalukya** (Kalyani Chalukya, c. 957-1189 CE) produced the early Deccan temple architecture at Badami, Aihole, Pattadakal. The **Rashtrakuta dynasty** (c. 753-982 CE) produced the **Kailasa Temple at Ellora** (cave temple complex, the world's largest monolithic excavation, completed under Krishna I c. 760 CE).

The **temple architecture** consolidated across the medieval period into three principal architectural traditions: **Nagara** (North Indian style) — characterised by the curvilinear *shikhara* (tower) over the *garbhagriha* (inner sanctum); **Dravidian** (South Indian style) — characterised by the pyramidal *vimana* over the inner sanctum and the *gopuram* (temple gate-tower) at the entrance; **Vesara** (a hybrid style of the Deccan region).

The medieval period also saw the consolidation of the *agama* (sectarian ritual-architectural-iconographic) and *tantra* (esoteric ritual-philosophical) textual corpora. The major *agama* texts: the **Vaikhanasa** and **Pancharatra** Vaishnava agamas; the **Shaiva** agamas (Saiva Siddhanta, Pratyabhijna/Kashmir Shaivism, Pashupata); the **Shakta** agamas (the various *tantras* of the Goddess tradition). The agama-tantra tradition organised the actual ritual practice of the medieval Hindu temples; the Sanskrit Vedic textual tradition operated in parallel as the legitimating philosophical-theological framework.

The encounter with Islamic political power produced the rupture.

The Islamic political-military entry into the subcontinent operated across multiple phases. The **Arab conquest of Sind** (711-712 CE) under the Umayyad general Muhammad bin Qasim (Chapter 5 § II anchor) was the first Islamic political-military presence on the subcontinent. The conquest established the Sind-Multan region as an Islamic political settlement; the rest of the subcontinent remained under Hindu and Buddhist political authority for the subsequent three centuries.

The **Ghaznavid raids** (c. 1000-1030 CE) under **Mahmud of Ghazni** (r. 998-1030 CE) were a series of 17 plundering raids into northern India producing the destruction of major Hindu temples, including the **Somnath Temple** in Gujarat (raided 1025-26 CE. Subsequently destroyed multiple times across the medieval period. 1299 by Alauddin Khilji, 1395 by the Muslim governor of Gujarat, 1665 by Aurangzeb). The Ghaznavid raids did not produce a sustained territorial conquest beyond the Punjab region.

The **Ghurid conquest** under **Muhammad of Ghor** (Muizz al-Din Muhammad Ghuri, r. 1173-1206 CE) produced the sustained Islamic political-military presence in northern India. The decisive battles: the **First Battle of Tarain** (1191, the Ghurid defeat by the Rajput coalition under Prithviraj Chauhan); the **Second Battle of Tarain** (1192, the Ghurid victory over Prithviraj Chauhan); the **Battle of Chandawar** (1194, the Ghurid victory over the Gahadavala dynasty). The Ghurid conquest produced the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate.

The **Delhi Sultanate** (1206-1526) was founded by **Qutb-ud-din Aibak** (a former slave of Muhammad of Ghor) following Muhammad of Ghor's death in 1206. The Sultanate operated through five successive dynasties. The **Mamluk / Slave dynasty** (1206-1290) under Qutb-ud-din Aibak, Iltutmish, **Razia Sultana** (1236-1240, one of the few female rulers in Islamic history), and Balban. The **Khilji dynasty** (1290-1320) under **Alauddin Khilji** (r. 1296-1316, the major expansionist who conquered Gujarat, the Rajput kingdoms, and large parts of the Deccan. His general Malik Kafur conducted the campaigns into the Deccan and South India 1308-1311; the Khilji period produced the destruction of multiple major Hindu temples and the consolidation of the Sultanate's Deccan presence). The **Tughlaq dynasty** (1320-1414) under Ghiyath al-Din Tughlaq, **Muhammad bin Tughlaq** (r. 1325-1351, the imperial overreach period), Firuz Shah Tughlaq. The **Sayyid dynasty** (c. 1414-1451, the post-Timur weakened sultanate). The **Lodi dynasty** (1451-1526, the final Afghan dynasty, ended by the Mughal conquest). **Timur's invasion** (1398-1399) sacked Delhi and produced massive casualties; the shock weakened the Delhi Sultanate across the subsequent century.

The **Mughal Empire** (1526-1857, the major imperial period to 1707) was founded by **Babur** (r. 1526-1530) following the **First Battle of Panipat** (21 April 1526) where Babur defeated the last Lodi sultan Ibrahim Lodi. The major Mughal emperors: **Babur** (r. 1526-1530, the Timurid-Mongol-descent founder); **Humayun** (r. 1530-1540, 1555-1556, after the Sur interregnum); **Akbar** (r. 1556-1605, the consolidator). Akbar's reign produced the architecture of Mughal accommodation with the Hindu majority. The abolition of the *jizya* (the non-Muslim head tax, abolished 1564); the integration of Rajput nobles into the Mughal administrative apparatus; the patronage of Hindu temples (a Krishna temple at Vrindavan); the *Din-i-Ilahi* syncretic religious project (an unsuccessful attempt to produce a syncretic court religion). **Jahangir** (r. 1605-1627). **Shah Jahan** (r. 1628-1658, the builder of the Taj Mahal as the mausoleum of his wife Mumtaz Mahal, completed 1648). **Aurangzeb** (r. 1658-1707, the imperial expansion and the reversal of Akbarian accommodation).

Aurangzeb reimposed the *jizya* (1679), conducted the temple-destruction campaign across the empire (the major destructions: the **Kashi Vishwanath Temple** at Varanasi destroyed 1669 CE with the Gyanvapi Mosque built on the site; the **Krishna Janmabhoomi Temple** at Mathura destroyed 1670 CE with the Shahi Idgah mosque built on the site; the Somnath Temple destroyed 1665 CE; multiple Vrindavan temples). The Aurangzeb period produced the rupture in Hindu-Muslim relations that has continued as the principal anchor of Hindu nationalist political-religious mobilisation through to the contemporary period. The contemporary scholarly position (Richard Eaton): the temple destruction was politically motivated (targeting temples patronised by rebellious nobles or operating as state-property of conquered territories) rather than primarily theologically motivated; the contemporary Hindu nationalist position treats the destructions as the anchor of the broader Hindu grievance against Islamic political authority. The dispute is operationally active across the contemporary period.

The post-Aurangzeb Mughal Empire (1707-1857) was the shell of the earlier imperial apparatus; the actual political authority across the 18th century shifted to the regional successor states (the Maratha Confederacy, the Sikh Confederacy, the Nizam of Hyderabad, the Nawab of Bengal, the various other regional polities) and (from the mid-18th century) to the East India Company. The final dissolution of the Mughal Empire followed the 1857 Indian Rebellion / Sepoy Mutiny.

The Vijayanagara Empire served as the principal medieval Hindu captured-by-state apparatus.

**Vijayanagara** (Sanskrit/Kannada, "City of Victory") was founded in 1336 CE by the brothers **Harihara I** (r. 1336-1356 CE) and **Bukka Raya I** (r. 1356-1377 CE) of the **Sangama dynasty**. The context: the Delhi Sultanate's southern campaigns under Alauddin Khilji (1308-1311) and Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1323-1327) had destroyed the major Hindu polities of southern India — the Kakatiya dynasty of Warangal, the Hoysala dynasty of Karnataka, the Pandyan dynasty of Madurai, the Yadava dynasty of Devagiri. Harihara and Bukka were Hoysala-Kakatiya officials who, according to the traditional account, were captured by the Tughlaq forces, converted to Islam, but subsequently re-asserted their Hindu identity under the influence of the sage **Vidyaranya** and established the Vijayanagara Empire on the banks of the Tungabhadra River at the site of present-day **Hampi** (Karnataka). The historical accuracy of the conversion narrative is contested.

The Empire operated across four successive dynasties: **Sangama** (1336-1485), **Saluva** (1485-1505), **Tuluva** (1505-1570), **Aravidu** (1570-1646). The apex was the reign of **Krishnadevaraya** (r. 1509-1529) of the Tuluva dynasty — the major military expansionist (defeated the Bahmani-derived Bijapur sultanate, conquered the Raichur Doab in 1512, took Udayagiri 1514), the cultural patron (the **Ashtadiggajas** — eight great Telugu poets at his court including Allasani Peddana and Tenali Ramakrishna), and the author of the Telugu *Amuktamalyada*.

Vijayanagara at its peak controlled the modern Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, Goa, southern Kerala, and parts of Telangana and Maharashtra. **Hampi** at its peak under Krishnadevaraya was one of the largest cities in the world, with around 500,000 population, described by Portuguese travellers (Domingo Paes, Fernão Nunes), Persian ambassadors (Abdur Razzaq), and Italian travellers (Niccolò Da Conti).

The Vijayanagara Empire was the bulwark of medieval Hindu political authority against the Islamic Sultanates of the north. The architecture: a centralised Hindu state apparatus organised around the *nayankara* military-administrative system (the *nayakas* as the regional military-administrative officers). The patronage of Vaishnava, Shaiva, Shakta, and Madhva-tradition religious institutions; the construction of the major southern Indian temple complexes of the late medieval period (the Hampi temple complex; the major additions to the Tirupati Venkateswara temple, the Kanchipuram temples, the Srirangam temple complex, the Madurai Meenakshi temple).

The **Battle of Talikota** (also Rakkasa-Tangadi, 23 January 1565 / 26 January 1565 — the precise date is contested in the sources) produced the defeat of Vijayanagara. The combined armies of the five Deccan sultanates (Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Golconda, Bidar, Berar) defeated the Vijayanagara army under **Aliya Rama Raya** (the son-in-law of Krishnadevaraya, the de facto ruler from 1542). Rama Raya was captured and beheaded; the city of Vijayanagara was systematically destroyed over the subsequent five months. The Aravidu dynasty continued operating from Penukonda and Chandragiri until 1646 but the power of the Empire was destroyed.

The Vijayanagara case is the analytic pattern of a medieval state-captured Hindu apparatus — the model that would be invoked by the contemporary Hindu nationalist political project (the Sangh Parivar, the BJP) as the historical-religious precursor of the contemporary state-captured apparatus the project seeks to construct.

The early modern Maratha consolidation produced the closing institutional development of the section.

The **Maratha** rise across the 17th century was the counterweight to the late Mughal apparatus in western India. **Shivaji** (Shivaji Bhonsale, 1630-1680, r. 1674-1680 as Chhatrapati) consolidated the Maratha kingdom in the western Deccan. Shivaji was crowned **Chhatrapati** at Raigad on 6 June 1674 in a Hindu-revivalist coronation ceremony designed to establish the legitimacy of a Hindu sovereign against the Mughal apparatus. The Marathas operated through guerrilla warfare against Aurangzeb's forces across the 1680-1707 Mughal-Maratha War.

The post-Aurangzeb period (1707 onward) saw the structural rise of the **Maratha Confederacy** as the principal political-military apparatus in the subcontinent across the 18th century. The Confederacy operated under the architecture of the Chhatrapati (the Shivaji-line successors at Satara) and the **Peshwa** (the Brahmin prime minister at Pune who became the actual political head of the Confederacy by the mid-18th century). The Confederacy at its peak (c. 1760) extended across the western, central, and northern Indian subcontinent — operating as the de facto successor of the Mughal Empire across the period before the British conquest. The **Third Battle of Panipat** (14 January 1761) — the catastrophic Maratha defeat by the Afghan Durrani forces of Ahmad Shah Durrani — produced the weakening of the Confederacy and opened the space for the subsequent British conquest of the subcontinent (deferred dossier to § III and Chapter 22).

The summary across § II. The 1,100-year medieval Hindu period produced the architecture that has organised the contemporary tradition. The bhakti movements emerged in southern India under the Tamil Alvars and Nayanars and spread northward through the medieval Sant tradition, operating as the dissenting religious-scholarly tradition pattern. The three medieval Vedanta sub-traditions (Shankara's Advaita c. 788-820 CE, Ramanuja's Vishishtadvaita c. 1017-1137 CE, Madhva's Dvaita c. 1238-1317 CE) produced the foundational philosophical positions of medieval Hindu philosophical theology and the governing architecture of the *mathas* and the sectarian centres. The medieval Hindu dynasties (Chola, Pallava, Hoysala, Kakatiya, Pandya, Pala, Sena, Pratihara, Chandela, Solanki, Chalukya, Rashtrakuta) produced the infrastructure of temple architecture, Brahmanical ritual, and Sanskrit-textual production across the subcontinent. The Islamic political-military entry (Arab Sind 712, Ghaznavid raids c. 1000-1030, Ghurid conquest 1192-1206, **Delhi Sultanate 1206-1526**, **Mughal Empire 1526-1857**) was the state-captured Islamic apparatus pattern against the Hindu religious-scholarly community, with the Aurangzeb temple-destruction campaign producing the rupture that organises contemporary Hindu nationalist political-religious mobilisation. The **Vijayanagara Empire (1336-1646)** under the Sangama-Saluva-Tuluva-Aravidu dynasties was the principal medieval Hindu co-opted apparatus and the model for contemporary Hindu nationalist political-religious projects. The **early modern Maratha** consolidation under Shivaji (1630-1680) and the post-Aurangzeb Maratha Confederacy produced the closing institutional development of the section.

§ III takes up the modern transition — the British colonial conquest (East India Company from the 1750s, Crown rule from 1858), the 19th-century Hindu reform movements (Brahmo Samaj under Rammohan Roy from 1828. Arya Samaj under Dayananda Saraswati from 1875; Ramakrishna-Vivekananda mission from the 1890s); the emergence of Hindu nationalism (Vinayak Damodar Savarkar's *Hindutva* of 1923; the **Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh** founded 1925 by K.B. Hedgewar; the broader Sangh Parivar architecture); the partition of British India in 1947; the post-1947 institutional trajectory through the founding of the Indian Republic, the assassination of Gandhi in 1948, the post-1950 Constitutional settlement, and the structural architecture leading to the contemporary BJP/Sangh Parivar political-religious settlement.

---


### III. The Modern Hindu Transition (c. 1700 – 1948)

The roughly 250-year period from the early 18th-century Mughal collapse through to the 14-15 August 1947 partition of British India and the 30 January 1948 assassination of Mahatma Gandhi. Six anchor developments organise the section: the British colonial conquest from the 1757 Battle of Plassey through the 1858 transition to direct Crown rule; the 19th-century Hindu reform movements (Brahmo Samaj 1828, Arya Samaj 1875, Ramakrishna-Vivekananda mission from the 1890s); the emergence of Hindu nationalism (Savarkar's *Hindutva* 1923, the **Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh** founded by **K.B. Hedgewar** on 27 September 1925, the Hindu Mahasabha under Savarkar); the Indian National Congress and the development of the Indian independence movement under Gandhi; the 1947 partition of British India and the partition violence; the post-1947 founding settlement and the 30 January 1948 assassination of Gandhi by Nathuram Godse.

The co-optation argument of the volume operates here at the construction register.
