The 19th-century reform movements was the construction of a modern self-conscious "Hinduism" as a unified religious-philosophical tradition presenting itself to the global modern context — a development that the contemporary scholarly position (Brian Pennington's *Was Hinduism Invented?*; Wendy Doniger; Romila Thapar) treats as novel rather than as the restoration of authentic pre-British Hindu tradition. The Hindu nationalist tradition emerged across the same period as the construction of a Hindu ethnic-civilisational political-religious identity organised through Savarkar's 1923 *Hindutva* framework and operationalised through the RSS apparatus founded 1925. The RSS apparatus is the central anchor of the contemporary Hindu state-captured apparatus that the section's trajectory leads toward — the dossier of the contemporary Sangh Parivar / BJP apparatus is deferred to § IV and Chapter 22.

The volume's anti-antisemitism guardrail and the symmetric application of the analytic frame remain binding across § III. The architecture of Hindu nationalism is dossiered with the same evidentiary standards that the volume has applied to Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, and Jewish captured apparatuses across Chapters 2-7. The dossier is documentary, not polemical.

The British colonial conquest operated across the 1757-1858 period.

The **East India Company** was chartered by Royal Charter of Queen Elizabeth I on 31 December 1600. The Company operated as a trading corporation through the 17th century, establishing factories at Surat (1612), Madras / Fort St. George (1639), Bombay (1668, transferred from the Portuguese dowry of Catherine of Braganza), and Calcutta / Fort William (1690, on the Hooghly River in Bengal). The Bengal factory architecture was established at Hugli in 1651 under permission from the Mughal subahdar Sultan Shuja (the second son of Shah Jahan). The **Carnatic Wars** (1746-1748, 1749-1754, 1757-1763) operated between the British and French East India Companies and their respective Indian client-states; the wars established the British Company as the principal European political-military presence on the subcontinent.

The **Battle of Plassey** (23 June 1757) was the foundational moment. British East India Company troops under **Robert Clive** (around 3,000 men, including around 2,100 Indian sepoys and around 800 Europeans, with 10 field guns) defeated the army of **Siraj-ud-Daulah**, the last independent Nawab of Bengal (around 50,000 men, with French-officered artillery). The victory was produced by the defection of the Nawab's commander **Mir Jafar** following a secret agreement with the Company that the British would install Mir Jafar as the new Nawab. Siraj-ud-Daulah was captured and assassinated. The **Battle of Buxar** (22 October 1764) extended Company territorial authority over Bihar and produced the foundation of the Mughal-Company settlement. The **Treaty of Allahabad** (1765) formalised the Company position — the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II granted the Company the *diwani* (the right to collect revenue) of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa, reducing the Mughal emperor to a nominal sovereign.

The Company's territorial expansion across the late 18th and early 19th centuries operated through military conquest, the subsidiary alliance system (the Wellesley doctrine), and outright annexation. The major expansionist phases: the **Anglo-Mysore Wars** (1767-1799, four wars) against the Mysore kingdom under Hyder Ali and **Tipu Sultan**, concluded at the **Siege of Seringapatam** (1799). The **Anglo-Maratha Wars** (1775-1782, 1803-1805, 1817-1818, three wars) against the Maratha Confederacy, concluded with the dissolution of the Confederacy at the Third War; the **Anglo-Sikh Wars** (1845-1846, 1848-1849) against the Sikh Empire, concluded with the annexation of the Punjab. By 1857 the East India Company controlled directly or indirectly the entirety of the subcontinent.

The **1857 Indian Rebellion** (also Sepoy Mutiny, First War of Indian Independence) was the rupture of Company rule. The Rebellion began on 10 May 1857 at Meerut following the disciplinary action against Indian sepoys who had refused to use the new Enfield rifle cartridges (rumoured to be greased with cow and pig fat, ritually offensive to both Hindu and Muslim sepoys). The Rebellion spread across the northern Gangetic plain and continued through 1858. The consequences: the collapse of the East India Company; the formal abolition of the Mughal Empire (the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah II tried for sedition, exiled to Burma, dying in Rangoon in 1862); the transition from Company rule to direct Crown rule under the **Government of India Act 1858** (UK Parliament).

The **British Raj** (1858-1947) operated through the architecture of a Secretary of State for India in London with cabinet rank, the Viceroy and Governor-General of India as the senior administrative figure. The Indian Civil Service as the elite administrative apparatus, and the Indian Army (around 240,000 troops in 1858, growing to around 2.5 million during World War II). Queen Victoria was proclaimed **Empress of India** (*Kaiser-i-Hind*) on 1 May 1876. The Raj period saw the development of the modern Indian railway network (around 65,000 kilometres by 1947), the postal and telegraph systems, the English-language educational apparatus (the English Education Act 1835 under Macaulay), and the codification of Indian law (the Indian Penal Code 1860).

The 19th-century Hindu reform movements was the construction of modern Hinduism.

**Raja Rammohan Roy** (1772-1833, the "Father of Indian Renaissance") founded the **Brahmo Sabha** on **20 August 1828** at Calcutta, subsequently renamed the **Brahmo Samaj**. The architecture: a rationalist-monotheist Hindu reform movement that rejected idolatry, the caste system, and ritual practices, and adopted a monotheistic theology drawing on the Upanishadic *brahman* concept and influenced by Christian and Islamic theological positions. The Brahmo Samaj does not accept the authority of the Vedas, does not affirm the doctrines of avatars, karma, or samsara, and incorporates Christian liturgical practices into its worship. Roy's other projects: the campaign for the abolition of *sati* (widow self-immolation), legalised by the colonial government in 1829 under Lord William Bentinck; the establishment of the first Indian-language reformist newspapers; the Hindu College (1817, subsequently Presidency College Calcutta). The Brahmo Samaj's successors included **Debendranath Tagore** (1817-1905, the father of Rabindranath Tagore) and **Keshab Chandra Sen** (1838-1884).

**Swami Dayananda Saraswati** (1824-1883) founded the **Arya Samaj** ("Noble Society") on **10 April 1875** at Bombay. The architecture: a Vedic-revivalist Hindu reform movement that affirmed the infallible authority of the Vedas, opposed idolatry and ritualism, opposed the contemporary caste system (while accepting the four-varna theoretical position), and produced the "Back to the Vedas" reform slogan. The Arya Samaj's principal text: Dayananda's *Satyartha Prakash* ("The Light of Truth," 1875). The apparatus included the *shuddhi* ("purification") campaign — the re-conversion of Hindu converts to Islam and Christianity back to the Hindu fold, an apparatus that operated as one of the first organised Hindu responses to the missionary activities of Christian and Muslim communities. The Arya Samaj grew rapidly across the Punjab and the United Provinces in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, and is the precursor of the subsequent Hindu nationalist political-religious mobilisation.

**Ramakrishna Paramhansa** (1836-1886, born Gadadhar Chattopadhyay) was the Bengali Hindu mystic at the Dakshineswar Kali temple near Calcutta. Ramakrishna operated as a non-institutional charismatic religious figure, producing the synthesising bhakti-Vedanta-Advaita theological position that integrated the major Hindu devotional and philosophical traditions and engaged sympathetically with Islam and Christianity. **Swami Vivekananda** (1863-1902, born Narendranath Datta) was Ramakrishna's principal disciple and the consolidator of the tradition. Vivekananda's foundational moment was his **1893 address at the Parliament of the World's Religions** at Chicago (the opening address on 11 September 1893), which produced the first major Hindu presence on the global stage and the model of the Hindu tradition as a "world religion" alongside the Christian, Muslim, Buddhist, Jewish, and other traditions. Vivekananda founded the **Ramakrishna Mission** on **1 May 1897** at Belur Math near Calcutta. The Ramakrishna Mission operates to the contemporary period as one of the major Hindu apparatuses worldwide.

The institutional consequence of the 19th-century reform movements: the consolidation of "Hinduism" as a modern self-conscious religious-philosophical tradition presenting itself to the global modern context. The contemporary scholarly position (Pennington, Doniger, Thapar): the modern category "Hinduism" as a unified religious tradition was produced through the 19th-century encounter between the indigenous religious-philosophical-textual traditions of the subcontinent and the colonial-Christian-Orientalist categorisation apparatuses of the British Raj. The reform movements was the construction of this modern self-conscious "Hindu" tradition.

The emergence of Hindu nationalism produced the foundational ideological and apparatus.

The institutional foundations of Hindu nationalism developed across the late 19th and early 20th centuries. **Bal Gangadhar Tilak** (1856-1920, "Lokmanya"), the major Pune-based Maharashtra political figure, operated through the Indian National Congress while developing the Hindu cultural-nationalist mobilisation apparatus — the Ganpati and Shivaji festivals (institutionalised from 1893 and 1895 respectively) as mass mobilisation events. The **Hindu Mahasabha** ("Great Assembly of Hindus") was founded in 1915 at Haridwar by Madan Mohan Malaviya and others as the political apparatus of the Hindu nationalist tradition.

**Vinayak Damodar Savarkar** (1883-1966) wrote *Essentials of Hindutva* (Nagpur, 1923; subsequently retitled *Hindutva: Who Is a Hindu?* in the 1928 edition) while imprisoned at Ratnagiri Central Jail. The text is the foundational ideological text of the Hindu nationalist tradition. The architecture: Savarkar defined "Hindutva" ("Hindu-ness") in ethnic-cultural rather than religious terms — a Hindu is anyone with shared ancestry (*pitrubhu*, "fatherland") and civilisational ties (*punyabhu*, "holyland") to the Indian subcontinent. Under Savarkar's framework, Sikhs, Jains, and Buddhists are  included as Hindus (since the subcontinent is their holyland); Indian Muslims and Christians are excluded (since their holylands are Mecca/Medina and Jerusalem/Rome respectively).

The Savarkar framework is the foundation of the contemporary Hindu nationalist position. The consequence: the construction of a Hindu ethnic-civilisational identity that organises the contemporary Hindu Rashtra ("Hindu Nation") political project of the Sangh Parivar / BJP. The framework has been described by contemporary scholars (Marzia Casolari's *In the Shadow of the Swastika*; Christophe Jaffrelot's *Hindu Nationalism: A Reader*) as proximate to the 1920s-30s European fascist movements with which Savarkar and the early Hindutva figures had documented ideological contact. Savarkar was released from the Ratnagiri internment in 1937 and assumed the presidency of the Hindu Mahasabha (1937-1943).

**Keshav Baliram Hedgewar** (1889-1940), a Nagpur-based doctor and political protégé of B.S. Moonje (a Tilakite Congressman and Hindu Mahasabha figure), founded the **Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh** ("National Volunteer Organisation," RSS) on **27 September 1925** (Vijayadashami / Dussehra) at Nagpur. Hedgewar had read Savarkar's 1923 *Hindutva* text and met Savarkar at the Ratnagiri prison in 1925; the RSS was founded the same year with Savarkar's blessing.

The architecture of the RSS: a Hindu nationalist paramilitary-cultural cadre organisation, organised through neighbourhood branches (*shakhas*) where uniformed male volunteers (*swayamsevaks*) gather for daily meetings — typically one hour each morning or evening — for physical exercise, paramilitary drill, ideological indoctrination, and community service. The design was for grassroots mobilisation and institutional invisibility — the RSS operated as a "sociocultural" rather than political organisation, allowing it to evade the British colonial security apparatus and to build mass support through the late colonial and post-colonial periods.

The scale of the RSS as of 2025: around 4 million active members, around 73,000 *shakhas* (branches), making it one of the largest volunteer organisations worldwide. The leadership: the **Sarsanghchalak** (Supreme Director) as the senior position (the current Sarsanghchalak is **Mohan Bhagwat**, in office since 2009); the **Sarkaryavah** (General Secretary) as the head (the current Sarkaryavah is **Dattatreya Hosabale**, in office since 2021); the Akhil Bharatiya Pratinidhi Sabha as the governing body. The successive Sarsanghchalaks: **K.B. Hedgewar** (1925-1940, founder); **Madhav Sadashivrao Golwalkar** ("Guruji," 1906-1973, Sarsanghchalak 1940-1973, the major ideological consolidator and author of *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* 1939 and *Bunch of Thoughts* 1966); Balasaheb Deoras (1973-1994); Rajendra Singh (1994-2000); K.S. Sudarshan (2000-2009); Mohan Bhagwat (2009-present).

The architecture of the **Sangh Parivar** ("Family of the Sangh") — the broader institutional federation of RSS-affiliated organisations — developed in the post-1940s period. Major Sangh Parivar organisations: the Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS, labour wing, 1955); the Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (ABVP, student wing, 1949); the **Vishva Hindu Parishad** (VHP, religious wing, 1964); the Bajrang Dal (militant youth wing, 1984); the **Bharatiya Janata Party** (BJP, political wing, founded 1980, the governing successor of the Bharatiya Jana Sangh founded 1951 by Shyama Prasad Mukherjee). The full operational dossier of the Sangh Parivar apparatus is deferred to Chapter 22.

The Indian National Congress and the independence movement operated in continuous tension with the Hindu nationalist apparatus.

The **Indian National Congress** was founded on **28 December 1885** at Bombay by a group of Indian intellectuals and the retired British colonial officer Allan Octavian Hume as the major political-electoral forum of the Indian intellectual class operating within the framework of British colonial sovereignty. The institutional radicalisation of the Congress occurred across the early 20th century, principally under the leadership of **Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi** ("Mahatma," 1869-1948) from his 1915 return to India from South Africa onward. Gandhi's architecture: the *satyagraha* ("truth-force") nonviolent civil disobedience methodology; the major mass movements — the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920-1922), the Civil Disobedience Movement / Salt March (1930-1934), the Quit India Movement (1942).

The Congress and the Hindu Mahasabha / RSS operated in continuous tension across the 1920s-1940s. The Congress position: a secular-pluralist Indian nationalism that included Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, and other communities alongside the Hindu majority. The Hindu Mahasabha / RSS position: a Hindu ethnic-civilisational nationalism that excluded Muslims and Christians. The **All-India Muslim League** (founded 30 December 1906 at Dacca) was the parallel Muslim political-electoral apparatus, increasingly demanding protections for the Muslim minority and (from 1940 onward) the construction of a separate Muslim state. The major Muslim League figure: **Muhammad Ali Jinnah** (1876-1948).

The 1947 partition was the foundational moment of the contemporary South Asian settlement.

The pressure for partition built across the 1940s. The Lahore Resolution of the Muslim League (23 March 1940) called for the construction of separate Muslim "homelands" in the Muslim-majority regions of British India. The 1942 Cripps Mission and the 1946 Cabinet Mission Plan failed to produce a settlement. The **Indian Independence Act 1947** (UK Parliament, 18 July 1947) partitioned British India into two independent dominions. The **Dominion of Pakistan** was established on **14 August 1947** with Karachi as the initial capital; **Muhammad Ali Jinnah** became the founding Governor-General. The **Dominion of India** was established on **15 August 1947** with New Delhi as the capital; **Lord Louis Mountbatten** became the first Governor-General; **Jawaharlal Nehru** became the founding Prime Minister. The Indian Constituent Assembly drafted the Constitution of India, adopted 26 November 1949 and came into effect 26 January 1950; India became a Republic on 26 January 1950.

The **partition violence** produced the rupture of the 1947 settlement. The casualty estimates range across the scholarly literature from around 200,000 (lower-end estimates) to around 2 million (upper-end estimates). The displacement: around 15 million people relocated across the new borders (around 10 million Hindus and Sikhs from the new Pakistan to India; around 5 million Muslims from India to Pakistan; the displacement on the western Punjab border was particularly intense). The consequence: the construction of a politicised Hindu-Muslim communal architecture that has continued to organise the contemporary South Asian political landscape.

The Gandhi assassination produced the post-1947 institutional settlement.

**Mahatma Gandhi** was assassinated on **30 January 1948** at the Birla House at New Delhi by **Nathuram Vinayak Godse** (1910-1949, a former RSS member and current Hindu Mahasabha activist). Godse shot Gandhi at point-blank range during the evening prayer meeting; Gandhi died within thirty minutes. The motivation per Godse's subsequent court statement: Gandhi's position as the protector of Muslim interests — in particular, his January 1948 fast pressuring the Indian government to transfer 550 million rupees of cash assets to Pakistan per the partition financial settlement, which the Indian government had withheld in response to the Pakistan-supported Kashmir invasion — had in Godse's reasoning betrayed Hindu interests, and the assassination was the response. Godse and his co-conspirator Narayan Apte were tried (May 1948 – February 1949), convicted, and executed by hanging on 15 November 1949 at Ambala Jail.

The Nehru-Patel government **banned the RSS** on **4 February 1948** following the assassination, citing the RSS's role in the broader Hindu nationalist apparatus that had produced Godse. The ban was lifted on **11 July 1949** following the RSS's adoption of a formal constitution swearing loyalty to the Indian Constitution and the national flag.

The post-assassination period produced the eclipse of the Hindu Mahasabha as the principal Hindu nationalist political-electoral apparatus and the consolidation of the RSS as the principal Hindu nationalist organisational apparatus. The Hindu Mahasabha continued operating as a marginal political party; the RSS operated as the cadre-organisational apparatus and constructed the Sangh Parivar including the political party **Bharatiya Jana Sangh** (founded 1951, the precursor of the BJP). The architecture leading to the contemporary Sangh Parivar / BJP — the Vajpayee government (1998-2004), the Modi governments (2014-present), the operational mechanics of the contemporary Hindu Rashtra political-religious project — is deferred to § IV and Chapter 22.

The summary across § III. The 250-year modern Hindu transition produced the architecture that organises the contemporary tradition. The British colonial conquest from the 1757 Battle of Plassey through to the 1858 transition to direct Crown rule produced the political-administrative apparatus within which the modern Hindu institutional reconstruction occurred. The 19th-century reform movements — Brahmo Samaj 1828 under Rammohan Roy; Arya Samaj 1875 under Dayananda Saraswati; Ramakrishna-Vivekananda Mission from the 1890s — was the construction of a modern self-conscious "Hinduism" as a unified religious-philosophical tradition. The Hindu nationalist tradition emerged through Savarkar's 1923 *Hindutva* text and was operationalised through the **Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh** founded by **K.B. Hedgewar** on **27 September 1925**, with the broader Sangh Parivar architecture developing since the post-1940s period. The Indian National Congress operated through the same period under Gandhi's leadership as the vehicle of a secular-pluralist Indian nationalism. The 1947 partition produced the structural rupture of the British colonial settlement and the construction of a politicised Hindu-Muslim communal architecture. The 30 January 1948 assassination of Gandhi by Nathuram Godse produced the post-1947 institutional settlement — the temporary ban on the RSS, the eclipse of the Hindu Mahasabha, and the trajectory leading to the contemporary Sangh Parivar / BJP state-captured apparatus.

§ IV takes up the contemporary period — the post-1950 Indian Constitutional settlement. The development of the Bharatiya Jana Sangh (1951) and the BJP (1980). The contemporary RSS and Sangh Parivar architecture. The major political-religious flashpoints of the post-1980 period (the Ram Janmabhoomi / Babri Masjid contestation and the 6 December 1992 demolition; the 2002 Gujarat violence; the 2014, 2019, and 2024 BJP electoral victories under Narendra Modi; the contemporary Hindu Rashtra political-religious settlement); the diasporic Hindu architecture; the operational dossier of contemporary Hindu apparatuses (deferred to Chapter 22).

---


### IV. The Contemporary Hindu Apparatuses (1948 – 2026)

The roughly 78-year period from the 26 January 1950 founding of the Republic of India through to the contemporary moment of May 2026. Six anchor architectures organise the section: the post-1950 Indian Constitutional settlement under Nehru and the secular-pluralist Congress-dominated period. The Bharatiya Jana Sangh (1951) and BJP (1980) as the political-electoral wing of the Sangh Parivar. The Ram Janmabhoomi / Babri Masjid contestation as the central anchor of contemporary Hindu nationalist political-religious mobilisation, from the 1990 Rath Yatra through the 6 December 1992 demolition and the 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration. The 2002 Gujarat violence under Modi as Chief Minister; the 2014, 2019, and 2024 BJP electoral victories under Modi and the contemporary Hindu Rashtra political-religious project; the contemporary RSS and Sangh Parivar architecture including the diasporic Hindu apparatus.

The capture argument of the volume reaches its contemporary register in this section. The Sangh Parivar / BJP apparatus operating across the 2014-2026 period represents the contemporary state-captured Hindu apparatus that the broader analytic frame has been building toward. A political-religious settlement in which one branch of the Hindu tradition (the Sangh Parivar Hindutva tradition) operates the state apparatus through electoral mobilisation, with the governing consequences for the religious minorities (Muslims, Christians, Sikhs) and for the dissenting Hindu traditions (the Dalit-Ambedkarite tradition, the secular-liberal Hindu position, the bhakti devotional traditions outside the Hindutva framework). The operational dossier of the apparatus — the specific mechanics of the Sangh Parivar political-religious operation; the architecture of the Modi government's Hindu Rashtra project; the consequences for the religious minorities — is the subject of Chapter 22 in Part III. § IV maps the architecture.

The volume's anti-antisemitism guardrail and the symmetric application of the analytic frame remain binding. The Hindu Rashtra apparatus is dossiered with the same evidentiary standards applied to the co-opted Catholic apparatus of Chapter 2, the seized Protestant apparatus of Chapter 3, captured Eastern Orthodox apparatus of Chapter 4, the Sunni Muslim apparatus of Chapter 5, the compromised Shia Muslim apparatus of Chapter 6, and the co-opted Jewish apparatus of Chapter 7. The dossier is documentary, not polemical.

The post-1950 Indian Constitutional settlement produced the architecture within which the contemporary apparatuses operate.

The **Constitution of India** was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26 November 1949 and came into effect on 26 January 1950. The architecture: a federal parliamentary democracy with a sovereign-socialist-secular-democratic Republic (the "secular" and "socialist" descriptors added by the 42nd Amendment in 1976 during the Emergency). A bicameral Parliament (the Lok Sabha of 543 directly elected members and the Rajya Sabha of around 245 indirectly elected members); a Prime Minister and Council of Ministers responsible to the Lok Sabha; a ceremonial President as head of state; an independent Supreme Court and judiciary; a federal architecture of around 28 states and 8 Union Territories (current numbers).

The Constitution's principal architect was **Bhimrao Ramji Ambedkar** (1891-1956), the Chairman of the Drafting Committee. Ambedkar was the major Dalit political-intellectual figure of the post-independence period; he subsequently broke with Hinduism and converted to Buddhism on 14 October 1956, leading around 365,000 Dalit followers in the **Deekshabhoomi** (Nagpur) mass conversion ceremony — the foundation of the contemporary Indian Buddhist revival (the Navayana / Ambedkarite Buddhism tradition, dossiered in Chapter 9).

The Indian National Congress dominated the immediate post-independence electoral architecture under **Jawaharlal Nehru** (Prime Minister 1947-1964). Nehru's position: a secular-pluralist Indian nationalism, a non-aligned foreign policy, and a state-led mixed-economy development model. The Congress retained electoral dominance through to the 1977 election under **Indira Gandhi** (Nehru's daughter; Prime Minister 1966-1977, 1980-1984) with the major rupture being the **Emergency** (25 June 1975 – 21 March 1977) during which Indira suspended civil liberties, censored the press, and imprisoned the opposition. The Emergency produced the rupture of Congress dominance — the **Janata Party** government (1977-1980) under Morarji Desai was the first non-Congress government of the post-independence period.

The **Mandal Commission Report** (1980) recommended 27% reservation of central government jobs and educational institutions for the **Other Backward Classes** (OBCs, the broader non-Dalit non-tribal subordinate caste populations). The Mandal recommendations were  implemented by V.P. Singh's National Front government in August 1990, producing the major political contestation of the early 1990s. The **Mandir-Mandal** architecture organised the political-religious mobilisation of the 1990-1992 period: the BJP and Sangh Parivar institutional response to the Mandal implementation operated through the parallel mobilisation around the Ram Janmabhoomi / Babri Masjid contestation.

The Bharatiya Jana Sangh and the Bharatiya Janata Party was the political-electoral wing of the Sangh Parivar.

The **Bharatiya Jana Sangh** (BJS) was founded on 21 October 1951 by **Shyama Prasad Mukherjee** (1901-1953) as the political-electoral wing of the Sangh Parivar following the post-1948 RSS ban and the eclipse of the Hindu Mahasabha. The BJS operated as a marginal political party across the 1950s-1970s with limited electoral success. The integration occurred in 1977 when the BJS merged with the broader anti-Indira coalition into the **Janata Party** that won the 1977 election against Indira Gandhi's Congress.

The Janata Party government (1977-1980) collapsed in 1980; the structural consequence was the formation of the **Bharatiya Janata Party** (BJP) on 6 April 1980. The early BJP figures: **Atal Bihari Vajpayee** (1924-2018, the first BJP President); **Lal Krishna Advani** (born 1927, the major institutional consolidator of the post-1980 BJP).

The BJP electoral trajectory across the 1980s: the starting position was 2 Lok Sabha seats in 1984 (the post-Indira Gandhi assassination election that produced Rajiv Gandhi's 414-seat Congress landslide). The 1980s saw the development of the Ram Janmabhoomi mobilisation as the principal Hindu nationalist political project — the strategic decision by Advani and the Sangh Parivar leadership to organise the BJP around the Ram temple campaign at Ayodhya as the vehicle for the Hindu nationalist political mobilisation.

The Ram Janmabhoomi / Babri Masjid contestation is the central anchor of contemporary Hindu nationalist political-religious mobilisation.

The **Babri Masjid** ("Babur's Mosque") was constructed in 1528-29 at Ayodhya (Uttar Pradesh) by Mir Baqi, a commander of the Mughal Emperor Babur. The Hindu nationalist position: the mosque was constructed on the precise site of the birthplace of the god Rama (the *Ram Janmabhoomi*) on the ruins of a pre-existing Hindu temple destroyed for the purpose. The historical scholarly position is contested — the 2003 Archaeological Survey of India excavation reported evidence of a "massive non-Islamic structure" beneath the mosque with pillars and architectural elements; the contested scholarly position (Romila Thapar, K.M. Shrimali, Sushil Srivastava) treats the ASI evidence as insufficient to demonstrate that the underlying structure was a Hindu temple specifically dedicated to Rama, or that the mosque was constructed on the birthplace of a historical Rama.

On the night of **22 December 1949**, a Ram idol appeared inside the mosque — the position of Hindu activists was that the idol's appearance was a divine revelation; the position of investigators was that the idol was smuggled in by Hindu activists, with the local district magistrate K.K. Nayar (subsequently a Jana Sangh MP) implicated in the operation. The government declared the site contested and locked the entrance.

The reactivation of the Ram Janmabhoomi project occurred in the late 1980s. The **Vishva Hindu Parishad** had been organising the campaign through the 1980s; the broader political-electoral mobilisation was taken up by the BJP under Advani's leadership. On **25 September 1990** Advani launched the **Rath Yatra** ("Chariot Journey") — a 10,000-kilometre political procession from the Somnath Temple at Gujarat to Ayodhya in Uttar Pradesh aboard a modified Toyota truck designed as a chariot. The Rath Yatra produced major communal mobilisation and Hindu-Muslim violence across the route. Advani was arrested at Samastipur (Bihar) on 23 October 1990 by the Lalu Prasad Yadav state government before reaching Ayodhya; the V.P. Singh government collapsed as a consequence.

The **6 December 1992 demolition** of the Babri Masjid was the central moment. Around 150,000 *karsevaks* gathered at Ayodhya for what was presented as a symbolic *kar seva* (religious volunteer service); senior BJP figures Advani, Murli Manohar Joshi, and others were present. The crowd breached the security cordon and demolished the 16th-century mosque over about five hours using improvised tools. The Uttar Pradesh BJP government of **Chief Minister Kalyan Singh** had undertaken to protect the structure; the failure to do so resulted in the dismissal of the Kalyan Singh government immediately after the demolition.

The consequences: the post-demolition Hindu-Muslim communal violence across India produced around 2,000 deaths (the December 1992 - January 1993 Bombay riots alone produced around 900 deaths) and major damage to Hindu-Muslim communal relations. The **Liberhan Commission** was constituted on 16 December 1992 under retired High Court judge M.S. Liberhan to investigate; the Commission reported in 2009 (the report was delayed for around 17 years) finding institutional responsibility on the part of BJP, RSS, VHP, Bajrang Dal, and Shiv Sena leaders for the demolition. The Narasimha Rao Congress government issued an ordinance on 7 January 1993 acquiring 67.7 acres of land at the site through the Acquisition of Certain Areas at Ayodhya Act, 1993.

The Ayodhya legal proceedings operated through the Allahabad High Court Lucknow Bench. The **30 September 2010 Allahabad High Court ruling** divided the disputed 2.77-acre site three ways — one-third to the Nirmohi Akhara (a Hindu monastic order); one-third to the Sunni Waqf Board; one-third to the Ram Lalla (the infant Rama deity, treated as a juristic person in Indian law). The Supreme Court of India heard the appeal across 2010-2019.

The **9 November 2019 Supreme Court verdict** (a unanimous five-judge bench under Chief Justice Ranjan Gogoi with Justices S.A. Bobde, D.Y. Chandrachud, Ashok Bhushan, and S.A. Nazeer) awarded the full 2.77 acres to a trust to be constituted for the construction of a Ram temple at the site, and directed the government to make available 5 acres at a "suitable and prominent" place in Ayodhya to the Sunni Waqf Board for the construction of a mosque. The Court explicitly ruled that the **22 December 1949 placement of the Ram idol inside the mosque was an unlawful act of desecration** and that the **6 December 1992 demolition was an unlawful act in breach of the Court's prior orders** — while still awarding the site to the Hindu side.

The 5-acre alternative site was allocated at Dhannipur village (around 22 kilometres from the Ayodhya site) in February 2020; the project remains largely uncompleted as of 2025. The **Ram Janmabhoomi Teerth Kshetra Trust** was constituted by the Government of India in February 2020 to manage the Ram temple construction. The temple's *bhoomi pujan* (ground-breaking ceremony) was performed by Prime Minister Modi on **5 August 2020**. The temple's *prana pratishtha* (consecration ceremony) was performed by Prime Minister Modi on **22 January 2024** as the centrepiece of the Modi government's pre-2024-election Hindu nationalist mobilisation.

The criminal proceedings against the demolition organisers produced the **30 September 2020 CBI Special Court verdict** at Lucknow under Judge Surendra Kumar Yadav, which **acquitted all the 32 surviving accused** (including Advani, Murli Manohar Joshi, Uma Bharti, Kalyan Singh, Vinay Katiyar, Sadhvi Rithambara), ruling that the prosecution had not produced sufficient evidence to establish a criminal conspiracy. The acquittal has been contested by the Indian Muslim community and by civil society organisations.

The 2002 Gujarat violence was the consolidating moment for the Modi political trajectory.

On the morning of **27 February 2002**, the **Sabarmati Express** train carrying around 1,700 Hindu pilgrims and *karsevaks* returning from Ayodhya stopped at Godhra station (Gujarat) at around 7:43 am. As the train was leaving the station, the emergency brake was pulled and an attack on the train occurred. Four coaches were set on fire; **59 people died** in the fire, most of them women and children among the Hindu pilgrims.

The cause of the fire is contested. The **Nanavati-Mehta Commission** (appointed by the Gujarat government, reporting 2008) concluded that the fire was a pre-planned arson attack by a Muslim mob. The **Banerjee Commission** (appointed by Railway Minister Lalu Prasad Yadav in 2004, reporting 2006) concluded that the fire was an accident. The Commission's institutional standing was subsequently challenged and the Gujarat High Court quashed the Commission's findings. 31 people were convicted by a special court in 2011 for the arson attack; 11 were sentenced to death and 20 to life imprisonment.

The Godhra train burning was followed by widespread anti-Muslim violence across Gujarat over the subsequent three months. The position of the Modi-led Gujarat BJP government was permissive. The official Gujarat government death toll: **1,044 dead (790 Muslims, 254 Hindus), 223 missing, and 2,500 injured**; the Concerned Citizens Tribunal (an independent civil society inquiry) estimated up to 1,926 deaths; other estimates from human rights organisations and journalists placed the death toll at over 2,000.

The Sangh Parivar mobilisation included the major Ahmedabad massacres — the **Gulberg Society massacre** (28 February 2002, around 69 Muslims killed including the former Congress MP **Ehsan Jafri** burned alive); the **Naroda Patiya massacre** (28 February 2002, around 97 Muslims killed); the **Naroda Gam massacre**; the **Best Bakery massacre** (1 March 2002, 14 killed). The pattern across the massacres: police inaction or active facilitation of the Hindu mobs; the involvement of VHP and Bajrang Dal cadres; the protection of the perpetrators by the Modi government.

Allegations against Modi: the Gujarat state Home Minister **Haren Pandya** (subsequently assassinated in 2003) alleged that Modi convened a meeting on 27 February 2002 at his residence in which he instructed police officials to allow Hindu retaliatory violence against Muslims; the IPS officer **Sanjiv Bhatt** filed an affidavit before the Supreme Court alleging the same. The contested institutional response: the Supreme Court-constituted Special Investigation Team (SIT) under R.K. Raghavan concluded in 2012 that there was no evidence to prosecute Modi; the *amicus curiae* Raju Ramachandran disagreed with the SIT conclusion and recommended prosecution.

The institutional consequences: the 2002 Gujarat violence has been characterised as state-facilitated communal violence (Human Rights Watch, *"We Have No Orders to Save You"*, 2002) and as meeting the institutional definition of genocide per the 1948 UN Genocide Convention (Genocide Watch and several genocide scholars). The contested position from the Indian government and the Sangh Parivar treats the violence as "communal riots" rather than state-facilitated violence.

The consequence for Modi's political trajectory: rather than ending Modi's political career, the 2002 violence consolidated his position within the Sangh Parivar — Modi served as Chief Minister of Gujarat from October 2001 through to May 2014, presiding over the Gujarat economic-development model that became the foundation of his 2014 national campaign.

The 2014, 2019, and 2024 BJP electoral victories under Modi produced the contemporary Hindu Rashtra political-religious project.

The **2014 Indian general election** (April-May 2014, results declared 16 May 2014) produced the BJP's first independent Lok Sabha majority. The BJP under Modi as Prime Ministerial candidate won **282 seats** (vote share 31%, the largest single-party vote share since 1989); the BJP-led **National Democratic Alliance** (NDA) won 336 seats. The Congress was reduced to 44 seats — the worst Congress performance in the party's history. Modi was sworn in as Prime Minister on 26 May 2014.

The **2019 Indian general election** produced the consolidation. The BJP won **303 seats** (vote share 37.36%, the largest single-party vote share since 1989); the NDA won 353 seats. The Congress was at 52 seats. Modi was sworn in for a second term on 30 May 2019. The post-2019 architecture: the **abrogation of Article 370** of the Constitution on 5 August 2019 (removing the special status of Jammu and Kashmir and bifurcating the state into two Union Territories). The **Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA)** of December 2019 (providing a fast-track citizenship path for Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi, and Christian refugees from Afghanistan, Bangladesh, and Pakistan, but explicitly excluding Muslims). The development of the **National Register of Citizens** (NRC) in Assam producing around 1.9 million people (mostly Muslims) excluded from the register; the 2019-2020 COVID-19 response; the 2020-2021 farmers' protests against the three farm laws (the laws were subsequently repealed in November 2021).

The **2024 Indian general election** (April-June 2024, results declared 4 June 2024) produced the rupture of the BJP majority. The BJP won **240 seats** (well below the 272 majority threshold and well below the 303 of 2019 and 282 of 2014); the NDA won 293 seats (well below the campaigned target of "400 paar" — 400+ seats); the **INDIA opposition alliance** under Rahul Gandhi's Congress (99 seats, up from 52 in 2019) won 234 seats. The BJP lost ground in the Hindi heartland — the Samajwadi Party + Congress alliance won the majority of Uttar Pradesh seats. Modi was sworn in for a third term on 9 June 2024 as the head of a coalition-dependent NDA government, with N. Chandrababu Naidu's Telugu Desam Party (16 seats) and Nitish Kumar's Janata Dal (United) (12 seats) as the principal coalition partners. The contemporary position as of May 2026: a coalition-dependent NDA majority with reduced manoeuvrability compared to the 2014-2024 majority period.

The contemporary RSS and Sangh Parivar architecture organises the governing apparatus of the Hindu nationalist political-religious project.

The **Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh** celebrated its 100th anniversary on **27 September 2025**. The contemporary architecture: around 4 million active members; around 73,000 *shakhas* (branches) across India and the broader Indian diaspora; the contemporary leadership under Sarsanghchalak **Mohan Bhagwat** (in office since 2009) and Sarkaryavah **Dattatreya Hosabale** (in office since 2021); the headquarters at Dr. Hedgewar Bhawan, Nagpur.

The contemporary Sangh Parivar architecture includes the RSS (the cadre-organisational apparatus). The **Bharatiya Janata Party** (the political-electoral wing). The **Vishva Hindu Parishad** (the religious wing). The **Bajrang Dal** (the militant youth wing). The **Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad** (the student wing). The **Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh** (the labour wing); **Vidya Bharati** (the educational network operating thousands of *Saraswati Shishu Mandir* schools); **Seva Bharati** (the social service wing operating in tribal and Dalit areas); the **Hindu Swayamsevak Sangh** (the diasporic wing operating in around 40 countries); the **Overseas Friends of BJP** (the diasporic BJP-affiliated organisation).

The diasporic Hindu architecture organises the worldwide Hindu institutional presence.

The **worldwide Hindu population** is estimated at around 1.2 billion (as of 2025), with around 1.1 billion in India (around 80% of the Indian population of around 1.4 billion) and around 100 million in the global Hindu diaspora. The major diasporic Hindu communities: **Nepal** (around 28 million Hindus, around 81% of the Nepal population); **Bangladesh** (around 13 million, around 8% of the population. Declined from around 22% at the 1951 partition); **Indonesia** (around 4.6 million, principally Bali); **Pakistan** (around 4 million, principally Sindh); **United States** (around 3.3 million, principally the post-1965 Hindu Americans); **Sri Lanka** (around 2.6 million. Principally the Tamil Hindu community); **Malaysia** (around 1.9 million, principally Tamil and Telugu Indian-origin); **United Kingdom** (around 1 million, principally the post-1947 Indian and East African Asian-origin community); **Canada** (around 830. 000); **Mauritius** (around 580,000, around 48% of the Mauritius population. The principal Hindu-majority country outside the subcontinent); **South Africa** (around 510,000, principally the 19th-century indentured labour-derived community); **Fiji**, **Trinidad and Tobago**, **Guyana**, **Suriname** (the smaller indentured-labour-derived communities); the post-1970s Hindu migration to the Gulf states, Singapore, Australia, New Zealand, and continental Europe.

The diasporic Hindu apparatus operates through temple complexes, cultural organisations, and the Sangh Parivar-affiliated institutional network (HSS, OFBJP, VHP of America, and parallel organisations).

The summary across § IV. The contemporary Hindu institutional architecture from 1948 to 2026 operates through three interlocking apparatuses: the post-1950 Indian Constitutional settlement under Nehru and the Congress-dominated period (1947-1977, with the 1975-1977 Emergency rupture and the 1990 Mandal Commission implementation as the major moments). The Bharatiya Jana Sangh (1951) and BJP (1980) as the political-electoral wing of the Sangh Parivar; the Ram Janmabhoomi / Babri Masjid contestation as the central anchor of contemporary Hindu nationalist political-religious mobilisation (Advani's 1990 Rath Yatra; the **6 December 1992 demolition**; the 9 November 2019 Supreme Court verdict; the 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration). The 2002 Gujarat violence under Modi as Chief Minister consolidated his trajectory toward the 2014 national breakthrough. The **2014 (282 seats), 2019 (303 seats), and 2024 (240 seats, NDA 293) BJP electoral victories** under Modi produced the architecture of the contemporary Hindu Rashtra project, with the post-2019 architecture (Article 370 abrogation August 2019; CAA December 2019; NRC operation; Ram Mandir consecration January 2024) operating as the operational manifestation. The contemporary RSS apparatus (4 million members, 73,000 shakhas, Bhagwat as Sarsanghchalak since 2009, Hosabale as Sarkaryavah since 2021, 100th anniversary 27 September 2025) and the broader Sangh Parivar architecture (BJP, VHP, Bajrang Dal, ABVP, BMS, Vidya Bharati, Seva Bharati, HSS, OFBJP) organise the apparatus of the project.

The co-option pattern of the broader dossier applies in its contemporary Hindu form. The contemporary BJP-led Indian state apparatus under Modi (2014-present) is named explicitly as the contemporary state-captured Hindu apparatus of the argument. The state apparatus operating under the guidance of one branch of the Hindu tradition (the Sangh Parivar Hindutva tradition), with the structural consequences for the religious minorities (the contested 2002 Gujarat violence record. The December 2019 Citizenship Amendment Act explicitly excluding Muslims. The NRC operation producing around 1.9 million people principally Muslims excluded from the Assam register. The 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration on the site of the demolished Babri Masjid; the broader institutional pattern dossiered fully in Chapter 22) and for the dissenting Hindu traditions (the Dalit-Ambedkarite tradition; the secular-liberal Hindu position; the bhakti devotional traditions outside the Hindutva framework). The volume's analytic frame applies symmetrically; the dossier of Chapter 8 is the dossier of the same pattern in its contemporary Hindu form, alongside the parallel contemporary captured apparatuses of the Catholic (Chapter 2 / Chapter 15 Vatican operations). Protestant (Chapter 3 / Chapter 16 American Evangelical operations), Eastern Orthodox (Chapter 4 / Chapter 17 Moscow operations), Sunni Muslim (Chapter 5 / Chapter 18 Saudi operations), Shia Muslim (Chapter 6 / Chapter 19 Iranian operations), and Jewish (Chapter 7 / Chapter 20 Israeli + Chapter 21 Haredi operations) traditions.

Chapter 8 closes here. The dossier of operational mechanics — the specific institutional operations of the contemporary Sangh Parivar / BJP apparatus and the contemporary Hindu Rashtra political-religious project — is the subject of Chapter 22 in Part III. Chapter 8 has mapped the architecture across the full historical arc: § I the emergence and the Vedic-Brahmanical foundation (c. 3300 BCE – c. 600 CE); § II the medieval architecture (c. 600 – c. 1700); § III the modern transition (c. 1700 – 1948); § IV the contemporary apparatuses (1948 – 2026). The seven-tradition Part II foundational dossier (Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish, Hindu) of the major captured traditions of the broader argument is now complete in chapters 2-8.

Part II continues with Chapter 9 — the Buddhist tradition.

---


## Chapter 9. The Buddhist Capture

The Buddhism emerged in the Gangetic plain in the 6th-5th century BCE through the teaching career of Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) and the formation of the early monastic sangha. The tradition spread across the broader Asian region across the subsequent two millennia, producing the three doctrinal traditions (Theravada, Mahayana, Vajrayana) and the contemporary worldwide demographic of around 506 million Buddhists across the major institutional populations of East Asia (Chinese Mahayana), Southeast Asia (Theravada. Thailand, Myanmar, Sri Lanka, Cambodia, Laos), East Asia (Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese traditions), the Tibetan-Himalayan region (Vajrayana — Tibet, Bhutan, Mongolia, the Indian Himalayan region), the post-1956 Indian Navayana / Ambedkarite tradition, and the Western Buddhist diaspora.

The analysis operates across the four-section structure of Part II. § I dossiers the emergence (c. 6th-5th c. BCE – c. 3rd c. CE). § II dossiers the medieval expansion (c. 3rd c. CE – c. 1700 CE). § III dossiers the modern transition (c. 1700 – 1948). § IV dossiers the contemporary apparatuses (1948 – 2026). The operational dossier of the contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses — the Sri Lankan Bodu Bala Sena and the Sinhala-Buddhist state machinery; the Myanmar 969 Movement and the Rohingya persecution; the Thailand Sangha-state machinery — is deferred to Chapter 23 in Part III. § IV maps the configuration.

The co-optation argument of the volume applies in the Buddhist register as follows. The Buddhist tradition has produced major apparatuses operating in the co-opted-by-state configuration across multiple contemporary state architectures. The Sinhala-Buddhist state apparatus of Sri Lanka under the 1972 constitutional settlement of Buddhism as the constitutionally privileged tradition and the contemporary BBS machinery. The Burmese Buddhist-state apparatus operating under the Myanmar military and the 969 Movement, producing the 2016-2017 Rohingya genocide that is the subject of the ongoing International Court of Justice prosecution; the Thai Theravada Sangha operating under the state-supervised order of the Sangha Supreme Council; the post-Khmer-Rouge Cambodian restoration under the Hun dynasty. The volume's analytic frame applies symmetrically to the Buddhist apparatuses as to captured Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish, and Hindu apparatuses already dossiered across Chapters 2-8. The dossier is documentary, not polemical.

The anti-antisemitism guardrail and the broader symmetric application of the analytic frame remain binding. The contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses are dossiered with the same evidentiary standards applied across the previous seven foundational traditions of Part II.

### I. The Emergence and the Early Sangha (c. 6th-5th c. BCE – c. 3rd c. CE)

The Buddhism emerged in the Gangetic plain through the teaching career of **Siddhartha Gautama** (Sanskrit: Siddhārtha Gautama; Pali: Siddhattha Gotama), known as the **Buddha** ("the Awakened One"). The historical figure is the foundation of the tradition. The conventional traditional dating: c. 563 BCE – c. 483 BCE (lifespan 80 years). The contemporary revised scholarly dating: c. 480 BCE – c. 400 BCE. The dating is contested in the scholarly literature; the contemporary mainstream Buddhist studies position narrows toward the later dating per Heinz Bechert, Richard Gombrich, and the broader contemporary scholarly synthesis. The biographical record operates through the textual sources of the broader Buddhist canonical literature, which the contemporary scholarly position treats as late (the earliest surviving textual sources are from the 1st century BCE onwards); the historical Buddha is reconstructed through the synthesis of the textual and archaeological evidence.

Per the traditional biographical account, Siddhartha was born at **Lumbini** (in present-day southern Nepal) to **Śuddhodana** (the leader of the **Shakya clan** of the **Kosala state** in the Gangetic periphery) and **Maya** (who died seven days after the birth; Maya's sister Mahaprajapati raised the child). The wife: **Yaśodharā**. The son: **Rāhula**. Siddhartha left the palace at age 29, after encountering the Four Sights (an aged man, a sick man, a corpse, an ascetic) that revealed the universal nature of human suffering. He undertook six years of ascetic practice under the major Brahmanical-sramana teachers of the period (Alara Kalama, Uddaka Ramaputta), and rejected the ascetic-extreme approach for the **Middle Way**. He attained enlightenment under the **Bodhi tree** at **Bodh Gaya** (in present-day Bihar) at age 35. The institutional name "Buddha" ("Awakened One") and the content of the **Four Noble Truths** (the noble truth of suffering, the cause of suffering, the cessation of suffering, the path leading to the cessation of suffering) and the **Noble Eightfold Path** (right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration) operate as the foundational doctrinal core.

The Buddha taught for 45 years across the Gangetic plain. The first sermon — the **Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta**, "Setting in Motion the Wheel of the Dhamma" — was delivered at the **Deer Park at Sarnath** (near Varanasi) to the five former companions who became the foundation of the early *sangha*. The teaching career produced the development of the lay supporting community and the monastic *sangha* across the major Magadhan and Kosalan states. The royal patrons of the early *sangha* included King **Bimbisara** of Magadha (r. c. 543-491 BCE per traditional dating) and his son King **Ajatashatru** (r. c. 491-461 BCE per traditional dating). The Buddha attained *parinirvana* (the final passing into Nirvana) at **Kushinagar** at age 80 per the traditional account. The four major institutional pilgrimage sites of the Buddha's life: **Lumbini** (birth), **Bodh Gaya** (enlightenment), **Sarnath** (first sermon), **Kushinagar** (parinirvana).

The early *sangha* operated as a peripatetic monastic community organised around the *vinaya* (the monastic disciplinary code) and the *dhamma* (the teaching). The lay community was the supporting apparatus through the *dana* (gift-giving) relationship — Buddhism operates through the reciprocity of the monastic and lay communities. The early *sangha* operated initially as a male-only community; per the traditional account, **Mahaprajapati** (the Buddha's aunt and stepmother) established the female monastic community (the *bhikkhuni sangha*) after seeking ordination from the Buddha through the intermediation of Ananda; the *bhikkhuni* tradition operated under the additional **Eight Garudhammas** (heavy disciplines) as the condition of the female ordination. The history of the *bhikkhuni* lineage is contested across the contemporary Theravada tradition (where the lineage was interrupted in the medieval period and the restoration is a major contemporary contestation) and continuous in the East Asian Mahayana traditions.

The canonical textual tradition operates through the three Buddhist councils of the foundation period. The **First Buddhist Council** (Pali: *Pathama Sangiti*, "First Recitation") was convened at **Rajagriha** (modern Rajgir, Bihar) around one year after the Buddha's *parinirvana* under the sponsorship of King Ajatashatru. The Council was led by **Mahakassapa**; **Ananda** (the Buddha's principal disciple and personal attendant) recited the *sutras*; **Upali** recited the *vinaya*. The purpose: to consolidate the oral textual tradition. The **Second Buddhist Council** (Pali: *Dutiya Sangiti*) was convened at **Vaishali** (Bihar) around 100 years after the *parinirvana* over disputes regarding the *vinaya* (specifically the **Ten Points** of monastic discipline, including the question of whether monks could accept gold and silver). The Council produced the rupture of the early *sangha* into the two principal early schools: the **Sthaviravada** (Sanskrit: "Way of the Elders"; Pali: *Theravada*) and the **Mahasanghika** ("Great Community"). The Theravada tradition traces its institutional lineage to the Sthaviravada. The **Third Buddhist Council** was convened at **Pataliputra** (the Mauryan capital, modern Patna) c. 250 BCE under the governing sponsorship of Emperor Ashoka. The Council consolidated the **Pali Canon** (the *Tipitaka*, "Three Baskets" — the *Sutta Pitaka* of discourses, the *Vinaya Pitaka* of monastic discipline, the *Abhidhamma Pitaka* of philosophical analysis) of the Theravada tradition. The Council was led by **Moggaliputta Tissa**.

Emperor **Ashoka** (r. c. 268 – c. 232 BCE) of the Mauryan dynasty was the foundation of the broader spread of Buddhism. following the **Kalinga War** of c. 262-261 BCE (around 100,000 killed, around 150,000 deported per Ashoka's own Major Rock Edict 13). Ashoka adopted Buddhism, promoted the *dhamma* across the Mauryan Empire, and dispatched Buddhist missions to the broader Indian Ocean and Mediterranean world. The **Ashokan Edicts** — around 33 inscriptions across the Mauryan Empire in Pali, Sanskrit, Greek, and Aramaic — operate as the foundational documentary record of the Mauryan-period Buddhist architecture and as among the earliest surviving Indian written records. Ashoka's Buddhist missions: per the **Mahavamsa** (the Sri Lankan chronicle), Ashoka dispatched nine missions across the broader region. Including the missions of **Mahinda** (Ashoka's son) and **Sanghamitta** (Ashoka's daughter) to Sri Lanka c. 250 BCE, the foundation of the Sri Lankan Buddhist tradition. The missions to Gandhara (modern northwestern Pakistan / eastern Afghanistan). To Suvarnabhumi (probably the Mon-Burmese region or the broader Southeast Asian mainland). To the Hellenistic world (Ashoka named in his edicts as having sent missions to the Greek kingdoms of Antiochus II Theos of the Seleucid Empire, Ptolemy II Philadelphus of Egypt, Antigonus II Gonatas of Macedon, Magas of Cyrene, and Alexander II of Epirus).

The post-Mauryan period operated through the **Sunga dynasty** (185-73 BCE, founded by Pushyamitra Sunga after the assassination of the last Mauryan emperor Brihadratha — dossiered in Chapter 8 § I). The Sunga period is treated as a period of Brahmanical resurgence; the contestation between the Brahmanical and Buddhist traditions occurred across the period. The Buddhist tradition continued through the **Kushan dynasty** (c. 30-375 CE) under the patronage of Emperor **Kanishka** (r. c. 127-150 CE), who convened the **Fourth Buddhist Council** at the **Kushan capital** of Purushapura (modern Peshawar) c. 100 CE. The Kushan-period Buddhist development produced the development of the **Gandharan Buddhist art** tradition (the syncretic Greco-Buddhist artistic style that is the foundational tradition of the broader Buddhist iconographic apparatus) and the major development of Mahayana doctrine across the same period.

### II. The Medieval Buddhist Architecture (c. 3rd c. CE – c. 1700 CE)

Buddhism split throughout the post-Ashokan period into three principal doctrinal-traditions that organise the present configuration. The faith spread across the major Asian regions, producing the South Asian heartland (which itself collapsed across the 12th-13th centuries), the Sri Lankan continuous Theravada, the Southeast Asian Theravada and Mahayana-Hindu syncretic architectures, the East Asian Mahayana architectures, and the Tibetan-Himalayan Vajrayana settlement.

The three doctrinal Buddhist traditions operate as follows. The **Theravada** ("Way of the Elders"; Pali; sometimes pejoratively called **Hinayana** "Lesser Vehicle" by Mahayana, a term contemporary scholars treat as tendentious) operates through the **Pali Canon** (the *Tipitaka*) as the canonical textual base. The monastic *sangha* as the principal apparatus, the *arhat* (the perfected disciple who has attained *nirvana*) as the soteriological goal, and the conservative *Vinaya* observance as the discipline. Theravada is the principal Buddhism of Sri Lanka and the Southeast Asian mainland (Myanmar/Burma, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos).

The **Mahayana** ("Great Vehicle"; Sanskrit) operates through the Mahayana sutras (from c. 1st century BCE onwards. *Prajnaparamita* "Perfection of Wisdom", *Saddharmapundarika* "Lotus Sutra", *Lankavatara*, *Avatamsaka*, *Vimalakirti*, the Pure Land sutras) as the canonical textual base alongside the broader corpus, the *bodhisattva* (the enlightenment-being who postpones final liberation to assist all sentient beings) as the soteriological goal, and the doctrines of *shunyata* (emptiness) and the **Madhyamaka** (founded by Nagarjuna c. 2nd century CE) and **Yogacara** (founded by Asanga and Vasubandhu c. 4th century CE) philosophical traditions as the principal doctrinal apparatus. Mahayana serves as the principal Buddhism of East Asia (China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam) and of the broader Mahayana-derived traditions.

The **Vajrayana** ("Diamond Vehicle"; sometimes **Mantrayana** "Mantra Vehicle" or **Tantrayana** "Tantric Vehicle"; sometimes treated as a sub-school of Mahayana) operates through the Mahayana sutras + the tantras as the textual base. The tantric ritual-yogic methodology (mantras, mandalas, mudras, deity yoga, the *guru* lineage as the principle), and the development from c. 5th-7th century CE in the broader Indian context, with the major consolidation in the Tibetan and Himalayan region from the 8th century onward. Vajrayana is the principal Buddhism of Tibet, Mongolia, Bhutan, the Indian Himalayan region (Ladakh, Sikkim), and the Japanese Shingon school.

Indian Buddhism operated from the 6th-5th c. BCE through to its collapse in the 12th-13th c. CE. The major institutional centres: **Nalanda** (Bihar), the major monastic university, founded under the Gupta dynasty c. 5th century CE, expanded under the Pala dynasty c. 750-1161 CE, the centre of the Mahayana and Vajrayana traditions, with the scale of around 10,000 students and around 2,000 teachers at its peak. The figures associated with Nalanda include the major Mahayana philosophers **Nagarjuna** (c. 150-250 CE, the founder of Madhyamaka), **Asanga** and **Vasubandhu** (4th century CE, the founders of Yogacara), **Dharmakirti** (c. 600-660 CE, the major Buddhist logician), and **Atisha** (982-1054, the major figure who subsequently brought Buddhism back to Tibet). The other major institutional centres: **Vikramashila** (Bihar, founded under the Pala dynasty c. 800 CE); **Odantapuri** (Bihar, founded under the Pala dynasty c. 7th-8th century CE); **Somapura Mahavihara** (modern Paharpur, Bangladesh, founded under the Pala dynasty c. 8th-9th century CE).

The collapse of Indian Buddhism occurred across the 12th-13th centuries. The institutional factors operating in the collapse: the structural destruction of the major monastic universities by the forces of the Mamluk Sultanate under **Bakhtiyar Khalji** c. 1193 (Nalanda, Vikramashila, and Odantapuri were destroyed in the same campaign). The broader decline of Buddhism in India relative to the resurgent Hindu Vedanta traditions (Shankara's Advaita, Ramanuja's Vishishtadvaita, dossiered in Chapter 8 § II); the absorption of Buddhist elements into the Hindu apparatus across the medieval period (the Hindu treatment of the Buddha as the ninth avatar of Vishnu operating as one of the mechanisms of the absorption). The contemporary scholarly position treats the collapse as the synthetic product of the destruction by the Sultanate forces and the decline relative to the Vedanta resurgence; the contestation of the relative weight of the factors continues in the contemporary historiographical literature.

Sri Lanka served as the principal continuous Theravada. the foundation under Ashoka's missions of Mahinda and Sanghamitta c. 250 BCE. The Sri Lankan **Pali Canon** redaction at the **Fourth Buddhist Council** at the **Aluvihara** cave temple c. 29 BCE under King Vattagamani Abhaya (the first written redaction of the Pali Canon). The split into three monastic centres (the **Mahavihara**, the **Abhayagiri Vihara**, and the **Jetavana Vihara**); the major Sri Lankan Theravada commentator **Buddhaghosa** (c. 5th century CE; *Visuddhimagga*, "The Path of Purification"); the Mahavihara operating as the foundation of contemporary Theravada. Sri Lanka across the medieval period developed under the patronage of the Sinhalese royal dynasties. The Anuradhapura period (377 BCE – 1017 CE) under the Mahavihara; the Polonnaruwa period (1056-1232) under the reform of King Parakramabahu I (r. 1153-1186) who unified the three monastic factions; the post-Polonnaruwa period (Dambadeniya, Yapahuwa, Kurunegala, Gampola, Kotte, Kandy) operating under the Mahavihara.

The Burmese Buddhist line operated from the early common era. **Mon-Burmese Theravada** consolidated under King **Anawrahta** (r. 1044-1077) of the **Pagan Kingdom** (c. 849-1297), with the reform under the Mon monk **Shin Arahan**. The Pagan-period architectural florescence (the major temples at Pagan including the Ananda Temple 1090s, the Shwezigon Pagoda, the Thatbyinnyu Temple) with around 10,000 temples constructed across the kingdom; the continuity of the Burmese Theravada across the post-Pagan period (the Ava Kingdom 1364-1555; the Toungoo dynasty 1510-1752; the Konbaung dynasty 1752-1885). The Burmese order operates through the Mahavihara Sri Lankan line; the governing reordination missions across the period connected the Burmese and Sri Lankan Theravada.

The Thai Buddhist line operated from the 13th century. the foundation under the **Sukhothai Kingdom** (c. 1238-1438) with the patronage of King Ramkhamhaeng (r. c. 1279-1298) who established the Thai script and the position of Theravada Buddhism. The consolidation under the **Ayutthaya Kingdom** (1351-1767). The reform under the **Chakri dynasty** (Rama I from 1782, with the major institutional founding of Bangkok as the new capital following the 1767 destruction of Ayutthaya by the Burmese Konbaung forces). The major Thai Theravada reform the **Thammayut Nikaya** founded by Prince Mongkut (subsequently Rama IV, r. 1851-1868); the broader **Maha Nikaya** as the original Theravada.

The Cambodian Buddhist line operated from the early common era. the early Hindu-Buddhist syncretism of the Funan and Chenla kingdoms. The **Khmer Empire** (c. 802-1431) under the Angkorian dynasty with the complex at **Angkor Wat** (built c. 1113-1150 under Suryavarman II as a Vishnu temple, subsequently converted to Buddhist use). The **Bayon** temple at Angkor Thom under **Jayavarman VII** (r. 1181-1218, a Mahayana Buddhist monarch); the post-Angkorian consolidation under Theravada Buddhism from the 13th-14th century. The Laotian Buddhist line operated under the **Lan Xang Kingdom** (1353-1707) under King Fa Ngum (r. 1353-1393); the Theravada apparatus parallel to the Thai and Cambodian traditions.

The Vietnamese Buddhist line operated under the broader Chinese-influenced Mahayana from the early common era, with the **Thien** (Chan/Zen) under the **Ly dynasty** (1009-1225) and the **Tran dynasty** (1225-1400); the **Truc Lam Zen** under King-Monk Tran Nhan Tong (1258-1308).

The Chinese Buddhist line operated from the 1st century CE. the foundation via the Silk Road trade routes. The major translator-monks **Kumarajiva** (344-413, the Kucha-born institutional translator of the Mahayana sutras into Chinese, who translated the *Lotus Sutra*, the *Diamond Sutra*, and the major Madhyamaka treatises of Nagarjuna) and **Xuanzang** (602-664, the Tang dynasty monk who travelled to India 629-645 and brought back the Mahayana corpus, translating around 1,330 fascicles of the Indian Buddhist literature). The development of the major Chinese Buddhist schools across the Sui (581-618) and Tang (618-907) periods. The major Chinese Buddhist schools: **Tiantai** (founded by Zhiyi 538-597, the centre at Mount Tiantai, the *Lotus Sutra* as the canonical text); **Huayan** (founded by Dushun 557-640, consolidated by Fazang 643-712, the *Avatamsaka Sutra* as the canonical text); **Chan** ("Meditation"; Japanese: Zen). Traced through the Indian monk **Bodhidharma** (5th-6th century CE) to the Chinese patriarchs, consolidated under the **Sixth Patriarch Huineng** (638-713), the development into the **Five Houses** of Chan (Linji, Caodong, Yunmen, Fayan, Guiyang); **Jingtu** ("Pure Land"). Centred on the **Buddha Amitabha** and the recitation of the *nianfo* (the Amitabha invocation), consolidated under Tan-luan (476-542), Daochuo (562-645), and Shandao (613-681). The Chinese Buddhist apparatus since the post-Tang period operated through the major persecutions (the **Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution** of 845 under Emperor Wuzong of the Tang dynasty, the destruction of around 4,600 monasteries and the forced laicization of around 260,000 monks and nuns). The continuity under the Song (960-1279) and Yuan (1271-1368) and Ming (1368-1644) and Qing (1644-1912) dynasties, with Chan Buddhism operating as the major contemplative line and Pure Land Buddhism as the major popular line.

The Korean Buddhist line operated from the 4th century CE. the foundation under the Three Kingdoms period (Goguryeo from 372, Baekje from 384, Silla from 528). The consolidation under the Goryeo dynasty (918-1392) with the **Jogye Order** (the contemporary principal Korean Buddhist order, founded as a Seon/Zen tradition); the production of the **Tripitaka Koreana** (the complete Buddhist canon carved on around 81,258 wooden printing blocks under the Goryeo dynasty 1236-1251, the foundation of the contemporary East Asian Buddhist canonical apparatus).

The Japanese Buddhist line operated from the 6th century CE. the foundation under Prince Shotoku (574-622) and the early Yamato court. The development of the **Nara schools** (Sanron, Hossō, Kegon, Ritsu, Kusha, Jōjitsu) c. 7th-8th century; the **Heian-period** development of the **Tendai** school under **Saicho** (767-822, institutional centre at Mount Hiei) and the **Shingon** (Japanese Vajrayana) school under **Kukai** (774-835, institutional centre at Mount Koya). The **Kamakura-period** (1185-1333) development produced the principal contemporary Japanese Buddhist schools: the Pure Land schools. **Jodo-shu** (founded by Honen 1133-1212) and **Jodo Shinshu** (founded by Shinran 1173-1263, the major contemporary Japanese Pure Land); the Zen schools — **Rinzai** (founded by Eisai 1141-1215) and **Soto** (founded by Dogen 1200-1253); **Nichiren Buddhism** (founded by Nichiren 1222-1282), the Japanese Lotus Sutra-centred reform. The post-Kamakura order operated under the patronage of the Ashikaga shogunate (1336-1573) and the Tokugawa shogunate (1603-1868); the Tokugawa-period *terauke* system (the registration of every Japanese household with a local Buddhist temple) consolidated Buddhism as the de facto state religion of the Edo-period Japan.

The Tibetan Buddhist line operated from the 7th century CE. The foundations occurred in two phases. The **First Diffusion** (*snga dar*, c. 7th-9th century CE) operated under the foundation of King **Songtsen Gampo** (c. 605-650, the founder of the Tibetan Empire who introduced Buddhism alongside the indigenous Bön religion). The major development under King **Trisong Detsen** (r. 755-797) with the arrival of the Indian masters **Padmasambhava** (Guru Rinpoche, the foundation of the **Nyingma** "ancient" school) and **Shantarakshita** (the Indian Madhyamaka master) c. 760-780. The founding of **Samye Monastery** (c. 779) as the first Tibetan monastic foundation. The Tibetan translation project of the Indian Buddhist textual corpus operating across the 8th-9th centuries; the **Samye Debate** (792-794) between the Indian Madhyamaka position of Kamalashila (Shantarakshita's disciple) and the Chinese Chan position of the Heshang Moheyan, won by the Indian side per the traditional Tibetan record. The persecution of Buddhism under King Langdarma (r. 838-842) produced the rupture of the First Diffusion.

The **Second Diffusion** (*phyi dar*, from c. 10th-11th century CE) operated under the re-foundation through the Bengali master **Atisha** (982-1054) who arrived in Tibet 1042 at the invitation of the western Tibetan king Yeshe-Ö, the founder of the **Kadam** school. The parallel development of the **Sakya** school (founded c. 1073 by Khön Könchok Gyalpo, institutional centre at Sakya Monastery); the **Kagyu** school (founded c. 1040 by Marpa Lotsawa 1012-1097 with the transmission from the Indian master Naropa, with the major figure being Marpa's disciple **Milarepa** 1052-1135); the re-foundation of the **Nyingma**. The four institutional Tibetan Buddhist schools — Nyingma, Sakya, Kagyu, Gelug — operate as the four schools of contemporary Tibetan Buddhism.

The **Gelug** ("Virtuous") school was founded by **Tsongkhapa** (1357-1419) in the late 14th and early 15th century. the **Ganden Monastery** (founded 1409) near Lhasa as the headquarters. The **Drepung Monastery** (founded 1416) and the **Sera Monastery** (founded 1419) as the parallel major Gelug institutional centres; the *Lamrim* ("Stages of the Path") teaching as the structural doctrinal core, derived  from Atisha's *Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment*; the strict *Vinaya* observance; the **Ganden Tripa** as the institutional head of the Gelug school. The **Dalai Lama** institutional lineage operated from the 14th century. the Dalai Lama treated as the *tulku* (reincarnation) lineage of **Avalokiteshvara** (Tibetan: Chenrezig), the bodhisattva of compassion. The institutional title "Dalai Lama" (Mongolian *dalai* = "ocean") bestowed by the Mongol ruler Altan Khan on the Third Dalai Lama in 1578. The consolidation of the Gelug school as the dominant Tibetan Buddhist apparatus under the **Fifth Dalai Lama** (Ngawang Lobsang Gyatso, 1617-1682). The major consolidator who unified the Tibetan political and religious apparatus from 1642 under the patronage of the Mongol Khoshut leader Gushri Khan, founding the position of the Dalai Lama as the governing ruler of Tibet (the **Ganden Phodrang** government). Tibet as of c. 1700 operated under the Gelug-dominated Ganden Phodrang government, with the parallel continuity of the Nyingma, Sakya, and Kagyu traditions.

The broader medieval Buddhist Asian world as of c. 1700 operated through the configurations summarised: the Sri Lankan Mahavihara Theravada under the Sinhalese royal patronage of the Kandy Kingdom. The Burmese Theravada under the Toungoo dynasty. The Thai Theravada under the Ayutthaya Kingdom. The Cambodian Theravada under the post-Angkorian dynasties. The Laotian Theravada under the Lan Xang Kingdom. The Vietnamese Mahayana under the Le and Nguyen dynasties. The Chinese Mahayana under the Qing dynasty; the Korean Seon-Jogye under the Joseon dynasty (the Confucian state apparatus restricting Buddhism); the Japanese Mahayana-Vajrayana under the Tokugawa shogunate (the *terauke* registration system); the Tibetan Gelug-Vajrayana under the Ganden Phodrang government; the Mongolian Gelug-Vajrayana under the patronage of the Mongol khanates. The configuration entered the modern transition under the pressure of the colonial-Christian encounter across the 18th-19th centuries.

### III. The Modern Buddhist Transition (c. 1700 – 1948)

The Buddhist Asian world underwent the colonial encounter across the 18th-20th centuries. The architectures of the principal Buddhist Asian polities operated through the configurations of European colonial sovereignty (Portuguese, Dutch, British, French) and the residual configurations of the Asian dynasties. The period produced the development of the 19th-century Buddhist revival movements (principally the Sri Lankan revival under Anagarika Dharmapala) and the early 20th-century formation of Buddhist nationalist apparatuses (Sri Lanka, Burma, Thailand) that operate as the foundations of the contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses dossiered in § IV.

The colonial encounter operated across the major Buddhist Asian polities. **Sri Lanka (Ceylon)** experienced the Portuguese colonial period 1505-1658, the Dutch colonial period 1658-1796, and the British colonial period 1796-1948 (with the consolidation under the British Crown from the 1815 Kandyan Convention and the formal British colonial sovereignty over the whole island from 1815). The Sinhalese Theravada tradition operated under the colonial pressure of the Portuguese Catholic, Dutch Reformed Protestant, and British Anglican missionary apparatuses across the period; the Buddhist apparatus experienced major institutional contraction across the Portuguese and Dutch periods. The British period operated under a more accommodative configuration (the British colonial administration recognised the position of Buddhism under the **Kandyan Convention of 1815**, Article 5 of which guaranteed the protection of the religion of the Boodhoo) but produced major pressures through the Christian missions.

**Burma (Myanmar)** experienced the three Anglo-Burmese Wars (1824-1826, 1852-1853, 1885-1886) producing the institutional British annexation of the Burmese kingdom; Burma operated as a British Indian province 1886-1937 and as a separate British colony 1937-1948. The consequence: the rupture of the Burmese-Buddhist state apparatus (the figure of the Burmese monarch had was the patron of the Burmese sangha, and the dissolution of the monarchy in 1885 produced major institutional disorientation in the Burmese Buddhist apparatus).

**Thailand (Siam)** never operated under formal colonial sovereignty. the accommodation with the British and French colonial powers under King **Chulalongkorn** (Rama V, r. 1868-1910) and the development of the Thai constitutional monarchy under the **Chakri reforms**; the state-supervised Theravada sangha architecture under the **Sangha Act of 1902**. The Thai system operated through the absorption of the modern administrative apparatus (postal system, railway network, modern education) while maintaining institutional Buddhist sovereignty.

**Cambodia** operated under the French Protectorate from 1863 within the broader French Indochina apparatus (1887-1954). **Laos** operated under the French Protectorate from 1893 within French Indochina. **Vietnam** operated under the French colonial period 1862-1954 with the Catholic missionary apparatus producing major contestation of the indigenous Mahayana Buddhist tradition.

**Tibet** operated under independence under the Ganden Phodrang government across the period, with the contestation with Qing dynasty China (the Qing sovereignty over Tibet from 1720 under the Amban resident commissioners), the brief British military expedition under **Younghusband** 1903-1904 (the Younghusband Expedition that reached Lhasa, producing the 1904 Convention of Lhasa  establishing British commercial access), and the declaration of Tibetan independence under the **Thirteenth Dalai Lama** (Thubten Gyatso, 1876-1933) in 1913 following the collapse of the Qing dynasty in 1912.

The 19th-century Buddhist revival movements was the principal response to the colonial encounter.

The **Sri Lankan Buddhist revival** served as the principal institutional Buddhist revival movement of the 19th century. Also institutionally characterised as "Buddhist Protestantism" per the contemporary scholarly literature (Gananath Obeyesekere and Richard Gombrich, *Buddhism Transformed*, 1988). the **Panadura Debate** (August 1873) was the structural public debate between the Buddhist monk **Migettuwatte Gunananda Thera** and the Methodist missionary David de Silva at Panadura, conducted across two days and attended by around 10,000 people.
