The position of the Buddhist literature was that Gunananda won the debate; the consequence was the reactivation of the Sri Lankan Buddhist apparatus and the foundation of the broader Buddhist revival.

The intervention of the **Theosophical Society** was the major Western contribution to the Sri Lankan revival. **Henry Steel Olcott** (1832-1907, the American Civil War veteran and Theosophical Society co-founder) and **Helena Petrovna Blavatsky** (1831-1891, the Russian-born Theosophical Society co-founder) arrived in Sri Lanka in May 1880 and took the **Five Precepts** of Buddhism, becoming the first Westerners to formally  convert to the Buddhist tradition. Olcott's *Buddhist Catechism* (1881) was the major institutional consolidating text of the revival, translated into around 20 languages and operating as the foundation of the modern Sri Lankan Buddhist educational apparatus. Olcott promoted the establishment of Buddhist schools across Sri Lanka as the response to the Christian missionary schools, producing the foundation of the contemporary Sri Lankan Buddhist schools.

**Anagarika Dharmapala** (1864-1933, born Don David Hewavitarane) was the principal institutional consolidator of the Sri Lankan Buddhist revival. Dharmapala adopted the title "Anagarika" ("homeless", a quasi-monastic intermediate position between layperson and ordained monk) as the vehicle for his work; he founded the **Maha Bodhi Society** in 1891 with the project of restoring Buddhist control of the **Bodh Gaya site** in India (in Hindu Mahant institutional control at that time, the major contestation that has continued into the contemporary period — the Bodh Gaya Temple Act of 1949 and the subsequent contestations); his positioning of Sri Lankan Buddhism as the foundational tradition of a Sinhala-Buddhist national project that is the foundation of the subsequent 20th-century Sinhala-Buddhist nationalist apparatus; his 1893 attendance at the **World Parliament of Religions** at Chicago (alongside Swami Vivekananda) as the major Buddhist presence on the global stage and the foundation of the broader Western Buddhist orbit. The contemporary scholarly position treats Dharmapala as operating in co-option register that organises the present volume — his positioning of Buddhism as the foundational tradition of an ethno-religious national project is the template of the subsequent Sinhala-Buddhist nationalist apparatus that produced the 1956 Sinhala Only Act, the 1972 constitutional settlement, and the 1983-2009 Sri Lankan civil war dossiered in § IV.

The **Burmese Buddhist revival** operated under British colonial rule. the development of the **Young Men's Buddhist Association** (YMBA, founded 1906 at Rangoon) modelled on the Christian YMCA. The consolidation of the Burmese Buddhist nationalist position under U Ottama (1879-1939), the major Buddhist monk-nationalist who combined Buddhist monastic practice with the Burmese independence movement and was imprisoned by the British colonial administration multiple times across the 1920s-1930s. U Wisara (1889-1929), the major figure who died after a 166-day hunger strike in British colonial detention; the major Burmese Buddhist nationalist project as the foundation of the Burmese independence movement. This movement produced the major Burmese independence figures **Aung San** (1915-1947, the founder of the Burmese independence movement and the father of Aung San Suu Kyi) and **U Nu** (1907-1995, the first post-independence Burmese Prime Minister 1948-1956 and 1957-1958 and 1960-1962).

The **Thai Buddhist reform** under the **Thammayut Nikaya** (founded c. 1833 by Prince Mongkut, subsequently King Rama IV r. 1851-1868) was the reform of the Thai Theravada tradition. Mongkut spent 27 years as a monk (1824-1851) prior to his ascent to the throne. His reform of the Thai Theravada operated through the return to the *Vinaya* observance and the governing rejection of the syncretic Brahmanical-folk-Buddhist popular tradition; the consolidation of the Thammayut Nikaya as the reformist minority alongside the broader **Maha Nikaya** as the original Theravada majority. The consolidation under King Chulalongkorn's reforms produced the modern Thai state-supervised Theravada sangha (the Sangha Act of 1902 established the state-supervised configuration of the Thai sangha that operates to the contemporary period).

The early 20th-century architectures was the foundations of the contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses dossiered in § IV.

The **Sinhala-Buddhist nationalist** movement developed across the 1910s-1940s. The moments: the 1915 anti-Muslim Sinhalese riots (the precursor of the subsequent 20th-21st century Sinhala-Buddhist-Muslim contestations; the colonial British administration's response involved the execution of several Sinhalese nationalist leaders and the internment of D.S. Senanayake, the future first Prime Minister of independent Ceylon); the development of the **Sinhala Maha Sabha** (founded 1936 by S.W.R.D. Bandaranaike, the future Prime Minister and founder of the Sri Lanka Freedom Party); the position of Buddhism as the foundational tradition of the projected Sinhala-Buddhist national state. This movement produced the contemporary Sinhala-Buddhist nationalist apparatus that operates through to the 1948 independence and beyond.

The **Burmese Buddhist nationalist** movement developed in parallel. The major figures: U Ottama, U Wisara, U Saw, Saya San (the leader of the 1930-1932 Saya San Rebellion that operated through Buddhist millenarian symbolism — Saya San claimed the title of "Galon Raja" the bird-king and operated the rebellion through institutional Buddhist millenarian apparatus); the major Burmese independence figures Aung San and U Nu. This movement produced the contemporary Burmese-Buddhist nationalist apparatus that operates through to the 1948 independence and the subsequent post-1948 architectures.

The **Indian Buddhist revival** operated within the broader Indian nationalist movement. The major Buddhist intellectual figures: **B.R. Ambedkar** (1891-1956) — the Dalit institutional intellectual who developed the **Navayana** ("New Vehicle") Buddhist tradition over the 1930s-1950s, culminating in the 14 October 1956 mass conversion at Deekshabhoomi Nagpur with around 365,000 Dalit followers (dossiered in Chapter 8 § IV). The consolidation of the Navayana tradition as the principal Indian Buddhist revival of the post-1956 period; the posthumous publication of Ambedkar's *The Buddha and His Dhamma* (1957) as the principal doctrinal text of the Navayana tradition. The Navayana is the foundation of the contemporary Indian Buddhist demographic (around 8.4 million Indian Buddhists per the 2011 Census).

The Western Buddhist scene was the parallel development. the 19th-century Western scholarly engagement with the Buddhist tradition through the **Pali Text Society** (founded 1881 by T.W. Rhys Davids, the foundation of the modern Western Buddhist textual scholarship); the German Buddhist scholarship of Hermann Oldenberg (*Buddha: His Life, His Doctrine, His Order*, 1881) and Wilhelm Geiger; the development of the Theosophical Society engagement; the early 20th-century development of Western Buddhist organisations. The **Buddhist Society** (London, founded 1907 by Christmas Humphreys as the Buddhist Society of Great Britain and Ireland); the development of the German Buddhist tradition under Paul Dahlke and the Berlin Buddhist Vihara (founded 1924, the first German Buddhist monastic foundation).

The institutional state of the Buddhist Asian world entering the 1948 watershed: Sri Lanka under late British colonial administration with the 1948 independence imminent. Burma under late British colonial administration with the 1948 independence imminent. Thailand under the post-1932 constitutional monarchy and the Chakri dynasty. Cambodia and Laos under French Indochina with the post-WWII independence movements emerging. Vietnam under French Indochina with the emergence of the Viet Minh. Tibet under the Ganden Phodrang government with the pressure of the post-1949 People's Republic of China apparatus; the East Asian Buddhist architectures under various configurations (post-imperial Republican China; the post-WWII restoration of the Japanese tradition; the post-1945 division of Korea; the post-1945 Mongolian People's Republic under Soviet supervision). The institutional transition to the contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses operated through the post-1948 settlement dossiered in § IV.

### IV. The Contemporary Buddhist Apparatuses (1948 – 2026)

The contemporary Buddhist apparatuses operate across five principal architectures: the compromised Sinhala-Buddhist state of Sri Lanka; the Burmese-Buddhist state of Myanmar producing the 2016-2017 Rohingya persecution; the co-opted Thai Theravada Sangha-state; the post-Khmer-Rouge Cambodian restoration; Tibet-in-exile under the 14th Dalai Lama and the Central Tibetan Administration. The parallel architectures include the Navayana / Ambedkarite tradition in India; the East Asian Mahayana traditions (Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese); and the Western Buddhist diaspora. The operational dossier of the contemporary captured Buddhist state apparatuses is the subject of Chapter 23 in Part III. § IV maps the configuration.

Sri Lanka achieved independence from British colonial sovereignty on **4 February 1948**. The post-independence Sri Lankan state operated through the development of the Sinhala-Buddhist national project. The **Sinhala Only Act of 1956** under Prime Minister **S.W.R.D. Bandaranaike** (the founder of the Sri Lanka Freedom Party) established Sinhala as the sole official language, marginalising the Tamil-speaking minority (around 25% of the population at the time). The **1972 Constitution** entrenched the Buddhist tradition. Article 9: "The Republic of Sri Lanka shall give to Buddhism the foremost place and accordingly it shall be the duty of the state to protect and foster the Buddha Sasana." The consequence: Buddhism as the state-supervised tradition with constitutional privileged position — the configuration that is the contemporary captured Sinhala-Buddhist state apparatus dossiered in Chapter 23.

The **Sri Lankan Civil War** (1983-2009) operated between the Sinhala-Buddhist-majority government and the Tamil minority militant organisations (principally the **Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam** (LTTE), founded 1976 by Velupillai Prabhakaran). The starting point: **Black July 1983**, the anti-Tamil pogrom following the LTTE's attack on the Sri Lankan Army that produced around 400-3,000 Tamil civilian deaths. Across the 26-year war: the major Buddhist monastic apparatuses operated in support of the Sinhala-Buddhist state position against the Tamil minority. The war ended in May 2009 under President **Mahinda Rajapaksa** with the military defeat of the LTTE; the casualty estimates for the final months range from 7,000 (UN initial estimate) to over 40,000 (UN Panel of Experts 2011) Tamil civilian deaths.

The post-2009 period produced the development of the **Bodu Bala Sena** (BBS, "Buddhist Power Force") under **Galagoda Aththe Gnanasara Thera** in 2012 as the principal contemporary Sri Lankan Buddhist nationalist organisation. the pivot of the BBS from the post-civil-war anti-Tamil position toward an anti-Muslim position. The major moments include the 2013-2014 anti-Muslim Aluthgama riots; the 2018 Ampara and Digana anti-Muslim violence; the response to the April 2019 **Easter bombings** by the National Tawheed Jamaat (around 270 deaths, the pretext for further anti-Muslim mobilisation). The 2014 institutional pact between BBS under Gnanasara and the Myanmar **969 Movement** under **Ashin Wirathu** consolidated the cross-border Theravada Buddhist nationalist alliance.

Myanmar achieved independence from British colonial sovereignty on **4 January 1948**. The post-independence Burmese state operated under a series of configurations. The U Nu civilian government (1948-1962) promoted Buddhism through the **State Religion Promotion Act of 1961** that established Buddhism as the state religion (subsequently reversed under the post-1962 military government). The military coup of **General Ne Win** on 2 March 1962 established the Burmese socialist military government; the configuration across the 1962-1988 period operated under the Burma Socialist Programme Party. The **8888 Uprising** of 8 August 1988 was the major pro-democracy movement. Suppressed by the State Law and Order Restoration Council (SLORC, subsequently State Peace and Development Council, SPDC) with the emergence of **Aung San Suu Kyi** (born 1945, daughter of independence figure Aung San) as the principal pro-democracy figure and the **National League for Democracy** (NLD) as the principal pro-democracy political party. The **Saffron Revolution** of August-September 2007 was the major Buddhist monastic pro-democracy mobilisation, suppressed by the military government with around 30-200 deaths and around 6,000 detentions.

The transition to quasi-democratic government under President **Thein Sein** (2011-2016) and the NLD government under Aung San Suu Kyi (de facto leader as State Counsellor 2016-2021) produced the present configuration. The **2021 military coup** on 1 February 2021 under Senior General **Min Aung Hlaing** suspended the elected government and re-established direct military rule under the **State Administration Council** (SAC). The Myanmar civil war from 2021 onward is the ongoing contestation between the SAC military government and the opposition forces (the National Unity Government, the People's Defence Force, the major ethnic armed organisations).

The **Rohingya persecution** is the major contemporary captured Buddhist-state operation. the **Rohingya** are a Muslim ethnic group of around 1.4 million (pre-2017) operating principally in the Rakhine State of western Myanmar. The position of the Myanmar state: the Rohingya are denied citizenship under the **1982 Citizenship Law** and treated as illegal Bangladeshi immigrants; the position of the international scholarly and human rights apparatuses: the Rohingya have historical presence in Rakhine going back to at least the 15th century. The institutional **969 Movement** under the monk **Ashin Wirathu** (born 1968) emerged in 2012 as the principal institutional Buddhist-nationalist anti-Muslim mobilisation. The 9-6-9 numerological reference (9 attributes of the Buddha + 6 attributes of the dhamma + 9 attributes of the sangha) operating in opposition to the Islamic 786 numerological reference. The institutional promotion of anti-Muslim boycotts; the development of the broader **Ma Ba Tha** organisation (Association for the Protection of Race and Religion) in 2014 as the principal Buddhist-nationalist legislative-political apparatus. The **2012 Rakhine State riots** (around 200 deaths, around 100,000 Rohingya displaced) was the starting point of the contemporary persecution.

The **2016-2017 military clearance operations** in northern Rakhine State following the Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army (ARSA) attacks of October 2016 and August 2017 produced the major ethnic cleansing operation. The consequences: around **700,000 Rohingya forced into Bangladesh** (the major institutional refugee population in the Cox's Bazar refugee camps, growing to around 1 million total Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh); around 25,000 Rohingya killed per the Médecins Sans Frontières estimate; institutional reports of mass rape, village burning, and other atrocities by the Myanmar military (the *Tatmadaw*). The response: the **UN Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar** (2017-2019) found that the Myanmar military operations against the Rohingya had been conducted with **"genocidal intent"** and recommended the structural prosecution of senior Myanmar military officers including Min Aung Hlaing. The **Gambia v. Myanmar** case before the International Court of Justice (filed November 2019, ongoing) is the vehicle for the genocide prosecution. The **International Criminal Court** opened a Rohingya investigation in November 2019. The position of the Myanmar state: the operations were directed against ARSA militants, not against the Rohingya population as such.

The position of **Aung San Suu Kyi**: as State Counsellor, she  defended the Myanmar military operations before the International Court of Justice in December 2019, producing major institutional damage to her international reputation as a Buddhist-democratic icon. Her position after the 2021 coup operates separately; she remains under detention as of May 2026.

Thailand operates as the Theravada Buddhist state. The Thai Sangha is supervised by the **Sangha Supreme Council** (Mahatherasamakhom) under the **Supreme Patriarch** (Sangharaja); the order is regulated by the Sangha Act of 1902 (subsequently revised); the state apparatus operates through the **National Office of Buddhism** (formerly the Department of Religious Affairs). The Thammayut Nikaya (the 19th-century reform tradition founded by Mongkut/Rama IV) operates as the reformist minority; the Maha Nikaya operates as the original Theravada majority. The Buddhist tradition operates as the state religion in practice; the post-1932 constitution does not formally establish Buddhism as the official state religion, but the privileged position is entrenched.

The figure of **Kittivuddho Bhikkhu** (1936-2017) produced a major institutional Buddhist nationalist position in the 1970s — the justification of anti-communist violence ("killing communists is not killing humans") that operated through the broader anti-communist movement of the 1976 Thammasat University massacre and the subsequent military coup. The contemporary Thai Sangha operates under state supervision with the broader system of the post-2014 military government and the post-2019 constitution under the position of King Vajiralongkorn (Rama X, r. 2016-present).

Cambodia operated under the Khmer Empire through to 1431; under the post-Angkorian Theravada order through to the 1863 French Protectorate; under French colonial rule 1863-1953; under the post-1953 Sihanouk-led independence and the Cambodian Buddhist tradition. The **Khmer Rouge** (the Communist Party of Kampuchea under **Pol Pot**) operated from 17 April 1975 to 7 January 1979. The consequences for the Cambodian Buddhist tradition: the Khmer Rouge targeted the Buddhist Sangha; around 60,000 monks were killed or forced to disrobe; the major institutional monasteries were destroyed; the Cambodian Buddhist tradition was suspended. The post-1979 Vietnamese-installed People's Republic of Kampuchea (1979-1989) and the subsequent State of Cambodia (1989-1993) operated under restricted Buddhist conditions; Buddhist restoration occurred under the 1993 Constitution following the UN-supervised transition (UNTAC, 1992-1993). The contemporary configuration: the Cambodian Buddhist tradition operates under the Sangha Supreme Council; the state apparatus operates under the Hun Sen / Hun Manet dynasty (Hun Sen as Prime Minister 1985-2023; Hun Manet from 2023).

The **Chinese annexation of Tibet** operated from 1950 onwards. the October 1950 People's Liberation Army invasion of Tibet (the **Battle of Chamdo**) established Chinese control over eastern Tibet. The **Seventeen Point Agreement** of 23 May 1951 between the Chinese government and the Tibetan government formalised the Chinese position (the position of the Tibetan government-in-exile: the Agreement was signed under duress and is invalid). The **1959 Tibetan Uprising** (10 March 1959) at Lhasa produced the governing flight of the **14th Dalai Lama** (Tenzin Gyatso, born 6 July 1935) to India on 31 March 1959. Around 80,000 Tibetans followed into exile across the subsequent period.

The development of the **Tibetan Government-in-Exile** (the Central Tibetan Administration, CTA) at **Dharamsala** (Himachal Pradesh, India) under the leadership of the 14th Dalai Lama produced the present configuration of the Tibetan exile community across India, Nepal, Bhutan, and the broader Tibetan diaspora. The institutional Chinese position of Tibet throughout the post-1959 period: the development of the **Tibet Autonomous Region** (TAR, established 1965); the Cultural Revolution period (1966-1976) producing major institutional destruction of Tibetan Buddhist monasteries and apparatus (the original Ganden Monastery was destroyed by Chinese troops); the post-1976 partial restoration of the Tibetan Buddhist architecture under Chinese state supervision.

The institutional issue of the Dalai Lama succession is the major contemporary contestation. The 14th Dalai Lama is 90 years old as of May 2026. The position of the Chinese government is that the next Dalai Lama will be selected per the **Golden Urn** procedure under Chinese state supervision (the configuration of state-supervised tulku selection that the Chinese state has progressively institutionalised since the 1995 Panchen Lama dispute). The position of the 14th Dalai Lama and the CTA is that the reincarnation will be selected through the traditional Tibetan Buddhist procedures and may be born outside the Chinese state's jurisdiction. The **Panchen Lama dispute**: the 10th Panchen Lama Choekyi Gyaltsen died in 1989. The 14th Dalai Lama identified **Gedhun Choekyi Nyima** (born 1989) as the 11th Panchen Lama in May 1995. The Chinese government rejected the identification, detained Gedhun Choekyi Nyima (who has not been seen publicly since, treated by human rights organisations as the world's longest-standing political prisoner) and installed **Gyaltsen Norbu** as the state-recognised 11th Panchen Lama in December 1995.

The Tibetan Buddhist tradition in exile serves as the principal continuous form of the broader Tibetan Buddhist tradition; the Dalai Lama's position (1959-present) is the structural centre of the contemporary tradition; the four schools (Nyingma, Sakya, Kagyu, Gelug) operates across the broader diaspora.

The **Navayana / Ambedkarite movement** is the principal contemporary Indian Buddhist movement. Ambedkar's institutional 14 October 1956 conversion at **Deekshabhoomi Nagpur** with around 365,000 Dalit followers (dossiered in Chapter 8 § IV); Ambedkar's death on 6 December 1956; the posthumous publication of Ambedkar's *The Buddha and His Dhamma* (1957) as the principal doctrinal text of the Navayana tradition. The contemporary scale: per the 2011 Census of India, around 8.4 million Buddhists in India (around 0.7% of the population), the majority being the Dalit-Buddhist Navayana community (principally in Maharashtra, where Buddhists constitute around 6% of the state population per the 2011 census). the Navayana movement operates outside the Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana traditions. The position of the Navayana tradition treats Buddhism as a rationalist-egalitarian philosophical-social order explicitly opposed to the Brahmanical Hindu caste architecture.

The architecture of the **Western Buddhist diaspora** developed across the 19th-21st centuries through multiple vectors. The post-WWII development of the Zen Buddhist Western current operated under the vectors of D.T. Suzuki (1870-1966, the major institutional translator of Zen into English) and the Beat Generation engagement (Allen Ginsberg, Jack Kerouac, Gary Snyder). The post-1959 development of the Tibetan Buddhist Western current operated under the vectors of the 14th Dalai Lama, **Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche** (1939-1987, the founder of the Shambhala tradition), Tarthang Tulku, and others. The post-1965 development of the Theravada Western current operated under the vectors of the **Insight Meditation Society** (founded 1975 by Joseph Goldstein, Sharon Salzberg, and Jack Kornfield) and the broader Western Vipassana tradition.

The development of the **mindfulness** apparatus across the 1970s-2010s operated under the vectors of **Jon Kabat-Zinn's Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction** (developed at the University of Massachusetts Medical School from 1979) and the broader corporate mindfulness apparatus. The consolidation that the contemporary scholarly position (Ronald Purser's *McMindfulness*, 2019; David Forbes's *Mindfulness and Its Discontents*, 2019) treats as the governing commodification and dehistoricisation of the Buddhist meditative tradition. The contemporary Western Buddhist demographic: around 5-7 million Western Buddhists worldwide.

The worldwide Buddhist demographic operates as follows. The worldwide Buddhist population is estimated at around **506 million** (Pew Research Center 2020 figure), around 6.6% of the global population. The major populations: **China** (around 244 million Buddhists, around 18% of the population, principally the Mahayana tradition); **Thailand** (around 64 million, around 93% of the population, Theravada); **Myanmar** (around 38 million, around 88% of the population pre-2021, Theravada); **Japan** (around 70 million in nominal institutional terms, around 36% of the population, Mahayana. Japanese Buddhist identity is fluid); **Vietnam** (around 14 million, around 16% of the population, principally Mahayana); **Sri Lanka** (around 14 million, around 70% of the population. Theravada); **Cambodia** (around 14 million, around 97% of the population, Theravada); **South Korea** (around 11 million, around 22% of the population, principally Seon-Jogye Mahayana); **India** (around 8.4 million, around 0.7% of the population. Principally Navayana plus Tibetan-exile and Himalayan populations). **Mongolia** (around 1.7 million, around 53% of the population, Vajrayana); **Laos** (around 5 million, around 66% of the population, Theravada); **Bhutan** (around 600,000, around 75% of the population, Vajrayana); **United States** (around 4 million, around 1.2% of the population); plus the broader Buddhist diaspora across the Asian and Western worlds.

The summary across § IV. The contemporary Buddhist tradition from 1948 to 2026 operates through five principal state-captured machines (Sri Lankan Sinhala-Buddhist state under the 1972 constitutional settlement and the contemporary BBS movement. Myanmar Buddhist-state operating under the military and the 969 Movement and producing the 2016-2017 Rohingya genocide that is the subject of the ongoing International Court of Justice prosecution. Thai Theravada Sangha operating under the state-supervised order of the Sangha Supreme Council; post-Khmer-Rouge Cambodian restoration under the Hun dynasty; Tibet-in-exile under the 14th Dalai Lama and the Central Tibetan Administration, with the contestation against the Chinese state apparatus over the contemporary Tibetan Buddhist apparatus and the future succession of the 14th Dalai Lama). The parallel architectures include the Navayana/Ambedkarite tradition in India (around 8.4 million as of 2011 census); the East Asian Mahayana traditions; the Western Buddhist diaspora (around 5-7 million worldwide); the worldwide Buddhist demographic of around 506 million.

The co-option pattern of the broader dossier applies in its contemporary Buddhist form. The contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses — the Sri Lankan Sinhala-Buddhist state under the BBS; the Myanmar Buddhist-state producing the Rohingya genocide; the Thai Theravada Sangha-state — are named explicitly as the contemporary captured Buddhist apparatuses of the argument, with the dossier deferred to Chapter 23 in Part III. The Tibetan tradition in exile operates differently — as the contested-against-state tradition operating in opposition to the Chinese state apparatus. Navayana/Ambedkarite Buddhism operates differently — as the egalitarian-rationalist tradition operating in opposition to the Brahmanical caste order. Western Buddhism operates differently — as the commodification tradition operating through the mindfulness apparatus.

Chapter 9 closes here. The configuration has been mapped across the full historical arc: § I emergence c. 6th-5th c. BCE through c. 3rd c. CE; § II medieval expansion c. 3rd c. CE through c. 1700 CE; § III modern transition c. 1700 – 1948; § IV contemporary apparatuses 1948 – 2026. The eight-tradition Part II foundational dossier (Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish, Hindu, Buddhist) of the major captured traditions of the broader argument is now complete in Chapters 2-9.

Part II continues with Chapter 10 — the Sikh tradition.

---


## Chapter 10. The Sikh Capture

The Sikh tradition emerged in the Punjab region of South Asia in the late 15th century through the teaching of Guru Nanak Dev Ji (1469-1539) and the foundation of the early Sikh community. The tradition operates across the ten Sikh Gurus (1469-1708), the post-Guru-Gobind-Singh Misl confederation period (1708-1799), the Sikh Empire under Maharaja Ranjit Singh (1799-1849), the British colonial Punjab (1849-1947), the post-Partition Indian Punjab (1947-present), and the global Sikh diaspora (principally United Kingdom, Canada, United States, Australia). The contemporary worldwide Sikh demographic is around 25-30 million, principally concentrated in the Indian state of Punjab (around 16 million Sikhs, around 58% of the Punjab population — the only Sikh-majority Indian state).

The evidence operates across the four-section structure of Part II. § I dossiers the emergence (c. 1469 – 1708). § II dossiers the medieval architecture (1708 – 1849). § III dossiers the modern transition (1849 – 1947). § IV dossiers the contemporary apparatuses (1947 – 2026). The operational dossier of the contemporary captured Sikh apparatus — the Akal Takht, the SGPC, the Akali Dal political vehicle, and the contemporary Khalistan diasporic mobilisation — is deferred to Chapter 24 in Part III. § IV maps the apparatus.

The co-optation argument of the volume applies in the Sikh register as follows. The Sikh tradition has produced major institutional apparatuses operating across multiple configurations — the Sikh Empire under Maharaja Ranjit Singh as the sovereign Sikh state architecture of 1799-1849. The post-1925 Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee (SGPC) and Shiromani Akali Dal as the post-Gurdwara-Reform-Act Sikh administrative-political apparatuses; the post-1984 Khalistan diasporic mobilisation operating principally in Canada, the United Kingdom, and the United States; and the broader institutional Sikh community position vis-à-vis the contemporary Indian state apparatus in the post-1984 period. The volume's analytic frame applies symmetrically — the Sikh tradition operates differently from the seven foundational co-opted apparatuses dossiered in Chapters 2-7 (Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish, Hindu) and the contemporary captured-by-state Buddhist apparatuses dossiered in Chapter 9 (Sri Lankan, Burmese, Thai). The Sikh tradition has been the contemporary persecuted minority operating against the seized-by-state Hindu apparatus of the Indian state under the Modi government (cross-reference Chapter 8 § IV and the broader operational dossier of Chapter 22 on the contemporary Sangh Parivar / BJP apparatus). The dossier is documentary, not polemical.

The anti-antisemitism guardrail and the broader symmetric application of the analytic frame remain binding. The contemporary captured Sikh apparatuses are dossiered with the same evidentiary standards applied across the previous nine foundational traditions of Part II.

### I. The Emergence and the Ten Gurus (c. 1469 – 1708)

The Sikhs operate through the ten Sikh Gurus across the 239-year period from 1469 to 1708, followed by the closure of the human Guru line at the death of Guru Gobind Singh Ji on 7 October 1708 and the elevation of the **Guru Granth Sahib** as the eternal Guru. The ten human Gurus per the Sikh tradition.

**Guru Nanak Dev Ji** (15 April 1469 – 22 September 1539) is the founder. Born at Talwandi (modern Nankana Sahib, Punjab, Pakistan) to Kalyan Chand Mehta (father) and Tripta Devi (mother), a Hindu merchant family. Established the foundation of Sikh teaching through the *udasis* (extensive travels across South Asia, with traditional accounts including travels to Tibet, Mecca, Medina, and Baghdad). Founded the first Sikh community at **Kartarpur** (on the Ravi River, modern Pakistan) c. 1521 with the *langar* (the communal kitchen) and the *sangat* (the spiritual community). Contributed 974 hymns to the eventual Guru Granth Sahib including the major prayers **Japji Sahib**, **Asa di Var**, and **Sidh Gosht**. The core teaching: *Ik Onkar* ("One God"), with the threefold practice of *Naam Japna* (remembrance of the divine name), *Kirat Karni* (honest livelihood), and *Vand Chakna* (sharing with others). Appointed his disciple Bhai Lehna as successor (the second Guru, Angad).

**Guru Angad Dev Ji** (Guru 1539-1552) standardised the **Gurmukhi script** as the written architecture of the Sikh tradition; institutionalised the *langar* practice with his wife Mata Khivi. **Guru Amar Das Ji** (Guru 1552-1574) consolidated the *langar*; established the *manji* system (the missionary architecture across 22 regional centres); rejected the practice of *sati* (widow burning) and supported widow remarriage. **Guru Ram Das Ji** (Guru 1574-1581) founded the centre at **Amritsar** (originally Ramdaspur); excavated the **Amrit Sarovar** (the sacred pool) that is the centre of the **Harmandir Sahib** (the Golden Temple) complex.

**Guru Arjan Dev Ji** (Guru 1581-1606) completed the construction of the **Harmandir Sahib** at Amritsar; **compiled the Adi Granth** in 1604 (the first canonical compilation of the Sikh scriptures, the foundational textual base of the tradition); founded the town of Tarn Taran Sahib near Goindwal Sahib. He became **the first great martyr in Sikh history** — executed at Lahore on 30 May 1606 under the order of the Mughal Emperor **Jahangir** (r. 1605-1627). The Sikh name: "Shaheedan-de-Sartaj" (the crown of martyrs).

**Guru Hargobind Sahib Ji** (Guru 1606-1644), the "soldier saint," introduced the concept of **Miri-Piri** (the balance between temporal sovereignty *miri* and spiritual sovereignty *piri*) symbolised by the two swords. He established the **Akal Takht** ("Throne of the Timeless One") at Amritsar in 1606 as the temporal seat of Sikh authority complementing the Harmandir Sahib as the spiritual seat. Hargobind institutionalised the Sikh military tradition and waged the early Sikh-Mughal wars against the Mughal Emperors Jahangir and Shah Jahan. **Guru Har Rai Ji** (Guru 1644-1661) maintained military preparedness while operating as a man of peace; gave shelter to Dara Shikoh, subsequently persecuted by Aurangzeb. **Guru Har Krishan Ji** (Guru 1661-1664) was the youngest Sikh Guru, ascending at age 5 and dying of smallpox at age 8.

**Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji** (Guru 1665-1675) was the **second martyred Guru** — executed at Delhi on 11 November 1675 under the order of the Mughal Emperor **Aurangzeb** (r. 1658-1707) for refusing to convert to Islam and for defending the religious freedom of the Kashmiri Pandit Hindu community against Aurangzeb's pressure for conversion. The Sikh name: "Hind di Chadar" (the shield of Hind).

**Guru Gobind Singh Ji** (Guru 1675-1708) was the tenth and final human Sikh Guru. Born 22 December 1666 at Patna (Bihar). He founded the **Khalsa** (the order of initiated Sikhs) on **Vaisakhi day, 30 March 1699** at Anandpur with the **Panj Pyare** ("Five Beloved Ones"), the *khande ki pahul* initiation (the *amrit* ceremony with the double-edged broadsword *khanda*), the adoption of "Singh" (lion) for men and "Kaur" (princess) for women, and the **Five Ks** (the *kakaars* — *kesh* uncut hair, *kara* steel bracelet, *kangha* wooden comb, *kirpan* sword, *kachera* military undergarments). He fought four major battles against Aurangzeb's forces and the allied Rajput rulers. He lost his father (Guru Tegh Bahadur), his mother, and all four sons (the *Chaar Sahibzaade* — Ajit Singh and Jujhar Singh killed at the Battle of Chamkaur 7 December 1705; Zorawar Singh and Fateh Singh bricked alive at Sirhind on 26 December 1705 under the order of the Sirhind faujdar Wazir Khan). He wrote the **Zafarnama** ("Epistle of Victory") to Aurangzeb. He appointed **Banda Singh Bahadur** as the Sikh military leader to confront the Mughal apparatus. He closed the line of human Sikh Gurus at his death on **7 October 1708** at Nanded (Maharashtra) and elevated the **Guru Granth Sahib** as the eternal Guru — the command "Sab Sikhan ko hukam hai Guru Manyo Granth" ("All Sikhs are commanded to take the Granth as Guru").

Sikhism emerged from the broader **Bhakti-Sant** tradition of medieval North India (cross-referenced in Chapter 8 § II) — the broader devotional movement that operated through the vectors of Kabir (c. 1440-1518), Ravidas (c. 1450-1520), Namdev (c. 1270-1350), and others. The Adi Granth incorporates the writings of these Bhakti-Sant figures (Kabir's 541 hymns; the contributions of Ravidas, Namdev, Sheikh Farid, and others). The contemporary scholarly position on the relationship: the Sikh tradition developed as a distinct tradition that incorporates the Bhakti-Sant elements while operating as a separate faith with its own scripture, doctrine, and structure. The traditional Sikh position treats the tradition as a distinct revealed faith from its foundation under Guru Nanak; the scholarly position (W.H. McLeod's *The Evolution of the Sikh Community* 1976 and others) treats the early tradition as emerging from the broader Bhakti-Sant landscape and developing into a distinct configuration across the 16th-18th centuries.

The **Guru Granth Sahib** (also called the **Adi Granth**, "First Book") is the canonical Sikh scripture. It was first compiled by Guru Arjan in 1604; expanded by Guru Gobind Singh c. 1705-1706 with the incorporation of the hymns of Guru Tegh Bahadur; elevated as the eternal Guru on 7 October 1708 by Guru Gobind Singh's command at Nanded. Composed in Gurmukhi script. Contains around 5,894 hymns (*shabads*) across 1,430 pages by 36 contributors — the six Sikh Gurus who contributed (Nanak, Angad, Amar Das, Ram Das, Arjan, Tegh Bahadur — Hargobind, Har Rai, Har Krishan, and Gobind Singh did not contribute hymns), the Bhakti-Sant figures (Kabir, Ravidas, Namdev, Sheikh Farid, Jaidev, Trilochan, and others), and the Sikh *bhatts* (the bardic composers).

### II. The Medieval Sikh Architecture (1708 – 1849)

The period from the death of Guru Gobind Singh in 1708 to the British annexation of the Punjab in 1849 is the major consolidation period of the Sikh military-political tradition. Three sub-periods organise the architecture: the post-Banda Bahadur period of intense Mughal-Afghan pressure (1708-c. 1748); the Misl confederation period (c. 1748-1799); the Sikh Empire under Maharaja Ranjit Singh (1799-1849).

**Banda Singh Bahadur** (1670-1716) was the military leader appointed by Guru Gobind Singh in 1708. He was the Sikh military commander against the Mughal apparatus across the 1708-1715 period. He captured Sirhind (the site of the bricking alive of Guru Gobind Singh's two younger sons) in May 1710 in the Battle of Chappar Chiri, executing the Sirhind faujdar Wazir Khan. He was captured by Mughal forces in December 1715 and executed at Delhi on 9 June 1716 by the Mughal Emperor Farrukhsiyar.

The post-Banda Bahadur period operated under intense Mughal-Afghan pressure across the 18th century. The major episodes: the **Lesser Sikh Holocaust** (*Chhota Ghallughara*) of 1746 under the Mughal governor of Lahore Yahiya Khan, producing around 7,000-10,000 Sikh deaths; the **Greater Sikh Holocaust** (*Wadda Ghallughara*) of 5 February 1762 under the Afghan invader Ahmad Shah Abdali (Durrani), producing around 30,000 Sikh deaths in a single day. The Sikh community survived the period through the architecture of mobile military encampments and the broader Punjabi population's accommodation of Sikh resistance.

The **Misl** apparatus was the post-Banda-Bahadur Sikh military-political configuration across the c. 1716-1799 period. The architecture: 12 Sikh military confederacies (the *Bhangi*, *Ahluwalia*, *Sukerchakia*, *Kanaiya*, *Nakai*, *Phulkian*, *Faizullapuria*, *Karorsingh*, *Ramgarhia*, *Dallewalia*, *Nishanwalia*, *Shaheed* Misls) operating across the Punjab region with overlapping territorial control. The Misls operated through the **Dal Khalsa** ("Army of the Khalsa") as the broader military coordination apparatus. The Misl period operated through the major engagements against the Afghan Durrani Empire (the Afghan-Sikh Wars across the 1748-1837 period) and the residual Mughal apparatus, producing the Sikh territorial consolidation across the Punjab. By the late 18th century, the Misls controlled the major part of the historic Punjab region.

**Maharaja Ranjit Singh** (1780-1839) was the major consolidator of the Sikh Empire. He was born to the Sukerchakia Misl chief Maha Singh; ascended as Sardar of the Sukerchakia Misl in 1792. He **united the 12 Sikh Misls and captured Lahore in 1799**, founding the Sikh Empire. He was formally crowned **Maharaja of Punjab on 12 April 1801** at Lahore Fort, the foundation moment of the sovereign Sikh state. Capital at Lahore.

The Sikh Empire (1799-1849) operated under a distinctive multi-religious configuration. The four provinces: Lahore, Multan, Peshawar, Kashmir. The reach extended from Gilgit and Tibet in the north to the deserts of Sindh in the south, from the Khyber Pass in the west to the Sutlej River in the east. The configuration was a multi-religious empire with Punjabi armies composed of Sikhs, Muslims, and Hindus, and commanders drawn from all religious communities. The Maharaja's wazirs (chief ministers) included Hindu Dogra figures (Dhian Singh Dogra, wazir 1818-1839) and Muslim figures alongside Sikh ministers.

The **Sikh Khalsa Army** under Ranjit Singh — at its greatest height in 1838-1839, around 120,000 men. The composition: around 5,500 *Fauj-i-Khas* elites (the French-trained brigade under French commanders Jean-François Allard, Jean-Baptiste Ventura). Around 60,000 *Fauj-i-Ain* regulars (the regular army of around 45,000 infantry, 10,000 cavalry, 5,000 artillery); around 50,000 *Fauj-i-Be Qawaid* irregulars (the irregular forces including *Jagirdari* levies, *Fauj-i-Kilajat* fortress garrisons, and *Ghorcharas* cavalry). The motto: *Deg Tegh Fateh* ("Cauldron, Sword, Victory" — prosperity in peace and victory in war).

The major engagements of the Sikh Empire: the Afghan-Sikh Wars — the **Battle of Attock** 13 July 1813 (the first significant Sikh victory over the Durrani Empire). The **Battle of Multan** May-June 1818. The conquest of Peshawar 1834; the conquest of Kashmir 1819 with the acquisition of the **Koh-i-Noor diamond** from the Afghan Shah Shuja; the **1809 Treaty of Amritsar** with the British East India Company that fixed the Sutlej River as the eastern boundary of the Sikh Empire; the **Sino-Sikh War** of May 1841 – August 1842 against the Qing dynasty over the Ladakh-Tibet contestation; the **Nepal-Sikh War** of March-August 1809. The institutional military commanders included the major Sikh general **Hari Singh Nalwa** (1791-1837), the Sikh Khalsa Army commander who  led the major Afghan-Sikh campaigns.

The **death of Maharaja Ranjit Singh on 27 June 1839** produced the rupture of the Sikh Empire. The successor architecture across the 1839-1849 period operated through rapid dynastic instability: Maharaja Kharak Singh (1839-1840); Maharaja Nau Nihal Singh (1840); Maharani Chand Kaur (regent 1840-1841); Maharaja Sher Singh (1841-1843); Maharaja Duleep Singh (the youngest son of Ranjit Singh, ascending at age 5 in 1843, deposed at age 11 in 1849). The power devolution operated through actual power since the post-Ranjit-Singh period residing with the army (the *panchs* or military committees) rather than with the court at Lahore. The institutional consequence: the political instability and the army's institutional autonomy served as the principal factor in the weakening of the Sikh Empire across the 1839-1849 period.

The institutional Sikh Empire across the broader analytic frame of the volume operates as a distinct configuration. The Sikh Empire was a sovereign Sikh state during the 1799-1849 period — the principal institutional moment of Sikh political sovereignty in the broader Sikh history. The configuration was multi-religious in composition (Sikh ruling architecture with Muslim and Hindu population and military), administratively integrated, and territorially extensive. The Sikh Empire fits captured-state pattern of the broader argument differently from the contemporary Hindu Rashtra apparatus dossiered in Chapter 8 § IV or the Sinhala-Buddhist state dossiered in Chapter 9 § IV. The Sikh Empire was a sovereign religiously-identified state that accommodated the religious minorities through the broader multi-religious administrative configuration, rather than a state apparatus operating  against the religious minorities. The contemporary scholarly position (J.S. Grewal's *The Sikhs of the Punjab* 1990 and others) treats the Sikh Empire as a major moment of Sikh political sovereignty with the distinctive multi-religious administrative configuration.

### III. The Modern Sikh Transition (1849 – 1947)

The period from the British annexation of the Punjab in 1849 through to the 1947 Partition is the major modern transition of the Sikh tradition. Four major elements organise the architecture: the two Anglo-Sikh Wars producing British annexation; the Singh Sabha reform movement from 1873; the Akali campaign of the 1920s producing the Gurdwara Reform; the Sikh role in the broader Indian independence movement and the rupture of the 1947 Partition of Punjab.

The **First Anglo-Sikh War** (1845-1846) was the first major contestation between the Sikh Empire and the British East India Company. The starting point: the December 1845 Sikh army crossing of the Sutlej River. The major engagements: the Battle of Mudki (18 December 1845); the Battle of Ferozeshah (21-22 December 1845); the Battle of Aliwal (28 January 1846); the Battle of Sobraon (10 February 1846). The consequence: the **Treaty of Lahore** (9 March 1846) established the British protectorate over the Sikh Empire; the cession of the Jalandhar Doab and Kashmir to the British; the cession of Kashmir from the British to the Hindu Dogra ruler Gulab Singh under the Treaty of Amritsar (16 March 1846, the foundation of the Jammu and Kashmir state. Cross-reference Chapter 8 § III on the broader Jammu and Kashmir question).

The **Second Anglo-Sikh War** (1848-1849) was the final contestation. The starting point: the April 1848 revolt of Mulraj, governor of Multan, which became the national Sikh revolt with the Sikh army joining the rebels on 14 September 1848. The major engagements: the Battle of Ramnagar (22 November 1848); the **Battle of Chillianwala** (13 January 1849, the indecisive engagement); the **Battle of Gujrat** (21 February 1849, the British victory). The consequence: the surrender of the Sikh army on 12 March 1849; the **annexation of the Punjab by the British East India Company on 29 March 1849** under Governor-General Lord Dalhousie. Maharaja Duleep Singh was deposed at age 11 and exiled to Britain on a pension; his mother Rani Jindan (Jind Kaur) was forcibly separated, confined at Ferozepur, and stripped of her wealth. The colonial apparatus: Sir John Lawrence was appointed as the first Chief Commissioner of Punjab; the **Koh-i-Noor diamond** was acquired by the British (transferred to Queen Victoria and operating as part of the British Crown Jewels to the contemporary period, with the contested position from the Indian, Pakistani, and Afghan governments regarding its return).

The **Singh Sabha** ("Singh Society") movement was the Sikh reform movement of the late 19th century. Founded in **Amritsar in 1873** as the response to the Christian missionary apparatus, the Hindu Arya Samaj reform movement (founded 1875, cross-reference Chapter 8 § III), and the Muslim Ahmadiyya movement (founded 1889) that targeted the Sikh community for conversion. The apparatus: the Singh Sabha operated through the Sikh educational network (the Khalsa Colleges, including the major **Khalsa College at Amritsar** founded 1892) and the Sikh publishing apparatus. The Singh Sabha leadership across the late 19th century included Thakur Singh Sandhanwalia, Giani Gian Singh, Bhai Vir Singh (1872-1957, the major Sikh literary figure), and others.

The Singh Sabha reform produced the major contestation over the question of Sikh identity — the **Tat Khalsa** ("True Khalsa") position defined the Sikh tradition as a distinct tradition separate from Hinduism, against the **Sanatan Sikhs** position that treated the Sikh tradition as a sub-current of Hinduism. The Tat Khalsa position prevailed and is the foundation of the contemporary Sikh self-definition. Harjot Oberoi's *The Construction of Religious Boundaries* (1994) treats the late 19th-century Singh Sabha reform as the principal architectural moment of contemporary Sikh self-definition.

The **Akali campaign** of the 1920s was the Sikh political-religious mobilisation around the control of the *gurdwaras* (Sikh places of worship). The apparatus: the control of the major gurdwaras had passed throughout the post-1849 colonial period to the *mahants* (the hereditary custodians, accommodating with the British colonial apparatus and departing from the Khalsa Sikh discipline). The Akali campaign mobilised to wrest the gurdwaras from the *mahants* and place them under Khalsa supervision.

The major moments: the **Nankana Sahib massacre** (20 February 1921, around 130-200 Akali Sikhs killed by the retainers of the Nankana Sahib mahant Narain Das, the major moment of the Akali campaign). The Akali mobilisations across the 1920-1925 period; the **Guru Ka Bagh** movement (August-November 1922, the non-violent satyagraha campaign at the Guru Ka Bagh gurdwara near Amritsar, producing around 5,605 arrests and the public attention to the Akali cause).

The consequence: the **Sikh Gurdwaras Act of 1925** established the **Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee** (SGPC) as the supreme management body of the historical gurdwaras in the Punjab region. The SGPC serves as the principal contemporary Sikh administrative apparatus, with jurisdiction over the major historical gurdwaras (the Harmandir Sahib, the Akal Takht, the major gurdwaras of Anandpur, Damdama, Patna, Nanded — though the latter two operate under separate management). The **Shiromani Akali Dal** political party emerged from the Akali campaign in 1920 as the principal contemporary Sikh political party.

Sikhs played a major role in the broader Indian independence movement across the 1900s-1940s. The architecture: the major Sikh contribution to the British Indian Army across the First and Second World Wars — Sikhs constituted around 20% of the British Indian Army at independence, despite being around 2% of the Indian population. The major Sikh figures in the Indian independence movement included **Bhagat Singh** (1907-1931, the revolutionary executed by the British at Lahore on 23 March 1931, an iconic figure of the Indian independence movement and contemporary South Asian political memory); **Baba Kharak Singh** (1867-1963, the Akali leader); **Master Tara Singh** (1885-1967, the Akali leader across the 1920s-1960s).

The **1947 Partition of Punjab** produced the major rupture of the Sikh community. The line drawn through the heart of the Punjab Sikh community divided the historic Sikh population between India (East Punjab) and Pakistan (West Punjab). The Sikh population on the Pakistan side (around 2 million pre-Partition Sikhs in West Punjab) was expelled or killed in the violence of August-November 1947. The Sikh refugee flow operated into East Punjab. The major Sikh pilgrimage sites lost to Pakistan included **Nankana Sahib** (Guru Nanak's birthplace), Panja Sahib, Kartarpur (Guru Nanak's final residence), and the major historic Sikh sites of West Punjab. The Partition violence produced contested casualty estimates ranging from 200,000 to 2 million deaths across the broader Hindu-Sikh-Muslim population, with around 14-18 million displaced across the broader population. The Sikh share of the casualties: around 2 million Sikh deaths and around 5 million Sikh refugees per contested estimates from contemporary historiography (Yasmin Khan's *The Great Partition* 2007. Urvashi Butalia's *The Other Side of Silence* 1998; Ian Talbot and Gurharpal Singh's *The Partition of India* 2009 treat the Partition as a multilateral atrocity affecting all three communities with major consequences for each).

The **Kartarpur Corridor** opened in November 2019 between India and Pakistan is the contemporary architectural moment of the post-Partition Sikh pilgrimage architecture. The visa-free corridor allowing Indian Sikh pilgrims access to the Gurdwara Darbar Sahib at Kartarpur (the site of Guru Nanak's final residence and passing),  inaugurated for the 550th birth anniversary of Guru Nanak in November 2019 under Prime Minister Modi (India) and Prime Minister Imran Khan (Pakistan).

### IV. The Contemporary Sikh Apparatuses (1947 – 2026)

The contemporary Sikh apparatuses operate across five principal moments: the post-1947 Indian-Punjab settlement and the 1966 Punjab Reorganisation Act. The Khalistan movement and the early 1980s contestation under Bhindranwale; **Operation Blue Star June 1984**; the Indira Gandhi assassination 31 October 1984 and the November 1984 anti-Sikh pogrom; the post-1984 Punjab insurgency, the contemporary Sikh diaspora architecture, and the contemporary contestations including the 2020-2021 farmers' protests and the June 2023 Hardeep Singh Nijjar assassination. The operational dossier of the contemporary Sikh apparatuses — the SGPC, the Akal Takht, the Akali Dal political vehicle, and the contemporary Khalistan diasporic mobilisation — is deferred to Chapter 24 in Part III. § IV maps the apparatus.

The post-1947 architecture of the Indian Punjab operated under the configuration of the Indian Union. The Sikh population was principally concentrated in East Punjab (around 5 million Sikhs at the 1951 Census); the Sikh settlement of the post-Partition refugees was principally in East Punjab and Delhi.

The major contestation of the post-1947 period: the Sikh demand for a Punjabi-speaking state (the **Punjabi Suba** movement) under the leadership of Master Tara Singh and the Shiromani Akali Dal. The Indian government position (under Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru, dossiered in Chapter 8 § IV) was initially hostile to the demand on the grounds that it would produce a Sikh-majority state organised on religious lines. The contestation operated through the 1950s and 1960s under Akali agitations and the broader political mobilisation.

The **1966 Punjab Reorganisation Act** resolved the contestation under Prime Minister Indira Gandhi. The architecture: the reorganisation of the broader Indian Punjab into three states — **Punjab** (Sikh-majority, Punjabi-speaking), **Haryana** (Hindu-majority, Hindi-speaking), and **Himachal Pradesh** (the hill territories). The consequence: the Sikh-majority Punjab state with around 60% Sikh population — the only Sikh-majority Indian state.

The **Anandpur Sahib Resolution** of 1973 (drafted at Anandpur Sahib by the Shiromani Akali Dal under the leadership of Sant Harchand Singh Longowal and others) was the Sikh political demand. The architecture: the demand for greater Sikh autonomy within the Indian federal configuration. The contested interpretation: the Resolution was interpreted by some Sikh figures as a demand for Sikh sovereignty, by others as a demand for federal-constitutional reform within the Indian Union.

The **Khalistan movement** emerged from the 1970s onwards as the Sikh separatist demand for the creation of a sovereign Sikh state in the Punjab. The major vectors: the diasporic Khalistan organisations principally in the United Kingdom, Canada, and the United States; the Khalistan-linked organisations in the Indian Punjab.

**Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale** (1947-1984) emerged as the major Sikh figure of the early 1980s. The architecture: Bhindranwale was the head of the **Damdami Taksal** (the major Sikh seminary tradition); he was the symbol of the Khalistan movement from the late 1970s onwards. The 1978 Sikh-Nirankari clashes (between Sikhs and the Sant Nirankari Mission, treated by many Sikhs as a heretical apparatus) operated as the precursor of the contemporary contestation. In April 1980 Bhindranwale took residence at the Akal Takht complex at the Harmandir Sahib and fortified the position across the 1980-1984 period. The institutional Indian government position across the period: initially the Congress Party promoted Bhindranwale as a counter-weight to the Akali Dal in Punjab politics; subsequently, when Bhindranwale's apparatus operated against Congress interests, the Indian government moved to confront him.

**Operation Blue Star** was the Indian Army military operation conducted against Bhindranwale and the Khalistan militants occupying the Harmandir Sahib complex at Amritsar. The operation was conducted **1 June 1984 – 10 June 1984** under the order of Prime Minister **Indira Gandhi**, with the command under Lt General **Kuldip Singh Brar** operating under General Sundarji as the broader commander.

On **3 June 1984** a 36-hour curfew was imposed in Punjab; the communications and transport apparatus suspended; thousands of Sikh pilgrims trapped inside the Golden Temple complex (the date coincided with the martyrdom anniversary of Guru Arjan Dev Ji on 30 May). The **5-7 June 1984** main assault phase: Indian Army tanks and troops engaged the Bhindranwale forces; the **Akal Takht** sustained major damage (almost entirely destroyed in the operation); Bhindranwale killed during the operation along with the major Khalistan figures including Major General Shabeg Singh (the former Indian Army officer who had trained the Bhindranwale forces).

Casualty estimates are contested. The Indian government position: 410 deaths total, 1,180 injured. The Wikipedia tabulation drawing on contemporary sources: 229 armed Sikh militants killed inside the Golden Temple complex plus at least 554 civilian deaths. Other estimates from the Sikh community and journalists place the deaths at 3,000+ civilians. The institutional Sikh community reaction was major outrage and the perception that the operation constituted the desecration of the holiest Sikh shrine. The Sikh comparison to the **Wadda Ghallughara** (the 1762 Sikh holocaust under Ahmad Shah Durrani); the Sikh designation **"Teeja Ghallughara"** ("the Third Holocaust"). The consequences across the Sikh community: the desertion of Sikh soldiers from their units in the Indian Army; the resignation of Sikh civil servants; the return of awards received from the Indian government by major Sikh figures.

The **Indira Gandhi assassination on 31 October 1984**: Prime Minister Indira Gandhi was assassinated at her New Delhi residence at around 9:20 am by her two Sikh bodyguards **Satwant Singh** and **Beant Singh** in retaliation for Operation Blue Star. Beant Singh was killed by other security personnel at the scene; Satwant Singh was tried and executed on 6 January 1989 along with the alleged co-conspirator Kehar Singh.

The **November 1984 anti-Sikh pogrom** was the state-organised anti-Sikh violence across India (principally in Delhi) from 31 October through to the declaration of military intervention on 3 November 1984. The architecture: the pogrom operated through the Congress Party apparatus under the broader institutional position of the post-Indira-Gandhi Rajiv Gandhi government. Rajiv Gandhi's infamous statement regarding the violence: "When a big tree falls, the earth shakes." The Indian government official figures: around 2,800-3,350 Sikh deaths in Delhi; the broader Indian total around 3,000-3,350. The contested estimates from independent organisations including the People's Union for Civil Liberties (PUCL) range from 8,000 to 17,000 Sikh deaths total across the major cities (Delhi, Kanpur, Bokaro, Indore, others).

The architecture of the pogrom: the targeting of Sikh men by mob violence (lynching, burning alive, beating); the sexual violence against Sikh women; the destruction of Sikh property and gurdwaras; the police inaction or active facilitation; the Congress Party leaders implicated as the organisers including Jagdish Tytler, Sajjan Kumar, H.K.L. Bhagat. The contemporary judicial proceedings have produced limited accountability — Sajjan Kumar was convicted of murder in December 2018 and sentenced to life imprisonment. The **Nanavati Commission Report** of 2005 (the formal Indian state commission of inquiry under retired Supreme Court Justice G.T. Nanavati) documented the architecture of the pogrom and the implication of the named Congress Party leaders.

The contemporary position of the Sikh community: the November 1984 pogrom is treated as the contemporary moment of the Sikh persecution in India. The Sikh designation **"1984 Sikh genocide"** is used in the contemporary Sikh discourse, with the contested institutional position from the Indian government and the Congress Party that treats the events as communal riots rather than state-organised genocide.

The post-1984 Punjab insurgency operated across the 1984-1993 period as the major Sikh militant contestation. The architecture: the Khalistan militancy across the period; the Indian state counter-insurgency operations under the architecture of the major Punjab DGPs including K.P.S. Gill (in office 1991-1995); the reports of extra-judicial killings, enforced disappearances, and torture by the Indian security forces (documented by Ensaaf, the Sikhs for Justice organisation, Human Rights Watch, and the People's Union for Civil Liberties); around 25,000 deaths across the insurgency period per contested estimates.

The contemporary position: the Khalistan movement operates principally through the diasporic apparatus — the **Sikhs for Justice** organisation in the United States under Gurpatwant Singh Pannun, the Khalistan-linked organisations in Canada and the United Kingdom. The contemporary Indian Punjab operates under the post-1993 settlement with low levels of Khalistan militancy.

The **2020-2021 farmers' protests** (cross-referenced in Chapter 8 § IV) operated through the Sikh-majority Punjab farmer community as the principal anchor of the broader Indian farmer protest movement against the three Modi-government farm laws. The Sikh community in Delhi (the siege of the Singhu border camp) was the principal anchor of the protest architecture. The Modi government repealed the three farm laws in November 2021. The contemporary scholarly position treats the 2020-2021 farmers' protests as the contemporary moment of broader Sikh-Indian state contestation, with the Modi government's institutional rhetoric institutionally accusing the protesters of Khalistani sympathies as the pretext for repression.

The **June 2023 Hardeep Singh Nijjar assassination** in Surrey, British Columbia (Canada) of the pro-Khalistan figure Hardeep Singh Nijjar (1977-2023) — the Canadian government's position under Prime Minister Justin Trudeau (September 2023): the assassination was conducted by Indian state agents operating under Indian government direction. The Indian government position: rejected the allegation as baseless and politically motivated. The institutional Canadian-Indian contestation has produced major diplomatic rupture, with the Canadian government expelling Indian diplomats in October 2024 following the further institutional investigation, and the Indian government making reciprocal expulsions. The institutional contestation operates as of May 2026 with the Canadian Royal Canadian Mounted Police institutional position maintaining the allegation against Indian state agents.

The contemporary Sikh demographic operates as follows. The worldwide Sikh population is around 25-30 million (Britannica estimate around 25 million as of early 21st century; contemporary estimates including the 2011 Indian Census figure of around 20.8 million Sikhs in India). The architecture: **India** (around 20.8 million Sikhs per 2011 Census, around 1.72% of the population; principally in Punjab. Around 16 million Sikhs, around 58% of the Punjab population, the only Sikh-majority Indian state. Plus around 5 million in other states including Delhi, Haryana, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh); **Canada** (around 800,000 Sikhs per 2021 Census, around 2.1% of the Canadian population. The major contemporary Sikh diaspora community with major political representation including in the federal NDP and Liberal Party). **United Kingdom** (around 525,000 Sikhs per 2021 Census, the major European Sikh community); **United States** (around 500,000 Sikhs per institutional estimates); **Australia** (around 210,000 Sikhs per 2021 Census); **Italy**, **Germany**, **Malaysia**, **Singapore**, **Thailand**, **Pakistan**, **Afghanistan**, **Iran**: the smaller Sikh diaspora communities. The contemporary Sikh diaspora architecture operates through the gurdwara network, the SGPC-affiliated and independent institutional configurations, and the diasporic political-cultural organisations.

The summary across § IV. The contemporary Sikh architecture from 1947 to 2026 operates through three principal moments: the post-1966 Punjab Reorganisation Act configuration with the Sikh-majority Punjab state. The post-1984 architecture of state violence against the Sikh community (Operation Blue Star June 1984, Indira Gandhi assassination October 1984, November 1984 anti-Sikh pogrom in Delhi and other cities, the post-1984 Punjab insurgency). The contemporary configuration of the global Sikh diaspora and the ongoing contestations (the 2020-2021 farmers' protests; the June 2023 Hardeep Singh Nijjar assassination; the contemporary Canadian-Indian diplomatic rupture).

The co-option pattern of the broader dossier applies in the contemporary Sikh form differently from the contemporary captured Hindu, Buddhist, or other configurations dossiered to this point. Sikhism has operated principally as the contemporary persecuted minority configuration — targeted by the Indian state under both Congress Party governments (the November 1984 pogrom under the Rajiv Gandhi government) and the BJP-led Sangh Parivar governments (the accusations of Khalistani sympathies against the 2020-2021 farmers' protests; the alleged Indian state involvement in the June 2023 Nijjar assassination). The captured-state architecture in the Sikh register operates not as Sikh-tradition-capturing-state but as state-apparatus-targeting-Sikh-tradition. The institutional Sikh political vehicles (the Shiromani Akali Dal; the SGPC) operate in the contemporary Indian context as the apparatuses of the persecuted minority position, not as the compromised-state apparatuses of the other traditions dossiered to this point. The Sikh Empire of 1799-1849 dossiered in § II operated as a distinct sovereign Sikh state configuration during the pre-modern period, with the multi-religious administrative architecture that the contemporary scholarly synthesis treats as a different pattern from the contemporary captured-state apparatuses operating across the modern religious-political landscape.

Chapter 10 closes here. The architecture has been mapped across the full historical arc: § I emergence c. 1469-1708; § II medieval architecture 1708-1849; § III modern transition 1849-1947; § IV contemporary apparatuses 1947-2026. The nine-tradition Part II foundational dossier (Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish, Hindu, Buddhist, Sikh) is now complete in Chapters 2-10.

Part II continues with Chapter 11 — the Indigenous tradition.

---


## Chapter 11. Indigenous Traditions Under Capture

The Indigenous traditions of the worldwide pre-colonial spiritual configurations operate as the principal counter-architecture to the nine major state-captured apparatuses dossiered in Chapters 2-10 of Part II. The contemporary worldwide Indigenous population is around 476 million across over 90 countries (UN Department of Economic and Social Affairs figures). Around 5,000 distinct Indigenous peoples; the contemporary Indigenous religious configurations operate across the major regions of the Americas, Australia and Oceania, sub-Saharan Africa, the Arctic and Siberia, Southeast Asia, and the residual Indigenous European configurations (principally the Sami of northern Scandinavia).

The argument operates across the four-section structure of Part II. § I dossiers the pre-contact configurations (through c. 1492). § II dossiers the colonial encounter (1492 – c. 1800). § III dossiers the modern transition (c. 1800 – 1948). § IV dossiers the contemporary apparatuses (1948 – 2026). The operational dossier of the contemporary Indigenous Commodification apparatus — the broader New Age appropriation; the ayahuasca tourism configurations; the mindfulness commodification; the contemporary spiritual marketplace — is deferred to Chapter 25 in Part III.

The capture argument of the volume applies in the Indigenous register as follows. Indigenous traditions operate differently from the co-opted-by-state Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Shia Muslim, Jewish, Hindu, and Buddhist apparatuses dossiered in Chapters 2-9. The Indigenous configurations across the contemporary world operate principally as the targeted-by-state-and-by-religious-apparatus configuration. The position that has been targeted by the co-opted Catholic and Protestant apparatuses (the broader colonial conquest of the Americas, Australia, Africa, and the Pacific). The captured Sunni Muslim apparatus (the Islamic expansion across sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia), the captured Hindu apparatus (the treatment of the Adivasi tribal communities of India), and the broader institutional captured apparatuses across the period. The closest analogue from the broader nine-tradition Part II dossier is the Sikh register dossiered in Chapter 10 § IV — both Indigenous and Sikh configurations operate principally as targeted-by-state communities rather than as co-opted apparatuses.

The volume's analytic frame applies symmetrically. The anti-antisemitism guardrail remains binding. The Indigenous chapter dossiers the principal architecture of the relationship with the same evidentiary discipline applied across the previous ten foundational traditions of Part II. A methodological note: "Indigenous" as a single category encompasses an enormous range of distinct traditions across thousands of peoples worldwide; the chapter operates through representative anchors across the major regions rather than the exhaustive enumeration of the distinct configurations.

### I. The Pre-contact Configurations (through c. 1492)

The pre-contact Indigenous spiritual configurations across the major regions operate through distinctive characteristics that distinguish the broader Indigenous architecture from the major proselytising universal-claim Abrahamic and Indic-East Asian traditions dossiered in Chapters 2-10. The characteristics:

The oral transmission of the spiritual tradition rather than written canonical scripture. The centrality of the land-relationship and place-specific spirituality (the configuration is bound to specific territories rather than operating through portable universal-claim configurations). Ancestor veneration as a core practice. Communal ceremonial practice rather than individual confession. The integration of the sacred and the everyday rather than the sacred-secular separation characteristic of the broader Abrahamic configurations; non-proselytising orientation (the Indigenous configurations do not seek institutional conversion of non-members in the manner of the broader Catholic, Protestant, Sunni Muslim, or Buddhist missionary apparatuses); relationship-based rather than doctrine-based configuration. These characteristics operate as the principal counter-configuration to the broader nine major proselytising traditions dossiered in the preceding chapters of Part II.

The Americas served as the principal pre-contact Indigenous demographic. The major Mesoamerican configurations across the pre-contact period: the **Aztec** (Mexica) civilisation centred on Tenochtitlán across the c. 14th c. - 1521 CE period, with the broader Triple Alliance configuration from 1428. The **Maya** civilisation of the Yucatán, Guatemala, Belize, and southern Mexico (Classic period c. 250-900 CE, with the post-Classic continuity through to Spanish contact). The **Zapotec** civilisation centred on Monte Albán (c. 500 BCE - 750 CE); the **Mixtec** configuration; the earlier **Olmec** mother-civilisation (c. 1500-400 BCE) operating as the foundational Mesoamerican configuration; the major Mesoamerican configuration **Teotihuacan** (c. 100 BCE - 550 CE) operating as the largest pre-contact Mesoamerican urban centre.

The Andean configurations: the **Inca Empire** (Tawantinsuyu, c. 1438-1572 CE) was the largest pre-contact American empire. The earlier Andean configurations included the **Wari** (c. 500-1000 CE) and the **Tiwanaku** (c. 300-1100 CE) civilisations as the precursors of the broader Inca consolidation.

The North American configurations: the major apparatuss included the **Iroquois Confederacy** (Haudenosaunee, c. 1142 CE foundation by Dekanawida and Hiawatha per the traditional dating, with the broader contested Western scholarly dating of c. 1450-1600 CE); the **Mississippian** mound-builder culture (c. 800-1600 CE, with the major institutional centre at Cahokia near St. Louis as the largest pre-contact urban configuration north of Mexico). The **Anasazi** (Ancestral Puebloan) of the Southwest (c. 100-1600 CE) with the major institutional architectural complexes at Chaco Canyon and Mesa Verde; the **Lakota**, **Cheyenne**, **Crow**, **Blackfoot**, and other Plains nations; the **Cherokee**, **Choctaw**, **Chickasaw**, **Creek**, **Seminole** (the institutional "Five Civilised Tribes") of the Southeast; the Iroquois confederacy nations (Mohawk. Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, plus Tuscarora from c. 1722); the broader Algonquian language family nations; the **Inuit** of the Arctic; the **Navajo** (Diné) and **Apache** of the Southwest.

The Amazonian and South American configurations: the broader institutional Amazonian Indigenous architecture with around 1,000+ distinct pre-contact peoples per the broader scholarly position; the Marajoara culture (c. 400-1300 CE) and other major institutional Amazonian configurations. The Caribbean configurations: the **Taino** (Arawak) of the Greater Antilles; the **Carib** of the Lesser Antilles.

Australia and Oceania was the second major pre-contact Indigenous region. The **Aboriginal Australian** traditions operate through the central concept of **Dreamtime** (or "The Dreaming", in Aboriginal English; *Tjukurpa* among the Anangu, *Jukurrpa* among the Warlpiri, and varied terms across the different Aboriginal languages). Both the creation period and the ongoing spiritual reality; the architecture of totemic relationships between human groups and specific animal/plant species; the architecture of the songlines (the ritual-geographical mapping of the continent). The continuous Aboriginal occupation of Australia: around 65,000 years per the contemporary archaeological consensus from the Madjedbebe rock shelter in Arnhem Land and other major sites. Around 250 distinct Aboriginal language groups pre-contact; around 120 of these survive in some form to the contemporary period.

The **Torres Strait Islander** traditions operate as the Melanesian-affiliated peoples of the Torres Strait between Australia and Papua New Guinea. The **Maori** of Aotearoa New Zealand — the structural Polynesian arrival c. 1280-1350 CE; the architecture of the *whakapapa* (genealogy), the *iwi* (tribe), the *whenua* (land), the *mana* (spiritual authority), the *tapu* (sacred prohibition); the major figures of the Maori pantheon — Ranginui sky-father, Papatūānuku earth-mother, Tāne (god of forests and birds), Tūmatauenga (god of war), and others. The broader Pacific Islander traditions operate across the Polynesian, Melanesian, and Micronesian regions.

Sub-Saharan Africa was the third major pre-contact Indigenous region. The **African Traditional Religions** (ATR) operate as the broader umbrella category for the pre-Islamic, pre-Christian Indigenous African spiritual traditions. The major configurations include the **Yoruba** religion of West Africa (Nigeria, Benin, Togo) with the *orisha* (deities) pantheon and the *Ifa* divination system. The **Igbo** religion of Nigeria with the *chi* personal spirit. The **Akan** religion of Ghana with the *Nyame* supreme deity; the **Bantu** religious configurations across the broader Bantu-speaking region; the **Khoisan** traditions of southern Africa; the **Ancient Egyptian** religious tradition (extinct by c. 4th-5th century CE following Christian-Roman suppression but operating as the foundational pre-Christian African religious civilisation).

The Arctic and Siberian configurations: the **Inuit** traditions across the Arctic region (Greenland, Canadian Arctic, Alaska, eastern Siberia) with the *angakok* (shaman) architecture and the broader Inuit spiritual configuration including Sedna (the sea goddess), Nanook (the polar bear spirit). The **Sami** of northern Scandinavia and the Kola Peninsula (Norway, Sweden, Finland, Russia) with the *noaidi* (shaman) architecture and the *sieidi* (sacred sites); the **Siberian** Indigenous peoples (the broader Tungusic, Turkic, Mongolic, Uralic peoples. Yakut, Buryat, Evenki, Chukchi, Koryak, Nenets, Khanty, Mansi, and others) with the architecture of shamanism (the term itself derives from the Evenki *šamán* — the broader Siberian shamanic tradition is the origin point of the contemporary global understanding of shamanism).

The Southeast Asian configurations operated across the pre-colonial Philippines (Indigenous animist-polytheist configurations across more than 100 distinct ethnic groups, with the architecture of *anito* spirit-veneration). Pre-colonial Indonesia (Indigenous traditions across the broader archipelago); the *Bön* tradition of pre-Buddhist Tibet (dossiered in Chapter 9 § II); the Indigenous Taiwanese peoples (the Austronesian-origin configurations operating as the source of the broader Austronesian expansion across the Pacific).

The pre-contact Indigenous configurations was the dominant spiritual architectures of the major regions outside the broader civilisational reach of the proselytising universal-claim Catholic, Protestant, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni Muslim, Hindu, and Buddhist apparatuses dossiered in the preceding chapters. The post-1492 institutional encounter dossiered in § II produced the major transformation of the Indigenous architecture across the worldwide configuration.

### II. The Colonial Encounter (1492 – c. 1800)

The 1492-1800 period is the major moment of the Indigenous architecture's contestation by the European Catholic and Protestant colonial apparatuses. Three principal moments organise the architecture: the Spanish conquest of the Americas (1492-1572); the broader European colonial expansion across the 16th-18th centuries; Christian missionary work across the colonial sphere.

**1492** was the starting point of the colonial encounter.
