The post-Obergefell period 2015-2026 has been characterised by: (1) the evangelical embrace of Donald Trump (who finished third among evangelical voters in the 2016 Iowa caucuses behind Ted Cruz and Marco Rubio but consolidated evangelical support through the primaries and general election); (2) the mobilisation around "religious liberty" framing as the principal counter-frame to LGBTQ+ rights; (3) the preparation for and execution of *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* (24 June 2022) which overturned *Roe v. Wade*; (4) the preparation of Project 2025 (dossiered in § IV).

**The 2016 Trump-evangelical alliance.** The documented 2016 Trump-evangelical alliance operated through several principal documented architects: Tony Perkins (Family Research Council); Ralph Reed (Faith and Freedom Coalition, founded 2009); Jerry Falwell Jr. (Liberty University president 2007-2020); Robert Jeffress (First Baptist Church Dallas); Franklin Graham (Billy Graham Evangelistic Association); James Dobson (Family Talk); Paula White-Cain (Trump's documented spiritual advisor from 2002, later appointed to White House Faith and Opportunity Initiative 31 October 2019). The 2016 alliance produced the White Evangelical Advisory Board (announced June 2016 with 25 founding members including all of the figures listed above). He received around 81% of the white evangelical vote in the 2016 general election per Pew Research Center — the highest evangelical vote share of any Republican presidential candidate in documented history at that point. The 2016 alliance is the foundation for the post-2016 evangelical-political configuration dossiered in §§ III and IV.

**The contemporary American Protestant evangelical demographic configuration.** Per Pew Research Center 2023-2024 surveys: white evangelical Protestants operate as around 14% of US adults (down from around 21% in 2008); religious "nones" (unaffiliated) operate as 28-29% of US adults (up from around 16% in 2007); the Black Protestant evangelical configuration is a separate documented political-voting bloc (with majority Democratic alignment); the Latino Protestant evangelical configuration is the fastest-growing US Protestant demographic with Republican shift across the 2020-2024 cycles. The Southern Baptist Convention dossiered separately in § III remains the largest single US Protestant denomination at 12.7 million members 2024 (down from peak 16.3 million 2003).

The summary of § II: the post-1979 evangelical-Republican infrastructure operates through a distributed network of around 50-100 principal documented organisations (denominations, parachurch bodies, advocacy organisations, broadcasting networks, donor coordination bodies, and educational institutions) coordinated principally through the Council for National Policy. The architecture runs with Republican political alignment across the 1980-2026 period. The principal institutional architects of the configuration are Paul Weyrich, James Dobson, Jerry Falwell Sr., Pat Robertson, and (in the contemporary period) Tony Perkins, Ralph Reed, Franklin Graham, Robert Jeffress, and (operationally in the 2016-2026 period) Paula White-Cain and the figures of the New Apostolic Reformation dossiered in § III.

---


### III. The New Apostolic Reformation and the Christian-Nationalist Apparatus

The post-2010 American evangelical-political configuration runs with a shift in its principal theological-organisational architecture. The pre-2010 configuration operated principally through the Southern Baptist Convention, the National Association of Evangelicals, and the Christian Right organisational network of § II — with theology centred on a Calvinist or Baptist-Reformed framework, an "engaged orthodox" cultural-political posture, and the post-1979 Moral Majority/Heritage/CNP coordination architecture. The post-2010 configuration runs principally through the New Apostolic Reformation (NAR) — a neo-charismatic-Pentecostal theological-organisational network with a "spiritual warfare" framework, a "Seven Mountain Mandate" cultural-political programme, and a apocalyptic-prophetic rhetorical architecture.

**The New Apostolic Reformation: definitional architecture.** The term "New Apostolic Reformation" was coined in 1996 by C. Peter Wagner (1930-2016) to describe the emerging neo-charismatic post-denominational movement. Wagner: missionary in Bolivia 1956-1971; Professor of Church Growth at Fuller Theological Seminary 1971-2001; founder of the Wagner Leadership Institute. Wagner's principal documented text on the NAR is *The New Apostolic Reformation: An Overview* 2003. The architectural claim: the contemporary period is a "second apostolic age" in which God has restored the offices of "apostle" and "prophet" to the contemporary Christian Church (the "fivefold ministry" — apostle, prophet, evangelist, pastor, teacher — drawn from Ephesians 4:11). NAR-aligned leaders therefore claim apostolic authority and prophetic gifts on equal footing with the biblical apostles and prophets. The claim is rejected by most mainstream Protestant theological traditions including the Southern Baptist Convention (which has issued multiple documented statements distancing itself from NAR theology).

**The Latter Rain antecedent.** The NAR's theological-organisational antecedent is the Latter Rain movement that emerged at North Battleford Saskatchewan in 1948 (the "Sharon Orphanage and Schools" revival under William Branham, George Hawtin, Percy Hunt, and others). The Latter Rain movement developed the theology of contemporary apostles and prophets, the restoration of "fivefold ministry," and the "manifest sons of God" eschatology — all of which operate as the theological foundations of the contemporary NAR. The Latter Rain was denounced by the Assemblies of God in 1949, but its theological framework operated underground through the 1950s-1990s before re-emerging through Wagner's documented codification in the 1990s.

**The Seven Mountain Mandate.** The principal contemporary NAR political-cultural programme is the "Seven Mountain Mandate" (also called the "Seven Mountains of Culture" framework). The architecture: Loren Cunningham (1935-2023, founder of Youth With a Mission YWAM 1960) and Bill Bright (1921-2003, founder of Campus Crusade for Christ 1951) reportedly received "the same vision from God" in a 1975 meeting that documented Christians should seek to "take dominion" over seven domains of cultural influence: (1) family; (2) religion/church; (3) education; (4) media; (5) arts and entertainment; (6) business; (7) government. The framework was codified by Lance Wallnau (born 1956) in his 2013 book *Invading Babylon: The 7 Mountain Mandate* (co-authored with Bill Johnson of Bethel Church). The Seven Mountain Mandate is the theological-political programme of the contemporary NAR: a programme of explicit Christian dominionism over the principal domains of cultural-political power.

**Bethel Church Redding California.** Bethel Church operates as one of the principal institutional vehicles of the contemporary NAR. The architecture: Bethel Church Redding California, Bill Johnson (born 1951) as lead pastor from 1996 (Johnson's father Earl Johnson was Bethel pastor from 1968-1982); Bethel School of Supernatural Ministry founded 1998 (now with enrolment of around 2,000-2,500 students annually drawn from around 70 countries); Bethel Music recording label founded 2001 (one of the leading music labels of the 2010s-2020s with multiple Billboard chart-topping releases); average weekly attendance around 11,000-11,500. Bethel is the structural centre of one of the principal NAR theological-organisational networks — and as the source of one of the principal contemporary Christian-music streaming repertoires (cross-reference Hillsong from Australia and Elevation Worship from North Carolina as the two other principal contemporary Christian-music labels).

**The International House of Prayer (IHOPKC) and the Mike Bickle case.** IHOPKC operated as one of the major NAR institutional centres across the 1999-2024 period. The architecture: founded 7 May 1999 in Kansas City by Mike Bickle (born 1955); operated a 24/7 prayer room from inception; produced missionary training; operated multiple documented affiliated organisations. The institution collapsed in 2023-2024 following multiple allegations of sexual misconduct against Bickle dating back decades. Bickle was removed from leadership in December 2023; the institution rebranded; the allegations operate as one of the contemporary abuse-and-cover-up scandals in the NAR network — parallel to the Catholic abuse pattern dossiered in Chapter 15 § III and the Southern Baptist Convention abuse pattern dossiered later in this section.

**Lance Wallnau and the "Trump as Cyrus" prophecy.** Lance Wallnau (born 1956, Texas-based NAR figure) operates as one of the major NAR political strategists. Wallnau published *God's Chaos Candidate: Donald J. Trump and the American Unraveling* (Killer Sheep Media, October 2016) framing Trump as a contemporary parallel to the Persian King Cyrus the Great (c. 600-530 BCE, who in the biblical record permitted the Jewish return from Babylonian exile and the rebuilding of the Jerusalem Temple). The "Trump as Cyrus" framing is the theological architecture through which the NAR justified evangelical support for Trump despite Trump's documented personal departures from traditional evangelical moral teaching: under the Cyrus framing, Trump is a non-believer chosen by God for a political mission to restore Christian America. The framing was elaborated by Wallnau across multiple subsequent works including the 2024 NAR mobilisation around Trump's second-term campaign.

**Project 19 (2024 NAR election operation).** The principal NAR institutional vehicle for the 2024 election cycle was Project 19 — an NAR initiative to mobilise evangelical Christians in 19 swing-state suburban counties to vote for Trump. The architecture: state-level evangelical pastoral networks; voter-registration drives operating through churches; documented "Seven Mountain Mandate" framing in church teaching; evangelical media coordination. Project 19 operated alongside Ralph Reed's Faith and Freedom Coalition voter mobilisation (founded 2009, operating with Republican Party coordination across the 2010-2026 period) and Charlie Kirk's Turning Point USA (founded 2012, with Turning Point Faith subsidiary launched 2021) — the post-2020 evangelical-political coordination network.

**The Trump "spiritual advisor" network.** The Trump White Evangelical Advisory Board (announced June 2016 with 25 founding members) operated across both Trump terms. Principal documented figures: Paula White-Cain (born 1966, prosperity-gospel preacher, formerly New Destiny Christian Center Florida, now City of Destiny Apopka Florida — Trump's principal documented "spiritual advisor" from 2002, appointed to White House Faith and Opportunity Initiative 31 October 2019); Robert Jeffress (born 1955, First Baptist Church Dallas pastor); Franklin Graham (Billy Graham Evangelistic Association and Samaritan's Purse); Ralph Reed (Faith and Freedom Coalition); James Dobson (Family Talk); Tony Perkins (Family Research Council); Jerry Falwell Jr. (Liberty University president until August 2020 resignation amid documented scandal); Greg Laurie (Harvest Christian Fellowship California); Jack Graham (Prestonwood Baptist Church Texas); James Robison (Life Outreach International); Mark Burns (NowNation Church South Carolina); and others. The advisory board is the principal mechanism through which the evangelical-political configuration has coordinated with the White House across both terms.

**The contemporary Christian-nationalist intellectual apparatus.** The post-2016 period has produced a scholarly literature on the rise of Christian nationalism within the evangelical configuration. The principal documented texts: Andrew L. Whitehead and Samuel L. Perry, *Taking America Back for God: Christian Nationalism in the United States* (Oxford University Press, 2020); Anthea Butler, *White Evangelical Racism: The Politics of Morality in America* (UNC Press, 2021); Kristin Kobes Du Mez, *Jesus and John Wayne: How White Evangelicals Corrupted a Faith and Fractured a Nation* (Liveright, 2020); Robert P. Jones, *White Too Long: The Legacy of White Supremacy in American Christianity* (Simon & Schuster, 2020); Tim Alberta, *The Kingdom, the Power, and the Glory: American Evangelicals in an Age of Extremism* (Harper, December 2023); Sarah Posner, *Unholy: How White Christian Nationalists Powered the Trump Presidency* (Random House, 2020); Katherine Stewart, *The Power Worshippers: Inside the Dangerous Rise of Religious Nationalism* (Bloomsbury, 2019); Matthew D. Taylor, *The Violent Take It By Force: The Christian Movement That Is Threatening Our Democracy* (Broadleaf, 2024). The literature dossiers a institutional-cultural pattern: the merger of white American Protestant evangelicalism with nationalist political ideology, documented anti-democratic political behaviour, documented racial-resentment politics, and documented gender-and-sexuality reactionary positions.

**The 6 January 2021 US Capitol attack.** The nationalist iconography of the 6 January 2021 attack on the US Capitol operates as one of the principal contemporary moments of the NAR-evangelical-political configuration's intersection with documented anti-democratic political violence. The architecture: the "Jericho March" rally Washington DC 12 December 2020 (with NAR figures including Eric Metaxas, the Sean Feucht "Worship Protest" tradition, Lance Wallnau participation) served as the principal religious-political precursor to the 6 January attack; the presence of Christian symbols (crosses, prayer banners, "Jesus Saves" signs) at the 6 January attack; the post-attack arrest and prosecution of multiple nationalist evangelical figures. The principal documented academic treatment of the religious dimension of the 6 January attack: Jeff Sharlet, *The Undertow: Scenes from a Slow Civil War* (Norton, 2023); Matthew D. Taylor, *The Violent Take It By Force* (2024).

**The Southern Baptist Convention contemporary configuration.** The SBC is the largest single US Protestant denomination but in institutional decline. The 2024 Annual Church Profile (Lifeway Research, released April 2025) documented: 12,722,266 members (down 259,824 from 2023, 18th consecutive year of decline, the lowest membership since 1974); 46,876 congregations (down 30); average 4.3 million weekly worship attendance; 250,643 baptisms (the highest since 2017); $9.55 billion total giving (down around $500 million from 2023). Three principal documented megachurches exited the SBC since 2023: Saddleback Church (Southern California, founded 1980 by Rick Warren, exited 2023 over women in pastoral leadership); Elevation Church (North Carolina, founded 2006 by Steven Furtick); NewSpring Church (South Carolina). The 2022 Guidepost Solutions investigation (commissioned by the SBC Executive Committee) documented the systematic mishandling of clergy sexual abuse claims by the SBC Executive Committee across the 2000-2021 period — the SBC abuse pattern parallel to the Catholic abuse pattern dossiered in Chapter 15 § III. The SBC is the principal Protestant institution where the theological-political conflicts of the contemporary evangelical configuration (women in ministry, abuse handling, LGBTQ+ exclusion, Trumpist alignment) operate visibly.

The summary of § III: the post-2010 American evangelical-political configuration operates through a shift in its principal theological-organisational architecture from the pre-2010 Calvinist-Baptist-Reformed setup (Southern Baptist Convention, National Association of Evangelicals, Heritage Foundation, traditional Christian Right) to the post-2010 NAR-neo-charismatic configuration (Bethel Church, the "Seven Mountain Mandate" programme, the "spiritual warfare" framework, the "Trump as Cyrus" theological-political architecture, the Project 19 mobilisation). The shift runs within a broader institutional decline of mainline American Protestantism (the SBC down from 16.3M to 12.7M across 2003-2024) and the rise of religious "nones" (28-29% of US adults as of 2024). The configuration produced the 2016, 2020, and 2024 evangelical electoral mobilisation around Trump dossiered in § IV.

---


### IV. The 2016 – 2026 Cycles, Project 2025, and the Second Trump Administration

The 2016-2026 period is the contemporary moment of the American evangelical-political apparatus's principal documented operational period. The configuration produced three presidential election outcomes — the 2016 win, 2020 loss, and 2024 win — and the post-2025 second-term operating with Project 2025 implementation. The section dossiers each.

**The 2016 election: Trump's evangelical breakthrough.** The 2016 Republican primary evangelical voters initially split between Ted Cruz (born 1970, the "evangelical first choice" candidate with Christian Right organisational backing), Marco Rubio (born 1971, the Catholic-evangelical alternative), and Trump. His breakthrough in the South Carolina primary 20 February 2016 (winning the evangelical vote despite Cruz's organisational lead) was the major moment of the evangelical primary shift to Trump. The June 2016 announcement of the White Evangelical Advisory Board with the 25 founding members dossiered in § III served as the principal institutional moment of the evangelical-Trump alliance. The November 2016 general election: He received around 81% of the white evangelical vote (per Pew Research Center 2016 exit poll analysis) — the highest evangelical share for any US presidential candidate. He received around 16% of the Black Protestant vote and around 67% of the Latino evangelical vote.

**The first Trump term (2017-2021): evangelical policy delivery.** The first Trump term produced evangelical policy wins through: (1) the Supreme Court appointments of Neil Gorsuch (sworn in 10 April 2017), Brett Kavanaugh (sworn in 6 October 2018), and Amy Coney Barrett (sworn in 27 October 2020) — three Federalist Society-vetted conservative jurists that produced the 6-3 conservative majority that overturned *Roe v. Wade* through *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* (24 June 2022); (2) the Jerusalem embassy move (announced 6 December 2017, embassy opened 14 May 2018) — a Christian Zionist priority; (3) the expansion of religious-exemption rules across federal regulation; (4) the appointment of around 230 federal judges (the largest single-presidential-term federal-judicial appointment record in recent history); (5) the Title IX guidance reversal protecting religious institutions; (6) the Faith and Opportunity Initiative under Paula White-Cain (31 October 2019).

**The Federalist Society and the judicial pipeline.** The Federalist Society for Law and Public Policy Studies (founded 1982 by Steven Calabresi, Lee Liberman Otis, and David McIntosh; long-time executive vice president Leonard Leo from 1991) served as the vehicle of conservative judicial nominations through both Trump terms. The architecture: Federalist Society Lawyers Division produced judicial shortlists; He campaigned in 2016 on the publication of his Federalist Society-vetted Supreme Court shortlist (the first time a presidential candidate had published such a list); the Supreme Court appointees and most federal-court appointees came from the Federalist Society network. Leonard Leo (born 1965) is the architect of the conservative-judicial machine; New York Times reporting and Senate hearings have traced the flows of funding through the Marble Freedom Trust (founded 2021 with a $1.6 billion donation from Barre Seid).

**The 2020 election and the January 6 attack.** The 2020 election: He received around 76-84% of the white evangelical vote (estimates varied per source) but lost the general election to Biden 306-232 in the Electoral College and by around 7 million in the popular vote. The post-2020 period produced the "Stop the Steal" movement with NAR participation through Lance Wallnau, Eric Metaxas, the "Jericho March" 12 December 2020 (dossiered in § III), and others. The 6 January 2021 US Capitol attack operated with nationalist iconography. The post-attack period: the Charles E. Grassley-led Senate Judiciary Committee investigation; the House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack (operating 1 July 2021-3 January 2023); the criminal prosecutions of around 1,500 individuals through the 2021-2025 period; the pardons of around 1,500 January 6 defendants on 20 January 2025 (first day of second term).

**Project 2025: architecture.** Project 2025 (formal name: 2025 Presidential Transition Project) is the Heritage Foundation-led policy and personnel apparatus for the post-2024 conservative presidential administration. The architecture: founded 21 April 2022; director Paul Dans (until August 2024 resignation amid Trump campaign distancing); subsequent leadership; Heritage Foundation president Kevin D. Roberts (from December 2021); $22 million budget; advisory board of over 100 conservative organisations including Heritage Foundation, Family Research Council, Concerned Women for America, Heritage Action for America, Susan B. Anthony Pro-Life America, Liberty University, Hillsdale College, Turning Point USA, Moms for Liberty, and many others.

**Project 2025 four pillars.** The architecture: (1) *Mandate for Leadership: The Conservative Promise* — the 922-page policy book published April 2023, edited by Paul Dans and Steven Groves, with 30 chapters covering policy recommendations for every major federal department and agency; (2) the Presidential Personnel Database — an ideologically-vetted database of around 20,000 Trump-loyalist candidates for federal political appointment; (3) the Presidential Administration Academy — a training programme to prepare prospective Trump-administration appointees to "operate successfully from Day One of the next conservative Administration"; (4) the "Playbook" — implementation plans for each federal agency including "Day One executive orders" to be signed.

**Project 2025 policy programme.** The 922-page *Mandate for Leadership* document is the principal policy programme of the contemporary American Christian Right. The architecture across major policy domains: (1) immigration — mass-deportation programme, restriction of birthright citizenship interpretation, expansion of border enforcement; (2) education — elimination of the Department of Education, expansion of school choice and religious-school vouchers, anti-DEI programmes; (3) federal workforce — restoration of Schedule F (allowing reclassification of around 50,000 federal civil-service positions as political appointees), loyalty-vetted appointments; (4) abortion and reproductive rights — restriction of mifepristone access, enforcement of the Comstock Act (1873) against mail-order abortion materials, opposition to abortion across federal policy; (5) LGBTQ+ rights — rollback of federal gender-identity protections (cross-reference Executive Order 14168 "Defending Women From Gender Ideology Extremism" 20 January 2025, dossiered in Chapter 14 § IV); (6) climate and environment — dismantling of climate-related regulations and agencies; (7) family and gender — promotion of nuclear-family policy framing; (8) foreign policy — "America First" framework; (9) executive authority — expansion of executive control over the federal bureaucracy through Schedule F and other mechanisms.

**Project 2025 documented implementation in the second term.** He publicly distanced himself from Project 2025 during the 2024 campaign (documented statements in summer 2024 claiming he had not read it and disagreed with portions), but the post-inauguration record shows major implementation. Per CNN analysis published 31 January 2025: more than two-thirds of the 53 executive orders Trump signed in his first week of office "evoked proposals outlined in [the] 'Mandate for Leadership.'" Documented first-100-days actions tracking Project 2025: EO 14168 "Defending Women From Gender Ideology Extremism" 20 January 2025 (cross-reference Chapter 14 § IV); documented restoration of Schedule F; mass-deportation programme commencement; elimination of DEI offices across the federal government; withdrawal from the Paris Climate Agreement (executed via Executive Order in January 2025, second US withdrawal under Trump); attempted dismantling of the Department of Education; Schedule A regulations restoring religious-exemption framing. The Project 2025 implementation operates through the second-term cabinet and senior appointments including Heritage Foundation/Project 2025 alumni in senior roles.

**The 2024 election: the second-term mandate.** The 2024 election: He won around 81-82% of the white evangelical vote (per AP VoteCast and Edison Research exit polls); won the popular vote by around 1.5% (the first Republican to win the popular vote since 2004); won the Electoral College 312-226; carried all seven documented swing states (Pennsylvania, Michigan, Wisconsin, Georgia, North Carolina, Arizona, Nevada). The NAR mobilisation through Project 19 (dossiered in § III) operated alongside Reed's Faith and Freedom Coalition mobilisation and the Republican Party voter-outreach operation. He was inaugurated as the 47th US President on 20 January 2025 — the first president since Grover Cleveland (term 1885-1889 and 1893-1897) to serve two non-consecutive terms. His first-day actions included: pardons of around 1,500 January 6 defendants; Executive Order 14168 on "gender ideology extremism"; withdrawal from the Paris Climate Agreement; declaration of national emergencies on the southern border; commencement of mass-deportation operations.

**The documented Catholic-evangelical convergence under the second Trump administration.** The 2025-2026 period has produced the convergence between American Catholic-integralist intellectual currents (centred on figures including Adrian Vermeule of Harvard Law School, Patrick Deneen of Notre Dame, Sohrab Ahmari, and the First Things and Compact magazine networks) and the evangelical-NAR political configuration. The convergence was anticipated in the 2019 *Civiltà Cattolica* article by Antonio Spadaro SJ and Marcelo Figueroa titled "Evangelical Fundamentalism and Catholic Integralism: A Surprising Ecumenism" published 13 July 2017 under the Francis pontificate (cross-reference Chapter 15 § IV). The Vatican apparatus under Pope Leo XIV (dossiered in Chapter 15 § IV) runs with institutional tensions with the Catholic-integralist-evangelical convergence. The Trump second-term Vice President JD Vance (born 1984, converted to Catholicism 2019) is the cross-over figure between the Catholic-integralist and evangelical-political configurations.

**The contemporary evangelical-political apparatus configuration.** The American evangelical-political apparatus as of 2026 operates as: (1) around 14% of US adults identifying as white evangelical Protestant (down from around 21% in 2008); (2) around 81% of those voters supporting Trump in 2024; (3) coordinated through around 50-100 principal institutional organisations (denominations, parachurch bodies, advocacy organisations, broadcasting networks, donor coordination bodies, educational institutions) plus the Council for National Policy coordination forum; (4) operating with the New Apostolic Reformation theological-political leadership and documented "Seven Mountain Mandate" cultural-political programme; (5) aligned with the Heritage Foundation/Project 2025 policy apparatus and the Federalist Society judicial apparatus; (6) producing sustained Republican electoral majorities and sustained influence over US federal policy across the 2017-2026 period excluding the 2021-2025 Biden interregnum.

**The captured-state pattern in the evangelical configuration.** The American evangelical-political apparatus is the Protestant captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period — operating not through a single juridical-territorial state apparatus (as the Vatican does) but through a distributed network of religious-organisational, political-advocacy, donor-coordination, and policy-research institutions that operate together to deliver sustained Republican political outcomes and sustained influence over US federal policy. The configuration generates institutional-political contradictions: the configuration claims to operate from Christian moral principles while operating in coalition with figures (Trump, the Republican donor class) whose personal conduct departs sharply from traditional evangelical moral teaching; the configuration claims to operate against "secularist" cultural domination while operating to capture state policy and federal-judicial appointments in the service of explicitly religious policy outcomes; the configuration runs with institutional opacity (the Council for National Policy non-disclosure conventions, the funding flows through the Marble Freedom Trust and similar vehicles) while accusing its political opponents of institutional opacity.

The summary of § IV: the 2016-2026 American evangelical-political cycle is the contemporary moment of the American evangelical-political apparatus's principal operational deployment. The configuration delivered: (1) the 2016 Trump electoral coalition with around 81% white evangelical vote share. (2) the first term policy delivery including three Supreme Court appointments and the Federalist Society judicial pipeline. (3) the 2020 loss and documented 6 January 2021 attack. (4) the 2024 Trump electoral comeback with documented around 81-82% white evangelical vote share. (5) the Project 2025 implementation through the Heritage Foundation/conservative-coalition apparatus across the 2025-2026 period. The configuration is the Protestant captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The section closes here. Chapter 17 dossiers the Moscow Patriarchate as the principal Eastern Orthodox captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period — operating in cross-reference with Chapter 4 (Eastern Orthodox foundational dossier) and with documented Russia-Ukraine war context.

---


## Chapter 17. The Moscow Patriarchate

Chapter 17 dossiers the Moscow Patriarchate as the principal Eastern Orthodox captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The analysis operates in cross-reference with Chapter 4 (Eastern Orthodox foundational dossier) and with Chapter 14 § IV (the Russian "gay propaganda" legal architecture). The record operates structurally as the Orthodox parallel to Chapter 15 (Vatican apparatus) and Chapter 16 (American Evangelical-Political apparatus): a contemporary working-apparatus dossier rather than a foundational-tradition dossier.

What follows covers four sections: § I the foundational architecture from the Baptism of Kievan Rus 988 through to Peter the Great's abolition of the Patriarchate in 1721. § II the Synodal period and the Soviet persecution 1721-1991. § III Patriarch Kirill. The post-Soviet revival, and the *Russkiy Mir* (Russian World) ideology 1991-2022. § IV the Russia-Ukraine war and the contemporary configuration 2022-2026.

The chapter's working configuration differs from both Chapter 15 (the Vatican as juridical-territorial sovereign state) and Chapter 16 (American evangelical distributed network). The Moscow Patriarchate operates as a the the captured religious-institutional apparatus integrated with the Russian Empire through a thousand-year history of church-state symphony (the Byzantine *symphonia* tradition), interrupted by the persecution period 1917-1991, and restored to operational church-state integration in the post-Soviet period under Patriarch Alexius II and Patriarch Kirill. The contemporary apparatus operates as one of the principal religious-ideological vehicles of the state's geopolitical and cultural-political project — including the war against Ukraine that began with the full-scale invasion of 24 February 2022.

### I. Foundational Architecture (988 – 1721)

**The Baptism of Kievan Rus 988** is the foundational moment of Russian-Ukrainian-Belarusian Orthodoxy. The architecture: Vladimir the Great (Volodymyr the Great, c. 958-1015, Grand Prince of Kiev from 980) adopted Byzantine Orthodox Christianity in 988 as the state religion of Kievan Rus, after the *Primary Chronicle*'s legendary account of his envoys evaluating Islam, Judaism, Western Christianity, and Eastern Christianity. The Baptism occurred at the Dnieper River in Kiev with mass baptism of Vladimir's subjects. The Russian Orthodox Church operates 988 as the foundational moment of all Eastern Slavic Orthodoxy — a position contested in the contemporary period by the Orthodox Church of Ukraine which claims 988 as the foundational moment of specifically Ukrainian Orthodoxy. The contested foundational claim is one of the moments of the contemporary Russia-Ukraine ecclesiastical conflict dossiered in §§ III-IV.

**The Kievan period (988-1240).** The Kievan Rus Orthodox configuration operated under the jurisdiction of the Patriarchate of Constantinople. The Metropolitan of Kiev and All Rus served as the principal hierarch of the Kievan Orthodox configuration. The architecture: the Cathedral of St Sophia in Kiev (built 1037 under Yaroslav the Wise, 978-1054); the Kiev Pechersk Lavra (founded 1051 by Saints Anthony and Theodosius of Kiev); the *Primary Chronicle* (compiled c. 1110-1118 by Nestor the Chronicler at the Kiev Pechersk Lavra) as the principal documentary record of the Kievan period. The Kievan Orthodox configuration served as the principal religious-cultural vehicle of the Kievan Rus state across the 988-1240 period.

**The Mongol invasion 1237-1241 and the relocation of the Metropolitan See.** The Mongol invasion of Rus under Batu Khan destroyed Kiev in December 1240 and shattered the Kievan Rus polity. The architecture of the post-Mongol period: the Metropolitan See relocated from Kiev to Vladimir in 1299 under Metropolitan Maximus (died 1305); the See relocated from Vladimir to Moscow in 1325 under Metropolitan Peter (c. 1260-1326, canonised as Peter of Moscow). The Moscow relocation was the foundational moment of the Moscow ecclesiastical configuration that is the centre of the ROC across the subsequent seven centuries.

**The Florence-Ferrara Council 1438-1439 and the Russian rejection of union with Rome.** The Council of Florence-Ferrara (begun Ferrara 8 January 1438, transferred to Florence February 1439, declaration of union 6 July 1439) served as the principal attempted East-West ecclesiastical union of the late medieval period. The Russian Metropolitan Isidore of Kiev (c. 1385-1463), a Greek by origin, signed the Florentine union on behalf of the Russian Church. On his return to Moscow in March 1441 Isidore was arrested by Grand Prince Vasily II, imprisoned, and escaped to Italy. The Russian Church rejected the Florentine union and declared *de facto* autocephaly under Metropolitan Jonah of Moscow (elected 15 December 1448 by Russian bishops without Constantinopolitan confirmation).

**The Fall of Constantinople 1453 and the "Third Rome" doctrine.** The fall of Constantinople to the Ottoman Empire on 29 May 1453 produced the principal post-Byzantine ideological reconfiguration of Russian Orthodoxy. The architecture: Philotheos of Pskov (c. 1465-1542), a monk of the Eleazar Monastery, wrote to Grand Prince Vasily III around 1510-1511 the principal text of the "Third Rome" doctrine — "Two Romes have fallen, the third [Moscow] stands, and a fourth there shall not be." The Third Rome doctrine is the principal ideological architecture of subsequent Russian Orthodox political theology — claiming Moscow as the divinely-ordained successor to Rome (fallen to barbarian heresy in 476) and Constantinople (fallen to Ottoman conquest in 1453). The doctrine operates across the contemporary period as one of the principal religious-ideological frameworks of the *Russkiy Mir* (Russian World) project dossiered in § III.

**Ivan IV "the Terrible" 1547 and the Russian autocracy.** Ivan IV (Ivan Grozny, 1530-1584, reign as Grand Prince from 1533, crowned as the first Tsar of All Russia on 16 January 1547) served as the architect of the post-Kievan Russian autocracy. The architecture: the formal title "Tsar" (Caesar) — claiming Byzantine imperial succession; the marriage of Ivan III (Ivan's grandfather) to Sophia Palaiologina (niece of the last Byzantine Emperor Constantine XI) in 1472, providing the Byzantine-succession claim; the adoption of the double-headed eagle as the Russian state symbol; the integration of the ROC into the autocratic state. The Ivan IV configuration is the foundational moment of the church-state symphony in the Russian context — operating across the 1547-1721 period as the architecture of pre-Patriarchal Russian Orthodoxy.

**The establishment of the Patriarchate of Moscow 1589.** The Russian Orthodox Church received formal Patriarchal status from the Patriarchate of Constantinople on 26 January 1589 (Jeremias II of Constantinople, then visiting Moscow, consecrated Job as first Patriarch of Moscow). The five-Patriarchate configuration of Eastern Orthodoxy was thereby established: Constantinople (founded as Patriarchate of Constantinople 451), Alexandria, Antioch, Jerusalem, and Moscow. The Moscow Patriarchate was the fifth and youngest of the historic Eastern Orthodox Patriarchates and the only one outside of the historic Byzantine territory.

**Patriarch Nikon and the Raskol 1652-1666.** Patriarch Nikon of Moscow (Nikita Minin, 1605-1681, Patriarch 1652-1666) served as the architect of the mid-17th-century liturgical reform programme. The architecture: Nikon's reforms aimed at bringing Russian liturgical practice into conformity with contemporary Greek Orthodox practice — changing the sign of the cross from two fingers to three, modifying liturgical texts, changing the spelling of Jesus's name in Slavonic, and other changes. The reforms produced the *Raskol* (Schism) of 1666: the Old Believers (*Starovery*) rejected the reforms and split from the Patriarchal Church. The Old Believers were subject to centuries of state persecution including the *strel'tsy* uprisings, the self-immolations of the late 17th century, and continued institutional marginalisation through to the 20th century. The Old Believers operate as one of the principal historical schisms within Russian Orthodoxy.

**Peter the Great's abolition of the Patriarchate 1721.** Peter the Great (Peter I of Russia, 1672-1725, Tsar from 1682, Emperor from 1721) served as the architect of the governing subordination of the Russian Orthodox Church to the Russian Empire. The architecture: Peter refused to fill the Patriarchal throne after Patriarch Adrian's death in 1700; Peter promulgated the *Spiritual Regulations* (drafted by Feofan Prokopovich) on 25 January 1721, abolishing the Patriarchate and replacing it with the Most Holy Synod — a collegial body of bishops and lay procurators chaired by an Imperial-appointed Chief Procurator (*Ober-Prokuror*). The Most Holy Synod operated as a department of the Russian Empire across the 1721-1917 period — the configuration of the Synodal Period dossiered in § II.

**The Synodal abolition.** The 1721 Synodal arrangement served as the historical instance of explicit Russian state capture of the Russian Orthodox Church. The Tsar operated as *de facto* head of the Church (with the *Spiritual Regulations* explicitly affirming this position); the Chief Procurator was the Tsar's direct representative in church governance; the Patriarchate was suppressed for 197 years (1721-1917). The configuration is the principal historical precedent for the contemporary church-state integration dossiered in §§ III-IV — and as the principal historical evidence that Russian Orthodox church-state integration is not a contemporary innovation but a thousand-year pattern interrupted only by the persecution period.

The summary of § I: the Moscow Patriarchate's foundational architecture operates across a thousand-year history from the 988 Baptism of Kievan Rus through to Peter the Great's 1721 abolition. The configuration is characterised by: (1) the church-state symphony of pre-Mongol Kievan Rus; (2) the post-1240 relocation to Moscow and the post-1453 "Third Rome" ideological architecture under Philotheos; (3) the 1547 Ivan IV autocratic configuration; (4) the 1589 Patriarchal establishment; (5) the 1666 Old Believer schism; (6) the 1721 Petrine abolition of the Patriarchate. The Russian Orthodox church-state integration pattern operates as a thousand-year configuration that the contemporary Moscow Patriarchate explicitly invokes as its historical-theological architecture.

---


### II. The Synodal Period and the Soviet Persecution (1721 – 1991)

The 1721-1991 period is the arc from Peter the Great's capture of the Russian Orthodox Church through to the post-Soviet revival. The arc has two principal sub-periods: the 1721-1917 Synodal Period (Russian Imperial state capture); the 1917-1991 Soviet period (Soviet state persecution, partial accommodation, and KGB infiltration).

**The Synodal Period 1721-1917.** The architecture: the Most Holy Synod served as the principal institutional body of the Russian Orthodox Church; the Chief Procurator (*Ober-Prokuror*) was the Tsar's direct representative in church governance; the principal Chief Procurators of the 19th century include Konstantin Pobedonostsev (1827-1907, Chief Procurator 1880-1905) — operationally the architect of late-Imperial Russian Orthodox conservative theology and tutor to both Tsar Alexander III and Tsar Nicholas II. The Synodal ROC operated as: (1) a department of the Russian Imperial state; (2) the principal religious-cultural vehicle of the Russian autocracy; (3) the vehicle through which the Russian state administered religious life across the Empire including the suppression of non-Orthodox religious communities (Old Believers, Jews, Catholics in Russian-occupied Poland-Lithuania, Lutherans in the Baltic provinces, Muslims, Buddhists, sectarian groups); (4) the vehicle of the Russification campaigns across the Empire's non-Russian territories.

**The 1905 Revolution and the post-1905 reforms.** The 1905 Russian Revolution produced the partial reform of the Russian Imperial religious configuration. The architecture: the 17 April 1905 Imperial decree on religious tolerance loosened restrictions on non-Orthodox religious communities; the Local Council of Moscow was permitted for the first time since the 17th century. The documented pre-revolutionary Orthodox configuration of 1914: around 54,000 Orthodox churches and chapels in the Russian Empire; over 100,000 Orthodox clergy (bishops, priests, deacons, monks, nuns); over 1,000 monasteries and convents; the ROC operated as one of the largest single institutional religious configurations in the world.

**The Local Council of Moscow 1917-1918 and the restoration of the Patriarchate.** The Russian Revolution of February 1917 produced the principal opportunity for reform of the Russian Orthodox Church. The architecture: the Local Council of Moscow (also called the All-Russian Sobor) opened 15 August 1917 (28 August new style) and continued through to 7 September 1918; the Council restored the Patriarchate that Peter the Great had abolished in 1721; Patriarch Tikhon (Vasily Belavin, 1865-1925) was elected on 5 November 1917 — three days before the Bolshevik October Revolution (7 November new style). The Local Council is the moment of post-Petrine Russian Orthodox reform — and its proceedings were interrupted by the Bolshevik seizure of power.

**Patriarch Tikhon and the early Bolshevik persecution.** Patriarch Tikhon's tenure (1917-1925) served as the moment of the Russian Orthodox response to the Bolshevik regime. The architecture: Tikhon anathematised the Bolshevik regime in his epistle of 19 January 1918 (1 February new style); the Bolshevik regime nationalised church property through the 23 January 1918 Decree on the Separation of Church and State (drafted by Lenin); the Bolshevik regime executed Tsar Nicholas II and the Imperial family on 17 July 1918 at Yekaterinburg. The 1922-1923 famine relief crisis: the Bolshevik regime demanded the surrender of Russian Orthodox liturgical objects ostensibly for famine relief; the Patriarchate resisted; documented mass arrests and executions of clergy followed across the 1922-1923 period (over 8,000 clergy and laity killed). The Bolshevik regime sponsored the *Living Church* (*Renovationist*) schism from 1922 as an vehicle for the Soviet state's capture of the Russian Orthodox Church. Patriarch Tikhon was placed under house arrest 1922-1923; he died on 7 April 1925 in circumstances widely believed to involve Bolshevik poisoning (the position remains contested but is the standard scholarly inference).

**The Sergius Declaration 29 July 1927.** Patriarch (then Metropolitan) Sergius Stragorodsky (1867-1944) issued the "Declaration of Loyalty" to the Soviet regime on 29 July 1927 — operating as the moment of Russian Orthodox accommodation to the Soviet regime. The Declaration stated that "your joys are our joys and your sorrows are our sorrows" — meaning the Soviet state's. The Declaration produced the split between the Sergian Patriarchal Church (which operated under Soviet accommodation) and various non-Sergian Russian Orthodox formations including: the Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia (ROCOR, founded 1921 at Sremski Karlovci Serbia) — operating in exile, refusing to recognise the Sergian accommodation through to the 2007 reunion; the Catacomb Church (the underground Russian Orthodox configuration that operated inside the Soviet Union from 1927). The Sergius Declaration is the moment of the historical Russian Orthodox church-state accommodation pattern — a pattern that returned in the post-Soviet period under Patriarch Kirill.

**The 1929-1941 Soviet persecution.** The principal moment of anti-Orthodox persecution operated across the 1929-1941 period under Stalin. The architecture: the 1929 Soviet Decree "On Religious Associations" sharply restricted religious activity; the closure of churches accelerated across the 1929-1939 period; the execution and imprisonment of clergy accelerated through the Great Purges of 1937-1938 (with the most senior Russian Orthodox clergy disproportionately targeted). Documented figures: around 54,000 functioning Russian Orthodox churches in 1914 declined to around 200-500 by 1939; around 100,000+ clergy in 1914 declined to around 4,500 functioning clergy by 1939; around 95% of pre-revolutionary clergy were killed, imprisoned, or exiled by 1939; estimated 100,000-200,000 Russian Orthodox clergy and active laity killed across the 1917-1939 period (the figures are contested with low and high estimates spanning a wide range).

**Stalin's 1943 rapprochement.** The architecture: on 4 September 1943 Stalin met in the Kremlin with the three remaining Russian Orthodox metropolitans — Sergius (Stragorodsky), Alexius (Simansky), and Nicholas (Yarushevich) — and offered restoration in exchange for ROC service to the Soviet war effort. The Patriarchate was restored within days; Sergius was elected Patriarch on 8 September 1943 (he died 15 May 1944); Alexius I (Sergei Simansky, 1877-1970) was elected on 2 February 1945 and served until his death in 1970. Subsequent Patriarchs: Pimen (Sergei Izvekov, 1910-1990, served 1971-1990); Alexius II (Aleksey Ridiger, 1929-2008, served 1990-2008); Kirill (Vladimir Gundyayev, born 1946, from 1 February 2009 — dossiered in § III). The Stalin rapprochement is the principal Soviet-era restoration moment of Russian Orthodox life — operating as the foundational moment of the post-1943 Russian Orthodox configuration that continues under direct institutional continuity to the contemporary period.

**The 1958-1964 Khrushchev anti-religious campaign.** Nikita Khrushchev (Soviet leader 1953-1964) launched the principal post-Stalin anti-Orthodox campaign across 1958-1964. The architecture: the closure of around 13,000 of the 22,000 functioning Russian Orthodox churches across the 1958-1964 period; the arrest and persecution of clergy and active laity; the increase in anti-religious propaganda. The campaign represented the second principal anti-Orthodox persecution moment after the 1929-1941 campaign.

**The 1961 World Council of Churches membership and KGB-vetted ecumenical engagement.** The Russian Orthodox Church joined the World Council of Churches in 1961 under KGB-vetted configurations. The architecture: the use of ROC clergy as KGB agents for foreign intelligence work; the integration of the ROC into the Soviet intelligence apparatus across the 1961-1991 period; the KGB operative names assigned to specific ROC hierarchs. The Christopher Andrew and Vasili Mitrokhin *Mitrokhin Archive* (1999) — based on Mitrokhin's smuggled KGB documents — operates as one of the documentary records of the KGB-ROC integration. The documented allegations of Patriarch Kirill's KGB-agent code name "Mikhailov" emerged from briefly-opened Soviet archives in the early 1990s and have been investigated by Soviet/Russian journalists including Yevgenia Albats and Andrey Soldatov.

**The 1988 Millennium of the Baptism of Rus.** The Mikhail Gorbachev rapprochement with the ROC across 1985-1988 served as the principal Soviet-era thaw moment. The architecture: the April 1988 meeting between Gorbachev and Patriarch Pimen and the Holy Synod (the first such meeting since Stalin's 1943 rapprochement); the permitted celebration of the Millennium of the Baptism of Rus across June 1988 with international participation; the partial restoration of churches and monasteries. The 1988 moment is the principal start of the post-Soviet ROC revival that accelerated through the 1991 Soviet collapse dossiered in § III.

**The pattern across the 1721-1991 period.** The arc is the two-and-a-half-century pattern of ROC church-state integration interrupted by the persecution period. The Synodal Period (1721-1917) integrated the ROC into the Russian Imperial state as a department; the Soviet Period (1917-1991) inverted the integration through persecution-then-accommodation — with the 1917-1943 persecution destroying the infrastructure and the 1943-1991 accommodation rebuilding it under KGB-vetted state-loyal terms. The 1988-1991 Gorbachev-era transition restored the Russian Orthodox Church to institutional vitality with the post-Soviet church-state relationship dossiered in § III.

The summary of § II: the 1721-1991 period is the arc that produced the contemporary Russian Orthodox configuration. The Synodal Period demonstrated the historical pattern of Russian state capture of the Russian Orthodox Church. The persecution period demonstrated the consequences of state hostility to the Russian Orthodox Church; the post-1943 Stalin rapprochement demonstrated the pattern of Russian Orthodox accommodation to state power; the KGB infiltration period (1961-1991) demonstrated the integration of Russian Orthodox clergy into the Soviet intelligence apparatus. The contemporary Russian Orthodox apparatus dossiered in §§ III-IV is the successor to all four of these patterns.

---


### III. Patriarch Kirill, the Post-Soviet Revival, and the Russian World Ideology (1991 – 2022)

The 1991-2022 period is the arc of the post-Soviet ROC revival under Patriarchs Alexius II (1990-2008) and Kirill (from 1 February 2009). The arc produced: (1) the and infrastructural rebuilding of the ROC from the Soviet wreckage. (2) the structural reintegration of the Russian Orthodox Church with the state under Yeltsin and especially under Putin (Russian President 2000-2008, 2012-present, Prime Minister 2008-2012). (3) the articulation of the *Russkiy Mir* (Russian World) ideology as the principal contemporary geopolitical-theological framework of the Russian Orthodox Church. (4) the 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly crisis that fractured Eastern Orthodox unity globally. (5) the preparation for the church's contemporary role in the post-2022 Russia-Ukraine war dossiered in § IV.

**Patriarch Alexius II (1990-2008).** Aleksey Mikhailovich Ridiger (1929-2008, born in Tallinn Estonia of Baltic-German Orthodox background) served as the architect of the post-Soviet ROC revival. The architecture: elected Patriarch on 7 June 1990; presided over the 1991 Soviet collapse and the re-emergence of the Russian Orthodox Church; oversaw the reopening of around 23,000 closed churches across the 1990-2008 period; oversaw the rebuilding of around 1,000 destroyed monasteries; oversaw the canonisation of Tsar Nicholas II and the Imperial family as "passion-bearers" on 20 August 2000; presided over the 2007 reunion with the Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia (ROCOR) through the Act of Canonical Communion signed at Christ the Saviour Cathedral on 17 May 2007. Alexius II died on 5 December 2008.

**The 1997 Federal Law "On Freedom of Conscience and Religious Associations."** The principal post-Soviet Russian religious-legal architecture: Federal Law 125-FZ adopted 26 September 1997 under President Yeltsin established a hierarchical configuration of "traditional" Russian religions — Russian Orthodoxy as the principal historical-cultural tradition, Islam, Buddhism, and Judaism as the other "traditional" religions, with newer or foreign religious groups subjected to restrictions. The 1997 Law is the legal architecture of the contemporary Russian privileging of the Russian Orthodox Church.

**The 2000 *Bases of the Social Concept* of the Russian Orthodox Church.** The Russian Orthodox Bishops' Council of 13-16 August 2000 adopted the document *Bases of the Social Concept of the Russian Orthodox Church* — the principal post-Soviet text of Russian Orthodox social and political theology. The architecture: the document defined the Russian Orthodox Church's positions on church-state relations (affirming the Byzantine *symphonia* model), on social issues (family, marriage, sexuality, abortion, bioethics), on economic life, on international relations, and on the contemporary Russian state. The document served as the architecture of the post-Soviet ROC political theology under Alexius II — and was authored under the principal influence of then-Metropolitan Kirill, who chaired the Synodal Working Group that produced the document.

**Patriarch Kirill: institutional background and ascent.** Vladimir Mikhailovich Gundyayev (born 20 November 1946 Leningrad — now Saint Petersburg) is the architect of the contemporary Russian Orthodox political-ideological configuration. The architecture: ordained deacon 1969; ordained priest 1969; appointed Bishop of Vyborg 1976; appointed Metropolitan of Smolensk and Kaliningrad 1984; appointed head of the Department for External Church Relations (DECR) 1989-2009 — operating as the Russian Orthodox foreign-policy architect throughout the post-Soviet period; elected Patriarch of Moscow and All Rus on 27 January 2009; enthroned 1 February 2009. Kirill's ascent through the DECR position 1989-2009 was the preparation for his Patriarchal tenure — including the integration of the Russian Orthodox Church with the post-Soviet state's foreign policy apparatus.

**The Kirill KGB-agent allegation.** The documented allegation that Kirill operated as a KGB agent under the code name "Mikhailov" emerged in the early 1990s from briefly-opened Soviet archives during the post-Soviet documentary thaw. The allegation: Kirill (then Archimandrite, then Bishop) operated as a KGB agent for the Soviet intelligence apparatus from the early 1970s through the 1991 Soviet collapse, primarily through his international travels as a Russian Orthodox ecumenical and foreign-policy representative (including World Council of Churches engagements). The allegation has been documented by multiple Russian investigative journalists including Yevgenia Albats (*The State Within a State* 1994); Andrey Soldatov and Irina Borogan (*The New Nobility* 2010); and by Western Sovietologists drawing on the briefly-opened KGB archives. The Russian Orthodox Church and Patriarch Kirill have denied the allegation. The position remains contested but the archival evidence (briefly available in the early 1990s before re-classification under Putin) is widely cited by Western scholars and Russian independent journalists.

**The "Russian World" (*Russkiy Mir*) ideology.** The principal contemporary Russian Orthodox geopolitical-theological framework is the *Russkiy Mir* ("Russian World") concept. The architecture: Patriarch Kirill articulated the *Russkiy Mir* concept in multiple speeches across his Patriarchate from 2009 onwards; the *Russkiy Mir* Foundation was established by Russian presidential decree on 21 June 2007 under Putin as the secular-state vehicle of the same project; the *Russkiy Mir* concept claims a transnational Russian Orthodox civilisational sphere encompassing Russia, Ukraine, Belarus, parts of Moldova, parts of Kazakhstan, the Russian Orthodox diaspora, and (in maximalist articulations) the historic Russian Empire and Soviet territories. The *Russkiy Mir* operates simultaneously as a religious-cultural concept (Russian Orthodox civilisational unity), a geopolitical project (Russian state influence over the post-Soviet space), and a political-ideological framework (Russian state and Russian Orthodox Church operating in coordination as defenders of "traditional values" against Western liberalism). The *Russkiy Mir* is the principal theological architecture of the contemporary ROC-Russian-state alliance.

**The church-state integration under Putin.** The 2000-2022 period served as the moment of Russian Orthodox church-state integration. The architecture: Putin's documented personal religious self-presentation as an Orthodox believer from the early 2000s; the collaboration between the ROC and the state across multiple policy domains — including the 2013 "gay propaganda" Federal Law 135-FZ (cross-reference Chapter 14 § IV), the 2016 anti-missionary "Yarovaya Law" (Federal Law 374-FZ adopted 7 July 2016 — restricting non-Orthodox religious activity), the 2020 constitutional amendments (effective 4 July 2020) including the explicit reference to "faith in God" in the Russian Constitution; the governing coordination on "traditional values" framing across the 2010s-2020s; the Russian Orthodox chaplaincy in the Russian military (formally reintroduced from 2010); the Russian Orthodox presence in Russian state schools through the *Foundations of Religious Cultures and Secular Ethics* curriculum (introduced 2012).

**The 2012 Pussy Riot trial.** The documented Pussy Riot trial operates as one of the principal post-Soviet documented moments of ROC-Russian-state collaboration against political opposition. The architecture: members of the Pussy Riot feminist punk-protest collective (Maria Alyokhina, Nadezhda Tolokonnikova, Yekaterina Samutsevich) performed the "Punk Prayer" at Christ the Saviour Cathedral in Moscow on 21 February 2012, calling for the Virgin Mary to "chase Putin away"; the three were arrested and prosecuted; the trial concluded on 17 August 2012 with two-year prison sentences for "hooliganism motivated by religious hatred"; the ROC publicly supported the prosecution; Samutsevich was released on probation in October 2012; Alyokhina and Tolokonnikova were released in December 2013 under a general amnesty. The Pussy Riot case is the principal contemporary moment of the church-state collaboration against political-cultural dissent in the Putin-Kirill period.

**The 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly crisis.** The principal post-Soviet ecclesiastical-political crisis in Eastern Orthodoxy: the Patriarchate of Constantinople under Patriarch Bartholomew I (born 1940, Patriarch from 1991) granted the *Tomos of Autocephaly* to the Orthodox Church of Ukraine on 6 January 2019 — establishing the OCU as an autocephalous Eastern Orthodox church independent of the Moscow Patriarchate. The architecture: the OCU was formally established on 15 December 2018 at the Unification Council in Kyiv with Metropolitan Epiphanius (Dumenko, born 1979) as its first head; the Moscow Patriarchate severed eucharistic communion with the Patriarchate of Constantinople on 15 October 2018; the ROC subsequently severed eucharistic communion with the Greek Orthodox Church of Greece (2020), the Patriarchate of Alexandria (2019), and the Orthodox Church of Cyprus (2020) over their recognition of the OCU. The Ukrainian autocephaly crisis is the principal contemporary fracturing of global Eastern Orthodox unity — and as one of the principal institutional triggers of the post-2022 ROC-Russian-state escalation against Ukraine dossiered in § IV.

**The Ukrainian Orthodox Church-Moscow Patriarchate (UOC-MP) configuration.** The Ukrainian Orthodox Church-Moscow Patriarchate operated in the post-Soviet period as the Moscow-aligned Ukrainian Orthodox configuration. The architecture: the UOC-MP under Metropolitan Onufry (Berezovsky, born 1944, head of UOC-MP from August 2014) served as the pre-2022 Moscow-aligned Ukrainian Orthodox jurisdiction with around 12,000-13,000 parishes in Ukraine as of 2014. The UOC-MP operates as one of the principal vehicles of Russian state influence in Ukraine across the post-Soviet period — alongside other instruments dossiered in § IV.

**The configuration as of 24 February 2022.** As of the eve of the Russian full-scale invasion of Ukraine, the ROC operated as: (1) the official religious-institutional partner of the state under Putin; (2) the major contemporary articulator of the *Russkiy Mir* civilisational-imperial framework; (3) one of the largest single Eastern Orthodox jurisdictions worldwide with around 150 million claimed faithful; (4) the centre of an Eastern Orthodox communion that had been fractured by the 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly crisis; (5) under the principal leadership of Patriarch Kirill (then aged 75) who had been Patriarch for 13 years and who served as the principal religious-ideological architect of the *Russkiy Mir* project. The configuration is the setup for the post-24 February 2022 Russian Orthodox role in the Russia-Ukraine war dossiered in § IV.

The summary of § III: the 1991-2022 post-Soviet revival operated under Patriarchs Alexius II (1990-2008) and Kirill (from 1 February 2009) as the reconstruction of the Russian Orthodox Church and its reintegration with the post-Soviet state. The principal architecture: the 1997 religious-legal framework privileging "traditional" religions. The 2000 *Bases of the Social Concept* under Kirill's authorship. Kirill's 2009 Patriarchal accession; the *Russkiy Mir* civilisational-imperial framework; the collaboration with the Putin-era Russian state on "traditional values" and on the 2013 "gay propaganda" framework (cross-reference Chapter 14 § IV); the 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly crisis as the ecclesiastical-political precursor of the post-2022 war.

---


### IV. The Russia-Ukraine War and the Contemporary Configuration (2022 – 2026)

The 2022-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of the Moscow Patriarchate's institutional role in the Russia-Ukraine war. The architecture has six principal documented arcs: (1) Patriarch Kirill's endorsement of the Russian invasion from the first weeks. (2) the Russian Orthodox Church's documented integration with the state's war effort and propaganda apparatus. (3) the Russian Orthodox punishment of dissenting clergy. (4) the international Eastern Orthodox response and the Kirill sanctions. (5) the Ukrainian response including the 20 August 2024 law banning Moscow-affiliated religious organisations. (6) the fracturing of the Russian Orthodox global jurisdiction across Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania, and other former Moscow-jurisdiction territories.

**Patriarch Kirill's endorsement of the Russian invasion.** The architecture: on the first Sunday after the Russian full-scale invasion of 24 February 2022, Patriarch Kirill delivered his Forgiveness Sunday sermon at Christ the Saviour Cathedral Moscow on 6 March 2022 — framing the Russian military operations as a defence of "Holy Rus" against Western "so-called values" including LGBT+ rights. The sermon stated that the invasion would determine "which side of God humanity will be on." Kirill articulated the framing: the Russian-Ukrainian conflict operates as a metaphysical conflict between the Russian Orthodox civilisational sphere ("Holy Rus") and a satanic Western secular liberalism that had captured Ukraine through its 2014 Maidan Revolution and the post-2014 Western-aligned Ukrainian government. The 6 March 2022 sermon operates as one of the principal contemporary moments of Russian Orthodox theological-political endorsement of the war.

**Patriarch Kirill's September 2022 "absolution" sermon.** On 25 September 2022 Kirill delivered a sermon at Alexander Nevsky Cathedral Moscow stating that "sacrifice in the course of carrying out your military duty washes away all sins" — operating as the principal Russian Orthodox theological framing of death in the Russian war effort against Ukraine. The framing operates as a departure from traditional Russian Orthodox just-war theology — operating to provide a quasi-soteriological framing of Russian military deaths in the war.

**The 27 March 2024 "Holy War" declaration.** The World Russian People's Council (*Vsemirnyy Russkiy Narodnyy Sobor*, VRNS), chaired by Patriarch Kirill, adopted on 27 March 2024 the document *On the Present and Future of the Russian World* declaring the Ukraine war a "Holy War" (*Svyashchennaya voyna*). The architecture: the document stated that "from a spiritual and moral point of view, the special military operation is a Holy War, in which Russia and its people, defending the unified spiritual space of Holy Rus, fulfil the mission of the 'Holder', protecting the world from the onslaught of globalism and the victory of the West that has fallen into Satanism." The 27 March 2024 declaration is the principal contemporary Russian Orthodox theological-political endorsement of the war — and as the moment of the Russian Orthodox abandonment of just-war theology in favour of explicit holy-war theology against Ukraine and the West.

**The institutional punishment of dissenting clergy.** The Russian Orthodox Church under Patriarch Kirill has documentedly punished dissenting clergy across the 2022-2026 period. The architecture: Patriarch Kirill mandated the "Prayer for Holy Rus" (also called the "Prayer for the Restoration of Peace") to be recited at the end of every Russian Orthodox liturgy from 2022 — with the prayer including the petition that God "aid Thy people and grant us victory" (against Ukraine). The prayer's text was not approved through the standard Russian Orthodox liturgical-commission process — operating as an irregularity that has been documented by Russian Orthodox liturgical scholars.

**Documented dissenting-clergy cases:** Ioann Koval (priest in the Moscow Diocese) was defrocked in May 2023 for replacing the word "victory" with the word "peace" in the prayer — the first documented defrocking case under the new prayer regime. Alexei Uminsky (rector of the Church of the Life-Giving Holy Trinity Moscow for 30 years, presided over the funeral of former Soviet President Mikhail Gorbachev on 3 September 2022) was dismissed and defrocked in early 2024 after refusing to recite the prayer; Patriarch Kirill affirmed the defrocking on 8 February 2024. The Christians Against War database (an online tracking project) documented at least 28 Russian Orthodox priests inside Russia punished for anti-war positions by early 2024, plus 6 from ROC parishes in Lithuania, 5 in Belarus, and 1 in Kazakhstan. The architecture is the Russian Orthodox suppression of internal dissent against the war.

**The international sanctions on Patriarch Kirill.** The architecture: the United Kingdom imposed sanctions on Patriarch Kirill on 16 June 2022 — making the UK the first Western state to formally sanction a religious leader in response to the Ukraine war. The European Commission proposed Kirill for EU sanctions in May 2022 but Hungary (under Prime Minister Viktor Orbán) reportedly blocked the inclusion. The Czech Republic imposed sanctions on Kirill in 2023 and expelled at least one Russian cleric. Lithuania imposed sanctions and travel restrictions on Russian Orthodox clergy. Bulgaria expelled three Russian Orthodox priests (two Belarusians, one Russian) on national security grounds in 2023. The international sanctions configuration is the contemporary Western state response to the Patriarchate's institutional role in the war.

**The international Eastern Orthodox response.** The principal documented international Eastern Orthodox responses to the war: Patriarch Bartholomew I of Constantinople condemned the invasion in multiple statements from February 2022 onwards; Patriarch Theodore II of Alexandria condemned the invasion; Patriarch Daniel of Romania condemned the invasion; Archbishop Anastasios of Albania condemned the invasion. The Russian Orthodox Church Outside of Russia (ROCOR, reunited with Moscow Patriarchate 17 May 2007) issued initial statements expressing "deep concern" but did not formally condemn Russia or Patriarch Kirill. The Serbian Orthodox Church, the Georgian Orthodox Church, and the Bulgarian Orthodox Church operated with more ambivalent positions through 2024 (with internal tensions in each case). The Greek Orthodox Church of Greece, the Patriarchate of Alexandria, and the Orthodox Church of Cyprus operated with documented sanctions of Russian Orthodox positions through the post-2018 autocephaly crisis dossiered in § III.

**The Estonian, Latvian, Lithuanian configurations.** The Russian Orthodox Church operates documented jurisdictions in the three Baltic states — territories with documented Russian-speaking minority populations and post-Soviet presence. The architecture of the post-2022 breaks: the Latvian Orthodox Church declared full autocephaly from Moscow by Latvian parliamentary law on 8 September 2022 — operating as the first post-2022 break with Moscow. The Estonian Orthodox Church severed ties with Moscow in 2024 under significant Estonian state pressure. The Lithuanian Orthodox Church has experienced documented internal tensions with Russian Orthodox clergy in Lithuania removed by the Lithuanian state on national security grounds. The Baltic configurations operate as contemporary moments of the post-2022 fragmentation of the Russian Orthodox global jurisdiction.

**The Ukrainian response.** The architecture: the Ukrainian Orthodox Church (UOC, formerly UOC-Moscow Patriarchate) under Metropolitan Onufry condemned the Russian invasion in May 2022; dropped "Moscow Patriarchate" from its name; modified its founding documents to claim independence from Moscow. The Ukrainian state under President Volodymyr Zelenskyy operated with growing pressure on the UOC across the 2022-2025 period: revocation of Metropolitan Onufry's Ukrainian citizenship 2024; over 600 UOC parishes transferred to the Orthodox Church of Ukraine (OCU) across the 2022-2024 period — with around 8,000 UOC parishes remaining under formal Moscow jurisdiction as of 2024-2025.

**The 20 August 2024 Ukrainian law banning Moscow-affiliated religious organisations.** The Verkhovna Rada (Ukrainian parliament) adopted the Law "On the Protection of the Constitutional Order in the Sphere of Activity of Religious Organisations" on 20 August 2024 by a vote of 265-29. The architecture: the law bans the activities of religious organisations affiliated with the Russian Orthodox Church or with the Russian state; the law required Ukrainian religious organisations to demonstrate complete independence from Moscow; the law's implementation has operated across the 2024-2026 period through Ukrainian state administrative and judicial processes. On 27 August 2025 the Ukrainian State Service on Ethnopolitics and Freedom of Conscience (DESS) formally found the UOC to be linked to the Moscow Patriarchate and petitioned the courts to ban UOC activities. As of 11 September 2025, over 180 criminal cases had been opened against UOC priests (including 23 bishops) and 38 priests had been convicted per Ukrainska Pravda reporting.

**The contemporary contested-jurisdiction configuration in Ukraine.** The architecture of contemporary Orthodox Ukraine: around 70% of Ukrainians self-identify as Orthodox per a 2024 Kyiv International Institute of Sociology survey; most Orthodox Ukrainians identify with the autocephalous Orthodox Church of Ukraine (OCU, recognised by Constantinople 6 January 2019); around 8,000 parishes still operating under formal UOC structures (with the UOC continuing to claim independence from Moscow despite the state's finding of continued Moscow links). The international human-rights response to the Ukrainian response has been mixed: the UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights expressed concern in October 2025 about potential religious persecution of the UOC, while also citing Russian state restrictions on religious freedom in Russian-occupied Ukrainian territories (targeting Catholics, Muslims, Jehovah's Witnesses, and non-Moscow-aligned Orthodox); Human Rights Watch criticised the Ukrainian ban as a violation of freedom of religion; the US Commission on International Religious Freedom raised concerns about the Ukrainian law in 2024 but emphasised that "Russia remains the most profound threat to religious freedom in Ukraine."

**The Russian Orthodox documented role in Russian-occupied Ukrainian territories.** The architecture: in Russian-occupied territories of Ukraine (Crimea from 2014; Donbas from 2014; expanded occupations from 2022), the ROC has absorbed UOC parishes into Moscow Patriarchate jurisdiction; documented cases of Russian Orthodox clergy passing Ukrainian military positions to Russian forces; closures of non-Moscow-Orthodox, Catholic, Protestant, Muslim, and Jewish religious organisations in occupied territories; restrictions on Ukrainian-language religious practice. The Russian Orthodox role in Russian-occupied territories is the principal contemporary moment of the Russian Orthodox Church's integration with Russian military and security forces — and as the principal evidence cited by Ukrainian and Western governments and human-rights organisations of the contemporary ROC-Russian-state alliance.

**The captured-state pattern in the Russian Orthodox configuration.** The Moscow Patriarchate is the principal Eastern Orthodox captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The configuration generates institutional contradictions parallel to those dossiered for the Vatican (Chapter 15) and the American Evangelical apparatus (Chapter 16): (1) claim to traditional Christian moral authority operates against the endorsement of military violence including the "Holy War" framing of March 2024 and the "sins washed away" framing of September 2022; (2) claim to independence from the Russian state operates against the collaboration with the Russian state across the 2000-2026 period including the religious-ideological alignment with the *Russkiy Mir* civilisational-imperial framework; (3) claim to pan-Orthodox unity operates against the fracturing of Eastern Orthodox unity through the 2018-2019 Ukrainian autocephaly crisis and the post-2022 breaks across the Baltic states and elsewhere; (4) claim to KGB-Soviet rupture operates against the institutional continuities from the post-1943 Stalin-era ROC configuration including the KGB-agent allegations regarding Patriarch Kirill himself.

**The contemporary Russian Orthodox configuration.** As of 2026, the ROC operates as: (1) the official religious-institutional partner of the state under Putin (third Putin term, fourth Putin tenure including the 2008-2012 Medvedev interregnum); (2) the principal religious-vehicle of the *Russkiy Mir* civilisational project; (3) under international sanctions from the UK, Czech Republic, and other Western states; (4) the centre of a contested global Eastern Orthodox jurisdiction with documented breaks from Latvia (2022), Estonia (2024), Lithuania (de facto restrictions), with the Ukrainian configuration under Ukrainian state action; (5) the principal religious-institutional architect of the contemporary Russian endorsement of the Russia-Ukraine war as a "Holy War" against the West. The configuration is the principal Eastern Orthodox captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period.

The summary of § IV: the 2022-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of the Moscow Patriarchate's institutional role as a Russian state apparatus in the Russia-Ukraine war. The configuration delivered: (1) theological endorsement of the war through the 6 March 2022 Forgiveness Sunday sermon, the 25 September 2022 "sins washed away" sermon. And the 27 March 2024 "Holy War" VRNS declaration. (2) institutional punishment of dissenting clergy through the Koval (May 2023) and Uminsky (February 2024) defrockings and the Christians Against War-documented at least 40 cases of clergy punishment. (3) international response through the UK sanctions June 2022 and subsequent Western sanctions. (4) Ukrainian response through the 20 August 2024 law and the subsequent UOC ban proceedings. (5) fracturing of the Russian Orthodox global jurisdiction across the Baltic states and Ukraine. The section closes here. Chapter 18 dossiers the Saudi religious-export apparatus as the principal Sunni Muslim captured-state configuration of the contemporary period — operating in cross-reference with Chapter 5 (Sunni Muslim foundational dossier) and with post-1979 global Wahhabi-Salafi institutional export.

---


## Chapter 18. The Saudi Religious-Export Apparatus

Chapter 18 dossiers the religious-export apparatus as the major Sunni Muslim captured-state configuration of the contemporary period. The evidence operates in cross-reference with Chapter 5 (Sunni Muslim foundational dossier) and operates structurally as the Sunni parallel to Chapter 15 (the Vatican apparatus), Chapter 16 (the American Evangelical-Political apparatus), and Chapter 17 (the Moscow Patriarchate): a contemporary working-apparatus dossier rather than a foundational-tradition dossier.

The evidence covers four sections: § I the foundational architecture from the 1744 Pact of Diriyah between Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and Muhammad ibn Saud through to the 1932 unification of Saudi Arabia under King Abdulaziz; § II the post-1973 Petro-Islam era and the global Wahhabi-Salafi export apparatus; § III the Saudi religious establishment architecture. The Council of Senior Scholars, the Grand Mufti, the Ministry of Islamic Affairs, the Holy Mosques presidency, and the Saudi religious universities; § IV Vision 2030, Mohammed bin Salman, and the contemporary religious-political reconfiguration 2015-2026.

The chapter's working configuration differs from the three preceding contemporary apparatus chapters. The Vatican operates a single juridical-territorial sovereign state with hierarchical institutional command. American evangelicalism operates a distributed network of denominations and parachurch organisations. The Moscow Patriarchate is a church-state integration with the Russian state. The Saudi apparatus operates through a third configuration: a state-religious-establishment alliance — the Al ash-Sheikh religious clerical family in pact with the Al Saud political dynasty since 1744 — that exports its religious doctrine globally through Saudi and affiliated organisations using oil revenue.
