The apparatus has served as the major Sunni Muslim religious-political configuration of the contemporary period, with influence over Sunni religious practice from West Africa to South-East Asia, and with contemporary reconfiguration under Mohammed bin Salman's Vision 2030 programme.

### I. The Foundational Architecture: The First Saudi State 1744 and the Al ash-Sheikh–Al Saud Pact

**Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (1703-1792)** served as the theological architect of the contemporary religious configuration. The architecture: born around 1703 at al-Uyaynah in the Najd region of central Arabia; studied at Medina under Hanbali scholars; studied briefly in Basra; returned to Najd around 1740 preaching a Hanbali-inflected revivalist Sunni doctrine focused on *tawhid* (the absolute oneness of God), denunciation of practices he labelled *shirk* (idolatry/polytheism), and rejection of theological accretions he labelled *bid'ah* (innovation). His principal theological texts: *Kitab al-Tawhid* ("The Book of Monotheism"); *Kashf al-Shubuhat* ("The Clearing of Doubts"). The theological programme: absolute monotheism interpreted to forbid intercession through saints, veneration of tombs and shrines, the celebration of the Prophet Muhammad's birthday (*Mawlid*), and any practice deemed an accretion to the original Islam of the first three generations of Muslims (*al-salaf al-salih* — hence "Salafi"). The programme was rejected by the Hanbali scholarly establishment of Medina, the Sufi orders that dominated popular Sunni Islam, the Shia communities of the Arabian Peninsula and Iraq, and most contemporary Sunni religious authorities. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's doctrine survives because of the political alliance dossiered next.

**The 1744 Pact of Diriyah.** Muhammad ibn Saud (c. 1687-1765), Emir of the small Najdi oasis settlement of Diriyah, entered into a religious-political alliance with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in 1744. The architecture: ibn Saud would provide political protection and military force to implement ibn Abd al-Wahhab's religious programme; ibn Abd al-Wahhab would provide religious legitimation to the political authority of ibn Saud. The pact established the principle of divided authority: religious authority (the *Imam*) in the Al ash-Sheikh family (literally "the family of the Sheikh," meaning ibn Abd al-Wahhab's descendants); political authority (the *Emir*, later *Malik* — King) in the House of Saud (Al Saud). The pact has was the founding architecture of every subsequent Saudi state through to the contemporary Kingdom of Saudi Arabia — making the 1744 Pact of Diriyah the operative founding document of contemporary Saudi political theology.

**The First Saudi State (Emirate of Diriyah, 1744-1818).** The First Saudi State expanded across the 1744-1818 period through military conquest under the Al Saud-Al ash-Sheikh alliance. The architecture: control of central Arabia (Najd) by the 1770s; expansion into eastern Arabia (al-Hasa) by the 1790s; expansion into the Hejaz including the conquest of Mecca and Medina across 1803-1805 under Saud bin Abdulaziz; the destruction of Sufi tombs and shrines in conquered territories; the sack of the Shia city of Karbala in present-day Iraq on 21 April 1802, with the destruction of the tomb of Husayn ibn Ali (the Shia Imam, grandson of Muhammad, killed at Karbala in 680 CE) and the killing of an estimated 2,000-5,000 Shia residents. The First Saudi State served as the principal historical instance of Wahhabi religious-political violence in expansionary mode — a pattern that contemporary scholars including Cole Bunzel (*Wahhābism: The History of a Militant Islamic Movement*, Princeton 2023) and David Commins (*The Wahhabi Mission and Saudi Arabia*, I.B. Tauris 2006) have traced as the foundational pattern of subsequent Salafi-Wahhabi militant configurations.

**The 1818 Ottoman destruction of the First Saudi State.** The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Mahmud II responded to Saudi-Wahhabi expansion through Ottoman-Egyptian military intervention under Muhammad Ali Pasha's son Ibrahim Pasha (1789-1848). The architecture: Ottoman-Egyptian forces reconquered the Hejaz in 1813 and pushed into Najd from 1816; the siege of Diriyah ended on 11 September 1818 with the Saudi-Wahhabi defeat; Diriyah was razed to the ground; the final Emir Abdullah bin Saud was taken to Istanbul and beheaded in December 1818. The 1818 destruction was the end of the First Saudi State and the apparent end of the Wahhabi religious-political project. The Wahhabi doctrine and the Al Saud-Al ash-Sheikh political-religious alliance survived through the Second and Third Saudi States dossiered next.

**The Second Saudi State (Emirate of Nejd, 1824-1891)** was the Wahhabi-Saudi restoration moment under Turki bin Abdullah Al Saud (1755-1834, Emir 1824-1834). The architecture: Turki recaptured Riyadh in 1824 and re-established the Al Saud-Al ash-Sheikh political-religious alliance on a reduced territorial basis (central Arabia only); the Second Saudi State operated through to 1891 when it was destroyed by the rival Al Rashid family of Ha'il (with documented Ottoman backing). The Al Saud family went into exile in Kuwait under Ottoman protection. The 1891-1902 interregnum was the second apparent end of the Wahhabi-Saudi project.

**The Third Saudi State and Ibn Saud (Abdulaziz bin Abdul Rahman Al Saud, c. 1875-1953).** The architecture: Ibn Saud (born around 1875 in Riyadh, exiled to Kuwait from 1891 onwards) recaptured Riyadh in January 1902 through a night raid against the Al Rashid garrison; he consolidated control of Najd across 1902-1913; he conquered al-Hasa (eastern Arabia) from the Ottoman Empire in 1913; he conquered Asir 1922; he conquered the Hejaz from the Hashemite Sharif Hussein bin Ali across 1924-1925 (Mecca taken 13 October 1924, Medina taken 5 December 1925, Jeddah taken 23 December 1925); he proclaimed the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia on 23 September 1932. The Third Saudi State is the contemporary Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in direct institutional continuity from 1902 onwards.

**The British protectorate framework.** The 1915 Treaty of Darin (26 December 1915, also called the Anglo-Najdi Treaty) established the British protectorate over Najd recognising Ibn Saud's authority. The architecture: British strategic interest in supporting Ibn Saud against the Ottoman Empire during the First World War; British recognition of Saudi independence through the 1927 Treaty of Jeddah (20 May 1927). The British architecture operated as the external state framework through which the Third Saudi State emerged.

**The Hejaz conquest and the destruction of Islamic heritage.** The Wahhabi-Saudi conquest of the Hejaz across 1924-1925 produced the systematic destruction of Sunni-Shia-Sufi Islamic heritage sites in Mecca and Medina. The architecture: the Jannat al-Baqi cemetery in Medina (the historic burial ground of the Prophet Muhammad's family including his daughter Fatima, his grandsons Hasan and Husayn — sacred to both Sunni and Shia traditions) was demolished on 21 April 1925 with the destruction of tombs and mausoleums; the Jannat al-Mu'alla cemetery in Mecca (the burial ground of the Prophet Muhammad's first wife Khadija and other early Muslim figures) was demolished in 1925; further destructions of historical Islamic buildings across the Hejaz across the 1925-2026 period have included the houses of the Prophet Muhammad's wives, the houses of the Companions, and other early Islamic sites — destroyed primarily to make way for hotel and infrastructure expansion serving the Hajj pilgrimage. The destruction is the principal Wahhabi-Saudi cultural-religious pattern of erasing the material heritage of pre-Wahhabi Islam. The Saudi state position: the destructions prevent *shirk* (idolatry) through the veneration of buildings.

**The oil concession and the Quincy Agreement.** The architecture: Standard Oil of California (SOCAL) received the Saudi oil concession on 29 May 1933; oil was discovered at Dammam Well No. 7 ("Prosperity Well") on 3 March 1938; the American Arabian Oil Company (Aramco) was established on 31 January 1944 (renamed Saudi Aramco after Saudi state purchase 1980); US President Franklin D. Roosevelt met King Abdulaziz aboard USS *Quincy* in the Suez Canal on 14 February 1945 — establishing the US-Saudi security-for-oil framework that has served as one of the principal architectures of post-WWII Saudi external relations. The Quincy Agreement operates as the external architecture through which Saudi oil revenue funded the post-1973 Petro-Islam apparatus dossiered in § II.

The summary of § I: the religious-export apparatus operates on a foundational architecture established by the 1744 Pact of Diriyah between Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and Muhammad ibn Saud. The pact produced three successive Saudi states (1744-1818, 1824-1891, 1902-present) operating through the Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud religious-political alliance. The Third Saudi State established the contemporary Kingdom of Saudi Arabia on 23 September 1932 and inherited control over Mecca and Medina — making the Saudi state the custodian of the two holiest sites in Sunni Islam (with the Saudi monarch styled "Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques" from 1986). The configuration's external framework operates through the post-1945 US-Riyadh security-for-oil architecture and the post-1933 American-Saudi oil concession architecture — the external conditions for the post-1973 global Wahhabi-Salafi export apparatus dossiered in § II.

---


### II. Post-1973: Petro-Islam and the Global Wahhabi-Salafi Export Apparatus

The post-1973 period is the principal moment of the global expansion of the religious-export apparatus. The architecture is conditioned by three documented external events of 1973-1979 that operated together to produce the apparatus: the 1973 OPEC oil embargo and the resulting fourfold increase in kingdom's revenue. The 1979 Iranian Revolution producing a Shia theocratic challenger to Saudi religious-political authority; the 1979 Soviet invasion of Afghanistan opening a channel for Saudi religious-military deployment in coalition with the US and Pakistan. The the kingdom's response across the 1973-2005 period was the construction of a global Wahhabi export apparatus through kingdom's institutions, Saudi-funded Islamic NGOs, and Saudi-financed mosque, madrassa, publishing, and broadcasting operations across the Muslim world and Muslim diaspora.

**The 1973 OPEC oil embargo and the Petro-Islam configuration.** The architecture: the 1973 Yom Kippur War (6-25 October 1973) between Israel and the Arab states; the OPEC oil embargo announced 16 October 1973 against Western states supporting Israel; oil prices quadrupled from around $3 per barrel to around $12 per barrel; kingdom's revenue increased commensurately. The term "Petro-Islam" — coined by Lebanese-American political scientist Fouad Ajami in the early 1980s — denotes the configuration produced by the application of post-1973 Saudi oil revenue to the global propagation of Wahhabi Sunni Islam. The principal scholarly accounts: Gilles Kepel, *Jihad: The Trail of Political Islam* (Harvard University Press 2002); David Commins, *The Wahhabi Mission and Saudi Arabia* (I.B. Tauris 2006). spending estimates on the global Wahhabi propagation programme range from $75 billion to over $100 billion across the 1973-2018 period per multiple scholarly assessments.

**The Muslim World League (MWL, *Rābiṭat al-ʿĀlam al-Islāmī*).** Founded on 18 May 1962 in Mecca by King Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Saud and Grand Mufti Muhammad bin Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh — originally established to counter the Nasser-era pan-Arab nationalist challenge to the Saudi monarchy. The architecture: headquartered in Mecca; coordinated under Riyadh from inception; opened offices in cities including Copenhagen, London, Moscow, Paris, Rome, Vienna, New York, and Washington across the 1960s-1980s; funded the construction of mosques, the distribution of Qurans, the training of imams, and the publication of Wahhabi theological literature globally. Per Gilles Kepel, the MWL operated as "the first organised and systematic institution whose declared intent was to Wahhabize Islam globally to counteract the wave of socialist Arab Nationalists led by Nasser's Egypt during the Arab Cold War." The MWL served as the principal kingdom's institutional vehicle of Wahhabi global propagation across the 1962-2016 period. Its post-2016 reconfiguration under Mohammed bin Salman's Vision 2030 is dossiered in § IV.

**The World Assembly of Muslim Youth (WAMY).** Founded 1972 in Riyadh — Saudi-affiliated NGO focused on Muslim youth globally. WAMY operated through to the 2010s as one of the principal Saudi vehicles of Wahhabi-Salafi propagation among Muslim youth in non-Muslim-majority countries, particularly through documented summer camps, scholarship programmes, and youth conferences.

**The International Islamic Relief Organization (IIRO).** Founded 1978 by the MWL — kingdom-affiliated humanitarian organisation operating across the Muslim world. IIRO has been linked in documented US Treasury and 9/11 Commission findings to extremist financing networks across the 1980s-2000s, with some IIRO branches sanctioned by the US Treasury post-2001.

**The Islamic Development Bank (IsDB).** Established by Articles of Agreement adopted 12 August 1974 at the Conference of Finance Ministers of Muslim Countries Jeddah; opened for business 20 October 1975; headquartered Jeddah. The IsDB is the multilateral Islamic-finance development bank serving member states of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC, founded 25 September 1969 in Rabat under Saudi-Moroccan-Pakistani initiative). The IsDB and the OIC operate as the principal Saudi-allied multilateral apparatus through which the post-1973 Petro-Islam configuration is coordinated at the intergovernmental level.

**The King Fahd Complex for the Printing of the Holy Quran.** Founded 1984 in Medina under King Fahd bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1921-2005, King 1982-2005); produced over 300 million copies of the Quran for global distribution by 2020. The architecture: the complex produces translations of the Quran into around 80 languages with Wahhabi-Salafi exegetical apparatus; copies are distributed free of charge through kingdom diplomatic missions, the Hajj pilgrimage, the MWL, and other Saudi vehicles. The complex is the principal kingdom's institutional vehicle of Quran-text propagation globally — operating with influence on the Quranic text and translation conventions used in Sunni Muslim communities worldwide.

**Petro-Islam in operation: documented scale.** Per multiple scholarly accounts including Riyadh's own historical records: from 1964 to 2004, Saudi Arabia built 1,359 mosques, 210 Islamic centres, 202 colleges, and 2,000 schools in non-Muslim-majority countries; 16 American mosques, 4 Canadian mosques, and mosques in London, Madrid, Brussels, and Geneva were Saudi-funded. The Brussels Grand Mosque was leased to Saudi Arabia in 1969 and operated under kingdom management until February 2018 (when Saudi management was relinquished under the Vision 2030 reconfiguration dossiered in § IV). The King Fahd Academy in Bonn Germany closed at the end of the 2016-2017 school year. The architecture operated through Riyadh's funding via the Saudi embassy religious-affairs attachés, through the MWL, through WAMY, through individual royal endowments, and through Saudi religious-charitable networks. The aggregate spending on global religious propagation across the 1973-2018 period is estimated at $75-100+ billion per multiple sources.

**Three 1979 triggers and the escalation.** Three documented events of 1979 operated together to produce the escalation of the Petro-Islam apparatus across the 1980s. First, the Iranian Revolution of February 1979 — establishing a Shia theocracy under Ayatollah Khomeini and producing the Shia-Sunni regional ideological-political competition that has served as one of the principal drivers of Middle East geopolitics across the 1979-2026 period. Second, the Grand Mosque seizure of 20 November 1979 in Mecca — Juhayman al-Otaybi and around 400-500 Ikhwan-inspired Islamist insurgents seized the Grand Mosque demanding the overthrow of the Al Saud and the implementation of a more rigid Salafi-Wahhabi programme; the two-week siege ended with the deaths of around 250+ people; Riyadh's response was to *increase* the religious establishment's social powers domestically while *accelerating* Wahhabi-Salafi propagation abroad (the response of the post-1979 kingdom's being to outflank domestic Salafi-Wahhabi challengers through increased religious orthodoxy rather than reform). Third, the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan of 25 December 1979 — opening the channel for Riyadh-CIA-Pakistani religious-military deployment through the Afghan mujahideen across the 1980s.

**The Afghan mujahideen and the Riyadh-CIA-Pakistani-ISI architecture.** The 1979-1989 Afghan war served as the principal intersection of Saudi religious-export apparatus with US Cold War operations and Pakistani-ISI institutional channels. The architecture: Riyadh's funding (matched around one-for-one with US CIA funding through the Pakistani Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI)) channelled through the Pakistani ISI to the Afghan mujahideen; the founding of Maktab al-Khidamat (MAK, the Services Office) in Peshawar 1984 by Abdullah Azzam (1941-1989) and Osama bin Laden (1957-2011); the growth of Pakistani Deobandi madrassas through the 1980s with Saudi funding — producing the foundation for the post-1994 Taliban movement and for the post-1988 al-Qaeda organisation. Per Carnegie analysis: around 85% of Pakistani military mosques operate on Deobandi teachings — giving the Pakistani-Deobandi-Salafi network influence over the Pakistani military religious sphere. The Afghan jihad is the intersection point at which post-1973 Petro-Islam met US Cold War strategy and Pakistani-ISI institutional channels — producing the post-1989 Salafi-jihadi configurations that operated globally across the 1989-2026 period.

**The Bosnia, Chechnya, Algeria, and Yemen channels (1990s).** The post-1989 period served as the principal moment of Saudi-funded Salafi-jihadi mobilisation across multiple zones. The architecture: Saudi funding of Salafi-jihadi fighters in the Bosnian War (1992-1995); Saudi institutional involvement in the First Chechen War (1994-1996) and Second Chechen War (1999-2009); Saudi funding of Algerian Salafi-jihadi configurations across the 1990s Algerian Civil War; Saudi institutional involvement in Yemen across the 1990s-2026 period including the post-2015 Saudi military intervention. The 1990s channel is the intermediate moment between the 1980s Afghan jihad and the post-9/11 global Salafi-jihadi configuration.

**9/11 and the post-2001 international scrutiny.** The 11 September 2001 attacks on the United States served as the principal external pressure moment on the religious-export apparatus. The architecture: 15 of the 19 hijackers were Saudi nationals; Osama bin Laden was a Saudi national (stripped of citizenship 1994); the 9/11 Commission Report (released 22 July 2004) documented the Saudi-source funding architecture of al-Qaeda and noted the structural ambiguities of kingdom's involvement (with the "28 pages" of the Commission Report — the Joint Inquiry into Intelligence Community Activities chapter on Saudi connections — declassified 15 July 2016 detailing Riyadh's diplomatic and consular interactions with the hijackers). The post-2001 period operated under documented sustained US and international scrutiny of religious-export operations — producing institutional pressure on Riyadh to reform the apparatus that contributed to the eventual Vision 2030 reconfiguration dossiered in § IV.

**The kingdom's response to post-9/11 scrutiny.** The architecture: kingdom's public commitments to combat extremist financing across the 2001-2015 period; Riyadh's crackdowns on al-Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP) following the 2003 Riyadh compound bombings; kingdom's institutional reforms of the IIRO and other Saudi-affiliated charity networks; documented continued spending on global Wahhabi-Salafi propagation through the MWL and other Saudi and affiliated vehicles since the post-2001 period. The post-2001 reforms was the insufficient incremental adjustment that preceded the post-2015 MBS-era reconfiguration dossiered in § IV.

The summary of § II: the post-1973 Saudi religious-export apparatus is the principal global Sunni Muslim religious-political configuration of the late 20th and early 21st centuries. The apparatus was constructed through the application of post-1973 Saudi oil revenue (estimated $75-100+ billion across 1973-2018) to the global propagation of Wahhabi Sunni Islam through the Muslim World League (founded 1962). The World Assembly of Muslim Youth (founded 1972), the King Fahd Complex for the Printing of the Holy Quran (founded 1984), the International Islamic Relief Organization (founded 1978), the Islamic Development Bank (founded 1975), and a network of Saudi and affiliated institutional vehicles. The apparatus produced influence over Sunni religious practice from West Africa to South-East Asia, with intersections with US Cold War strategy through the 1980s Afghan jihad and institutional consequences through the post-1989 Salafi-jihadi configurations. The post-2001 period operated under international scrutiny that produced the post-2015 reconfiguration under Mohammed bin Salman dossiered in § IV.

---


### III. The Saudi Religious Establishment Institutional Architecture

The Saudi religious establishment is the embodiment of the 1744 Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud pact within the contemporary Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Council of Senior Scholars as the senior collegial juridical body. (2) the Grand Mufti as the senior individual religious authority. (3) the Ministry of Islamic Affairs. Dawah and Guidance as the coordinating state ministry. (4) the General Presidency for the Affairs of the Two Holy Mosques as the vehicle of the kingdom's custodianship of Mecca and Medina. (5) the Saudi religious universities including Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University and the Islamic University of Medina as the central training institutions of the apparatus. (6) the (now stripped) Committee for the Promotion of Virtue and Prevention of Vice. The religious police. The documentation covers each.

**The Council of Senior Scholars (*Hai'at Kibar al-'Ulama*).** Established 1971 by King Faisal — the senior kingdom's juridical body. The architecture: composed of senior Saudi religious scholars appointed directly by the King; issues binding fatwas (religious rulings) on matters of Islamic law and Saudi state policy; is the supreme religious authority below the Grand Mufti; meets in Riyadh under the chairmanship of the Grand Mufti. The Council is the mechanism through which kingdom's juridical authority is exercised — and as the body that has provided religious legitimation for major state policy decisions including the 1990-1991 invitation of US forces during the Gulf War (against Salafi-jihadi opposition), the post-2001 counter-terror policies, the 2015-2017 social reforms, and the broader Vision 2030 reform programme. Per documented Baker Institute (Mohammad Fadel) and Carnegie scholarly analysis, the Council remains aligned with the royal family — reinforcing rather than challenging the political authority of the Al Saud.

**The Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia (*Mufti 'Amm al-Mamlaka*).** Operates as the senior individual religious authority of the Saudi state. The architecture: historically appointed from the Al ash-Sheikh family — direct descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab; chairs the Council of Senior Scholars and the Standing Committee for Scholarly Research and Ifta; issues principal Saudi juridical pronouncements; operates with rank equivalent to a Saudi minister. The Grand Mufti chronology of the contemporary period: Muhammad bin Ibrahim Al ash-Sheikh (1893-1969, Grand Mufti 1953-1969); the post was vacant 1969-1993 with religious authority distributed among the Council of Senior Scholars; Abdulaziz bin Baz (Abd al-Aziz ibn Baz, 1910-1999, Grand Mufti 1993-1999) — blind from childhood; one of the most documented influential Salafi religious figures of the late 20th century; Abdulaziz Al ash-Sheikh (1943-2025, Grand Mufti 1999-2025) — the third Grand Mufti of the contemporary period; Saleh al-Fawzan (born 28 September 1935 in Ash-Shamsiyyah Al-Qassim Province, Grand Mufti from 22 October 2025) — the fourth Grand Mufti and the first non-Al ash-Sheikh Grand Mufti since the 1953 establishment of the post.

**The Saleh al-Fawzan appointment (October 2025).** The architecture: Saleh al-Fawzan (born 1935, studied at Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University, member of the Council of Senior Scholars from 1991) was appointed Grand Mufti on 22 October 2025 following the death of Abdulaziz Al ash-Sheikh. The appointment is a institutional moment in the contemporary kingdom's religious-political reconfiguration: al-Fawzan is the first non-Al ash-Sheikh Grand Mufti — breaking the pattern of the Grand Mufti position being held by direct descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab. The break is a institutional signal of the post-2015 MBS-era reconfiguration of the religious establishment dossiered in § IV — although al-Fawzan himself operates within the Salafi-Wahhabi theological tradition and has been positioned as a continuity-conservative appointment.

**The Standing Committee for Scholarly Research and Ifta (*Lajnat al-Ifta*).** Operates as the principal Saudi state mechanism for the issuance of fatwas; chaired by the Grand Mufti; composed of senior religious scholars including current and former Council of Senior Scholars members. The committee issues fatwas on individual cases referred by Saudi citizens and Saudi state organs — operating as the centre of Saudi day-to-day religious-juridical governance.

**The Ministry of Islamic Affairs, Dawah and Guidance.** Established 1993 — the principal Saudi state ministry coordinating religious policy. The architecture: oversees Saudi mosques, imams, and religious educational institutions; coordinates the Saudi-state export operations including the Muslim World League, World Assembly of Muslim Youth, and King Fahd Complex for the Printing of the Holy Quran; is the central Saudi state vehicle through which the contemporary religious-export apparatus is administered. Current Minister: Abdullatif Al ash-Sheikh from 2018.

**The Committee for the Promotion of Virtue and Prevention of Vice (the *Mutawa'in*, religious police).** The architecture: established in its contemporary form in the 1940s under King Abdulaziz; operated through to April 2016 with documented broad enforcement powers over Saudi public morality including dress codes, gender segregation, prayer-time observance, and the prohibition of alcohol; documented mutawa'in arrests, beatings, and excessive enforcement actions across the 1940s-2016 period produced international scrutiny and domestic Saudi resentment. The Royal Decree of April 2016 (under Crown Prince Mohammed bin Nayef and Deputy Crown Prince MBS) stripped the mutawa'in of arrest powers, transferring enforcement authority to the regular Saudi police; the mutawa'in continue to operate in a advisory-monitoring capacity only. The April 2016 stripping is one of the principal moments of the post-2015 MBS-era religious establishment reconfiguration dossiered in § IV — and as the end of around seven decades of mutawa'in enforcement authority.

**The General Presidency for the Affairs of the Two Holy Mosques.** Operates as the central Saudi state body managing al-Masjid al-Haram (the Grand Mosque of Mecca, the holiest site in Sunni Islam) and al-Masjid an-Nabawi (the Prophet's Mosque, Medina, the second-holiest site). The architecture: directly accountable to the Saudi King; coordinates the annual Hajj pilgrimage (in 2024, the Hajj drew around 1.83 million Muslims from around 180+ countries) and the year-round Umrah pilgrimage; coordinates the massive Mecca and Medina infrastructure expansion programmes that have operated across the 1980s-2026 period (including the multi-billion-dollar Grand Mosque Expansion Project and the expansion of Mecca hotel and transport infrastructure). The Saudi monarch's title "Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques" (*Khadim al-Haramayn al-Sharifayn*) was adopted by King Fahd on 26 October 1986 — operating as the central religious-political legitimation framing of the contemporary Saudi state.

**Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU).** Founded as the College of Sharia in 1953; chartered as Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University in 1974; headquartered Riyadh — the senior kingdom's religious-educational institution. The architecture: produces graduates trained in Hanbali-Wahhabi-Salafi theology and Islamic jurisprudence; served as the principal institutional training ground for Saudi clerics throughout the post-1974 period; reportedly trained thousands of Salafi clerics from across the Muslim world through scholarship programmes for foreign students; operates branch campuses including in Indonesia. The university has been positioned as one of the principal vehicles of the export apparatus through its production of foreign-trained Salafi clerics who returned to their home countries to operate as Saudi-aligned religious authorities.

**The Islamic University of Medina (IUM).** Founded 1961 by King Saud — Medina-based — the senior kingdom's educational institution focused on training non-Saudi Muslim students. The architecture: founded specifically to train foreign Muslim students from across the Muslim world in Salafi-Wahhabi theology; documented to have produced graduates who returned to their home countries to operate as religious authorities and religious educators carrying the Salafi theological-juridical framework. The IUM is one of the principal vehicles of the export apparatus — operating through the production of Saudi-trained Salafi clerics globally rather than through direct Saudi-state physical presence.

**Umm al-Qura University (Mecca).** Operates as one of the major kingdom's academic institutions serving the Holy Mosques; founded 1981 (as a development of the earlier Mecca religious educational infrastructure dating to 1949); operates with juridical research operations and produces graduates serving Saudi religious institutions globally.

**The institutional architecture as a Saudi-state apparatus.** The Saudi religious establishment is the central Saudi-state institutional apparatus of the contemporary Sunni Muslim world. The architecture: every senior religious-position is appointed by the Saudi King; every senior juridical body operates under state oversight; every kingdom's religious-educational institution operates with state funding and state curricular control; every Saudi religious-export operation (MWL, WAMY, King Fahd Quran Complex) operates under state coordination. The Saudi religious establishment is not an independent religious authority operating in parallel to the Saudi state; the apparatus is the kingdom's religious arm — providing religious legitimation for state policy while implementing the Saudi state's policy objectives both domestically and through the global Wahhabi-Salafi export operations. This pattern of complete state-religious-establishment integration is the foundational configuration of contemporary Saudi religious politics — and as the architecture that has produced both the post-1973 Petro-Islam global export apparatus and the post-2015 Vision 2030 reconfiguration dossiered in § IV.

The summary of § III: the religious establishment is a fully Saudi-state-controlled institutional apparatus through six principal institutional nodes. The Council of Senior Scholars (1971), the Grand Mufti position (with the October 2025 break from the Al ash-Sheikh institutional pattern under Saleh al-Fawzan), the Ministry of Islamic Affairs (1993). The General Presidency for the Affairs of the Two Holy Mosques, the Saudi religious universities (Imam Muhammad ibn Saud Islamic University, Islamic University of Medina, Umm al-Qura University), and the (post-2016 stripped) Committee for the Promotion of Virtue and Prevention of Vice. The architecture is the embodiment of the 1744 Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud pact within the contemporary Kingdom of Saudi Arabia — and as the governing vehicle through which the post-1973 Petro-Islam apparatus and the post-2015 Vision 2030 reconfiguration are administered.

---


### IV. Vision 2030, Mohammed bin Salman, and the Contemporary Reconfiguration (2015 – 2026)

The post-2015 period is the principal contemporary moment of reconfiguration of the religious-export apparatus under Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (MBS, born 31 August 1985). The architecture has six principal documented arcs: (1) the consolidation of MBS political power across 2015-2017. (2) the Vision 2030 economic-diversification programme launched April 2016. (3) the post-2015 social-cultural liberalisation programme including the lifting of the women driving ban and the reopening of cinemas. (4) the repression of dissenting Awakening (*Sahwa*) clerics from September 2017. (5) the reorientation of the global Saudi religious-export operations under Muhammad al-Issa at the Muslim World League from August 2016. (6) the continuity of the underlying Al Saud-religious-establishment alliance under reconfigured terms. The configuration generates institutional contradictions: the post-2015 reforms have produced liberalisation of Saudi domestic religious-cultural life and reorientation of the Saudi religious-export operations, but operate alongside documented escalation of Saudi-state authoritarian repression of dissenting religious and political figures and operate without dismantling the underlying Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud architecture dossiered in §§ I-III.

**The ascent of Mohammed bin Salman 2015-2017.** The architecture: King Abdullah bin Abdulaziz Al Saud died 23 January 2015 at age 90; King Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (born 31 December 1935) ascended the throne the same day; Mohammed bin Salman (MBS) — sixth son of King Salman by his third wife Fahda bint Falah — was appointed Defence Minister 23 January 2015 (at age 29, the youngest defence minister in the world); MBS was appointed Deputy Crown Prince April 2015; MBS replaced Mohammed bin Nayef as Crown Prince on 21 June 2017 in a palace consolidation; MBS was appointed Prime Minister 27 September 2022 (with King Salman remaining as monarch). The architecture is the contemporary moment of generational power transition within the House of Saud — and as the consolidation of executive authority in MBS personally.

**Vision 2030 (launched 25 April 2016).** The Saudi state economic-diversification and social-reform programme — operating as the principal state policy framework of the post-2016 period. The architecture: economic-diversification objectives aiming to reduce Saudi state dependence on hydrocarbon revenue; social-reform objectives including the lifting of social restrictions on women, entertainment, and public life; institutional-reform objectives including the streamlining of state administration; the Public Investment Fund (PIF) as the principal sovereign-wealth vehicle operating with assets target of $2 trillion by 2030 (over $1.7 trillion by 2025 per Saudi statements). The Vision 2030 programme is the framework within which the post-2015 reconfiguration of the religious-export apparatus has been articulated — although the explicit religious-reform content of the programme operates with institutional ambiguity (continuing engagement with the Wahhabi-Salafi religious establishment while reorienting external religious-export operations).

**The November 2017 Ritz-Carlton purge.** The architecture: on 4 November 2017, the Saudi state arrested over 200 Saudi princes, businessmen, and clerics on documented corruption charges and held them at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel Riyadh under conditions of effective detention; the "corruption purge" produced financial settlements with the Saudi state estimated at over $100 billion; the arrests removed potential rivals to MBS political power. The purge is the consolidation moment of MBS personal authority — and as the moment of the post-2015 the kingdom's political reconfiguration.

**The September 2017 *Sahwa* cleric arrests.** The architecture: from September 2017, the Saudi state initiated arrests of Awakening (*Sahwa*) movement clerics — the religious-political opposition movement that emerged in the 1990s as a critical-from-within Salafi configuration challenging both the Saudi state's pro-Western policies and the Saudi religious establishment's quietist accommodation. Principal arrests: Salman al-Awda (born 1955, prominent Saudi cleric) arrested September 2017 — held without trial through 2026; Saudi prosecutors sought the death penalty against him September 2018; Safar al-Hawali (born 1950, prominent *Sahwa* cleric) arrested July 2018; Awad al-Qarni (born 1957) arrested September 2017 with prosecutors seeking the death penalty. The arrests operate as the post-2015 state repression of dissenting religious figures — operating alongside the liberalisation of Saudi social-cultural life. The architecture demonstrates that the post-2015 MBS reforms operate selectively: liberalising Saudi social-cultural life while repressing Salafi-Islamist political-religious opposition with institutional severity. Per Madawi al-Rasheed (*The Son King: Reform and Repression in Saudi Arabia*, Oxford 2020), the post-2015 Saudi configuration combines social-cultural reform with documented political authoritarianism — the "reform and repression" pattern.

**The Khashoggi assassination 2 October 2018.** The architecture: Jamal Khashoggi (1958-2018), Saudi journalist and Washington Post columnist (formerly a Saudi establishment journalist who had documented critical of MBS from 2017), was assassinated at the Saudi consulate in Istanbul on 2 October 2018 by a Saudi state team; the assassination produced international scrutiny; the US Director of National Intelligence (ODNI) report released 26 February 2021 concluded that MBS approved the operation. The architecture is the international moment of the post-2015 Saudi state's authoritarian repression — and as the external limit on the post-2015 MBS reform narrative.

**The Mutawa'in powers stripped April 2016 and the social-cultural reforms.** The architecture: as dossiered in § III, the Royal Decree of April 2016 stripped the Committee for the Promotion of Virtue and Prevention of Vice (the *Mutawa'in*) of arrest powers, transferring enforcement authority to the regular Saudi police. Subsequent social-cultural reforms across the 2017-2020 period: cinemas reopened 18 April 2018 (first cinema since the 1980s); women allowed to attend stadium sporting events from 12 January 2018; the women driving ban lifted 24 June 2018 (with documented continuing repression of women's-rights activists including Loujain al-Hathloul who had campaigned for the lifting); the General Entertainment Authority established 7 May 2016; documented public concerts, festivals, and entertainment events including the Riyadh Season festival 2019-2026 with international entertainment headliners. The architecture is the post-2015 Saudi social-cultural liberalisation programme — operating alongside the continued political repression dossiered above.

**The Muslim World League reconfiguration under Muhammad al-Issa.** The architecture: Muhammad bin Abdul Karim al-Issa (born 1965, former Saudi Minister of Justice 2009-2015) was appointed Secretary General of the Muslim World League in August 2016 — operating as the vehicle of the MBS-era reconfiguration of the religious-export apparatus. Al-Issa has documented disavowed Wahhabism as an institutional category in multiple public statements; visited Auschwitz-Birkenau in January 2020 (the first senior Saudi religious figure to visit the Holocaust memorial); coordinated the May 2019 "Charter of Makkah" — a multi-confessional MWL statement signed by around 1,200 Islamic figures from 139 countries affirming religious diversity, anti-extremism, and inter-religious dialogue. Per Carnegie Endowment analysis (Yasmine Farouk, "Influence Abroad: Saudi Arabia Replaces Salafism in its Soft Power Outreach," December 2024), the post-2016 MWL is the vehicle of the kingdom's reorientation away from Salafi-Wahhabi religious-export and toward broader "moderate Islam" soft-power configurations.

**The reorientation of Saudi religious-export operations.** The architecture: the Brussels Grand Mosque was returned by Saudi Arabia to Belgian authorities in February 2018 — ending around 49 years of Saudi management; the King Fahd Academy Bonn Germany closed at the end of the 2016-2017 school year; documented reductions in Saudi funding of Salafi-Wahhabi mosques and religious institutions across the Balkans, Western Europe, and the United States across 2017-2024 per Carnegie analysis; MWL pivot toward broader inter-religious and "moderate Islam" framings including the October 2024 "Faith in a Changing World" conference under the patronage of King Mohammed VI of Morocco and the May 2024 Kuala Lumpur conference with Malaysian PM Anwar Ibrahim drawing around 2,000 religious leaders from 57 countries. The architecture is the post-2016 Saudi state reorientation of the global religious-export apparatus away from the post-1973 Petro-Islam Salafi-Wahhabi configuration and toward a broader religious-soft-power configuration aligned with the Vision 2030 economic-diversification and Western-engagement programme.

**The Saudi-Iran reconciliation March 2023.** The architecture: on 10 March 2023, the Saudi-Iran diplomatic agreement was announced under Chinese brokerage — restoring bilateral diplomatic relations after the 2016 rupture (caused by the Saudi execution of Sheikh Nimr al-Nimr 2 January 2016 and the resulting attack on the Saudi embassy in Tehran). The agreement is the contemporary moment of post-1979 Saudi-Iranian regional ideological-political competition being placed on a diplomatic-pragmatic basis — with institutional consequences for the religious-export apparatus's anti-Shia framing.

**The institutional continuity of the underlying alliance.** The architecture: despite the post-2015 reforms, the underlying Al Saud-religious-establishment institutional alliance dossiered in §§ I-III operates with documented continuity. The Council of Senior Scholars continues to operate as the principal Saudi religious-juridical body; the Grand Mufti position continues (with the 22 October 2025 appointment of Saleh al-Fawzan as the first non-Al ash-Sheikh Grand Mufti); the Ministry of Islamic Affairs continues to coordinate Saudi religious policy; the Saudi religious universities continue to operate; the Saudi state remains the "Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques" with institutional authority over the global Hajj pilgrimage. Per Baker Institute and Carnegie analysis: the post-2015 reforms have produced social-cultural liberalisation and religious-export reorientation but operate *without* dismantling the underlying Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud architecture — operating instead as a reconfiguration of the apparatus's external operations and domestic enforcement profile rather than a structural rupture.

**The captured-state pattern in the Saudi configuration.** The Saudi religious-export apparatus is the principal Sunni Muslim captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The configuration generates institutional contradictions parallel to those dossiered for the Vatican (Chapter 15), the American Evangelical apparatus (Chapter 16), and the Moscow Patriarchate (Chapter 17): (1) the kingdom's claim to operate as the "Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques" and defender of Sunni Islamic orthodoxy operates against the post-1979 Saudi state's role in producing the contemporary global Salafi-jihadi configuration through Petro-Islam funding and the Afghan jihad architecture; (2) the post-2015 MBS reform narrative of "moderate Islam" operates against the continued state repression of religious-political dissenters and against the authoritarian assassination of Khashoggi October 2018; (3) the kingdom's claim to traditional Islamic moral authority operates against Saudi involvement in the Yemen war 2015-present (with civilian casualties including attacks on religious sites, schools, and hospitals); (4) the Saudi state's post-2016 reorientation of the global religious-export apparatus operates against the continuity of the underlying Al Saud-religious-establishment alliance — leaving the apparatus's structural configuration documentedly intact while reconfiguring its external operations.

**The contemporary Saudi religious-export configuration.** As of 2026, the religious-export apparatus operates as: (1) the official religious-institutional partner of the kingdom under King Salman (in effect retired from active rule) and effective ruler MBS (Crown Prince and Prime Minister); (2) the principal contemporary Sunni Muslim captured-state apparatus operating through the architecture dossiered in § III with post-2015 reconfiguration; (3) operating with documented reduced external Salafi-Wahhabi propagation per the post-2016 MWL reorientation under Muhammad al-Issa; (4) operating with continued Saudi custodianship of Mecca and Medina and continued institutional control over the global Hajj pilgrimage; (5) operating with institutional alignment with Vision 2030 economic-diversification and Western-engagement framing while maintaining the underlying apparatus of the 1744 Pact of Diriyah.

The summary of § IV: the post-2015 MBS-era religious-political reconfiguration is the contemporary moment of partial-reform of the post-1973 Petro-Islam apparatus. The configuration delivered: (1) social-cultural liberalisation including the 24 June 2018 lifting of the women driving ban, the 18 April 2018 reopening of cinemas. And the stripping of mutawa'in arrest powers April 2016. (2) reorientation of Saudi global religious-export operations through the Muhammad al-Issa-led MWL from August 2016. (3) continued political repression of dissenting Saudi religious-political figures including Salman al-Awda September 2017. Safar al-Hawali July 2018, and Awad al-Qarni September 2017. (4) assassination of Jamal Khashoggi 2 October 2018; (5) institutional continuity of the underlying Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud religious-political alliance with the October 2025 appointment of Saleh al-Fawzan as the first non-Al ash-Sheikh Grand Mufti. The section closes here. Chapter 19 dossiers the Iranian Shia theocratic apparatus as the principal Shia Muslim captured-state configuration of the contemporary period. Operating in cross-reference with Chapter 6 (Shia Muslim foundational dossier), with the 1979 Iranian Revolution and Khomeini *velayat-e faqih* theological-political architecture, and with the post-2022 Mahsa Amini protests and the contemporary institutional tensions of the Islamic Republic.

---


## Chapter 19. The Iranian Shia Theocratic Apparatus

Chapter 19 dossiers the theocratic apparatus as the principal Shia Muslim captured-state configuration of the contemporary period. The argument operates in cross-reference with Chapter 6 (Shia Muslim foundational dossier), with Chapter 18 (the Saudi Sunni captured-state apparatus) as the principal Sunni-Shia institutional parallel, and operates structurally as the Shia parallel to the contemporary working-apparatus dossiers of Chapters 15-18. The section opens with the foundational doctrinal and revolutionary architecture of the 1979 Islamic Republic and closes with the dramatic 2025-2026 Iran-Israel war that culminated in the joint US-Israeli assassination of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei on 28 February 2026 and the 8 March 2026 elevation of his son Mojtaba Khamenei as the third Supreme Leader of Iran.

The record covers four sections: § I the foundational architecture from Ruhollah Khomeini's *velayat-e faqih* doctrinal innovation through the 1979 Islamic Revolution; § II the Islamic Republic apparatus. The Supreme Leader, Assembly of Experts, Guardian Council, Expediency Council, Majles, the Presidency, the IRGC, the Quds Force, the Basij, and the regular Artesh. § III the post-revolution repression and dissent record including the 1988 mass executions, the 2009 Green Movement. The 2019 Bloody November, and the 2022-2023 "Woman, Life, Freedom" Mahsa Amini protests. § IV the contemporary Iran-Israel confrontation including the post-7 October 2023 escalation, the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, and the February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation that killed Khamenei.

The chapter's working configuration differs from each of the three preceding contemporary apparatus chapters. The Vatican operates a single juridical-territorial sovereign state. American evangelicalism operates a distributed network. The Moscow Patriarchate is a church-state integration with the post-Soviet Russian state. The Saudi apparatus operates through the Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud religious-political alliance since 1744. The Iranian Shia theocratic apparatus operates through a fifth distinct configuration: a single juridical-territorial state in which the clerical class (the *ulama*) constitutes the supreme political authority through the *velayat-e faqih* doctrine. Making the Islamic Republic of Iran the most institutionally explicit clerical-supremacist state in the contemporary world, with the senior jurist (the Supreme Leader) operating as both the spiritual successor of the Twelfth Imam and the supreme political authority of the Iranian state.

### I. The Foundational Architecture: The 1979 Iranian Revolution and the Khomeini Doctrinal Framework

**Ruhollah Khomeini (c. 24 September 1902 – 3 June 1989)** is the principal architect of the contemporary Iranian Shia theocratic configuration. The architecture: born around 24 September 1902 in Khomeyn Markazi Province Iran into a family of senior clerics (his grandfather Sayyid Ahmad Musawi Hindi and his father Sayyid Mostafa Musawi were both Shia clerics); studied at Qom Seminary under Ayatollah Abdolkarim Haeri Yazdi and later Ayatollah Hossein Borujerdi; reached the rank of *Ayatollah* by the 1940s and *Marja-e Taqlid* (the senior juridical authority — "Source of Emulation") by the 1960s. He opposed Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi's 1963 White Revolution land-reform and women's-suffrage programme; was arrested June 1963; was exiled in November 1964 to Turkey, then Najaf Iraq from October 1965, then Neauphle-le-Château France from October 1978; returned to Tehran on 1 February 1979 after the Shah's departure on 16 January 1979.

**The *Velayat-e Faqih* doctrine.** The principal contemporary doctrinal innovation in Twelver political theology — articulated by Khomeini in his lectures at Najaf 1970 published as *Hukumat-e Islami* ("Islamic Government"). The architecture: traditional Twelver political theology held that during the Occultation of the Twelfth Imam (Imam Mahdi, in concealment since 941 CE per Twelver doctrine), no fully legitimate political authority could exist on earth — making the senior Shia clerics political quietists in principle. Khomeini's *velayat-e faqih* doctrine reversed this: argued that during the Occultation, the senior Shia jurist (*faqih*) should hold supreme political authority — the *Vali-ye Faqih* operating as the deputy of the Hidden Imam in matters of state. The doctrine was a major theological innovation within the Twelver juridical tradition and was rejected by most of the senior Shia clerics of the time — including the most senior Iraqi-Najaf marajiyya. The doctrine survives because of the 1979 revolutionary victory dossiered next — a survival contingent on the political triumph rather than the theological consensus of the Shia tradition.

**The Iranian Revolution 1978-1979.** The architecture: mass protests began January 1978 (the Qom protest of 9 January 1978 over an anti-Khomeini newspaper article); the Cinema Rex fire in Abadan on 19 August 1978 (around 470 killed, blamed on the Shah's SAVAK but later evidence suggested Islamist arsonists); the Black Friday massacre 8 September 1978 (over 100 killed in Tehran's Jaleh Square); months of accelerating protests and strikes through autumn 1978; Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi (1919-1980) departed Tehran on 16 January 1979 — never to return; He returned from Paris exile on 1 February 1979 to a reception of millions in Tehran; the Pahlavi caretaker government under Prime Minister Shapour Bakhtiar collapsed on 11 February 1979; the Islamic Republic of Iran was proclaimed on 1 April 1979 following the referendum of 30-31 March 1979 (with around 98% reported support — the only ballot option being "Islamic Republic: yes or no").

**The 1979 Constitution and the architecture.** The Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Iran was drafted by an Assembly of Experts elected August 1979; ratified by referendum 2-3 December 1979 (around 99% reported support); subsequently amended on 28 July 1989 (the day after Khomeini's death) to consolidate the post-Khomeini institutional architecture. The architecture: Article 1 — the Islamic Republic; Article 4 — all laws and regulations must be based on Islamic criteria; Article 5 — during the Occultation of the Twelfth Imam, authority rests with the just and pious *faqih* aware of the times; Articles 107-110 — Supreme Leader powers and selection; Articles 91-99 — Guardian Council; Article 107 (revised 1989) — the Assembly of Experts elects the Supreme Leader. The constitutional architecture established the structural embodiment of the *velayat-e faqih* doctrine as the foundational law of the Iranian state.

**The US Embassy seizure and the hostage crisis 4 November 1979 – 20 January 1981.** The Iranian student-revolutionary seizure of the US Embassy in Tehran on 4 November 1979 produced a 444-day hostage crisis with 52 American hostages held until 20 January 1981 (released within hours of the Reagan inauguration). The architecture: the seizure was initially supported by Khomeini through subsequent revolutionary consolidation; the crisis produced the rupture of US-Iranian diplomatic relations that has continued through to 2026; the Algiers Accords of 19 January 1981 produced the formal release framework. The hostage crisis is the foundational moment of the contemporary US-Iran institutional hostility — and as one of the principal architectural events of the post-1979 Iranian state's self-positioning as an explicitly anti-Western revolutionary power.

**The Iran-Iraq War 22 September 1980 – 20 August 1988.** Saddam Hussein's Iraq invaded Iran on 22 September 1980 — exploiting post-revolutionary military weakness in Iran. The architecture: eight years of total war producing an estimated 500,000-1,000,000 total casualties (with the Iranian state framing the war as a continuation of the 7th-century Shia martyrdom narrative — using the *karbalaee* framing of self-sacrificial martyrdom that draws on the 680 CE Battle of Karbala in which Imam Husayn was killed); the Iraqi use of chemical weapons against Iranian troops and Kurdish civilians (the Halabja attack 16 March 1988 killing around 5,000 Kurds); UN Security Council Resolution 598 calling for ceasefire 20 July 1987; his acceptance of the ceasefire on 20 August 1988 — which he compared to "drinking the chalice of poison." The Iran-Iraq War is the foundational external war of the Islamic Republic — producing the apparatus of the IRGC, the Basij, and the *Sepah-e Pasdaran* military-political configuration that is the post-1988 Iranian power structure.

**The Mass Executions of Summer 1988.** Following acceptance of the ceasefire with Iraq, Khomeini issued a fatwa in late July 1988 ordering the execution of imprisoned political opponents — primarily members of the Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK) and leftist groups. The architecture: the secret execution of an estimated 3,000-30,000 prisoners (the range reflects the uncertainty given Iranian state secrecy) across July-September 1988 in prisons including Evin Prison Tehran and Gohardasht Prison Karaj; the "Death Commissions" operating in each prison to determine execution; the mass graves at Khavaran Cemetery and other sites. The 1988 mass executions operate as the principal post-1979 atrocity of the Islamic Republic — and as the foundational moment of the governing Iranian repressive apparatus dossiered in § III. Notable: then-prosecutor Ebrahim Raisi (1960-2024) participated on a Tehran Death Commission — a record that operated as one of the principal international human rights concerns during his presidency 2021-2024.

**His death 3 June 1989 and the succession.** Ruhollah Khomeini died on 3 June 1989; his funeral on 6 June 1989 was attended by an estimated 10 million people in Tehran. The Assembly of Experts elected Ali Khamenei (then Iranian President 1981-1989) as the second Supreme Leader on 4 June 1989 — a institutional innovation given that Khamenei held only the rank of *Hojatoleslam* (the rank below Ayatollah) at the time, and that the 1979 Constitution required the Supreme Leader to be a senior *Marja*. The Constitution was amended on 28 July 1989 to lower the rank requirement — accommodating Khamenei's ascent through political rather than juridical seniority. The 1989 succession is the founding moment of the post-Khomeini Islamic Republic configuration that dossiered in § II.

The summary of § I: the theocratic apparatus operates on a foundational architecture established by Ruhollah Khomeini's *velayat-e faqih* doctrinal innovation (articulated 1970 in *Hukumat-e Islami*), the 1979 Iranian Revolution, the December 1979 ratification of the Islamic Republic Constitution, and the post-1989 institutional consolidation under Khamenei. The configuration's foundational features: a doctrinal innovation against most senior Shia clerical opinion of the time. A successful revolutionary seizure of state power in 1979. The institutional embodiment of *velayat-e faqih* in the 1979/1989 Constitution; the post-1980 self-positioning as an anti-Western revolutionary power through the US Embassy seizure and the Iran-Iraq War; the 1988 mass executions as the foundational repressive event; the 1989 succession moment that produced the Ali Khamenei tenure dossiered in § II.

---


### II. The Islamic Republic Institutional Architecture

The Islamic Republic of Iran operates through a dual-track apparatus: an elected track (President, Majles, Assembly of Experts elected by Iranian voters) operating under a clerical-supremacist track (Supreme Leader, Guardian Council, Expediency Council, IRGC under direct Supreme Leader command). The architecture has eight principal nodes that operate together to deliver the *velayat-e faqih* configuration in practice. What follows covers each.

**The Supreme Leader (*Rahbar-e Mo'azzam-e Enghelab*).** The principal political-religious authority under Articles 107-110 of the 1979/1989 Constitution. The architecture: Supreme Leader powers include commander-in-chief of the armed forces; appointment of the head of the judiciary; appointment of six of the twelve Guardian Council members; appointment of its commander; appointment of the heads of state broadcasting; veto over Majles legislation through the Guardian Council; final authority on all major matters of state. Supreme Leader succession: Ruhollah Khomeini (1979 – 3 June 1989); Ali Khamenei (4 June 1989 – 28 February 2026); Mojtaba Khamenei (from 8 March 2026). The post is the supreme position of the Iranian state — the embodiment of the *velayat-e faqih* doctrine in political practice.

**Ali Khamenei (Sayyid Ali Hosseini Khamenei, 19 April 1939 – 28 February 2026).** Operated as the second Supreme Leader for 36 years 8 months — the longest-serving political leader of any contemporary state at the time of his death. The architecture: born Mashhad 19 April 1939; studied at Mashhad and Qom seminaries under Ayatollah Borujerdi and Ayatollah Milani; arrests and exile under the Shah 1963-1978; close associate of Khomeini in the post-1979 revolutionary leadership; second President of Iran 9 October 1981 – 4 August 1989; elected Supreme Leader by the Assembly of Experts on 4 June 1989; killed in joint US-Israeli airstrikes on his Tehran office on 28 February 2026 (dossiered in § IV). The Khamenei tenure 1989-2026 was the post-Khomeini consolidation of the Islamic Republic — including the ascent of the IRGC and the Khamenei family within the Iranian state.

**Mojtaba Khamenei (Mojtaba Hosseini Khamenei, born 8 September 1969 Mashhad).** The third Supreme Leader of Iran from 8 March 2026. The architecture: second son of Ali Khamenei; clerical training at Qom Seminary; IRGC Ground Forces service 1987-1988 in the Iran-Iraq War (27th Mohammad Rasulullah Division, Habib ibn Mazahir Battalion); active in Basij from 2009; appointed *Vakil* (deputy) of the Supreme Leader on 21 August 2008; held that position through to his father's death 28 February 2026; elected third Supreme Leader by the Assembly of Experts on 8 March 2026 — despite the Israeli military statement of 8 March 2026 warning it would pursue every successor to Khamenei and every cleric involved in the succession. The Mojtaba Khamenei elevation is the dynastic succession moment of the Islamic Republic — with Iranian and Western analyst concerns about the precedent of father-to-son Supreme Leader succession in a clerical-supremacist state.

**The Assembly of Experts (*Majles-e Khobregan*).** An 88-member body of senior Shia clerics directly elected by Iranian voters every eight years from a Guardian-Council-vetted candidate list. The architecture: constitutionally charged with electing, supervising, and theoretically removing the Supreme Leader; meets bi-annually in regular session; convenes in extraordinary session for Supreme Leader succession. The Assembly has elected three Supreme Leaders: Khomeini's confirmation 1979; Khamenei 4 June 1989; Mojtaba Khamenei 8 March 2026. The Assembly's theoretical supervisory authority over the Supreme Leader has not been documentedly exercised in practice across the 1979-2026 period — operating as the pattern of clerical-elite ratification rather than effective check on Supreme Leader authority.

**The Guardian Council (*Shora-ye Negahban*).** A 12-member body that is the gatekeeper of the Iranian electoral and legislative system. The architecture: six Islamic jurists (*fuqaha*) appointed directly by the Supreme Leader for six-year terms; six lay jurists (*huquqdanan*) appointed by the head of the judiciary subject to Majles approval; vets all Majles legislation for compatibility with Islamic law and the Constitution; vets all candidates for the Majles, the Presidency, and the Assembly of Experts. The Guardian Council is the principal institutional mechanism through which the clerical-supremacist track of the Islamic Republic constrains the elected track. Documented contemporary examples of Guardian Council vetting: the 2021 presidential election in which the Council disqualified all major reformist candidates leaving Ebrahim Raisi as the predetermined winner; the 2024 presidential election in which the Council permitted Masoud Pezeshkian as a tolerated reformist candidate following the death of Raisi.

**The Expediency Council (*Majma'-e Tashkhis-e Maslahat-e Nezam*).** Established in 1988 by Khomeini decree — operating as the structural mechanism for resolving disputes between the elected Majles and the clerical-supremacist Guardian Council. The architecture: members appointed by the Supreme Leader; chaired by senior figures (the chair from 2017 to his death 9 January 2017 was Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani, then Sadegh Larijani from 2018, then Mohammad Mokhber from 2022 until his elevation to acting President May 2024). The Expediency Council operates as the advisory body to the Supreme Leader on matters of state and as the vehicle through which post-Khomeini institutional disputes within the clerical-political establishment have been resolved.

**The Islamic Consultative Assembly (*Majles*).** A 290-member parliament directly elected for four-year terms with all candidates vetted by the Guardian Council. The architecture: drafts and votes on legislation subject to Guardian Council approval; documented role in approving cabinet appointments and questioning ministers; current speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf (from 28 May 2020). The Majles is the governingly most-elected body of the Iranian system — but operates within the constraints of Guardian Council vetting of candidates and legislation.

**The Presidency (*Ra'is-e Jomhur*).** The most-visible elected position. The architecture: directly elected by Iranian voters for four-year terms; eligible for one re-election; serves as head of government but subordinate to Supreme Leader on all major matters; commander-in-chief of the regular Artesh (but not the IRGC). President chronology: Abolhassan Banisadr 1980-1981 (impeached); Mohammad-Ali Rajai 1981 (assassinated 30 August 1981); Ali Khamenei 1981-1989; Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani 1989-1997; Mohammad Khatami 1997-2005 (the reformist president); Mahmoud Ahmadinejad 2005-2013 (the hardline populist); Hassan Rouhani 2013-2021 (the moderate-pragmatist who negotiated the JCPOA); Ebrahim Raisi 2021-2024 (the hardliner with the 1988 Death Commission record); Mohammad Mokhber (acting May-July 2024 following Raisi's death 19 May 2024 in a helicopter crash in East Azerbaijan); Masoud Pezeshkian (from 28 July 2024 — the reformist permitted by the Guardian Council after Raisi's death). The Presidency is the most-elected position but with documented subordination to the Supreme Leader in practice.

**The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC, *Sepah-e Pasdaran-e Enghelab-e Eslami*).** Established 5 May 1979 under Khomeini decree. The architecture: parallel military force to the regular army (Artesh); reports directly to the Supreme Leader (not through the President or the Defence Minister); commands ground forces, naval forces, aerospace forces, the Basij paramilitary, and the Quds Force external operations branch; documented control over major sectors of the Iranian economy through Khatam al-Anbiya Construction Headquarters and other IRGC-linked enterprises; documented control over Iran's ballistic missile programme. IRGC Commander chronology: Mohsen Rezaee 1981-1997; Yahya Rahim Safavi 1997-2007; Mohammad Ali Jafari 2007-2019; Hossein Salami (1960-2025, IRGC Commander 21 April 2019 – 13 June 2025, killed in Israeli airstrikes); Mohammad Pakpour (1961-2026, IRGC Commander 13 June 2025 – 28 February 2026, killed in joint US-Israeli airstrikes); Ahmad Vahidi (IRGC Commander from 28 February 2026). The IRGC is the principal institutional vehicle of the post-1979 Iranian revolutionary state — and as the principal target of the 2025-2026 Israeli-US military operations dossiered in § IV.

**The Quds Force.** The IRGC external operations branch — operating across the Iranian "Axis of Resistance" network including Hezbollah Lebanon, Hamas and PIJ in Palestine, the Iraqi Shia militias (Kataib Hezbollah, Asaib Ahl al-Haq, etc.), the Houthis in Yemen, and the Syrian state under Bashar al-Assad until December 2024. The architecture: commanded by Qasem Soleimani (1957-2020) from 1998 until his documented assassination by US drone strike at Baghdad airport on 3 January 2020 under Trump first-term orders; commanded by Esmail Qaani from 3 January 2020. The Quds Force is the vehicle of Iranian regional power projection and as the coordinator of the Axis of Resistance configuration dossiered in § IV.

**The Basij (*Niru-ye Moqavemat-e Basij*).** Paramilitary militia formed by Khomeini decree on 26 November 1979; under IRGC command. The architecture: documented mobilisation capacity claimed at over 12 million members in maximum configuration; documented role in Iran-Iraq War human-wave attacks; contemporary role in domestic repression including the 2022-2023 Mahsa Amini protests crackdown dossiered in § III; institutional integration with the IRGC through Basij command. The Basij is the mass-mobilisation paramilitary arm of the post-1979 Iranian theocratic apparatus.

**The Artesh (regular Iranian military).** The pre-1979 Iranian army — operating with reduction in influence relative to the IRGC across the post-1979 period. The architecture: separate command structure under the President as Commander-in-Chief; rivalry with the IRGC; documented complementary deployment with the IRGC in the Iran-Iraq War and in the 2025-2026 Israeli-Iranian war.

The summary of § II: the Islamic Republic of Iran operates through a dual-track architecture combining nominally-democratic elected institutions (Presidency, Majles, Assembly of Experts) with explicitly clerical-supremacist institutions (Supreme Leader, Guardian Council, Expediency Council, IRGC under Supreme Leader direct command). The institutional configuration centres on the Supreme Leader as the embodiment of the *velayat-e faqih* doctrine — operating with documented hereditary succession from Ali Khamenei to Mojtaba Khamenei on 8 March 2026. The configuration produces institutional contradictions: the elected-track institutions are vetted and constrained by the clerical-supremacist track; the "Islamic" framework operates against institutional patterns of repression and corruption documented in §§ III-IV.

---


### III. Repression, Dissent, and the Post-2022 Mahsa Amini Protests

The Islamic Republic operates a record of internal repression across the 1979-2026 period that has produced sustained dissent, periodic mass-protest moments, and institutional responses through arrest, execution, and the expansion of internal security operations. The architecture has six principal arcs: (1) the 1981-1988 Khomeini-era repression including the summer 1988 mass executions. (2) the 1999 student protests. (3) the 2009 Green Movement. (4) the 2017-2018 and 2019 protests over economic conditions. (5) the September 2022 – mid-2023 "Woman, Life, Freedom" Mahsa Amini protests. (6) the post-2022 institutional repression including the September 2024 Hijab and Chastity Law and the expansion of executions.

**The 1981-1988 Khomeini-era repression.** The architecture: executions and disappearances of post-revolutionary political opponents including monarchists, liberal nationalists, Tudeh Party communists, and Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK) members; total post-1979 executions through 1985 estimated at over 12,000 per Amnesty International. The principal atrocity of the period: the summer 1988 mass executions dossiered in § I — Khomeini's fatwa ordering the secret execution of an estimated 3,000-30,000 imprisoned political prisoners (primarily MEK and leftists) across July-September 1988. The Death Commissions operating in Evin Prison Tehran and Gohardasht Prison Karaj — with then-prosecutor Ebrahim Raisi participating on the Tehran Death Commission. The international human-rights record: 1988 UN Special Representative Reynaldo Galindo Pohl's reports; subsequent Amnesty International documentation; the mass graves at Khavaran Cemetery and other sites that remain documentedly unmarked and contested by the Iranian state through to 2026.

**The 1999 Tehran University protests.** The architecture: student protests at Tehran University from 8 July 1999 over the closure of the reformist *Salam* newspaper; Basij and law enforcement raid on the Kuy-e Daneshgah dormitory; multiple student deaths (the count remains contested); arrests of hundreds of students; post-1999 retreat of the Khatami reformist project. The 1999 protests operate as the first major post-revolutionary urban-student protest moment — and as the structural moment of the post-Khatami consolidation of the clerical-IRGC-Basij apparatus over the reformist current.

**The 2009 Green Movement.** The architecture: mass protests following the 12 June 2009 presidential election in which Mahmoud Ahmadinejad was declared the winner over Mir-Hossein Mousavi amid widespread allegations of electoral fraud; protest slogan "Where is my vote?"; protest size estimated at multiple millions in Tehran; IRGC and Basij crackdown including the shooting of Neda Agha-Soltan on 20 June 2009 (filmed and broadcast globally as the symbol of the protest crackdown); Mousavi and Mehdi Karroubi house arrest from February 2011 — continuing through 2026 without formal trial. The Green Movement is the first major post-1979 contested-election moment of the Islamic Republic — and as the moment of the IRGC's expanded internal-security role.

**The 2017-2018 and 2019 economic protests.** The architecture: December 2017 – January 2018 protests in over 100 Iranian cities over economic conditions; protest expansion from initial conservative-cleric organisation against rising food prices to anti-regime political protest; multiple deaths and over 4,900 arrests per state figures. The 2019 "Bloody November" protests of 15-18 November 2019: mass protests across over 100 Iranian cities following the overnight 50% increase in fuel prices; IRGC and Basij crackdown; protest deaths estimated at over 1,500 per Reuters reporting (the figure remains contested with state figures lower); the internet blackout for around a week as the principal institutional response to the protests.

**The January 2020 Ukraine Flight 752 protests.** The architecture: on 8 January 2020 — three days after the US assassination of Qasem Soleimani — the IRGC shot down Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752 over Tehran, killing all 176 people on board (including 138 passengers with Canadian connections). The state initially denied responsibility; international pressure and forensic evidence forced admission on 11 January 2020. Documented protests followed across Iran in mid-January 2020 with anti-regime slogans. The flight downing is the governing moment of IRGC technical incompetence intersecting with institutional dishonesty — producing international and domestic legitimacy damage.

**The 16 September 2022 Mahsa Amini death and the "Woman, Life, Freedom" protests.** The architecture: Mahsa Amini (also known as Jina Amini, 1999-2022), a 22-year-old Iranian Kurdish woman from Saqqez, was arrested by the Iranian morality police (*Gasht-e Ershad*) on 13 September 2022 in Tehran for allegedly wearing her hijab improperly; she died in custody on 16 September 2022 after a beating by morality police officers (the Iranian position is that she died of a pre-existing medical condition — a position rejected by her family and by forensic analysis). The Amini death triggered mass protests beginning 17 September 2022 in Saqqez, spreading to Tehran and across Iran. The protest slogan: "Zan, Zendegi, Azadi" ("Woman, Life, Freedom") — a Kurdish-feminist political slogan adopted by the Iranian protests. The architecture: protests across over 160 Iranian cities and 140 universities across September 2022 – mid-2023; protests in over 150 cities worldwide in diaspora solidarity; protest characteristics including women removing hijabs publicly, schoolgirl protests in classrooms, Kurdish protests in Sanandaj, Mahabad, and other Kurdish cities with Iranian security force violence in Kurdish-majority areas. The 2022-2023 protests operate as the largest sustained protest movement in Iran since 1979 — and as the principal contemporary moment of the theocratic apparatus's institutional crisis.

**The state response 2022-2024.** The architecture: IRGC, Basij, and Law Enforcement Command crackdown across September 2022 – mid-2023; protester deaths estimated at over 500 per Iran Human Rights and Human Rights Activists in Iran (HRANA); arrests estimated at over 20,000; executions of at least 8 protest-related defendants including Mohsen Shekari (executed 8 December 2022, the first execution related to the protests) and Majidreza Rahnavard (executed publicly in Mashhad 12 December 2022); post-protest expansion of surveillance and enforcement including IRGC moral-policing operations. The Hijab and Chastity Bill: passed Iranian parliament September 2023; signed into law by President Pezeshkian December 2024 (after his July 2024 election with partial reformist mandate); provides for sentences up to 10-15 years for "improper" hijab and for promotion of "nudity." The architecture is the Iranian state escalation of hijab enforcement following the protest movement's effective collapse of voluntary hijab compliance among millions of urban Iranian women.

**The post-2022 execution escalation.** The architecture: Iranian executions across 2024 — over 850 executions per Amnesty International, the highest in decades (with a spike to over 900 in 2025 per HRANA); expansion of execution categories including drug-related charges (the historical primary execution category) and political-protest-related charges; contemporary executions of religious minorities including Baha'i, Sunni Kurdish, and Sunni Baluchi defendants; international scrutiny including the June 2024 UN Special Rapporteur reports; US Treasury and EU sanctions on Iranian officials including the designation of the morality police under the Magnitsky framework.

**The pattern.** The Islamic Republic's repression record is the contemporary illustration of the clerical-supremacist state pattern: an explicitly Islamic-religious state framework operates documentedly to repress mass-protest movements including women's-rights, minority-rights, and political-reform movements. The pattern: each major protest moment (1999, 2009, 2017-2018, 2019, 2022-2023) has produced IRGC-Basij crackdown, mass arrests, executions, and post-protest expansion of the surveillance and enforcement apparatus — without reform of the underlying clerical-supremacist architecture. The pattern is the contemporary expression of the underlying institutional contradiction of the *velayat-e faqih* state: the "Islamic" framework operates documentedly to deliver authoritarian-religious repression rather than the liberatory framing of Khomeini's 1979 revolutionary rhetoric.

The summary of § III: the Islamic Republic operates a half-century record of internal repression from the 1981-1988 Khomeini-era atrocities through the 2022-2023 "Woman, Life, Freedom" protests and the post-2024 execution escalation. The configuration produces institutional contradictions: the "Islamic Republic" claims religious-juridical legitimacy while operating repressive patterns including the 1988 mass executions, the 2009 Green Movement crackdown, the 2019 Bloody November mass-deaths, the 2022-2023 Mahsa Amini protest crackdown, and the post-2024 execution escalation. The configuration is the contemporary illustration of the clerical-supremacist state pattern and as one of the principal contemporary moments of the tension between a theocratic state and a secular-protest movement.

---


### IV. The Contemporary Iran-Israel Confrontation and the 2025-2026 War

The 2023-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of the confrontation between the Islamic Republic of Iran and the State of Israel. The architecture has six principal documented arcs: (1) the post-7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the Israeli campaign in Gaza. (2) the 2024 escalation including the Hamas leader assassinations, the Hezbollah-Israel war and Nasrallah killing September 2024.
