And the Iranian direct missile attacks on Israel April and October 2024. (3) the December 2024 collapse of the Bashar al-Assad regime in Syria and the Iranian loss of the land bridge to Lebanon. (4) the 13-24 June 2025 Twelve-Day War with Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities and the US strikes on Fordow-Natanz-Isfahan 21-22 June 2025. (5) the 28 February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. (6) the 8 March 2026 elevation of Mojtaba Khamenei as the third Supreme Leader. The evidence covers each.

**The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the Israeli Gaza campaign.** The architecture: on 7 October 2023, Hamas militants from Gaza launched a surprise attack on southern Israel killing around 1,200 Israelis and taking around 250 hostages; Israel under Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched a sustained military campaign in Gaza producing Palestinian casualties estimated at over 65,000 by 2026 per Gaza Health Ministry figures (with international human-rights concerns including the ICJ provisional measures order of 26 January 2024 and the ICC arrest warrants for Netanyahu and former Defence Minister Yoav Gallant issued 21 November 2024). The 7 October attack and the subsequent Gaza campaign operate as the foundational event of the post-2023 Iran-Israel escalation — with Iran's Axis of Resistance partners including Hamas, Hezbollah, the Houthis, and Iraqi Shia militias operating with coordinated escalation against Israel and US forces since the post-October 2023 period.

**The April 2024 first direct Iran-Israel exchange.** The architecture: on 1 April 2024, Israel struck the Iranian consulate in Damascus killing seven IRGC officers including IRGC Quds Force commander for Syria-Lebanon Mohammad Reza Zahedi. On 13 April 2024 Iran launched its first documented direct attack on Israeli territory — "Operation True Promise" — with around 300 missiles and drones; most were intercepted by Israeli, US, UK, French, and Jordanian air defences with minimal damage in Israel. Israel responded with a limited strike on Iranian air defence radar at Isfahan on 19 April 2024. The April 2024 exchange is the first direct missile exchange between Iran and Israel — breaking the post-1979 pattern of proxy-only confrontation.

**The 2024 leadership assassinations.** The architecture: on 31 July 2024 Ismail Haniyeh (Hamas political bureau chief, born 1962) was assassinated in Tehran by an Israeli-attributed bomb in a guesthouse where he was staying after attending the inauguration of President Pezeshkian — operating as the Iranian humiliation of the Israeli operation on Iranian soil. On 27 September 2024 Hassan Nasrallah (Hezbollah Secretary-General from 1992, born 1960) was killed in a Israeli airstrike on Hezbollah's underground headquarters in the southern suburbs of Beirut — operating as the decapitation of Hezbollah's senior leadership. Hashem Safieddine (Nasrallah's heir apparent) was killed in a Israeli airstrike on 4 October 2024. Naim Qassem was confirmed as Hezbollah's new Secretary-General on 29 October 2024. The 2024 leadership assassinations operate as the Israeli campaign against the Axis of Resistance command — and as the architectural prelude to the post-October 2024 Iranian escalation.

**The October 2024 Iranian attack and the December 2024 Assad collapse.** The architecture: on 1 October 2024 Iran launched "Operation True Promise II" with around 180-200 ballistic missiles against Israel — the largest single Iranian missile attack to that date; multiple impacts in Israel with one Palestinian killed; majority interception by Israeli and US defences. Israel responded with "Operation Days of Repentance" on 26 October 2024 — strikes on Iranian military facilities including degradation of Iranian air defences. On 8 December 2024 the Bashar al-Assad regime collapsed under the Hayat Tahrir al-Sham offensive led by Ahmed al-Sharaa (Abu Mohammad al-Jolani); Assad fled to Russia; the Iranian land bridge to Lebanon was severed; the Iranian-Hezbollah-Syrian Axis of Resistance configuration was documentedly fractured. The December 2024 Assad collapse is the principal post-WWII strategic setback for the regional position — eliminating the 1979-2024 Iranian-Syrian institutional alliance.

**The 13-24 June 2025 Twelve-Day War.** The architecture: on 13 June 2025 Israel launched "Operation Rising Lion" — a sustained air campaign against Iran's nuclear programme, IRGC senior leadership, and military infrastructure. Documented Iranian leaders killed in the opening days: IRGC Commander Hossein Salami (1960-2025, killed 13 June 2025); IRGC Aerospace Force commander Amir Ali Hajizadeh (killed 13 June 2025); Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces Mohammad Bagheri (killed 13 June 2025); Khatam al-Anbiya Central HQ commander Gholam Ali Rashid; multiple nuclear scientists. Mohammad Pakpour was appointed IRGC Commander on 13 June 2025. Iran responded with sustained ballistic missile barrages on Israel — including missile hits in Tel Aviv, multiple casualties in Israeli cities, Iranian missile penetration of Israeli air defences in several strikes. On 21-22 June 2025 the United States under President Trump joined the Israeli campaign with strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities at Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan using B-2 bombers and GBU-57 bunker-buster bombs. On 23 June 2025 Iran launched a 14-missile retaliatory strike against the Al Udeid US Air Base in Qatar — with advance notification to Qatar and the US producing zero casualties; the strike operated as face-saving symbolic retaliation rather than strategic damage. On 23 June 2025 Trump announced a ceasefire; the ceasefire took effect on 24 June 2025 with initial violations producing 16 Iranian and 4 Israeli deaths in the first hours; subsequent ceasefire held through to the February 2026 escalation dossiered next. Documented assessment of the June 2025 strikes: US DIA initial low-confidence assessment indicated nuclear programme set back only months; CIA subsequent assessment claimed severe damage; Iran moved much of its enriched-uranium stockpile before the strikes per intelligence reports.

**The 28 February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation and the Khamenei assassination.** The architecture: on 28 February 2026, the United States and Israel launched a joint air operation against Iran citing Iranian nuclear ambitions and a "desire for change in government" per Trump statements. The operation included joint US-Israeli airstrikes on Tehran targeting senior Iranian leadership at Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei's office at his residence. Documented Iranian officials killed in the strike: Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei (86); Defence Minister Aziz Nasirzadeh (1964-2026, Defence Minister 21 August 2024 – 28 February 2026); IRGC Commander Mohammad Pakpour (1961-2026); Security Council Secretary Ali Shamkhani (a former IRGC Commander and close adviser to Khamenei); two additional senior figures (total of 5 killed in the decapitation strike). Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Trump publicly claimed credit for the operation. Iran initially denied Khamenei's death — with Tasnim and Mehr news agencies reporting that Khamenei remained "steadfast and firm in commanding the field" — before state media confirmation on 1 March 2026.

**The post-Khamenei interim arrangement.** The architecture: per the Constitution (Article 111), upon Supreme Leader incapacitation or death, an interim three-person leadership council holds power until the Assembly of Experts elects a successor. The council established 1 March 2026: President Masoud Pezeshkian (the moderate-reformist); the head of the judiciary Gholamhossein Mohseni-Eje'i (the hardliner); and a senior cleric Alireza Arafi. The Iranian government announced a 40-day national mourning period for Khamenei. Documented Israeli military statement of 8 March 2026 (via X in Farsi): the Israeli military would continue pursuing every successor of Khamenei and every person who sought to appoint a successor — operating as the contemporary moment of Israeli direct threat against the Iranian clerical succession process.

**The 8 March 2026 elevation of Mojtaba Khamenei.** The Assembly of Experts (the 88-member body of senior Shia clerics) elected Mojtaba Khamenei as the third Supreme Leader on 8 March 2026 — despite the Israeli warning. The architecture: dynastic succession from father to son in the explicitly clerical-supremacist state; Mojtaba Khamenei positioning throughout the post-2008 period as *Vakil* (deputy) of the Supreme Leader; Mojtaba IRGC and Basij relationships; contested theological credentials (Mojtaba holds only the *Hojatoleslam* rank — below the *Ayatollah* rank that the 1979 Constitution originally required for the Supreme Leader position before the 1989 amendment). The Mojtaba Khamenei elevation is the contemporary moment of the Iranian theocratic apparatus's institutional crisis: dynastic father-to-son succession in a clerical-supremacist state operates against the egalitarian rhetoric of the 1979 revolution; Mojtaba Khamenei's ascent is the contemporary illustration of the post-1989 Iranian institutional pattern of clerical-IRGC-family network consolidation.

**The captured-state pattern in the Iranian configuration.** The Iranian Shia theocratic apparatus is the principal Shia Muslim captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The configuration generates institutional contradictions parallel to those dossiered for the Vatican (Chapter 15), the American Evangelical apparatus (Chapter 16), the Moscow Patriarchate (Chapter 17), and the Saudi apparatus (Chapter 18): (1) the Islamic-revolutionary legitimacy framing operates against dynastic father-to-son succession from Ali Khamenei to Mojtaba Khamenei; (2) the *velayat-e faqih* doctrinal innovation operates against majority Shia clerical opinion (the Iraqi-Najaf marajiyya under Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani has documentedly maintained the Shia juridical-quietist tradition against Khomeini's political-theological innovation); (3) the "Axis of Resistance" anti-imperialist framing operates against Iranian state repression of protest movements including the 2022-2023 Mahsa Amini movement (Chapter 19 § III); (4) the "Islamic Republic" framework operates against institutional patterns of corruption, IRGC-economic capture, and hereditary-family consolidation of power.

**The contemporary Iranian Shia theocratic configuration.** As of late 2026, the Shia theocratic apparatus operates as: (1) the Shia Muslim captured-state apparatus under dynastic succession (Mojtaba Khamenei as third Supreme Leader from 8 March 2026); (2) operating under sustained Israeli-US military pressure following the February 2026 joint operation and the June 2025 Twelve-Day War; (3) operating with degraded Axis of Resistance configuration following the Assad collapse December 2024 and the Nasrallah and Haniyeh assassinations 2024; (4) operating with contested nuclear programme following the US-Israeli strikes on Fordow-Natanz-Isfahan 21-22 June 2025; (5) operating with continued internal repression including the post-2022 execution escalation and the September 2024 Hijab and Chastity Law; (6) operating with institutional dynastic succession that documentedly contradicts the egalitarian-revolutionary 1979 rhetoric of the Khomeini era.

The summary of § IV: the 2023-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of the Shia theocratic apparatus's confrontation with Israel and the US. The configuration delivered: (1) post-7 October 2023 escalation through the Axis of Resistance. (2) documented April and October 2024 direct Iranian missile strikes on Israel. (3) documented December 2024 Assad regime collapse and regional strategic setback. (4) June 2025 Twelve-Day War and US strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities. (5) documented 28 February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. (6) documented 8 March 2026 elevation of Mojtaba Khamenei as third Supreme Leader. The section closes here. Chapter 20 dossiers the Israeli religious-political apparatus as the principal Jewish captured-state configuration of the contemporary period. Operating in cross-reference with Chapter 7 (Jewish foundational dossier), with the Religious Zionist and Haredi institutional configurations within the contemporary State of Israel, with the post-October 2023 Israeli-Palestinian configuration, and with the post-February 2026 Israeli-Iranian configuration following the Khamenei assassination.

---


## Chapter 20. The Israeli Religious-Political Apparatus

Chapter 20 dossiers the apparatus as the principal Jewish captured-state configuration of the contemporary period. The analysis operates in cross-reference with Chapter 7 (Jewish foundational dossier), with Chapter 19 (the Iranian Shia theocratic apparatus) as the principal contemporary regional counterpart, and operates structurally as the Jewish parallel to the contemporary working-apparatus dossiers of Chapters 15-19. The analysis applies the same investigative-journalism standards and the same captured-state analytic framework to the Israeli configuration that the preceding chapters apply to the Catholic, Protestant-Evangelical, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni, and Shia configurations — without exceptionalism in either direction. The analysis operates against any framing that would treat the Israeli configuration as either uniquely innocent or uniquely culpable among contemporary configurations; the analysis here is symmetric to that applied to the four preceding contemporary apparatus chapters.

The documentation covers four sections: § I the foundational architecture from the 1897 First Zionist Congress through to the 1967 Six-Day War and the post-1967 emergence of the Religious Zionist settler movement; § II the contemporary architecture. The Chief Rabbinate, the Haredi-state arrangements, the Religious Services Ministry, the Halacha-based personal-status regime, and the Haredi political parties (Shas, UTJ). § III the Religious Zionist settler movement and its post-1967 integration with the Israeli state including the contemporary figures Bezalel Smotrich and Itamar Ben-Gvir. § IV the post-7 October 2023 configuration including the Gaza campaign. The international response (ICJ, ICC, multi-state sanctions), and the February 2026 Israeli-US joint operation killing the Iranian Supreme Leader Khamenei.

The chapter's working configuration differs from each of the preceding contemporary apparatus chapters. The Vatican is a single juridical-territorial sovereign state. American evangelicalism operates a distributed network. The Moscow Patriarchate is a church-state integration. The Saudi apparatus operates through the Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud religious-political alliance. The Iranian apparatus operates through *velayat-e faqih* clerical supremacy. The Israeli religious-political apparatus operates through a sixth distinct configuration: a Jewish ethno-religious state in which (1) the foundational political philosophy is Zionism. Itself a contested category combining secular and religious currents. (2) the architecture preserves the pre-state status quo arrangements granting Haredi religious authorities control over Jewish personal status. (3) the post-1967 Religious Zionist settler movement has produced contemporary integration with the state apparatus through Knesset coalition mathematics. (4) the configuration operates against a Palestinian population under military occupation in the West Bank, Israeli siege of Gaza, and citizenship hierarchy. The configuration is the embodiment of a religious-ethno-national project — analytically parallel to the Iranian Islamic Republic in its religious-ethno-national institutional structure while operating with documented different relationships to electoral democracy, civil rights, and external war.

### I. Foundational Architecture: Zionism, the Religious-Secular Tension, and the State of Israel 1897-1967

**The 1897 First Zionist Congress.** The foundational moment of organised political Zionism. The architecture: Theodor Herzl (1860-1904, Austro-Hungarian Jewish journalist) published *Der Judenstaat* ("The Jewish State") in February 1896 — the foundational text of political Zionism arguing for the establishment of a Jewish state as the solution to European antisemitism (Herzl's principal pre-Zionist work as a journalist covering the Dreyfus Affair 1894-1906 produced his documented intellectual trajectory toward Zionism). Herzl convened the First Zionist Congress in Basel 29-31 August 1897 — establishing the World Zionist Organization and the architecture of pre-state Zionism. The Congress is the foundational moment of contemporary Jewish nationalism — and as the moment from which the secular-religious tension within Zionism emerges. Most early Zionists including Herzl were documented secular Jews; the Mizrachi Religious Zionist movement was founded in March 1902 in Vilna by Rabbi Yitzchak Yaakov Reines (1839-1915) as the religious wing of the movement.

**Religious Zionism's foundational theological architecture: Rav Kook.** Rabbi Abraham Isaac Kook (Avraham Yitzchak HaCohen Kook, 1865-1935) is the principal theological architect of mainstream Religious Zionism. The architecture: born in Griva (then Russian Empire, now Latvia); served as rabbi in multiple positions in the Russian Empire and Mandatory Palestine; first Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi of Mandatory Palestine 1921-1935; founded Yeshivat Mercaz HaRav in Jerusalem 1924 (the principal Religious Zionist yeshiva). Rav Kook's principal theological doctrine: the modern Zionist project — including the secular Zionist majority — is the *atchalta degeula* ("beginning of redemption") — a theological framing that recast the secular Zionist movement as a religiously-significant moment in Jewish messianic history. The doctrine provided the theological grounding for Religious Zionist support in the secular Zionist project — and produced the structural bridge between Orthodox Jewish religious authority and political nationalism that has operated as one of the principal architectural features of the contemporary Israeli configuration.

**Rabbi Zvi Yehuda Kook and the post-1967 settler theology.** Rabbi Zvi Yehuda Kook (1891-1982), son of Rav Kook, served as the principal teacher at Yeshivat Mercaz HaRav 1952-1982 and as the principal theological architect of the post-1967 Religious Zionist settler movement. The architecture: Zvi Yehuda Kook developed his father's doctrine with documented additional emphasis on Israeli sovereignty over the Land of Israel (including Judea and Samaria — the West Bank) as a messianic imperative; his documented lectures at Mercaz HaRav across 1952-1982 trained the generation of Religious Zionist rabbis who founded Gush Emunim in 1974 and who produced the post-1967 settler movement; his documented 1967 sermon (delivered weeks before the Six-Day War — interpreted post-1967 as prophetic) lamented the loss of Hebron, Shechem (Nablus), Jericho, and other West Bank locations. The post-1967 Israeli conquest of these territories produced the religious-political framing of the territorial victory as messianic vindication of the Kook doctrine — and produced the theological architecture of the post-1967 Religious Zionist settler movement dossiered in § III.

**The pre-1948 architecture.** The architecture: the Jewish Agency for Palestine (founded 1929 under the leadership of David Ben-Gurion 1886-1973); the Histadrut labour federation (1920); the Haganah defence organisation (1920); the Revisionist Irgun (1931) under Vladimir Jabotinsky (Ze'ev Jabotinsky, 1880-1940); the Lehi (Stern Gang, 1940-1948). The pre-state configuration operated through documented competition between Labor Zionism (Ben-Gurion's Mapai, the dominant pre-state and post-state force through 1977), Revisionist Zionism (Jabotinsky's institutional descendants — Herut, then Likud from 1973), and Religious Zionism (Mizrachi, then the National Religious Party from 1956 to its 2008 splits). The pre-state architecture established the political configuration that subsequently produced the post-1948 Israeli state.

**The 1947-1949 establishment of the State of Israel.** The architecture: UN General Assembly Resolution 181 of 29 November 1947 (the partition plan) — adopted 33-13 with 10 abstentions; David Ben-Gurion declared the State of Israel on 14 May 1948; the 1948 Arab-Israeli War followed (15 May 1948 – 20 July 1949) with documented casualties on both sides; the war is named the War of Independence in Israel and the *Nakba* ("Catastrophe") in Palestinian institutional memory — with Palestinian displacement of an estimated 700,000-750,000 Palestinians from their homes across the 1947-1949 period (the *Nakba* is the foundational event of contemporary Palestinian institutional consciousness, with Palestinian return to pre-1948 homes prevented by state policy through to 2026). The 1949 Armistice Agreements established the pre-1967 territorial configuration of the State of Israel — with the West Bank under Jordanian rule (annexed 1950) and the Gaza Strip under Egyptian administration.

**The June 1947 status quo agreement.** The principal accommodation of Haredi religious authority within the secular-Zionist Israeli state. The architecture: in a letter of June 1947 from Ben-Gurion (then chairman of the Jewish Agency Executive) to Agudat Yisrael (the Haredi political organisation), Ben-Gurion committed the future state to preserving the authority of religious authorities over Jewish personal status (marriage, divorce, conversion); preserving Shabbat as the official day of rest; preserving kashrut in state institutions; and preserving the existing autonomy of the Haredi educational system. The 1947 status quo agreement is the foundational moment of the Haredi-state institutional arrangement — and as the principal explanation for the absence of civil marriage in Israel, the authority of the Chief Rabbinate over Jewish personal status, and the Haredi yeshiva-student conscription exemption (initially around 400 students under Ben-Gurion arrangement, growing to over 60,000 yeshiva-student exemption demands by 2024 — dossiered in § II).

**The Chief Rabbinate of Israel.** Established 1921 under the British Mandate with the appointment of Rav Kook as Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi and Rabbi Yaakov Meir as Sephardi Chief Rabbi; reorganised 1947 under the framework that operates to 2026; two Chief Rabbis (one Ashkenazi, one Sephardi) elected by a 150-member electoral assembly composed of rabbis and lay representatives for 10-year terms (after the 1980 statutory reform — extended from 5-year terms). The Chief Rabbinate is the principal juridical authority of the Israeli state —  analogous to the Saudi Council of Senior Scholars (Chapter 18 § III) and the Iranian Council of Guardians (Chapter 19 § II) within their respective configurations.

**The 1967 Six-Day War and the post-1967 territorial-configuration.** The Six-Day War of 5-10 June 1967 produced the Israeli conquest of East Jerusalem (including the Old City and the Temple Mount/Haram al-Sharif), the West Bank, Gaza Strip, Sinai Peninsula, and the Golan Heights — multiplying Israeli-controlled territory and producing the configuration of administration over an additional Palestinian population. The Israeli annexation of East Jerusalem (in effect from June 1967, formalised by the 1980 Jerusalem Law) and the administration of the West Bank and Gaza Strip operated through military occupation under international law — with the Israeli position that the West Bank is "disputed" rather than "occupied" territory (a position rejected by virtually every other state and international body including the UN Security Council, ICJ, ICRC, and the EU). The post-1967 territorial configuration is the foundational architecture of the contemporary Israeli-Palestinian conflict and as the context within which the Religious Zionist settler movement dossiered in § III subsequently emerged.

The summary of § I: the apparatus operates on a foundational architecture established by Herzl's 1897 First Zionist Congress, Rav Kook's theological doctrine of the secular Zionist state as *atchalta degeula*. The June 1947 status quo agreement establishing Haredi authority over personal status, the 14 May 1948 declaration of the State of Israel, and the 1967 Six-Day War with its post-1967 territorial expansion. The configuration's foundational features: secular-religious tension within Zionism from its inception. Accommodation of Haredi religious authority through the status quo arrangements; post-1967 territorial configuration producing the Religious Zionist settler movement; Palestinian *Nakba* of 1948 as the foundational counter-narrative; and Israeli ethno-religious state institutional structure operating with documented unresolved tension between democratic-liberal and ethno-religious-nationalist institutional currents.

---


### II. The Contemporary Institutional Architecture: Chief Rabbinate, Haredi Parties, and the Status Quo Arrangements

The contemporary Israeli religious-political architecture operates through five principal documented nodes: (1) the Chief Rabbinate as the principal juridical authority of the Israeli state. (2) the Halacha-based personal-status regime under which Jewish marriage. Divorce, conversion, kashrut, and burial are governed by courts under state authority. (3) the Religious Services Ministry as the coordinating state ministry. (4) the Haredi political parties. Shas (Sephardi Haredi) and United Torah Judaism (Ashkenazi Haredi) — as the vehicles of Haredi political power; (5) the yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement and its 2024-2025 institutional collapse under the Supreme Court rulings. The record covers each.

**The Chief Rabbinate of Israel (*HaRabbanut HaRashit LeYisrael*).** Established 1921 under the British Mandate; reorganised 1947 under the framework that operates to 2026. The architecture: two Chief Rabbis (one Ashkenazi serving the European-descended Jewish community, one Sephardi serving the Middle-Eastern and North-African-descended Jewish community) elected by a 150-member electoral assembly for 10-year terms; the assembly composed of around half rabbis and half lay representatives appointed by the Religious Services Minister and Israeli local-government religious councils. The Chief Rabbinate is the principal juridical authority of the state — issuing rulings on Jewish religious law (Halacha) applied through the religious system; supervising kashrut certification; coordinating the Rabbinical Courts; appointing local-government rabbis; supervising educational institutions; overseeing conversion to Judaism. The 2024 Chief Rabbi elections (28 September 2024) elected Rabbi Kalman Bar (born 1956) as Ashkenazi Chief Rabbi and Rabbi David Yosef (born 1953, son of the late Shas founder Ovadia Yosef) as Sephardi Chief Rabbi — both for 10-year terms through to October 2034.

**The Halacha-based personal-status regime.** The principal consequence of the June 1947 status quo agreement. The architecture: Jewish marriage in Israel is conducted exclusively through the Chief Rabbinate-supervised rabbinical courts under Orthodox Halachic standards; civil marriage is not available within Israel (Israeli couples who cannot marry through the rabbinate — including documented mixed-faith couples, documented same-sex couples, and documented Jews who cannot satisfy the rabbinate's Halachic Jewish-status requirements — travel abroad for civil marriage which is then recognised under Israeli law); Jewish divorce requires a rabbinical-court-issued *get* (the Halachic divorce document) with consequences for women whose husbands refuse to grant the *get* (the *agunah* problem affecting an estimated thousands of Israeli women through to 2026); conversion to Judaism is governed by Orthodox rabbinical authority — with non-recognition of Conservative and Reform conversions performed in Israel; kashrut certification is controlled by the Chief Rabbinate with consequences for restaurants and food producers; burial is governed by religious authorities with documented consequences for non-Halachically-Jewish Israeli citizens including documented Russian immigrants under the Law of Return who are not Halachically Jewish. The architecture operates as evidence of ethno-religious-state structure within an  democratic framework — the governing contradiction that operates as one of the principal features of the contemporary Israeli system.

**The Religious Services Ministry.** Operates as the coordinating state ministry for Israeli religious affairs — coordinating funding for religious bodies, supervising council infrastructure, and operating as the vehicle through which the Haredi political parties (Shas, UTJ) exercise control over religious funding when they are in coalition government. Religious Services Minister under the post-29 December 2022 Netanyahu coalition: Michael Malkieli (Shas, from 29 December 2022).

**Shas (Sephardi Haredi political party).** Founded 1984 by Rabbi Ovadia Yosef (1920-2013) as the political vehicle of Sephardi-Mizrahi Haredi Jewry — operating with success in the post-1984 period as one of the principal Israeli political parties. The architecture: Ovadia Yosef was the spiritual leader of Sephardi-Mizrahi Jewry across the post-1980s period including as Sephardi Chief Rabbi 1973-1983; Shas operated electorally with documented 11-17 Knesset seats across most post-1984 elections; success in capturing the Sephardi-Mizrahi Israeli political-cultural-religious constituency through Shas-controlled educational institutions, Shas-controlled council infrastructure, and documented Shas-controlled welfare and community-services networks. Shas leader from 1999: Aryeh Deri (born 13 February 1959 Meknès Morocco) — Deri was convicted of bribery 1999 (served 22 months in prison) and convicted of tax offences 2022 (suspended sentence). The 2022 Knesset election produced 11 Shas seats; Deri was appointed Interior Minister and Health Minister in the December 2022 Netanyahu coalition but was removed in January 2023 following the Supreme Court ruling that his criminal conviction precluded ministerial service; he remains as Shas party leader and is a coalition kingmaker.

**United Torah Judaism (UTJ, Ashkenazi Haredi political party).** Knesset bloc combining Degel HaTorah (Lithuanian Haredi, founded 1988 under Rabbi Eliezer Shach 1899-2001) and Agudat Yisrael (Hasidic, founded 1912 in Katowice). The architecture: UTJ operated electorally with documented 6-8 Knesset seats across most post-1992 elections; control over Ashkenazi-educational institutions; position on the Haredi-state arrangement including the principal Haredi-political-party defence of the yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement dossiered next. The 2022 Knesset election produced 7 UTJ seats — operating as one of the principal vehicles of Netanyahu's December 2022 coalition.

**The yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement (1948-2024).** The institutional centrepiece of the Haredi-state arrangement — and the contemporary crisis of the Israeli system. The architecture: the original 1948 Ben-Gurion arrangement exempted around 400 yeshiva students from military service under the rationale of preserving the Haredi yeshiva-study tradition after the Holocaust; the number of exemptions grew over decades to documented tens of thousands by the 1990s and documented 63,000-70,000 by 2024 per state figures; the arrangement was formalised through successive legislative arrangements (the *Tal Law* 2002 and successor legislation) that operated through to documented expiration in June 2023 without renewal — producing the post-June 2023 institutional vacuum that is the principal crisis of the contemporary Israeli system.

**The 25 June 2024 the Supreme Court ruling.** Operates as the juridical moment of the contemporary Haredi-state crisis. The architecture: the High Court of Justice ruled unanimously (in *Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. Minister of Defense*) that — given the expiration of the legislative framework allowing for yeshiva-student exemption in June 2023 — Haredi yeshiva students must be drafted into the IDF just as other Israeli citizens; and that state funding to non-compliant yeshivas is illegal. The 25 June 2024 ruling is the juridical end of the 1948-2023 yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement — and as the moment from which the contemporary Haredi-state crisis emerged.

**The 19 November 2025 Supreme Court ruling.** Required the Israeli government to formulate an effective enforcement plan for Haredi conscription. The architecture: the Israeli state's failure to enforce the 25 June 2024 ruling produced the petition for contempt-of-court that led to the 19 November 2025 ruling. The 19 November 2025 ruling is the juridical escalation of the post-June 2024 crisis — and as the contemporary moment of constitutional crisis within the Israeli system.

**The post-2024 Haredi conscription data.** Per Israel Democracy Institute analysis (October 2025): in the 2024 conscription year (July 2024 to June 2025), around 24,000 initial conscription orders were issued to members of the Haredi public; only 2,738 attended IDF conscription centres; only 1,508 continued the conscription process; only 798 were enlisted; documented IDF reservist days averaged around 110 days per reservist in 2025 — producing the context within which the Haredi conscription exemption is a source of secular-Haredi tension during sustained wartime; state position that the IDF could not immediately absorb all 63,000-70,000 eligible Haredi yeshiva students — agreed minimum target of 4,800 recruits by June 2025 (with only 232 actually conscripted by early June 2025 per IDI). The data operate as evidence of the failure of the post-June 2024 enforcement architecture — and as the contemporary moment of unresolved institutional tension within the ethno-religious-state configuration.

**The contradictions of the Haredi-state arrangement.** The documented contemporary Haredi-state arrangement operates with four contradictions: (1) the constitutional principle of equal citizenship operates against yeshiva-student conscription exemption while other citizens (Jewish-secular, Religious Zionist, Druze, Bedouin, Circassian) serve compulsory military service; (2) the Israeli financial arrangement (state funding of Haredi yeshivas, state recognition of educational institutions) operates against the Haredi political-religious separateness (most Haredi figures do not serve in the IDF, do not work in mainstream Israeli economy at the same rates, and operate within parallel social structures); (3) the Haredi coalition power (Shas, UTJ as documented coalition kingmakers) operates against Haredi demographic minority status (around 13-14% of the Jewish population per documented Israel Democracy Institute data); (4) the secular-Israeli majority institutional preferences operate against Haredi institutional preferences across multiple policy domains including marriage law, public transportation on Shabbat, and military service — producing the tension that has operated as one of the principal features of the contemporary Israeli system through to 2026.

The summary of § II: the contemporary Israeli religious-political architecture operates through the Chief Rabbinate (with the October 2024 election of Rabbi Kalman Bar and Rabbi David Yosef as the contemporary Chief Rabbis). The Halacha-based personal-status regime, the Religious Services Ministry, the Haredi political parties Shas (founded 1984) and UTJ (founded 1992), and the post-2024 crisis around the Supreme-Court-ordered end of the yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement. The configuration operates with contradictions between secular-democratic and ethno-religious-state institutional principles — and operates alongside the Religious Zionist settler movement and its post-1967 integration with the state apparatus dossiered in § III.

---


### III. The Religious Zionist Settler Movement and the West Bank 1967-2026

The Religious Zionist settler movement is the principal vehicle of the post-1967 Israeli territorial-configuration. The architecture has six principal documented arcs: (1) the 1974 founding of Gush Emunim ("Bloc of the Faithful") as the embryo of the settler movement. (2) the post-1977 Likud-era integration of the settler movement with the Israeli state through settlement-expansion policies. (3) the Kahanist current including Rabbi Meir Kahane (1932-1990) and the post-1990 Kahanist political continuation. (4) the post-1995 settler killing of Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin by Yigal Amir. (5) the contemporary institutional figures Bezalel Smotrich (Finance Minister from 29 December 2022) and Itamar Ben-Gvir (National Security Minister from 29 December 2022) as the principal Religious Zionist figures of the Netanyahu post-2022 coalition. (6) the post-2023 institutional escalation including West Bank settler violence, the May 2025 22-settlement expansion, the August 2025 E1 plan, and the June 2025 multi-state sanctions against Ben-Gvir and Smotrich.

**Gush Emunim founded 1974.** The embryo of the contemporary Religious Zionist settler movement. The architecture: founded 1974 in Kfar Etzion by graduates of Yeshivat Mercaz HaRav under the theological framework of Zvi Yehuda Kook dossiered in § I; principal founding figures included Hanan Porat (1943-2011), Rabbi Moshe Levinger (1935-2015), Rabbi Yoel Bin-Nun (born 1946), and others; institutional objective: establishment of Jewish settlements throughout the West Bank (Judea and Samaria in Religious Zionist usage) and Gaza as fulfilment of the Religious Zionist doctrine. The architecture operated through documented direct-action settlement attempts (including the 1975 Sebastia settlement attempts producing the Elon Moreh settlement) and documented political pressure on Israeli governments under both Labor (1974-1977) and Likud (1977-) to authorise settlement expansion. Gush Emunim served as the precursor to the contemporary Religious Zionist political-configuration — and as the embryo of the contemporary settlement enterprise.

**The post-1977 Likud-era integration.** The architecture: the Likud government under Menachem Begin (1913-1992, Prime Minister 1977-1983) operated with institutional support for the Religious Zionist settler project — producing documented accelerated settlement construction since the post-1977 period; settler population growth from around 7,500 in 1977 to around 115,000 by the 1993 Oslo Accords moment to around 250,000 by 2000 to around 400,000 by 2010 to over 500,000 by 2024 in the West Bank excluding East Jerusalem (with around 750,000 including East Jerusalem); institutional consolidation through around 150+ formal Israeli settlements and around 200+ outposts across the post-1977 period. The integration operated through state funding of settlement infrastructure (roads, water, electricity, schools), state legal-administrative recognition of settlement institutions, and state military protection of settlement populations against Palestinian opposition.

**The Kahanist current.** Rabbi Meir Kahane (1932-1990) was the theological-political architect of the most-explicitly-ethno-nationalist current within the post-1967 Israeli configuration. The architecture: born Brooklyn 1932; founded the Jewish Defense League (JDL) in the US 1968; emigrated to Israel 1971; founded the Kach political party 1971 in Israel; documented advocacy of the expulsion of Arab citizens of Israel and Palestinians from the West Bank and Gaza; elected to the Knesset 1984 (one seat); Kach was banned from running in Knesset elections in 1988 under the Anti-Racism Law amendment to the Basic Law: the Knesset; Kahane was assassinated in New York on 5 November 1990 by Egyptian-American El Sayyid Nosair; Kach was designated as a terrorist organisation by the Israeli government 1994 (following the Baruch Goldstein massacre at the Cave of the Patriarchs Hebron on 25 February 1994 — Goldstein, a Brooklyn-born Kach-affiliated Religious Zionist settler doctor, killed 29 Palestinian worshippers and wounded over 100 before being killed by survivors); the US State Department designated Kach and its Kahane Chai successor as Foreign Terrorist Organisations in 1994 (the designation was lifted in 2022 by the Biden administration). The Kahanist current served as the structurally-extreme wing of the post-1967 Israeli configuration — and as the institutional progenitor of the contemporary Otzma Yehudit party under Itamar Ben-Gvir dossiered next.

**The 4 November 1995 Yitzhak Rabin assassination.** The principal post-1948 internal Israeli political assassination. The architecture: Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin (1922-1995) was assassinated on 4 November 1995 in Tel Aviv by Yigal Amir (born 1970) — a Religious Zionist yeshiva student affiliated with the Religious Zionist settler movement. Amir's religious-political motivation: Amir invoked the Halachic doctrine of *din rodef* ("pursuer doctrine" — the principle that one may kill a person actively endangering Jewish lives) — claiming that Rabin's documented 1993 Oslo Accords and subsequent territorial-compromise process endangered Jewish lives by transferring territory to Palestinian Authority control. The juridical framing operated through Religious Zionist rabbinical-figure pronouncements in the months preceding the assassination — including documented public discussion of whether *din rodef* applied to Rabin. The 4 November 1995 assassination is the foundational moment of the contemporary Israeli political-religious-violence configuration — and as the moment from which the post-1995 Israeli political-religious trajectory diverged from the pre-1995 Oslo-process trajectory.

**Itamar Ben-Gvir.** Born 6 May 1976 Mevaseret Zion — is the principal contemporary Kahanist figure. The architecture: teenage admiration of Meir Kahane; the 1995 incident in which Ben-Gvir held up a Cadillac hood ornament stolen from Rabin's car on television three weeks before the Rabin assassination, threatening "we got to his car, we'll get to him too"; portrait of Baruch Goldstein in Ben-Gvir's living room until at least 2020 (when Ben-Gvir documentedly removed it amid the mainstreaming of his political career); 2007 conviction for incitement to racism and for supporting a terrorist organisation; entry to the Knesset 2021; the 2022 Knesset election as part of the Religious Zionism-Otzma Yehudit-Noam combined list; appointment as National Security Minister on 29 December 2022 in the Netanyahu coalition. As National Security Minister, Ben-Gvir documentedly oversaw the Police, the Border Police, the Prison Service, and the National Guard initiative; loosening of firearm-ownership regulations with around 230,000 new firearm licenses issued per Ben-Gvir's office (August 2025); multiple provocative visits to the Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif 2023-2024 violating the long-standing status quo prohibition on Jewish prayer at the site; institutional role in changing the ascent and prayer arrangements at the Mount — with large groups of Jews praying at the site in contrast to the pre-2023 institutional restrictions per Middle East Institute Switzerland analysis (August 2025).

**Bezalel Smotrich.** Born 27 February 1980 Haspin Golan Heights — is the principal contemporary mainstream Religious Zionist figure. The architecture: Religious Zionist yeshiva training; settler residence in Kedumim settlement (illegal under international law per the International Court of Justice 19 July 2024 advisory opinion); founding of the Regavim NGO 2006 (settler political organisation focused on Palestinian construction in Area C); entry to the Knesset 2015 with HaBayit HaYehudi (Jewish Home); leadership of Religious Zionism Party from 2019; appointment as Finance Minister and adjunct Minister in the Defence Ministry on 29 December 2022 in the Netanyahu coalition. Smotrich's "Decisive Plan" 2017 — a programme for settlement expansion in the West Bank with the intended consequence of foreclosing Palestinian statehood. Documented 26 February 2023 statement that the West Bank town of Huwara should be "wiped out" (following settler vigilante violence in Huwara on 26 February 2023 in which one Palestinian was killed and around 100 Palestinian properties were burned); Smotrich subsequently documentedly apologised for the statement after international and US-government criticism. Documented November 2023 statements about Palestinians as "two million Nazis"; statements about "voluntary emigration" of Gaza Palestinians. Documented institutional role as adjunct Defence Minister: Smotrich documentedly authorised West Bank settlement expansion including the May 2025 establishment/regularisation of 22 new West Bank settlements (the largest single settlement expansion since 1992 per international analysts) and the August 2025 resumption of the E1 settlement plan (3,400 homes) — which would create a physical link between Ma'ale Adumim and Jerusalem and which is widely documented as designed to foreclose contiguous Palestinian statehood in the West Bank.

**The settler violence 2024-2025.** Per UN Office for Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA) data: between 1 January 2024 and 30 April 2025, at least 616 Palestinians (including 115 children) were killed by settlers or the Israeli military in the West Bank; 1,936 incidents of violence toward Palestinians were recorded; 41,272 Palestinians were displaced from their West Bank homes. The pattern: settler vigilante violence operating with IDF passivity or active facilitation per multiple human-rights organisation reports (B'Tselem, Yesh Din, Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International); police failure to prosecute settler violence per police data; Ben-Gvir-directed police laxity on settler-violence enforcement per US officials cited in Times of Israel reporting July 2024.

**The documented June 2025 multi-state sanctions.** The architecture: on 10 June 2025, the governments of the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and Norway imposed travel bans on Ben-Gvir and Smotrich and froze their assets in those countries, citing the incitement of settler violence and serious abuses of Palestinian human rights; UK Foreign Secretary David Lammy publicly stated the sanctions framework; US opposition under Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Subsequently joined by Slovenia and the Netherlands (July 2025) and Spain (September 2025). The June 2025 sanctions operate as the first contemporary moment of formal Western-state sanctions against sitting Israeli government ministers — and as the moment of post-2023 international response to the Netanyahu coalition's Religious Zionist-led West Bank policy.

**The documented September 2025 Hebron drone plot.** On 7 September 2025, Israeli Shin Bet announced the foiling of a Hamas-funded plot by a cell of operatives in Hebron to assassinate Ben-Gvir using an explosive drone during a planned visit by Ben-Gvir to the Cave of the Patriarchs. The architecture operates as evidence of the contemporary risk profile of the principal Religious Zionist figures within the post-2023 Israeli-Palestinian configuration.

**The documented November 2025 Turkish genocide charges.** On 7 November 2025, the Chief Public Prosecutor's Office of Turkey charged Ben-Gvir and 36 other Israeli officials with genocide and crimes against humanity. The architecture operates as evidence of the governing international response to the post-2023 Netanyahu coalition's policy in Gaza and the West Bank.

The summary of § III: the Religious Zionist settler movement is the principal post-1967 vehicle of the ethno-religious-state territorial-religious-national project. The configuration produces contradictions: constitutional-democratic Israeli state framework operates against Religious Zionist political programme. Israeli sovereignty over East Jerusalem and de-facto control over the West Bank operates against documented international-law non-recognition of those configurations. Documented contemporary Religious Zionist power (Ben-Gvir and Smotrich as documented kingmakers in the Netanyahu coalition with 20 of the 67-seat coalition) operates against Religious Zionist minority demographic status; Israeli state Halacha-based personal-status regime operates against Israeli secular-majority preferences. The contemporary configuration operates alongside the post-7 October 2023 Israeli configuration dossiered in § IV.

---


### IV. The Post-7 October 2023 Configuration and the 2025-2026 Iran-Israel War

The 2023-2026 period is the principal the contemporary moment of the apparatus's crisis. The architecture has five principal documented arcs: (1) the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the Israeli military campaign in Gaza producing Palestinian casualties >65. 000 by 2026 and international response. (2) the Netanyahu coalition's dependence on the Religious Zionist and Haredi political-party blocs producing institutional continuation of the post-2022 policy configuration. (3) the post-October 2023 expansion of West Bank settler activity including the May 2025 22-settlement expansion and the August 2025 E1 plan resumption. (4) the June 2025 Twelve-Day War with Iran and the 28 February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation killing Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. (5) the contradictions of the contemporary Israeli configuration including documented international-law concerns, secular-Haredi tension, and contemporary unresolution.

**The 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and the Israeli Gaza campaign.** Cross-reference Chapter 19 § IV. The architecture: on 7 October 2023, Hamas militants from Gaza launched a surprise attack on southern Israel killing around 1,200 Israelis (the largest single-day Israeli civilian and military casualty event since 1948) and taking around 250 hostages; Israel under Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu launched a sustained military campaign in Gaza from 7 October 2023 onward producing Palestinian casualties estimated at over 65,000 by 2026 per Gaza Health Ministry figures, destruction of around 90% of Gaza buildings per UN data, and displacement of around 2 million Palestinians within the Gaza Strip; ground operations across Gaza City, Khan Younis, Rafah, and other Gaza locations; ground operations into southern Lebanon following the September 2024 escalation; ground and air operations into Syria following the December 2024 Assad regime collapse.

**The international response.** The architecture: South Africa filed the case at the International Court of Justice (ICJ) on 29 December 2023 alleging Israeli breaches of the Genocide Convention in Gaza; the ICJ issued provisional measures on 26 January 2024 ordering Israel to prevent genocidal acts, ensure humanitarian aid, and preserve evidence — followed by further provisional measures on 28 March 2024 and 24 May 2024 (the latter ordering Israel to halt the Rafah offensive); the ICC arrest warrants of 21 November 2024 issued by the International Criminal Court for Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu, former Defence Minister Yoav Gallant, and Hamas military commander Mohammed Deif (the warrants alleging war crimes and crimes against humanity); the UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese reports of July 2024 ("Anatomy of a Genocide") and October 2024; the multi-state sanctions on Ben-Gvir and Smotrich dossiered in § III. The international response is the contemporary moment of formal international-juridical and response to the post-October 2023 Israeli campaign — with state and US-government rejection of the framings.

**The Netanyahu coalition mathematics.** The architecture: the 29 December 2022 Netanyahu coalition is composed of Likud (32 seats), Religious Zionism-Otzma Yehudit-Noam (14 seats — composed of Smotrich's Religious Zionism party, Ben-Gvir's Otzma Yehudit, and Maoz's Noam), Shas (11 seats), United Torah Judaism (7 seats), and Otzma Yehudit (6 seats — operating separately under the post-election reorganisation) — producing a 67-seat coalition in the 120-seat Knesset. The consequence: the Religious Zionist bloc (Smotrich + Ben-Gvir + Noam combined) holds around 20 of the 67 coalition seats, making them kingmakers — with capacity to collapse the coalition if Netanyahu does not deliver on Religious Zionist policy preferences. The Haredi political-bloc (Shas + UTJ combined) holds 18 of the 67 coalition seats — with similar kingmaker capacity especially on Haredi-state arrangement preservation. The coalition mathematics produce the explanation for the post-2022 Netanyahu government's Religious Zionist-Haredi-led policy direction — operating without the restraints that would obtain under a broader coalition with non-Religious-Zionist, non-Haredi members.

**Documented Ben-Gvir resignation and return 2025.** On 19 January 2025, Ben-Gvir resigned from his ministerial position in documented protest at the phase-one Gaza ceasefire agreement; other Otzma Yehudit ministers resigned. The architecture: Ben-Gvir documentedly threatened to collapse the Netanyahu coalition if the ceasefire phases proceeded; documented return of Ben-Gvir and other Otzma Yehudit ministers to the government in March 2025 following the breakdown of the ceasefire and the resumption of Israeli military operations in Gaza. The January-March 2025 episode operates as evidence of the kingmaker position of the Religious Zionist bloc within the Netanyahu coalition.

**The May 2025 22-settlement expansion.** Cross-reference § III. The architecture: in May 2025, the Israeli Security Cabinet decided to establish and regularise 22 new settlements in the West Bank — the largest single settlement expansion since 1992 per international analysts; Smotrich-led institutional advocacy for the expansion; accompaniment by the August 2025 resumption of the E1 settlement plan (3,400 homes between Ma'ale Adumim and Jerusalem). The architecture is the expression of the post-October 2023 Religious Zionist territorial-political programme — and as the contemporary moment from which contiguous Palestinian statehood in the West Bank becomes documentedly more difficult per international analysis.

**The documented June 2025 Twelve-Day War.** Cross-reference Chapter 19 § IV. The architecture: on 13 June 2025, Israel launched "Operation Rising Lion" — a sustained air campaign against Iran's nuclear programme, IRGC senior leadership, and military infrastructure; Iranian leaders killed including IRGC Commander Hossein Salami (1960-2025), IRGC Aerospace Force commander Amir Ali Hajizadeh, Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces Mohammad Bagheri, and multiple nuclear scientists; Iranian retaliatory ballistic missile barrages on Israel producing Israeli casualties and Iranian missile penetration of Israeli air defences in several documented strikes; US strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities at Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan on 21-22 June 2025; Iranian 14-missile retaliatory strike on Al Udeid US Air Base in Qatar on 23 June 2025; Trump-announced ceasefire on 23 June 2025 taking effect on 24 June 2025. The June 2025 war is the contemporary moment of the Iran-Israel direct confrontation — and as the prelude to the February 2026 escalation dossiered next.

**The 28 February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation and the Khamenei killing.** Cross-reference Chapter 19 § IV. The architecture: on 28 February 2026, the United States and Israel launched a joint air operation against Iran citing Iranian nuclear ambitions and a "desire for change in government" per Trump statements; joint US-Israeli airstrikes on Tehran targeted senior Iranian leadership at Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei's residence/office; Iranian officials killed in the strike included Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei (86), Defence Minister Aziz Nasirzadeh, IRGC Commander Mohammad Pakpour, and Iranian Security Council Secretary Ali Shamkhani; Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and US President Trump publicly claimed credit for the operation; Iranian state media confirmation of Khamenei's death on 1 March 2026 with 40-day national mourning period; the 8 March 2026 elevation of Mojtaba Khamenei as third Supreme Leader of Iran. The February 2026 operation is the contemporary peak moment of the Iran-Israel confrontation — and as the contemporary moment from which the apparatus's external configuration operates distinctly from the pre-February 2026 configuration.

**The captured-state pattern in the Israeli system.** The Israeli apparatus is the principal Jewish captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The configuration generates contradictions parallel to those dossiered for the Vatican (Chapter 15), the American Evangelical apparatus (Chapter 16), the Moscow Patriarchate (Chapter 17), the Saudi apparatus (Chapter 18), and the Iranian Shia apparatus (Chapter 19): (1) the constitutional-democratic Israeli state framework operates against Halacha-based personal-status institutional arrangements that produce inequality between citizens (Jewish-secular, Jewish-religious, Druze, Bedouin, Christian, Muslim) in marriage, divorce, conversion, and burial matters; (2) the sovereign-equality claim operates against citizenship hierarchy and administration of around 5 million Palestinians under military occupation in the West Bank, siege in Gaza, and citizenship differential between Jewish and Palestinian citizens (the 20% Palestinian-Arab Israeli citizen population); (3) the liberal-democratic self-positioning operates against post-2022 Religious Zionist-led judicial-overhaul programme (the 2023 protests against judicial overhaul producing the largest sustained Israeli protest movement since 1948); (4) the Haredi-state arrangement operates against Supreme Court rulings of 25 June 2024 and 19 November 2025 with continued non-compliance; (5) the Israeli defensive-war framing of the post-October 2023 Gaza campaign operates against international findings including the ICJ provisional measures and ICC arrest warrants.

**The contemporary Israeli configuration.** As of late 2026, the apparatus operates as: (1) the Jewish captured-state apparatus operating through Religious Zionist and Haredi political-configurations in the Netanyahu coalition government; (2) operating with integration of the Religious Zionist settler movement with the state apparatus through Smotrich (Finance + adjunct Defence Minister) and Ben-Gvir (National Security Minister); (3) operating against sustained Palestinian casualties in Gaza (>65,000 by 2026) and sustained Palestinian displacement and killing in the West Bank (>616 Palestinians killed January 2024 - April 2025 per UN OCHA); (4) operating with multi-state sanctions on Religious Zionist ministers (UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Norway from June 2025; Slovenia and Netherlands July 2025; Spain September 2025); (5) operating with kingmaker mathematics between Religious Zionist and Haredi blocs producing institutional foreclosure of mainstream Israeli moderation; (6) operating with documented unresolved crisis around the Supreme-Court-ordered end of the Haredi yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement; (7) operating in documented direct military confrontation with Iran following the February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation killing Supreme Leader Khamenei.

The summary of § IV: the 2023-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of the apparatus's configuration. The configuration delivered: (1) sustained Gaza military campaign producing Palestinian casualties >65,000 and international response through ICJ provisional measures, ICC arrest warrants. And multi-state sanctions. (2) Netanyahu coalition dependence on Religious Zionist and Haredi blocs producing post-2022 policy configuration. (3) post-October 2023 West Bank settler expansion including May 2025 22 settlements and August 2025 E1 resumption. (4) documented June 2025 Twelve-Day War with Iran. (5) documented 28 February 2026 joint US-Israeli operation killing Iranian Supreme Leader Khamenei. (6) documented continuing contradictions including secular-Haredi tension, citizenship-hierarchy issues, international-law concerns, and unresolved crisis. The section closes here. Chapter 21 dossiers the Haredi configuration as a separate institutional dossier — cross-referencing Chapter 20 §§ I-II and dossiering the United Torah Judaism, Shas, Lithuanian Litvish, Hasidic dynasty, Edah HaChareidis, and Neturei Karta configurations including the 2024-2025 Supreme Court conscription confrontation as the central crisis of contemporary Haredi-Israeli relations.

---


## Chapter 21. The Haredi Institutional Configuration

Chapter 21 dossiers the Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) configuration as a separate dossier from the Religious Zionist movement covered in Chapter 20 § III. The evidence operates in cross-reference with Chapter 7 (Jewish foundational dossier), with Chapter 20 §§ I-II (the contemporary Israeli religious-political architecture), and is a focused sub-dossier of the Israeli Jewish captured-state configuration. The Haredi sector is distinct from the Religious Zionist sector along several axes: institutionally, the Haredi sector operates through the *Moetzet Gedolei HaTorah* (Council of Torah Sages) rabbinical-leadership bodies rather than through Religious Zionist political parties. Theologically, the Haredi mainstream has historically maintained either anti-Zionist or non-Zionist positions in contrast to the explicitly Zionist theological position of Religious Zionism under Rav Kook. Politically, the Haredi parties (Shas, UTJ) have prioritised the yeshiva-student conscription exemption and state funding of Haredi educational institutions, in contrast to the Religious Zionist focus on the West Bank settlement enterprise.

What follows covers four sections: § I the foundational architecture from the 18th-century Hasidic and Lithuanian-Litvish institutional origins through the Holocaust and the post-1945 reconstitution. § II the Israeli Haredi configuration including Shas, UTJ (Degel HaTorah + Agudat Yisrael), the Council of Torah Sages structures. The major Hasidic dynasties (Gur, Belz, Vizhnitz, Karlin-Stolin, Slonim, Boyan, Sanz, Bobov, Skver) and the Lithuanian-Litvish yeshiva architecture (Ponevezh, Mir, Hebron, Brisk). § III the 2024-2025 Israeli Supreme Court conscription crisis as the central contemporary institutional confrontation. § IV the anti-Zionist Haredi configurations (Edah HaChareidis, Satmar, Neturei Karta) and the contemporary Haredi institutional contradictions including the demographic projections that produce the contemporary institutional crisis around long-term Haredi integration into the Israeli labour market, military, and political economy.

The chapter's working configuration is a sub-dossier within the broader Israeli religious-political apparatus dossiered in Chapter 20 — and operates against any framing that would conflate the Haredi sector with the Religious Zionist sector or that would treat the Haredi sector as monolithic. The Haredi sector is  heterogeneous, containing institutional currents from full anti-Zionist non-cooperation (Satmar, Edah HaChareidis, Neturei Karta) through pragmatic non-Zionist political participation (Agudat Yisrael, Degel HaTorah, Belz) through Sephardi-Mizrahi institutional revival (Shas) through Chabad's distinctive post-1994 emissary configuration. The argument operates with the same investigative-journalism standards and the same captured-state analytic framework applied to the preceding contemporary apparatus chapters — and applied here to the Haredi sub-configuration without exceptionalism in either direction.

### I. Foundational Architecture: Hasidic Origins, Lithuanian-Litvish Counter-Movement, and the Pre-State European Configuration

**The Baal Shem Tov and the foundation of Hasidism.** Rabbi Israel ben Eliezer (the Baal Shem Tov, c. 1698-1760) is the foundational figure of Hasidism. The architecture: born around 1698 in Okopy (then Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, now western Ukraine); active across Podolia from the 1730s; settled at Mezhibozh (now Medzhybizh, Ukraine) from around 1740 until his death in 1760; principal teacher of the mystical-ecstatic Jewish spiritual revival movement that became Hasidism. The Baal Shem Tov's principal teachings were transmitted orally and recorded by his students after his death; his principal published collection is *Keter Shem Tov* (compiled 1794-1795). Hasidism emerged as an alternative to the dominant Lithuanian-Litvish Talmud-study tradition — emphasising devotional prayer (*kavanah*), ecstatic worship (*hitlahavut*), divine immanence (*panentheism* in some readings), and the role of the *tzaddik* (righteous person) as spiritual intercessor between his followers and the divine. The 18th-century Hasidic revival operated through documented mass appeal among the Jewish peasantry and lower-middle classes of Eastern European Jewry — and produced the configuration of the *court* with a hereditary *Rebbe* (*Admor* — *Adonenu Morenu V'Rabbenu*, "Our Master, Teacher, and Rabbi") as the centre of each dynasty.

**Dov Ber of Mezeritch and the spread of Hasidism.** Rabbi Dov Ber (the Maggid of Mezeritch, c. 1704-1772) is the principal architect of Hasidism's spread across Eastern Europe. The architecture: principal student of the Baal Shem Tov; relocated to Mezeritch (now Mezhirichi, Ukraine) after the Baal Shem Tov's death; trained the generation of Hasidic disciples who founded the major Hasidic dynasties — including Schneur Zalman of Liadi (Chabad), Levi Yitzchak of Berditchev, Elimelech of Lizhensk, Aharon ben Yaakov of Karlin, and others. The Maggid's achievement: the transformation of Hasidism from a regional Podolian movement into a continent-spanning configuration with dynasties active across Poland, Galicia, Hungary, Ukraine, Belarus, and Romania by the late 18th century.

**The Vilna Gaon and the opposition.** Rabbi Eliyahu ben Shlomo Zalman (the Vilna Gaon, 1720-1797) is the principal theological-juridical opponent of Hasidism — and as the foundational figure of the distinct Lithuanian-Litvish (*Mitnagdim* — "opponents") tradition. The architecture: the Vilna Gaon issued the 1772 *cherem* (rabbinical excommunication) against Hasidism, citing concerns about Hasidic theological innovations (particularly around divine immanence and the role of the *tzaddik*) as crossing into doctrinal heresy. The Lithuanian-Litvish tradition operates through intensive Talmud study (*lomdus*) rather than Hasidic ecstatic spirituality — and produced the model of the great Lithuanian yeshiva.

**Yeshivat Volozhin and the Lithuanian yeshiva model.** Founded 1803 by Rabbi Chaim of Volozhin (1749-1821, principal student of the Vilna Gaon) — is the foundational model of the contemporary Lithuanian yeshiva tradition. The architecture: full-time intensive Talmud study by young unmarried and married men; independence from local rabbinical authority; documented expansion of the Volozhin model across Lithuanian-Litvish Jewry through the 19th century with the founding of the major pre-WWII Lithuanian yeshivot — Mir (founded 1815), Slabodka (1882), Telz (1875), Ponevezh (1908), Kelm (1872), Brisk, Lomzhe, Kletsk, Radin, Kamenetz. The Lithuanian yeshiva system is the foundational model from which the contemporary Israeli yeshiva architecture dossiered in § II descends.

**Major pre-WWII Hasidic dynasties.** The pre-WWII Hasidic configuration centred on multiple dynastic courts across Eastern Europe — distinct from one another in ritual practice, theological emphasis, and political-cultural position. The principal dynasties: Ger (founded 1859 in Góra Kalwaria, Poland, by Rabbi Yitzchak Meir Alter, the Chiddushei HaRim, 1799-1866 — the largest pre-WWII Polish court); Belz (founded 1817 in Belz, Eastern Galicia, by Rabbi Shalom Rokeach, 1779-1855); Satmar (the Teitelbaum dynasty active from the 19th century in Hungarian and Romanian Transylvania, refounded under Rabbi Yoel Teitelbaum 1887-1979 from Satu Mare); Vizhnitz (founded 1854 in Vyzhnytsia, Bukovina, by Menachem Mendel Hager of Kosov); Chabad-Lubavitch (founded 1775 in Liadi by Schneur Zalman of Liadi, 1745-1812 — the most distinctive Lithuanian-Belarusian Hasidic dynasty); Bobov (founded 1881 in Bobowa, Galicia, by Shlomo Halberstam); Sanz (founded 1830 in Nowy Sącz, Galicia, by Rabbi Chaim Halberstam, 1797-1876); Karlin-Stolin (founded by Aharon ben Yaakov of Karlin, 1736-1772, in Belarusian Karlin); Bratslav-Breslov (founded by Rabbi Nachman of Breslov, 1772-1810, in Bratslav, Ukraine — distinctive as the dynasty whose Rebbe explicitly refused to be succeeded). The Chabad theological text *Tanya* (Schneur Zalman of Liadi, 1796) is the foundational systematic theological work of Chabad Hasidism — and as one of the principal philosophical contributions of the Hasidic tradition.

**The Holocaust and the destruction of European Jewry.** The Holocaust 1941-1945 produced the near-total destruction of Eastern European Hasidic and Lithuanian-Litvish infrastructure. The architecture: Nazi destruction of major yeshivot including Volozhin, Mir, Slabodka, Telz, Lomzhe, and Kletsk; Nazi destruction of major Hasidic communities at Ger, Belz, Bobov, Sanz, and others; deaths of an estimated 5-6 million European Jews including a majority of pre-war European Hasidic and Lithuanian-Litvish populations; destruction of the Aleksander dynasty (which numbered tens of thousands in 1939 and barely exists today); documented near-destruction of many other smaller dynasties. The Holocaust is the foundational catastrophe of contemporary Haredi memory — and as the context within which the post-1945 reconstitution dossiered in § II operated.

**Post-Holocaust reconstitution and the Mir, Ponevezh, and Hasidic re-establishment.** The post-1945 period produced the reconstitution of Hasidic and Lithuanian-Litvish Jewry through the survival of key figures and the transplantation of centres to Israel and the United States. The architecture: the Mir Yeshiva relocated to Shanghai 1941 and then to Jerusalem 1947 — preserving the Lithuanian yeshiva architecture; the Ponevezh Yeshiva was refounded in Bnei Brak in 1944 by Rabbi Yosef Shlomo Kahaneman (1886-1969); Satmar Rebbe Yoel Teitelbaum (1887-1979) survived on the Kasztner train of June 1944 and reconstituted Satmar in Williamsburg, Brooklyn from 1947; the Lubavitcher Rebbe Joseph Isaac Schneersohn (the sixth Rebbe, 1880-1950) reconstituted Chabad in Brooklyn from 1940; the Gerrer Rebbe Avraham Mordechai Alter (1866-1948) reconstituted Ger in Jerusalem from 1940; the Belz Rebbe Aharon Rokeach (1880-1957) reconstituted Belz in Jerusalem from 1944. The post-1945 reconstitution operated through fund-raising in the post-war American Jewish community and integration with the post-1948 Israeli state through the June 1947 status quo arrangement (cross-reference Chapter 20 § I).

**Agudat Yisrael (founded 12-15 May 1912 in Katowice).** Operates as the vehicle of Ashkenazi-Hasidic non-Zionist Haredi Jewry. The architecture: founded at the May 1912 conference in Katowice under principal founding figures including Rabbi Chaim Soloveitchik of Brisk (1853-1918), Rabbi Israel Meir Kagan (the Chofetz Chaim, 1839-1933), and the Gerrer Rebbe Avraham Mordechai Alter (1866-1948); explicitly non-Zionist (and in some currents anti-Zionist) position; established the Council of Torah Sages (*Moetzet Gedolei HaTorah*) as the rabbinical-leadership body; operated across pre-WWII Eastern Europe and post-1945 Israel and the United States as the principal vehicle of non-Zionist Ashkenazi-Hasidic Haredi politics. The Agudat Yisrael position was historically distinguished from the Satmar position by willingness to participate in the Israeli political process (as "damage-control" per documented Agudah reasoning) — in contrast to the Satmar position of total non-participation dossiered in § IV.

**The Three Oaths doctrine.** The Talmudic doctrine (Ketubot 111a) cited by the Satmar and anti-Zionist Haredi tradition as the Halachic prohibition on Jewish mass migration to the Land of Israel and Jewish political-state establishment before the Messianic age. The architecture: the Talmud (Babylonian Talmud Ketubot 111a) records three oaths sworn between the Jewish people, the nations of the world, and God — including the oath that the Jewish people would not "ascend the wall" (interpreted as forbidding mass migration to the Land of Israel) and the oath that the Jewish people would not "rebel against the nations" (interpreted as forbidding the establishment of a Jewish state before the Messianic age). The Satmar Rebbe Yoel Teitelbaum's *Vayoel Moshe* (1959) is the principal post-WWII theological exposition of the Three Oaths doctrine — and as the foundational text of the contemporary anti-Zionist Haredi theological position dossiered in § IV.

The summary of § I: the Haredi configuration operates on a foundational architecture established by the 18th-century Hasidic-Litvish split (the Baal Shem Tov, the Vilna Gaon). The 19th-century consolidation through dynastic courts and the Lithuanian yeshiva tradition; the 1912 founding of Agudat Yisrael as the vehicle of non-Zionist Ashkenazi-Hasidic Haredi politics; the Holocaust destruction of European Haredi infrastructure; and the post-1945 reconstitution of the principal Hasidic dynasties and Lithuanian yeshivot in Israel and the United States. The configuration's foundational features: pluralism across Hasidic dynasties and Lithuanian yeshiva traditions; documented theological diversity from anti-Zionist (Satmar) through non-Zionist (Agudat Yisrael) positions; post-1945 reconstitution through Israeli state accommodation and American diaspora fund-raising.

---


### II. The Israeli Haredi Institutional Configuration: Shas, UTJ, and the Major Dynasties

The contemporary Israeli Haredi configuration operates through five principal documented nodes: (1) the Council of Torah Sages structures across the three principal Haredi political bodies (Shas, Degel HaTorah, Agudat Yisrael) as the rabbinical-leadership bodies. (2) the United Torah Judaism (UTJ) Knesset bloc combining Degel HaTorah (Lithuanian-Litvish) and Agudat Yisrael (Hasidic). (3) Shas as the Sephardi-Mizrahi Haredi vehicle. (4) the major contemporary Hasidic dynasties (Ger, Belz, Vizhnitz, and others) with their distinctive dynastic-court architectures. (5) the Lithuanian-Litvish yeshiva architecture centred on Ponevezh (Bnei Brak), Mir (Jerusalem), Hebron (Jerusalem), and Brisk (Jerusalem). The evidence covers each.

**The Council of Torah Sages (*Moetzet Gedolei HaTorah*).** The principal rabbinical-leadership institutional body of Ashkenazi Haredi Jewry — and the model from which the Sephardi-Mizrahi Council of Torah Sages (*Moetzet Chachmei HaTorah*) of Shas descends. The architecture: each of the three principal Haredi political bodies operates its own Council of Torah Sages; the Councils are composed of the senior rabbinical authorities of the respective communities; the Councils issue rulings on political-religious questions that are  binding on the relevant political-party Knesset members. The Agudat Yisrael Council of Torah Sages includes the principal Hasidic Rebbes including the Gerrer Rebbe Yaakov Aryeh Alter (born 1939, Gerrer Rebbe from 1996), the Belzer Rebbe Yissachar Dov Rokeach (born 1948, Belzer Rebbe from 1966), the Vizhnitz Rebbe(s), and others. The Degel HaTorah Council of Torah Sages includes the senior Lithuanian-Litvish rabbinical authorities — historically including Rabbi Eliezer Shach (1899-2001), Rabbi Aharon Leib Shteinman (1914-2017), Rabbi Gershon Edelstein (1923-2023), and their successors. The Shas Council of Torah Sages was historically chaired by Rabbi Ovadia Yosef (1920-2013) until his death; the current chair is Rabbi Shalom Cohen (1931-2022 — replaced after Cohen's death).

**United Torah Judaism (UTJ) — the Ashkenazi Haredi Knesset bloc.** Cross-reference Chapter 20 § II. The architecture: UTJ is a Knesset bloc combining Degel HaTorah (Lithuanian-Litvish, founded 1988 by Rabbi Eliezer Shach) and Agudat Yisrael (Hasidic, founded 1912); the two parties maintain independence at the leadership level but run a joint Knesset list; documented 6-8 Knesset seats across most post-1992 elections (7 seats in the 2022 election); priority on yeshiva-student conscription exemption preservation, state funding of Haredi educational institutions, and Sabbath public-life observance. UTJ leadership under the December 2022 Netanyahu coalition: Moshe Gafni (Degel HaTorah, Finance Committee chair); Yitzhak Goldknopf (Agudat Yisrael, Housing Minister). The post-2024 institutional tension within UTJ around the Haredi conscription crisis is dossiered in § III.

**Shas — the Sephardi-Mizrahi Haredi political vehicle.** Founded 1984 by Rabbi Ovadia Yosef (1920-2013) — is the vehicle of Sephardi-Mizrahi Haredi Jewry in Israeli politics. The architecture: Ovadia Yosef (Sephardi Chief Rabbi 1973-1983) as the foundational figure; Shas operating electorally with documented 11-17 Knesset seats across most post-1984 elections (11 seats in the 2022 election); success in capturing the Sephardi-Mizrahi political-religious-cultural constituency through the Maayan school network (the *Maayan HaChinuch HaTorani* school network), Shas-controlled welfare and community-services networks, and documented Shas-controlled religious-council infrastructure. Shas's positioning differs from UTJ in two documented respects: Shas is a Sephardi-Mizrahi vehicle (not Ashkenazi-Hasidic-Lithuanian); Shas has historically operated with flexibility on coalition partners (joining both right and centre-left governments across the 1984-2026 period) where UTJ has typically operated only in right-wing coalitions since 1992.

**Aryeh Deri.** Born 13 February 1959 Meknès, Morocco — is the principal contemporary Shas political figure since 1990s; cross-reference Chapter 20 § II. The architecture: Shas political leader from 1990s; documented Interior Minister 1988-1993 and 1996-1999; documented bribery conviction 1999 with 22 months prison sentence; documented return to Shas leadership 2013; documented tax-offence conviction 2022 with suspended sentence; documented Interior Minister and Health Minister appointment December 2022 — followed by Israeli Supreme Court ruling January 2023 that his criminal-conviction record precluded ministerial service; documented Deri removal from cabinet January 2023; documented continued operation as Shas political leader and documented coalition kingmaker. The Deri position operates as documented evidence of the contradictions of the post-2022 Netanyahu coalition — with Supreme Court ruling against his ministerial appointment but documented continued political role.

**The major contemporary Hasidic dynasties.** The architecture: contemporary Hasidic Jewry operates through documented multiple dynastic courts each with its own architecture.
