The principal dynasties: Ger (the largest Israeli Hasidic dynasty with around 110,000 members across the country per Jerusalem Post analysis); Belz (the second-largest Israeli Hasidic dynasty with around 50,000 members; Belzer Rebbe Yissachar Dov Rokeach from 1966; Belz 1979 split from the Edah HaChareidis and Belz pragmatic-engagement approach with the Israeli state); Vizhnitz (mostly Bnei Brak); Karlin-Stolin (with the Karlin presence in Givat Ze'ev from 1991 — the first court to settle beyond the 1967 line per Powerbase analysis); Slonim; Boyan; Sanz-Klausenburg (divided into New York and Israeli branches with around 3,800 households total); Skver (around 3,300 households globally); Bobov (around 4,500 households; post-2005 succession dispute producing the Bobov and Bobov-45 institutional split with around 3,000 and 1,500 households respectively); Toldos Aharon ( extreme anti-Zionist Edah HaChareidis-affiliated dynasty; cross-reference § IV).

**The Gerrer Hasidic configuration.** The architecture: founded 1859 in Góra Kalwaria, Poland, by Rabbi Yitzchak Meir Alter (the Chiddushei HaRim, 1799-1866); reconstituted post-Holocaust in Jerusalem under Rebbe Avraham Mordechai Alter (1866-1948) and successors; current Gerrer Rebbe Yaakov Aryeh Alter (born 1939, Gerrer Rebbe from 1996); Ger position as the largest Israeli Hasidic dynasty with around 110,000 members; Gerrer positions on strict separation of the sexes (including Ger-instigated separation on Bnei Brak buses), restrictions on married couples' relationships (the "Gerrer customs" *takanot*), separation of husbands and wives in public; Ger involvement in Jerusalem municipal politics since 1993 (Ehud Olmert coalition arrangement with the Gerrer community per Jerusalem Post analysis). The Ger configuration operates as documented evidence of the autonomy of Hasidic dynasties within the broader Israeli social configuration — with Gerrer-internal religious-legal rulings (*takanot*) operating with binding effect on Gerrer Hasidim outside formal Israeli legal authority.

**The Lithuanian-Litvish yeshiva architecture.** The architecture: contemporary Lithuanian-Litvish Jewry operates through documented intensive Talmud-study yeshiva institutions descended from the pre-WWII Lithuanian yeshiva model dossiered in § I. The principal contemporary Lithuanian yeshivot: Ponevezh Yeshiva in Bnei Brak (refounded 1944 by Rabbi Yosef Shlomo Kahaneman 1886-1969 after the Holocaust destruction of the original Ponevezh Yeshiva in Lithuania — documented largest contemporary Lithuanian-Litvish yeshiva with around 4,000-5,000 students); Mir Yeshiva in Jerusalem (refounded post-Holocaust — documented large contemporary Lithuanian yeshiva); Hebron Yeshiva in Jerusalem (Lithuanian-Litvish tradition); Brisk Yeshiva (Soloveitchik dynasty institutional tradition with multiple Brisk yeshiva institutions). The Lithuanian yeshivot operate with priority on intensive full-time Talmud study by men aged 18 through the 30s; support for unmarried-yeshiva-student conscription exemption; non-engagement with mainstream Israeli secular education, labour market, or military service. The contemporary Lithuanian-Litvish configuration is the centre of the post-2024 Israeli Haredi conscription crisis dossiered in § III.

**The Chabad-Lubavitch configuration.** Operates as the structurally most-distinctive Hasidic dynasty within the contemporary configuration. The architecture: founded 1775 by Schneur Zalman of Liadi (1745-1812) with the *Tanya* (1796) as foundational text; distinctive through the post-1950 *shaliach* (emissary) network — Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson (1902-1994, seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe 1950-1994) sent thousands of Chabad emissary couples to establish Chabad institutional presence in hundreds of cities globally; distinctive position on Israel — Chabad as the Hasidic dynasty most documentedly supportive of the Israeli state and most documentedly engaged in Israeli political-religious public life (in contrast to the Satmar anti-Zionist position dossiered in § IV); distinctiveness post-1994 — Schneerson left no successor after his death on 12 June 1994; Chabad subsequently operates without a contemporary Rebbe through the emissary-network configuration; institutional Messianist faction (*meshichistim*) maintaining that Schneerson is the Messiah and will be revealed as such — operating against the mainstream Chabad position that does not formally endorse the Messianist position. The Chabad post-1994 configuration operates as documented evidence of the flexibility of Hasidic configurations — a Hasidic dynasty without a Rebbe but operating through a global emissary network.

**The Wodziński 2016 demographic study.** Per the principal scholarly study of contemporary Hasidic demographics by Marcin Wodziński (Professor of Jewish History, University of Wrocław): 129,211 Hasidic households worldwide as of 2016; 62,062 households (48%) in Israel; 53,485 households (41%) in the United States; 5,519 households (4%) in the United Kingdom; 3,392 households (3%) in Canada. Per Powerbase analysis: Satmar is the largest single Hasidic sect worldwide with around 26,000 member households (around 20% of all Hasidim) — based in Williamsburg, Brooklyn; Kiryas Joel, New York; and Boro Park, Brooklyn. The Wodziński data is the contemporary demographic baseline for institutional analysis of the Hasidic configuration — with the projection of continued institutional growth through the next decades dossiered in § IV.

**Haredi population in Israel.** Per Israel Democracy Institute data: Haredim constitute around 13-14% of the Israeli Jewish population as of 2024 — around 1.3 million people; institutional demographic growth rate of around 4% per year (the highest of any institutional sub-group in Israel) producing projected Haredi share of Israeli Jewish population of around 16% by 2030, around 25% by 2040, around 32% by 2050, and around 50% by 2065 per Israel Central Bureau of Statistics projections — producing the contemporary crisis around long-term integration of the Haredi sector into the Israeli labour market, military, and political economy dossiered in § IV.

The summary of § II: the contemporary Israeli Haredi configuration operates through the Council of Torah Sages structures, the UTJ Knesset bloc (Degel HaTorah + Agudat Yisrael), Shas as the Sephardi-Mizrahi vehicle, the major Hasidic dynasties (Ger, Belz, Vizhnitz, and others), and the Lithuanian-Litvish yeshiva architecture (Ponevezh, Mir, Hebron, Brisk). The configuration operates with heterogeneity across dynastic, theological, and political dimensions — operating against any framing of the Haredi sector as monolithic, and operating within the Israeli ethno-religious-state configuration dossiered in Chapter 20. The 2024-2026 crisis around the Haredi conscription exemption is the principal contemporary moment of the confrontation between the Haredi sector and the Israeli secular-majority — and is dossiered in § III.

---


### III. The 2024-2025 Israeli Supreme Court Conscription Crisis

The 2024-2026 Israeli Supreme Court conscription crisis is the principal contemporary confrontation between the Haredi sector and the Israeli secular-majority — and as the principal contemporary crisis of the post-1948 Haredi-state arrangement dossiered in Chapter 20 § II. The architecture has six principal documented arcs: (1) the historical pre-2024 institutional context including the 2002 Tal Law, the 2012 Supreme Court invalidation. And the June 2023 statutory expiration without renewal. (2) the 28 February 2024 interim Supreme Court order. (3) the 25 June 2024 unanimous Supreme Court ruling that yeshiva students must be drafted. (4) the post-June 2024 non-compliance producing the April 2025 and November 2025 follow-on Supreme Court actions. (5) the 2024-2025 conscription data showing failure of the enforcement architecture. (6) the late 2025-early 2026 contempt-of-court proceedings and the contemporary moment of institutional crisis. The documentation covers each.

**The historical pre-2024 institutional context.** Cross-reference Chapter 20 § II. The architecture: the original 1948 Ben-Gurion arrangement exempted around 400 yeshiva students from military service under the rationale of preserving the Haredi yeshiva-study tradition after the Holocaust; the governing arrangement was preserved through documented successive legislative frameworks throughout the post-1948 period; the exempt-yeshiva-student number grew over decades to documented tens of thousands by the 1990s and documented 63,000-70,000 by 2024 per Israeli state figures; the Tal Law of 2002 (named after Justice Tzvi Tal who chaired the drafting committee) served as the framework allowing yeshiva-student conscription exemption from 2002 to 2012; the 11 February 2012 Supreme Court ruling that the Tal Law was unconstitutional produced the vacuum that successive legislative attempts (the 2014 Equal Service Law, the 2017 Knesset amendments, and others) failed to resolve; the June 2023 statutory expiration of the framework without legislative renewal produced the vacuum from which the 2024 Supreme Court rulings emerged.

**The 28 February 2024 interim Supreme Court order.** The architecture: on 28 February 2024, the Israeli Supreme Court issued an interim order freezing state funding to non-compliant yeshivas — operating as the juridical opening of the contemporary Haredi conscription crisis. The architecture: the Court accepted the petition arguments from the Movement for Quality Government in Israel and other petitioners; the Court ruled that the post-June 2023 vacuum meant that documented state funding to yeshivas whose students were exempt from conscription operated as documented illegal state funding without legal basis. The 28 February 2024 interim order served as the moment from which the post-October 2023 wartime Haredi-secular institutional tension escalated into juridical confrontation.

**The 2 June 2024 Supreme Court 9-justice panel hearing.** The architecture: on 2 June 2024, the Israeli Supreme Court convened with an expanded 9-justice panel to consider the conscription question — operating as the juridical preparation for the principal meaningful ruling. The architecture: petitions from the Movement for Quality Government, Brothers in Arms, Mothers on the Frontline (demanding immediate drafting of yeshiva students); petitions from the Ayalon Forum for Human Rights and the Civil Democracy Movement (seeking conversion of the February 2024 interim order into final order); a sixth petition challenging the government's parallel-consultation approach. The 2 June 2024 hearing served as the preparation for the 25 June 2024 ruling.

**The 25 June 2024 unanimous Supreme Court ruling.** The principal the juridical moment of the contemporary Haredi conscription crisis. The architecture: the Israeli Supreme Court ruled unanimously in *Movement for Quality Government in Israel v. Minister of Defense* (5819/24) that — given the expiration of the legislative framework allowing for yeshiva-student exemption in June 2023 — Haredi yeshiva students must be drafted into the Israeli Defence Forces (IDF) just as other Israeli citizens; and that state funding to non-compliant yeshivas is illegal. The architecture: the Court ruled that the Government Decision 628 (which had instructed the Minister of Defence not to draft Haredi yeshiva students) was institutionally illegal — since the Minister of Defence had no legal authority to exempt Haredim from military service, the government had no legal power to instruct the Minister to do so; the Court explicitly noted the absence of legal basis for sweeping exemption; the Court declined to address the question of Haredi women conscription explicitly. The 25 June 2024 ruling is the juridical end of the 1948-2023 yeshiva-student conscription exemption arrangement — and as the moment from which the contemporary Haredi-state institutional crisis escalated.

**The post-June 2024 non-compliance.** The architecture: Israeli state response was to attempt enforcement through incremental measures rather than mass conscription; Israeli government position that the IDF could not immediately absorb all 63,000-70,000 eligible Haredi yeshiva students; documented agreement on a minimum target of 4,800 Haredi recruits by June 2025 — described as a "minimum starting figure" with the IDF working to increase the number; the 5-6 August 2024 initial enforcement moment with 900 ultra-Orthodox yeshiva students required to present at IDF enlistment centres; Haredi-political-bloc protest including road blocks and street demonstrations in the post-July 2024 period. The post-June 2024 non-compliance operates as evidence of the incapacity of the post-2022 Netanyahu coalition to deliver effective Supreme Court ruling enforcement given the coalition mathematical dependence on the Haredi political bloc.

**The documented 2024-2025 conscription data.** Per Israel Democracy Institute analysis (October 2025 + January 2026): in the 2024 conscription year (July 2024 to June 2025), around 24,000 initial conscription orders were issued to members of the Haredi public; only 2,738 attended IDF conscription centres; only 1,508 continued the conscription process; only 798 were enlisted — producing a enforcement rate of around 3.3% of orders issued resulting in actual enlistment. Per IDI June 2025 analysis (one year after the 25 June 2024 ruling): of the 19,000 summons issued by early June 2025, only around 5% (996) reported to induction centres; only 1.2% (232) were actually conscripted — far below the 4,800 minimum target. Per Jerusalem Post: the IDF projected 50,000+ draft orders to be issued July 2025 - June 2026 (the 2025-2026 draft year). The data operates as evidence of the failure of the post-June 2024 enforcement architecture — and as the moment from which the April 2025 and November 2025 follow-on Supreme Court actions emerged.

**The 27 April 2025 provisional order.** The architecture: on 27 April 2025, the Israeli High Court issued a provisional order demanding the Israeli government explain its failure to issue enough conscription orders to ultra-Orthodox yeshiva students to meet the IDF needs, and its failure to enforce the orders it had issued. The architecture: the provisional order switched the burden of proof from the petitioners to the respondent (the defence minister and the government); the order indicated that the Court considered there to be merit in the petition; the Court instructed the government to explain those failures in a written statement by 24 June 2025. The 27 April 2025 provisional order was the juridical escalation of the post-June 2024 institutional crisis.

**The 19 November 2025 Supreme Court ruling.** The architecture: on 19 November 2025, the Israeli Supreme Court issued a ruling requiring the Israeli government to formulate an effective enforcement plan for Haredi conscription — operating as the juridical escalation of the post-April 2025 institutional crisis. The architecture: the Court considered the government 27 October 2025 response (which presented the 24,000-orders/2,738-attendance/1,508-continued/798-enlisted data); the Court ruled that the response was  insufficient; the Court ordered the government to produce an effective enforcement plan. The 19 November 2025 ruling is the juridical opening of the contemporary contempt-of-court proceedings dossiered next.

**The contempt-of-court proceedings late 2025-early 2026.** The architecture: following the 19 November 2025 ruling, the Movement for Quality Government in Israel filed a contempt-of-court motion against the Israeli government — arguing that the government had documentedly not complied with the November 2025 ruling. Per Israel Democracy Institute analysis (January 2026): the contempt-of-court proceedings operate as the most-serious juridical confrontation between the Israeli Supreme Court and the Netanyahu government since the post-2022 period — with commentators considering whether the Israeli configuration faces the risk of a constitutional crisis or whether the crisis is already present. The architecture is the contemporary juridical moment of unresolved confrontation between the Israeli Supreme Court (operating as the guardian of the rule-of-law principle) and the Netanyahu coalition (operating with documented mathematical dependence on the Haredi political bloc).

**The IDF capacity projection.** Per IDI January 2026 analysis: starting July 2026, the IDF will be  able to absorb all those obligated to enlist from the Haredi public — producing the moment from which the post-July 2026 institutional Haredi-state confrontation moves from a capacity-justification framing to a compliance framing. The architecture is the projection of the immediate future — with uncertainty around how the Netanyahu coalition will navigate the incompatibility between Supreme Court enforcement requirements and Haredi political-bloc institutional preferences.

The summary of § III: the 2024-2026 Israeli Supreme Court conscription crisis is the principal contemporary confrontation between the Haredi sector and the Israeli secular-majority. The configuration delivered: (1) the June 2023 statutory expiration of the yeshiva-student exemption framework. (2) the 28 February 2024 interim Supreme Court order freezing state funding to non-compliant yeshivas. (3) documented 25 June 2024 unanimous Supreme Court ruling that yeshiva students must be drafted. (4) post-June 2024 non-compliance with the 798 of 24. 000 conscription orders resulting in enlistment July 2024 - June 2025. (5) documented 19 November 2025 Supreme Court ruling requiring effective enforcement plan. (6) late 2025-early 2026 contempt-of-court proceedings producing the contemporary moment of unresolved institutional crisis. The configuration operates as evidence of the contradictions of the post-2022 Netanyahu coalition and as the contemporary illustration of the post-1948 Haredi-state arrangement at its institutional limit.

---


### IV. Anti-Zionist Haredi Configurations and Contemporary Institutional Contradictions

The anti-Zionist configurations operate as the distinctive sub-current within the contemporary Haredi sector — opposing the existence of the State of Israel as a Halachic abomination under the Three Oaths doctrine dossiered in § I, and operating separate from the pragmatic-engagement Haredi political bodies (Shas, UTJ) dossiered in § II. The architecture has six principal documented arcs: (1) the Satmar configuration as the principal contemporary anti-Zionist Hasidic dynasty under the post-Yoel Teitelbaum architecture. (2) the Edah HaChareidis as the Jerusalem-based anti-Zionist Haredi body. (3) Neturei Karta as the anti-Zionist political-theological group. (4) the tensions across the contemporary Haredi sector between the pragmatic-engagement and anti-Zionist currents including the late 2025-early 2026 UTJ-internal Belz/Degel HaTorah tensions. (5) the Haredi demographic projections and the contemporary crisis around long-term Haredi-state sustainability. (6) the contradictions of the contemporary Haredi sector within the broader Israeli ethno-religious-state configuration.

**The Satmar configuration.** Operates as the principal contemporary anti-Zionist Hasidic dynasty — and as the largest single Hasidic sect worldwide with around 26,000 member households per the Wodziński 2016 study. The architecture: founded 1905 in Satu Mare (then Hungary, now Romania) — refounded post-Holocaust by Rabbi Yoel Teitelbaum (1887-1979) in Williamsburg, Brooklyn from 1947; based in Williamsburg, Brooklyn; Kiryas Joel, New York; Boro Park, Brooklyn; Monsey, New York; and Haredi centres in North America, Europe, Israel, and Argentina; Yoel Teitelbaum's anti-Zionist theological position formulated in his principal works *Vayoel Moshe* (1959) and *Al HaGeulah V'Al HaTemurah* (1968); Satmar position that any participation in the state political process — including documented voting in Israeli elections — operates as Halachic sin contributing to documented spiritual and physical destruction; Satmar refusal to receive state social benefits or other monetary aid; Satmar condemnation of other Haredi groups that accept state funding or political participation; Satmar Rebbe Yoel Teitelbaum refusal to allow Satmar Hasidim to approach the Western Wall after 1967 as institutional support for the Zionist state.

**The post-Yoel Teitelbaum Satmar architecture.** The architecture: following Yoel Teitelbaum's death in 1979, his nephew Moshe Teitelbaum (1914-2006) served as Satmar Rebbe 1979-2006; following Moshe Teitelbaum's death in 2006, institutional succession dispute between his two sons produced the Satmar split into two factions — Aaron Teitelbaum (born 1947) led the Williamsburg-based Aroynem faction; Zalman Teitelbaum (born 1951) led the Kiryas Joel-based Zalonim faction; both factions claim Satmar succession; Satmar succession dispute documentedly taken to the secular courts per scholarly accounts; contemporary Satmar configuration operates through documented parallel institutional structures in Williamsburg and Kiryas Joel. The post-2006 Satmar split is documented evidence of the fragility of the dynastic-succession model — and as the contemporary configuration of the largest Hasidic sect.

**The Satmar 2025 New York State Education Department funding crisis.** The architecture: in February 2025, the New York State Education Department documentedly cut funding to two Satmar yeshivas for institutional failure to meet documented secular education standards in core subjects (English and mathematics); May 2025 pending legislation that would documentedly delay consequences for Hasidic and other religious schools failing to meet secular standards and would documentedly make compliance significantly easier; documented continued Satmar position against secular education curriculum requirements per the Satmar position that exclusively religious education is the Halachic and priority. The architecture is documented evidence of the confrontation between the Satmar educational autonomy preference and US-state educational-standards requirements — parallel to the post-2024 Israeli Supreme Court conscription confrontation dossiered in § III.

**The Edah HaChareidis (*HaEdah HaChareidis*).** Operates as the Jerusalem-based anti-Zionist Haredi body — distinct from the Satmar New York-based configuration but documentedly aligned with the Satmar anti-Zionist theological position. The architecture: institutional descendant of the pre-state Old Yishuv (the pre-1880s established Jewish community in Mandatory Palestine); consistently  anti-Zionist position throughout the post-1948 period; governing body operating through a Rabbinical Court (*Beit Din*); affiliated communities including Toldos Aharon (the largest Edah HaChareidis-affiliated dynasty), Toldos Avrohom Yitzhak, Dushinsky, partial Satmar presence, the Brisk faction, and Mishkenos HaRoim; Edah HaChareidis position against state institutions including refusal to participate in Israeli elections or accept state funding. The Edah HaChareidis is the principal contemporary anti-Zionist Haredi body within Israel — operating parallel to the Satmar configuration in the United States.

**The Central Rabbinical Congress (CRC) of the USA and Canada.** Operates as the Satmar-led anti-Zionist Haredi body in North America — institutional counterpart to the Edah HaChareidis in Israel. The architecture: position against state institutions; alignment with the Satmar configuration. The CRC is the North American vehicle of the anti-Zionist Haredi position.

**Neturei Karta (*Guardians of the City*).** Founded 1938 in Jerusalem — operates as the anti-Zionist political-theological group. The architecture: founded by Rabbi Amram Blau (1900-1974) and Rabbi Aharon Katzenelbogen as a split from the Agudat Yisrael over the Agudah willingness to engage with the pre-state Zionist configuration; Neturei Karta position against the existence of the State of Israel as a Halachic abomination under the Three Oaths doctrine; Neturei Karta positions including historical contacts with the Palestine Liberation Organization, contacts with the Iranian Islamic Republic (including Neturei Karta attendance at the 2006 Tehran Holocaust-questioning conference), and positions on Hamas. The Neturei Karta configuration is the most-publicly-visible anti-Zionist Haredi vehicle — operating with minority position within the broader anti-Zionist configuration (most anti-Zionist Haredim including mainstream Satmar position do not endorse Neturei Karta contacts with external anti-Israel political actors).

**The late 2025-early 2026 UTJ-internal tensions.** Per contemporary analysis (Luke Ford February 2026 reporting): late 2025-early 2026 tensions within the United Torah Judaism Knesset bloc around the Belz "pragmatic broker" institutional approach to the Haredi conscription crisis; Degel HaTorah threat to documentedly split the UTJ over Belz institutional discussions with the IDF on draft protocols (early February 2026); Belz late 2025-early 2026 plans for a new "Kiryas Belz" 1,200-acre Hasidic metropolis in the US Tri-State area; Satmar (specifically Aaron Teitelbaum-led Williamsburg faction) condemnation of Belz as "Zionist collaborators" and "North Koreans of Hasidism." The late 2025-early 2026 UTJ-internal tensions operate as documented evidence of the spectrum within the contemporary Haredi sector — with pragmatic-engagement (Belz, Agudat Yisrael mainstream) operating against anti-Zionist refusal (Satmar) operating against Lithuanian-Litvish hardline refusal of state-curriculum or conscription concessions (Degel HaTorah, Ponevezh, Brisk, Hebron yeshiva positions).

**The Haredi demographic projections.** Per Israel Central Bureau of Statistics projections: Haredim projected to constitute around 16% of the Israeli Jewish population by 2030; around 25% by 2040; around 32% by 2050; around 50% by 2065 — producing projected configuration in which the Haredi sector will constitute the majority of the Israeli Jewish population within roughly 40 years of the contemporary moment. The architecture carries implications for: (1) the long-term institutional integration of the Haredi sector into the Israeli labour market — contemporary Haredi labour-force participation around 50% for men (well below the Israeli national average of around 80%) producing the question of how the Israeli economy can sustain a projected 50%-Haredi Jewish population with low Haredi male labour-force participation; (2) the military service question (dossiered in § III); (3) the political-economy question of state funding of Haredi educational institutions; (4) the question of Haredi cultural-political integration into the Israeli secular-majority Jewish configuration.

**The contemporary contradictions of the Haredi sector.** The configuration generates contradictions specific to the Haredi sub-configuration within the broader Israeli religious-political apparatus: (1) the Haredi position of preserving the post-Holocaust yeshiva-study priority operates against Israeli Supreme Court rulings requiring Haredi compliance with universal-conscription requirements; (2) the Haredi preference for the Halacha-based personal-status regime operates against documented secular-majority preferences for civil marriage, Conservative and Reform Jewish institutional recognition, and gender-equality institutional reforms; (3) the Haredi preference for state funding of Haredi educational institutions operates against Israeli Supreme Court rulings around state funding of non-compliant yeshivas and around secular-curriculum requirements; (4) the Haredi non-engagement with the Israeli military operates against Israeli secular-majority institutional resentment during sustained wartime; (5) the Haredi-political-bloc kingmaker position in the post-2022 Netanyahu coalition operates against Haredi demographic minority status and Israeli secular-majority preferences.

**The contemporary Haredi configuration.** As of late 2026, the Haredi configuration operates as: (1) the structurally most-documented heterogeneous Haredi configuration in modern history — with pluralism across Hasidic dynasties, Lithuanian-Litvish yeshivot, Sephardi-Mizrahi configurations, and anti-Zionist currents; (2) operating under contemporary crisis around the post-June 2024 Supreme Court conscription rulings and the late 2025-early 2026 contempt-of-court proceedings; (3) operating with projected demographic growth that produces crisis around long-term Haredi-state sustainability; (4) operating with documented internal tensions between pragmatic-engagement (Shas, UTJ-Agudah mainstream, Belz) and anti-Zionist (Satmar, Edah HaChareidis, Neturei Karta) currents; (5) operating with kingmaker position in the post-2022 Netanyahu coalition producing disproportionate political-policy influence relative to documented demographic share; (6) operating within the broader Israeli religious-political apparatus dossiered in Chapter 20 — and operating with consequences for the long-term Israeli ethno-religious-state configuration.

The summary of § IV: the anti-Zionist configurations operate as the distinctive sub-current of the contemporary Haredi sector — through Satmar (the largest single Hasidic sect worldwide), Edah HaChareidis (the principal Jerusalem-based anti-Zionist Haredi body), the CRC of the USA and Canada, and Neturei Karta (the governingly most-publicly-visible anti-Zionist political-theological group). The configuration stands alongside the pragmatic-engagement Haredi political bodies (Shas, UTJ) dossiered in § II — and sits within the contemporary institutional crisis dossiered in § III. The section closes here. Chapter 22 dossiers the Sangh Parivar configuration as the principal Hindu captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. Operating in cross-reference with Chapter 8 (Hindu foundational dossier), with the contemporary Indian Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) institutional integration with the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS) and the broader Sangh Parivar institutional network under Prime Minister Narendra Modi. With the post-2014 Hindutva institutional ascendance, and with the contemporary Indian configuration including tensions around Indian Muslim, Christian, Sikh, and Dalit communities.

---


## Chapter 22. The Sangh Parivar / Hindu BJP-RSS Apparatus

Chapter 22 dossiers the Sangh Parivar configuration as the principal Hindu captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The analysis operates in cross-reference with Chapter 8 (Hindu foundational dossier), and operates structurally as the Hindu parallel to the contemporary working-apparatus dossiers of Chapters 15-21. The analysis applies the same investigative-journalism standards and the same captured-state analytic framework to the Hindu configuration that the preceding chapters apply to the Catholic, Protestant-Evangelical, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni, Shia, and Jewish configurations — without exceptionalism in either direction.

The record covers four sections: § I the foundational architecture from V.D. Savarkar's 1923 *Essentials of Hindutva* through Hedgewar's 1925 founding of the RSS, Golwalkar's mid-century consolidation, the Gandhi assassination 1948, the 1951 Bharatiya Jana Sangh, the 1980 founding of the BJP, the 1992 Babri Masjid demolition. And the Vajpayee governments 1996-2004. § II the contemporary Sangh Parivar architecture including the RSS as parent body, the BJP as political wing, the VHP, Bajrang Dal, ABVP. And the broader network of 32+ affiliated organisations. § III the Narendra Modi government 2014-2026 including the post-2014 Hindutva ascendance, the August 2019 abrogation of Article 370, the December 2019 Citizenship Amendment Act. The January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration, and the June 2024 third-term coalition government formation. § IV the contemporary tensions including the May 2023 Manipur ethnic violence, the post-2014 communal incidents, and the RSS-Modi tension 2024-2026 around the principle of *vyakti* (individual) subordination to *sangathan* (organisation).

The chapter's working configuration operates through a structure distinct from the preceding contemporary apparatus configurations. The Vatican operates as a juridical-territorial sovereign state. American evangelicalism operates a distributed network without a central organisational structure. The Moscow Patriarchate operates as a church-state integration. The Saudi apparatus operates through the Al ash-Sheikh-Al Saud religious-political alliance. The Iranian apparatus operates through *velayat-e faqih* clerical supremacy. The Israeli apparatus operates through Jewish ethno-religious state structure. The Sangh Parivar operates through a seventh distinct configuration: a network of ostensibly-independent civil-society organisations bound together by ideological adhesion to Hindutva, with the RSS as parent body operating without formal legal-organisational existence (the RSS has no membership records. Its adherents are *swayamsevaks* or "volunteers"), with the BJP as the political-electoral wing operating through the Indian parliamentary democracy framework, and with the wider Sangh Parivar network operating through institutional penetration of Indian civil society, education, culture, and security.

### I. Foundational Architecture: Hindutva, Hedgewar, Golwalkar, and the Pre-2014 Trajectory

**V.D. Savarkar and the formulation of Hindutva 1923.** Vinayak Damodar Savarkar (1883-1966) is the principal theoretical architect of Hindu nationalism. The architecture: Savarkar formulated the concept of *Hindutva* in his 1923 work *Essentials of Hindutva*, written while imprisoned by the British Raj in Ratnagiri (Savarkar had been imprisoned 1911-1924 for anti-British activity including the killing of Nashik District Magistrate A.M.T. Jackson). Savarkar's principal theoretical contribution: the redefinition of Indian national identity from the geographic-civic framework toward a Hindu-religious-cultural framework — defining "Hindus" as those whose *pitribhumi* (fatherland) and *punyabhumi* (holyland) are both within the geographical bounds of India. The Hindutva framework thereby excluded Indian Muslims and Indian Christians from full Indian national identity — since their holyland was located outside India (Mecca, Bethlehem, Rome). Savarkar served as Hindu Mahasabha president 1937-1943; he was acquitted of conspiracy in the Gandhi assassination 1948 trial but the connections of Savarkar to the assassins were noted in the Kapur Commission report of 1969.

**Keshav Baliram Hedgewar and the founding of the RSS 27 September 1925.** Hedgewar (1889-1940) — Nagpur physician and Hindu nationalist organiser — founded the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS, "National Volunteer Organisation") on 27 September 1925 in Nagpur. The architecture: Hedgewar designed the RSS as a *cadre-based* organisation operating through the *shakha* (daily local gathering) institutional unit — a daily one-hour gathering combining physical training, ideological instruction, and *bhagwa dhwaj* (saffron flag) ritual; the RSS as vehicle of long-term cultural-political transformation rather than short-term electoral politics; the model influenced by the European fascist movements of the 1920s (Mussolini's Italy, the Nazi paramilitary formations in Germany) as well as the Hindu martial-revival tradition. The 1925 founding date — *Vijayadashami* (the festival of victory) in the Hindu calendar — served as the signal of the RSS as a movement of Hindu-civilisational revival. The RSS *Sarsanghchalak* (Supreme Leader) position operates with documented lifetime tenure: Hedgewar 1925-1940; M.S. Golwalkar 1940-1973; Madhukar Dattatreya Deoras (1915-1996) 1973-1994; Rajendra Singh (1922-2003) 1994-2000; K.S. Sudarshan (1931-2012) 2000-2009; Mohan Bhagwat (born 11 September 1950) from 21 March 2009.

**M.S. Golwalkar and the mid-century consolidation.** Madhav Sadashiv Golwalkar (1906-1973), known as *Guruji*, operated as second RSS Sarsanghchalak 1940-1973 — the architect of the post-Hedgewar consolidation of the RSS. The architecture: Golwalkar's principal text *We, or Our Nationhood Defined* (1939, attributed to Golwalkar though authorship contested) provided the theoretical framework for Hindu Rashtra (Hindu nation); Golwalkar's *Bunch of Thoughts* (1966) is the principal theoretical statement of post-independence Hindutva. The *We* text documented the "Internal Threats" framework — defining Muslims, Christians, and Communists as the three principal internal threats to the Hindu nation; documented passages of *We* praised the Nazi treatment of German Jewry as a model for India's treatment of Muslims (the passages were subsequently withdrawn from later editions by the RSS but remained on the historical record). The Golwalkar consolidation operated through the post-1948 RSS reconstitution after the Gandhi assassination ban and through the expansion of the Sangh Parivar institutional network across the 1950s-1970s including the founding of Bharatiya Jana Sangh (1951), Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (1948), Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (1955), and Vishva Hindu Parishad (1964).

**The 30 January 1948 assassination of Mahatma Gandhi.** Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi (1869-1948) — leader of the Indian independence movement and principal figure of post-independence Indian secular configuration — was assassinated on 30 January 1948 in New Delhi by Nathuram Godse (1910-1949), a former RSS member and Hindu Mahasabha activist. The architecture: Godse's motivation was Gandhi's institutional commitment to Hindu-Muslim communal harmony and Gandhi's Partition-era pressure on the new Indian government to release Rs. 55 crore to Pakistan under the 1947 financial agreement; Godse was executed 15 November 1949; the RSS was banned 4 February 1948 by Home Minister Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel under the allegation of RSS involvement in the assassination; the RSS ban was lifted 11 July 1949 after the RSS submitted a written constitution and committed to political non-involvement. The 1948 Gandhi assassination is the foundational moment of post-independence Hindutva configuration — and as the historical event that the contemporary Sangh Parivar architecture has documentedly worked to recast as Godse-as-patriot rather than Godse-as-assassin (cross-reference BJP MP Sadhvi Pragya Singh Thakur 2019 statement calling Godse a "patriot" producing Modi response).

**The Bharatiya Jana Sangh founded 21 October 1951.** Shyama Prasad Mookerjee (1901-1953) — former minister in Nehru's interim government — founded the Bharatiya Jana Sangh (BJS) as the political wing of Hindu nationalism in independent India. The architecture: BJS operated electorally through the 1950s-1970s with documented limited electoral success; principal BJS figures included Mookerjee (until his death in 1953 in detention in Kashmir), Atal Bihari Vajpayee (1924-2018), Lal Krishna Advani (born 8 November 1927), and others. The BJS-Sangh institutional relationship is the precedent for the contemporary BJP-RSS institutional relationship — with the political wing operating as the electoral-public face of the RSS-led ideological movement.

**The 1975-1977 Emergency and the Janata Party coalition.** Indira Gandhi (1917-1984) declared a state of Emergency from 25 June 1975 to 21 March 1977 — suspending Indian constitutional rights and producing institutional arrest of opposition figures including RSS and BJS leaders. The RSS was banned during the Emergency from 4 July 1975 until 22 March 1977. The post-Emergency 1977 elections produced the institutional Janata Party victory — a coalition including BJS, the Congress (O), Bharatiya Lok Dal, and Socialist Party — under Prime Minister Morarji Desai (1896-1995); the consequence was BJS members serving in the central government cabinet for the first time. The Janata Party coalition collapsed in 1979 over the "dual membership" controversy — the opposition demand that BJS members renounce their RSS membership; the BJS refusal produced the 1980 split and the founding of the BJP.

**The Bharatiya Janata Party founded 6 April 1980.** Atal Bihari Vajpayee and L.K. Advani founded the BJP on 6 April 1980 as successor to the Bharatiya Jana Sangh — operating with documented expanded ideological framework attempting to combine Hindutva with documented "Gandhian socialism" positioning under Vajpayee's initial leadership. The architecture: BJP operated electorally through the 1980s with documented limited success (2 Lok Sabha seats in 1984 — the post-Indira-assassination Congress sweep); BJP pivot toward documented sharper Hindutva positioning under L.K. Advani's leadership from 1986; BJP involvement in the Ayodhya Ram temple campaign producing the 1989-1992 electoral expansion (85 seats 1989, 120 seats 1991, 161 seats 1996).

**The Ram Rath Yatra September-October 1990 and the 6 December 1992 demolition of Babri Masjid.** L.K. Advani's *Ram Rath Yatra* (Ram Chariot Journey) from 25 September 1990 to 30 October 1990 — a mobilisation tour from Somnath in Gujarat to Ayodhya in Uttar Pradesh, designed to mobilise Hindu nationalist sentiment around the structural demand for a Ram temple at the site of the Babri Masjid (a 16th-century mosque in Ayodhya). The Babri Masjid had been claimed by Hindu nationalist organisations as the site of the birth of Lord Ram and as the site of a previously-existing Hindu temple destroyed by the Mughal emperor Babur in 1528. On 6 December 1992, an estimated 150,000+ Hindu nationalist *kar sevaks* (volunteer workers) — coordinated by RSS, BJP, VHP, and Bajrang Dal institutional leadership — demolished the Babri Masjid in Ayodhya; the demolition produced subsequent inter-communal riots across India with documented 2,000+ killed including documented 900+ in Bombay/Mumbai riots December 1992 - January 1993. The 6 December 1992 demolition is the foundational moment of the contemporary Hindutva ascendance — and as the precursor to the post-2014 Modi-era consolidation dossiered in § III.

**The Vajpayee governments 1996-2004 and the 2002 Gujarat riots.** Atal Bihari Vajpayee served as Prime Minister three times: 13 days in May 1996; 13 months from March 1998; and a full term from October 1999 to May 2004 — operating as the first BJP Prime Minister and the principal precedent for the contemporary BJP government. The architecture: Vajpayee government conducted the May 1998 Pokhran-II nuclear tests; Kargil War with Pakistan May-July 1999; neoliberal economic reform; Gujarat riots February-March 2002 under then-Chief Minister Narendra Modi with documented 1,044 killed per Indian government figures (790 Muslims, 254 Hindus). The 2002 Gujarat riots operate as the moment from which Modi's contemporary political trajectory emerged — with post-2002 international response including the US State Department visa denial to Modi 2005-2014 under the International Religious Freedom Act provisions. The 2004-2014 period operated under Congress-led United Progressive Alliance (UPA) governments under Manmohan Singh (born 26 September 1932, Prime Minister 2004-2014) — producing the context within which the post-2014 BJP ascendance occurred.

The summary of § I: the Sangh Parivar configuration operates on a foundational architecture established by Savarkar's 1923 *Essentials of Hindutva*, Hedgewar's 27 September 1925 founding of the RSS in Nagpur, Golwalkar's mid-century consolidation. The post-Gandhi-assassination 1948 ban, the 1951 founding of the Bharatiya Jana Sangh, the 1980 founding of the BJP, the 1990 Ram Rath Yatra, the 6 December 1992 demolition of Babri Masjid, and the 1996-2004 Vajpayee governments. The configuration's foundational features: Hindutva ethno-cultural framework explicitly excluding Indian Muslims and Christians. Cadre-based RSS architecture operating through *shakha* daily gatherings; Sangh Parivar institutional network of affiliated organisations bound through ideological rather than formal organisational adhesives; BJP as electoral-political wing; capacity for mass-mobilisation as demonstrated in 1992 Babri Masjid demolition; connection between Sangh Parivar ideological framework and the 1948 Gandhi assassination by former RSS member Nathuram Godse.

---


### II. The Contemporary Sangh Parivar Institutional Architecture

The contemporary Sangh Parivar configuration operates through five principal nodes: (1) the RSS as parent organisation operating through the *shakha* network and the Sarsanghchalak institutional leadership. (2) the BJP as political-electoral wing operating through Indian parliamentary democracy. (3) the Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP) and Bajrang Dal as religious-cultural and youth-militant wings. (4) the 32+ component organisations of the Sangh Parivar covering education, labour, women, students, tribal peoples, and other sectors. (5) the penetration of Indian state institutions including education, history, culture, and security through Sangh-linked appointees. What follows covers each.

**The RSS as parent organisation.** The Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh is the parent body of the Sangh Parivar through a structure distinctive in modern political-institutional history. The architecture: no formal legal-organisational existence (the RSS has no registered membership, no electoral participation, no statutory standing); adherents are *swayamsevaks* (volunteers) rather than members; cohesion operates through ideological adhesion to Hindutva rather than through formal organisational hierarchy; the Sarsanghchalak (Supreme Leader) operates with documented lifetime tenure; the *shakha* (daily one-hour local gathering) is the foundational institutional unit. RSS *shakha* network expansion under the Modi era: 44,982 daily shakhas as of March 2014; 83,129 daily shakhas as of March 2025 — an 84% increase across the Modi decade per Diplomat / Hindutva Watch analysis September 2025. Weekly *milan* gatherings: 10,146 (March 2014) to 32,147 (March 2025). Monthly *mandali* gatherings: 7,387 (March 2014) to 12,091 (March 2025). The *shakha* network expansion operates as evidence of the reach of the RSS into Indian civil society under the Modi government — and as the principal infrastructure through which Hindutva adhesion is sustained at the local-community level across India.

**Mohan Bhagwat as Sarsanghchalak.** Mohan Madhukar Bhagwat (born 11 September 1950 Chandrapur Maharashtra) serves as the sixth Sarsanghchalak of the RSS from 21 March 2009. The architecture: Bhagwat's father Madhukar Rao Bhagwat was an RSS *pracharak* (full-time worker); Bhagwat himself joined the RSS as a *pracharak* in 1975 and served in multiple positions before his elevation to Sarsanghchalak in 2009; Bhagwat's positioning combines ideological commitment to Hindutva with documented public statements on Hindu-Muslim coexistence, on the historical critique of caste, and on the importance of *sangathan* (organisation) over *vyakti* (individual). The contemporary Modi-Bhagwat tension is dossiered in § IV. Bhagwat's position: the RSS centenary on 27 September 2025 — operating as the moment of the RSS at 100 years from its 1925 founding, with centenary programmes including the Squire Patton Boggs $330,000 lobbying contract for US Congressional engagement per TRT World December 2025 reporting.

**The Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) as political wing.** The BJP is the political-electoral wing of the Sangh Parivar — operating through the Indian parliamentary democracy framework while maintaining ideological adhesion to RSS-led Hindutva. The architecture: BJP national president J.P. Nadda (born 2 December 1960 Patna Bihar) — Nadda is a former RSS-ABVP activist; BJP structure operating through state-level units (the BJP state presidents in each Indian state); BJP integration with Sangh Parivar through the appointment of RSS *pracharaks* to key BJP positions; BJP fundraising through the Electoral Bonds system (the February 2024 Supreme Court ruling holding the Electoral Bonds scheme unconstitutional — operating as evidence of the opacity of BJP fundraising during the 2014-2024 decade). Documented BJP institutional leadership figures under the Modi government: Narendra Modi (Prime Minister); Amit Shah (Union Home Minister, born 22 October 1964 Mumbai — Shah was previously BJP national president 2014-2020 and is the principal architect of post-2014 BJP electoral strategy); Rajnath Singh (Defence Minister, former BJP president); Nitin Gadkari (Road Transport Minister); J.P. Nadda (BJP national president); Yogi Adityanath (CM Uttar Pradesh); Devendra Fadnavis (CM Maharashtra from December 2024); Shivraj Singh Chouhan (former CM Madhya Pradesh).

**The Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP).** The VHP — World Hindu Council — was founded 29 August 1964 by S.S. Apte and Swami Chinmayananda with institutional support from M.S. Golwalkar (then RSS Sarsanghchalak) — is the religious-cultural wing of the Sangh Parivar. The architecture: VHP position on Hindu religious-cultural revival; VHP involvement in the Ayodhya Ram temple campaign 1984-1992 producing the 6 December 1992 Babri Masjid demolition; VHP positioning on Hindu-Muslim communal questions including anti-conversion advocacy and documented "*ghar wapsi*" (homecoming) Hindu reconversion programmes; VHP structure operating through state and district-level units across India and the Hindu diaspora; VHP institutional relationship with Hindu monastic figures (*sadhus*, *sants*, and *mahants*) including VHP institutional coordination through the *Dharma Sansad* (Religious Parliament) institutional gatherings.

**The Bajrang Dal.** The Bajrang Dal — VHP youth-militant wing founded 8 October 1984 — is the vehicle of Hindu nationalist youth mobilisation. The architecture: Bajrang Dal involvement in the 1992 Babri Masjid demolition; Bajrang Dal involvement in documented "*ghar wapsi*" programmes; Bajrang Dal involvement in documented "*love jihad*" campaigns (the Hindutva framing of inter-religious marriages — particularly Hindu women marrying Muslim men — as institutional Muslim conspiracy against Hindu demographic interests); Bajrang Dal involvement in cow-protection vigilantism producing multiple lynching incidents 2014-2026. The Bajrang Dal is the principal contemporary vehicle of street-level Hindutva militancy.

**The Akhil Bharatiya Vidyarthi Parishad (ABVP).** The ABVP — student wing founded 9 July 1948 — is the vehicle of Hindutva on Indian university and college campuses. The architecture: ABVP presence on most major Indian university campuses; ABVP involvement in campus political contestation including confrontations with leftist and minority student organisations; ABVP pipeline into BJP electoral politics (multiple BJP politicians including Arun Jaitley 1952-2019 were former ABVP activists). The ABVP is the principal contemporary vehicle of Hindutva student-political mobilisation.

**The Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS).** The BMS — trade union wing founded 23 July 1955 — is the vehicle of Hindutva trade-union politics. The architecture: BMS membership of around 17 million as of 2024 per BMS figures (the largest Indian trade union federation); BMS positioning on labour issues distinct from the leftist trade-union federations (AITUC, CITU, INTUC); BMS priority on Hindu nationalist economic-cultural framework over class-based labour mobilisation.

**The Bharatiya Kisan Sangh (BKS) and Vanvasi Kalyan Ashram.** The BKS — farmers' wing founded 4 March 1979 — is the vehicle of Hindutva agricultural-political mobilisation. The Vanvasi Kalyan Ashram — tribal-peoples wing founded 26 December 1952 — is the vehicle of Hindutva mobilisation among India's *adivasi* (tribal) populations including emphasis on resisting Christian missionary conversion. Documented Vanvasi Kalyan Ashram presence in over 17,000 villages across tribal-majority districts including presence in Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Odisha, Madhya Pradesh, and Northeast India.

**The Vidya Bharati educational network.** The Vidya Bharati — Sangh Parivar educational wing founded 1977 — operates thousands of schools across India under the umbrella of *Saraswati Shishu Mandir* (Saraswati Children's Temple) and similar structures. The architecture: Vidya Bharati operates over 12,000 schools with over 3 million students per Vidya Bharati figures; curriculum combining standard educational content with Hindutva instruction including *Vande Mataram* and *Saraswati Vandana* recitation, Hindu mythology and Hindu nationalist historiography, and physical training including yoga and martial arts. The Vidya Bharati is the principal contemporary vehicle of Hindutva transmission through the Indian educational system.

**The Sewa Bharati social-service network.** The Sewa Bharati — Sangh Parivar social-service wing founded 1979 — is the vehicle of Hindu nationalist social work in slums, tribal areas, and disaster-affected regions. The architecture: Sewa Bharati positioning of social work as means of furthering Hindutva adhesion per Diplomat analysis September 2025; Sewa Bharati presence as alternative to Christian missionary social-service networks; Sewa Bharati involvement in post-disaster relief efforts (post-2001 Gujarat earthquake, post-2004 tsunami, post-2014 Kashmir floods, post-2020 COVID response).

**The 32+ Sangh Parivar component organisations and the Samanvay Baithak.** Per Diplomat / Hindutva Watch September 2025 analysis: the Sangh Parivar comprises over 32 component organisations covering sectors including political, religious-cultural, educational, labour, women, students, tribal peoples, farmers, history, science, social work, culture, border security, and sports; annual gathering of national presidents and national organising secretaries at the *Samanvay Baithak* (Coordination Meeting); institutional coordination through ideological adhesion rather than formal organisational hierarchy producing capacity for synchronised institutional action across the Sangh Parivar network. Documented examples of synchronised institutional action: the 1990-1992 Ayodhya campaign; the post-2014 implementation of the National Education Policy with involvement of five Sangh Parivar organisations per Diplomat analysis; the post-2019 Citizenship Amendment Act mobilisation; the January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration coordination.

**The institutional penetration of Indian state institutions.** The architecture: post-2014 appointment of Sangh Parivar-linked figures to key positions in Indian state institutions including appointments to the Indian Council of Historical Research, the National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT), the University Grants Commission (UGC), the Censor Board of Film Certification, the Sahitya Akademi (National Academy of Letters), and universities including Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU), Delhi University, and Banaras Hindu University; consequences including revisions to school history textbooks reducing Mughal-period and Muslim-period content while expanding Hindu-historical content; renaming of Mughal-era institutions and city names (Allahabad to Prayagraj 2018; Mughalsarai railway station to Deen Dayal Upadhyay Junction 2018; multiple road and renamings); appointment of Sangh-linked figures to state-level positions including Yogi Adityanath as CM Uttar Pradesh from 19 March 2017.

The summary of § II: the contemporary Sangh Parivar architecture operates through the RSS as parent body, the BJP as political wing, the VHP and Bajrang Dal as religious-cultural and youth-militant wings, and the 32+ component organisations covering Indian civil society sectors. The configuration operates through cohesion via ideological adhesion to Hindutva rather than formal organisational hierarchy — producing capacity for synchronised mass-action while maintaining opacity of the RSS itself (no formal membership, no electoral participation, no statutory standing). The post-2014 Modi government period dossiered in § III is the moment of the Sangh Parivar's ascendance into Indian state institutions.

---


### III. The Narendra Modi Government 2014-2026: Hindutva Institutional Ascendance

The Narendra Modi government period from 26 May 2014 is the contemporary moment of the Sangh Parivar's ascendance into Indian state institutions. The architecture has seven principal arcs: (1) Modi's biographical institutional trajectory from RSS *pracharak* to Gujarat Chief Minister to Prime Minister. (2) the 2014 and 2019 general election victories establishing the Modi-era configuration. (3) the August 2019 abrogation of Article 370 ending the special status of Jammu and Kashmir. (4) the December 2019 Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) and the consequences. (5) the January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration at Ayodhya. (6) the 2024 general election producing the BJP loss of outright majority and coalition government formation. (7) the late 2024-2025 BJP state election recovery in Haryana and Maharashtra. The evidence covers each.

**Narendra Damodardas Modi.** Born 17 September 1950 Vadnagar Gujarat — is the principal institutional figure of contemporary Hindutva politics. The architecture: Modi joined the RSS as a *balswayamsevak* (child volunteer) in 1958; Modi institutional trajectory as RSS *pracharak* (full-time worker) from 1971; Modi involvement in the 1975-1977 Emergency-era underground operations against Indira Gandhi's government; Modi positioning within Gujarat BJP from 1985; Modi Gujarat Chief Minister from 7 October 2001 to 22 May 2014 — through three Gujarat state elections (2002, 2007, 2012); Gujarat 2002 riots February-March 2002 under Modi's CM tenure with documented 1,044 killed per Indian government figures (790 Muslims, 254 Hindus); the US State Department visa denial to Modi 2005-2014 under the International Religious Freedom Act provisions; Indian Supreme Court Special Investigation Team (SIT) 2012 ruling against prosecution of Modi for the 2002 riots; Modi BJP Prime Ministerial candidate from September 2013; Modi election as Prime Minister from 26 May 2014.

**The 2014 Indian general election.** Held 7 April – 12 May 2014 in nine phases; BJP won 282 seats (out of 543 Lok Sabha) producing the first single-party majority since 1984; NDA coalition won 336 seats; the Congress collapse to 44 seats (its worst electoral performance); Modi sworn in 26 May 2014. The architecture: BJP campaign focused on economic development promises ("*Sabka Saath, Sabka Vikas*" — "Together with all, development for all"), anti-corruption framing against the Congress-led UPA government, and Hindutva mobilisation; Sangh Parivar involvement in the BJP campaign including RSS shakha network mobilisation and Sangh-affiliated organisation electoral campaigning. The 2014 election is the moment from which the contemporary Hindutva ascendance began.

**The 2019 Indian general election.** Held 11 April – 19 May 2019 in seven phases; BJP won 303 seats (an increase from the 282 of 2014); NDA coalition won 353 seats; Congress recovery to 52 seats; Modi sworn in for second term 30 May 2019. The architecture: BJP campaign focused on national security framing (the February 2019 Pulwama attack killing 40 Indian paramilitary personnel and the subsequent Balakot air strike); Hindutva mobilisation including documented "*Modi Hai To Mumkin Hai*" ("If Modi, then possible") slogan; BJP consolidation across the Hindi heartland and BJP expansion in eastern states (West Bengal, Odisha) and the Northeast. The 2019 election is the moment of consolidated post-2014 Hindutva electoral hegemony.

**The 5 August 2019 abrogation of Article 370.** The architecture: on 5 August 2019, the Modi government — through documented Presidential order under Article 370(1)(d) and Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act 2019 — abrogated Article 370 of the Indian Constitution and Article 35A; consequence: the special autonomy of Jammu and Kashmir (which had operated since 1949 under institutional terms allowing J&K its own constitution, flag, and limited internal autonomy) was ended; documented reorganisation of J&K into two Union Territories — Jammu and Kashmir (with legislature) and Ladakh (without legislature); context: BJP-led Hindu nationalist movement had documentedly opposed Article 370 since the Bharatiya Jana Sangh agitation of 1952-1953 under Shyama Prasad Mookerjee. The consequences of the August 2019 abrogation: communications blackout in Kashmir for over 7 months; documented arrest of thousands of Kashmiri political figures including documented arrest of former CMs Mehbooba Mufti and Omar Abdullah; sustained Indian military presence; integration of Kashmir into Indian Union Territory governance; changes including documented end of Kashmir-specific land laws restricting non-Kashmiri purchase of Kashmir land (operating as opening to demographic change in Muslim-majority Kashmir Valley).

**The 11 December 2019 Citizenship (Amendment) Act 2019.** The architecture: passed by Parliament 11 December 2019; provisions: expedited Indian citizenship for religious minorities — Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, and Christians — fleeing religious persecution from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan; exclusion of Muslims from the expedited citizenship provisions. The consequences: widespread protests across India December 2019 – March 2020 including protests at Shaheen Bagh Delhi (sit-in by Muslim women from 15 December 2019 to 24 March 2020 — the longest continuous protest in modern Indian history); protests at Jamia Millia Islamia and Aligarh Muslim University; violent police response at Jamia Millia Islamia on 15 December 2019; Delhi riots February 2020 with over 50 killed (majority Muslim) and response framing the riots as Hindu-Muslim communal violence; documented international concern from the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, the US Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF), and documented others. The CAA is the contemporary moment from which Indian secular constitutional framework — which had operated since 1950 on the principle of non-religious criteria for Indian citizenship — was  amended to introduce religious criteria for the first time.

**The Ram Mandir consecration 22 January 2024.** The architecture: on 22 January 2024, the Ram Mandir (Ram Temple) was consecrated at the Ayodhya site of the demolished Babri Masjid through documented *pran pratishtha* (consecration ceremony) performed by Prime Minister Modi with RSS Sarsanghchalak Mohan Bhagwat as principal religious-institutional officiant; context: the 9 November 2019 Indian Supreme Court ruling in the *Ayodhya Title Suit* awarded the disputed site to Hindu petitioners with documented alternative land for the Muslim community; construction of the Ram Mandir from 2020-2024 at documented estimated cost of around 18 billion rupees (around $216 million USD) per Ram Janmabhoomi Teerth Kshetra Trust figures; Modi's positioning of the consecration as the fulfilment of the Hindu nationalist demand from the 1990 Ram Rath Yatra onward. The Ram Mandir consecration is the symbolic peak moment of contemporary Hindutva — and as the precursor to the 2024 general election dossiered next.

**The 2024 Indian general election.** Held 19 April – 1 June 2024 in seven phases; BJP won 240 seats (down from 303 in 2019); NDA coalition won 293 seats; Congress-led INDIA opposition alliance won 234 seats (Congress 99, INDIA total 234); BJP lost outright majority requiring coalition partners — particularly Telugu Desam Party under N. Chandrababu Naidu (TDP, 16 seats) and Janata Dal (United) under Nitish Kumar (JD(U), 12 seats); Modi sworn in for third term 9 June 2024. The architecture: BJP campaign focused on the "*Modi Ki Guarantee*" ("Modi's Guarantee") and "*Abki Baar 400 Paar*" ("This Time 400+ Seats") slogans; Sangh Parivar involvement in the campaign including but less-enthusiastic RSS engagement per analyst reporting; BJP setbacks: Uttar Pradesh (37 seats down from 62 in 2019); Maharashtra; Faizabad (the symbolic loss of the Ayodhya Ram Mandir constituency); Tamil Nadu (no gains); first BJP decision since 1996 to not field candidates in Kashmir Valley. The 2024 election is the moment of the post-2014 Modi configuration's first documented major electoral setback — and as the moment from which the post-2024 RSS-Modi tension dossiered in § IV emerged.

**The late 2024 BJP state election recovery.** The architecture: October 2024 Haryana state election — BJP victory securing a third consecutive term in Haryana with 48 of 90 seats; November 2024 Maharashtra state election — the Mahayuti coalition (BJP + Eknath Shinde Shiv Sena + Ajit Pawar NCP) victory with 230 of 288 seats — documented largest single-state institutional victory in BJP history; Devendra Fadnavis sworn in as Maharashtra CM December 2024. The architecture operates with consequences: RSS interpretation of the Haryana and Maharashtra victories as victories of *Sangathan* (organisation) and *Hindutva vichardhara* (Hindutva thinking) rather than as Modi-personality victories per Federal December 2024 analysis; RSS position that the post-2024 state-election victories demonstrate that Hindutva electoral support has documentedly crossed the threshold level at which dependency on Modi's individual personality becomes unnecessary. The late 2024 BJP state election recovery is evidence of the post-2024 realignment between Modi and the RSS dossiered in § IV.

**The contemporary post-2024 configuration.** As of late 2026, the Modi government operates as: (1) Indian Prime Minister in third term from 9 June 2024 with coalition government structure (BJP 240, NDA 293, requiring TDP + JD(U) coalition partners); (2) documented continued control of Indian state apparatus through BJP dominance at Centre and in BJP-ruled states (Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Maharashtra, Haryana, Uttarakhand, Assam, others); (3) operating with post-2024 consolidation following the late 2024 state-election victories; (4) operating in documented ongoing tension with the RSS leadership around the *vyakti*/*sangathan* question dossiered in § IV; (5) operating with Hindutva ascendance across Indian state institutions including education, history, culture, and security per Diplomat / Hindutva Watch September 2025 analysis; (6) operating against opposition including Congress-INDIA alliance opposition, opposition state governments (Tamil Nadu DMK, Karnataka Congress, Kerala CPI(M)-led LDF, West Bengal TMC, Punjab AAP), and opposition civil-society institutions.

The summary of § III: the Modi government period from 26 May 2014 is the moment of post-2014 Hindutva ascendance into Indian state institutions. The configuration delivered: the 2014 + 2019 BJP single-party majority victories; the 5 August 2019 abrogation of Article 370; December 2019 CAA producing documented protests and Delhi riots February 2020; the 22 January 2024 Ram Mandir consecration; the 2024 general election setback producing coalition government formation; the late 2024 state-election recovery in Haryana and Maharashtra. The configuration operates with contradictions including secular constitutional framework versus Hindutva institutional programme; democratic-electoral institutional framework versus concentration of executive power; federal institutional framework versus Modi-era central consolidation. The contemporary tensions dossiered in § IV operate within this post-2014 configuration.

---


### IV. Documented Institutional Tensions: Manipur, Communal Incidents, and the RSS-Modi Realignment

The 2023-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of tension within the Sangh Parivar configuration and between the configuration and the Indian secular-democratic institutional framework. The architecture has five principal arcs: (1) the Manipur ethnic violence from May 2023 as the crisis around Meitei-Kuki-Zo communal violence. (2) the communal incidents 2014-2026 including cow-vigilante lynchings. Anti-conversion law expansion, and inter-communal riots. (3) the tension between Modi and the RSS leadership around the *vyakti*/*sangathan* question 2024-2026. (4) the Indian contradictions between Hindutva ascendance and the constitutional secular framework. (5) the contemporary configuration as captured-state pattern in the Hindu register parallel to the configurations dossiered in Chapters 15-21.

**The Manipur ethnic violence from 3 May 2023.** The architecture: ethnic violence erupted on 3 May 2023 between the Meitei community (the Hindu valley population, around 53% of Manipur state population, around 1.7 million people) and the Kuki-Zo community (the Christian hill population, around 16% of Manipur state population, around 500,000 people) over the Manipur High Court 27 March 2023 order recommending Scheduled Tribe status for Meiteis — which the Kuki-Zo community opposed as documented threat to their constitutional Scheduled Tribe protections; documented violence escalated rapidly with church burnings, village destruction, mass displacement, and inter-community killings; over 200 killed and around 60,000 displaced by mid-2024 per Indian government figures; continuing low-grade violence through 2025-2026; complete geographic separation of the two communities within a month of the onset of violence with the Meiteis confined to the Imphal valley and the Kuki-Zo to the hill districts. The consequences: Modi government delay in addressing the crisis at the highest institutional level — Modi public silence for over 70 days following the 3 May 2023 outbreak; opposition criticism including Indian Parliament no-confidence motion July 2023 under Congress leadership; Modi first public statement on the violence on 20 July 2023 (only after viral video of two Kuki-Zo women being paraded naked); Modi first official visit to Manipur not occurring until September 2025 — over two years after the violence began; the BJP-led Manipur government under CM N. Biren Singh (CM from 15 March 2017) operating with accusations of Meitei partisanship; the February 2025 imposition of President's Rule in Manipur following Biren Singh resignation. The Manipur violence is evidence of the fragility of the Hindutva-state configuration when confronting inter-communal violence in tribal-majority regions — and as documented illustration of the pattern of Modi government delay in addressing communal crises that complicate Hindutva electoral framing.

**The documented communal incidents 2014-2026.** The architecture: "cow vigilante" lynching incidents from 2014 onward — Mohammad Akhlaq lynching at Dadri Uttar Pradesh 28 September 2015; Pehlu Khan lynching at Alwar Rajasthan 1 April 2017; Junaid Khan lynching on Mathura-bound train 22 June 2017; Akbar Khan lynching at Alwar Rajasthan 21 July 2018; multiple additional cow-vigilante lynchings 2014-2026 per Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International reports; anti-conversion laws — multiple BJP-ruled state laws restricting religious conversion under the "love jihad" framing (Uttar Pradesh 2020, Madhya Pradesh 2021, Karnataka 2022, Haryana 2022, Uttarakhand 2022, Gujarat 2021, Himachal Pradesh, Rajasthan, others); the February 2024 Haldwani Uttarakhand riots following demolition of a madrasa and adjacent mosque — six killed; the April-May 2023 Hubballi Karnataka communal incidents; the April 2022 Khargone Madhya Pradesh communal riots; the 2020 Delhi riots February 2020 producing over 50 killed during the CAA protests dossiered in § III. The architecture operates with patterns: institutional police laxity in prosecuting Hindutva-affiliated violence per Human Rights Watch reports; institutional BJP-led government accommodation of cow-vigilante and Bajrang Dal-affiliated networks; contrast with prosecutorial vigour against Muslim protest activity (the post-CAA arrests of student activists including Umar Khalid arrested 14 September 2020 under UAPA and held without bail).

**The Modi-Bhagwat tension 2024-2026.** The architecture: post-2024 tension between Modi and RSS Sarsanghchalak Mohan Bhagwat around the post-2024 electoral setback and the *vyakti*/*sangathan* question. The architecture per Federal December 2024 analysis: RSS lack of enthusiastic 2024 general election campaigning per analyst reporting — RSS interpretation of Modi's "*Modi Ki Guarantee*" and "*Abki Baar 400 Paar*" slogans as documented embodiment of conceit and personality cult conflict with the Sangh principle of *vyakti pujan* (individual idolisation) being subordinate to *sangathan* (organisation); Bhagwat public statements June-September 2024 critiquing personality cult dynamics without naming Modi explicitly; question of Modi's position after the 2024 setback — including questions about RSS retirement-age conventions (Modi will turn 75 on 17 September 2025; BJP convention has historically operated with the 75-year retirement age that was applied to L.K. Advani and Murli Manohar Joshi 2014); RSS endorsement of Yogi Adityanath at the Mathura national executive October 2024 and RSS endorsement of Devendra Fadnavis as Maharashtra CM choice November 2024 — operating as signals of RSS post-Modi succession considerations; Bhagwat 100-years RSS centenary speech 27 September 2025 — operating as moment of RSS centenary commemoration with implications for Modi-RSS post-Modi configuration. The Modi-Bhagwat tension is evidence of the structure of the Sangh Parivar — in which the RSS as parent organisation outranks the BJP as electoral wing, and in which Modi-era consolidation through Modi's personality cult operates against foundational Sangh principles of *sangathan* over *vyakti*.

**Yogi Adityanath and Devendra Fadnavis as successor figures.** The architecture: Yogi Adityanath — born 5 June 1972 Panchur Uttarakhand as Ajay Mohan Bisht; Hindu monk; Mahant of Gorakhnath Math from 12 September 2014 (succeeding his guru Mahant Avaidyanath who died 12 September 2014); documented MP for Gorakhpur 1998-2017; documented Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh from 19 March 2017; Hindutva hardline positioning; institutional RSS endorsement at the Mathura national executive October 2024. Devendra Fadnavis — born 22 July 1970 Nagpur Maharashtra (the RSS headquarters city); RSS *swayamsevak* from documented childhood; Maharashtra Chief Minister 2014-2019 and from December 2024; institutional RSS-preferred figure. The two figures operate as contemporary candidates for post-Modi BJP leadership succession — operating with institutional RSS backing distinct from the Modi-era personality-cult dynamic.

**Documented contradictions of the Hindutva-state configuration.** The configuration generates contradictions parallel to those dossiered for the preceding contemporary apparatus chapters: (1) the Indian Constitution's secular framework (Article 25-28 on religious freedom; Articles 14-15 on equality; the Preamble's "Secular" declaration added by the 42nd Amendment 1976) operates against the Hindutva institutional programme of ethno-religious-cultural redefinition of Indian national identity; (2) the Indian democratic-electoral framework operates against concentration of executive power through Modi-era centralisation; (3) the Indian federal framework operates against Modi-era centralisation including opposition-state governments operating with institutional constraint by central government institutions (the Enforcement Directorate, the Central Bureau of Investigation, the Income Tax Department, the Election Commission of India); (4) the Indian constitutional protection of religious minorities operates against Hindutva institutional programme of demographic-cultural reframing including CAA, anti-conversion laws, the "love jihad" framing, and cow-vigilante accommodation; (5) the Indian pluralism operates against Sangh Parivar institutional penetration of the state institutions per Diplomat September 2025 analysis.

**The captured-state pattern in the Hindu register.** The Sangh Parivar configuration is the principal Hindu captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period. The configuration operates through a seventh distinct structural pattern parallel to the preceding contemporary apparatus configurations: (1) operating through a network of ostensibly-independent civil-society organisations rather than through a single juridical-territorial sovereign state structure; (2) operating with ideological adhesion to Hindutva as the principal cohesion mechanism; (3) operating through Indian parliamentary democracy framework as the electoral-political vehicle; (4) operating with ascendance under the Modi government 2014-2026 producing integration with the state institutions; (5) operating against Indian Muslim (around 14% of population, around 200 million people), Indian Christian (around 2.3% of population), Indian Sikh (around 1.7%), and Indian Dalit and Adivasi populations; (6) operating against opposition Indian secular institutional framework and opposition political parties.

**The contemporary 2026 configuration.** As of late 2026, the Sangh Parivar configuration operates as: (1) the principal Hindu captured-state apparatus of the contemporary period; (2) operating through the BJP Modi government in third term coalition with TDP and JD(U); (3) operating through the RSS *shakha* network of 83,129 daily gatherings as of March 2025; (4) operating through the Sangh Parivar network of 32+ component organisations across Indian civil society; (5) operating with tension between Modi and RSS leadership around the *vyakti*/*sangathan* question; (6) operating with continuing Manipur crisis and continuing communal incidents; (7) operating with contemporary Hindutva ascendance through Sangh-linked appointments to the state institutions including education, history, culture, and security; (8) operating against opposition INDIA alliance, opposition state governments, and opposition civil-society institutions including documented academic, journalistic, and judicial resistance to Hindutva ascendance.

The summary of § IV: the 2023-2026 period is the principal contemporary moment of tension within the Sangh Parivar configuration and between the configuration and the Indian secular-democratic framework. The configuration produces contradictions including the constitutional secular framework versus Hindutva institutional programme; democratic-electoral framework versus centralisation; the federal framework versus Modi-era centralisation; the constitutional minority protection versus Hindutva demographic-cultural reframing; pluralism versus Sangh institutional penetration. The section closes here. Chapter 23 dossiers the Buddhist captured configurations including documented Sri Lankan Sinhala Buddhist nationalist apparatus, documented Myanmar Buddhist nationalist apparatus including post-2017 Rohingya persecution, documented Thai Buddhist establishment configuration, and documented Tibetan Buddhist configuration in exile — operating in cross-reference with Chapter 9 (Buddhist foundational dossier).

---


## Chapter 23. Buddhist Captured Configurations

Chapter 23 returns to the dossier sequence deferred from after Chapter 22. The documentation covers the principal Buddhist captured configurations — patterns in which Buddhist religious authority has been instrumentalised by state or political-movement actors, in which Buddhist authority has produced legitimating discourse for state operations including violence against religious minorities, and in which the sangha (the Buddhist monastic community) has been subordinated to political-state authority.
