The dossier operates with the same investigative-journalism standards applied across Part III and applies them symmetrically to Buddhist configurations alongside the Christian, Muslim, Hindu, and Jewish configurations dossiered in preceding chapters.

The record covers four sections: § I Myanmar — the 969 movement (from c. 2012), Ma Ba Tha (formed 2014), the 2015 "Protection of Race and Religion" laws, the Rohingya genocide proceedings (ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar 2019), and the Tatmadaw alignment of Buddhist nationalism with military rule following the 1 February 2021 coup; § II Sri Lanka. The Bodu Bala Sena (founded 2012), the Mahanayaka hierarchy, the 2014 Aluthgama riots, the 2018 Kandy clashes, the post-2019-Easter-bombings configuration, the Gotabaya Rajapaksa presidency 2019-2022, and the post-Aragalaya 2022-2026 reconfiguration; § III Thailand. The Sangha Act 1962, the December 2016 and July 2018 amendments restoring royal prerogative, the Vajiralongkorn monarchical capture of the sangha post-2016 succession, the November 2020 Supreme Sangha Council prohibition of monk protest participation, and the broader pattern of sangha capture by military-monarchical state; § IV China-controlled Tibetan Buddhism. The rupture between Chinese state and Tibetan Government in Exile, the 11th Panchen Lama controversy (1995 rupture), the 2007 State Religious Affairs Bureau Order No. 5 claiming Chinese authority over reincarnation, the Dalai Lama July 2025 succession statement, and the ongoing contestation around the Dalai Lama succession question.

The evidence operates against any framing that would treat Buddhism as distinct from other religious traditions in its susceptibility to political capture and instrumentalisation. The pattern of Buddhist capture observed across Myanmar, Sri Lanka, Thailand, and China-controlled Tibet is structurally analogous to the patterns of Christian, Muslim, Hindu, and Jewish capture dossiered in preceding chapters. Operating through state-sangha alignment, through political-movement instrumentalisation of monastic authority, through sangha-issued legitimating discourse for state violence, and through institutional subordination of sangha to political authority. What follows covers these patterns with the same evidentiary standards applied across the volume.

### I. Myanmar: the 969 Movement, Ma Ba Tha, and the Tatmadaw Alignment

The Myanmar Buddhist nationalist configuration is the principal contemporary illustration of monk-led Buddhist political movement producing legitimating discourse for state operations against a religious minority — and producing parliamentary legislation restricting religious freedom of that minority. The architecture has five principal nodes: (1) the 969 movement led by Wirathu and other monks from c. 2012. (2) the Ma Ba Tha (Association for the Protection of Race and Religion) formed January 2014 as successor. (3) the 2015 "Protection of Race and Religion" laws passed by USDP-controlled parliament. (4) the 2016-2017 Tatmadaw "clearance operations" against Rohingya producing exodus of over 700,000 to Bangladesh and the resulting ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar case; (5) the post-2021 coup Tatmadaw realignment with Buddhist nationalist figures including Wirathu rehabilitation.

**The 969 movement.** Emerged c. 2012 in Mandalay as monk-led Buddhist nationalist campaign. The architecture: three digits "969" said to symbolise the virtues of the Buddha, the Dhamma (Buddhist teachings), and the Sangha (monastic community) per Justice For Myanmar analysis; movement led by Ashin Wirathu (born 10 July 1968 Kyaukse) and Ashin Wimala; campaign articulating Muslim demographic-economic threat to Buddhist Myanmar through boycott of Muslim-owned businesses, opposition to Buddhist-Muslim inter-religious marriage, and articulation of conspiracy framings of Muslim population expansion per Tandfonline 2019 analysis. Wirathu trajectory: educated at Ma Soe Yein monastery Mandalay from 1991; Dhamma lecturer from 1999; 2003 sentenced to 25 years imprisonment for inciting deadly violence in Kyaukse through distribution of inflammatory anti-Muslim pamphlets; freed 2011 under President Thein Sein amnesty per South Asia Citizens Web analysis. Wirathu Time magazine cover "The Face of Buddhist Terror" July 2013. The 969 movement banned by State Sangha Maha Nayaka Committee (Sangha Council, the government-appointed body of high-ranking monks overseeing Buddhist clergy) late 2013 per South Asia Citizens Web — the official ban citing the movement's unauthorised positioning rather than its anti-Muslim incitement content.

**Ma Ba Tha (Association for the Protection of Race and Religion).** Formed as successor to the banned 969 movement. The architecture: official Burmese acronym Ma Ba Tha (full name Patriotic Association of Myanmar, PAM); upper Myanmar branch established Mandalay 15 January 2014 per Wikipedia Patriotic Association of Myanmar; chairman Ywama Sayadaw Ashin Tilokabhivamsa; vice chairman Ashin Wirathu; headquartered Insein Township, Yangon Region. Founding monks publicly stated the organisation was intended both to support 969 ideology and to rein in outspoken "younger monks" including Wirathu prompting domestic and international criticism per South Asia Citizens Web analysis — the structural rebranding operating as response to Sangha Council ban while preserving the movement content. Ma Ba Tha officially dissolved July 2017 by Sangha Council ruling; rebranded as Buddha Dhamma Parahita Foundation 2017 (later also banned); the movement is still popularly referred to as Ma Ba Tha per Justice For Myanmar.

**The 2015 Protection of Race and Religion laws.** Ma Ba Tha drafted and campaigned for four laws passed by Myanmar parliament 2015 under USDP (Union Solidarity and Development Party, military-proxy party) control. The architecture: Religious Conversion Law (Law 8/2015, May 2015) regulating religious conversion through state-administrative procedure; Buddhist Women's Special Marriage Law (Law 50/2015, August 2015) regulating marriage between Buddhist women and men of other religions; Population Control Healthcare Law (Law 28/2015, May 2015) authorising regional birth-spacing measures; Monogamy Law (Law 54/2015, August 2015) criminalising polygamous arrangements. Per Justice For Myanmar: "the laws authorised state agencies to regulate religious conversion, birth control and marriage between Buddhists and religious minorities" — operating as legislative consolidation of Ma Ba Tha's anti-Muslim platform.

**Mytel and the 969 dog-whistle.** Per Justice For Myanmar: Mytel (Myanmar's fourth telecommunications operator, co-owned by the Myanmar military) was allocated the "969" number for its national roll-out in 2018 — a decision within Myanmar's numbering plan managed by the communications ministry. The pattern: at a time when "969" was commonly used to denote anti-Muslim hate, the numbering allocation was widely perceived as a dog-whistle showing Mytel support for Ma Ba Tha's anti-Muslim rhetoric. The pattern operates as illustration of military-Ma Ba Tha alignment running through corporate and bureaucratic channels rather than only through movement rhetoric.

**The Rohingya crisis and ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar.** The architecture: 2012 Rakhine State riots between Buddhist Rakhine and Muslim Rohingya populations producing first major displacement waves; 2016-2017 Tatmadaw "clearance operations" in northern Rakhine State producing exodus of over 700,000 Rohingya to Bangladesh by late 2017 per UN OCHA reporting; UN Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar reports September 2018 and August 2019 finding Tatmadaw operations against Rohingya constituted crimes against humanity and acts of genocide; ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar — application instituting proceedings filed 11 November 2019 by Gambia alleging Myanmar violations of Genocide Convention; ICJ provisional measures order 23 January 2020 requiring Myanmar to prevent genocidal acts, prevent destruction of evidence, and report on measures taken; ICJ jurisdiction confirmed 22 July 2022 over Myanmar's preliminary objections; case continuing 2023-2026. The consequence: Ma Ba Tha-articulated anti-Muslim discourse provided ideological framework within which Tatmadaw operations were conducted — producing convergence between sangha-articulated discourse and state-military operations against religious minority population.

**Tatmadaw 1 February 2021 coup and Buddhist nationalist realignment.** The architecture: Tatmadaw coup 1 February 2021 against Aung San Suu Kyi government following November 2020 election; State Administration Council under Senior General Min Aung Hlaing; civil war continuing through 2026 with operations against People's Defence Force (PDF), ethnic armed organisations including Karen National Union, Kachin Independence Organisation, Arakan Army, and Three Brotherhood Alliance. The Buddhist nationalist realignment: Min Aung Hlaing post-2021 patronage of Buddhist nationalist figures producing rehabilitation of Wirathu (released from prison September 2022 after his 2021 arrest on sedition charges per LSE South Asia analysis December 2023); Min Aung Hlaing positioning as "protector of Buddhism" per LSE analysis; monk-led militias supporting Tatmadaw operations including Pyu Saw Htee militia 2021-present. The pattern: post-2021 Tatmadaw operations exhibit alignment with Buddhist nationalist authority similar to the pre-2021 Ma Ba Tha-USDP alignment — operating as continuity of state-sangha alignment across the democratic-transition rupture.

**The summary of § I.** The Myanmar Buddhist nationalist configuration operates through the 969 movement (from c. 2012), Ma Ba Tha (from January 2014), the 2015 Protection of Race and Religion laws, the 2016-2017 Tatmadaw operations against Rohingya producing ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar (from 11 November 2019), and the post-2021 Tatmadaw-Buddhist nationalist realignment. The architecture is illustration of monk-led Buddhist political movement producing legitimating discourse for state violence against religious minority — operating through channels (Ma Ba Tha-drafted legislation, sangha-issued endorsements, monk-led mobilisation) producing structural consequence in state-military operations. The Sri Lankan configuration dossiered in § II operates as parallel illustration of monk-led Buddhist nationalist movement producing communal violence and state-aligned political action in different national configuration.

---


### II. Sri Lanka: Bodu Bala Sena, the Mahanayaka Hierarchy, and Sinhalese Buddhist Nationalism

The Sri Lankan Sinhalese Buddhist nationalist configuration is the principal contemporary Theravada Buddhist nationalist movement operating within a parliamentary-democratic state context. The architecture has five principal nodes: (1) the Bodu Bala Sena (BBS) founded 2012 as principal monk-led Sinhalese nationalist organisation; (2) the broader Mahanayaka senior-monk hierarchy operating across the three principal nikāyas (monastic fraternities). Siyam, Amarapura, Ramanna; (3) the 2014 Aluthgama riots and 2018 Kandy clashes as principal communal-violence episodes attributed to BBS-aligned mobilisation; (4) the post-2019-Easter-bombings Gotabaya Rajapaksa presidency 2019-2022 and the post-Aragalaya 2022-2026 reconfiguration; (5) the broader pattern of Sinhalese Buddhist nationalism cross-referenced from earlier Jathika Hela Urumaya (JHU, National Heritage Party, founded 2004 as world's first political party comprised of Buddhist monks).

**Bodu Bala Sena (BBS, "Buddhist Power Force").** Founded 2012 by monks including Galagoda Aththe Gnanasara. The architecture: first national convention 28 July 2012 at Bandaranaike Memorial International Conference Hall Colombo; headquartered at 32 Sri Sambuddhathva Jayanthi Mawatha, Colombo; general secretary Galagoda Aththe Gnanasara from 2012; chief executive Dilanthe Withanage; earlier patron Kirama Wimalajothi until May 2015 resignation following BBS involvement in 2014 Aluthgama riots per People's Rights Group analysis. BBS stated objectives include "establishing a Buddhist society, fostering brave and independent generations of bhikkhus, and transforming Buddhist temples into centers of social service and education" per Grokipedia analysis January 2026 — operating as broader Sinhalese Buddhist nationalist programme beyond movement rhetoric.

**Gnanasara Thera trajectory.** Born Galagodaaththa in southern Galle district. The architecture: alumnus of Sarananda Piriwena, Wathuravila Aranya Senasanaya, University of Kelaniya, University of Sri Jayewardenepura per Wikipedia; involvement in Buddhist activist groups in the 2000s including unsuccessful Jathika Hela Urumaya parliamentary candidacy; BBS general secretary from 2012; arrests and convictions over the post-2012 period including June 2018 conviction for criminal intimidation; May 2019 presidential pardon by Maithripala Sirisena per multiple reports. Gnanasara November 2021 appointment by President Gotabaya Rajapaksa to chair the "One Country, One Law" presidential task force — position framed as reforming legal pluralism to address "Islamic influences" on family and personal laws per Grokipedia analysis, framed by minority advocates as legitimisation of anti-Muslim agenda.

**The BBS anti-Muslim campaign 2012-2018.** The architecture: BBS launched anti-Muslim campaign mid-2012 framing Muslim cultural practices including Islamic dress and halal meat slaughter as negative impact on Sinhala public per 9DASHLINE March 2021 analysis; contested Muslim architectural sites suggesting construction on or near Buddhist sites with one Muslim shrine destroyed; framed Muslim demographic-economic position as threat to Sinhala-Buddhist majority. CNN July 2014: BBS anti-Muslim rhetoric tapped into "atavistic fear" of high Muslim birth rates and resentment of perceived Muslim business community success per Paikiasothy Saravanamuttu (Centre for Policy Alternatives) — operating as illustration of communal-economic anxiety articulated in religious-nationalist framing.

**Aluthgama riots 15 June 2014.** Anti-Muslim violence in southwestern Sri Lanka. The architecture: BBS rally featuring Gnanasara address in Aluthgama 15 June 2014; following the rally, Buddhist mobs marched through Muslim neighbourhoods of Dharga Town and surrounding areas ransacking dozens of homes and shops; four Muslims killed, over 50 wounded per CNN reporting; widespread property destruction; Sri Lankan Special Task Force deployed. The consequence: Gnanasara gave statement to police but did not initially face charges; national police spokesman cited that officers were still considering whether he had played a role in inciting the violence per CNN July 2014 — operating as illustration of state reluctance to prosecute monk-led incitement.

**Kandy clashes March 2018.** Anti-Muslim violence in central Sri Lanka. The architecture: violence following local incident escalating into broader anti-Muslim mob action; state of emergency declared 6 March 2018; multiple Muslim-owned properties burned; one Muslim killed in initial incident, additional deaths in subsequent violence; BBS-aligned mobilisation attributed to violence framing. The consequence: institutional confirmation of pattern from Aluthgama 2014 — monk-led mobilisation producing communal violence with limited state prosecution.

**The 2019 Easter bombings and post-Easter reconfiguration.** The architecture: coordinated Islamist suicide attacks on churches (St Anthony's Shrine Kochchikade, St Sebastian's Church Negombo, Zion Church Batticaloa) and luxury hotels (Shangri-La, Cinnamon Grand, Kingsbury Colombo) on 21 April 2019 (Easter Sunday); over 269 people killed; attribution to National Thawheed Jamaat (NTJ) Sri Lankan Islamist splinter group with claimed ISIS allegiance. The consequence: post-Easter Sri Lankan political reconfiguration producing November 2019 presidential election victory of Gotabaya Rajapaksa (former Secretary of Defence under brother Mahinda Rajapaksa 2005-2015); BBS positioning aligned with Rajapaksa security framework producing opportunity for Buddhist nationalist consolidation post-2019.

**The Gotabaya Rajapaksa presidency 2019-2022.** The architecture: presidential inauguration 18 November 2019; pattern of Sinhalese Buddhist nationalist consolidation including BBS-aligned policy positioning; November 2021 Gnanasara appointment to chair "One Country, One Law" presidential task force; April 2022 economic crisis producing Aragalaya ("People's Power Struggle") mass protests March-July 2022; 9 July 2022 storming of presidential residence; Rajapaksa flight to Maldives 13 July 2022 and resignation 14 July 2022. The consequence: end of Rajapaksa political configuration after 2022 economic collapse and Aragalaya pressure — producing political opening for non-Rajapaksa governance.

**Anura Kumara Dissanayake presidency from September 2024.** Per multiple reports: National People's Power (NPP) candidate Anura Kumara Dissanayake (Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna leader, traditional Marxist-Leninist party) won presidential election 21 September 2024 — first leftist presidency in Sri Lankan history; subsequent NPP parliamentary majority in November 2024 elections. The consequence: BBS institutional influence reduced under Dissanayake government; question whether Sinhalese Buddhist nationalist configuration retains political-electoral salience or whether 2022-2024 reconfiguration represents lasting shift away from BBS-aligned governance.

**The Mahanayaka hierarchy.** Operates as senior-monk institutional structure across the three principal Sri Lankan Buddhist nikāyas. The architecture: Siyam Nikāya (founded 1753 with royal Siamese ordination at Kandy; principal nikāya with two main chapters — Asgiriya and Malwathu, each with Mahanayaka); Amarapura Nikāya (founded 1800 with Burmese ordination at Amarapura); Ramanna Nikāya (founded 1864 from Burmese Ramanna country ordination); senior Mahanayakas operating as public-religious figures across post-1948 Sri Lankan political life. The pattern: Mahanayaka public statements throughout the post-1948 period have supported Sinhala-Buddhist majoritarianism in varying configurations — operating adjacent to BBS-style movement mobilisation rather than as parallel separate hierarchy. The Mahanayakas operate as recognised state-religious authorities while BBS operates as movement-mobilisation vehicle — together constituting the architecture of Sinhalese Buddhist nationalism.

**The Jathika Hela Urumaya (JHU).** Cross-reference institutional background. The architecture: founded 2004 following December 2003 sudden death of charismatic Buddhist monk Soma Thero; world's first political party comprised of Buddhist monks per People's Rights Group analysis; precursor to BBS positioning; multiple JHU electoral campaigns 2004-2015 with declining vote share; BBS positioned as movement-mobilisation vehicle outside parliamentary contest while drawing on JHU's earlier monk-political-party precedent.

**The summary of § II.** The Sri Lankan Sinhalese Buddhist nationalist configuration operates through the Bodu Bala Sena (founded 2012), the Mahanayaka senior-monk hierarchy, the 2014 Aluthgama riots and 2018 Kandy clashes as principal communal-violence episodes, the post-2019-Easter-bombings Gotabaya Rajapaksa presidency 2019-2022, and the post-Aragalaya 2022-2026 reconfiguration culminating in the Dissanayake September 2024 presidential victory. The architecture is illustration of monk-led Buddhist nationalist movement producing communal violence within parliamentary-democratic state context — operating through channels (BBS, JHU, Mahanayaka hierarchy) producing structural consequence in communal violence and electoral configuration. The Thai configuration dossiered in § III operates as parallel illustration of state-sangha alignment in different national configuration where sangha capture operates through military-monarchical channels rather than through monk-led nationalist movement.

---


### III. Thailand: the Sangha Act, the Monarchy, and the Politics of Sangha Capture

The Thai sangha-state configuration is the principal contemporary illustration of formal state-capture of Buddhist monastic authority through legislative, monarchical, and bureaucratic channels. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the historical Sangha Act 1902 (Rama V), 1941, 1962 (Sarit). And amendments through 2018 producing centralised state-sangha alignment. (2) the Vajiralongkorn ascension (October 2016) and the December 2016 and July 2018 Sangha Act amendments restoring royal prerogative to appoint Supreme Patriarch and senior monks. (3) the Wat Phra Dhammakaya raid March 2017 as illustration of state-military operations against perceived "corrupt" monastic elements. (4) the November 2020 Supreme Sangha Council prohibition of monk protest participation against Prayut Chan-o-cha government. (5) the pattern through which Thai sangha capture operates as routine state administrative governance of religion rather than as movement-based mobilisation. (6) the post-2023 political reconfiguration including Move Forward Party dissolution and the question of sangha positioning in continuing Thai political transformation.

**The historical Sangha Act framework.** The architecture: Sangha Act 1902 under King Chulalongkorn (Rama V, reign 1868-1910) — establishment of centralised sangha administration; Thammayut sect royal patronage from Rama IV (Mongkut, reign 1851-1868) reforms originating in his pre-coronation monastic years; configuration producing royal-Thammayut alignment alongside the more numerous Mahanikai sect. Sangha Act of 1941 — democratising reform under Phibul Songkhram government; reorganisation of sangha administration into three bodies (Sangha Council, Cabinet, Tribunal) per New Mandala August 2018 analysis. Sangha Act of 1962 — under General Sarit Thanarat (military dictator 1957-1963 following 1957 coup) — restoration of authoritarian sangha governance with Supreme Patriarch as head and Sangha Supreme Council subordinate; alignment of sangha with anti-communist Cold War-era Thai state.

**The Vajiralongkorn ascension and the December 2016 Sangha Act amendments.** The architecture: King Bhumibol Adulyadej (Rama IX, reign 1946-2016) death 13 October 2016; King Vajiralongkorn (Rama X, born 28 July 1952) ascension 13 October 2016 and coronation 4 May 2019; Sangha Act amendments passed by junta-appointed National Legislative Assembly on 29 December 2016 (217-0 vote) per Khaosod English July 2018 reporting; January 2017 Wikipedia/Buddhistdoor: parliament passing bill empowering monarch to appoint Supreme Patriarch; Section 7 of amended Sangha Act stating "the king reserves the right to appoint a successor if the supreme patriarch passes away or is unable to perform his duties, and that the prime minister may nominate the candidate to be appointed". Prime Minister Prayut Chan-o-cha reportedly nominated five senior monks for the king's final decision per Buddhistdoor analysis.

**Supreme Patriarch appointment February 2017.** The architecture: King Vajiralongkorn appointed Somdet Phra Maha Muniwong (Ariyavongsagatanana IX, born 26 June 1927) as Supreme Patriarch 12 February 2017 — the first Supreme Patriarch appointed under the amended Sangha Act bypassing prior Sangha Supreme Council nomination procedure per multiple reports. The consequence: per Buddhist scholar Surapot Thaweesak commentary cited in Khaosod English July 2018 — "the move is tantamount to returning royal power relations between the King and the Sangha to those of King Rama V's time… [Thailand] should be a secular state. The act in general will make Buddhism and the Sangha a mechanism to support conservative ideology." The pattern: post-2016 amendments producing structural return to pre-1932 royal-prerogative sangha governance, reversing post-1932 separation between royal authority and sangha administration.

**The July 2018 Monk Act amendments.** The architecture: National Legislative Assembly endorsing further Monk Act amendments in 217-vote unanimous endorsement per Khaosod English July 2018 — enabling the King to appoint or remove senior monks and members of the Supreme Sangha Council. Deputy Prime Minister Wissanu Krea-ngam justification: "King Rama X is recognized in the constitution as the patron of Buddhism and other religions… it would be fitting for the King to appoint or strip senior monks and members of the Sangha Supreme Council of Thailand of their titles, as stipulated in Article 3 of the act" per Khaosod English. The consequence: amendments cemented the monarchical capture of sangha administrative authority that the December 2016 amendments initiated — operating as completion of the post-2016 royal-prerogative restoration.

**The Wat Phra Dhammakaya raid March 2017.** The architecture: Wat Phra Dhammakaya — Buddhist temple network founded 1970 by Phra Dhammachayo (born 22 April 1944) and Phra Dattajeevo; Dhammakaya Movement vehicle with millions of adherents internationally; Wat Phra Dhammakaya central temple at Khlong Luang Pathum Thani producing infrastructure (the Maha Dhammakaya Cetiya 1995 housing one million Buddha images); accusations from 1999 onwards of unorthodox doctrine including Dhammakaya meditation technique and merit-making framework. The March 2017 raid: Department of Special Investigation deployment over February-March 2017 attempting to arrest Phra Dhammachayo on money-laundering charges related to the Klongchan Credit Union scandal; thousands of devotees forming human barriers around the temple; raid producing significant disruption without successful arrest of Phra Dhammachayo (whose whereabouts remained undisclosed). The pattern: the operation illustrating state-sangha alignment in operations against perceived "corrupt" or "deviant" monastic elements — operating in parallel to the post-2016 Sangha Act amendments through which state authority over sangha was  expanded.

**The November 2020 Supreme Sangha Council protest prohibition.** The architecture: Thailand 2020 democracy protests from July 2020 to early 2021 calling for resignation of Prime Minister Prayut Chan-o-cha and reform of monarchy; Buddhist monks visibly participating in protests including monk presence at November 2020 rallies per Reuters reporting; Supreme Sangha Council ruling 11 November 2020 prohibiting monks and novices from joining protests or "expressing political opinions" per Reuters / Malay Mail reporting. The consequence: under Prayut's junta the King was given power to appoint Thailand's chief monk to head the Sangha even if the council had picked another candidate; alignment of Supreme Sangha Council with military-monarchical state operating against protest-monk dissent. The pattern: sangha authority operating as administrative enforcement vehicle of military-monarchical state position rather than as autonomous religious authority.

**The redshirt monks 2010 and continuing sangha-rank-and-file dissent.** The architecture: pro-Thaksin "redshirt" monks operating in the March to May 2010 mass demonstrations against Abhisit Vejjajiva government; sangha-rank-and-file drawn from same regions and social classes as broader redshirt movement per Tomas Larsson Critical Asian Studies 2012 analysis. The continuing pattern: post-2014 coup and post-2016 royal succession period producing tightening of sangha discipline — Khemthong Tonsakulrungruang 2019/2022 analysis noting "notable increase in monk dismissals for disciplinary infractions following the 2016 royal succession, indicating a concerted effort to 'purify' the sangha" per cited Academia.edu analysis.

**The post-2023 political reconfiguration.** The architecture: 2023 general election won by Move Forward Party (MFP) under Pita Limjaroenrat; MFP-led coalition negotiations producing resistance from royalist establishment; Move Forward Party dissolved 7 August 2024 by Constitutional Court on charges of violating lèse-majesté reform position; Pheu Thai-led coalition government from August 2023; Srettha Thavisin Prime Minister 22 August 2023 to 14 August 2024 (Constitutional Court removal on ethics complaint); Paetongtarn Shinawatra (daughter of Thaksin) Prime Minister 16 August 2024 to 1 July 2025 (Constitutional Court suspension on ethics complaint over leaked Hun Sen phone call); Anutin Charnvirakul Prime Minister from 7 September 2025. The question: continuing Thai political-transformation operating alongside continuing post-2016 sangha-monarchical alignment — sangha capture persisting through successive civilian governments.

**The summary of § III.** The Thai sangha-state configuration operates through the Sangha Act framework (1902, 1941, 1962, 2016 amendments, 2018 amendments) producing state capture of Buddhist monastic authority; the Vajiralongkorn ascension (October 2016) and post-2016 royal-prerogative restoration; the Wat Phra Dhammakaya raid March 2017 as illustration of state operations against perceived "corrupt" monastic elements; the November 2020 Supreme Sangha Council protest prohibition; and the pattern through which Thai sangha capture operates as routine state administrative governance of religion. The architecture is illustration of state-capture of Buddhist monastic authority through legislative-bureaucratic channels rather than through movement-based mobilisation — operating distinct from the Myanmar Ma Ba Tha and Sri Lankan BBS movement-mobilisation patterns dossiered in §§ I-II. The China-controlled Tibetan Buddhism configuration dossiered in § IV is the most extreme contemporary illustration of state capture of Buddhist authority — operating through a non-Buddhist state's assertion of authority over Tibetan Buddhist reincarnation and succession processes.

---


### IV. China-controlled Tibetan Buddhism: the Institutional Rupture and the Dalai Lama Succession Question

The China-controlled Tibetan Buddhism configuration is the principal contemporary illustration of a non-Buddhist state asserting institutional authority over Buddhist reincarnation and succession processes — operating against the position of the displaced Tibetan religious authority hierarchy. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Dalai Lama history from Gendun Drup (1391-1474) through Tenzin Gyatso fourteenth (born 1935). (2) the 1950-1959 Chinese-Tibetan rupture producing the 14th Dalai Lama flight to India and establishment of the Tibetan Government in Exile. (3) the 10th Panchen Lama (1938-1989) trajectory and the 1995 11th Panchen Lama controversy (Gedhun Choekyi Nyima detention from 17 May 1995 and Chinese-recognised Gyaincain Norbu enthronement 8 December 1995). (4) the 2007 State Religious Affairs Bureau Order No. 5 claiming Chinese state authority over reincarnation recognition. (5) the Dalai Lama 14th Tenzin Gyatso succession position including July 2025 succession statement on 90th birthday. (6) the broader pattern of Chinese state governance of Tibetan religious affairs through United Front Work Department channels.

**The Dalai Lama history.** The architecture: Gendun Drup (1391-1474) — first Dalai Lama, recognised post-mortem as bearer of the title following Altan Khan's 1578 designation of Sonam Gyatso as third Dalai Lama; Sonam Gyatso (1543-1588) — third Dalai Lama, established Mongol-Gelugpa alliance through Altan Khan relationship; Ngawang Lobsang Gyatso (1617-1682) — fifth ("Great Fifth") Dalai Lama, established the Ganden Phodrang government in 1642 unifying Tibetan political-religious authority; Tenzin Gyatso (born 6 July 1935 Taktser Amdo) — fourteenth Dalai Lama, recognised 1937, enthroned 22 February 1940 at Potala Palace Lhasa. The Dalai Lama position operates through Gelugpa (Yellow Hat) school recognition processes including state oracle consultations, dreams and visions of senior monks, and child-candidate identification procedures producing institutional continuity across reincarnations.

**The 1950-1959 Chinese-Tibetan rupture.** The architecture: Chinese People's Liberation Army entry into Chamdo (eastern Tibet) October 1950; assumption of full political authority by 14th Dalai Lama 17 November 1950 at age 15; 17-Point Agreement 23 May 1951 between Tibet and PRC ("Agreement of the Central People's Government and the Local Government of Tibet on Measures for the Peaceful Liberation of Tibet"); the 17-Point Agreement signed under conditions widely contested as duress per multiple historical analyses including Tsering Shakya's *The Dragon in the Land of Snows*; Lhasa Uprising 10 March 1959 following years of escalating tensions; Dalai Lama flight from Lhasa night of 17 March 1959; arrival Tezpur India 18 April 1959; establishment of Tibetan Government in Exile (later Central Tibetan Administration) at Dharamshala 1959-1960 — vehicle continuing Ganden Phodrang governmental tradition in exile per Central Tibetan Administration history.

**The 10th Panchen Lama trajectory.** The architecture: 10th Panchen Lama (Choekyi Gyaltsen, born 19 February 1938 Xunhua Salar Autonomous County Qinghai) — recognised by Republic of China authorities in 1949 in disputed institutional process; cooperation with PRC authorities through 1950s including 17-Point Agreement endorsement; 1962 "70,000-Character Petition" to Premier Zhou Enlai critically detailing Tibetan suffering under Chinese policies including starvation, forced collectivisation, and religious persecution; subsequent denunciation as "anti-Party black book" and 1964 public denunciation; 14-year detention 1964-1977 including time at Qincheng Prison; release 1977; rehabilitation including Vice-Chairman of National People's Congress Standing Committee from 1980s; death 28 January 1989 at Xigatse days after delivering critical speech at Tashilhunpo Monastery (his official seat) — positioning at death framed as institutionally critical of Chinese state Tibetan policies despite long Chinese-state cooperation period.

**The 11th Panchen Lama controversy 1995.** The architecture: 14th Dalai Lama recognition of Gedhun Choekyi Nyima (born 25 April 1989 Lhari County Nagqu) as 11th Panchen Lama on 14 May 1995 — recognition produced from divination, dreams, and traditional recognition processes; Chinese state authorities detained Gedhun Choekyi Nyima with his family three days later on 17 May 1995 — when Gedhun Choekyi Nyima was six years old, making him among youngest political prisoners globally; Chinese state authorities organised separate recognition process producing recognition of Gyaincain Norbu (born 13 February 1990) as 11th Panchen Lama; Gyaincain Norbu enthronement at Tashilhunpo Monastery 8 December 1995 under Chinese state oversight. The consequence: ongoing rupture between the Dalai Lama-recognised Panchen Lama (Gedhun Choekyi Nyima, whereabouts undisclosed per Chinese state position throughout the post-1995 period) and the Chinese-recognised Panchen Lama (Gyaincain Norbu, operating in official Chinese positions including Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference Standing Committee membership). The pattern: 1995 controversy operates as template for the contemporary Chinese state position on Dalai Lama succession.

**The 2007 State Religious Affairs Bureau Order No. 5.** The architecture: Chinese State Religious Affairs Bureau Order No. 5 of 2007 titled "Management Measures for the Reincarnation of Living Buddhas in Tibetan Buddhism" — Chinese state regulation claiming authority over reincarnation recognition processes; position requiring all reincarnation recognitions to receive Chinese state approval; position requiring reincarnation candidates to be born within Chinese territory; position prohibiting reincarnation candidates with "foreign influence". The consequence: Order No. 5 operating as Chinese state legal framework within which contemporary state position on Dalai Lama succession is articulated — claim that any Dalai Lama successor must be born within Chinese territory and receive Chinese state approval, against the position of the Dalai Lama and Tibetan Government in Exile.

**The Dalai Lama July 2025 succession statement.** The architecture: 14th Dalai Lama public statements on succession over 2011-2025 including 24 September 2011 statement, 2014 statements, 2019 statements, and the consolidating July 2025 statement on his 90th birthday (6 July 2025); pre-90th-birthday position affirming that he would consult with Tibetan Buddhist authorities on succession question around 90th birthday; July 2025 statement: affirming continuation of the Dalai Lama institution; affirming exclusive institutional authority of the Gaden Phodrang Trust (founded 2015 by the 14th Dalai Lama as vehicle for succession matters) to recognise his successor; affirming his successor will be born in the "free world" rather than within Chinese territory; reaffirming pre-stated position against Chinese state succession claims. The consequence: July 2025 statement operating as definitive Tibetan Buddhist position on succession question — produced by Dalai Lama himself,  consolidating Tibetan Government in Exile and Tibetan Buddhist position against Chinese state claims under 2007 Order No. 5.

**The broader Chinese state governance of Tibetan religious affairs.** The architecture: Tibet Autonomous Region (TAR) — Chinese administrative entity established 1 September 1965; PRC governance of Tibetan religious affairs through United Front Work Department (UFWD, party-state vehicle), State Administration of Religious Affairs (merged into UFWD 2018 under Xi Jinping consolidation), and Tibet Autonomous Region Religious Affairs Bureau; pattern of state-aligned monk training programmes, state-approved monastery rebuilding, and state-approved religious-cultural promotion — operating alongside continuing suppression of independent religious expression. The 2008 Tibetan unrest 10-25 March 2008 — Lhasa protests producing Chinese security response including detentions and deaths; subsequent self-immolation protests including over 150 documented self-immolations 2009-2019 per International Campaign for Tibet reporting — operating as continuing illustration of Tibetan religious-cultural resistance to Chinese state capture.

**The position approaching the succession transition.** As of mid-2026, the configuration operates as: (1) the 14th Dalai Lama Tenzin Gyatso operating from Dharamshala at 91 years old; (2) the Central Tibetan Administration (Sikyong Penpa Tsering from 27 May 2021) operating as Tibetan Government in Exile vehicle; (3) the Gaden Phodrang Trust positioned as exclusive succession-recognition authority per July 2025 statement; (4) the Chinese state position under 2007 Order No. 5 maintaining claim over reincarnation recognition; (5) the question of how the post-14th Dalai Lama configuration will operate including potential for parallel Tibetan-recognised and Chinese-recognised 15th Dalai Lamas operating in rupture analogous to the post-1995 Panchen Lama configuration; (6) the broader question of Tibetan Buddhist global recognition of the eventual succession outcome — operating across Tibetan Government in Exile-recognised diaspora networks, broader international Tibetan Buddhist institutional networks, and Chinese-state-recognised institutional networks within TAR.

**The summary of § IV.** The China-controlled Tibetan Buddhism configuration operates through the Dalai Lama history; the 1950-1959 Chinese-Tibetan rupture producing the 14th Dalai Lama flight to India; the 10th Panchen Lama trajectory and the 1995 11th Panchen Lama controversy (Gedhun Choekyi Nyima detention from 17 May 1995, Chinese-recognised Gyaincain Norbu enthronement 8 December 1995); the 2007 State Religious Affairs Bureau Order No. 5 claiming Chinese state authority over reincarnation; the Dalai Lama July 2025 succession statement on 90th birthday; and the broader Chinese state governance of Tibetan religious affairs through United Front Work Department channels. The architecture is the most extreme contemporary illustration of non-Buddhist state capture of Buddhist religious authority — operating through state legal framework (Order No. 5), state-administrative apparatus (UFWD, religious affairs bureaus), and state-recognised parallel religious authority (Gyaincain Norbu, future Chinese-recognised 15th Dalai Lama) against the position of the displaced Tibetan religious authority hierarchy.

Chapter 23 closes here. The evidence covers the principal Buddhist captured configurations across Myanmar (§ I monk-led movement-state alignment), Sri Lanka (§ II monk-led movement and Mahanayaka hierarchy), Thailand (§ III state-monarchical capture), and China-controlled Tibet (§ IV non-Buddhist state capture of Buddhist religious authority). The configurations operate as illustrations of structurally analogous patterns of religious capture observed across the volume — operating through different institutional mechanisms (movement mobilisation, parliamentary legislation, royal prerogative, state legal framework) but producing structurally similar outcomes of religious authority instrumentalised by state or movement-political actors. Chapter 24 dossiers Sikh captured configurations.

---


## Chapter 24. Sikh Captured Configurations

Chapter 24 continues the deferred dossier sequence. The documentation covers the principal Sikh captured configurations. Patterns through which Sikh religious authority has been bound to political-party vehicles, the rupture of 1984 producing a sustained insurgency and its aftermath. The post-2023 international assassination configurations linking Indian state vehicles to killings of pro-Khalistan activists abroad, and the contemporary post-Amritpal Singh moment in which a new political-party vehicle has emerged from the Khalistan diaspora-and-Punjab convergence. The dossier operates with the same investigative-journalism standards applied across Part III and applies them symmetrically to Sikh configurations alongside the Buddhist, Christian, Muslim, Hindu, and Jewish configurations dossiered in preceding chapters.

The record covers four sections: § I the SGPC-Akali Dal nexus and the Punjab architecture. The Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee (statutory body under the Sikh Gurdwara Act 1925), the Shiromani Akali Dal (Sikh political party founded 14 December 1920), their continuous binding from 1920 to the present. The Akal Takht Jathedar appointment architecture, the contemporary SAD vote-share collapse, and the 4 December 2024 Sukhbir Singh Badal shooting at Golden Temple during Akal Takht-imposed religious penance. § II the Khalistan movement and the 1984 rupture. Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale, Operation Blue Star 1-8 June 1984, the Indira Gandhi assassination 31 October 1984, the November 1984 anti-Sikh pogroms, the sustained insurgency 1984-1995, and the post-1995 aftermath; § III the Khalistan diaspora and the 2023-2025 international assassination configurations. The Hardeep Singh Nijjar killing 18 June 2023 Surrey BC, the Gurpatwant Singh Pannun plot, the Vikram Yadav FBI Most Wanted listing, the Nikhil Gupta extradition, the documented RAW involvement per Washington Post April 2024 reporting, and the resulting India-Canada and India-US diplomatic ruptures; § IV Amritpal Singh, Waris Punjab De, and the contemporary post-2023 configuration. The September 2022 Waris Punjab De leadership transition, the 23 February 2023 Ajnala incident, the 18 March 2023 Punjab police operations, the Amritpal Singh detention from 23 April 2023, the 2024 Lok Sabha election from detention, and the January 2025 Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) party formation.

The argument operates against any framing that would treat Sikh life as distinct in its susceptibility to political capture and movement-mobilisation. The patterns dossiered here are structurally analogous to the patterns dossiered across Christian (cross-reference Chapters 14-18), Muslim (cross-reference Chapters 19-21), Hindu (cross-reference Chapter 22), Jewish (cross-reference Chapter 20), and Buddhist (cross-reference Chapter 23) configurations. Operating through statutory religious bodies bound to political parties, through state operations against perceived militant religious figures, through diaspora networks engaged with foreign-state intelligence operations, and through contemporary movement-mobilisation producing electoral consequence. What follows covers these patterns with the same evidentiary standards applied across the volume.

### I. The SGPC-Akali Dal Nexus and the Punjab Institutional Architecture

The Sikh architecture in Indian Punjab is structured around the binding of the Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee (SGPC) and the Shiromani Akali Dal (SAD) — a binding running from their parallel 1920 origins as components of the Gurdwara Reform Movement to the contemporary moment. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the SGPC as statutory body under the Sikh Gurdwara Act 1925 controlling historic gurdwaras and Sikh religious authority. (2) the SAD as principal Sikh political party from 1920 with continuous SGPC control across most of the post-1925 period. (3) the Akal Takht Jathedar appointment architecture through which SAD-controlled SGPC selects the head of Sikh temporal-religious authority. (4) the 2003-2016 Sehajdhari Sikh voting exclusion and the contemporary Sikh-definition contestation. (5) the contemporary SAD electoral collapse including 2024 Lok Sabha single-seat outcome. (6) the 4 December 2024 Sukhbir Singh Badal shooting at Golden Temple during Akal Takht-imposed penance.

**The Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee.** Statutory body created under the Sikh Gurdwara Act 1925. The architecture: the Act passed by Punjab Legislative Council under British colonial administration following the Gurdwara Reform Movement (Akali movement) 1920-1925 to remove Sikh shrines from custodians (mahants) appointed under Hindu-style hereditary control and place them under elected Sikh management; SGPC manages historic Sikh gurdwaras in Punjab, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh, and Chandigarh; framed as "mini parliament of Sikhs" per ThePrint analysis; sole authority to print and distribute saroops (printed forms) of the Guru Granth Sahib; runs schools, colleges, polytechnics, a university, a medical college, and a dental college; final authority in cases where Sikhs or non-Sikhs come into conflict with conventions and traditions of the Sikh religion; takes up Sikh issues with government of India and foreign governments. SGPC members are required to be baptised Sikhs adept in Sikh history and scriptures and adhering to the Sikh rehat maryada per ThePrint analysis. The 175-person general house structure (now 159 elected seats after Haryana separated to form its own SGPC in 2022) operating as principal Sikh vehicle in India.

**The Shiromani Akali Dal.** Sikh political party founded 14 December 1920 as part of the Gurdwara Reform Movement parallel to SGPC formation. The architecture: SAD positioning as principal Sikh political vehicle 1920-present; per Sikh Formations vol. 17 (2021) — both SGPC and SAD were "created in 1920 as a part of the larger Gurdwara Reform Movement — the SGPC to administer historic Sikh shrines and the SAD to represent Sikh political interests in politics"; both institutions firmly institutionalised the Khalsa identity as core of Sikh ethnic-religious identity from 1920; both were at the centre of the Punjab conflict during the 1980s and 1990s. SAD continuous control of SGPC across most of the post-1925 period — when the last full elections to the SGPC were held in 2011, SAD along with Sant Samaj won 157 of 170 seats per ThePrint analysis. The consequence: SGPC has functioned as the venue through which various Akali factions vie for community leadership — the majority Akali faction defining both Sikh ethnic-religious identity contours and the Sikh political relationship with the Indian central state per Sikh Formations analysis.

**The Akal Takht Jathedar architecture.** Position of head of the Akal Takht (literally "throne of the timeless one") — supreme temporal seat of Sikh authority, located at Amritsar adjacent to the Golden Temple. The architecture: position formation 15 June 1606 under Guru Hargobind; first holder Bhai Gurdas; appointer Sarbat Khalsa (assembly of the Khalsa Panth) or SGPC; the position selected by the SAD-controlled SGPC is the supreme seat of Sikh religious authority while remaining bound to SAD political configuration. The current dispute: contested incumbency between Dhian Singh Mand (from 10 November 2015, claiming Sarbat Khalsa-conferred authority) and Kuldeep Singh Gargaj (from 7 March 2025, SGPC-appointed) per Wikipedia Jathedar of the Akal Takht. The Akal Takht position operates through SGPC appointment — and thus through SAD political control of the SGPC — producing structural binding of supreme Sikh religious authority to SAD political vehicle. Per Sikh Formations: "The Akali faction controlling the SGPC also selects the Jathedar of the Akal Takht, arguably the supreme seat of temporal and religious authority for the Sikhs. As such, the SGPC and SAD form the interrelated nexus of Sikh religious and political life."

**The Sehajdhari Sikh voting exclusion.** Sehajdhari Sikhs — Sikhs who may not maintain the five Ks (kesh, kanga, kara, kachhera, kirpan) of the Khalsa identity. The architecture: 1944 amendment to Sikh Gurdwara Act allowed Sehajdhari Sikhs to vote in SGPC elections; 2003 Punjab government notification revoked Sehajdhari voting rights; Sehajdhari Sikh Federation challenged in Punjab and Haryana High Court following 2011 SGPC elections; 2016 amendment to Sikh Gurdwara Act formalised exclusion of Sehajdhari Sikhs from SGPC voting per ThePrint analysis. The structural consequence: the SGPC-Khalsa identity binding consolidated through legislative exclusion of non-Khalsa Sikhs from religious-governance voting — analytically parallel to Sikh Formations observation that "the SGPC moved in the other direction and limited Sehajdhari Sikhs (Sikhs who do not maintain the outward symbols of Sikhism such as uncut hair) from voting in the SGPC elections as of 2016" while SAD itself moved in opposite direction by 1996 Moga conference stopping limiting party membership to Sikhs only.

**The Dhami presidency and SGPC contestation 2022-2025.** Harjinder Singh Dhami SGPC President trajectory. The architecture: fourth re-election October 2022 defeating Akali rebel Bibi Jagir Kaur (Akali Sudhar Lehar breakaway faction) 107-33 votes per ThePrint analysis; Dhami announced resignation 17 February 2025; resignation rejected by SGPC executive board per Wikipedia Harjinder Singh Dhami; fifth election November 2025 defeating breakaway faction challenger with 117 of 136 votes cast — Dhami "handily secured victory in his fifth consecutive election to the presidency". The consequence: SAD-aligned Dhami presidency continuation through 2025 despite Akali rebel and breakaway faction challenges — illustrating resilience of SAD-SGPC binding even as SAD electoral position outside Sikh institutions has collapsed.

**The SAD electoral collapse 2024.** The architecture: SAD vote share decline observed across post-2017 period per IRB Canada April 2025 reporting and multiple analyses; 2024 Lok Sabha election in Punjab — of 13 Punjab Lok Sabha seats, only 1 SAD candidate won (Bathinda constituency) per PTI April 2025 / The Statesman April 2025 reporting; Sukhbir Singh Badal re-elected SAD president April 2025 per IRB Canada; contradiction — SAD electoral collapse outside Sikh religious institutions while SAD-aligned figures retain SGPC control. The pattern: SGPC position operating as last remaining stronghold of SAD organisational vehicle as broader Punjab electorate shifts to Aam Aadmi Party (AAP), Congress, and emerging vehicles including Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) dossiered in § IV.

**The 4 December 2024 Sukhbir Singh Badal shooting.** Operates as illustration of fracture within Sikh authority architecture. The architecture: Sukhbir Singh Badal performing religious penance at Golden Temple Amritsar as part of punishment laid down by Akal Takht per Reuters / Zee Media reporting 4 December 2024 — Akal Takht penance imposed for SAD failures during the post-2007 SAD-BJP coalition government in Punjab (2007-2017) including handling of the 2015 Guru Granth Sahib sacrilege incidents; gunman opened fire at Badal during the penance; Badal survived; gunman arrested with reported ties to Khalistani militantism per The Statesman / India TV. The consequence: the Akal Takht  censured the Akali Dal president — and a Khalistani-aligned actor then attempted his life during the censure itself — illustrating the contestation between SGPC-SAD configuration on one hand and Khalistan-aligned configuration on the other.

The summary of § I: the SGPC-Akali Dal nexus is the principal contemporary Sikh architecture in Indian Punjab. The configuration operates through the SGPC statutory body under the 1925 Sikh Gurdwara Act, the SAD political party from 14 December 1920, the Akal Takht Jathedar appointment architecture through which SAD-controlled SGPC selects supreme Sikh religious authority. The 2003-2016 Sehajdhari Sikh voting exclusion consolidating Khalsa-identity binding, the contemporary SAD electoral collapse with SGPC stronghold retained, and the 4 December 2024 Sukhbir Singh Badal shooting illustrating contestation. The Khalistan movement dossiered in § II is the principal historical and contemporary contestation against the SGPC-SAD-Indian-state configuration — producing the 1984 rupture and sustained insurgency that continues to shape contemporary Sikh life.

---


### II. The Khalistan Movement and the 1984 Institutional Rupture

The Khalistan movement and the 1984 rupture is the principal historical configuration shaping contemporary Sikh life. The architecture has seven principal nodes: (1) the Khalistan demand origins from the 1940s Sikh nationalist articulation through the 1971 Jagjit Singh Chauhan declaration and the 1973 Anandpur Sahib Resolution; (2) Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale (1947-1984) as principal figure of the early-1980s Khalistan militancy; (3) Operation Blue Star 1-8 June 1984. The Indian Army assault on Golden Temple complex Amritsar ordered by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi and led by General Kuldip Singh Brar. (4) the Indira Gandhi assassination 31 October 1984 by her Sikh bodyguards Satwant Singh and Beant Singh. (5) the November 1984 anti-Sikh pogroms killing over 8. 000 Sikhs across India. (6) the sustained Punjab insurgency 1984-1995 including the 23 June 1985 Air India Flight 182 bombing killing 329 people, the 10 August 1986 General AS Vaidya assassination, and the 31 August 1995 Punjab Chief Minister Beant Singh assassination. (7) the post-1995 aftermath shaping contemporary diaspora and post-Amritpal Singh configurations dossiered in §§ III-IV.

**The Khalistan demand origins.** The architecture: 1940s Sikh nationalist articulation in the context of partition deliberations producing initial conceptual articulation of a separate Sikh state; 1947 partition producing massive Sikh displacement from West Punjab (Pakistan) to East Punjab (India); 1956 States Reorganisation Commission redrawing Indian state boundaries on linguistic basis but declining to create a Punjabi-speaking state distinct from Hindi-speaking Haryana; 1966 Punjab Reorganisation Act producing creation of present-day Punjab, Haryana, and Himachal Pradesh after sustained SAD-led Punjabi Suba agitation per Britannica analysis. The 1971 Jagjit Singh Chauhan Khalistan declaration: Dr Jagjit Singh Chauhan (1929-2007), former Punjab Finance Minister, declared Khalistan formation on 13 October 1971 from London — emerging as principal diaspora spokesman of the Khalistan demand through 1978-1992 period. The 1973 Anandpur Sahib Resolution: SAD-issued resolution demanding greater Punjab autonomy including state powers over agriculture, water, and language policy; reissued and revised in subsequent years; framed by Indian government as Khalistan-adjacent though formally limited to autonomy demands within Indian Union.

**Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale.** Born 1947 Rode village Faridkot district Punjab. The architecture: head of Damdami Taksal Sikh seminary from 1977 succeeding Gurbachan Singh Khalsa; positioning as orthodox Sikh preacher articulating opposition to Sant Nirankari sect (a Sikh-adjacent group viewed as heterodox by orthodox Sikhs); 13 April 1978 Sant Nirankari-Bhindranwale supporters Amritsar clash producing 13 Sikh deaths and marking the beginning of militancy in the state per Sikh Formations analysis; Bhindranwale subsequent emergence as figure of pro-Khalistan militancy 1978-1984; Bhindranwale-Congress relationship including allegations of Congress support to Bhindranwale as counterweight to SAD per Sikh Formations analysis — "Congress supported the Sant as a counterweight to the SAD for control of the SGPC". Bhindranwale taking residence in Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple) complex Amritsar from April 1980; making the Akal Takht his command headquarters with armed followers per multiple analyses including Pakistan Today June 2021 and Britannica. The consequence: the Sikh's holiest shrine became headquarters of armed pro-Khalistan group through 1982-1984 — producing the confrontation that culminated in Operation Blue Star.

**Operation Blue Star 1-8 June 1984.** Indian Army operation to dislodge Bhindranwale and his armed followers from Golden Temple complex per Britannica analysis. The architecture: ordered by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi; led by General Kuldip Singh Brar (Lieutenant General, Western Command); operation began 1 June 1984 with army deployment; assault proper conducted 3-8 June 1984 (sometimes given as 3-6 June for the principal assault); heavy artillery, tanks, and helicopters used to enter the temple complex per Quint analysis; Bhindranwale killed in the operation along with dozens of his followers; Akal Takht buildings sustained heavy damage including structural destruction. The casualties: official Indian government death toll around 400 people including soldiers, militants, and civilian pilgrims trapped in the complex; Sikh groups dispute the figure and say the number of people that died was in the thousands per CBC News reporting June 2024 on 40th anniversary. The consequences: Sikh soldiers and administrative officers reported to have rebelled and quit services per Quint; prominent Sikh writers including Khushwant Singh returned government awards; framed within Sikh discourse as "Genocide Day" (Teeja Ghallughara, the Third Holocaust per Britannica reference) commemorated annually 3-8 June at Golden Temple. Per Pakistan Today June 2021 from inside the Sikh institutional framing: "the Indian army led by General Kuldip Singh Brar, supported by troops and armoured vehicles, broke all records of state terrorism and extra-judicial killings through that barbaric operation."

**The Indira Gandhi assassination 31 October 1984.** The architecture: assassination at her residence 1 Safdarjung Road New Delhi by her two Sikh bodyguards Satwant Singh and Beant Singh per multiple sources including Quint analysis; Beant Singh killed by other security personnel at scene; Satwant Singh apprehended and later executed 6 January 1989 along with conspirator Kehar Singh; assassination framed by perpetrators as retaliation for Operation Blue Star; consequence — sustained Sikh discourse honouring the assassins as Shaheeds (martyrs) including SGPC controversies over honouring the assassins, contrasted with mainstream Indian discourse framing the killing as terrorism.

**The November 1984 anti-Sikh pogroms.** The architecture: following Indira Gandhi assassination 31 October 1984, organized anti-Sikh mob violence across India principally in Delhi 1-3 November 1984; over 8,000 Sikhs killed across India per Quint and multiple Sikh organisation estimates; Delhi pogrom organized with Congress-aligned figures' involvement per Justice Misra Commission and subsequent investigations; Sikh men dragged from homes and burned alive; Sikh-owned businesses looted; voter lists used to identify Sikh households per investigations; Delhi police inaction during pogrom documented across multiple investigations; commissions including Misra (1986-1987), Kapur Mittal (1990), Jain Banerjee (1990), Ahuja (1987), Poti-Rosha (1990), Jain Aggarwal (1990), Narula (1993), Nanavati (2000-2005), and the Special Investigation Team configurations producing partial prosecutions over the post-1984 period; Congress figure Sajjan Kumar convicted December 2018 in two killings; Jagdish Tytler under prosecution as of 2023-2026. The consequence: November 1984 pogroms producing sustained Sikh grievance against the Indian central state and the Congress party — operating as foundation of contemporary Sikh diaspora Khalistani political mobilisation dossiered in §§ III-IV.

**The sustained Punjab insurgency 1984-1995.** Following Operation Blue Star and the November 1984 pogroms — Punjab entered sustained militant-state violence through 1995. The architecture: multiple militant groups including Khalistan Commando Force, Babbar Khalsa International, Khalistan Liberation Force, Khalistan Zindabad Force, International Sikh Youth Federation operating across Punjab and diaspora; counter-insurgency operations by Punjab Police under KPS Gill (Director General Punjab Police 1988-1990, 1991-1995) and Indian security forces producing extra-judicial killings widely documented by human rights organisations including Punjab Human Rights Organisation, Ensaaf, and the Committee for Coordination on Disappearances in Punjab; tens of thousands of deaths across the period.

**The principal violence episodes 1985-1995.** The architecture: Air India Flight 182 bombing 23 June 1985 — Sikh nationalists based in Canada destroyed Air India flight from Montreal-Toronto-London-Delhi-Bombay killing 329 people per Quint analysis; Talwinder Singh Parmar (Babbar Khalsa International) identified as principal organiser; trial outcomes — Ripudaman Singh Malik and Ajaib Singh Bagri acquitted 2005; Inderjit Singh Reyat (the only person convicted) sentenced 2003 for manslaughter on bomb-construction charges. General AS Vaidya assassination 10 August 1986 — Pune by two motorcycle-borne militants per Quint analysis; unsigned note on behalf of so-called "Khalistan Commando Force" claimed responsibility; Vaidya was Indian Army Chief of Staff at time of Operation Blue Star. Punjab Chief Minister Beant Singh (Congress Punjab CM 1992-1995) assassination 31 August 1995 — suicide bomber at Punjab civil secretariat Chandigarh per Quint analysis; militant Davinder Singh Bhullar later sentenced to death (commuted to life imprisonment 2014); 1995 Beant Singh killing operating as concluding major militant operation of the post-Blue Star insurgency phase.

**The post-1995 aftermath.** The architecture: Punjab insurgency largely ended by late 1990s through combined military-police counter-insurgency operations and 1992-1995 SAD return to electoral politics; SAD-BJP coalition government in Punjab 1997-2002 (Prakash Singh Badal CM); 1997-2002 return to constitutional politics within Indian Union; sustained Sikh discourse honouring Bhindranwale as Shaheed across SGPC, Akal Takht, and SAD-Amritsar (Simranjit Singh Mann faction); annual June 6 Operation Blue Star anniversary commemorations at Golden Temple with pro-Khalistan slogans by hardliner organisations per multiple Deccan Herald reporting including the 2018 34th anniversary clash between radicals and SGPC task force; sustained diaspora Khalistan mobilisation through 1995-present period setting the stage for the post-2023 international assassination configurations dossiered in § III.

The summary of § II: the Khalistan movement and the 1984 rupture is the principal historical configuration shaping contemporary Sikh life. The configuration operates through the Khalistan demand origins, Bhindranwale's 1978-1984 trajectory, Operation Blue Star 1-8 June 1984, the Indira Gandhi assassination 31 October 1984, the November 1984 anti-Sikh pogroms killing over 8,000. The sustained Punjab insurgency 1984-1995 including Air India 182 bombing 23 June 1985, General AS Vaidya assassination 10 August 1986, and Beant Singh assassination 31 August 1995, and the post-1995 aftermath through which sustained Sikh discourse honours Bhindranwale as Shaheed while diaspora Khalistani political mobilisation continues. The diaspora configurations dossiered in § III operate as the post-1995 international extension of the 1984 rupture — producing the post-2023 crisis in India-Canada and India-US relations through the contemporary international assassination operations.

---


### III. The Khalistan Diaspora and the 2023-2025 International Assassination Configurations

The 2023-2025 international assassination configurations constitute the contemporary rupture between Indian state intelligence vehicles and the Khalistan diaspora — producing diplomatic crises with Canada (from September 2023) and the United States (from November 2023) and reshaping the contemporary Sikh position in the West. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Hardeep Singh Nijjar killing 18 June 2023 outside the Guru Nanak Sikh Gurdwara Surrey British Columbia. (2) the parallel Gurpatwant Singh Pannun plot in New York thwarted by US authorities. (3) the Vikram Yadav FBI Most Wanted listing from October 2024 and the Samant Goel (former RAW chief) involvement per Washington Post April 2024 reporting. (4) the Nikhil Gupta arrest June 2023 Czech Republic and June 2024 extradition to US. (5) the Trudeau 18 September 2023 statement on "credible allegations" producing the India-Canada diplomatic rupture. (6) the Sikhs for Justice Khalistan Referendum October 2024 Surrey drawing 200,000 participants and the broader diaspora response.

**Hardeep Singh Nijjar (1977-2023).** Sikh activist and gurdwara leader. The architecture: born 1977 Bhar Singh Pura Punjab; grew up during sustained Punjab insurgency dossiered in § II; emigrated to Canada late 1990s; settled Surrey British Columbia; gurdwara leader (chairman) at Guru Nanak Sikh Gurdwara Surrey BC; pro-Khalistan activist with Sikhs for Justice; Indian government 2020 terrorist designation under Unlawful Activities Prevention Act per multiple sources; subject of Indian extradition requests denied by Canadian authorities; according to Indian authorities accused of alleged terrorism offences and conspiracy to commit murder which he denied per Malay Mail October 2024 analysis. The killing 18 June 2023: Nijjar shot 34 times by two masked gunmen as he attempted to drive his pickup truck off the lot of Surrey's Guru Nanak gurdwara per Globe and Mail March 2026 reporting; killing produced immediate Canadian Sikh community response framing Nijjar as Shaheed; killing producing fracture in Canada-India diplomatic relationship.

**The Trudeau 18 September 2023 statement.** Operates as moment of diplomatic rupture. The architecture: Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau statement 18 September 2023 in House of Commons that the Canadian government was "actively pursuing credible allegations" of a "potential link between agents of the government of India and the killing of a Canadian citizen, Hardeep Singh Nijjar" per multiple reports including AP / PBS NewsHour and CBC News. India's response: foreign ministry dismissed allegations as "absurd and motivated" per AP September 2023 reporting; tit-for-tat diplomatic expulsions; India suspended visa services for Canadian citizens September 2023 (later resumed). The consequence: Trudeau public condemnation framed by New Lines Institute January 2026 analysis as "the ultimate sign that the political organization of Canadian Sikhs had made their diaspora politics official state policy" — operating as integration of Canadian Sikh community advocacy into Canadian state foreign-policy positioning.

**The May 2024 RCMP arrests.** Operates as initial criminal-prosecution stage. The architecture: 3 May 2024 RCMP announced arrests of four Indian nationals in Edmonton Alberta on charges of first-degree murder and conspiracy to commit murder — Karan Brar, Amandeep Singh, Kamalpreet Singh, and Karanpreet Singh per CBC News reporting; the four men had lived in Canada for only three to five years per New Lines Institute analysis; Superintendent Mandeep Mooker (RCMP Integrated Homicide Investigation Team) framing per CBC May 2024 — "the evidence will show the roles these individuals played" without confirming "guns for hire" framing at that stage. The Globe and Mail March 2026 reporting on additional persons of interest including Indian consular officials.

**The parallel Pannun plot New York 2023.** Operates as US-side configuration. The architecture: Gurpatwant Singh Pannun — Sikhs for Justice general counsel; US citizen and US legal practitioner; vocal critic of Indian government; co-organiser with Nijjar of Khalistan Referendum campaign per Globe and Mail reporting; 2023 plot to assassinate Pannun in New York thwarted by US authorities; DOJ indictment unsealed November 2023 of Indian national Nikhil Gupta (arrested Czech Republic 30 June 2023 on US warrant; extradited US 14 June 2024); indictment alleging Gupta hired hit-man on behalf of "CC-1" (later identified as Vikram Yadav of RAW per Washington Post April 2024 reporting) to murder Pannun. The October 2024 DOJ superseding indictment: indicted Vikram Yadav of RAW on murder-for-hire conspiracy charges per Onmanorama October 2024 reporting; indictment included photograph of Yadav in military attire and another showing individuals exchanging money in car in New York on 9 June 2023 — payment allegedly made to hired killer.

**The Vikram Yadav FBI Most Wanted listing.** Operates as US position against Indian state intelligence official. The architecture: Vikram Yadav — RAW officer per Washington Post April 2024 reporting by Greg Miller and Gerry Shih (subsequent extensive reporting through 2025-2026); on FBI Most Wanted List from October 2024 per Globe and Mail March 2026 reporting; accused by US of orchestrating plot to kill Pannun; alleged to have shared private video of Nijjar's dead body with a man he had asked to kill Pannun per US prosecutorial filings cited in Globe and Mail. Per Bloomberg November 2025 detailed account: "Then, on the night of June 18, 2023, the RAW officer sent Gupta a video clip, which showed a bullet-ridden body slumped in the cab of a pickup truck. The victim was Hardeep Singh Nijjar, a friend of Pannun's and a fellow leader in the Khalistan movement. Hours earlier, two masked gunmen had ambushed Nijjar as he departed a temple in a suburb of Vancouver, shooting him 34 times." The linkage: the same RAW officer allegedly orchestrating both the Nijjar killing (Canada, 18 June 2023) and the parallel Pannun plot (US, summer 2023) — producing structural alignment between the two cases.

**Samant Goel and the RAW involvement.** The architecture: Samant Goel — former RAW (Research and Analysis Wing) chief through 2019-2023; identified per Washington Post April 2024 report as having approved plan to hire hit-team to assassinate Pannun; Washington Post reporting based on interviews with "more than three dozen current and former senior security officials in Australia, Britain" and other allied intelligence services per Global Security reporting; India promptly rejected Washington Post report as "unwarranted and unsubstantiated" per Indian Ministry of External Affairs statements. The consequence: RAW involvement in 2023 plots producing US and Canadian state-level response — including Canadian declaration of Bishnoi gang as terrorist organisation September 2025 per Globe and Mail reporting, US prosecutorial proceedings against Nikhil Gupta, and continuing diplomatic strain.

**Sanjay Verma and the Indian high commissioner expulsion October 2024.** Operates as second-stage diplomatic rupture. The architecture: Sanjay Verma — Indian High Commissioner to Canada; expelled by Canada October 2024 following RCMP statements naming Verma and other Indian diplomats as persons of interest in homicide investigations into Nijjar killing; Verma had been reassigned to Cyprus from July 2023 (three weeks after Nijjar killing); exited Canada May 2024 per Globe and Mail reporting. The consequence: Indian response framing the diplomatic expulsions as further illegitimate Canadian interference; Canadian response framing the expulsions as self-defence against foreign intelligence operations.

**The Sikhs for Justice Khalistan Referendum October 2024 Surrey.** Operates as diaspora response. The architecture: Sikhs for Justice — pro-Khalistan advocacy group; Khalistan Referendum campaign across diaspora communities 2021-present; October 2024 Surrey BC vote drawing 200,000 Sikh participants with 30% travelling to polls from outside the city per New Lines Institute January 2026 analysis; organisers reported that Nijjar's killing motivated many voters to participate. The consequence: the killing producing reverse-mobilising effect — Indian state operations against Khalistan diaspora figures producing increased diaspora cohesion and electoral-mobilisation capacity rather than diminishment.

**The Avtar Singh Khanda death 15 June 2023 UK.** Operates as adjacent case requiring investigation. The architecture: Avtar Singh Khanda — UK-based pro-Khalistan activist; died 15 June 2023 in Birmingham hospital from acute myeloid leukaemia per official medical certificate; death three days before Nijjar killing; circumstances under continuing investigation by UK authorities; family alleging poisoning per multiple Sikh organisation statements; UK government 2024 statements declining to formally investigate beyond initial determination. The pattern: Khanda death adjacent to the Nijjar killing timeline producing diaspora concern about broader pattern of Indian state operations against Khalistan figures — though formally not established as connected to Indian state operations as of mid-2026.

**The Lawrence Bishnoi gang configuration.** Operates as criminal-intermediary configuration. The architecture: Lawrence Bishnoi — Indian organized-crime figure detained in Indian prison Bhatinda Punjab; Bishnoi gang implicated in multiple Canadian and US plots per Globe and Mail March 2026 reporting; Yadav engaged members of Bishnoi gang to hire the four alleged gunmen accused of opening fire on Nijjar per Globe and Mail sources; Canada declared Bishnoi gang a terrorist organization in September 2025 blaming it for extortions and violence in South Asian community per Globe and Mail; Bishnoi gang operating as criminal-intermediary structure between Indian state intelligence and foreign-soil operations.

The summary of § III: the 2023-2025 international assassination configurations are the contemporary rupture between Indian state intelligence and the Khalistan diaspora. The configuration operates through the Hardeep Singh Nijjar killing 18 June 2023 Surrey BC, the parallel Gurpatwant Singh Pannun plot in New York thwarted by US authorities, the Vikram Yadav FBI Most Wanted listing from October 2024, the Samant Goel involvement per Washington Post April 2024 reporting. The Nikhil Gupta extradition June 2024, the Sanjay Verma expulsion October 2024, the Sikhs for Justice Khalistan Referendum October 2024 Surrey drawing 200,000 participants, the Avtar Singh Khanda death 15 June 2023 UK as adjacent case, and the Lawrence Bishnoi gang criminal-intermediary configuration. The contemporary Amritpal Singh and Waris Punjab De configuration dossiered in § IV is the parallel domestic Punjab configuration shaped by the same 1984-1995 rupture dossiered in § II. Producing the contemporary moment in which Khalistani-aligned political-party formation has entered Indian parliamentary politics through the 2024 Lok Sabha election from detention and the January 2025 Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) party formation.

---


### IV. Amritpal Singh, Waris Punjab De, and the Contemporary Post-2023 Configuration

The Amritpal Singh and Waris Punjab De configuration is the principal contemporary Khalistani-adjacent political-mobilisation vehicle operating inside Indian Punjab. The architecture has seven principal nodes: (1) the Waris Punjab De institutional founding 30 September 2021 by actor-activist Deep Sidhu and the September 2022 leadership transition to Amritpal Singh following Sidhu's 15 February 2022 death. (2) the 23 February 2023 Ajnala police station incident producing first major confrontation with Punjab state authority. (3) the 18 March 2023 Punjab police operation against Waris Punjab De producing mass detentions, internet shutdown. And National Security Act invocation. (4) the Amritpal Singh detention from 23 April 2023 to date at Dibrugarh Central Jail Assam under NSA. (5) the 2024 Lok Sabha election victory from Khadoor Sahib constituency by margin over 197. 000 votes while still in NSA detention. (6) the parallel 2024 Lok Sabha victory of Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa (son of Beant Singh, Indira Gandhi assassin) from Faridkot constituency. (7) the 14 January 2025 Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) party formation at Mela Maghi Sri Muktsar Sahib and the 13 April 2025 Vaisakhi Talwandi Sabo political conference.

**The Waris Punjab De founding and Deep Sidhu.** The architecture: Waris Punjab De — pro-Khalistan and Punjabi nationalist organisation founded 30 September 2021 by Deep Sidhu (born 17 December 1984 Punjab); Deep Sidhu trajectory — Punjabi actor and lawyer, prominent figure in 2020-2021 farmers' protests (the Indian farm laws protest movement October 2020 to December 2021), controversial figure during Republic Day 26 January 2021 Red Fort incident in which protestors entered Red Fort complex and hoisted Sikh religious flag (Nishan Sahib) on flagpole producing widespread Indian condemnation; Sidhu founded Waris Punjab De ("Heirs of Punjab") as political-cultural organisation operating in Khalistani-adjacent intellectual space; Sidhu died 15 February 2022 in road accident on Delhi-Chandigarh expressway aged 37; circumstances framed in Sikh discourse as suspicious though formally determined as accidental.

**Amritpal Singh Sandhu.** Born 17 January 1993 Jallupur Khera Punjab. The architecture: lived Dubai 2012-2022 working in transport business; returned to India 2022; assumed Waris Punjab De leadership September 2022 following Sidhu's death; positioning as orthodox Sikh preacher articulating pro-Khalistan and anti-drug-trade message; media framing as "Bhindranwale 2.0" per multiple Indian press reports drawing parallel with the 1980s Bhindranwale trajectory dossiered in § II; positioning at Akal Takht 29 September 2022 amritpan (Sikh initiation ceremony) producing formal entry into Khalsa identity; rapid emergence as principal contemporary domestic-Punjab Khalistani-adjacent figure 2022-2023.

**The 23 February 2023 Ajnala police station incident.** Operates as first major confrontation with Punjab state authority. The architecture: Lovepreet Singh "Toofan" (Amritpal Singh aide) arrested in alleged kidnapping case Ajnala area near Amritsar; Waris Punjab De mobilisation 23 February 2023 — Amritpal Singh supporters armed with swords and firearms storming Ajnala police station demanding Lovepreet Toofan's release per multiple reports including NewsCanvassEdu analysis; clashes with police; Amritpal Singh supporters carried Guru Granth Sahib into the confrontation  framing the action as religious-defensive operation per multiple analyses; Lovepreet Toofan released after the operation.
