The consequence: Ajnala incident framing in Punjab state response as institutional challenge requiring decisive state response — producing the 18 March 2023 operation.

**The 18 March 2023 Punjab police operation.** Operates as Punjab state response. The architecture: wide-scale Punjab police operations against Waris Punjab De supporters from 18 March 2023; National Security Act invoked against multiple Waris Punjab De associates; internet shutdown across Punjab 18-23 March 2023; over 200 Waris Punjab De supporters detained per multiple reports; Amritpal Singh evaded immediate capture producing month-long manhunt across Punjab and adjacent states; institutional framing in Indian Government discourse as security-state operation against pro-Khalistan militant figure. Per International Policy Digest July 2023 analysis: "After revealing crimes committed against his community, Sikh activist Amritpal Singh Sandhu was arrested and reportedly tortured by Indian police. His arrest sparked protests and a subsequent violent crackdown in Punjab, the Sikhs' ancestral homeland. In the face of protests, Indian authorities imposed curfews, severed communication lines, and launched aggressive operations."

**The Amritpal Singh arrest 23 April 2023.** The architecture: surrendered to police 23 April 2023 at Rode village Moga district per multiple Indian and international press reports; Rode village historically significant as ancestral village of Bhindranwale (cross-reference § II); Amritpal Singh sent to Dibrugarh Central Jail Assam under National Security Act detention — far from Punjab to remove him from local mobilisation capacity; NSA detention initially for one year, subsequently extended; Amritpal Singh positioning as detained Khalistani-adjacent figure operating parallel to the 1980s detention of multiple militant figures during the post-Blue Star insurgency period.

**The 2024 Lok Sabha election from detention.** Operates as moment of Khalistani-adjacent electoral entry into Indian parliament. The architecture: Amritpal Singh contested 2024 Lok Sabha election from Khadoor Sahib constituency Punjab as independent candidate while still in Dibrugarh detention; won by margin over 197,000 votes per multiple reports; Khadoor Sahib constituency historically significant as Sikh institutional centre; Amritpal Singh victory producing Khalistani-adjacent figure entering Lok Sabha as elected MP from detention — parallel to multiple historical cases of detained candidates winning elections in Indian electoral history but with unique Khalistani-adjacent positioning. The consequence: integration of Khalistani-adjacent politics into Indian parliamentary system through 2024 Lok Sabha election outcome.

**The Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa Faridkot victory.** Operates as parallel moment. The architecture: Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa — son of Beant Singh (one of the two Sikh bodyguards who assassinated Indira Gandhi 31 October 1984, cross-reference § II); elected from Faridkot constituency Punjab 2024 Lok Sabha as independent candidate; pro-Khalistan affiliated; Waris Punjab De / Khalistani-adjacent positioning. The consequence: 2024 Lok Sabha election producing two Khalistani-adjacent figures (Amritpal Singh, Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa) entering Indian parliament —  illustrating contemporary post-1984 Punjab political reconfiguration in which Khalistani-adjacent figures operate within constitutional electoral framework while remaining adjacent to pro-Khalistan diaspora networks dossiered in § III.

**The Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) party formation 14 January 2025.** Operates as formal political-party vehicle. The architecture: party formed by Amritpal Singh supporters 14 January 2025 at Mela Maghi Sri Muktsar Sahib per Wikipedia Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De); president Amritpal Singh; Lok Sabha leader Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa; founded date 14 January 2025; ideology Punjabi Separatism, Punjabiyat, Sikh Nationalism per Wikipedia; political position right-wing; colours saffron and dark blue; Election Commission of India status unregistered; 2 of 543 Lok Sabha seats (Amritpal Singh from Khadoor Sahib, Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa from Faridkot); 0 of 117 Punjab Legislative Assembly seats; 0 of 245 Rajya Sabha seats. The 13 April 2025 second political conference Talwandi Sabo: coinciding with Vaisakhi celebration — Talwandi Sabo historically significant as Damdama Sahib (one of the five Takhts of Sikhism); party leaders shared updates regarding party's activities in the prior three months and discussed preparations for the 2027 Punjab Legislative Assembly election per Wikipedia. The consequence: formal political-party formation operating as Khalistani-adjacent alternative to the SGPC-aligned SAD configuration dossiered in § I — producing contestation between SAD (electoral collapse, SGPC retention) and Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) (parliamentary entry from 2024) within Punjab Sikh politics.

**The Punjab AAP government and the response.** Operates as state-government positioning. The architecture: Bhagwant Mann (Aam Aadmi Party) Punjab Chief Minister from 16 March 2022 following AAP electoral victory March 2022; positioning aligned with central-government security response against Waris Punjab De through 18 March 2023 operations; positioning aligned with Indian central-government framing of Amritpal Singh as security threat requiring NSA detention; contestation with Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) positioning as Mann government and Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) approach 2027 Punjab Assembly election. The consequence: Punjab political configuration as of mid-2026 includes AAP state government, SAD declining presence, Congress declining presence, BJP marginal presence, and emerging Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) — producing reconfiguration of Punjab political-religious landscape in which Khalistani-adjacent political-party vehicles operate alongside non-Khalistani-adjacent vehicles.

The summary of § IV: the Amritpal Singh and Waris Punjab De configuration is the principal contemporary Khalistani-adjacent political-mobilisation vehicle operating inside Indian Punjab. The configuration operates through the Waris Punjab De institutional founding 30 September 2021 by Deep Sidhu and the September 2022 leadership transition to Amritpal Singh. The 23 February 2023 Ajnala police station incident. The 18 March 2023 Punjab police operation producing the manhunt period. The Amritpal Singh arrest 23 April 2023 at Rode village Moga district. The NSA detention at Dibrugarh Central Jail Assam. The 2024 Lok Sabha victory from detention with over 197. 000-vote margin from Khadoor Sahib constituency. The parallel Sarabjeet Singh Khalsa Faridkot victory; the 14 January 2025 Akali Dal (Waris Punjab De) party formation at Mela Maghi Sri Muktsar Sahib; the 13 April 2025 Vaisakhi Talwandi Sabo political conference; and the Punjab AAP government response under Bhagwant Mann.

Chapter 24 closes here. The evidence covers the principal Sikh captured configurations across the SGPC-Akali Dal institutional nexus (§ I), the Khalistan movement and the 1984 rupture (§ II), the Khalistan diaspora and the 2023-2025 international assassination configurations (§ III), and the Amritpal Singh and Waris Punjab De contemporary post-2023 configuration (§ IV). The configurations operate as illustrations of structurally analogous patterns of religious capture observed across the volume. Operating through statutory religious bodies bound to political parties, through state operations against perceived militant religious figures, through diaspora networks engaged with foreign-state intelligence operations, and through contemporary movement-mobilisation producing electoral consequence — but operating in distinctly Sikh configurations shaped by the 1984 rupture, the sustained 1984-1995 insurgency, and the contemporary post-2023 international and domestic re-mobilisation. Chapter 25 dossiers Indigenous commodification configurations.

---


## Chapter 25. Indigenous Commodification Configurations

Chapter 25 continues the deferred dossier sequence. The documentation covers the principal Indigenous commodification configurations — patterns through which Indigenous spiritual traditions, sacred sites, sacred medicines, and religious practices are commodified, appropriated, and instrumentalised by non-Indigenous actors operating in commercial, scientific, and religious-regulatory contexts. The dossier operates with the same investigative-journalism standards applied across Part III, applies them symmetrically across the dossiered configurations, and operates against any framing that would treat Indigenous spiritual life as  available for non-Indigenous instrumentalisation regardless of consent.

The record covers four sections: § I the New Age commercialisation of Native American spirituality and the "plastic shaman" phenomenon. The 1993 Declaration of War Against Exploiters of Lakota Spirituality, the Lisa Aldred and Christopher Ronwanien:te Jocks scholarly framework, the Sun Bear, Lynn Andrews, and Carlos Castaneda cases, and the contemporary Tribal Alliance Against Frauds documentation; § II ayahuasca tourism, the Peruvian regulatory configuration, and the "psychedelic renaissance". The 2008 Peruvian National Institute of Culture cultural-heritage declaration, the 1971 UN Convention on Psychotropic Substances Peru reservation, the contemporary US Embassy Lima 23 January 2025 health alert. The Costa Rica Health Ministry 28 January 2025 warning, and the broader contestation over commercial-tourist vs traditional-Indigenous ayahuasca use. § III sacred sites, land dispossession, and the Mauna Kea / Thirty Meter Telescope contestation. The Native Hawaiian Kanaka Maoli framework on Mauna Kea, the 2014-2020 protests, the 2019 Puʻuhonua o Puʻuhuluhulu sanctuary, the 2022 Mauna Kea Stewardship and Oversight Authority, and the broader pattern of Indigenous sacred-site dispossession including Standing Rock, Oak Flat, and Bears Ears; § IV peyote, the Native American Church, and the contemporary US legal-protection contestation. The Native American Church 1918 incorporation, AIRFA 1978, AIRFA Amendments 1994, the 1990 *Employment Division v. Smith* ruling, RFRA 1993, the 21 CFR § 1307.31 federal exemption, the contemporary "psychedelic churches" challenge, the late-2025 / early-2026 DOJ opinion controversy, and the NCAI Peyote Working Group response.

The analysis operates against any framing that would treat Indigenous configurations as residual or peripheral to the principal captured-religious configurations dossiered across the volume. Patterns of Indigenous commodification operate as central illustration of how religious authority and tradition are appropriated, instrumentalised, and converted to non-Indigenous use through commercial channels (§ I, § II), state-development channels (§ III), and regulatory-religious-protection channels (§ IV). What follows covers these patterns with the same evidentiary standards applied across the volume.

### I. The New Age Commercialisation of Native American Spirituality and the "Plastic Shaman" Phenomenon

The New Age commercialisation of Native American spirituality is the principal commercial configuration through which Indigenous North American spiritual traditions have been appropriated and converted to non-Indigenous commercial use since the post-1960s counterculture. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the 10 June 1993 Declaration of War Against Exploiters of Lakota Spirituality issued by Lakota Summit V. (2) the scholarly framework articulated by Lisa Aldred (2000), Christopher Ronwanien:te Jocks (2000), Wendy Rose (1992). And Vine Deloria Jr (1933-2005). (3) the principal "plastic shaman" cases including Sun Bear, Lynn Andrews, Carlos Castaneda, and Mary Summer Rain. (4) the broader category-confusion through which the Siberian-origin term "shaman" came to be applied to Indigenous spiritual leaders across North America, South America, and globally; (5) the *Playing Indian* (Philip J. Deloria, 1998) historical analysis of non-Indigenous appropriation; (6) the contemporary Tribal Alliance Against Frauds documentation of fraudulent Indigenous identity claims.

**The 1993 Declaration of War Against Exploiters of Lakota Spirituality.** Operates as principal Lakota institutional statement against non-Indigenous appropriation. The architecture: 10 June 1993 Lakota Summit V at Pine Ridge Indian Reservation; declaration penned by three spiritual leaders — Wilmer Mesteth, Darrell Standing Elk, and Phyllis Swift Hawk; ratified by representatives from over 40 Sioux nations per Project MUSE analysis. The text decried a long process of "having [their] most precious Lakota ceremonies and spiritual practices desecrated, mocked and abused by non-Indian 'wannabes,' hucksters, cultists, commercial profiteers and self-styled 'New Age shamans' and their followers." The declaration also denounced "plastic shamans" — members of Lakota cultural communities who shared knowledge with outsiders for profit without authorisation from the community of Lakota spiritual leaders. The consequence: the declaration provided foundational textual framework against which subsequent Lakota and broader Native American positions on appropriation have been articulated — operating as Indigenous self-defence against the wider commercial appropriation pattern.

**The scholarly framework.** Operates as analytical articulation of the commercial-appropriation pattern. The architecture: Lisa Aldred's "Plastic Shamans and Astroturf Sun Dances: New Age Commercialization of Native American Spirituality" (*American Indian Quarterly* 24, no. 3, 2000) — foundational scholarly framework; Aldred's observation per ResearchGate citation: "their imperialistically nostalgic fetishisation of Native American spirituality hinders any recognition of their own historical and social complicity in the oppression of Indigenous peoples"; Christopher Ronwanien:te Jocks (Mohawk) "Spirituality for Sale: Sacred Knowledge in the Consumer Age" (in Lee Irwin ed., *Native American Spirituality: A Critical Reader*, University of Nebraska Press, 2000); Wendy Rose (Hopi) "The Great Pretenders: Further Reflections on Whiteshamanism" (in M. Annette Jaimes ed., *The State of Native America: Genocide, Colonization, and Resistance*, South End Press, 1992); Vine Deloria Jr (Standing Rock Sioux scholar, 1933-2005) *Custer Died for Your Sins: An Indian Manifesto* (Macmillan, 1969) and *God Is Red: A Native View of Religion* (Grosset & Dunlap, 1973). The pattern: Native American scholars and Native American studies scholars articulated the analytical framework that subsequent activist and advocacy continues to operate within. Cynthia R. Kasee's "Identity, Recovery, and Religious Imperialism: Native American Women and the New Age" (*Women & Therapy* 1995) is a parallel articulation. The 1996 documentary *White Shamans and Plastic Medicine Men* (directed by Terry Macy and Daniel Hart) is the principal documentary illustration.

**The Sun Bear case.** Operates as contested commercial figure. The architecture: Sun Bear (Vincent LaDuke, 1929-1992) — Chippewa-descent figure from White Earth Reservation; founded the Bear Tribe Medicine Society 1970; framed in his self-presentation as Native American spiritual teacher with "white wives, mostly young," producing books, seminars, "medicine wheel gatherings," and ceremonial services for non-Native audiences across the 1970s-1990s period; condemned by Native American Studies figures including Vine Deloria Jr and the broader Native advocacy as illustration of the commercial-appropriation pattern operating from within nominally Native positioning; contestation between Sun Bear's self-positioning as Indigenous teacher and Native positioning framing his commercial operations as instrumentalisation. The Sun Bear case illustrates the contestation over who has authority to claim Indigenous spiritual transmission and on what terms.

**The Lynn Andrews case.** Operates as principal non-Native author case. The architecture: Lynn V. Andrews (born c. 1942) — non-Native American author; *Medicine Woman* (Harper & Row, 1981) and subsequent series ("Crystal Woman," "Star Woman," "Jaguar Woman," "Woman at the Edge of Two Worlds") presenting fictional accounts of apprenticeship to two Native American "Medicine Women" (Agnes Whistling Elk and Ruby Plenty Chiefs) of disputed actual existence; series produced extensive commercial success across the 1980s-1990s; condemned by Native advocacy including SPIRIT (Support and Protection of Indian Religions and Indigenous Traditions) and AIM (American Indian Movement) positions framing Andrews as principal exemplar of non-Native commercial appropriation operating without consent of any verified Indigenous institutional source. The Andrews case consequence: documented template for subsequent non-Native authors operating in the "channelled-Indigenous-wisdom" commercial-publishing category.

**The Carlos Castaneda case.** Operates as foundational ethnographic-fiction case. The architecture: Carlos Castaneda (1925-1998) — Peruvian-born American author; UCLA anthropology Ph.D 1973 dissertation; *The Teachings of Don Juan: A Yaqui Way of Knowledge* (University of California Press, 1968) and ten subsequent volumes presented as ethnographic accounts of apprenticeship to "Don Juan Matus," a Yaqui sorcerer; works produced massive commercial and intellectual influence across the 1960s-1990s including extensive university curriculum incorporation; subsequent investigative work including Richard de Mille's *Castaneda's Journey* (1976) and *The Don Juan Papers* (1980) documenting internal contradictions framed by de Mille and subsequent scholars as evidence the works were not genuine ethnographic documentation; UCLA institutional retention of Castaneda's PhD continuing despite scholarly contestation; Castaneda's commercial estate continuing to produce Tensegrity workshops and publications. The consequence: Castaneda's framework producing template for subsequent "ethnographic-Indigenous-wisdom" commercial-publishing genre operating in liminal space between fiction and reported ethnography — and producing damage to anthropological-credibility on Indigenous-spiritual reporting.

**The category-confusion around "shaman."** Operates as structural condition of appropriation. The architecture: "shaman" — term originating in Tungusic languages of Eastern Siberia (saman, šaman) referring to a specific category of religious-spiritual practitioner among Tungusic peoples per Obscure Clouds analysis; term applied through 19th and 20th century anthropological literature to hundreds of Indigenous religious-practitioner roles across Indigenous traditions in the Americas, Africa, Asia, and Australasia; the conceptual conflation operates as condition through which an Indigenous spiritual practitioner role from one tradition becomes available conceptually as a template for non-Indigenous appropriation across all traditions. The pattern: "plastic shamanism" — Westerners learning about Indigenous shamanic practices and then selling shamanic services to other Westerners for commercial profit per Obscure Clouds analysis; associated with "spiritual tourism" and the "romanticization and objectification of the native Other." Society for Shamanic Practice self-examination produces ongoing contestation within the broader Western shamanic-practice community over appropriation framings.

**The contemporary Tribal Alliance Against Frauds documentation.** Operates as Indigenous response. The architecture: Tribal Alliance Against Frauds — vehicle organising contemporary documentation of fraudulent Indigenous identity claims and appropriation per ResearchGate citation 2023; documented response producing ongoing 2024-2026 publication of identified cases including academic figures alleged to be self-indigenizing per cited researchers (Cidleon 2021; Anonymous 2021; Boyl 2024). The broader contemporary "pretendian" documentation: Joseph M. Pierce (Cherokee Nation) and Kim TallBear (Sisseton Wahpeton Oyate) scholarly work on contemporary Indigenous identity contestation; Jacqueline Keeler (Diné/Dakota) ongoing investigative work documenting non-Native scholars and public figures making contested Indigenous identity claims including Andrea Smith (Cherokee claim contested by Cherokee Nation positions), Sacheen Littlefeather (multiple contested Indigenous-identity claims), and others.

The summary of § I: the New Age commercialisation of Native American spirituality is the principal post-1960s commercial configuration through which Indigenous North American spiritual traditions have been appropriated and converted to non-Indigenous commercial use. The configuration operates through the 1993 Declaration of War Against Exploiters of Lakota Spirituality, the scholarly framework articulated by Aldred, Jocks, Rose, and Deloria, the principal "plastic shaman" cases including Sun Bear, Lynn Andrews, and Carlos Castaneda, the broader category-confusion around "shaman," the Philip J. Deloria *Playing Indian* historical analysis, and the contemporary Tribal Alliance Against Frauds documentation. The architecture is illustration of religious-tradition appropriation operating through commercial-publishing, workshop-economy, and identity-claim channels. The ayahuasca tourism configuration dossiered in § II operates as parallel illustration of Indigenous-spiritual commercial appropriation operating through international tourism in distinct national-regulatory configurations.

---


### II. Ayahuasca Tourism, the Peruvian Regulatory Configuration, and the "Psychedelic Renaissance"

The contemporary ayahuasca-tourism configuration is the principal Amazonian-Indigenous commercial-appropriation pattern in the 21st century. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the traditional Amazonian-Indigenous ayahuasca practice across Shipibo-Konibo, Shuar, Ashaninka, Kichwa. And other Amazonian peoples. (2) the 2008 Peruvian National Institute of Culture Resolution 836 declaration recognising traditional Indigenous ayahuasca use as national cultural heritage. (3) the 1971 UN Convention on Psychotropic Substances DMT listing and the Peru reservation. (4) the explosive growth of ayahuasca-tourism economy from c. 2000 onward principally in Iquitos. Peru. (5) the contemporary regulatory crisis including the 23 January 2025 US Embassy Lima Health Alert and the 28 January 2025 Costa Rica Health Ministry warning; (6) the broader "psychedelic renaissance" producing parallel commodification dynamics in North American and European contexts including the November 2022 Colorado Natural Medicine Health Act decriminalisation framework.

**The traditional Amazonian-Indigenous ayahuasca practice.** Ayahuasca — Quechua name (aya: spirit/dead; waska: vine/rope) — brew traditionally prepared from *Banisteriopsis caapi* vine (containing harmala alkaloids) and *Psychotria viridis* leaves (containing DMT) per multiple ethnobotanical sources. The architecture: traditional preparation by Amazonian Indigenous peoples including Shipibo-Konibo (in Peruvian Amazon Ucayali region), Shuar (Ecuadorian-Peruvian Amazon), Ashaninka (Peruvian central Amazon), Kichwa (Ecuadorian Amazon), Yawanawa, Huni Kuin (Kaxinawa), and many others; traditional use across centuries for healing, divination, hunting guidance, and intra-community diplomatic processes. Per ICEERS analysis: "Ayahuasca grows wild in the Upper Amazon and indigenous peoples there have used it for healing, divination, hunting, and diplomacy since time immemorial." The position of traditional Amazonian-Indigenous communities operates distinct from the contemporary tourism configuration dossiered below.

**The 2008 Peruvian National Institute of Culture declaration.** Operates as principal state-level Peruvian recognition. The architecture: Peruvian National Institute of Culture (Instituto Nacional de Cultura) Resolution 836 issued 2008 recognising traditional Indigenous ayahuasca use as national cultural heritage per ICEERS and Down to Earth June 2025 analysis. The declaration's framing: traditional ayahuasca use distinct from "decontextualized, consumerist and commercial western uses" per ICEERS citation. The consequence: declaration establishes Peruvian institutional framework recognising traditional Indigenous practice as cultural heritage while simultaneously framing commercial-tourism use as distinct — without producing legal-regulatory framework distinguishing the two operationally. Per The Yale Globalist 2016 reporting citing Andres Valladolid of Indecopi: "there is no official registration process to start an ayahuasca business. Anyone, Peruvian or foreign, can come to the country and earn a living by engaging tourists in the ancient cultural tradition without certification."

**The 1971 UN Convention reservation.** Operates as international-law basis. The architecture: 1971 UN Convention on Psychotropic Substances — DMT (a principal active compound in ayahuasca) listed as Schedule I controlled substance internationally; Peru's signatory reservation specifically protecting ayahuasca use by Amazonian ethnic groups (and the cactus San Pedro / huachuma for Andean ethnic groups) per ICEERS analysis. The consequence: Peru one of the only countries where ayahuasca is institutionally legal under domestic law as a result of the 1971 reservation — producing the governing preconditions for the contemporary tourism economy.

**The Iquitos tourism economy.** Operates as principal commercial concentration. The architecture: Iquitos, Loreto Region, Peru — capital of Peruvian Amazon; widely accessible to international tourism through Iquitos International Airport from c. 2000 onward; principal hub of contemporary ayahuasca-tourism economy; per ICEERS analysis "the local ayahuasca economy [is] the largest in any city in the world"; estimated thousands of international tourists annually 2010-2020 with significant decline during 2020-2022 COVID-19 period and rebound 2023-present per Chacruna Institute analyses by Emily Sinclair; multiple "retreat centres" operating across Iquitos, Pucallpa, Tarapoto, and other Peruvian Amazon locations. The economy operates without formal regulatory certification of facilitators ("shamans"), preparation protocols, safety procedures, or participant screening procedures per Chacruna analysis — producing structural conditions for the safety incidents dossiered below.

**The Olivia Arevalo case April 2018.** Operates as illustration of structural crisis. The architecture: Olivia Arevalo Lomas (c. 1937-2018) — 81-year-old Shipibo-Konibo healer in Ucayali region; murdered April 2018 in her community; subsequent lynching of Canadian Sebastian Woodroffe by Shipibo community members at the murder scene per Chacruna analysis. Per Chacruna: "When a Canadian man was lynched by a Shipibo community in Peru earlier this year, after murdering one of their most treasured healers, 81-year-old Olivia Arevalo, there were cries of outrage worldwide over the fact that ayahuasca tourism remains unregulated. Through sources in Iquitos, I discovered that the culprit-turned-victim, Sebastian Woodroffe, had previously been refused entry to Canadian ayahuasca circles due to mental instability, yet he was able to access ayahuasca ceremonies in Peru." The consequence: Arevalo case illustrating both the lethal consequences of the unregulated tourism economy and the pattern through which the Peruvian Amazon ayahuasca economy operates without screening or institutional accountability mechanisms.

**The 23 January 2025 US Embassy Lima Health Alert.** Operates as principal contemporary state-level Western warning. The architecture: US Embassy in Lima issued formal Health Alert 23 January 2025 — explicitly urging Americans to avoid ayahuasca and kambo retreats due to escalating risks per Zombie Mycology February 2025 analysis and Weles Group October 2025 analysis. Citation specifics: "In 2024, several US citizens died or experienced severe illness, including mental health episodes, following consumption of ayahuasca. These incidents often occur in remote areas near or within the Peruvian Amazon, far away from modern medical facilities" per Down to Earth June 2025 citation. The alert framed risks including "recent deaths and overdoses at ayahuasca retreat centers; serious health complications from plant medicine interactions; rising reports of violent crimes, including sexual assault, robbery, and fraud at some retreat centers." Prior US Embassy ayahuasca-tourism warnings issued 2012, 2013, and 2022 — January 2025 warning stronger than prior advisories per Zombie Mycology analysis. The consequence: US position shifting from passive monitoring to active discouragement of US-citizen ayahuasca-tourism participation.

**The 28 January 2025 Costa Rica Health Ministry warning.** Operates as parallel state-level warning in expanding ayahuasca-tourism market. The architecture: Costa Rica Health Ministry issued public warning 28 January 2025 against both ayahuasca and ibogaine (a separate plant-based psychoactive medicine used in addiction therapy contexts) per Zombie Mycology February 2025 analysis. The Costa Rica positioning: ayahuasca lacks legal sanitary registration in Costa Rica — neither explicitly legal nor illegal — but operating without regulatory framework or Indigenous protections comparable to Peru's 2008 cultural-heritage declaration per Zombie Mycology analysis. The consequence: Costa Rica emerging as parallel commercial ayahuasca-tourism market without the protections that exist in Peru — producing position of explicit Costa Rican state warning against the commercial industry.

**The Canadian Sebastian Woodroffe lynching aftermath.** Operates as illustration of community-response. The architecture: Sebastian Woodroffe — 41-year-old Canadian man — Woodroffe accused of murdering Olivia Arevalo April 2018 in Ucayali region of Peruvian Amazon; subsequently lynched by Shipibo community members per multiple reports including Chacruna analysis. The consequence: Shipibo community direct institutional action operating outside Peruvian state law-enforcement institutional framework — illustrating gap in Peruvian state-protection coverage of remote Amazonian community contexts. The pattern: Indigenous self-defence operating in absence of effective Peruvian state regulatory and law-enforcement institutional coverage of the ayahuasca-tourism economy.

**The "psychedelic renaissance" North American configuration.** Operates as parallel commodification pattern in North American regulatory context. The architecture: post-2015 increase in academic-medical research on psilocybin (Johns Hopkins, NYU, UCLA, others) producing credibility for therapeutic-psychedelic framework; subsequent state-level regulatory reform — Colorado Natural Medicine Health Act passed November 2022 as first US state decriminalising mescaline-bearing plants, DMT (in ayahuasca), and iboga for adult use, with regulatory framework for psilocybin therapy effective from late 2024 per APL Journeys analysis; Oregon Measure 109 November 2020 establishing psilocybin therapy program from 1 January 2023. The consequence: state-level US framework producing commercial-therapeutic configuration through which ayahuasca and related Indigenous-sacred plants are converted to therapeutic-commercial use within Western regulatory framework operating distinct from but cross-referenced with the broader Indigenous-sacred-medicine commercial-appropriation pattern. The broader contestation between Indigenous-religious-protection framing and "psychedelic-therapy" commercial framing dossiered further in § IV.

The summary of § II: the contemporary ayahuasca-tourism configuration is the principal Amazonian-Indigenous commercial-appropriation pattern in the 21st century. The configuration operates through the traditional Amazonian-Indigenous ayahuasca practice across Shipibo-Konibo and other peoples, the 2008 Peruvian National Institute of Culture declaration recognising traditional use as cultural heritage, the 1971 UN Convention reservation establishing institutional legal basis. The explosive growth of the Iquitos tourism economy, the structural-crisis cases including Olivia Arevalo April 2018, the 23 January 2025 US Embassy Lima Health Alert, the 28 January 2025 Costa Rica Health Ministry warning, and the broader "psychedelic renaissance" producing parallel commodification dynamics. The architecture is illustration of Indigenous-sacred-medicine commercial appropriation operating through international tourism and contemporary Western state-regulatory frameworks. The Mauna Kea contestation dossiered in § III operates as illustration of Indigenous sacred-site contestation operating through state-development institutional channels.

---


### III. Sacred Sites, Land Dispossession, and the Mauna Kea / Thirty Meter Telescope Contestation

The contemporary Mauna Kea contestation is the principal North American Indigenous sacred-site contestation in the 21st century — operating as template for the broader pattern of Indigenous sacred-site dispossession across Standing Rock, Oak Flat, Bears Ears, and other contemporary configurations. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Native Hawaiian Kanaka Maoli framework on Mauna Kea as piko of Hawaiʻi Island and sacred site of creation. (2) the Thirty Meter Telescope (TMT) International Observatory project and its 2003-present institutional trajectory. (3) the 2014-2020 protests producing 64 arrests including the 2019 Puʻuhonua o Puʻuhuluhulu sanctuary. (4) the legal and regulatory trajectory including December 2015 Hawaiʻi Supreme Court ruling. September 2017 Board of Land and Natural Resources ruling, and 2024 NPS identification of 425+ consulting parties. (5) the 2022 Mauna Kea Stewardship and Oversight Authority transferring management from University of Hawaiʻi. (6) the broader pattern of Indigenous sacred-site contestation including Standing Rock Dakota Access Pipeline 2016-2017, Oak Flat Apache vs Resolution Copper, and Bears Ears National Monument configuration.

**The Native Hawaiian Kanaka Maoli framework on Mauna Kea.** Operates as principal Indigenous-religious framework against which the TMT contestation has been articulated. The architecture: Mauna Kea (literally "white mountain") — dormant volcano on Hawaiʻi Island; tallest mountain in world from base to peak per BYU-Hawaii Ke Alakai May 2025 analysis (over 33,000 feet base-to-peak measured from Pacific Ocean floor); piko (umbilical cord) of Hawaiʻi Island per Lihau Thielk citation. The Native Hawaiian creation framework: Wakea (sky father) and Papahānaumoku (earth mother) coming together at Mauna Kea producing the birth of the Hawaiian islands per HuffPost July 2019 reporting and multiple sources; summit associated with multiple akua (gods and goddesses) including Poliʻahu (snow goddess); site of numerous burials, altars, shrines, and ceremonial sites — at least 263 archaeologically significant sites identified at summit alone per Kali Review analysis. Per NARF: "Kanaka Maoli regard every part of Mauna Kea as a living temple. On Mauna Kea, children learn more about their history and identity. Ancestors remain at rest in designated and secret burial spots. Kanaka Maoli collect foods and materials on Maunakea to maintain traditional and customary practices and lifeways. Shrines, Native Hawaiian astronomical and navigation observatories, places of worship, historic trails, and ceremonial sites cover the mountain. Kanaka Maoli regard Mauna Kea as a sacred umbilical cord that connects each Native Hawaiian person to ancestors, the land, and the gods."

**The Thirty Meter Telescope project.** Operates as principal contemporary scientific-development pressure on Mauna Kea summit. The architecture: Thirty Meter Telescope International Observatory — international scientific consortium project including Caltech, University of California, Canada, China, India, Japan; project planning from c. 2003 onward; proposed 18-story, five-acre observatory complex on Mauna Kea summit per Truthout August 2019 analysis. The TMT project framing: state and TMT officials justifying construction through Mauna Kea's positioning as "the best place for the telescope's successful operation" per Cultural Survival 2019 analysis. The Native Hawaiian-side framing: per Kealoha Pisciotta (Mauna Kea Anaina Hou president) cited in HuffPost July 2019 — "The TMT is non-negotiable for [state and TMT officials]. Well, at this point, building the TMT is non-negotiable for us." The 13 existing telescopes on Mauna Kea summit per YES! Magazine reporting — pattern of cumulative development against which TMT contestation operates.

**The 2014-2020 protests.** Operates as principal contemporary Native Hawaiian self-defence configuration. The architecture: Thirty Meter Telescope protests dated 7 October 2014 to early 2020 per Wikipedia Thirty Meter Telescope protests; located at Mauna Kea Access Road near Mauna Kea Visitors Center (19.7591583°N 155.4564083°W); 64 arrests across the protest period. The 2015 phase: in 2015 Native Hawaiians and allies halted the TMT project by camping out and blocking the road to construction crews for months until the Hawaiʻi Supreme Court stopped construction in December 2015 per Truthout. The 2019 phase: 10 July 2019 Governor David Ige construction-resumption announcement triggering Native Hawaiian kiaʻi (protectors) return to Mauna Kea per Truthout August 2019 analysis; 15 July 2019 law enforcement "swept" Mauna Kea of all members of the public and prohibited access via Mauna Kea Access Road and Mana Road; 17 July 2019 state of emergency declared by Governor Ige; National Guard called in per Kali Review analysis; over 34 protesters detained and arrested for civil disobedience including many elders per Cultural Survival 2019. The Puʻuhonua o Puʻuhuluhulu sanctuary: established at base of Mauna Kea during 2019 protests; organised society governed by principle of "kapu aloha" (prohibition against acting without kindness and love towards all) per Truthout; offered free meals, medical care, and classes on Hawaiian language, history, environment, and related topics; vehicle of Native Hawaiian-Indigenous solidarity mobilisation.

**The institutional legal and regulatory trajectory.** The architecture: December 2015 Hawaiʻi Supreme Court ruling officially stopping TMT construction per Truthout; subsequent state Board of Land and Natural Resources (BLNR) revised contested case hearing; September 2017 BLNR ruling in favour of TMT — finding "no physical evidence" that it would affect historic cultural sites such as burials or altars because such sites are not within direct vicinity per YES! Magazine analysis; appeals to Hawaiʻi Supreme Court including by Mauna Kea Anaina Hou; subsequent Hawaiʻi Supreme Court ruling upholding board's decision per YES! Magazine; July 2019 final building permits reissued. The 2024 NPS / NFS identification of 425+ consulting parties: National Park Service / National Forest Service formal identification of over 425 consulting parties deeply impacted by TMT proposed development per NARF October 2024 reporting; vehicle for Native Hawaiian community consultation; Native Hawaiian Legal Corporation (NHLC) positioning to provide training and preparation for affected community members and leaders. Per NHLC Senior Staff Attorney Ashley Obrey: "Native Hawaiians have long sought inclusion in decisions that impact sacred places and our survival as Kanaka Maoli, yet seldom have we been allowed to participate." The 2025 status per BYU-Hawaii Ke Alakai May 2025: "Over a decade ago, native Hawaiians began protesting the construction of the Thirty Meter Telescope on Maunakea on the Island of Hawai'i, also known as Big Island, but the protests continue in 2025."

**The 2022 Mauna Kea Stewardship and Oversight Authority.** Operates as governance reform. The architecture: Hawaiʻi legislature 2022 Act 255 creating Mauna Kea Stewardship and Oversight Authority — transferring management of Mauna Kea from University of Hawaiʻi to new authority; transitional period to 2028; vehicle including Native Hawaiian community representation; response to sustained protest pressure producing legislative governance reform. The question: continuing contestation 2022-2026 over whether the Mauna Kea Authority will operate as vehicle for Native Hawaiian-led mountain stewardship or as continuation of prior state-development pattern under reformed administrative architecture.

**The broader pattern of Indigenous sacred-site contestation.** The architecture: Standing Rock Sioux Tribe vs Dakota Access Pipeline (DAPL) 2016-2017 — at Standing Rock Indian Reservation North Dakota; sustained #NoDAPL protest movement; Sacred Stone Camp and Oceti Sakowin Camp from April 2016; over 700 arrests; December 2016 Obama administration denial of easement; January 2017 Trump administration reversal; April 2017 pipeline operational; subsequent legal proceedings continuing 2020-2026. Oak Flat (Chí'chil Biłdagoteel) Apache sacred site Arizona — site of San Carlos Apache Holy Ground ceremonies including girls' Sunrise Ceremony coming-of-age ritual; threatened by Resolution Copper (Rio Tinto / BHP joint venture) proposed copper mine; 2014 National Defense Authorization Act provision authorising land swap; sustained Apache Stronghold legal-advocacy challenge; 2024 Ninth Circuit en banc ruling against Apache Stronghold; 2025 US Supreme Court denied certiorari; mining project advancing 2025-present. Bears Ears National Monument Utah — 1.35-million-acre designation December 2016 by Obama administration in response to Inter-Tribal Coalition (Hopi, Navajo, Ute Indian Tribe, Ute Mountain Ute, Pueblo of Zuni) consultation; December 2017 Trump administration reduction to 16% of original size; October 2021 Biden administration restoration; contestation continuing through Trump second-term 2025-present configuration. Chaco Canyon (Chaco Culture National Historical Park) New Mexico — sustained Pueblo and Diné advocacy against oil-and-gas leasing in 10-mile buffer zone; 2023 Biden administration 20-year mineral withdrawal order under Public Land Order 7923; contestation under Trump second-term continuing 2025-present.

**The UNDRIP institutional framework.** Operates as international-law basis. The architecture: UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) — adopted by UN General Assembly 13 September 2007 with 144 states in favour, 4 against (US, Canada, Australia, New Zealand), 11 abstentions; subsequent endorsement by all four initial-opposing states including US endorsement December 2010 under Obama administration; key articles for sacred-site contestation including Article 11 (cultural traditions and ceremonies), Article 12 (spiritual and religious traditions), Article 25 (spiritual relationship with land), and Article 31 (intellectual and cultural heritage). The consequence: UNDRIP providing institutional framework for Indigenous sacred-site advocacy operating across the cases dossiered above — though without formal binding-law effect against state-development decisions in the domestic US, Canadian, Australian, and New Zealand jurisdictions.

The summary of § III: the contemporary Mauna Kea contestation is the principal North American Indigenous sacred-site contestation in the 21st century. The configuration operates through the Native Hawaiian Kanaka Maoli framework on Mauna Kea as piko of Hawaiʻi Island, the Thirty Meter Telescope International Observatory project and its 2003-present trajectory, the 2014-2020 protests producing the Puʻuhonua o Puʻuhuluhulu sanctuary. The institutional legal trajectory including the December 2015 Hawaiʻi Supreme Court ruling and the 2024 NPS identification of 425+ consulting parties, the 2022 Mauna Kea Stewardship and Oversight Authority governance reform, and the broader pattern of Indigenous sacred-site contestation including Standing Rock, Oak Flat, Bears Ears, and Chaco Canyon. The architecture is illustration of Indigenous sacred-site contestation operating through state-development institutional channels and against state-development pressure. The peyote and Native American Church regulatory configuration dossiered in § IV operates as parallel illustration of Indigenous-religious contestation operating through US federal regulatory-religious-protection channels.

---


### IV. Peyote, the Native American Church, and the Contemporary US Legal-Protection Contestation

The peyote-Native American Church contemporary US legal-protection contestation is the principal regulatory-religious-protection configuration in which Indigenous-sacramental-medicine protections face pressure from the broader "psychedelic renaissance" commodification dynamic dossiered in §§ I-II. The architecture has seven principal nodes: (1) the Native American Church institutional history from Quanah Parker (Comanche, c. 1845-1911) through 1918 Oklahoma incorporation to contemporary position; (2) the American Indian Religious Freedom Act 1978 and the 1994 Amendments protecting NAC peyote use; (3) the 1990 *Employment Division v. Smith* Supreme Court ruling and the 1993 Religious Freedom Restoration Act response; (4) the 21 CFR § 1307.31 federal regulatory exemption framework; (5) the contemporary peyote habitat depletion crisis in the Texas Rio Grande Valley; (6) the contemporary "psychedelic churches" institutional challenge including the 2022 Colorado Natural Medicine Health Act framework. The multiple DEA religious-exemption petitions 2016-2024, and the Church of Gaia 2025 ARRA exemption; (7) the late-2025 / early-2026 DOJ Office of Legal Counsel opinion controversy and the NCAI Peyote Working Group response.

**Native American Church institutional history.** The architecture: Quanah Parker (Comanche, c. 1845-1911) — principal figure in late-19th-century emergence of organised peyote religion in Indian Territory (Oklahoma); 1880s-1890s peyote ceremonies among Comanche and adjacent peoples drawing on earlier Indigenous Mexican peyote practices and broader Plains religious-cultural exchange networks; James Mooney (Bureau of American Ethnology ethnographer 1861-1921) early ethnographic documentation. The incorporation: Native American Church first incorporated 1918 Oklahoma (Native American Church of Oklahoma) per multiple sources; subsequent NAC institutional expansion across Plains tribes and adjacent Indigenous communities; Native American Church of North America (NACNA) operating as umbrella vehicle. The Mexican-American institutional connection: peyote (Lophophora williamsii) native to Mexico and southwestern United States (Texas Rio Grande Valley); pre-Columbian Indigenous Mexican Wirrarika (Huichol) and Rarámuri (Tarahumara) peyote ceremonial traditions providing institutional-cultural background for North American emergence.

**The American Indian Religious Freedom Act 1978.** Operates as foundational US federal religious-protection framework. The architecture: American Indian Religious Freedom Act (AIRFA) — Public Law 95-341 signed by President Carter 11 August 1978 declaring federal policy of "protect[ing] and preserv[ing] for American Indians their inherent right of freedom to believe, express, and exercise the traditional religions of the American Indian, Eskimo, Aleut, and Native Hawaiians, including but not limited to access to sites, use and possession of sacred objects, and the freedom to worship through ceremonials and traditional rites" per AIRFA text. The 1978 gap: AIRFA framed as policy declaration without enforcement mechanism — producing position that did not protect against subsequent state-level prosecution of NAC peyote use until the 1994 Amendments.

**The 1990 *Employment Division v. Smith* ruling.** Operates as crisis moment for NAC. The architecture: *Employment Division, Department of Human Resources of Oregon v. Smith*, 494 U.S. 872 (1990) — US Supreme Court ruling 6-3 with Justice Scalia majority opinion holding that the First Amendment Free Exercise Clause did not protect Native American Church members from termination of employment and denial of unemployment benefits for peyote use; ruling overturned strict-scrutiny review of religious-burden government actions established in *Sherbert v. Verner* (1963) and subsequent cases. The consequence: AIRFA's policy framework providing no protection against state-level criminal-prosecution and employment-termination consequences for NAC peyote use — producing crisis for NAC traditional practice and broader Native American religious freedom.

**The 1993 Religious Freedom Restoration Act.** Operates as congressional response to *Smith*. The architecture: Religious Freedom Restoration Act (RFRA) — Public Law 103-141 signed by President Clinton 16 November 1993 re-establishing strict-scrutiny review of religious-burden government actions; response by US Congress to *Smith* ruling's narrowing of Free Exercise protections; RFRA applies to federal actions following *City of Boerne v. Flores* (1997) ruling state-application unconstitutional; RFRA institutional framework subsequently invoked across multiple religious-claim contexts including *Burwell v. Hobby Lobby* (2014) and *Gonzales v. O Centro Espirita Beneficente União do Vegetal* (2006, UDV ayahuasca church exemption).

**The 1994 AIRFA Amendments.** Operates as principal contemporary NAC peyote-protection framework. The architecture: American Indian Religious Freedom Act Amendments 1994 — Public Law 103-344 signed by President Clinton 6 October 1994 explicitly protecting "the use, possession, or transportation of peyote by an Indian for bona fide traditional ceremonial purposes in connection with the practice of a traditional Indian religion" by enrolled members of federally recognised tribes per Native News Online and Native American Church reporting; expanded federal trust responsibility framework providing institutional basis for tribe-specific peyote protections. The consequence: 1994 Amendments establishing tribal-specific peyote-protection framework operating distinct from broader RFRA religious-exemption framework — producing question of whether non-tribal religious organisations are entitled to comparable peyote-religious-exemption protections.

**21 CFR § 1307.31 regulatory framework.** Operates as DEA implementation. The architecture: 21 CFR § 1307.31 — federal regulatory exemption stating "the listing of peyote as a controlled substance in Schedule I does not apply to the nondrug use of peyote in bona fide religious ceremonies of the Native American Church, and members of the Native American Church so using peyote are exempt from registration" per New Jersey Administrative Code parallel citation. The consequence: DEA regulatory framework providing operational protection for NAC peyote use — and providing template for subsequent religious-exemption petitions by non-tribal religious organisations.

**The peyote habitat depletion crisis.** Operates as ecological-pressure on NAC tradition. The architecture: Lophophora williamsii natural habitat concentrated in Texas Rio Grande Valley (Webb, Zapata, Starr, Jim Hogg, Brooks counties) per multiple ecological-botanical sources; sustained overharvesting and habitat loss producing significant decline in wild peyote populations over the post-1980s period; commercial peyote suppliers (peyoteros) licensed by DEA operating in declining-supply context; Morning Star Conservancy — NAC-affiliated cultivation vehicle led by Leonardo Mercado (Cultivation Director) — response producing on-site cultivation framework for habitat preservation per Friends Peace Teams 2024 reporting. The consequence: ecological pressure producing pressure on NAC traditional-practice continuity — and producing additional concern about non-NAC peyote demand depleting limited supply available for traditional NAC religious use.

**The "psychedelic churches" institutional challenge.** Operates as principal contemporary pressure on NAC peyote protections. The architecture: multiple religious organisations framed around psychedelic substances seeking DEA religious-exemption registration including Church of Gaia (Spokane, Washington — first ARRA religious exemption issued without DEA lawsuit 2025 per Last Real Indians November 2025 reporting), Church of the Tree of Life, Oklevueha Native American Church (contested positioning), Peyote Way Church of God, and others; pattern of "psychedelic churches" producing certification programs and training to certify "shamans" or traditional practitioners — per Last Real Indians citation of Hershel Walker (Navajo Nation public health official) at NCAI Peyote Working Group 19 November 2025: "This opens the door for many because anyone can self-identify as Indigenous." The contestation: NAC position framing peyote as "federally protected for tribes — not nonprofits, not psychedelic organizations" per Last Real Indians citation: "Peyote is not a drug, it is a sacred covenant for our ceremonial nations." The 2016-2024 DEA petition pattern: 24 religious-exemption petitions received under American Religious Restoration Act (ARRA) framework; 6 reviewed (3 withdrawn, 3 pending review 2026) per Last Real Indians November 2025.

**The 2022 Colorado Natural Medicine Health Act and broader state-level decriminalisation.** Operates as state-level regulatory pressure on federal-NAC institutional framework. The architecture: Colorado Natural Medicine Health Act passed November 2022 — Colorado first US state decriminalising mescaline-bearing plants (including peyote), DMT (in ayahuasca), iboga, and psilocybin for adult use per APL Journeys analysis; regulatory framework for psilocybin therapy effective from late 2024; NAC concerns about consequences for federal peyote-tribal protections framework — concerns that state-level decriminalisation would produce additional pressure on already-limited peyote supply and would erode institutional rationale for tribe-specific federal protection. The pattern: state-level decriminalisation operating distinct from federal NAC protections — producing complexity that NCAI and NACNA advocacy continues to work through 2024-2026.

**The late-2025 / early-2026 DOJ Office of Legal Counsel opinion controversy.** Operates as principal contemporary federal-pressure. The architecture: Department of Justice Office of Legal Counsel memorandum opinion late 2025 / early 2026 — the opinion specifically citing the 1981 "peyote exemption" issued by the Justice Department referring to NAC sacramental use of peyote as "firmly grounded" in NAC history per Buffalo's Fire February 2026 reporting; the DOJ opinion discussed at NCAI Peyote Task Force meeting 10 February 2026 Washington DC. The consequence per Buffalo's Fire: NAC concern that DOJ opinion provides opening for non-tribal peyote-religious-exemption expansion — operating against the 1994 AIRFA Amendments tribal-specific framework. The NCAI response: NCAI Peyote Task Force / Peyote Working Group reignited efforts to strengthen AIRFA protections per Last Real Indians November 2025 reporting; positioning to defend tribe-specific peyote-religious-exemption framework against broader "psychedelic religion" institutional expansion.

The summary of § IV: the peyote-Native American Church contemporary US legal-protection contestation is the principal regulatory-religious-protection configuration in which Indigenous-sacramental-medicine protections face pressure from the broader "psychedelic renaissance" commodification dynamic. The configuration operates through the NAC institutional history from Quanah Parker to 1918 Oklahoma incorporation, the AIRFA 1978 and 1994 Amendments framework, the 1990 *Smith* ruling and 1993 RFRA response, the 21 CFR § 1307.31 federal exemption framework. The peyote habitat depletion crisis in Texas Rio Grande Valley, the contemporary "psychedelic churches" institutional challenge including the November 2022 Colorado decriminalisation framework and the 2025 Church of Gaia ARRA exemption, and the late-2025 / early-2026 DOJ opinion controversy producing NCAI Peyote Working Group response.

Chapter 25 closes here. The evidence covers the principal Indigenous commodification configurations across the New Age commercialisation of Native American spirituality (§ I), ayahuasca tourism and Peruvian regulatory configuration (§ II), sacred-site contestation and Mauna Kea (§ III), and peyote-Native American Church legal-protection contestation (§ IV). The configurations operate as illustrations of structurally analogous patterns of religious-tradition commodification operating through different channels. Commercial-publishing and workshop economy (§ I), international tourism and "psychedelic renaissance" therapeutic frameworks (§ II), state-development pressure on sacred-site protection (§ III), and federal regulatory-religious-protection contestation (§ IV) — producing structurally similar outcomes of Indigenous-religious authority and tradition appropriated and instrumentalised by non-Indigenous actors. Chapter 26 dossiers contemporary religious persecution configurations.

---


## Chapter 26. Contemporary Religious Persecution Configurations

Chapter 26 continues the deferred dossier sequence. The documentation covers the principal contemporary religious-persecution configurations — patterns through which religious-ethnic communities have been subject to systematic state-led or state-tolerated persecution in the contemporary period, with documented atrocities reaching the threshold of crimes against humanity, genocide, or sustained mass-scale persecution. The dossier operates with the same investigative-journalism standards applied across Part III and applies them symmetrically across the dossiered configurations regardless of the perpetrating state, the religious-ethnic identity of the persecuted community, or the geopolitical-diplomatic configuration in which the persecution operates.

The record covers four sections: § I Uyghur Muslims in Xinjiang, China. The contemporary mass-atrocity configuration including the 1+ million detained since 2017, the 31 August 2022 OHCHR Assessment, the contemporary forced-labour-transfer system, and the January 2026 UN Special Rapporteurs statement on potential crimes against humanity of forcible transfer and/or enslavement. § II Rohingya Muslims. The Myanmar genocide configuration and the contemporary protracted refugee situation including the 1982 Burma Citizenship Law statelessness condition, the August 2017 Tatmadaw clearance operations producing 742,000+ refugees to Bangladesh, the ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar case from 11 November 2019, and the post-2024 escalation including around 150,000 new arrivals; § III Yazidi genocide aftermath. The 3 August 2014 ISIS Sinjar attack, the 80+ mass graves, the 6,500+ abducted including 2,500+ still unaccounted for as of mid-2025, the Iraqi 2015 National Card Law perpetuation of harm, the UNITAD closure September 2024, and the contemporary YSL (Yazidi Survivors Law) implementation; § IV Christian persecution global landscape. The Open Doors World Watch List 2026 configuration including 388 million Christians enduring high levels of persecution, the Nigerian 3,490 Christian killings 2026 reporting year (70%+ of global total). The Syria largest-ever WWL single-year increase post-Assad fall, the sub-Saharan Africa 14-15 countries in WWL top 50, the China September 2025 Religious Clergy regulations, and the broader architecture of US IRFA 1998 and USCIRF reporting.

The evidence operates against any framing that would treat religious persecution as distinct depending on the perpetrating state or the religious-ethnic identity of the persecuted community. The patterns dossiered here operate as illustrations of structurally analogous persecution operations — through state-led mass detention (§ I), state-led ethnic-religious cleansing operations (§ II), non-state-actor genocide operations (§ III), and sustained militant-and-state persecution across multiple geographies (§ IV) — producing structurally similar outcomes of mass killing, displacement, and institutional erasure of religious-ethnic communities. What follows covers these patterns with the same evidentiary standards applied across the volume.

### I. Uyghur Muslims in Xinjiang, China: the Contemporary Mass-Atrocity Configuration

The Uyghur persecution in Xinjiang is the principal contemporary state-led mass-atrocity configuration targeting a Muslim community in the post-2017 period. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the around 11 million Turkic-speaking Uyghur population in Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR). (2) the 2017 onward mass-detention configuration including more than one million Uyghurs in "Vocational Education and Training Centers". (3) the 31 August 2022 OHCHR Assessment by then-departing UN High Commissioner Michelle Bachelet concluding that violations "may constitute international crimes, in particular crimes against humanity". (4) the documented practices including forced labour. Forced sterilizations, family separations, AI-driven surveillance, mosque demolitions, and restrictions on Uyghur language and Islamic practice. (5) the state-level genocide determinations including US January 2021, UK House of Commons April 2021. Canadian House of Commons February 2021, and Dutch parliament February 2021. (6) the contemporary January 2026 five-UN-Special-Rapporteurs statement and the May 2025 joint civil-society investigation documenting expanded labor-transfer system.

**The Uyghur community.** The architecture: around 11 million Turkic-speaking predominantly Muslim ethnic group in Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region (XUAR) — the largest of China's province-level administrative divisions, occupying northwestern China; long-standing Uyghur cultural-religious tradition including Sunni Islamic practice, Uyghur language (Turkic language family), and connections to broader Central Asian Turkic communities including Kazakh, Kyrgyz, and Uzbek populations; per Council on Foreign Relations October 2025: "More than eleven million Uyghurs—a mostly Muslim, Turkic-speaking ethnic group—live in the northwestern region of Xinjiang." Pre-2017 Uyghur population existed under sustained Chinese state pressure including the "Strike Hard" campaigns against perceived separatism and religious extremism; post-2014 escalation following Kunming and Ürümqi attacks attributed to Uyghur separatists.

**The post-2017 mass-detention configuration.** Operates as principal state-led atrocity vehicle. The architecture: 2017 onward Chinese government detention of more than one million Uyghurs and other predominantly Muslim minorities (Kazakhs, Kyrgyz, Hui) in what Chinese state framing calls "Vocational Education and Training Centers" and external observers call "re-education camps" or mass internment facilities per CFR October 2025 backgrounder. The consequence: by 2019 multiple investigative reports estimated 1-2 million detained; UN Committee on the Elimination of Racial Discrimination August 2018 statement noting "credible reports" of mass arbitrary detention. The documented practices: mass arbitrary detention without due process; forced labour both in detention facilities and through "labor transfer" programs relocating Uyghur workers to factories across China; forced sterilizations and birth-control mandates targeting Uyghur women per Adrian Zenz research; family separations producing children placed in state boarding schools; intense surveillance including AI-driven facial recognition systems, integrated joint operations platform (IJOP), and Han-resident "homestay" assignments; demolition of mosques and Islamic cultural sites including the historic Kashgar old city; restrictions on Uyghur language education and Islamic practice including fasting during Ramadan, beard-wearing, and veil-wearing per CFR + multiple investigative reports.

**The Xinjiang Police Files May 2022.** Operates as principal documentary evidence release. The architecture: Xinjiang Police Files — leaked photographs and documents from Xinjiang public-security database obtained from hack of police computers in Konasheher (Shufu) and Tekes counties; analysis by Adrian Zenz (Victims of Communism Memorial Foundation senior fellow); published May 2022 by BBC, Der Spiegel, USA Today, ABC Australia, and other outlets; documents included thousands of photographs of detainees, internal security speeches, and detailed lists of detention numbers. The consequence: Xinjiang Police Files providing principal contemporary documentary corroboration of mass-detention system framed by Chinese state as "vocational training" — internal documents showing armed-guard architecture and shoot-to-kill orders for escapees inconsistent with Chinese state "vocational" framing.

**The 31 August 2022 OHCHR Assessment.** Operates as principal UN determination. The architecture: OHCHR (UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights) Assessment of human rights concerns in the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region released 31 August 2022 by then-departing UN High Commissioner Michelle Bachelet — released as Bachelet completed her term per Wikipedia UN Human Rights Office report on Xinjiang. The finding: per Amnesty International citation, the assessment concluded "serious human rights violations" in the Uyghur region "may constitute international crimes, in particular crimes against humanity." Per RFE/RL September 2022, US Secretary of State Antony Blinken statement 1 September 2022: the report "authoritatively described China's 'appalling treatment' of Uyghurs and other minorities" and "deepens and reaffirms our grave concern regarding the ongoing genocide and crimes against humanity that [Chinese] government authorities are perpetrating against Uyghurs." The consequence: OHCHR Assessment producing principal UN-system finding without subsequent UN Human Rights Council action — October 2022 vote to put Xinjiang on Council agenda narrowly defeated 19-17 with 11 abstentions per HRW August 2024 reporting.

**The state-level genocide determinations.** Operates as parallel positioning by Western democracies. The architecture: US January 2021 outgoing Trump administration Pompeo determination labelling Chinese treatment as genocide; Biden administration March 2021 confirmation of genocide designation; UK House of Commons motion 22 April 2021 declaring genocide per Inquirer News reporting; Canadian House of Commons February 2021 declaring genocide; Dutch parliament February 2021 declaring genocide; Czech, Lithuanian, Belgian, French parliamentary determinations subsequent. The consequence: pattern of Western parliamentary genocide determinations operating distinct from UN Human Rights Council position — producing contestation in which Chinese state framing of Western "interference" operates against the multilateral positions.

**The Uyghur Tribunal December 2021.** Operates as independent finding. The architecture: Uyghur Tribunal (London) — independent tribunal initiated 2020 by Uyghur Human Rights Project and World Uyghur Congress; chaired by Sir Geoffrey Nice KC (former lead prosecutor at International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia in Slobodan Milošević trial); December 2021 judgment finding genocide and crimes against humanity. The consequence: parallel non-state finding providing vehicle for genocide framing operating outside state-government channels.

**The contemporary forced-labour configuration.** Operates as principal post-2022 configuration. The architecture: US Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) December 2021 establishing rebuttable presumption that goods produced wholly or in part in Xinjiang involve forced labour; EU Forced Labor Regulation adopted November 2024 prohibiting import, export, and sale of products made with forced labor in EU market; UK Modern Slavery Act enforcement framework; multiple national-level frameworks. The May 2025 joint civil-society investigation: per Global Centre for the Responsibility to Protect — "China's labor transfer system is more widespread than previously understood, with some Uyghur workers relocated over 2,000 miles from the region." The 22 January 2026 five-UN-Special-Rapporteurs statement: "expressed grave concern over persistent allegations of forced labor affecting Uyghur, Kazakh, Kyrgyz and Tibetan communities in the Uyghur Region and elsewhere in China, noting that the severity of coercion in some cases may amount to the crimes against humanity of forcible transfer and/or enslavement" per Global Centre for the Responsibility to Protect. The Chinese state planning documents: official Chinese planning documents per Global R2P showed Xinjiang's five-year plan (2021 to 2025) projected 13.75 million labor transfers — illustrating systematic state-led labor-transfer scale.

**The contemporary advocacy configuration.** The architecture: August 2024 OHCHR update — engaging with Chinese authorities; "many problematic laws and policies remain in place" per IPI June 2025 analysis; August 2025 Amnesty International "Free Xinjiang Detainees" campaign — contacted families and sources for 126 individuals featured per Tribune India August 2025 + Amnesty August 2025; European Parliament emergency resolution October 2024 condemning persecution; Amsterdam City Parliament July 2025 motion using "East Turkestan" rather than "Xinjiang" per CFR October 2025. The sources: World Uyghur Congress (Rebiya Kadeer founding figure; Dolkun Isa current president); Uyghur Human Rights Project; East Turkestan National Awakening Movement; Campaign for Uyghurs — advocacy vehicles operating across diaspora communities.

The summary of § I: the Uyghur persecution in Xinjiang is the principal contemporary state-led mass-atrocity configuration targeting a Muslim community. The configuration operates through the around 11 million Uyghur population in XUAR, the 2017 onward mass-detention of more than one million in "Vocational Education and Training Centers," the May 2022 Xinjiang Police Files documentary release. The 31 August 2022 OHCHR Assessment by Michelle Bachelet finding "may constitute international crimes, in particular crimes against humanity," the state-level genocide determinations by US (January 2021). UK (April 2021), Canada (February 2021), Netherlands (February 2021), and other parliaments, the December 2021 Uyghur Tribunal judgment, the contemporary forced-labour configuration including UFLPA December 2021 and EU Forced Labor Regulation November 2024, the May 2025 joint civil-society investigation, and the 22 January 2026 five-UN-Special-Rapporteurs statement. The Rohingya configuration dossiered in § II operates as parallel illustration of contemporary mass-atrocity targeting a Muslim community — operating through state-led ethnic-religious cleansing operations producing the world's largest stateless-refugee population in Bangladesh's Cox's Bazar camps.

---


### II. Rohingya Muslims: the Myanmar Genocide Configuration and the Protracted Refugee Situation

The Rohingya persecution is the principal contemporary state-led ethnic-religious cleansing configuration producing the world's largest stateless-refugee population. The architecture has seven principal nodes: (1) the Rohingya as predominantly Muslim ethnic minority indigenous to Rakhine State (formerly Arakan). Western Myanmar. (2) the 1982 Burma Citizenship Law producing institutional statelessness condition. (3) the August 2017 Tatmadaw "clearance operations" producing 742. 000+ refugees to Bangladesh by July 2019. (4) the Cox's Bazar refugee camps configuration including 1.1+ million refugees across 33 camps. (5) the ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar case from 11 November 2019 and the UN IIFFMM finding genocide; (6) the post-2021 Tatmadaw coup configuration including the Independent Investigative Mechanism for Myanmar (IIMM) reports; (7) the post-2024 escalation producing around 150,000 new arrivals and the contemporary humanitarian funding collapse.

**The Rohingya community.** The architecture: Rohingya — predominantly Muslim ethnic minority indigenous to Rakhine State (formerly Arakan), western Myanmar; longstanding institutional presence with Sunni Islamic religious tradition, Rohingya language (Indo-Aryan, related to Chittagonian Bengali), and cultural-historical connections to broader Bengal region and Arakan kingdom; pre-2017 Rohingya population in Myanmar estimated around 1 million; pattern of sustained Myanmar state pressure including denial of citizenship rights, restrictions on marriage, movement, and religious practice, and recurrent expulsion operations in 1978 (Operation Naga Min producing 200,000+ refugees to Bangladesh) and 1991-1992 (producing 250,000+ refugees to Bangladesh) per Yale MacMillan and Rohingya Human Rights Center.

**The 1982 Burma Citizenship Law.** Operates as foundational architecture of statelessness. The architecture: 1982 Burma Citizenship Law under Ne Win military government establishing three-tier citizenship framework — full citizens (members of indigenous "national races"), associate citizens, and naturalized citizens; Rohingya not included among the 135 recognized "national races" producing institutional denial of citizenship rights per Rohingya Human Rights Center; consequence of statelessness condition affecting all subsequent Rohingya generations. Per Rohingya Human Rights Center: "Systematically denied citizenship under Myanmar's 1982 Citizenship Law and subjected to repeated waves of violence, they were forced into a mass exodus in 2017."

**The August 2017 Tatmadaw clearance operations.** Operates as principal contemporary atrocity event. The architecture: 25 August 2017 ARSA (Arakan Rohingya Salvation Army) attacks on around 30 Myanmar Border Guard Police posts and one Tatmadaw army base in northern Rakhine State; Tatmadaw response framed as "clearance operations" producing systematic mass killings, sexual violence, and village burnings across northern Rakhine State August-September 2017; over 742,000 Rohingya refugees fled to Bangladesh by July 2019 per Yale MacMillan Refugee Program; an estimated 6,700+ Rohingya killed in the first month including 730+ children under 5 per MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières) survey; documented practices: systematic village burnings, mass killings, sexual violence against Rohingya women including gang rape, mutilation, and forced disappearance of Rohingya men. The UN IIFFMM finding: UN Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar reports September 2018 and August 2019 found Tatmadaw operations against Rohingya constituted crimes against humanity and acts of genocide; specifically named senior Tatmadaw leadership including Min Aung Hlaing (then-Commander-in-Chief, subsequently 2021 coup leader) as bearing principal responsibility.

**The Cox's Bazar refugee camps configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary displacement architecture. The architecture: Cox's Bazar refugee camps in Ukhia and Teknaf sub-districts, Chittagong Division, Bangladesh — world's largest refugee settlement; 33 extremely congested camps per IOM Bangladesh Crisis Response Plan 2024 analysis; over 1.1 million Rohingya refugees as of contemporary counts per UNHCR + COMPAS November 2025 analysis. Per Dr Mohammad Tarikul Islam (COMPAS November 2025): "With more than one million stateless refugees now living in Cox's Bazar, Bangladesh, long-term displacement has generated consequences that extend far beyond immediate humanitarian needs — affecting both the Rohingya refugees and Bangladeshi host communities." The consequence: protracted refugee situation (PRS) configuration with full Rohingya dependence on humanitarian aid; Bangladesh government Bhasan Char relocation site as supplementary settlement architecture; restrictions on Rohingya employment, residence outside camps, and movement producing condition of indefinite dependency.

**The ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar case.** Operates as principal international-law accountability vehicle. The architecture: The Gambia v. Myanmar (Application of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide) — case filed at International Court of Justice 11 November 2019 by The Gambia (with support of Organisation of Islamic Cooperation member states) alleging Myanmar violations of 1948 Genocide Convention; ICJ Provisional Measures Order 23 January 2020 ordering Myanmar to take measures to prevent acts of genocide and preserve evidence; ICJ Preliminary Objections judgment 22 July 2022 dismissing Myanmar objections and finding jurisdiction; merits proceedings continuing 2023-2026. Cross-reference Chapter 23 § I for parallel framing of Myanmar Buddhist Captured Configurations. The consequence: principal contemporary international-law accountability vehicle for Rohingya genocide framing.

**The post-2021 Tatmadaw coup configuration.** Operates as context shaping contemporary Rohingya situation. The architecture: Tatmadaw 1 February 2021 coup against Aung San Suu Kyi National League for Democracy government (cross-reference Chapter 23 § I); Min Aung Hlaing leading State Administration Council; subsequent civil-war escalation across Myanmar; consequence: the leadership responsible for August 2017 Rohingya clearance operations (Min Aung Hlaing) became the leadership of post-coup Myanmar. The Independent Investigative Mechanism for Myanmar (IIMM): established UN Human Rights Council Resolution 39/2 (September 2018); ongoing evidence-collection mandate; August 2024 IIMM Annual Report per UNRIC analysis finding "extensive evidence that brutal war crimes and crimes against humanity committed by the Myanmar military have escalated at an alarming rate across the country." The 27 May 2025 Volker Türk briefing: UN High Commissioner for Human Rights briefing to Human Rights Council Geneva — "Since the military coup in Myanmar in February 2021, nearly 6,800 civilians have been killed and over 22,000 remain arbitrarily detained" per UNRIC.

**The post-2024 escalation.** Operates as contemporary acute-phase configuration. The architecture: around 150,000 new Rohingya arrivals in Bangladesh since early 2024 — fleeing escalating Myanmar conflict including Arakan Army operations in Rakhine State and Tatmadaw counter-operations per UNHCR + UN Refugees + Rohingya Human Rights Center July 2025 analysis. Per UN Refugees January 2026 analysis: "While the overwhelming majority of Rohingya refugees fled persecution and violence in Myanmar in 2017, around 150,000 were forced to flee the country since early 2024." The consequence: largest Rohingya displacement wave since 2017 — operating against background of contracting humanitarian funding configuration including US aid cuts following USAID dismantling under Trump second-term administration per Rohingya Human Rights Center 1 July 2025 Reuters reporting; UNHCR appeal funding collapse to 35% per 11 July 2025 UNHCR statement.

**The 11 July 2025 UNHCR humanitarian funding statement.** Operates as principal contemporary humanitarian-system warning. The architecture: per Rohingya Human Rights Center July 2025 analysis of UNHCR 11 July 2025 statement — "its US$255 million appeal for Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh is only 35% funded." The consequence: humanitarian funding collapse producing ration cuts, healthcare service reductions, and shelter shortages affecting over 1.1 million Rohingya refugees in Cox's Bazar; institutional warning of "one of their gravest crises yet" per Rohingya Human Rights Center. The UN Secretary-General António Guterres March 2025 Cox's Bazar visit: "stark reminder of the world's collective failure to find solutions" per UN News September 2025.
