The 28 March 2025 Myanmar earthquake compounding factor: 7.7 magnitude Myanmar earthquake centered Sagaing region producing around 3,800 deaths per UNRIC — adding to humanitarian burden across an already-collapsing Myanmar civil-conflict context.

**The repatriation impossibility.** Operates as central contradiction. The architecture: per UN News September 2025 Guterres framing — "the primary solution is the safe, voluntary and dignified return of Rohingya refugees to Myanmar"; position requiring conditions of "conflict and systematic persecution end" which "do not yet exist, making returns impossible for now." The consequence: Rohingya refugees trapped between (1) impossible return to Myanmar without ending of state-led persecution architecture, (2) increasingly constrained humanitarian conditions in Bangladesh Cox's Bazar camps, (3) Bangladesh pressure on host communities and political-cycle pressure on government to reduce refugee burden, and (4) absence of third-country resettlement capacity at scale comparable to refugee population — producing dead-end configuration with no near-term resolution pathway.

The summary of § II: the Rohingya persecution is the principal contemporary state-led ethnic-religious cleansing configuration producing the world's largest stateless-refugee population. The configuration operates through the Rohingya community indigenous to Rakhine State, the 1982 Burma Citizenship Law institutional statelessness, the August 2017 Tatmadaw clearance operations producing 742,000+ refugees and an estimated 6,700+ killed including 730+ children under 5, the Cox's Bazar camps configuration with 1.1+ million refugees across 33 camps, the ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar case from 11 November 2019 with January 2020 Provisional Measures Order and July 2022 Preliminary Objections judgment, the UN IIFFMM 2018-2019 finding genocide, the IIMM ongoing evidence collection, the post-2021 Tatmadaw coup with Min Aung Hlaing State Administration Council. The post-2024 around 150,000 new arrivals, the 11 July 2025 UNHCR humanitarian funding collapse to 35% of appeal, the 27 May 2025 Volker Türk briefing on 6,800+ civilians killed and 22,000+ detained, and the repatriation impossibility producing dead-end configuration. The Yazidi genocide aftermath dossiered in § III operates as illustration of non-state-actor genocide configuration — operating through ISIS 2014 architecture against an indigenous ethnoreligious minority — and the contemporary aftermath including the YSL implementation, the UNITAD closure September 2024, and the 2015 National Card Law perpetuation of harm.

---


### III. Yazidi Genocide Aftermath and the Continuing Institutional Persecution

The Yazidi genocide aftermath is the principal contemporary non-state-actor genocide configuration with continuing institutional persecution operating through aftermath patterns. The architecture has seven principal nodes: (1) the Yazidis as ancient ethnoreligious minority indigenous to northwest Iraq with community of around 400,000-500. 000 pre-2014. (2) the 3 August 2014 ISIS attack on Sinjar producing the genocidal campaign. (3) the documented atrocities including 80+ mass graves. 6,500+ abducted, and the Qiniyeh, Kocho. And Jdali massacres. (4) the Iraqi 2015 National Card Law producing perpetuation of harm against Yazidi children born of ISIS captivity. (5) the accountability vehicles including US House March 2016 genocide resolution. UNITAD (UN Security Council Resolution 2379, September 2017), and the Yazidi (Female) Survivors Law (YSL) March 2021. (6) the September 2024 UNITAD closure without successor mechanism. (7) the contemporary 2024-2026 configuration including the December 2024 IDP camp closure plans, the April 2025 Real Estate Registration ownership letters, and the May 2025 600+ IDPs returning to Shingal.

**The Yazidi community.** The architecture: Yazidis (also Yezidis, Êzîdîs) — ancient ethnoreligious minority indigenous to northwest Iraq; principal communities in Sinjar (Shingal) region of Nineveh Governorate and Sheikhan district; community of around 400,000-500,000 pre-2014 per OHCHR August 2024; Yazidi religious tradition centered on veneration of Melek Taus (Peacock Angel) as principal angelic figure; seven holy angels framework; sacred mountain Lalish and Lalish Temple as principal pilgrimage and ceremonial centre; history of recurrent persecution across Ottoman, Iraqi monarchical, and Baathist periods including 73 documented historical "ferman" (decrees of extermination) per Yazidi community institutional memory. The pre-2014 condition: vulnerable indigenous minority with no state-protection apparatus comparable to majority Sunni Arab, Shia Arab, or Kurdish populations of Iraq; dependence on Kurdish Peshmerga forces for security in Sinjar region 2003-2014; position of statelessness-adjacent vulnerability comparable to Rohingya configuration dossiered in § II.

**The 3 August 2014 ISIS Sinjar attack.** Operates as principal atrocity event. The architecture: 3 August 2014 ISIS forces attacked Sinjar region following retreat of Kurdish Peshmerga forces from Sinjar without notification to Yazidi civilian population per multiple analyses including USCIRF 2024 Iraq Genocide Issue Brief and Nadia's Initiative; ISIS classifying Yazidis as "heretical devil worshippers" subject to elimination per ISIS theological framework articulated in *Dabiq* magazine and other ISIS publications; per PBS Frontline March 2025: "The self-proclaimed Islamic State of Iraq and Syria considered Yazidis, who are mostly of Kurdish descent, to be heretical devil worshippers. In August 2014, shortly after ISIS declared a caliphate, they attacked Sinjar, the northern Iraqi Yazidi homeland." The consequence: ISIS attack producing the principal contemporary genocide event against an indigenous ethnoreligious community.

**The documented atrocities.** The architecture: per Chaldean News August 2024 and Nadia's Initiative analyses — "An estimated 200,000 Yazidi civilians fled for their lives, with at least 50,000 heading to Sinjar Mountain, where they were trapped in the scorching summer heat for days without food or water"; large-scale massacres of Yazidi men and boys at villages of Qiniyeh, Kocho, and Jdali — ISIS systematic killing of men over age 12 and elderly women; women and children enslaved and transported to Syria to be sold as sex slaves and sexually exploited by ISIS fighters per ICCT July 2024 analysis; 80+ mass graves identified throughout Sinjar region per Nadia's Initiative. Per ICCT: "Tens of thousands of Yazidi civilians were forced to flee from their homes with little warning and few supplies, resulting in hundreds dying of dehydration and exposure on Mount Sinjar. Those who were captured by ISIS were separated into groups, with Yazidi men being executed in large numbers in front of their families. Hundreds of elderly women and children were killed, including some who were buried alive. The remaining women and children were enslaved and transported to Syria to be sold as sex slaves." Per OHCHR August 2024: ISIS members "subjected Yazidi women and girls to enslavement, torture, inhuman treatment, murder and rape, including through sexual slavery, as part of their genocidal campaign." The abduction figures: 6,500+ Yazidis abducted by ISIS; around 2,500 remain unaccounted for as of mid-2024 per EU Agency for Asylum + IBAHRI analyses.

**The 2015 National Card Law perpetuation.** Operates as illustration of state-law perpetuation of atrocity harm. The architecture: per Michelle Xiao Liu, "The Enduring Genocide Against the Yazidis: How Iraq's Law on Religious Identity Violates the Human Rights of Yazidi Survivors of ISIS Captivity and Their Children Born of Sexual Violence," 23 Nw. J. Hum. Rts. 75 (2025) — Iraq's 2015 National Card Law operating under Iraqi patrilineal nationality framework producing condition that children born of Yazidi women raped by ISIS classified as Muslim, not Yazidi. Per Nadia's Initiative on ISIS genocidal architecture: "Raping women to ensure that children born to Yazidi women would be considered Muslim, not Yazidi, under Iraq's patrilineal nationality law." Per Liu Northwestern Journal analysis: "In hindering these women from returning to their homeland of Sinjar with their children and in preventing this new generation from carrying on the Yazidi religion, the National Card Law violates their fundamental human rights under international treaties and the Iraqi Constitution and perpetuates the devastating effects of ISIS's genocide against the Yazidis." The consequence: Iraqi state-law architecture operating to perpetuate Yazidi genocidal harm through state-imposed religious classification of children of ISIS sexual-violence survivors — illustrating pattern in which post-genocide state architecture continues genocidal harm patterns through facially-neutral legal frameworks.

**The genocide recognitions.** Operates as accountability architecture. The architecture: US House of Representatives 14 March 2016 unanimous resolution finding ISIS crimes against religious minorities in Iraq and Syria constitute genocide per USCIRF 2024 Iraq Genocide Issue Brief; subsequent US State Department determination 17 March 2016 under Secretary John Kerry; UN Commission of Inquiry on the Syrian Arab Republic findings of genocide; European Parliament 4 February 2016 resolution; UK House of Commons 20 April 2016 resolution; Council of Europe Parliamentary Assembly 2016; Canada, Australia, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg parliamentary genocide determinations; OHCHR 3 August 2024 Commission of Inquiry on the Syrian Arab Republic position paper calling for justice on 10th anniversary. The consequence: comprehensive multilateral institutional genocide recognition without comparable prosecution-and-accountability architecture — pattern of recognition-without-accountability paralleling the Uyghur and Rohingya configurations dossiered above.

**The UNITAD configuration and September 2024 closure.** Operates as principal contemporary international-accountability vehicle and its collapse. The architecture: Investigative Team to Promote Accountability for Crimes Committed by Da'esh/ISIL (UNITAD) — established by UN Security Council Resolution 2379 (September 2017) requested by Government of Iraq; mandate to collect, preserve, and store evidence of ISIS atrocities to support prosecutions; sustained evidence-collection operation 2018-2024 including mass-grave exhumations, witness testimony preservation, and forensic analysis. The September 2024 closure: per IBAHRI analysis on 10th anniversary — "In September 2024, the Investigative Team to Promote Accountability for Crimes Committed by Da'esh/ISIL (UNITAD) – the mechanism established to collect and preserve evidence of the Daesh atrocities — is to close. There is no indication whether the evidence collected so far will be used, or how, nor is there sign of whether anyone will take over this important work, which includes the exhumation of mass graves." The consequence: principal contemporary international-accountability vehicle closing without successor mechanism — producing gap in ongoing prosecution and evidence-preservation architecture.

**The Yazidi (Female) Survivors Law March 2021.** Operates as Iraqi response. The architecture: Yazidi (Female) Survivors Law (YSL) — Iraqi Council of Representatives legislation passed March 2021 providing recognition of genocide against Yazidi women and other religious-minority women; monthly compensation; housing/land plot or free housing unit eligibility; education and rehabilitation programs per ICCT July 2024 analysis. Per ICCT: "The YSL also provides for eligible survivors to be granted a residential plot of land with a real estate loan or a free housing unit. On the 29th of May 2024, the Directorate distributed the first 250 plots to Yazidi survivors in Sinjar." The implementation problems: per EU Agency for Asylum analysis — "Despite the 2023 federal budget allocating 25 billion Iraqi dinars [around €16.4 million] to support the Yazidi Female Survivors Act, effective disbursement remains limited due to difficulties stemming from missing documentation, lack of identification papers, and logistical barriers"; per Coalition for Just Reparations C4JR March 2024: "More than 'ink on paper'" — pattern of legislative recognition without effective implementation.

**The contemporary 2024-2026 configuration.** Operates as principal moment of Yazidi crisis. The architecture: December 2024 Iraqi federal government plan to close IDP (internally displaced persons) camps for Yazidi community and other religious-minority IDPs and pressure returns to Sinjar without security guarantees per IBAHRI; contradiction with Sinjar region's continuing security vulnerability including ISIS sleeper cells, Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF) presence, PKK-affiliated YBŞ (Sinjar Resistance Units) presence, and Turkish drone-strike operations against PKK-affiliated targets producing recurrent civilian casualties. The April 2025 Real Estate Registration: per EU Agency for Asylum — "Following the recognition by the Iraqi government of the Yazidi land rights in 2022, in April 2025 the Real Estate Registration Department authorities in Sinjar launched the distribution of 1,338 ownership letters and issued around 100 title deeds to Yazidi residents in Dugrey." The May 2025 Shingal returns: per Rudaw and EU Agency for Asylum — "Although over 600 Yazidis returned to Sinjar from Duhok governorate in May 2025, most have not received the promised return grant of 4 million dinars [around €2,624] nor the household items pledged by the government." The advocacy vehicles: Nadia Murad (Nobel Peace Prize 2018 with Denis Mukwege) Nadia's Initiative; Free Yezidi Foundation; Yazda; Sinjar Academy; Coalition for Genocide Response — advocacy operating across 2024-2026 period through UN Human Rights Council Universal Periodic Review submissions and other advocacy channels.

The summary of § III: the Yazidi genocide aftermath is the principal contemporary non-state-actor genocide configuration with continuing institutional persecution operating through aftermath patterns. The configuration operates through the Yazidi community of around 400,000-500,000 pre-2014 indigenous to Sinjar region of northwest Iraq, the 3 August 2014 ISIS attack producing the genocidal campaign. The documented atrocities including 80+ mass graves and 200. 000 fleeing with 50,000 trapped on Mount Sinjar and massacres at Qiniyeh, Kocho, and Jdali, the 6,500+ abducted with 2,500+ still unaccounted for, the 2015 National Card Law perpetuation of harm against children of Yazidi women raped by ISIS. The US House March 2016 genocide resolution and parallel parliamentary determinations, the UNITAD September 2017 establishment and September 2024 closure without successor mechanism, the YSL March 2021 implementation with limited effective disbursement, the December 2024 Iraqi federal government IDP camp closure plans, the April 2025 1,338 ownership letters distribution, and the May 2025 600+ IDPs return to Shingal. The Christian persecution global configuration dossiered in § IV operates as illustration of contemporary persecution configuration affecting the largest persecuted religious community globally — operating through the Open Doors World Watch List 2026 architecture including 388 million Christians enduring high persecution and the documented 3,490 Nigerian Christian killings in 2026 reporting year.

---


### IV. Christian Persecution: the Contemporary Global Landscape and the Open Doors World Watch List Configuration

The Christian persecution global landscape is the principal contemporary configuration of sustained large-scale religious persecution affecting the world's largest religious community. The architecture has eight principal nodes: (1) the Open Doors World Watch List (WWL) as principal contemporary documentation vehicle producing annual ranking of 50 countries. (2) the WWL 2026 release 14 January 2026 documenting 388 million Christians enduring high levels of persecution globally. (3) the WWL 2026 top 10 led by North Korea (24th consecutive year, score 97). (4) the Nigerian configuration with 3. 490 Christian killings in 2026 reporting year representing 70%+ of global total. (5) the Syria post-Assad fall configuration producing largest single-year WWL increase. (6) the sub-Saharan Africa 14-15 countries top-50 concentration. (7) the China September 2025 Religious Clergy online regulations producing China's highest-ever WWL score. (8) the US architecture including International Religious Freedom Act 1998, USCIRF reporting, and Ambassador-at-Large for International Religious Freedom Trump second-term restoration 2025.

**The Open Doors World Watch List.** Operates as principal contemporary Christian-persecution documentation vehicle. The architecture: Open Doors International — founded 1955 by Brother Andrew (Anne van der Bijl, 1928-2022) initially as Bible-distribution vehicle into Eastern Bloc; subsequent institutional expansion into Christian-persecution documentation and advocacy; WWL methodology measures violent incidents and pressure across five "spheres of life" (private, family, community, national, church) per Open Doors documentation; verification by International Institute for Religious Freedom (IIRF) producing methodological rigour framework. The WWL position: annual ranking of 50 countries where Christians face most persecution; release annually in January; vehicle cited across Vatican News, USCIRF, US State Department International Religious Freedom Reports, and broader multilateral channels.

**The WWL 2026 release 14 January 2026.** Operates as principal contemporary documentation. The architecture: per Baptist Press 14 January 2026 — "Violence against Christians is at an all-time high in 15 nations including Nigeria and Syria, Open Doors International said Jan. 14 in releasing its annual World Watch List of the 50 most dangerous countries for believers"; 388 million Christians enduring high levels of persecution and discrimination globally per Baptist Press + Vatican News, up from 380 million in 2025 reporting year per Word&Way January 2025; per Open Doors CEO Ryan Brown: "It's because the Church is advancing. That's why the Church is coming under attack." The WWL 2026 top 10 per Baptist Press + Vatican News + Open Doors UK: 1. North Korea (24th consecutive year, score 97); 2. Somalia; 3. Yemen; 4. Sudan; 5. Eritrea; 6. Syria (largest single-year increase in WWL history); 7. Nigeria; 8. Pakistan; 9. Libya; 10. Iran. The WWL 2025 top 10 (release January 2025) per Word&Way + Open Doors UK: North Korea, Somalia, Yemen, Libya, Sudan, Eritrea, Nigeria, Pakistan, Iran, Afghanistan.

**The Nigerian configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary single-country atrocity scale within Christian persecution. The architecture: per Baptist Press January 2026 — "more Christians were killed in the 2026 study period for their faith than in all other nations combined, 3,490 deaths in Nigeria among a global tally of 4,849. The numbers are up from the 2025 report, which counted 3,100 deaths in Nigeria among a global count of 4,476." Per Vatican News January 2026: "Nigeria is confirmed as the epicenter of the violence, with 3,490 victims, about 70 percent of the global total of people killed." The principal militant groups: Boko Haram, ISWAP (Islamic State West Africa Province), JNIM (Jama'a Nusrat ul-Islam wa al-Muslimin), Fulani ethnic militia, Lakurawa, and the emergence in 2025 of new group Mahmuda per Baptist Press 2026 analysis. The context per Open Doors analysis cited in Baptist Press: "The country is beset by a severe combination of ethno-religious hostility, Islamist militancy, weak governance and organized crime." Per Open Doors UK 2025: "Nigeria remains one of the most dangerous places for Christians, ranking 7th. Attacks by Fulani militia and jihadist groups continue, with Christians disproportionately affected." Per Word&Way January 2025 citing Observatory for Religious Freedom in Africa: "30,880 civilians have been killed in Nigeria from 2020 to 2023, including 22,360 Christians and 8,315 Muslims."

**The Syria post-Assad fall configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary acute-change configuration. The architecture: per Open Doors UK 2026 analysis — "[Syria is] the biggest riser in this year's list and now a place of extreme persecution for many of the country's tiny minority of believers. This is largely down to a sharp rise in the number of Christians killed and churches attacked since the fall of the Assad regime; the suicide bomb at Saint Elias Church, Damascus, in June that killed 22 people." Bashar al-Assad regime collapse December 2024 producing Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS, led by Ahmed al-Sharaa formerly Abu Mohammed al-Jolani) Syrian Caretaker Government; post-Assad context producing rising attacks on Syrian Christian minorities including Eastern Orthodox, Catholic, and Protestant communities. Per Vatican News January 2026 citing Open Doors official Nani: "Based on our data, only 300,000 Christians remain in Syria—that is, hundreds of thousands fewer than ten years ago." The Saint Elias Church Damascus bombing June 2025: 22 killed in suicide bombing at Saint Elias Church Damascus per Open Doors UK 2026; consequence of post-Assad-fall institutional vulnerability of Syrian Christian communities.

**The sub-Saharan Africa concentration.** Operates as principal contemporary regional concentration. The architecture: per Open Doors UK 2025 — "Fourteen countries in the top 50 of the World Watch List are in sub-Saharan Africa, and four of these are in the top 10 (Somalia, Eritrea, Sudan and Nigeria). A key driver is weak governance — for example, five of the 14 countries have experienced government overthrows in the past five years"; per Word&Way January 2025: "All 15 countries of the sub-Saharan region were among the top 50 list, and 13 had 'extremely high' persecution scores." Per Open Doors 2026 trends: "In 2026, there are only three countries on the World Watch List with the maximum score for violence. All three of them are sub-Saharan: Sudan, Nigeria, and Mali. Multiple complex co-factors in the region, ranging from instability and insurgency to weak governance, have left Christians vulnerable and subject to violent attacks from extremists." The Sudan WWL trajectory: moved from 5th (2025) to 4th (2026) WWL ranking — driven by civil-war intensification from 15 April 2023; 7.7 million internally displaced by mid-2024 per Open Doors Hong Kong; cross-reference Chapter 30 § III for Sudanese civil war framing.

**The China and India configurations.** Operate as principal large-population non-African configurations. The architecture: China — WWL highest-ever score 2026 per Open Doors UK; September 2025 implementation of "Regulations on the Online Behaviour of Religious Clergy" producing institutional tightening across Chinese religious-clergy online communication; pattern cross-referencing the Uyghur persecution configuration dossiered in § I — Chinese state pressure operating across religious-minority communities including but not limited to Muslims. India (WWL 12 in 2026 per Vatican News, prior years higher rankings): Hindu nationalist mobilisation operating cross-referenced with Chapter 22 § III Hindu Captured Configurations; anti-conversion legislation across multiple BJP-ruled states; Manipur violence 2023-2024 affecting Kuki Christian community; pattern of sustained Hindutva-aligned violence against Christian congregations producing 700+ documented incidents annually per United Christian Forum India tracking. Per Open Doors WWL methodology, India ranking driven by combination of violence and "squeeze" (pressure across spheres of life) — illustrating pattern of pressure operating without state-led atrocity at scale of Nigerian or Sudanese situations.

**The global persecution figures.** The architecture: per Word&Way January 2025 — "The report also counted 209,771 Christians displaced because of their faith and 54,780 Christians physically or mentally abused, 10,000 of whom lived in Pakistan. In total, 1 in 7 Christians faced 'high-level' persecution." Per Vatican News January 2026 on 2026 reporting year: 4,849 Christians killed globally in 2026 reporting period (October 2024-September 2025) — "13 a day." The sources for figures: Open Doors research methodology operating across around 100 countries through "experts on the ground"; verification by International Institute for Religious Freedom (IIRF); per Open Doors position cross-referenced with USCIRF Annual Reports, US State Department International Religious Freedom Reports, and Pew Research Center "Global Restrictions on Religion" reports producing convergent documentation of mass-scale Christian persecution.

**The US architecture.** Operates as principal Western-state framework. The architecture: International Religious Freedom Act (IRFA) 1998 — Public Law 105-292 signed by President Clinton 27 October 1998; established US Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF) as independent bipartisan federal entity; established Office of International Religious Freedom at State Department; established Ambassador-at-Large for International Religious Freedom position. USCIRF Annual Reports: annual documentation including Country of Particular Concern (CPC) designations and Special Watch List designations producing vehicle for state-level diplomatic engagement on religious-persecution issues. The Trump second-term restoration 2025: institutional restoration of Ambassador-at-Large position following Biden-administration tenure; positioning aligned with Open Doors and broader US evangelical-Protestant advocacy networks. The pattern: US architecture operating as principal Western-state advocacy vehicle for global Christian-persecution documentation and diplomatic engagement — operating distinct from but cross-referenced with European Union frameworks and broader multilateral channels including UN Human Rights Council Special Rapporteur on Freedom of Religion or Belief.

**The Open Doors Arise Africa campaign.** Operates as principal contemporary response architecture. The architecture: per Open Doors UK 2026 — "Open Doors' Arise Africa campaign remains crucial in raising awareness, support and prayer, including through a petition aimed at raising one million signatures"; multi-year response to sub-Saharan persecution; positioning to enable African church to "stand in faith where persecution is most extreme" per Open Doors HK 2025. The consequence: principal contemporary international-advocacy campaign operating in sub-Saharan Christian-persecution context — analogue to Yazidi advocacy through Nadia's Initiative dossiered in § III and to Uyghur advocacy through World Uyghur Congress dossiered in § I.

The summary of § IV: the Christian persecution global landscape is the principal contemporary configuration of sustained large-scale religious persecution affecting the world's largest religious community. The configuration operates through the Open Doors World Watch List 2026 release 14 January 2026 documenting 388 million Christians enduring high persecution globally, the WWL 2026 top 10 led by North Korea (24th consecutive year, score 97) followed by Somalia. Yemen, Sudan, Eritrea, Syria, Nigeria, Pakistan, Libya, and Iran, the Nigerian configuration with 3,490 Christian killings in 2026 reporting year representing 70%+ of global total perpetrated by Boko Haram + ISWAP + JNIM + Fulani militia + Lakurawa + Mahmuda. The Syria post-Assad fall configuration with largest single-year WWL increase including Saint Elias Church Damascus bombing June 2025 killing 22, the sub-Saharan Africa 14-15 countries top-50 concentration with three maximum-violence-score countries (Sudan, Nigeria, Mali), the China September 2025 Religious Clergy online regulations producing highest-ever WWL score. The India WWL 12 with Hindu nationalist mobilisation cross-referenced with Chapter 22, the global figures of 4,849 killed and 209,771 displaced and 54,780 abused, and the US architecture including IRFA 1998 and USCIRF and the Trump second-term restoration of Ambassador-at-Large 2025.

Chapter 26 closes here. The evidence covers the principal contemporary religious-persecution configurations across the Uyghur mass-atrocity configuration in Xinjiang (§ I), the Rohingya genocide configuration in Myanmar with protracted refugee situation in Bangladesh (§ II), the Yazidi genocide aftermath with continuing institutional persecution (§ III), and the Christian persecution global landscape through the Open Doors WWL configuration (§ IV). The configurations operate as illustrations of structurally analogous persecution operations — through state-led mass detention (§ I), state-led ethnic-religious cleansing operations (§ II), non-state-actor genocide operations (§ III), and sustained militant-and-state persecution across multiple geographies (§ IV) — producing structurally similar outcomes of mass killing, displacement, and institutional erasure of religious-ethnic communities. The evidence dossiers these patterns with the same evidentiary standards applied across the volume. Chapter 27 dossiers Other Captured Configurations completing Part III.

## Chapter 27. Other Captured Configurations

Chapter 27 concludes the Part III dossier sequence. The documentation covers the principal captured configurations that did not fit within the major tradition-specific dossiers of Chapters 14-26 — patterns through which religious-architectures have been bound to political-party vehicles, state-government configurations, foreign-state intelligence operations, or mass-mobilisation electoral infrastructure operating across geographies and traditions not dossiered above. The dossier operates with the same investigative-journalism standards applied across Part III and applies them symmetrically across the dossiered configurations regardless of geography, tradition, or geopolitical alignment.

The record covers four sections: § I African Pentecostal-political fusion configurations. The prosperity gospel architecture, the Nigerian "mega-church" pastor continental weight, the Kenya / Uganda / South Africa / Zimbabwe alignments, and the TB Joshua / SCOAN April 2024 BBC institutional documentation. § II Brazilian and Latin American evangelical-political configurations. The Igreja Universal do Reino de Deus (IURD) founded 9 July 1977 by Edir Macedo, the Frente Parlamentar Evangélica (FPE) "Bancada Evangélica" with 219 deputies and 26 senators 2025. The 25 February 2025 Gilberto Nascimento FPE presidency election, the Bolsonaro-evangelical alignment 2018-2022, the Republicanos party IURD-aligned configuration, and the broader Latin American configurations including Guatemala, El Salvador, Costa Rica, and Mexico. § III Russian Orthodox Church satellite configurations. The Belarusian Orthodox Church (BOC) exarchate from 16 October 1989, the Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa established 29 December 2021, the post-2022 Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) rupture. The Serbian and Georgian Orthodox configurations, and the 2024 Swedish Security Service investigation of Russian intelligence-operations exploitation of ROC vehicles. § IV Southeast Asian captures: Iglesia ni Cristo (INC) and Catholic-political configurations in the Philippines. The INC founded 27 July 1914 by Felix Manalo with current Executive Minister Eduardo V. Manalo, the "command voting" / bloc-voting architecture with 2,806,524 members per 2020 Census, the January 2025 and November 2025 INC mass-mobilisation rallies with 650,000 attendees, the Kingdom of Jesus Christ (KOJC) Quiboloy 8 September 2024 arrest configuration, the Catholic Bishops' Conference of the Philippines (CBCP) positioning, and the broader Duterte / Marcos political-religious configuration.

The argument operates against any framing that would treat these as marginal or peripheral configurations. Patterns dossiered here operate as central illustrations of religious-political capture occurring in geographies and traditions outside the principal-dossier configurations of preceding chapters — but operating through structurally analogous architectures of political-religious fusion, electoral capture, and state-aligned positioning. What follows covers these patterns with the same evidentiary standards applied across the volume.

### I. African Pentecostal-Political Fusion Configurations

The African Pentecostal-political fusion is the principal contemporary religious-political capture configuration across sub-Saharan Africa. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the post-1970s explosive growth of Pentecostal Christianity producing 200+ million African Pentecostals by mid-2020s. (2) the prosperity gospel theological framework articulating material wealth as evidence of divine favour. (3) the Nigerian "mega-church" pastor continental weight with David Oyedepo. Enoch Adeboye, TB Joshua, and Chris Oyakhilome as principal figures. (4) The Kenya William Ruto presidency from 13 September 2022 alignment with African Inland Church and Pentecostal-evangelical networks. (5) the Uganda Yoweri Museveni alignment with Pentecostal-evangelical networks including the 29 May 2023 Anti-Homosexuality Act. (6) the TB Joshua / SCOAN April 2024 BBC investigation "Disciples: The Cult of TB Joshua" documenting torture, sexual abuse, and forced abortions allegations.

**The post-1970s African Pentecostal growth.** Operates as principal contemporary religious-demographic shift. The architecture: post-1970s explosive growth of Pentecostal and charismatic Christianity across sub-Saharan Africa per multiple scholarly analyses including Paul Gifford *African Christianity: Its Public Role* (Hurst, 1998) and Ruth Marshall *Political Spiritualities: The Pentecostal Revolution in Nigeria* (University of Chicago Press, 2009); estimated 200+ million African Pentecostals by mid-2020s per Pew Research Center estimates; demographic transition from mission-Protestant and Catholic-majority Christianity in many sub-Saharan countries to Pentecostal-charismatic majority within Christian community. Per Theologia Viatorum (2022) on Kenya configuration: "after President Daniel arap Moi left the political scene as the president of Kenya on 30 December 2002, the political role of African Pentecostals increased." The pattern: the explosive Pentecostal growth producing structural conditions for the political-religious capture configurations dossiered below.

**The prosperity gospel theological framework.** Operates as principal theological architecture of African Pentecostal-political alignment. The architecture: prosperity gospel — theological framework articulating material wealth as evidence of divine favour; institutional roots in US Word of Faith movement (Kenneth Hagin 1917-2003, Kenneth Copeland born 1936, Oral Roberts 1918-2009); subsequent African transmission via Nigerian Pentecostal pastor networks from 1980s onward per Ebenezer Obadare HTS Teologiese Studies (2016) and broader scholarly literature. Per ResearchGate analysis: "some Pentecostal church pastors use manipulative tactics by preaching sermons on seed sowing as the only means to attract financial breakthroughs, protection or healing from God and then ask for large sums of money from their congregants who are already struggling financially." Per Nimi Wariboko *Nigerian Pentecostalism* (2014) cited in HTS Teologiese Studies: Nigerian Pentecostals "struggling to raise the global profile of blackness, reshaping the identity of Africans and reconceptualising Christian Africanness" and "develop[ing] Pentecostal spirituality as the best ground and lens for interpreting, evaluating, and guiding economic policy and for producing a new ethic of economic development."

**The Nigerian "mega-church" pastor continental weight.** Operates as principal contemporary African religious-architecture. The architecture: David Oyedepo (born 27 September 1954) — Living Faith Church Worldwide (Winners' Chapel) founded 1981; Lagos-Ota mega-church configuration including 50,400-capacity Faith Tabernacle; Covenant University founder. Enoch Adeboye (born 2 March 1942) — Redeemed Christian Church of God (RCCG) General Overseer from 1981; institutional expansion to 196+ countries; monthly RCCG Holy Ghost Service drawing hundreds of thousands; principal Nigerian Pentecostal architect. Chris Oyakhilome — Christ Embassy / Believers' LoveWorld; transnational televangelism architecture. The continental weight per Obadare HTS Teologiese Studies (2016): "no disputing, for instance, the visibility and influence of the country's leading Pentecostal pastors across the continent, especially in Pentecostal hotspots like Ghana, Zimbabwe, South Africa, Uganda and Kenya. Here and elsewhere, magnetic Nigerian pastors circulate freely, galvanising a continental spiritual economy in which devotional principles and modalities are freely traded. As a matter of fact, it is widely held that most of what passes for African Pentecostalism today traces its roots back to an original Nigerian source." The consequence: Nigerian pastor architecture operating as continental religious-political vehicle producing influence in election cycles across multiple African states.

**The Nigerian Pentecostal-political endorsement architecture.** Operates as principal contemporary Nigerian configuration. The architecture: per Obadare *Pentecostal Republic* (2018) and subsequent corpus — Nigerian Pentecostal pastor endorsement weight in election cycles including Muhammadu Buhari (2015, 2019) and Bola Tinubu (2023 election); pastor endorsement positioning in northern Christian-belt swing-state configuration; position of Pentecostal pastors as kingmakers in Nigerian electoral architecture. Per ResearchGate citation: "Recognising the pivotal role of Pentecostal constituencies, political players, such as candidates in presidential elections seek mega-church leaders' blessings or a favourable political prophecy." The pattern: Nigerian Pentecostal-political endorsement architecture cross-references the Christian persecution global Nigerian configuration dossiered in Chapter 26 § IV — operating in institutional parallel to the Boko Haram, ISWAP, JNIM, Fulani militia, Lakurawa, and Mahmuda militant configurations as contesting architectures of Nigerian religious-political life.

**The Kenya William Ruto configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary East African evangelical-political configuration. The architecture: William Ruto (born 21 December 1966) — Kenyan politician; previously Deputy President 2013-2022 under Uhuru Kenyatta; elected President 13 September 2022; self-identified "evangelical Christian"; alignment with African Inland Church (AIC) and broader Pentecostal-evangelical networks; "Bible-in-hand" presidential framing; constructed presidential prayer chapel at State House Nairobi 2024 producing contestation per Kenyan civil-society objection. The 2024-2025 protest crisis configuration: Finance Bill protests June 2024 producing Gen Z protest movements; 25 June 2024 storming of Kenyan Parliament; crisis producing Ruto cabinet reshuffles July 2024 and continuing 2025 protest cycles. The pattern: Kenyan Pentecostal-evangelical alignment operating in contestation with secular and Catholic-mainline-Protestant civil-society configurations including National Council of Churches of Kenya (NCCK) and Kenya Conference of Catholic Bishops (KCCB) positioning.

**The Uganda Museveni configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary East African anti-LGBT religious-political configuration. The architecture: Yoweri Museveni (born 15 September 1944) — Ugandan politician; President from 26 January 1986 — 39+ years; alignment with Pentecostal-evangelical networks including Martin Ssempa (Makerere Community Church) and other figures; Uganda Anti-Homosexuality Act 29 May 2023 (cross-reference Chapter 28 § II for broader cross-tradition coordination architecture). The pattern: Ugandan Pentecostal-evangelical alignment operating in coordination with US evangelical institutional networks including World Congress of Families, Family Watch International, and other vehicles producing the Anti-Homosexuality Act framework.

**The TB Joshua / SCOAN BBC investigation April 2024.** Operates as principal contemporary documentation of Nigerian Pentecostal institutional abuse architecture. The architecture: Temitope Balogun Joshua (TB Joshua, 1963-2021) — Synagogue Church of All Nations (SCOAN); Lagos-based; healer-prophet framing; estimated 100+ million YouTube views during life; international religious-tourism vehicle drawing pilgrims from across Africa, Latin America, and beyond; June 2021 death producing succession crisis. The April 2024 BBC investigation: "Disciples: The Cult of TB Joshua" — three-part documentary series; investigation by BBC Eye Investigations team based on testimony from over 25 former SCOAN members spanning decades; documented torture, sexual abuse including rape of teenage girls, forced abortions, and broader institutional abuse architecture per BBC reporting. The consequence: April 2024 BBC documentation producing reckoning across global SCOAN community; broader question of accountability mechanisms operating across Nigerian Pentecostal mega-church configuration.

The summary of § I: the African Pentecostal-political fusion is the principal contemporary religious-political capture configuration across sub-Saharan Africa. The configuration operates through the post-1970s explosive growth producing 200+ million African Pentecostals, the prosperity gospel theological framework, the Nigerian "mega-church" pastor continental weight with David Oyedepo / Enoch Adeboye / TB Joshua / Chris Oyakhilome as principal figures. The Kenya William Ruto presidency alignment with Pentecostal-evangelical networks, the Uganda Museveni configuration including the 29 May 2023 Anti-Homosexuality Act, and the April 2024 BBC "Disciples: The Cult of TB Joshua" institutional documentation. The Brazilian and Latin American evangelical-political configurations dossiered in § II operate as parallel illustration of Pentecostal-evangelical political capture in distinct national-contexts — operating through the Igreja Universal do Reino de Deus architecture, the Frente Parlamentar Evangélica electoral vehicle, and the broader Bolsonaro-era alignment.

---


### II. Brazilian and Latin American Evangelical-Political Configurations

The Brazilian evangelical-political configuration is the principal contemporary Latin American religious-political capture architecture. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the post-1970s explosive growth of Brazilian evangelical Christianity producing over 30% of the population by 2022. (2) the Igreja Universal do Reino de Deus (IURD) founded 9 July 1977 by Edir Macedo with subsequent expansion to 100+ countries and the Rede Record media architecture. (3) the Frente Parlamentar Evangélica (FPE) "Bancada Evangélica" with 219 deputies and 26 senators in 2025. (4) the Republicanos party IURD-aligned configuration with 44 federal deputies. (5) the Bolsonaro-evangelical alignment 2018-2022 with 62% / 66% / 69% evangelical voter shares across the three principal polling moments. (6) the broader Latin American configurations including Guatemala, El Salvador, Costa Rica, and Mexico.

**The Brazilian evangelical demographic shift.** Operates as principal demographic foundation. The architecture: Brazilian evangelical Christianity — post-1970s explosive growth from around 5% of population in 1970 to over 30% of the population by 2022 per Datafolha and multiple Brazilian-Census-based estimates; projected to surpass Catholic share by 2032 per José Eustáquio Diniz Alves (former IBGE researcher) cited in Al Jazeera 2022; configuration producing structural transition of Brazilian religious-political life from Catholic-majority to evangelical-Catholic-bipartite configuration. Per Aeon Essays January 2026 analysis: "After Bolsonaro left office..." and the broader positioning of evangelical voting blocs through post-Bolsonaro period. The pattern: Brazilian evangelical-political configuration operating as principal Latin American illustration of post-Cold War evangelical political capture.

**The Igreja Universal do Reino de Deus (IURD).** Operates as principal Brazilian neo-Pentecostal vehicle. The architecture: IURD (Universal Church of the Kingdom of God) — founded 9 July 1977 by Edir Macedo in Rio de Janeiro; positioning at intersection of neo-Pentecostal theology and prosperity gospel architecture; subsequent expansion to 100+ countries including Portugal, Angola, South Africa, USA, UK. Edir Macedo (born 18 February 1945) — IURD founder; owner of Rede Record (second-largest Brazilian TV network); estimated billionaire status per Forbes estimates. Per Aeon Essays January 2026: "Macedo also owns Record, the second-biggest channel in Brazil, which gave Bolsonaro plenty of free airtime." The 2021 Angola configuration: IURD pastor deportations from Angola July 2021 producing Brazil-Angola diplomatic incident; Itamaraty (Brazilian Foreign Ministry) intervention with FPE leadership pressure on Bolsonaro administration per Folha de São Paulo May 2021 + Deutsche Welle July 2021. The Marcelo Crivella position: Crivella is Macedo's nephew; former IURD bishop; Mayor of Rio de Janeiro 2017-2020; nominated as Ambassador to South Africa 2021 then withdrawn after IURD-Angola incident per Folha November 2021.

**The Frente Parlamentar Evangélica (FPE) / Bancada Evangélica.** Operates as principal contemporary Brazilian evangelical-political vehicle. The architecture: Frente Parlamentar Evangélica — Evangelical Parliamentary Front; registered 9 September 2015 per Wikipedia FPE; loosely-organised cross-party caucus of evangelical lawmakers in Brazilian National Congress; 219 federal deputies + 26 senators in 2025 representing over one-third of Chamber of Deputies (513 seats total) per Rio Times February 2025; would be third-largest party in Brazilian government if formally counted per Wikipedia. The 25 February 2025 FPE leadership election: Gilberto Nascimento (PSD-SP) elected FPE president with 117 votes defeating Otoni de Paula (MDB-RJ) with 61 votes per Rio Times February 2025; Nascimento backed by former president Jair Bolsonaro and influential pastor Silas Malafaia; Greyce Elias (Avante-MG) withdrew as third candidate supporting Nascimento and assumed vice-presidency. The consequence per Rio Times: Nascimento election "underscores the bloc's continued alignment with Bolsonaro-style conservatism" producing institutional complication for Lula's evangelical engagement strategy. Per Atlas Institute June 2025: "A significant level of [evangelical churches] provided direct voting guidance to congregants. Statistics from the 2022 elections show that 28% of evangelicals received voting suggestions directly from their churches, compared to 13% among Catholics."

**The Republicanos party IURD-aligned configuration.** Operates as principal evangelical-aligned political-party vehicle. The architecture: Republicanos (Republicans of Brazil) — political party founded 2005; vehicle of IURD per Aeon Essays January 2026: "The Republicanos party, founded in 2005, is a creature of the IURD"; current president Marcos Pereira (former IURD bishop, held position in Michel Temer administration after Dilma Rousseff impeachment 2016); 44 federal deputies per Aeon analysis. The consequence: integration of IURD organisational architecture with formal political-party vehicle operating across Brazilian electoral architecture. The Silas Malafaia configuration: Assembleia de Deus Vitória em Cristo founder; principal evangelical political broker; key Bolsonaro institutional supporter; 2025 positioning aligned with FPE Nascimento leadership.

**The Bolsonaro-evangelical alignment 2018-2022.** Operates as principal contemporary illustration of evangelical-political capture. The architecture: Jair Bolsonaro presidency 2019-2022 — first Brazilian president openly aligned with evangelical institutional networks. The 2018 election evangelical voter share: nearly 70% of evangelical voters supported Bolsonaro in 2018 first round per Rio Times February 2025; consequence producing Bolsonaro evangelical-aligned cabinet including Damares Alves (Minister for Women, Family and Human Rights 2019-2022) and André Mendonça (Justice Minister and later Supreme Court appointment). The 2022 election evangelical voter share: 62% first round / 66% second round per Revista Mexicana de Opinión Pública 2025; 69-31 split in final 2022 pre-election poll per Aeon Essays. The 2022 second round outcome: Lula 50.9% to Bolsonaro 49.1%; margin of 2,139,645 votes out of 118,552,353 total per Revista Mexicana 2025 — narrow institutional defeat against background of overwhelming evangelical bloc support for Bolsonaro. Per Aeon Essays: "During the COVID-19 pandemic, Bolsonaro's denial of the severity of the virus was, in part, a demonstration of evangelical coronafé, or corona-faith." The Bolsonaro administration evangelical-media subsidy: per Atlas Institute June 2025 — "Bolsonaro's administration spent over R$30 million in public funds on evangelical media outlets, particularly on radio and television stations owned by supportive pastors."

**The Lula third-presidency engagement configuration.** Operates as contemporary contestation. The architecture: Lula da Silva third presidency from 1 January 2023 following 2022 election victory; institutional engagement with evangelical leaders producing limited success; per Rio Times February 2025 — "56% disapproving of his presidency" among evangelical voters as of February 2025 Datafolha; positioning of Lula administration to engage evangelical institutional networks while sustaining the broader Workers' Party (PT) progressive-coalition base. The contradiction: Lula positioning seeking to neutralise evangelical-bloc opposition while sustaining commitments on abortion access, LGBT rights, and other issues producing opposition from FPE leadership.

**The "evangelical foreign policy" architecture.** Operates as Brazilian foreign-policy configuration. The architecture: per Third World Quarterly (2023) — Brazilian evangelical foreign-policy model defined by convergence of views and interests between conservative cabinet members and domestic evangelical groups on four foreign-policy issues critical to evangelicals worldwide: relationship with Israel, persecution of Christian minorities, abortion rights, and evangelical missions in Africa. The consequence: "In the case of Brazil, this is the first time that typical religious considerations have been officially adopted by the country's foreign policy" per Third World Quarterly database analysis of 207 FPE parliamentarian speeches + 1,992 discourses from Bolsonaro cabinet members January 2019-July 2022. Cross-reference Chapter 28 § II for parallel cross-tradition coordination architecture.

**The broader Latin American configurations.** Operate as parallel illustrations of evangelical-political capture across Latin American contexts. The architecture: Guatemala — Jimmy Morales (President 2016-2020) Pentecostal-evangelical aligned; Bernardo Arévalo (President from 14 January 2024) Movimiento Semilla alignment producing contestation against entrenched evangelical-conservative architecture; ongoing 2025-2026 crisis. Costa Rica — Fabricio Alvarado 2018 presidential first-round leader of National Restoration Party; institutional emergence of evangelical-political vehicle in historically Catholic-majority country; 2025 December configuration cross-referenced with Chapter 28 § II. El Salvador — Nayib Bukele (President from 1 June 2019, re-elected 1 June 2024) alignment with broader Latin American Christian-conservative configuration despite secular framing. Mexico — Andrés Manuel López Obrador (AMLO, President 2018-2024) alignment with Encuentro Solidario party evangelical-aligned coalition; Claudia Sheinbaum (Morena, President from 1 October 2024) positioning operating against evangelical-conservative architecture.

The summary of § II: the Brazilian evangelical-political configuration is the principal contemporary Latin American religious-political capture architecture. The configuration operates through the post-1970s Brazilian evangelical demographic shift to over 30% of the population, the IURD founded 9 July 1977 by Edir Macedo with Rede Record media architecture. The Frente Parlamentar Evangélica "Bancada Evangélica" with 219 deputies and 26 senators 2025 led by Gilberto Nascimento from 25 February 2025, the Republicanos party IURD-aligned configuration with Marcos Pereira presidency. The Bolsonaro-evangelical alignment 2018-2022 with 62%/66%/69% evangelical voter shares and the R$30+ million evangelical-media subsidy, the Lula third-presidency engagement configuration with 56% evangelical disapproval as of February 2025, the "evangelical foreign policy" architecture, and the broader Latin American configurations including Guatemala, El Salvador, Costa Rica, and Mexico. The Russian Orthodox Church satellite configurations dossiered in § III operate as parallel illustration of religious-political capture operating through a different architecture — the Eastern Orthodox autocephalous-and-exarchate framework operating as vehicle for Russian state foreign-policy projection across post-Soviet, African, and broader diaspora geographies.

---


### III. Russian Orthodox Church Satellite Configurations: Belarusian, Post-Soviet, and African Expansion

The Russian Orthodox Church (ROC) satellite configuration is the principal contemporary illustration of religious-architecture operating as vehicle for state foreign-policy projection across multiple geographies. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the ROC core position under Patriarch Kirill aligned with Putin regime (cross-reference Chapter 18 Russian Orthodox capture and Chapter 30 § II Russian-Ukrainian war). (2) the Belarusian Orthodox Church (BOC) exarchate from 16 October 1989 with 1. 737 parishes and 17 bishops as of 2024. (3) the Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa established 29 December 2021 with Metropolitan Constantine from 11 October 2023. (4) the post-2022 Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) rupture cross-referenced with Chapter 30. (5) the Serbian, Georgian, and broader Orthodox-autocephalous alignments. (6) the 2024 Swedish Security Service investigation of Russian intelligence-operations exploitation of ROC vehicles.

**The ROC core position.** Operates as principal contemporary religious-vehicle aligned with Russian state. The architecture: Russian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) — vehicle from 988 Kievan Rus' Christianization through Russian Empire, Soviet suppression, post-1991 Russian Federation reconstruction; Patriarch Kirill (Vladimir Mikhailovich Gundyayev, born 20 November 1946) — Patriarch from 1 February 2009; position aligned with Putin regime including February 2022 invasion of Ukraine support including 6 March 2022 sermon framing war as "metaphysical" struggle; subsequent World Russian People's Council (WRPC) 27-28 March 2024 declaration of Ukraine war as "holy war" cross-referenced with Chapter 30 § II. The pattern: ROC positioning as principal religious-vehicle of Russian state foreign-policy projection — operating through architecture distinct from but functionally parallel to direct state diplomatic vehicles. The DECR (Department of External Church Relations) vehicle: ROC foreign-relations operations vehicle; Metropolitan Anthony of Volokolamsk current chairman; positioning across post-Soviet, African, Middle Eastern, and broader diaspora communities per DECR / mospat.ru institutional reporting.

**The Belarusian Orthodox Church (BOC) exarchate.** Operates as principal post-Soviet ROC satellite configuration. The architecture: Belarusian Orthodox Church — exarchate of Russian Orthodox Church; autonomy granted by Moscow Patriarchate 16 October 1989 per Wikipedia BOC; current Patriarchal Exarch Metropolitan Veniamin (Vital Tupieka) from 2020; architecture per Wikipedia BOC — 1,737 parishes (2024), 17 bishops (2024), 36 monasteries (2024), 15 dioceses (2024), 1,676 priests (2019); claims 81% of Belarusian Christian population. The consequence: BOC operating as principal religious-vehicle in Belarus under sustained Lukashenko regime configuration 1994-present; dependence on ROC architecture parallel to Belarusian political dependence on Russian Federation. Per Church Times August 2025: ROC reinforcing presence in Belarus — "Moscow Synod resists hints of Belarusian separatism." The 2020 Belarusian protests context: post-2020 Belarusian protest cycle following contested August 2020 presidential election; BOC positioning aligned with Lukashenko government against protest movement; pattern of religious-alignment with authoritarian state apparatus during crisis.

**The Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa.** Operates as principal contemporary ROC institutional expansion vehicle. The architecture: Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa — ROC vehicle established 29 December 2021 Moscow; current Metropolitan Constantine (Ostrovsky) since 11 October 2023 per Wikipedia Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa; configuration including 2 dioceses (formally); headquarters Cairo, Egypt (de jure); Moscow, Russia (de facto); territorial framing 30,370,000 km² covering all of Africa; languages including English, French, Church Slavonic, and local languages including Swahili, Afrikaans, Malagasy, Tiv, Kirundi, Kinyarwanda, Kikuyu, Tiriki, Lingala; positioning as ROC African expansion vehicle in competition with Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria; condemnation by Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria and Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople. The consequence: ROC African expansion positioning aligned with broader Russian foreign-policy expansion in Africa including Wagner Group / Africa Corps operations in Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Central African Republic, Sudan cross-referenced with Chapter 30 § III on Sudanese civil-war configuration. The contestation: 2019 precipitating event — Patriarch Theodore II of Alexandria recognition of Orthodox Church of Ukraine (OCU) autocephaly triggering ROC retaliation through Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa establishment 2021; pattern of ROC retaliation against Orthodox communions recognising OCU.

**The post-2022 Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) rupture.** Operates as principal post-invasion reconfiguration. The architecture: Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) — UOC-MP; history of "self-rule within the Moscow Patriarchate" from 1990 per Wikipedia UOC-MP; 8,097 parishes (May 2024) per Wikipedia; 12,551 clerics (2022); 4,620 monastics; 161 monasteries (2022). The 27 May 2022 institutional declaration: UOC-MP council declaration of "self-governing" status protesting February 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine and particularly Patriarch Kirill's support for invasion per Wikipedia UOC-MP. The contradiction per Wikipedia: "The UOC did not (and has never) declared full autocephaly from the Russian Orthodox Church... As of 2025, its leadership is also still published in the ROC's calendar." The 24 March 2023 Ukrainian state action: Ukrainian government action against UOC-MP framework producing eviction proceedings from Kyiv Pechersk Lavra and other historic monasteries; subsequent August 2024 Ukrainian Verkhovna Rada legislation prohibiting Russian-affiliated religious organisations; position of UOC-MP contested between (1) ROC reality, (2) Ukrainian state position framing UOC-MP as Russian vehicle, and (3) Orthodox Church of Ukraine (OCU) granted tomos from Ecumenical Patriarchate January 2019 as alternative. Cross-reference Chapter 30 § II for broader Russian-Ukrainian war configuration.

**The Serbian Orthodox Church configuration.** Operates as principal non-Moscow Slavic-Orthodox configuration aligned with Russian positioning. The architecture: Serbian Orthodox Church — vehicle of Serbian national-religious identity; Patriarch Porfirije (born 22 July 1961) from 19 February 2021; alignment with Aleksandar Vučić government (President from 31 May 2017); pro-Russian positioning during Ukraine war producing tension with Western institutional partners; position on Kosovo question producing sustained opposition to 2008 Kosovo independence declaration; pattern of Serbian Orthodox Church operating as principal vehicle of Serbian national-religious identity in contestation with Albanian-Kosovo position. The 2024-2025 Serbian configuration: ongoing contestation between Vučić government and student-led protest movements producing crisis; Serbian Orthodox Church positioning aligned with Vučić government through 2024-2025 protest cycle.

**The Georgian Orthodox Church configuration.** Operates as distinctive autocephalous-Orthodox configuration in post-Soviet space. The architecture: Georgian Orthodox Church — vehicle of Georgian national-religious identity; Catholicos-Patriarch position; Ilia II (born 4 January 1933) extended illness; recent succession to Metropolitan Shio of Senaki and Chkhorotsku as Primate per Russian Orthodox Church DECR; positioning during Georgian Dream government 2024-2025 crisis including December 2024 EU-accession reversal triggering sustained protest movement; contestation between Georgian Orthodox Church positioning aligned with Georgian Dream government and broader Georgian civil-society pro-EU positioning.

**The 2024 Swedish Security Service investigation.** Operates as principal contemporary documentation of Russian intelligence-operations institutional exploitation of ROC vehicles. The architecture: per Wikipedia Russian hybrid warfare — "In 2024, the Swedish Security Service concluded that a recently consecrated Russian Orthodox church built in Västerås was linked to Russian intelligence operations, adding that the Putin regime uses the Russian Orthodox Church in Sweden as a platform for such operations." The pattern: parallel investigations across Western European jurisdictions producing documented position that ROC architecture in Western Europe operates as cover for Russian intelligence operations; consequence of producing contestation across European jurisdictions over ROC positioning. The broader contemporary configuration: Russia hybrid warfare architecture per Wikipedia documentation including October 2024 MI5 head Ken McCallum warning of Russian military intelligence campaign to "generate mayhem"; October 2025 French intelligence services Holocaust Memorial vandalism Paris May 2024 attribution to Russian-paid proxies; December 2025 Lithuania state of emergency following Belarus-launched balloon campaign; February 2026 Swedish Armed Forces drone identification — broader context of Russian hybrid warfare operating across multiple vehicles including ROC architecture.

The summary of § III: the Russian Orthodox Church satellite configuration is the principal contemporary illustration of religious-architecture operating as vehicle for state foreign-policy projection across multiple geographies. The configuration operates through the ROC core position under Patriarch Kirill aligned with Putin regime, the Belarusian Orthodox Church exarchate from 16 October 1989 with 1,737 parishes as of 2024, the Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa established 29 December 2021 with Metropolitan Constantine. The post-2022 Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate) rupture with 8. 097 parishes contested between ROC reality and Ukrainian state action, the Serbian and Georgian Orthodox configurations during contemporary protest-cycle institutional crises, and the 2024 Swedish Security Service investigation documenting ROC architecture exploitation by Russian intelligence operations. The Southeast Asian captures dossiered in § IV operate as final illustration of religious-political capture in distinct national-contexts — the Philippines Iglesia ni Cristo and Catholic-political configurations producing illustration of indigenous Filipino restorationist Christian capture alongside Catholic Church positioning during contemporary 2024-2025 crisis.

---


### IV. Southeast Asian Institutional Captures: Iglesia ni Cristo and Catholic-Political Configurations in the Philippines

The Philippines religious-political configuration is the principal contemporary Southeast Asian illustration of religious-architecture operating across national electoral and mass-mobilisation life. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Catholic Church Philippines as predominant religious-vehicle through Catholic Bishops' Conference of the Philippines (CBCP); (2) the Iglesia ni Cristo (INC) founded 27 July 1914 by Felix Manalo with current Executive Minister Eduardo V. Manalo and 2,806,524 members per 2020 Census. (3) the INC "command voting" / bloc-voting architecture. (4) the January 2025 and November 2025 INC mass-mobilisation rallies including the 16-17 November 2025 "Rally for Transparency and a Better Democracy" with 650. 000 attendees. (5) the Kingdom of Jesus Christ (KOJC) Apollo Quiboloy 8 September 2024 arrest configuration. (6) the broader Duterte / Marcos political-religious configuration including the Rodrigo Duterte 11 March 2025 ICC-warrant arrest and the Sara Duterte impeachment configuration.

**The Catholic Church Philippines configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary Philippines religious-vehicle. The architecture: Catholic Church Philippines — predominant religious-vehicle; over 80% of Filipino population identifying as Catholic per 2020 Philippine Statistics Authority Census; Catholic Bishops' Conference of the Philippines (CBCP) vehicle; positioning across post-1986 People Power transition, Estrada-Arroyo period, Aquino-Duterte period, and contemporary Marcos-Duterte period; pattern of CBCP positioning operating from independent religious-position rather than political-party-aligned framing. The Parish Pastoral Council for Responsible Voting (PPCRV): CBCP vehicle for election-monitoring; deployed 450,000 volunteers for May 2025 midterm polls monitoring per International Christian Concern May 2025. The contemporary CBCP positioning: November 2025 CBCP statement to Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP) to "defend the constitution amidst challenging times" per Wikipedia November 2025 INC protests; cautions against "political exploitation" ahead of INC November 2025 protests and Zaldy Co video statement context.

**The Iglesia ni Cristo architecture.** Operates as principal Filipino indigenous restorationist Christian vehicle. The architecture: Iglesia ni Cristo (INC, Church of Christ) — Filipino indigenous restorationist Christian denomination; founded 27 July 1914 by Felix Manalo (1886-1963); architecture including succession from Felix Manalo to Eraño G. Manalo (1925-2009, son) and then current Executive Minister Eduardo V. Manalo (born 31 May 1955, grandson) from 7 September 2009 per records. The architecture per Inquirer May 2025: 2,806,524 members per 2020 Philippine Statistics Authority Census representing 2.6% of country population; presence concentrated in National Capital Region (NCR), Region III (Central Luzon), and other urbanised regions; vehicle Philippine Arena (Bocaue, Bulacan) — 55,000-capacity stadium inaugurated 21 July 2014 for INC centennial as world's largest indoor arena. The INC theological framework: restorationist nontrinitarian Christology distinct from both Catholic and Protestant institutional traditions; doctrinal positioning producing separation from broader Philippine Christian ecumenical configurations.

**The INC "command voting" / bloc-voting architecture.** Operates as principal architecture of INC political weight. The architecture per Inquirer May 2025: "For decades, the Iglesia ni Cristo (INC) has been a well-recognized force in Philippine elections, largely due to its practice of bloc voting. The church, founded in 1914 by Felix Manalo, has consistently instructed its members to vote as a single unit during elections, making its endorsement highly coveted by politicians... This practice, widely known as 'command voting,' has cemented INC's reputation as a kingmaker in tightly contested races." The 2020 Census geographic concentration per Inquirer: "one in every 70 people in NCR is an INC member. In Region III, the proportion is even higher at one in every 60 people. This concentration translates to considerable sway in election outcomes, particularly when the margin of victory is narrow." The Social Weather Stations (SWS) Mahar Mangahas analysis per Inquirer: "4.23 percent of valid votes for president were cast by people who declared their religion as INC" in 2010 elections; around 1.53 million INC presidential votes in 2010 election from total 36.14 million per Comelec; pattern of bloc-vote configuration producing kingmaker positioning. The Rappler contestation per September 2025: SWS "previous exit polls" indicating "around 20 to 25% of INC voters do not vote for candidates endorsed by their church" — question of whether INC bloc-vote positioning operates at full weight or whether defection rate produces reduced effective bloc-vote weight.

**The January 2025 INC "Meeting for Peace and Unity."** Operates as illustration of contemporary INC mass-mobilisation architecture. The architecture: 13 January 2025 INC rallies — "Meeting for Peace and Unity" — series of demonstrations led by INC in support of President Bongbong Marcos's statement opposing Sara Duterte impeachment per Wikipedia January 2025 INC protests; INC positioning concurring with Marcos statement of November 2024 framing Duterte-impeachment efforts as "unconstructive"; consequence: INC institutional mobilisation operating in support of Marcos-aligned political position against Duterte-aligned political position; positioning illustrating INC alignment with Marcos administration in 2024-2025 Marcos-Duterte rupture period.

**The 16-17 November 2025 INC "Rally for Transparency and a Better Democracy."** Operates as principal contemporary INC mass-mobilisation event. The architecture: 16-17 November 2025 INC mass-mobilisation in Manila — "Rally for Transparency and a Better Democracy" also known as "Rally for Transparency, Accountability, Justice, and Peace"; 650,000 attendees per Wikipedia November 2025 INC protests; flood-control projects controversy framing; contextualisation per Medium Institute for Nationalist Studies December 2025: "More than half a million people on the streets is an unmistakable display of 'unity' that no political party, labor federation, or civil society coalition in the Philippines today can match." The consequence per Medium analysis: "By the time INC brought 650,000 people into the streets in November 2025, its role in Philippine politics had long been a structural fact that proved its capacity to swing elections, shelter allies, and threaten even the hand of presidents." The INC outcome: protests called off one day early when INC declared goals of "justice, accountability, transparency, and peace" already met per INC spokesperson Edwil Zabala cited in Wikipedia.

**The May 2025 Philippine midterm Senate configuration.** Operates as illustration of contemporary contested weight of INC bloc vote. The architecture: May 2025 Philippine midterm Senate election — Rodante Marcoleta finishing 6th in Senate race after pro-Duterte campaign per Rappler September 2025 analysis; contestation over whether Marcoleta victory operated through INC bloc vote architecture or through broader Duterte sympathy vote following Rodrigo Duterte 11 March 2025 ICC-warrant arrest. Per Rappler citation of Pulse Asia president Ronnie Holmes: "the Duterte vote, especially in Central Visayas and Mindanao, pulled up the numbers of Marcoleta. The INC factor gave Marcoleta a bit of an edge over other pro-Duterte candidates who failed to make it... but it was not that significant to make him win relative to the geographic votes." The consequence: contested weight of INC bloc vote architecture in contemporary Philippine electoral configuration — question of whether INC bloc vote operates as principal electoral kingmaker architecture or as supplementary architecture alongside broader Duterte-Marcos political-coalition configurations.

**The Kingdom of Jesus Christ (KOJC) Quiboloy configuration.** Operates as parallel Filipino indigenous religious configuration. The architecture: Kingdom of Jesus Christ (KOJC) — Davao-based church founded 1985 by Pastor Apollo Quiboloy (born 25 April 1950); positioning as restorationist Christian denomination with Quiboloy framing as "Appointed Son of God"; alignment with Duterte families across Davao political base. The FBI Most Wanted listing 2021: Quiboloy listed for sex trafficking and other federal charges; subsequent US extradition request and Philippine contestation. The 8 September 2024 arrest: Quiboloy arrested at KOJC Davao compound after months-long manhunt; consequence: KOJC crisis producing contestation over institutional succession and ongoing Duterte-aligned positioning. Per BenarNews March 2025: "indigenous churches Iglesia ni Cristo (INC) and Kingdom of Jesus Christ (KOJC) held separate rallies for Vice President Sara Duterte" early 2025 — pattern of indigenous Filipino restorationist Christian denominations operating in alignment with Duterte-aligned political configurations.

**The Rodrigo Duterte 11 March 2025 ICC arrest configuration.** Operates as principal contemporary rupture context. The architecture: Rodrigo Duterte (born 28 March 1945) — Philippines President 2016-2022; "war on drugs" configuration producing 6,000+ documented extrajudicial killings per Philippine government figures and 30,000+ per civil-society estimates including Karapatan and Human Rights Watch documentation; ICC arrest warrant March 2025 for crimes against humanity in connection with "war on drugs" operations; arrested at Manila airport 11 March 2025 returning from Hong Kong trip; transferred to ICC custody The Hague within hours of arrest per Philippine government cooperation with ICC request despite contestation about Duterte's earlier withdrawal of Philippines from ICC architecture March 2018 (effective March 2019). The consequence: Duterte arrest 11 March 2025 producing crisis across Duterte-aligned political configurations including KOJC positioning (with Quiboloy already in detention from September 2024) and Sara Duterte impeachment-defence configuration. The Sara Duterte impeachment configuration: Vice President Sara Duterte impeachment complaints over confidential-funds use and alleged threat of assassination against President Marcos; contestation between Marcos and Duterte factions of UniTeam alliance from 2022 Philippine general election; February 2025 House impeachment vote followed by Senate positioning producing sustained crisis through 2025.

The summary of § IV: the Philippines religious-political configuration is the principal contemporary Southeast Asian illustration of religious-architecture operating across national electoral and mass-mobilisation life. The configuration operates through the Catholic Church Philippines with 80%+ of population and CBCP vehicle deploying 450,000 PPCRV volunteers for May 2025 midterm polls monitoring, the Iglesia ni Cristo founded 27 July 1914 by Felix Manalo with current Executive Minister Eduardo V. Manalo and 2,806,524 members per 2020 Census, the INC "command voting" architecture with weight in narrow-margin races particularly in NCR and Region III, the January 2025 "Meeting for Peace and Unity" supporting Marcos against Duterte impeachment. The 16-17 November 2025 "Rally for Transparency and a Better Democracy" with 650,000 attendees, the Kingdom of Jesus Christ Quiboloy 8 September 2024 arrest, and the broader Rodrigo Duterte 11 March 2025 ICC-warrant arrest configuration with Sara Duterte impeachment proceedings.

Chapter 27 closes here. The evidence covers the principal Other Captured Configurations across the African Pentecostal-political fusion (§ I), the Brazilian and Latin American evangelical-political configurations (§ II), the Russian Orthodox Church satellite configurations (§ III), and the Southeast Asian captures in the Philippines (§ IV). The configurations operate as illustrations of structurally analogous religious-political capture occurring in geographies and traditions outside the principal-dossier configurations of Chapters 14-26 — but operating through architecturally similar patterns of political-religious fusion, electoral capture through bloc-voting or pastor-endorsement architectures, state-aligned positioning, and contestation through mass-mobilisation vehicles. Chapter 27 closes Part III dossiers. Part IV (Chapters 28-30) is complete. Part VI (Chapters 31-34: Believers, Devil, Demons, Mask That Smiles) and Part VII (Chapters 35-37: Exits) follow, with closing Chapter 38 and supplementary materials completing the volume.

---


## Chapter 28. Cross-Tradition Coordination

Chapter 28 opens Part IV. Where Part III dossiered the principal captured-tradition configurations (Catholic, Protestant-Evangelical, Eastern Orthodox, Sunni, Shia, Jewish, Hindu, and the remaining dossiers to be returned to), Part IV dossiers the cross-tradition coordination architecture — the network of vehicles through which captured-religious actors from different traditions cooperate, share methodology, share funding, and share political-electoral strategy across confessional lines. The chapter's working configuration: captured-religious vehicles operate distinct from one another in theological terms, but operate increasingly in coordinated fashion on overlapping policy objectives — particularly opposition to LGBTQ rights, opposition to reproductive rights, opposition to "gender ideology" frameworks, opposition to secular human-rights frameworks, and advocacy of "traditional values" and "natural family" frameworks.

The documentation covers four sections: § I the Christian Right international coordination architecture including the World Congress of Families (1997), Alliance Defending Freedom (1994), CitizenGO (2013), and the wider US-based Christian Right network operating globally. § II the Russian Orthodox international coordination including the Konstantin Malofeev network, the Russkiy Mir Foundation, the World Russian People's Council. And Russian-Orthodox-aligned networking. § III the Catholic-Evangelical-Orthodox cooperation including the "ecumenism of hate" framing (Spadaro and Figueroa La Civiltà Cattolica July 2017), the Catholic Integralist current, the Christian nationalist coordination. And the cross-tradition cooperation across confessional lines. § IV the trans-religious coordination at the UN, EU, OAS, and African Union levels including the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC), the Cairo Declaration on Human Rights in Islam (1990), the Istanbul Process, and the Mexico Declaration 2022.

The analysis operates with the same investigative-journalism standards and the same captured-state analytic framework applied across Part III — and applied here to the cross-tradition coordination architecture without exceptionalism for any particular tradition's vehicles. The record operates against any framing that would treat the Christian Right network as  innocent of the coordination patterns dossiered, or that would treat Muslim-majority state coordination as distinct in kind from Christian Right international coordination.
