Anthropic founded 2021 by Dario Amodei (former OpenAI VP of Research), Daniela Amodei (former OpenAI VP of Safety), and former OpenAI colleagues; positioning on AI safety; Constitutional AI framework; Series E funding rounds 2024-2025 including investments from Google, Amazon, and others. DeepMind founded 2010 by Demis Hassabis, Shane Legg, Mustafa Suleyman; Google acquisition 2014. xAI founded March 2023 by Elon Musk; Grok vehicle. The architecture is AI-development corporate configuration emerging from EA-rationalist intellectual networks producing patterns through which AI development is articulated in eschatological-religious framings per multiple analyses.

**The TESCREAL framework critique.** Per Émile P. Torres and Timnit Gebru's "The TESCREAL Bundle" (*First Monday*, April 2024) and the broader Torres-Gebru analytical corpus: TESCREAL is analytical framework articulating the intellectual cluster including Transhumanism, Extropianism, Singularitarianism, Cosmism, Rationalism, Effective Altruism, and Longtermism. The architecture: Torres-Gebru thesis that the TESCREAL intellectual cluster operates as contemporary techno-utopian ideology producing consequences including risk-prioritisation patterns favouring "long-term-future" existential-risk concerns over contemporary harm patterns including racial bias, labour exploitation, environmental harm, and contemporary AI-safety concerns; the critique articulating the TESCREAL cluster as "secular religion" producing religious-veneration patterns around AI development; Torres-Gebru intellectual reception including uptake across critical AI studies and religion-and-technology scholarly fields.

**The Anthony Levandowski "Way of the Future" church.** This is illustration of contemporary AI-veneration discourse. The architecture: Anthony Levandowski (former Google self-driving car engineer, former Uber engineer, the 2017 figure in Waymo-Uber trade-secret litigation) founding of "Way of the Future" religious organisation September 2017 — purpose articulated as "development and promotion of the realization of a Godhead based on Artificial Intelligence"; registration as a California religious nonprofit organisation; dissolution 2021 with assets distributed to the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund per multiple reports. The architecture is illustration of contemporary AI-veneration discourse producing explicit religious-organisational articulation — and as illustration of the broader pattern through which AI development has been  framed in religious-eschatological terms within Silicon Valley tech-elite intellectual networks per Religion News May 2026 analysis.

**Yuval Noah Harari and AI-veneration intellectual current.** Per multiple analyses: Yuval Noah Harari (Hebrew University historian, author of *Sapiens* 2011, *Homo Deus* 2015, *21 Lessons for the 21st Century* 2018) operates as mainstream intellectual articulator of an AI-veneration intellectual framework. The architecture: Harari thesis articulated in *Homo Deus* (2015) that contemporary technocratic-AI development is producing emergence of "homo deus" (human-as-god) configuration; Harari intellectual influence across Silicon Valley tech-elite networks and broader mainstream intellectual culture; positioning of Harari's framework as intellectual articulator of contemporary AI-veneration discourse per Religion News analysis. The architecture operates as intellectual illustration of the mainstream emergence of AI-veneration framing — operating adjacent to the TESCREAL intellectual cluster dossiered above.

**The contemporary AI-development configuration as religious-veneration ecosystem.** As of mid-2026, the AI-development configuration operates as: (1) corporate vehicles (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, xAI, Meta AI, others) operating with religious-veneration discourse patterns including eschatological framing of AI development; (2) intellectual-philanthropic cluster (EA, longtermism, rationalism, LessWrong, MIRI, Open Philanthropy) producing intellectual articulation of AI-development as ethical-eschatological priority; (3) the critical framework (TESCREAL analysis, Torres-Gebru critique, Adam Becker's *More Everything Forever* 2025, Religion News analysis) articulating pattern of AI-development as "secular religion"; (4) intersection with the Silicon Valley religious turn dossiered in § I including Thiel's framing of AI critics as "legionnaires of the Antichrist"; (5) intersection with the Yarvin neoreactionary intellectual current dossiered in § III including techno-authoritarian advocacy patterns; (6) intersection with the Catholic Integralist intellectual current dossiered in § II including Vance positioning on AI policy.

The summary of § IV: the AI-development corporate-religious interface is the contemporary terrain on which AI-development corporate vehicles, longtermist-effective-altruist intellectual currents, and rationalist online communities have produced patterns of religious-veneration discourse around AI development. The configuration operates through Yudkowsky's foundational LessWrong/MIRI vehicle, the EA movement (MacAskill, Ord, Centre for Effective Altruism, Open Philanthropy, Giving What We Can), the longtermist ethical framework, the Sam Bankman-Fried FTX collapse crisis November 2022. The OpenAI/Anthropic/DeepMind/xAI corporate configurations, the TESCREAL critical framework articulated by Torres and Gebru, the Anthony Levandowski Way of the Future AI-deity church (founded 2017, dissolved 2021), and the Yuval Noah Harari mainstream intellectual articulation. The architecture is the contemporary terrain on which the Silicon Valley religious turn (§ I), the Catholic Integralist positioning in executive power (§ II), and the Yarvin neoreactionary intellectual current (§ III) all converge — producing the contemporary technocratic-religious interface dossiered across this chapter.

Chapter 29 closes here. What follows covers the contemporary technocratic-religious interface operating between Silicon Valley billionaire networks, Catholic Integralist intellectual current, Yarvin neoreactionary intellectual current, and AI-development corporate configurations. Chapter 30 dossiers the wars that wore faith — contemporary armed-conflict configurations in which captured-religious vehicles operate as combatant figures, beneficiaries, or framing-discourse providers — including the post-October 2023 Gaza configuration, the post-February 2022 Russian-Ukrainian war configuration, and the broader pattern of contemporary armed-conflict religious-configuration.

---


## Chapter 30. The Wars That Wore Faith

Chapter 30 opens Part V. Where Part IV dossiered the cross-tradition coordination architecture (Chapter 28) and the technocratic-religious interface (Chapter 29), Part V dossiers the wars that wore faith — contemporary armed conflicts in which captured-religious vehicles operate as combatants, beneficiaries, or framing-discourse providers, and in some cases all three. The working configuration: religious framing does not in itself produce the wars dossiered in this chapter; the wars are produced by territorial dispute, resource competition, ethnic conflict, state-collapse dynamics, geopolitical confrontation, and elite political calculation. But religious framing is what these wars wore — the language in which they were declared, the banner under which combatants were mobilised, the vehicle through which atrocity was sanctified, and the discourse through which they were exported to global audiences.

The evidence covers four sections: § I the October 2023 Gaza war as religious framing on all sides including Hamas's "Al-Aqsa Flood" Islamic-eschatological framing, Israeli religious Zionist coalition partners' messianic framing. And Operation Gideon's Chariots biblical naming May-August 2025. § II the Russian-Ukrainian war and the Moscow Patriarchate as "holy war" framing vehicle including Patriarch Kirill's September 2022 "sins washed away" sermon. The World Russian People's Council 27-28 March 2024 "Holy War" decree against Ukraine and the West. And the persecution of non-Moscow-Patriarchate Christian clergy in Russian-occupied Ukraine. § III the Sudanese civil war 2023-present and Islamist factions on both sides including the SAF-NCP Islamist alliance and the RSF Islamist narrative variants. § IV patterns across the wars that wore faith including Nigeria. Myanmar, DRC, and Sahel configurations, the cross-tradition pattern of religious framing as legitimising-mobilising discourse, and the International Criminal Court / International Court of Justice response.

The argument operates with the same investigative-journalism standards applied across the preceding chapters. The evidence operates symmetrically across confessional boundaries — applying the same analytic to Israeli religious Zionist framing, Hamas Islamic-eschatological framing, Russian Orthodox "holy war" framing, Sudanese Islamist framing, and the broader contemporary pattern, without exceptionalism for any tradition. The chapter operates against the framing that would treat any of these conflicts as caused by religion, and against the framing that would treat religious framing as merely decorative; the reality is that religious framing operates as a structuring discourse through which contemporary armed conflict is produced, legitimised, and exported — independently from the underlying material drivers of the conflicts.

### I. The October 2023 Gaza War: Religious Framing on All Sides

The Gaza war beginning 7 October 2023 serves as the principal contemporary armed conflict in which religious framing has been deployed by all major actors. The architecture has five principal nodes: (1) the Hamas attack named "Operation Al-Aqsa Flood" as Islamic-eschatological framing of the assault on Israeli communities. (2) the Israeli military response operating under religious Zionist coalition framing including Operation Gideon's Chariots biblical naming May-August 2025. (3) the destruction of religious sites across Gaza including mosques and churches. (4) the broader Middle East configuration including Iranian involvement. Hezbollah operations in Lebanon, and Houthi operations in Yemen-Red Sea framed within Shia and Sunni eschatological narratives; (5) the Gaza peace plan signed 9 October 2025 and the post-ceasefire configuration.

**Hamas and the "Al-Aqsa Flood" framing.** The 7 October 2023 attack — in which Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad, and other Palestinian armed groups crossed from Gaza into Israeli territory killing over 1,200 people including 815 civilians and taking 251 hostages — was named by Hamas "Operation Al-Aqsa Flood" (Tufan al-Aqsa) — reference to the Al-Aqsa Mosque in the old city of Jerusalem. The architecture: Hamas (Harakat al-Muqawama al-Islamiyya) — Sunni Islamist movement founded 1987 in the first intifada from Muslim Brotherhood institutional origins; 1988 Hamas Covenant calling for destruction of Israel and articulating Islamic-eschatological framework of the Israeli-Palestinian conflict; 2017 revised Hamas Document of General Principles and Policies accepting 1967 borders without recognising Israel; Hamas governance of Gaza Strip from 14 June 2007 following Battle of Gaza against rival Fatah forces. The "Al-Aqsa Flood" framing operates as Islamic-religious articulation of the attack — the name cites Israeli encroachments at Al-Aqsa Mosque as institutional grievance and frames the operation within Islamic-eschatological vocabulary.

**Hamas religious-eschatological discourse during the war.** Per Jerusalem Post August 2024 analysis and the Combating Terrorism Center at West Point October 2025 report "Inside Hamas: How It Thinks, Fights, and Governs": Hamas spokesman Abu Obeida — speaking for the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades (Hamas military wing) — referred to the people of Gaza several times as Al-Ta'ifah Al-Mansurah (The Victorious Sect) — a mystical group originating in the Levant according to Islamic traditions, bearing witness to the reign of Allah at the end of days. Yahya Sinwar (Hamas Gaza chief from 2017, killed October 2024) reportedly sported messianic views per Jerusalem Post sources, viewing himself and Hamas as messengers bringing Allah's promise for the end of days (Wa'ed al-Akhira). The Qassam Brigades May 2023 statement articulated aim to enter a "large scale battle of the hereafter promise, with the participation of all fronts and fields and the entering of our occupied lands, freeing and calling for Allah's name, Allah willing" per Jerusalem Post analysis. The Hamas "Our Narrative — Operation Al-Aqsa Flood" English-language document published January 2024 attempted to recast Hamas as moderate national-religious movement while obscuring Muslim Brotherhood origins and 1988 charter call for Israel's destruction.

**Israeli religious Zionist coalition partners and the war's framing.** Cross-reference Chapter 20. The Israeli government during the war operated as Benjamin Netanyahu's sixth government (from 29 December 2022) — including religious Zionist coalition partners Bezalel Smotrich (Religious Zionism Party, Finance Minister) and Itamar Ben-Gvir (Otzma Yehudit, National Security Minister until January 2025 resignation over Phase 1 ceasefire, returned March 2025). The architecture: Smotrich and Ben-Gvir public statements throughout the war articulating Israeli operations within messianic-religious Zionist framework; Smotrich November 2023 statement on "voluntary migration" of Palestinians from Gaza; Ben-Gvir public framings of West Bank Palestinian violence and Gaza operations within Kahanist ideological framework. The pattern: Israeli operations in Gaza were articulated not solely in security-state terms but increasingly in religious-Zionist messianic terms — particularly by coalition partners whose ascendance to ministerial position from late 2022 represented an shift in Israeli governance configuration cross-referenced from Chapter 20.

**Operation Gideon's Chariots May-August 2025.** The architecture: Israeli Defence Forces offensive throughout the Gaza Strip 16 May to 4 August 2025; named after the Israelite warrior Gideon (Judges 6) who led successful battle against the Midianites in Hebrew Bible narrative; operational naming pattern with explicit biblical reference operates as articulation of Israeli military operations within biblical-religious framework. The consequence: the operation produced significant Palestinian casualties (Gaza Ministry of Health reporting bringing total Palestinian deaths over 60,000 by mid-2025), significant additional destruction of Gaza civilian infrastructure, and significant additional international response including UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs reports and Human Rights Watch documentation of operations. The biblical naming pattern operates as illustration of the religious framing through which the war was conducted at official Israeli military level — not as decoration but as material articulation of operational identity.

**Destruction of religious sites across Gaza.** Per Wikipedia "Attacks on religious sites during the Israeli invasion of Gaza" and BBC reporting January 2024: Israeli operations damaged or destroyed multiple religious sites in Gaza. The architecture: strikes including the Great Omari Mosque (Gaza City, one of the oldest mosques in historic Palestine) hit by Israeli airstrike; Khalid bin Alwaleed Mosque (Khan Yunis) significantly damaged; multiple smaller mosques across Gaza damaged or destroyed including a May 2024 Israeli bombing on a Gaza City mosque reportedly killing at least 10 people; three churches damaged including the Saint Porphyrius Greek Orthodox Church (one of the oldest churches in the world, founded c. 1150 on earlier foundations from the 5th century AD) struck by Israeli airstrike 19 October 2023 killing 18 Palestinian Christians sheltering there per multiple reports. The consequence: the destruction of religious sites operates as illustration of the contemporary pattern through which armed conflict produces destruction of religious-cultural infrastructure regardless of operational targeting intent.

**The broader Middle East configuration.** The architecture: Iranian involvement including Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps Quds Force support to Hamas, Palestinian Islamic Jihad, and Hezbollah; Hezbollah operations from Lebanon from 8 October 2023 producing Israel-Hezbollah war 2024 ending with November 2024 ceasefire; Houthi operations from Yemen against Red Sea shipping framed within Houthi institutional Zaidi Shia framework; April 2024 direct Iran-Israel exchange (Iranian drone-missile attack 13-14 April 2024, Israeli response 19 April 2024); October 2024 Iran-Israel direct exchange; June 2025 Israeli-Iranian war (12-day conflict including US strike on Iranian nuclear facilities 22 June 2025). The pattern: each actor in the broader Middle East configuration operated within distinct religious-eschatological frameworks — Iranian Shia revolutionary framework, Hezbollah Lebanese Shia framework, Houthi Zaidi Shia framework, Hamas Sunni Islamist framework, Israeli religious-Zionist messianic framework — operating as overlapping articulations of geopolitical conflict in religious-framing vocabulary.

**The Gaza peace plan and post-ceasefire configuration.** The architecture: Gaza peace plan signed 9 October 2025 — Trump-mediated framework following extended US-Egyptian-Qatari mediation; Phase 1 hostage-prisoner exchange beginning October 2025; Hamas retention of de facto control over 47% of Gaza territory; Israeli military control over 53% of Gaza territory per Wikipedia Gaza Strip under Hamas analysis as of 10 October 2025; continuing uncertainty regarding Phase 2 and Phase 3 framework provisions including governance arrangement for post-conflict Gaza and reconstruction financing. The consequence: the post-ceasefire configuration operates as illustration of the incompleteness of the war's resolution — with religious framing remaining as substrate through which post-conflict politics will be articulated by all sides.

The summary of § I: the October 2023 Gaza war is the principal contemporary armed conflict in which religious framing has been deployed by all major actors. The configuration operates through Hamas's "Al-Aqsa Flood" Islamic-eschatological framing, Israeli religious Zionist coalition partners' messianic framing, Operation Gideon's Chariots biblical naming, destruction of mosques and churches across Gaza, broader Middle East Iranian-Hezbollah-Houthi-Israeli configuration with each actor operating in distinct religious-eschatological framework, and the 9 October 2025 Gaza peace plan with continuing uncertainty. The architecture operates as illustration of the contemporary pattern through which armed conflict is articulated in religious-framing vocabulary by all sides — operating as structuring discourse rather than as decoration. The Russian-Ukrainian war dossiered in § II operates as parallel illustration of religious framing in armed conflict under different confessional configuration.

---


### II. The Russian-Ukrainian War: The Moscow Patriarchate as "Holy War" Framing Vehicle

The Russian-Ukrainian war beginning 24 February 2022 is the principal contemporary armed conflict in which an established Christian Patriarchate has deployed "holy war" framing to articulate its support for state military operations. The architecture has five principal nodes: (1) Patriarch Kirill as the principal Russian Orthodox figure articulating "holy war" framing from February 2022 onward. (2) the World Russian People's Council 27-28 March 2024 decree formalising the "Holy War" declaration against Ukraine and the West. (3) the response from World Council of Churches. Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, Pope Francis, and broader Christian world. (4) the persecution of non-Moscow-Patriarchate clergy in Russian-occupied Ukraine. (5) the Ukrainian Orthodox response including the autocephalous Orthodox Church of Ukraine position.

**Patriarch Kirill and the "holy war" framing.** Cross-reference Chapter 17. Patriarch Kirill (Vladimir Mikhailovich Gundyayev) — born 20 November 1946 Leningrad — operates as Patriarch of Moscow and all Rus' since 1 February 2009. The architecture: Kirill positioning of Russian Orthodox Church as vehicle of Russian-state geopolitical-cultural influence from the post-2009 period; Kirill weekly sermons aired live on Russian TV from February 2022 articulating the Russian invasion as apocalyptic battle against evil forces seeking to destroy "the God-given unity of Holy Russia" per AP/LA Times reporting; Kirill September 2022 sermon claiming Russian military personnel killed in Ukraine would have "all sins washed away" by their sacrifice — language paralleling Islamist martyrdom framings per Atlantic Council April 2024 analysis. The consequence: Kirill's articulations operate as theological-political articulation of Russian military operations rather than as separate religious commentary — operating as vehicle through which Russian state and Russian Orthodox Church merge into single discursive operation.

**The World Russian People's Council 27-28 March 2024 decree.** Operates as the formal articulation of the Russian Orthodox "holy war" position. The architecture: World Russian People's Council (Vsemirnyy Russkiy Narodnyy Sobor) — founded 1993, headed by Patriarch Kirill, cross-reference Chapter 28 § II; 27-28 March 2024 congress in Moscow producing decree titled documents declaring Russian operations in Ukraine a "Holy War"; institutional thesis articulating Russia "protecting the world from the onslaught of globalism and the victory of the West that has fallen into Satanism" per OSV News February 2026; institutional framework declaring all of Ukraine should enter Russia's "zone of exclusive influence" per Atlantic Council April 2024 reporting; institutional framework articulating Ukraine war as "new stage of the national liberation struggle of the Russian people against the criminal Kiev regime and the collective West behind it, conducted in the lands of South-Western Russia since 2014" per WCC statement 12 April 2024. The consequence: the WRPC decree operates as formal Russian Orthodox Patriarchate articulation of position previously articulated in Kirill sermons — moving from sermon-level rhetoric to formal Church Council declaration.

**World Council of Churches and Ecumenical Patriarchate response.** Per WCC statement 12 April 2024 issued by General Secretary Jerry Pillay: WCC "cannot reconcile" the WRPC decree presentation of Russia's invasion as "Holy War" with prior WCC governing-body positions including June 2022 Central Committee statement and September 2022 11th Assembly statement denouncing the invasion as "illegal and unjustifiable" and rejecting "any misuse of religious language and authority to justify armed aggression and hatred". The architecture: WCC ecumenical alignment against the Russian Orthodox "holy war" framing; Russian Orthodox Church presence in WCC governing-body meetings preceding the 2022 statements; institutional tension between Russian Orthodox Patriarchate positions and broader Orthodox-ecumenical positions including the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople positions (cross-reference Chapter 17 § III). The consequence: rupture between Moscow Patriarchate and broader Orthodox-ecumenical world deepening across 2022-2026 period.

**Pope Francis "Putin's altar boy" critique.** Cross-reference Chapter 15. Per Reuters interview May 2022: Pope Francis explicitly warned Kirill against becoming "Putin's altar boy" — articulating Catholic critique of Kirill's alignment with Russian state military operations. The architecture: Francis position against Russian Orthodox "holy war" framing operating alongside broader Catholic position; the February 2016 Havana Francis-Kirill meeting (cross-reference Chapter 28 § III) rupture post-February 2022; continuing Catholic opposition to "holy war" framing under Pope Francis until April 2025 death and under successor Pope Leo XIV from May 2025. The consequence: broader Christian opposition to Russian Orthodox "holy war" framing operating across Catholic, Protestant, broader Orthodox, and ecumenical vehicles.

**Persecution of non-Moscow-Patriarchate clergy in Russian-occupied Ukraine.** Per OSV News February 2026 reporting on "No Priests Left" documentary series and Diane Moore (Harvard Religion and Public Life) US Helsinki Commission testimony: Russian operations in occupied Ukrainian territory included persecution of Catholic, Protestant, Jewish, Muslim, and non-Moscow-Patriarchate Orthodox clergy. The architecture: up to 80 pastors and priests killed in Russian-occupied territories per Moore testimony; closure of every church in occupied Ukraine not controlled by Kremlin per OSV News reporting; Russian Orthodox consolidation of religious sphere in occupied territories under Moscow Patriarchate direct administration; the Holy Synod of Russian Orthodox Church July 2022 removal of Crimea from Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate jurisdiction with direct subordination to Moscow Patriarchate; appointment of Tikhon (Georgy Shevkunov, born 2 July 1958 — believed to be Putin's confessor) as Metropolitan of Simferopol and Crimea 2024 per OSW analysis. The consequence: assessment of Russian campaign as genocide per New Lines Institute + Raoul Wallenberg Center for Human Rights joint reports — documenting Russian operations targeting Christian, Jewish, and Muslim communities in Ukraine as part of "weaponized" Orthodox Christianity per Moore analysis.

**Ukrainian Orthodox response and the autocephalous Orthodox Church of Ukraine.** The architecture: Orthodox Church of Ukraine (Pravoslavna Tserkva Ukrayiny, OCU) — autocephalous Orthodox church recognised by Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople through Tomos of Autocephaly issued 6 January 2019; position against Russian Orthodox "holy war" framing; growth across Ukraine during the post-2022 period as Ukrainian believers relocated from Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate (UOC-MP) to OCU; Ukrainian government measures from October 2023 to limit UOC-MP operations following allegations of UOC-MP cooperation with Russian intelligence; Bulgarian Orthodox Church July 2024 election of Patriarch Daniil reflecting divisions in Orthodox world over Russian invasion per Euronews. The consequence: broader Orthodox realignment in the post-2022 period with the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, Orthodox Church of Ukraine, Bulgarian Orthodox Church, Romanian Orthodox Church, and other Orthodox jurisdictions operating in distance from Moscow Patriarchate.

**The "metaphysical realm" defence and the contradiction.** Per WCC May 2023 meeting with Patriarch Kirill in Moscow: Kirill told WCC General Secretary Pillay that any "Holy War" references he had made were related to "the metaphysical realm" rather than physical armed conflict — agreeing with Pillay that no war of armed violence can be "holy". The consequence: the 27-28 March 2024 WRPC decree articulating "Holy War" framing against Ukraine and the West contradicted Kirill's May 2023 WCC institutional statement per Pillay 12 April 2024 statement. The pattern: contradiction between Kirill's diplomatic-level positioning to ecumenical bodies and positioning to Russian domestic and Russian World audiences — operating as illustration of contemporary captured-religious pattern in which positioning varies by audience while material alignment with state military operations remains consistent.

The summary of § II: the Russian-Ukrainian war is the principal contemporary armed conflict in which an established Christian Patriarchate has deployed "holy war" framing to articulate support for state military operations. The configuration operates through Patriarch Kirill's articulations from February 2022, the World Russian People's Council 27-28 March 2024 "Holy War" decree, the rupture with World Council of Churches and Ecumenical Patriarchate. The persecution of non-Moscow-Patriarchate clergy in Russian-occupied Ukraine, the Ukrainian Orthodox response through the autocephalous Orthodox Church of Ukraine, and the contradiction between Kirill's diplomatic-level positioning and the WRPC decree articulation. The architecture operates as illustration of an established Christian Patriarchate  aligning itself with state military operations through "holy war" framing — operating in parallel to the Hamas Islamic-eschatological framing dossiered in § I and to the Sudanese Islamist framing dossiered in § III. The Sudanese civil war dossiered in § III operates as illustration of religious framing across both sides of an intra-Muslim institutional conflict configuration.

---


### III. The Sudanese Civil War 2023-Present: Islamist Factions on Both Sides

The Sudanese civil war beginning 15 April 2023 between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) serves as the principal contemporary armed conflict in which Islamist factions operate on both sides of an intra-Muslim institutional conflict. The architecture has five principal nodes: (1) the SAF-NCP (National Congress Party) Islamist alliance reactivating the pre-2019 Bashir-era Islamist architecture. (2) the RSF Islamist narrative variants operating as different genre of Islamist framing. (3) the Al-Baraa Bin Malik Brigade Islamist militia operating within SAF coalition. (4) the attacks on religious sites including the All Saints Anglican Cathedral seizure 15 April 2023. (5) the humanitarian catastrophe with Sudan as world's largest displacement crisis by 2025.

**The conflict origins and the governing collapse.** The architecture: Sudan civil war origins in 2018-2019 popular uprising ending Omar al-Bashir's 30-year Islamist military regime (1989-2019); transitional government formed August 2019 under Sovereignty Council combining military and civilian leadership; transitional government collapse 25 October 2021 in joint coup by SAF commander General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan (born 1960) and RSF commander General Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo "Hemedti" (born c. 1974); subsequent institutional tension over RSF integration into SAF producing the 15 April 2023 outbreak of armed conflict. The consequence: intra-military institutional conflict producing the contemporary Sudanese civil war — operating on top of pre-existing religious-ethnic-regional tensions including Darfur conflict legacy, South Sudan secession 2011 institutional legacy, and broader Sudanese state-formation dynamics.

**The SAF-NCP Islamist alliance.** Per Observer Research Foundation October 2025 analysis "Sudan at a Crossroads: The Potential Resurgence of Political Islam" and Arab Center DC November 2025 analysis "Sudan's War: The Failure of Mediation and the Struggle for Civilian Rule": SAF operates in alliance with Bashir-era Islamist figures and National Congress Party (NCP) figures. The architecture: Burhan January 2025 appointment of prominent NCP figures to senior judicial positions signalling formal rehabilitation of party's old guard per Observer Research Foundation; inclusion of Bashir-era Islamists alongside Darfuri ex-rebels and tribal elements in SAF coalition per Arab Center DC; the Al-Baraa Bin Malik Brigade Islamist militia (named after early Islamic warrior Al-Baraa Bin Malik who fought in the Battle of Yamamah) playing significant role in major SAF operations including the March 2025 SAF seizure of the Republican Palace in Khartoum per Observer Research Foundation. The consequence: question whether SAF can be ally of democracy given Islamists' efforts to punish civil activists, restore military-Islamist rule, and dismantle grassroots revolutionary movement per Arab Center DC analysis.

**The RSF Islamist narrative variants.** Per Observer Research Foundation October 2025 analysis: while SAF Islamist alignment has garnered attention, RSF also draws upon Islamist narratives of different genre. The architecture: RSF articulation including Sufi-aligned and tribal-Islamist framings operating distinctly from SAF Bashir-era Islamist framework; RSF Nairobi Charter February 2025 signed by RSF leadership and allies in Nairobi Kenya articulating post-war framework including secularism, democracy, decentralised structure, and unified national army per Council on Foreign Relations Global Conflict Tracker; institutional tension between RSF's secularism articulation in Nairobi Charter and RSF operational deployment of Islamist narratives in field operations. The pattern: contradiction in RSF positioning operating as illustration of contemporary captured-religious pattern in which positioning varies by audience and operational context while material alignment with armed conflict operations remains consistent — parallel to Russian Orthodox pattern dossiered in § II.

**The All Saints Anglican Cathedral seizure 15 April 2023.** Per Canopy Forum April 2025 analysis by Christopher Tounsel: on the opening day of the civil war 15 April 2023, RSF fighters seized the All Saints Anglican Cathedral in Khartoum and transformed it into operational base. The architecture: Archbishop Ezekiel Kondo (Anglican Archbishop of Sudan) was in church office preparing for Sunday service when "heavy sound of gunfire" began per Tounsel reporting; cathedral foundation stone laid 1904 by British clergyman Llewellyn H. Gwynne during Anglo-Egyptian Condominium period (1899-1956); 1904 ceremony stood in marked contrast to 2023 paramilitary takeover per Tounsel analysis. The consequence: the seizure operates as illustration of broader pattern through which religious sites are targeted, occupied, or destroyed during contemporary armed conflict — operating in parallel to Gaza mosque-and-church destruction dossiered in § I and to Russian targeting of non-Moscow-Patriarchate clergy in Ukraine dossiered in § II.

**The al-Fasher siege and humanitarian catastrophe.** The architecture: RSF siege of al-Fasher (capital of North Darfur) from April 2024 preventing food and lifesaving assistance from entering and preventing civilians from escaping per Arab Center DC; al-Fasher had 36 clinics and hospitals before siege; by 2025 only one remained partially functional; medical staff gone into hiding; families reduced to eating leaves and animal feed; UN reported over 7.5 million people displaced by mid-2024 per Wikipedia Timeline of Sudanese civil war 2024; over 235 villages burned by mid-2024 per NGO reporting; Joint Darfur Force (JDF) operations 2025 including the killing of 462 RSF fighters in attack on Al-Malha, North Darfur per Wikipedia Timeline 2025. The consequence: Sudan operates as world's largest displacement crisis by 2024-2025 with the humanitarian situation deteriorating across 2024-2025 as international aid sharply declined following US humanitarian funding cuts (cross-reference Chapter 29 § II for USAID dismantling January-May 2025).

**SAF military gains 2024-2025 and the implications.** Per CFR Global Conflict Tracker and Wikipedia Timeline 2025: SAF launched coordinated offensive around Khartoum, Omdurman, and Bahri in second half 2024 catching RSF forces off guard; SAF seized significant Khartoum territory for first time since RSF took control at war's onset; January 2025 SAF drove RSF forces out of Omdurman and recaptured vital oil refinery just north of Khartoum and regained near-total control of Bahri; February 2025 SAF forces ended RSF's two-year siege of Obeid; March 2025 SAF seized Republican Palace in Khartoum. The consequence: SAF military gains 2024-2025 coincide with NCP/Islamist rehabilitation — operating as illustration of pattern through which military victory by Islamist-aligned faction produces reconfiguration favouring Islamist political-restoration. The institutional question per Observer Research Foundation: should SAF achieve decisive military dominance, processes may pave way for complete political restoration of NCP or comparable Islamist entity operating under different nomenclature.

**The institutional foreign involvement and the religious-framing pattern.** The architecture: UAE support to RSF including arms transfers per multiple reports and UN expert panel findings; Egyptian support to SAF; Saudi Arabian positioning across both sides; Iranian engagement particularly with SAF in 2024-2025; Russian Wagner Group (later Africa Corps) involvement particularly with RSF earlier and shifting positioning across the conflict per CFR analysis. The pattern: foreign involvement operates adjacent to religious-framing pattern on both sides — operating as illustration of contemporary armed-conflict pattern in which Islamist framing by both sides exists alongside foreign-state involvement framed in geopolitical-strategic rather than religious terms.

The summary of § III: the Sudanese civil war 2023-present is the principal contemporary armed conflict in which Islamist factions operate on both sides of an intra-Muslim institutional conflict. The configuration operates through SAF-NCP Islamist alliance reactivating pre-2019 Bashir-era Islamist architecture, RSF Islamist narrative variants operating as different genre. Al-Baraa Bin Malik Brigade Islamist militia within SAF coalition, RSF seizure of All Saints Anglican Cathedral 15 April 2023, al-Fasher siege from April 2024, SAF military gains 2024-2025, and foreign involvement (UAE, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Iran, Russia). The architecture operates as illustration of contemporary armed-conflict pattern in which religious framing operates on both sides of intra-Muslim conflict configuration — parallel to the Gaza war Israeli-Hamas configuration in § I (Sunni Islamist vs. Jewish religious-Zionist framing) and the Russian-Ukrainian war Russian Orthodox-Ukrainian Orthodox configuration in § II (Moscow Patriarchate vs. Orthodox Church of Ukraine-Ecumenical Patriarchate framing). The patterns across the wars that wore faith dossiered in § IV operate as articulation of the contemporary pattern through which religious framing operates as structuring discourse in armed conflict.

---


### IV. Patterns Across the Wars That Wore Faith

The patterns across the wars that wore faith operate as the analytical articulation of contemporary armed-conflict configurations in which religious framing operates as structuring discourse. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Nigerian configuration including Boko Haram and Islamic State West Africa Province operations and the Christian-Muslim Middle Belt pattern. (2) the Myanmar configuration including Tatmadaw post-2021 operations and Buddhist nationalist 969 Movement / Ma Ba Tha context. (3) the Democratic Republic of Congo configuration including Allied Democratic Forces Islamist insurgency and the broader Eastern DRC pattern. (4) the Sahel configuration including JNIM Al-Qaeda affiliate and ISGS Islamic State Greater Sahara operations and the broader French-Russian shift. (5) the International Criminal Court / International Court of Justice response including ICC arrest warrants against Netanyahu, Gallant, and Hamas leadership November 2024 and the ICJ South Africa v. Israel case from January 2024; (6) the synthetic pattern through which religious framing operates as legitimising-mobilising discourse across contemporary armed conflict.

**The Nigerian configuration.** The architecture: Boko Haram (Jamāʿat Ahl al-Sunnah lil-Daʿawah wa-l-Jihād) — Salafi-jihadist insurgent group founded 2002 by Mohammed Yusuf in Maiduguri; insurgency from 2009 following extra-judicial killing of Yusuf by Nigerian security forces; Abubakar Shekau leadership 2009-2021; Islamic State allegiance pledge March 2015; ISWAP (Islamic State West Africa Province) splinter from 2016 under Abu Musab al-Barnawi; Shekau death May 2021 in clash with ISWAP forces. The consequences: over 350,000 deaths attributable to Boko Haram-Nigeria conflict by 2024 per UN estimates; millions of internally displaced persons in northeastern Nigeria; Chibok girls abduction 14 April 2014 (276 schoolgirls, ~57 escaping initially); systematic targeting of Christian communities, secular schools, and Muslim figures opposed to Boko Haram framework. The broader Nigerian pattern: Fulani herder-farmer violence in Nigeria's Middle Belt with religious-ethnic overlay; Open Doors 2024 World Watch List ranking Nigeria as principal site of Christian persecution by deaths; contemporary contestation around whether Middle Belt violence operates as primarily religious, primarily ethnic, primarily resource-competition (land, water), or primarily state-collapse pattern — with reality that all four operate simultaneously in mutually-reinforcing pattern.

**The Myanmar configuration.** The architecture: Tatmadaw (Myanmar Armed Forces) coup 1 February 2021 against Aung San Suu Kyi government; return to military rule under State Administration Council headed by Senior General Min Aung Hlaing; continuing civil war including Tatmadaw operations against People's Defence Force (PDF), ethnic armed organisations (EAOs) including Karen National Union, Kachin Independence Organisation, Arakan Army, Three Brotherhood Alliance, and others. The religious framing context: cross-reference Chapter 23 (when written) for full Buddhist Captured tradition dossier; the 969 Movement articulation from c. 2012 by Ashin Wirathu and other Buddhist nationalist monks; Ma Ba Tha (Committee for the Protection of Race and Religion, Buddhist nationalist organisation) articulation from c. 2014; positioning of Buddhist nationalist framings adjacent to Tatmadaw operations including operations against Rohingya Muslim minority producing 2017 refugee crisis and ICJ Gambia v. Myanmar case from November 2019. The consequence: the Myanmar configuration operates as illustration of Buddhist-nationalist religious framing operating adjacent to military-state operations against religious minorities — operating in parallel to Sunni-Islamist framing in § III SAF coalition and Russian Orthodox framing in § II.

**The Democratic Republic of Congo configuration.** The architecture: Allied Democratic Forces (ADF) — Ugandan-origin Islamist insurgency operating in Eastern DRC and Uganda border region from 1995; ADF affiliation with Islamic State Central Africa Province (ISCAP) from 2019 declaration; systematic ADF attacks on civilian populations across North Kivu and Ituri provinces 2019-2026 producing thousands of deaths and hundreds of thousands of displacements; Christian-targeting pattern in ADF operations including attacks on churches and Christian villages; the Uganda People's Defence Force (UPDF) and Forces Armées de la République Démocratique du Congo (FARDC) joint operations against ADF from 2021 ("Operation Shujaa"); continuing security crisis in Eastern DRC alongside separate M23 rebellion configuration. The consequence: the DRC configuration operates as illustration of Islamist-insurgency religious framing operating in armed conflict context where underlying drivers also include ethnic conflict (Hutu-Tutsi-Congolese), resource competition (coltan, gold, tin, tungsten), and Rwandan-Ugandan-DRC geopolitical configuration — operating as illustration of contemporary pattern through which religious framing exists alongside material conflict drivers without exclusively determining them.

**The Sahel configuration.** The architecture: Sahel region jihadist insurgencies including JNIM (Jamāʿat Nuṣrat al-Islām wal-Muslimīn, Group for the Support of Islam and Muslims) — Al-Qaeda affiliate formed March 2017 from merger of Ansar Dine, Al-Mourabitoun, Macina Liberation Front, and Saharan branch of AQIM under Iyad Ag Ghali; ISGS (Islamic State in the Greater Sahara) — splinter from MUJAO operating across Mali-Niger-Burkina Faso tri-border region from 2015 under Adnan Abu Walid al-Sahrawi until al-Sahrawi's death August 2021 in French Operation Barkhane strike. The consequence: French Operation Barkhane ended 2022 following deteriorating France-Mali institutional relationship after May 2021 Mali military coup; Russian Wagner Group (later Africa Corps following Yevgeny Prigozhin death 23 August 2023) involvement in Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Central African Republic from 2021-2023; Mali (May 2021 coup), Burkina Faso (September 2022 coup), Niger (26 July 2023 coup) military governments operating distinct from prior French-aligned governance; Mali-Burkina Faso-Niger Alliance of Sahel States (AES) formation September 2023; continuing JNIM and ISGS operations across the region producing thousands of deaths and millions of displacements 2023-2026.

**The International Criminal Court and International Court of Justice response.** The architecture: ICJ South Africa v. Israel case — application instituting proceedings filed 29 December 2023 by South Africa alleging Israel violating obligations under Genocide Convention regarding Palestinians in Gaza; ICJ provisional measures order 26 January 2024 finding plausibility of genocide claim and ordering Israel to prevent genocidal acts, prevent and punish incitement, and ensure aid delivery; subsequent ICJ orders 28 March 2024 and 24 May 2024 strengthening provisional measures; ICJ advisory opinion 19 July 2024 finding Israeli occupation of Palestinian territory unlawful. The consequence on ICC: ICC arrest warrants issued 21 November 2024 against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and former Defence Minister Yoav Gallant on charges of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza; ICC arrest warrants issued same period against Hamas military leader Mohammed Deif (later confirmed killed July 2024) on similar charges. The consequence on Sudan: ICC continuing investigation of Sudan situation from 2005 Darfur referral including continuing institutional interest in Burhan and Hemedti accountability for 2023-present civil war operations. The consequence on Russia-Ukraine: ICC arrest warrant against Vladimir Putin issued 17 March 2023 for alleged unlawful deportation of Ukrainian children; subsequent ICC investigations of Russian operations in Ukraine continuing 2023-2026.

**The synthetic pattern.** Across the wars that wore faith — Gaza (§ I), Russia-Ukraine (§ II), Sudan (§ III), Nigeria, Myanmar, DRC, Sahel (this section) — religious framing operates as legitimising-mobilising discourse rather than as exclusive conflict-driver. The architecture: religious framing operates institutionally even where underlying conflict drivers include territorial dispute (Gaza, Ukraine), resource competition (Sudan, DRC, Sahel), ethnic conflict (Sudan, DRC, Myanmar, Nigeria), state-collapse dynamics (Sudan, Sahel), and geopolitical confrontation (Russia-Ukraine, Iran-Israel-Saudi-UAE Middle East). The pattern: religious framing is what these wars wore — the language in which they were declared (Al-Aqsa Flood, Operation Gideon's Chariots, Russian Orthodox holy war decree, Al-Baraa Bin Malik Brigade naming), the banner under which combatants were mobilised, the vehicle through which atrocity was sanctified (Kirill's "sins washed away" sermon, Hamas martyrdom framing, Buddhist nationalist Myanmar framing of operations against Rohingya), and the discourse through which they were exported to global audiences. The contemporary pattern: captured-religious vehicles increasingly provide ideological framing for armed conflict — operating as illustration of the broader contemporary captured-state-and-captured-religion configuration dossiered across the volume.

The summary of § IV: the patterns across the wars that wore faith operate as analytical articulation of contemporary armed-conflict configurations in which religious framing operates as structuring discourse. The configuration operates across Nigeria (Boko Haram, ISWAP, Middle Belt), Myanmar (Tatmadaw post-2021, Buddhist nationalist 969/Ma Ba Tha), DRC (ADF Islamist insurgency, ISCAP affiliation), and Sahel (JNIM Al-Qaeda affiliate, ISGS Islamic State Greater Sahara, Russian Wagner-Africa Corps shift); the International Criminal Court and International Court of Justice response including ICJ South Africa v. Israel and ICC arrest warrants against Netanyahu, Gallant, Hamas leadership, and Putin; and the synthetic pattern through which religious framing operates as legitimising-mobilising discourse rather than as exclusive conflict-driver. The architecture closes Chapter 30.

Chapter 30 closes Part V's opening on contemporary armed-conflict religious-configurations. Chapter 31 opens Part VI on the believers — the reality of contemporary captured-religious congregations, communities, and individual believers operating within captured-religious vehicles dossiered across the volume.

---


## Chapter 31. The Believers, Across the Traditions

Chapter 31 opens Part VI. The analysis operates as the positive-space counterpoint to the co-opted-configurations dossier of Parts II-V. The dossier of Parts II-V documented the co-opted apparatuses across every tradition — the fusions, the political-party-religious-body alignments, the state-aligned religious vehicles, the mass-atrocity configurations, the persecution architectures, the prosperity-gospel commercial captures, the foreign-state-intelligence ROC operations, the bloc-voting electoral mobilisations. This chapter documents what the believers across those traditions have shared — operating as the architectural counter-claim to the unity of the apparatuses across traditions. The evidence does not adjudicate which interpretation of that shared believer-level reality is correct; it documents the shared reality that the comparative-religion scholarly record has produced across the past century-and-a-quarter and names the contesting interpretive positions without endorsing one over another.

The documentation covers four sections: § I the shared themes documented in the comparative-religion record. From William James's 1901-1902 Gifford Lectures through Rudolf Otto's 1917 *Das Heilige*, Mircea Eliade's mid-20th-century corpus, Ninian Smart's seven-dimensional schema, John Hick's pluralist hypothesis, Joseph Campbell's monomyth, Huston Smith's *The Religions of Man*, Karen Armstrong's contemporary corpus, Wendy Doniger's comparative-mythology scholarship, and Robert N. Bellah's *Religion in Human Evolution* (2011); § II the documented contemplative-traditions overlap. The Christian, Buddhist, Islamic Sufi, Hindu, Jewish, and Daoist contemplative traditions, the comparative-contemplative scholarship of Huxley's *The Perennial Philosophy*, Forman, and Bernadette Roberts, and the contemporary contemplative-research configurations including Mind & Life Institute from 1987 and Brown / Columbia / Naropa Contemplative Studies programs; § III the documented mystical-experience parallels — W.T. Stace's *Mysticism and Philosophy* (1960), Evelyn Underhill's *Mysticism* (1911), Forman's "pure consciousness event" framework, Steven T. Katz's contextualist response, F.C. Happold's *Mysticism: A Study and an Anthology* (1963), Pahnke's Marsh Chapel Experiment 20 April 1962, the Johns Hopkins Roland Griffiths psilocybin-mystical-experience research from 2006, and David Yaden's *The Varieties of Spiritual Experience* (2022); § IV cognitive science of religion and the contesting interpretive positions. Pascal Boyer's *Religion Explained* (2001), Scott Atran's *In Gods We Trust* (2002), Harvey Whitehouse's modes-of-religiosity account, Justin Barrett's HADD account, Stewart Guthrie's anthropomorphism account, T.M. Luhrmann's *How God Becomes Real* (2020), Joseph Henrich's *The WEIRDest People in the World* (2020), Robin Dunbar's *How Religion Evolved* (2022), and the contesting perennialist position of Guénon / Schuon / Coomaraswamy / Huxley / Wilber and its contestation by Katz and post-modern scholarship.

The evidence operates against any framing that would conflate believer-level shared reality with institutional captured-apparatus reality. The dossiers of Parts II-V documented configurations operating at the-apparatus level — the vehicles bound to political parties, state-government configurations, foreign-state operations, and commercial-mobilisation infrastructures. The believers across those captured configurations exist as a distinct category — operating at the level of contemplative practice, mystical experience, ethical formation, and shared cognitive-and-cultural inheritance documented across the scholarly record dossiered here. The record covers what the scholarly record has documented at the believer level, names the contesting interpretive positions without endorsing one over another. And operates as architectural counter-claim to the unity of captured apparatuses without resolving the deeper interpretive question of whether the believer-level unity reflects a metaphysical reality, a cognitive-evolutionary substrate, a cultural-transmission pattern, or some configuration of these.

### I. The Shared Themes Documented in the Comparative-Religion Record

The shared themes documented in the comparative-religion record constitute the principal scholarly architecture against which any claim about believer-level commonality across traditions can be assessed. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) William James's 1901-1902 Gifford Lectures producing *The Varieties of Religious Experience* (1902) as foundational text. (2) Rudolf Otto's 1917 *Das Heilige* (English: *The Idea of the Holy*, 1923) introducing the "numinous" category. (3) Mircea Eliade's mid-20th-century corpus including *The Sacred and the Profane* (1957/1959) and *Patterns in Comparative Religion* (1958). (4) Ninian Smart's seven-dimensional schema articulated in *The Religious Experience of Mankind* (1969). (5) John Hick's pluralist hypothesis articulated in *An Interpretation of Religion* (1989). (6) the contemporary configuration including Karen Armstrong's *A History of God* (1993), Huston Smith's *The World's Religions* (1991), and Robert N. Bellah's *Religion in Human Evolution* (Harvard University Press, 2011).

**William James and the foundational text.** The architecture: William James (1842-1910) — American philosopher and psychologist; Harvard University; Gifford Lectures 1901-1902 at University of Edinburgh producing *The Varieties of Religious Experience: A Study in Human Nature* (Longmans, Green & Co., 1902); foundational text of comparative religious-experience scholarship; methodological positioning treating religious experience as object of empirical-psychological investigation across traditions rather than confessional-theological articulation. The Jamesian schema: distinction between "healthy-minded" and "sick souls" religious temperaments; documentation of conversion experiences across Christian, Buddhist, Hindu, and other traditions; framework of religious experience operating through "the More" — the religious individual's experience of being in contact with something beyond their ordinary self. The pattern: James establishing methodological framework for treating cross-tradition religious experience as scholarly-empirical object rather than confessional-doctrinal object.

**Rudolf Otto and the numinous.** The architecture: Rudolf Otto (1869-1937) — German Lutheran theologian; University of Marburg; *Das Heilige: Über das Irrationale in der Idee des Göttlichen und sein Verhältnis zum Rationalen* (1917) translated into English as *The Idea of the Holy* (John W. Harvey, 1923). The Ottonian schema: category of "the numinous" (from Latin *numen*, divine presence); structure of *mysterium tremendum et fascinans* — the experience of a presence that is simultaneously "wholly other," overwhelming (tremendum), and compelling (fascinans); subsequent scholarly position operating across traditions to characterise the experiential dimension of religious encounter. The pattern: Otto establishing experiential-phenomenological schema that subsequent cross-tradition scholarship has used to characterise the shared experiential dimension of religious encounter — Christian mystical vision, Sufi divine encounter, Hindu *darshan*, Buddhist enlightenment-encounter, Indigenous shamanic vision.

**Mircea Eliade and the sacred-profane distinction.** The architecture: Mircea Eliade (1907-1986) — Romanian historian of religions; University of Chicago Divinity School from 1957; principal corpus including *The Sacred and the Profane: The Nature of Religion* (1957/1959), *Patterns in Comparative Religion* (1958), *The Myth of the Eternal Return* (1949), and *A History of Religious Ideas* (3 vols, 1976-1985). The Eliadean schema: distinction between sacred and profane time and space; framework of "hierophanies" (manifestations of the sacred); cross-cultural patterns of cosmic mountain, world tree, cosmic centre (*axis mundi*); recurring patterns of initiation, ritual death-and-rebirth, sacred time inserted into profane time. The Eliadean contestation: subsequent scholarly critique by Steven Wasserstrom *Religion after Religion* (1999) of Eliade's pro-Romanian-Iron-Guard political associations and methodological assumptions; sustained scholarly defence and critical engagement with Eliadean schema across late-20th and early-21st century. The pattern: Eliadean schema establishing systematic cross-tradition pattern-recognition operating across religious-studies academic programs from 1957-present.

**Ninian Smart and the seven-dimensional schema.** The architecture: Ninian Smart (1927-2001) — British-American scholar of religious studies; founded religious studies department University of Lancaster 1967; *The Religious Experience of Mankind* (1969, subsequent editions). The Smartian schema: seven-dimensional schema for analyzing religious traditions — (1) practical and ritual dimension; (2) experiential and emotional dimension; (3) narrative and mythological dimension; (4) doctrinal and philosophical dimension; (5) ethical and legal dimension; (6) social and institutional dimension; (7) material dimension. The pattern: Smart schema providing systematic analytic apparatus widely used across religious-studies undergraduate and graduate teaching for cross-tradition analysis — operating as scholarly-pedagogical vehicle for treating religious traditions through structured comparative framework rather than confessional-apologetic framework.

**John Hick and the pluralist hypothesis.** The architecture: John Hick (1922-2012) — British philosopher of religion; Birmingham, Claremont; *An Interpretation of Religion: Human Responses to the Transcendent* (Yale University Press, 1989); *The Fifth Dimension: An Exploration of the Spiritual Realm* (1999). The Hickian position: pluralist hypothesis articulating major religious traditions as different culturally-conditioned human responses to a single transcendent reality (which Hick called "the Real"); distinguished from exclusivist (one tradition correct), inclusivist (one tradition principally correct with partial validity in others), and naturalist (no tradition tracking transcendent reality) positions. The Hickian position's reception: significant scholarly contestation including exclusivist critiques from Christian theological positions (Alvin Plantinga, William Lane Craig) and post-modern critiques (Mark C. Taylor) emphasising irreducible difference. The pattern: Hick schema operating as principal contemporary philosophical articulation of pluralist-religious-experience account — without resolving the underlying metaphysical question.

**The contemporary comparative configuration.** The architecture: Karen Armstrong (born 14 November 1944) — British author; *A History of God* (Alfred A. Knopf, 1993); *The Great Transformation* (2006) on Karl Jaspers's Axial Age framework; *The Case for God* (2009); Charter for Compassion 2009 — vehicle drawing on cross-tradition ethical-formation framework. Huston Smith (1919-2016) — American religious-studies scholar; *The Religions of Man* (1958) revised as *The World's Religions* (1991) — sustained reference text used across undergraduate teaching for cross-tradition introduction. Wendy Doniger (born 20 November 1940) — American Indologist; University of Chicago; *The Hindus: An Alternative History* (2009) producing controversy with Hindu-nationalist objections (cross-reference Chapter 22 § III); sustained comparative-mythology scholarship. Robert N. Bellah (1927-2013) — *Religion in Human Evolution: From the Paleolithic to the Axial Age* (Harvard University Press, 2011) — synthetic evolutionary-historical framework for emergence of religious-traditions from Paleolithic ritual through Axial Age (c. 800-200 BCE) traditions of Confucian China, Vedic India, Persian Zoroastrianism, Hebrew prophets, Greek philosophy. Joseph Campbell (1904-1987) — *The Hero with a Thousand Faces* (1949) producing monomyth schema subsequently popularised through George Lucas *Star Wars* and broader cinematic application; sustained scholarly contestation regarding methodological rigour.

The summary of § I: the shared themes documented in the comparative-religion record constitute the principal scholarly architecture against which any claim about believer-level commonality across traditions can be assessed. The configuration operates through James's *The Varieties of Religious Experience* (1902), Otto's *The Idea of the Holy* (1917/1923) and the numinous category, Eliade's sacred-profane framework and cross-cultural pattern-recognition corpus. Smart's seven-dimensional schema, Hick's pluralist hypothesis, Armstrong's *A History of God* (1993), Smith's *The World's Religions* (1991), Doniger's comparative-mythology scholarship, Bellah's *Religion in Human Evolution* (2011), and Campbell's monomyth schema. The architecture documents the existence of recurring themes across traditions without resolving whether those themes reflect a shared transcendent reality (Hickian pluralism), shared cognitive-evolutionary substrate (cognitive science of religion dossiered in § IV), shared cultural-transmission patterns (Bellah's framework), or some configuration of these. The contemplative-traditions overlap dossiered in § II operates as parallel documentation of believer-level commonality — operating through the contemplative-practice traditions across Christianity, Buddhism, Islam, Hinduism, Judaism, and Daoism.

---


### II. The Documented Contemplative-Traditions Overlap

The contemplative-traditions overlap is the principal documented believer-level cross-tradition commonality at the level of sustained spiritual-practice traditions. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) the Christian contemplative tradition from desert fathers through Meister Eckhart, Teresa of Ávila, John of the Cross, Thomas Merton, and Bede Griffiths. (2) the Buddhist contemplative tradition across Theravāda Vipassanā, Mahāyāna Zen. And Vajrayāna Tibetan configurations. (3) the Islamic Sufi tradition from Rabia al-Adawiyya through al-Hallaj, al-Ghazali, Ibn Arabi, and Rumi. (4) the Hindu contemplative tradition including Yoga, Advaita Vedanta, and modern figures Ramana Maharshi. Nisargadatta Maharaj, and Aurobindo. (5) the Jewish contemplative tradition including Merkabah mysticism, Kabbalah, Lurianic Kabbalah, Hasidic tradition, and Heschel. (6) the Daoist contemplative tradition and the comparative-contemplative scholarship of Huxley, Forman, and the contemporary Mind & Life Institute configurations from 1987.

**The Christian contemplative tradition.** The architecture: desert fathers tradition — Anthony the Great (c. 251-356) Egyptian desert ascetic; Evagrius Ponticus (345-399) writer on prayer and contemplation; Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (c. 5th-6th century) — *The Mystical Theology*, *The Divine Names* establishing apophatic theology (knowledge of God through negation); Meister Eckhart (c. 1260-1328) Dominican preacher producing speculative mystical theology including framework of "Godhead" (Gottheit) beyond "God" (Gott) and "ground of the soul" (Seelengrund) where divine birth occurs; *The Cloud of Unknowing* (anonymous, late 14th century English) producing apophatic contemplative-practice manual. The early-modern configuration: Teresa of Ávila (1515-1582) — Carmelite reformer; *Interior Castle* (1577) producing systematic seven-stage interior-castle schema for prayer development; John of the Cross (1542-1591) Teresian collaborator producing *Dark Night of the Soul* and *Ascent of Mount Carmel*. The modern configuration: Thomas Merton (1915-1968) Trappist monk Abbey of Gethsemani Kentucky; *Seven Storey Mountain* (1948) autobiographical; *Mystics and Zen Masters* (1967) and *The Asian Journal* (1973) producing cross-tradition contemplative dialogue including 1968 meetings with 14th Dalai Lama; Bede Griffiths (1906-1993) Benedictine monk producing sustained Christian-Hindu contemplative integration at Shantivanam Ashram India; Thomas Keating (1923-2018) Trappist abbot producing Centering Prayer movement from 1975 onward.

**The Buddhist contemplative tradition.** The architecture: Theravāda Vipassanā tradition centred on Burmese Mahasi Sayadaw (1904-1982) producing sustained 20th-century Vipassanā revival including Mahasi method of noting; Thai Forest Tradition Ajahn Chah (1918-1992) Wat Pah Pong subsequently producing Ajahn Sumedho, Ajahn Pasanno, Ajahn Brahm; S.N. Goenka (1924-2013) Indian lay teacher producing global Vipassanā Centers configuration from 1969; Theravāda configurations operating across Sri Lanka, Burma, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos. Mahāyāna Zen tradition: Bodhidharma (c. 5th-6th century) traditional founder of Chan/Zen lineage; Dōgen Zenji (1200-1253) Sōtō Zen founder producing *Shōbōgenzō* and *zazen* (seated meditation) framework; D.T. Suzuki (1870-1966) producing principal 20th-century Western transmission through *Essays in Zen Buddhism* (1927+) and broader corpus; Thich Nhat Hanh (1926-2022) Vietnamese Zen teacher producing engaged Buddhism framework and Plum Village vehicle from 1982. Vajrayāna Tibetan tradition: Padmasambhava (c. 8th century) traditional founder of Tibetan Buddhism; Tsongkhapa (1357-1419) Gelug school founder; 14th Dalai Lama Tenzin Gyatso (born 6 July 1935) — configuration cross-referenced with Chapter 23 § IV — producing sustained contemplative dialogue with Western scholarly configurations including Mind & Life Institute from 1987.

**The Islamic Sufi tradition.** The architecture: early formative figures — Rabia al-Adawiyya (c. 717-801) Iraqi female mystic producing devotional-love framework; Junayd of Baghdad (c. 830-910) "sober" Sufi school principal figure; al-Hallaj (Mansur al-Hallaj, 858-922) executed Baghdad 26 March 922 for "Ana al-Haqq" ("I am the Truth/Real") declaration; al-Ghazali (1058-1111) producing *Ihya' Ulum al-Din* (Revival of the Religious Sciences) integrating Sufi practice with orthodox Sunni framework; Ibn Arabi (1165-1240) producing *Fusus al-Hikam* (Bezels of Wisdom) and *Futuhat al-Makkiyya* (Meccan Revelations) systematic mystical-philosophical corpus; Rumi (Jalal al-Din Muhammad Rumi, 1207-1273) Persian poet producing *Masnavi* and *Divan-e Shams-e Tabrizi* sustained mystical poetic corpus and Mevlevi Order foundation. The Sufi architecture: subsequent silsila (chains of transmission) including Naqshbandiyya (Bahauddin Naqshband 1318-1389), Qadiriyya (Abdul Qadir Gilani 1078-1166), Chishtiyya (Moinuddin Chishti 1141-1236), Mevleviyya (Mevlevi Order from Rumi's followers); configuration operating across Sunni and Shi'a Islamic geographies through institutional contemplative-practice traditions including dhikr (remembrance), sama (musical-poetic gathering), muraqaba (meditative contemplation), and master-disciple transmission.

**The Hindu contemplative tradition.** The architecture: Yoga tradition Patañjali's *Yoga Sutras* (c. 200 BCE-200 CE) producing systematic ashtanga (eight-limbed) yoga path including yama, niyama, asana, pranayama, pratyahara, dharana, dhyana, samadhi; Advaita Vedanta tradition Adi Shankara (c. 788-820) producing non-dualist (advaita) Brahman-Atman identification; subsequent Advaita transmission through Vidyaranya (14th century), Sadasiva Brahmendra, and contemporary teachers. The modern Hindu configuration: Ramana Maharshi (1879-1950) Tiruvannamalai sage producing self-inquiry (atma vichara) schema — "Who am I?" method; Nisargadatta Maharaj (1897-1981) Bombay teacher producing *I Am That* (1973) corpus through translator Maurice Frydman; Ramakrishna (1836-1886) Bengali devotee producing cross-tradition devotional schema including practising Islam and Christianity to "verify" their validity; Vivekananda (1863-1902) producing 1893 World's Parliament of Religions Chicago speech and Ramakrishna Mission configuration; Aurobindo (1872-1950) producing *The Life Divine* (1939-1940) and integral yoga; Krishnamurti (Jiddu Krishnamurti, 1895-1986) producing post-Theosophical anti-organisational contemplative teaching. Cross-reference Chapter 22 § I for Hindu Captured Configurations institutional context.

**The Jewish contemplative tradition.** The architecture: Merkabah mysticism (Hekhalot literature c. 1st-7th century) — heavenly-palace ascent mystical tradition; Kabbalah — *Sefer Yetzirah* (Book of Formation, c. 200-900 CE) producing letters-and-creation framework; *Zohar* attributed to Moses de León (c. 1250-1305 13th-century Spain) producing principal medieval Kabbalistic corpus through Aramaic pseudepigraphic attribution to Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai (2nd century); Isaac Luria (1534-1572) Safed producing Lurianic Kabbalah schema including *tzimtzum* (divine contraction), *shevirat ha-kelim* (breaking of vessels), and *tikkun olam* (repair of the world). The Hasidic configuration: Baal Shem Tov (Israel ben Eliezer, 1698-1760) Polish Jewish mystic producing Hasidic devotional movement emphasising joyful devotion, divine immanence, and tzaddik (righteous one) architecture; subsequent Hasidic transmission through Maggid of Mezeritch (Dov Ber, c. 1700-1772), Schneur Zalman of Liadi (1745-1812, founder of Chabad), and contemporary Hasidic dynasties. The modern Jewish contemplative configuration: Abraham Joshua Heschel (1907-1972) Polish-American rabbi producing *God in Search of Man* (1955), *Man Is Not Alone* (1951), *The Sabbath* (1951) integrating phenomenological framework with Jewish prophetic tradition; Heschel's positioning including 1965 Selma march with Martin Luther King Jr. Cross-reference Chapter 20 for Jewish Captured Configurations institutional context.

**The Daoist contemplative tradition and comparative-contemplative scholarship.** The architecture: Daoist tradition — Laozi (c. 6th-4th century BCE) attributed *Daodejing* producing wu wei (non-action) and dao (way) framework; Zhuangzi (c. 4th century BCE) *Zhuangzi* corpus producing skeptical-philosophical contemplative tradition; subsequent Quanzhen (Complete Reality) and Zhengyi (Orthodox Unity) traditions producing systematic Daoist contemplative-practice schema including neidan (internal alchemy), zuowang (sitting in oblivion), and other practice configurations. The comparative-contemplative scholarship: Aldous Huxley *The Perennial Philosophy* (Harper & Brothers, 1945) producing principal mid-20th-century synthetic articulation of cross-tradition contemplative commonality; Robert K.C. Forman ed., *The Problem of Pure Consciousness* (Oxford University Press, 1990) producing scholarly debate on "pure consciousness event" framework; Bernadette Roberts *The Experience of No-Self* (1982) producing Christian-contemplative articulation drawing cross-tradition account. The contemporary contemplative-research configurations: Mind & Life Institute founded 1987 by Adam Engle, Francisco Varela (1946-2001), and 14th Dalai Lama producing sustained dialogue between Buddhist contemplative tradition and Western neuroscience including Mind & Life Dialogues at Dharamsala from 1987; Contemplative Studies programs at Brown University (founded 2014), Columbia, Naropa Institute (founded 1974 by Chögyam Trungpa), University of Virginia Contemplative Sciences Center, and other configurations.

The summary of § II: the contemplative-traditions overlap is the principal documented believer-level cross-tradition commonality at the level of sustained spiritual-practice traditions. The configuration operates through the Christian contemplative tradition from desert fathers through Merton and Griffiths, the Buddhist contemplative tradition across Theravāda Vipassanā / Mahāyāna Zen / Vajrayāna Tibetan configurations, the Islamic Sufi tradition from Rabia al-Adawiyya through Rumi and the silsila architecture. The Hindu contemplative tradition through Patañjali and Shankara to Ramana Maharshi and Aurobindo, the Jewish contemplative tradition through Merkabah, Kabbalah, Lurianic Kabbalah, and Heschel, and the Daoist contemplative tradition with the comparative-contemplative scholarship of Huxley, Forman, and the contemporary Mind & Life Institute / Contemplative Studies configurations. The mystical-experience parallels dossiered in § III operate as documentation at the experiential rather than practice-tradition level — operating through the W.T. Stace, Underhill, Forman, Katz, Pahnke, Griffiths, and Yaden scholarly configurations.

---


### III. The Documented Mystical-Experience Parallels

The mystical-experience parallels are the principal documented believer-level commonality at the experiential level — operating through the scholarly characterisation of mystical experience across traditions. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) W.T. Stace's *Mysticism and Philosophy* (1960) producing the seven common characteristics framework; (2) Evelyn Underhill's *Mysticism* (1911) producing the five-stage developmental schema; (3) Robert K.C. Forman's "pure consciousness event" schema articulated in *The Problem of Pure Consciousness* (1990); (4) Steven T. Katz's contextualist response in *Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis* (1978) and subsequent corpus; (5) the Marsh Chapel Experiment 20 April 1962 (Walter Pahnke) and subsequent Johns Hopkins Roland Griffiths research from 2006; (6) the contemporary research configuration including David Yaden's *The Varieties of Spiritual Experience* (Oxford University Press, 2022) and Andrew Newberg's religious-experience neuroscience.

**W.T. Stace and the seven common characteristics.** The architecture: Walter Terence Stace (1886-1967) — Princeton philosopher; *Mysticism and Philosophy* (Macmillan, 1960) producing principal mid-20th-century scholarly position for cross-tradition mystical-experience analysis. The Stacean schema: distinction between extrovertive mysticism (perception of unity in multiplicity of natural world) and introvertive mysticism (perception of unity by withdrawal from sensory contents to "pure consciousness"); seven characteristics of introvertive mystical experience — (1) unitary consciousness, (2) non-spatial and non-temporal, (3) sense of objectivity or reality, (4) blessedness, peace, joy, (5) feeling of holy/sacred/divine, (6) paradoxicality, (7) ineffability. The pattern: Stacean schema providing systematic analytical vocabulary used across late-20th-century mystical-experience scholarship; reception including subsequent empirical operationalisation through Mysticism Scale (Hood, 1975) producing quantitative cross-tradition measurement. The contestation: Stacean schema treated as articulating perennialist position by Katz contextualist critique; Stacean response that contextualist position cannot account for cross-tradition reportorial commonality.

**Evelyn Underhill and the five-stage schema.** The architecture: Evelyn Underhill (1875-1941) — British author; *Mysticism: A Study in the Nature and Development of Spiritual Consciousness* (Methuen, 1911) producing principal early-20th-century systematic framework. The Underhillian schema: five-stage developmental schema — (1) awakening of the self (initial mystical encounter), (2) purgation (purification of self), (3) illumination (sustained sense of divine presence), (4) dark night of the soul (period of apparent divine absence following John of the Cross framework), (5) union (sustained unitive consciousness). The pattern: Underhillian schema operating across early-20th-century Christian and broader contemplative scholarship; subsequent reception including critique that schema imposed Christian-tradition developmental architecture on cross-tradition material — itself illustrating the contested-perennialism debate dossiered below.

**Robert K.C. Forman and the "pure consciousness event."** The architecture: Robert K.C. Forman — philosopher of religion; *The Problem of Pure Consciousness: Mysticism and Philosophy* (Oxford University Press, 1990); *The Innate Capacity: Mysticism, Psychology, and Philosophy* (Oxford University Press, 1998). The Formanian schema: "pure consciousness event" (PCE) — experience of consciousness without content, characterised by awareness-without-object; argument that PCE represents irreducible cross-tradition experiential commonality not adequately explained by contextualist (tradition-specific) interpretive positions; documentation of PCE reportorial commonality across Christian (Eckhart, *Cloud of Unknowing*), Buddhist (Theravāda *nirodha*, Zen *kensho*), Hindu (*nirvikalpa samadhi*, *turiya*), and other traditions. The Formanian contestation: response to Steven T. Katz constructivist position; subsequent scholarly debate continuing through 1990s-2010s; contestation operating through philosophy-of-religion and religious-studies journals.

**Steven T. Katz and the contextualist response.** The architecture: Steven T. Katz (born 1944) — Boston University philosopher of religion; *Mysticism and Philosophical Analysis* (Oxford University Press, 1978); subsequent corpus including *Mysticism and Sacred Scripture* (Oxford University Press, 2000), *Mysticism and Language* (1992), *Comparative Mysticism: An Anthology of Original Sources* (2013). The Katzian position: contextualist position arguing that mystical experiences are not "pure" but are always conditioned by the cultural-religious-conceptual framework of the experiencer; argument that what appears as cross-tradition commonality is artefact of scholarly position-imposition rather than experiential reality; experience and interpretation cannot be cleanly separated. The reception: Katz position providing principal philosophical-theoretical articulation of contextualist alternative to perennialist position; subsequent scholarly debate operating across Forman, Robert M. Gimello, Bernard McGinn, and broader configurations through 1990s-2020s.

**The Marsh Chapel Experiment and the psychedelic-mystical-experience research.** The architecture: Marsh Chapel Experiment ("Good Friday Experiment") 20 April 1962 Boston University Marsh Chapel — Walter N. Pahnke (1931-1971) Harvard doctoral student conducting double-blind study under Timothy Leary supervision; 20 Protestant divinity students; 10 received psilocybin and 10 received niacin placebo; subsequent ratings against mystical-experience criteria producing finding that psilocybin group reported mystical-experience characteristics at significantly higher rates than placebo group. The Johns Hopkins continuation: Roland Griffiths (1946-2023) Johns Hopkins Psychedelic Research Unit producing post-2006 psilocybin-mystical-experience research configuration; principal 2006 paper "Psilocybin can occasion mystical-type experiences having considerable and sustained personal meaning and spiritual significance" (*Psychopharmacology* 187: 268-283) producing renewed scientific engagement with mystical-experience research; subsequent Griffiths-team research including 14-month follow-up studies, dose-response studies, and clinical-population applications.
