Chapter 35 dossiers the named figures and movements across traditions that have publicly resisted capture, the documented resistance movements, and the documented institutional responses to the resistance. Chapter 36 dossiers what happens if the church continues. Chapter 37 dossiers what happens if the church is refused. Chapter 38 closes the volume.

The volume GUIDANCE establishes the substance of this chapter: *"The shared exit infrastructure — the named figures and institutions across traditions who have publicly resisted capture, the documented resistance movements, and the documented institutional responses to the resistance."* Chapter 35 dossiers that infrastructure. The analysis does not advocate one resistance configuration over another. The evidence does not present a programme. What follows covers what is already happening — the figures who have resisted, the movements that have organised, and the responses they have received.

The argument is organised in four sections. Section I dossiers the exits framing — what "solutions" means in the architecture of this volume; the distinction between exit (a believer choice) and reform (an institutional outcome); the limits of what this chapter can offer; the relationship between Chapter 35 and the counter-claim established in Chapter 31. Section II dossiers named resisters across the traditions — figures who have publicly resisted capture from inside or alongside each tradition. Section III dossiers documented resistance movements — institutional and grassroots configurations that have organised against capture. Section IV dossiers documented institutional responses to the resistance — what the seized apparatus has done in response to those who resist.

### I. The Exits Framing: What "Solutions" Means in This Volume

The exits framing has four principal nodes: (1) the distinction between exit and reform; (2) the limits of what this chapter can offer; (3) the relationship between exit, the believer, and captured apparatus; (4) the architectural position of Part VII within the volume.

**The distinction between exit and reform.** The architecture: the chapter distinguishes between two principal current configurations through which the apparatus is contested. The first configuration is reform — the configuration in which the apparatus is reformed from within through institutional accountability work, governance changes, leadership transitions, and broader reform efforts. The dossiers of Chapter 33 § IV documented the contemporary institutional-accountability vehicles operating across the apparatus — CIASE Report October 2021, MHG Study September 2018, Australia Royal Commission December 2017, Murphy Report November 2009, Ryan Report May 2009, Boston Globe Spotlight 2002, Pennsylvania Grand Jury August 2018, Karadima Chilean case from 2010, Cardinal Becciu Vatican criminal trial 2021-2023, broader configurations. These are reform-track work. The figures and bodies operate to reform the apparatus from within. The second configuration is exit — the believer-level configuration in which the believer or the community of believers leaves the apparatus or constructs alternative structures alongside it. It operates at several scales: individual exit from a single denomination or jurisdiction; communal departure including breakaway movements, alternative congregations, deliberate communities; broader cultural departure including populations leaving organised religion entirely while retaining the underlying tradition in personal or familial practice. The evidence covers figures and movements operating across both configurations.

**The limits of what this chapter can offer.** The architecture: the chapter dossiers what is already happening. The evidence does not advocate one resistance configuration over another. The record does not present a programme. The chapter does not tell the believer what to do. The reasons are three. First reason: the architectural counter-claim established in Chapter 31 — the believers across the traditions are not the demons; the believers are the inheritors of the underlying tradition. The volume's authorial position is documentation, not direction. Direction belongs to the believer. The believer chooses what to do with the documentation. Second reason: the dossiers of Chapters 14-34 establish the contemporary state of the compromised apparatus across every tradition. Different people across different settings face different operational scales of capture, different architectures of capture, different cultural and legal contexts. A single programme would misrepresent the diversity of what believers face. Third reason: the resistance configurations dossiered in §§ II-IV operate across the spectrum from reform-from-within to exit-altogether to construction-alongside. The configurations are pluralistic by structure. The analysis respects the pluralism.

**The relationship between exit, the believer, and the co-opted apparatus.** The architecture: the chapter operates from the counter-claim established in Chapter 31 and named through Chapters 32-34. Believers across the traditions are not the demon. The co-opted apparatus is the demon. The faith is not the demon. The captured name spoken by the apparatus is the mask. The pastoral register the apparatus speaks is the language of the faith — genuine, not corrupted, spoken by an apparatus that operates in financial-political coordination with the four-century mechanism documented in Volume One. The affective register through which the apparatus presents itself to the believer is the smile. The taking is what the smile is for. The analysis operates from these dossiers. Exit, in this framing, is not exit from the faith. Exit is leaving the apparatus. Those who leave the apparatus do not abandon the underlying tradition. The believer who leaves the apparatus separates the tradition from the structure built on top of the tradition. The tradition continues. The apparatus is what the exit is from.

**The architectural position of Part VII within the volume.** The architecture: Part VII closes the substance of the volume. Chapter 35 dossiers the resistance configurations across traditions. Chapter 36 dossiers what happens if the church continues — the projected operational scale of the apparatus across the coming decades under continuation of current trends. Chapter 37 dossiers what happens if the church is refused — the configurations through which the apparatus loses operational capacity through believer-level departure, reform-track accountability, broader cultural shifts. Chapter 38 closes the volume by returning to the believer and the demon and naming the closing distinction. Part VII is the practical part of the volume. The dossiers of Chapters 14-34 establish what the co-opted apparatus is. Part VII establishes what is being done about it and what could be done about it.

The summary of § I: the exits framing of this chapter operates through four principal nodes — the distinction between exit and reform; the limits of what this chapter can offer; the relationship between exit, the believer, and the apparatus; the architectural position of Part VII within the volume. The documentation covers what is already happening. The record does not advocate. The analysis does not direct. The analysis respects the pluralism of the resistance configurations across traditions. The named resisters across the traditions dossiered in § II constitute the figures operating across this exit infrastructure.

---


### II. Named Resisters Across the Traditions

The named resisters across the traditions are documented through the principal contemporary figures who have publicly resisted capture from inside or alongside each tradition. The architecture has eleven nodes per tradition family: (1) Catholic resisters; (2) Russian Orthodox resisters; (3) Sunni Islamic resisters; (4) Shia Islamic resisters; (5) Jewish resisters; (6) Hindu resisters; (7) Buddhist resisters; (8) Sikh resisters; (9) Protestant evangelical resisters; (10) prosperity-gospel resisters; (11) Indigenous resisters.

**Catholic.** The architecture: liberation theology lineage produces the principal current Catholic resistance line. The named figures: Gustavo Gutiérrez (1928-2024) Peruvian Dominican theologian whose *A Theology of Liberation* (Lima 1971, English translation Orbis 1973) articulated the foundational position of liberation theology including the preferential option for the poor; Óscar Romero (1917-1980) Archbishop of San Salvador assassinated 24 March 1980 while saying Mass at the chapel of the Divine Providence Hospital in San Salvador in retaliation for his denunciations of Salvadoran state violence — canonised by Pope Francis 14 October 2018; Jon Sobrino (born 1938) Spanish-Salvadoran Jesuit theologian; Leonardo Boff (born 1938) Brazilian Franciscan theologian silenced by the Vatican Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith in 1985 under Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger (later Pope Benedict XVI); Ignacio Ellacuría (1930-1989) Spanish-Salvadoran Jesuit philosopher killed at Universidad Centroamericana in San Salvador 16 November 1989 along with five fellow Jesuits + housekeeper and her daughter; broader liberation theology configuration across Latin American progressive figures. The Catholic Worker Movement configuration: Dorothy Day (1897-1980), a journalist and activist, co-founded the Catholic Worker Movement in May 1933 with Peter Maurin (1877-1949); the movement operates contemporary hospitality houses across the US and worldwide producing Catholic-anarchist-pacifist line. Catholic Church reform names: James Carroll (born 1943) former priest and journalist on Catholic Church accountability; Thomas Doyle Catholic priest and canonist on Catholic Church abuse accountability; Hans Küng (1928-2021) Swiss Catholic theologian on Catholic Church reform whose teaching authority was withdrawn by the Vatican 18 December 1979; broader Vatican II reform. Catholic survivor-and-accountability configuration: SNAP (Survivors Network of those Abused by Priests) founded 1988 by Barbara Blaine (1956-2017) producing a leading US Catholic survivor-network body; Voice of the Faithful founded 2002 in response to Boston Globe Spotlight investigation producing a leading US Catholic lay-reform group.

**Russian Orthodox.** The architecture: current ROC resistance centred on figures defrocked or disciplined by Patriarch Kirill for criticism of the war in Ukraine. Names: Andrei Kuraev (born 15 February 1963) ROC priest and theologian defrocked in 2024 by the Patriarchal Court of the Russian Orthodox Church on charges related to his blog posts including criticism of ROC institutional positioning on the war in Ukraine; Aleksey Uminsky (born 1960) ROC priest dismissed January 2024 from his Moscow parish for refusing to read the prayer for "Holy Rus" victory in Ukraine; Public Orthodoxy initiative operating through Fordham University Orthodox Christian Studies Center producing the principal academic-theological resistance to ROC institutional positioning on Ukraine war; broader Orthodox-Christian-civil-society bodies.

**Sunni Islamic.** The architecture: current Sunni Islamic reform spans academic, theological, and civil-society figures. Names: Khaled Abou El Fadl (born 1963) Egyptian-American UCLA law professor + author *The Great Theft: Wrestling Islam from the Extremists* (HarperOne, 2005) on Islamic legal reform; Ziauddin Sardar (born 1951) British-Pakistani scholar and writer on Islamic reform; Amina Wadud (born 1952) African-American Islamic studies scholar + author *Qur'an and Woman: Rereading the Sacred Text from a Woman's Perspective* (Oxford University Press, 1999) representing a leading Islamic feminist position; Asma Barlas (born 1950) Pakistani-American scholar producing further Islamic feminist scholarship; broader Islamic-reform groups. The Tariq Ramadan position is contested: Tariq Ramadan (born 1962) Swiss Islamic scholar previously positioned as principal contemporary Islamic reform figure was charged in France beginning 2018 with multiple counts of rape; trials ongoing 2018-2025; institutional position contested by allegations.

**Shia Islamic.** The architecture: current Shia Islamic reform centred on Iranian religious-political reformist figures. Names: Abdolkarim Soroush (born 1945) Iranian religious-political reformist exiled from Iran; a leading Iranian reformist Islamic thinker producing critique of Wilayat al-Faqih configuration; Mohsen Kadivar (born 1959) Iranian cleric and scholar advocating an Islamic-democratic synthesis, exiled from Iran; broader Iranian reform movements including the Green Movement 2009 (post-election protests June 2009-February 2010) and the 2022-2023 Mahsa Amini protests (September 2022 death in police custody of Mahsa Jina Amini producing sustained protest configuration across Iran).

**Jewish.** The architecture: current Jewish progressive line spans rabbinic, political-advocacy, and activist configurations. Names and bodies: J Street founded April 2008 by Jeremy Ben-Ami (born 1962) as the principal current US-Jewish-progressive Israel-advocacy group; T'ruah: The Rabbinic Call for Human Rights founded 2002 by Rabbi Jill Jacobs (born 1975) producing the principal current rabbinic human-rights group; If Not Now founded 2014 as the principal current US Jewish-youth Israel-Palestine group; Jewish Voice for Peace (JVP) founded 1996 producing the principal current US Jewish-progressive anti-occupation group; New Israel Fund founded 1979 by Eleanor Friedman producing the principal current US Jewish-progressive Israel-civil-society body; Sharon Brous (born 1973) Rabbi at IKAR Los Angeles; Sharon Kleinbaum (born 1959) Rabbi Emerita at Congregation Beit Simchat Torah New York; broader Jewish-progressive bodies across multiple denominations.

**Hindu.** The architecture: current Hindutva-critic line spans academic, journalistic, and civil-society figures. Names: Aakar Patel (born 1971) journalist + former Amnesty India executive director; Romila Thapar (born 30 November 1931) Indian historian on Hindutva historiography + author *The Past as Present: Forging Contemporary Identities Through History* (Aleph, 2014); Ramachandra Guha (born 29 April 1958) Indian historian on Indian secular tradition + author of multiple volumes on Indian post-1947 history; Arundhati Roy (born 24 November 1961) Indian writer on Indian state-religious configurations + author *The God of Small Things* (1997, Booker Prize) and subsequent political essays; Pratik Sinha co-founder of AltNews (founded February 2017) producing the principal current Indian fact-checking outlet documenting Hindutva disinformation; Mohammed Zubair AltNews co-founder; broader Hindutva-critic positions across Indian civil society.

**Buddhist.** The architecture: current Engaged Buddhism line spans Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana traditions. Names: Sulak Sivaraksa (born 27 March 1933) Thai Buddhist activist and INEB (International Network of Engaged Buddhists) founder 1989 producing the principal current transnational Engaged Buddhist body; Bhikkhu Bodhi (born 10 December 1944) American Theravada monk and translator on Buddhist social engagement; Thich Nhat Hanh (1926-2022) Vietnamese Zen master and Order of Interbeing founder 1966 producing principal Engaged Buddhist body; Joanna Macy (born 2 May 1929) Buddhist scholar and ecological activist; Ashin Issariya (also known as King Zero, born 1959) Myanmar monk leader of Saffron Revolution September-October 2007 against military junta; broader Engaged Buddhism bodies.

**Sikh.** The architecture: current Sikh civil-society line spans human-rights, humanitarian, and reform configurations. Names and bodies: Jaswant Singh Khalra (1952-disappeared 6 September 1995) Punjab human-rights activist who documented Punjab Police extrajudicial-killing and disappearances of Sikhs during 1980s-1990s Punjab insurgency; Khalra Mission Committee continues his work; Khalsa Aid founded 1999 by Ravi Singh producing the principal current Sikh humanitarian body operating across global crisis zones; SikhsForJustice founded 2007 producing the principal current Sikh-political-advocacy body; broader Sikh civil-society bodies including critics of SGPC institutional capture across Punjab and diaspora settings.

**Protestant evangelical.** The architecture: current post-evangelical line spans theological, journalistic, and reform-from-within configurations. Names: Jim Wallis (born 4 June 1948) Sojourners founder 1971 + author *God's Politics: Why the Right Gets It Wrong and the Left Doesn't Get It* (HarperOne, 2005); Tony Campolo (1935-2024) sociologist and evangelist; Brian McLaren (born 1956) Emergent Church figure + author *A New Kind of Christianity* (HarperOne, 2010); Russell Moore (born 9 October 1971) former ERLC (Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission of Southern Baptist Convention) head + *Christianity Today* editor-in-chief from August 2021 + author *Losing Our Religion: An Altar Call for Evangelical America* (Sentinel, 2023) on critique of Trump-era evangelicalism; David French (born 24 January 1969) lawyer + writer at *The Dispatch* + *The New York Times* from 2023 on critique of Trump-era evangelicalism; Beth Moore (born 16 June 1957) Bible teacher critical of Trump-era evangelicalism including March 2021 exit from Southern Baptist Convention; broader post-evangelical positions.

**Prosperity-gospel.** The architecture: current Pentecostal-reform body critical of prosperity-gospel architecture. Names: Costi Hinn (born 28 December 1985, nephew of Benny Hinn) whose For the Gospel ministry produces the the principal current US Pentecostal critique of prosperity-gospel; broader Pentecostal-reform bodies across multiple national contexts producing critique of prosperity-gospel financial architecture dossiered in Chapter 27 § I + Chapter 33 § III.

**Indigenous.** The architecture: current Indigenous resistance line spans Land Back movement, sacred-site protection, and legal-advocacy configurations. Names and bodies: Winona LaDuke (born 18 August 1959) Anishinaabe activist + author *All Our Relations: Native Struggles for Land and Life* (South End Press, 1999); Standing Rock Sioux protest 2016-2017 against Dakota Access Pipeline producing the principal current US Indigenous-rights movement; Mauna Kea protest from 2014 against Thirty Meter Telescope on sacred Hawaiian site; Oak Flat (Chí'chil Biłdagoteel) protest against Rio Tinto / BHP Resolution Copper mine on sacred Apache site; Wet'suwet'en Coastal GasLink contestation in British Columbia from 2018; broader Indigenous Land Back movements across North America, Pacific, Latin American, Sahel, broader geographies; Native American Church peyote-NAC legal advocacy against Church of Gaia ARRA exemption attempts 2025 dossiered in Chapter 33 § IV + Chapter 33 § IV.

The summary of § II: the resisters across the traditions are documented through eleven principal nodes producing the contemporary figure-level architecture of resistance to capture. The configuration operates through Gutiérrez + Romero + Sobrino + Boff + Ellacuría + Dorothy Day + Catholic Worker + James Carroll + Thomas Doyle + Hans Küng + SNAP + Voice of the Faithful (Catholic). Kuraev + Uminsky + Public Orthodoxy + Fordham Orthodox Christian Studies Center (Russian Orthodox). Abou El Fadl + Sardar + Wadud + Barlas (Sunni Islamic). Soroush + Kadivar + Green Movement 2009 + Mahsa Amini protests 2022 (Shia Islamic). J Street + T'ruah + If Not Now + JVP + New Israel Fund + Brous + Kleinbaum (Jewish). Aakar Patel + Thapar + Guha + Roy + AltNews (Hindu); Sulak Sivaraksa + INEB + Bhikkhu Bodhi + Thich Nhat Hanh + Joanna Macy + Ashin Issariya (Buddhist); Jaswant Singh Khalra + Khalsa Aid + SikhsForJustice (Sikh); Wallis + Campolo + McLaren + Russell Moore + David French + Beth Moore (Protestant evangelical); Costi Hinn (prosperity-gospel); LaDuke + Standing Rock + Mauna Kea + Oak Flat + Wet'suwet'en + Native American Church (Indigenous). The documented resistance movements dossiered in § III constitute the grassroots configurations through which these figures and others have organised against capture.

---


### III. Documented Resistance Movements

The documented resistance movements operate at scales beyond individual figures. The architecture has nine principal nodes: (1) Vatican II reform; (2) Catholic Church abuse accountability movement; (3) liberation theology; (4) Catholic Worker Movement; (5) Engaged Buddhism; (6) Islamic reform movements; (7) Jewish progressive movements; (8) Hindu civil-society resistance + Indigenous Land Back works; (9) survivor networks across traditions.

**Vatican II reform.** The architecture: Second Vatican Council 1962-1965 under Pope John XXIII (called the council 25 January 1959; convened 11 October 1962; died 3 June 1963) and Pope Paul VI (continued council from 21 June 1963 election through 8 December 1965 closing). The principal documents producing institutional-reform outcomes: *Nostra Aetate* (Declaration on the Relation of the Church to Non-Christian Religions, 28 October 1965) producing principal Catholic position on Jews and on non-Christian religions including the formal repudiation of the charge of deicide against the Jewish people; *Dignitatis Humanae* (Declaration on Religious Freedom, 7 December 1965) producing principal Catholic position on religious freedom and the relationship between Church and state; *Sacrosanctum Concilium* (Constitution on the Sacred Liturgy, 4 December 1963) producing vernacular-liturgy reform; *Lumen Gentium* (Dogmatic Constitution on the Church, 21 November 1964) producing reform of Catholic ecclesiology including the priesthood of the laity; *Gaudium et Spes* (Pastoral Constitution on the Church in the Modern World, 7 December 1965) producing principal Catholic position on social-political questions. The current status: Vatican II continues as the principal current reference point for Catholic reform efforts across multiple sites of contestation including liturgical reform, lay participation, ecumenical and interfaith engagement.

**Catholic Church abuse accountability.** The architecture: contemporary survivor-and-accountability configuration spanning multiple national investigations dossiered in Chapter 33 § IV. The principal national investigations: CIASE Report (Commission indépendante sur les abus sexuels dans l'Église, Sauvé Report) published October 2021 — documented 216,000 victims of French the the the French Church's clerical sexual abuse 1950-2020; MHG Study (Sexueller Missbrauch an Minderjährigen durch katholische Priester) published September 2018 — documented 3,677 victims and 1,670 accused clerics in German Church 1946-2014; Boston Globe Spotlight investigation 2002 producing the initial systematic exposure of Catholic Church abuse pattern; subsequent USCCB Dallas Charter (June 2002), Pennsylvania Grand Jury Report (August 2018) documenting 1,000+ victims across six Pennsylvania dioceses 1947-2018, Royal Commission into Institutional Responses to Child Sexual Abuse Final Report (Australia, December 2017), Commission of Investigation Report into the Archdiocese of Dublin (Murphy Report, November 2009), Commission to Inquire into Child Abuse (Ryan Report, May 2009), Chilean Karadima case from 2010 + 2018 mass-resignation of 34 Chilean bishops, broader national investigations. The institutional-architecture configuration: the accountability movement produces contemporary documentation of Catholic abuse pattern at scale across multiple jurisdictions.

**Liberation theology.** The architecture: liberation theology from Gustavo Gutiérrez 1971 produces principal Latin American progressive line. The Medellín Conference (Second General Conference of Latin American Bishops, August-September 1968 in Medellín, Colombia, CELAM II) produced principal articulation of preferential option for the poor and Latin American Catholic-progressive line; the Puebla Conference (Third General Conference, January-February 1979 in Puebla, Mexico, CELAM III) produced further articulation under Pope John Paul II; the base ecclesial communities (CEBs, Comunidades Eclesiales de Base) movement from 1960s-1970s producing grassroots Catholic-progressive configuration across Latin America and broader configurations. Liberation theology continues as the principal progressive position across Latin American and broader geographies despite contestation under John Paul II (1978-2005) and Benedict XVI (2005-2013) including the Vatican Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith's two instructions on liberation theology (*Libertatis Nuntius* 1984, *Libertatis Conscientia* 1986) and the silencing of Leonardo Boff 1985; later partial rehabilitation under Pope Francis (2013-2025) including the canonisation of Óscar Romero (14 October 2018) and broader institutional positioning.

**Catholic Worker.** The architecture: Catholic Worker Movement founded May 1933 by Dorothy Day and Peter Maurin produces the principal current Catholic anarchist-pacifist line. The institutional structure: the movement operates contemporary hospitality houses across the US and worldwide producing voluntary-poverty, hospitality to the homeless, pacifism, broader Catholic radical positioning. Currently estimated 200+ Catholic Worker houses worldwide producing the principal current US radical lay tradition across the post-1933 period.

**Engaged Buddhism.** The architecture: Engaged Buddhism from Thich Nhat Hanh's articulation of the term in the 1960s produces principal contemporary transnational Buddhist-progressive configuration. The apparatus: Order of Interbeing founded by Thich Nhat Hanh 1966 producing the principal Engaged Buddhist body; Plum Village monastery in Dordogne, France from 1982 producing the principal Vietnamese-Buddhist-progressive transnational presence; INEB (International Network of Engaged Buddhists) founded 1989 by Sulak Sivaraksa producing the leading transnational Engaged Buddhist coordination. Engaged Buddhism continues as the principal current transnational Buddhist-progressive line across multiple national settings.

**Islamic reform movements.** The architecture: current Islamic reform across academic, theological, and civil-society contexts. The principal configurations: contemporary Islamic feminist scholarship including Amina Wadud, Asma Barlas, Riffat Hassan, Margot Badran producing principal contemporary Islamic feminist position; contemporary Islamic legal-reform scholarship including Khaled Abou El Fadl, Mohammad Hashim Kamali producing principal contemporary Islamic legal-reform position; Iranian Green Movement 2009 (post-election protests June 2009-February 2010) producing principal contemporary Iranian political-religious reform movement; 2022-2023 Mahsa Amini protests (September 2022 death in police custody of Mahsa Jina Amini producing sustained protest configuration across Iran from September 2022 through 2023) producing principal contemporary Iranian woman-life-freedom movement. Islamic reform continues across contested institutional and political contexts.

**Jewish progressive movements.** The architecture: current Jewish progressive line spans rabbinic, political-advocacy, and activist configurations. The principal organisations: J Street founded April 2008 by Jeremy Ben-Ami; T'ruah: The Rabbinic Call for Human Rights founded 2002 by Rabbi Jill Jacobs; If Not Now founded April 2014 in opposition to organised American Jewish institutional positioning on the Israel-Palestine conflict; Jewish Voice for Peace (JVP) founded 1996 in Berkeley California producing principal contemporary US Jewish-progressive anti-occupation body; New Israel Fund founded 1979 producing principal contemporary US Jewish-progressive Israel-civil-society body. Jewish progressive movements continue to operate as the principal current contestation of mainstream American Jewish institutional positioning on the Israel-Palestine conflict, particularly following the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and subsequent Israeli military operations in Gaza dossiered Chapter 30 § I.

**Hindu civil-society resistance + Indigenous Land Back works.** The architecture: contemporary Hindu civil-society and Indigenous Land Back works operate across academic, journalistic, and political-advocacy configurations. The Hindu civil-society line: anti-Hindutva academic and journalistic positioning across Indian universities and media including JNU (Jawaharlal Nehru University) academic configurations, AltNews founded February 2017 by Pratik Sinha and Mohammed Zubair producing the principal Indian fact-checking outlet documenting Hindutva disinformation, broader Indian civil-society configurations under contemporary BJP government institutional pressure. The Indigenous Land Back work: Standing Rock 2016-2017 (Dakota Access Pipeline protest April 2016 through February 2017); Mauna Kea 2019 protest configuration against Thirty Meter Telescope; Oak Flat (Chí'chil Biłdagoteel) ongoing legal contestation including Apache Stronghold v. United States case through 2025; Wet'suwet'en Coastal GasLink contestation in British Columbia from 2018; Pope Francis Vatican statement on Doctrine of Discovery (30 March 2023) repudiating the 15th-century papal bulls that legitimated European colonial expropriation of Indigenous lands; broader Indigenous-rights work.

**Survivor networks across traditions.** The architecture: contemporary survivor networks across multiple religious traditions producing principal contemporary accountability work across captured-apparatus abuse architecture. The principal configurations: SNAP (Survivors Network of those Abused by Priests) from 1988 producing the leading US Catholic survivor network; Bishop Accountability documentation work from 2003 producing principal contemporary online archive of Catholic abuse cases; Sikh survivor networks responding to multiple gurdwara and sant-tradition abuse exposures; Buddhist survivor networks responding to Tibetan and Theravada abuse exposures including the Sogyal Rinpoche revelations in 2017 and other figures; Jewish survivor networks responding to ultra-Orthodox and broader Jewish-institutional abuse exposures including ZA'AKAH from 2013; Islamic survivor networks responding to madrasa and mosque-based abuse exposures including FACE (Facing Abuse in Community Environments) and broader configurations.

The summary of § III: the documented resistance movements operate at scale beyond individual figures across nine nodes. The configuration operates through Vatican II reform (1962-1965). Catholic Church abuse accountability movement (CIASE + MHG + Boston Globe + PA Grand Jury + Australia Royal Commission + Murphy + Ryan + Karadima). Liberation theology (Gutiérrez 1971 + Medellín 1968 + Puebla 1979 + CEBs). Catholic Worker Movement (Day + Maurin 1933). Engaged Buddhism (Thich Nhat Hanh + Order of Interbeing 1966 + INEB 1989). Islamic reform movements (Islamic feminist scholarship + Islamic legal-reform scholarship + Green Movement 2009 + Mahsa Amini protests 2022). Jewish progressive movements (J Street 2008 + T'ruah 2002 + If Not Now 2014 + JVP 1996 + New Israel Fund 1979); Hindu civil-society + Indigenous Land Back (AltNews 2017 + Standing Rock 2016-2017 + Mauna Kea 2019 + Oak Flat + Wet'suwet'en); survivor networks (SNAP 1988 + Bishop Accountability 2003 + broader networks across multiple traditions). The documented institutional responses to the resistance dossiered in § IV document the contemporary captured-apparatus response to the resistance configurations dossiered in §§ II-III.

---


### IV. Documented Institutional Responses to the Resistance

The responses to the resistance describe the current captured-apparatus reaction to the resistance dossiered in §§ II-III. The architecture has ten nodes across the tradition families.

**Catholic responses.** The pattern: papal addresses + episcopal pastoral letters + institutional reforms + contestation of resistance configurations. The pattern: reform-track responses under Pope Francis 2013-2025 including the canonisation of Óscar Romero (14 October 2018), the Motu Proprio *Vos Estis Lux Mundi* (9 May 2019, revised 25 March 2023) producing the principal current accountability framework, followed by the Vatican statement on the Doctrine of Discovery (30 March 2023), and broader institutional positioning. The contestation-track responses: discipline of liberation theology under John Paul II (1978-2005) and Benedict XVI (2005-2013) including the silencing of Leonardo Boff 1985 by Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger (later Benedict XVI) at the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith, the withdrawal of Hans Küng's teaching authority on 18 December 1979, broader discipline of theological dissent; subsequent partial rehabilitation under Pope Francis 2013-2025. The contemporary configuration under Pope Leo XIV from May 2025: continuity of Francis positioning on abuse accountability and Doctrine of Discovery; broader contemporary positioning across multiple sites of contestation.

**Russian Orthodox responses.** The pattern: discipline of dissent across ROC apparatus. The cases: the defrocking of Andrei Kuraev in 2024 by Patriarchal Court of the Russian Orthodox Church on charges related to his blog posts including criticism of ROC institutional positioning on the Ukraine war; Aleksey Uminsky dismissal January 2024 from his Moscow parish for refusing to read the prayer for "Holy Rus" victory in Ukraine; broader discipline of clerical dissent across ROC apparatus dossiered Chapter 18. The pattern: discipline against clerical dissent in the ROC at scale since the post-February-2022 period.

**Sunni Islamic responses.** The pattern: contested reception of Islamic reform configurations across multiple jurisdictions producing pattern of state-clerical responses to reform. The cases: state-clerical configurations in Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Iran, Pakistan, Indonesia, Turkey, and other configurations producing contestation of Islamic reform efforts; particular contestation of Islamic feminist scholarship and Islamic legal-reform scholarship across multiple jurisdictions; broader contemporary Sunni Islamic architecture producing pattern of contested reception of reform.

**Shia Islamic responses.** The pattern: Iranian state responses to Green Movement 2009 + Mahsa Amini protests 2022-2023 producing principal contemporary Iranian state-religious response to religious-political dissent. The cases: Iranian state machinery including IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps), Basij paramilitary, Iranian Judiciary producing principal contemporary state-religious discipline of dissent including arrests, executions, broader institutional responses; sustained discipline of Iranian reform efforts throughout the post-1989 period, which led to the exile of Soroush and Kadivar, and the expansion of the broader Iranian state apparatus.

**Jewish responses.** The pattern: contested reception of progressive Jewish bodies across mainstream American Jewish apparatus. The cases: contestation of J Street, T'ruah, If Not Now, JVP, New Israel Fund across mainstream American Jewish architecture including AIPAC, ADL (Anti-Defamation League), Jewish Federations of North America; particular current contestation following 7 October 2023 Hamas attack and subsequent Israeli military operations in Gaza dossiered Chapter 30 § I; broader Jewish bodies across denominational lines including Orthodox, Conservative, Reform, Reconstructionist denominations, which produce varied institutional positioning.

**Hindu responses.** The pattern: BJP-RSS-VHP institutional responses to civil-society critique producing a leading current state-religious response to dissent. The cases: Foreign Contribution Regulation Act (FCRA) cancellations including Amnesty India 2020 producing closure of Amnesty India operations; broader civil-society registration cancellations across India under the BJP government; discipline of academic dissent including JNU and other Indian universities; UAPA (Unlawful Activities Prevention Act) used against activists including journalists Mohammed Zubair (June 2022 arrest) and other figures; broader Indian state-religious machinery producing state discipline of civil-society dissent.

**Buddhist responses.** The pattern: state-sangha discipline across Thailand, Myanmar, Sri Lanka producing pattern of state-religious discipline of Engaged Buddhism. The cases: Thai monastic disciplinary actions across multiple cases including state pressure on dissident monks; Myanmar military-government policy on monastic dissent including post-2021 discipline of monks supporting the National Unity Government; broader state-sangha settings dossiered Chapter 23.

**Sikh responses.** The pattern: SGPC producing the principal current Sikh response to civil-society critique. The cases: SGPC positioning against critique of institutional capture; broader Punjab state-religious settings dossiered Chapter 24; contestation across SGPC, SAD, Akal Takht Jathedars producing pattern of Sikh body in contested settings.

**Protestant evangelical responses.** The pattern: SBC (Southern Baptist Convention) responses to abuse-accountability + broader US evangelical structures producing a pattern of contested reception. The cases: SBC 2022 Guidepost Solutions Report (22 May 2022) documenting decades of sexual-abuse cover-up at SBC Executive Committee level producing the principal current US evangelical accountability documentation; subsequent challenge through Russell Moore exit from ERLC 2021 and broader restructuring; contestation of post-evangelical figures, such as Beth Moore's exit from the SBC in March 2021; broader US evangelical structures producing a pattern of contested reception.

**Indigenous responses.** The pattern: ongoing legal and political challenge across multiple Indigenous-rights movements producing pattern of response to Indigenous resistance. The cases: Pope Francis Vatican statement on Doctrine of Discovery (30 March 2023) producing principal contemporary Catholic repudiation of 15th-century papal bulls (Dum Diversas 1452, Romanus Pontifex 1455, Inter Caetera 1493) that legitimated European colonial expropriation of Indigenous lands; DOJ OLC memorandum late 2025 / early 2026 on First Amendment religious-use framework dossiered in Chapter 33 § IV; broader current responses across multiple jurisdictions producing contested reception of Indigenous Land Back configurations.

The summary of § IV: the responses to the resistance describe the current captured-apparatus reaction across ten nodes within the tradition families. The configuration operates through Catholic institutional responses (reform-track under Francis 2013-2025 + contestation-track under John Paul II + Benedict XVI), Russian Orthodox discipline of dissent (Kuraev 2024 + Uminsky January 2024), Sunni Islamic state-clerical contestation, Shia Islamic state-religious discipline (Green Movement 2009 + Mahsa Amini protests 2022 + Iranian Judiciary), Jewish contestation of progressive configurations across mainstream American Jewish architecture. Hindu institutional responses (FCRA cancellations + Amnesty India 2020 + JNU + UAPA + Mohammed Zubair June 2022), Buddhist state-sangha disciplinary responses, Sikh institutional positioning, Protestant evangelical institutional contestation (SBC Guidepost Solutions Report 22 May 2022 + Russell Moore + Beth Moore March 2021), Indigenous institutional contestation (Doctrine of Discovery 30 March 2023 + DOJ OLC late 2025/early 2026). The current captured-apparatus reaction operates across the spectrum: from reform-track accountability under Pope Francis to discipline of dissent across multiple national settings.

Chapter 35 closes here. The record dossiered the exit infrastructure across four sections. The exits framing of what "solutions" means in the architecture of this volume (§ I), the named resisters across the traditions producing the contemporary figure-level architecture of resistance to capture (§ II), the documented resistance movements operating at scale beyond individual figures across nine principal nodes (§ III), the responses to the resistance across ten nodes within the tradition families (§ IV). The record covers what is documented as already happening. The analysis does not advocate. The evidence does not direct. The evidence establishes the current documented resistance and the current documented response to that resistance. Chapter 36 dossiers what happens if the church continues — the projected operational scale of captured apparatus across the coming decades under continuation of contemporary patterns. Chapter 37 dossiers what happens if the church is refused — the configurations through which captured apparatus loses operational capacity through believer-level exit, reform-track institutional accountability, broader cultural shifts.

---


## Chapter 36. If the Church Continues

Chapter 36 is the second chapter of Part VII. Chapter 35 dossiered the exit infrastructure already documented as operating — the named figures who have publicly resisted capture, the documented resistance movements, the structural responses to that resistance. Chapter 36 dossiers what the compromised apparatus produces if no exit happens at scale, if no reform-track accountability succeeds at scale, if the cultural shifts producing pressure on the apparatus do not reach the threshold at which the apparatus loses operational capacity. What follows covers continuation. The record is not prediction. The record is the extrapolation of the patterns documented through Chapters 14-34 forward across the coming decades.

The argument is organised in four sections. Section I dossiers what "continues" means in the methodological frame of this chapter — extrapolation rather than prediction, documented trajectory of named patterns, what the chapter does and what it does not do. Section II dossiers the projected financial-political trajectory — the captured-apparatus operational scale across the coming decades under continuation of current patterns. Section III dossiers the projected wars and the projected consent architecture — what the four-century mechanism continues producing through the religious mask if no exit happens at scale. Section IV dossiers the accumulating cost — what continues to be taken from the believer across every tradition if the church continues.

### I. What "Continues" Means: The Methodological Frame

The methodological frame of this chapter has four principal nodes: (1) extrapolation rather than prediction; (2) documented trajectory of named patterns; (3) what the chapter does and does not do; (4) the relationship between Chapter 36 and the architectural counter-claim established across Chapters 31-35.

**Extrapolation rather than prediction.** The architecture: the chapter does not predict. Prediction requires modelling. Modelling requires assumptions about the rate at which the patterns change, the rate at which exit-infrastructure capacity grows, the rate at which reform-track accountability succeeds, the rate at which broader cultural shifts produce pressure on the apparatus. Those rates are not knowable in advance. What is documented is the contemporary state of the apparatus — the financial flows, the named senior figures, the per-tradition operational scales, the cross-tradition coordination architecture, the wars-worn-as-faith pattern, the consent framings. The analysis extrapolates: if the documented trends continue at the documented scale, what does the apparatus produce across the coming decades? The extrapolation does not claim the patterns will continue. The extrapolation describes what happens if they do. The reader who concludes the patterns will not continue — because of exit, reform, broader cultural shift, or any other reason — reads this chapter as the trajectory Chapter 37's church-refusal prevents.

**Documented trajectory of named patterns.** The architecture: the chapter extrapolates only what has already been dossiered. The documented trends span Chapters 14-34 of this volume and across Volumes One and Two. The principal documented trends: ~$1.2T+ annual global religious revenue routed through the co-opted apparatus; ~$30T+ aggregate religious real-estate holdings; ~1.5B+ exposed to religious electoral guidance per 2020s electoral cycles; the eleven tradition-family operational scales dossiered in Chapter 33 § III; the cross-tradition coordination architecture dossiered in Chapter 28; the technocratic-religious interface dossiered in Chapter 29; the wars-worn-as-faith pattern dossiered in Chapter 30; the four consent framings dossiered in Chapter 32 § IV; the per-tradition captured configurations dossiered across Chapters 14-27. The evidence does not introduce trends that have not been dossiered. The analysis does not speculate about trends that have not emerged. The analysis extrapolates the dossiered trends forward.

**What the chapter does and does not do.** The architecture: the chapter does four things. First thing: the chapter dossiers the projected financial-political trajectory of the apparatus across the coming decades under the projection. Second thing: the chapter dossiers the projected wars and consent architecture under the projection. Third thing: the chapter dossiers the accumulating cost across the four-century-mechanism extraction documented in Volume One, the political-mask extraction in Volume Two, the religious-mask extraction across Chapters 14-34. Fourth thing: the chapter names the position of Chapter 36 in the architectural sequence — the demonstration of what happens if the church continues, which prepares the ground for the demonstration in Chapter 37 of what happens if the church is refused. The record notes five things it does not do. First non-thing: the chapter does not predict. Second non-thing: the chapter does not advocate any specific exit configuration. Third non-thing: the chapter does not direct the believer. Fourth non-thing: the chapter does not introduce new operational-scale documentation beyond what is dossiered in prior chapters. Fifth non-thing: the chapter does not litigate the dossiers of prior chapters; the dossiers stand.

**The relationship between Chapter 36 and the architectural counter-claim.** The architecture: the chapter operates from the architectural counter-claim established in Chapter 31 and named through Chapters 32-34. The believers across the traditions are not the demons. The captured apparatus is the demon. The faith is not the demon. If the church continues — if the apparatus continues operating at the current scales — the trajectory is what this chapter dossiers. The trajectory is not the trajectory of the faith. The trajectory is the trajectory of what has been built on top of the faith. The believer across every tradition who reads this chapter reads the projected trajectory of what is being done in the believer's name. The record is not a description of what the believer is doing. The evidence is a description of what the apparatus is doing while the believer prays, fasts, gives charity, raises children, mourns, celebrates, dies — while the believer lives the faith.

The summary of § I: the methodological frame of this chapter operates through extrapolation of documented trends rather than prediction. The analysis extrapolates only patterns dossiered in Chapters 14-34. The analysis does four things and does not do five things. The evidence operates from the architectural counter-claim established across Chapters 31-35 — the believers across the traditions are not the demons; the seized apparatus is. The projected financial-political trajectory dossiered in § II constitutes the first extrapolation across the coming decades.

---


### II. Projected Financial-Political Trajectory

The trajectory extrapolates the contemporary patterns dossiered in Chapter 33 across the coming decades. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) projected aggregate religious revenue; (2) projected real-estate accumulation; (3) the demographic-political reach; (4) per-tradition scales; (5) projected cross-tradition coordination; (6) projected technocratic-religious interface.

**Projected aggregate revenue.** The contemporary scale dossiered in Chapter 33 § I: ~$1.2T+ annual revenue routed through captured apparatus across every tradition. The extrapolation: under continuation of institutional financial accumulation in the post-2000 period, with ~3-5% annual nominal growth (consistent with broader global economic growth + documented revenue patterns), the aggregate annual scale projects to ~$1.6-2.0T by 2035 and ~$2.2-3.5T by 2050. The cumulative aggregate religious-institutional revenue across the 2026-2050 period under continuation projects to ~$30-50T routed through the co-opted apparatus. Under the projection, the apparatus accumulates an additional aggregate revenue flow comparable in scale to the current global GDP across the projection period.

**Projected real-estate growth.** The contemporary scale dossiered in Chapter 33 § I: ~$30T+ aggregate religious real-estate holdings worldwide. The extrapolation: under continuation of institutional real-estate growth since the post-1945 period — through bequests, purchases, retained tax-exempt holdings, broader vehicles — the aggregate rises to ~$40-50T by 2035 and ~$50-80T by 2050 in nominal terms. The pattern: under continuation, real-estate holdings in the religious sector remain the principal contemporary tax-exempt asset class held by non-state institutional actors at scale globally, accumulating in parallel with broader asset-price inflation across the projection period.

**Projected demographic reach.** The contemporary scale dossiered in Chapter 33 § I: ~1.5+ billion individuals exposed to electoral guidance per 2020s electoral cycles. The Pew Research Center 2017 "Changing Global Religious Landscape" projections through 2060: global Christian population projects from ~2.3B in 2015 to ~3.1B by 2060; global Muslim population projects from ~1.8B in 2015 to ~3.0B by 2060; global Hindu population projects from ~1.1B in 2015 to ~1.4B by 2060; global Buddhist population projects flat at ~0.5B; unaffiliated population projects from ~1.2B in 2015 to ~1.2B by 2060 (declining as share due to differential birth rates). The extrapolation of electoral-guidance exposure under continuation: ~2.0+ billion exposed by 2040 across major electoral cycles; ~2.5+ billion exposed by 2060 across major electoral cycles. The pattern: under continuation, electoral-guidance reach grows in absolute terms across the projection period despite secularising trends in particular national settings.

**Projected per-tradition scale.** The current per-tradition scales in Chapter 33 § III project forward under continuation. Catholic apparatus: current ~$170B+ annual through Vatican Bank + APSA + Secretariat for the Economy + worldwide diocesan operations + religious orders, rises to ~$220-280B annual by 2040 + ~$300-400B annual by 2060 under continuation; current Vatican Bank ~$5B+ assets project to ~$7-10B by 2040; cumulative Catholic apparatus revenue 2026-2060 projects to ~$8-12T. US evangelical: current ~$120B+ annual through megachurch + broadcasting + publishing + education + political-religious vehicles, rises to ~$160-200B annual by 2040 + ~$220-300B annual by 2060 under the projection. Russian Orthodox: current ~$400M+ annual Moscow Patriarchate + 50,000+ properties, rises to ~$600M-1B annual by 2040 under the projection of the post-2012 ROC-state alignment. Saudi religious-export: current ~$10B+ annual through 2020s, rises to ~$15-20B annual by 2040 under the projection of documented Saudi state petroleum revenue allocation to religious export. Hindu nationalist: current ~$15B+ annual Sangh Parivar, rises to ~$25-40B annual by 2040 + ~$50-100B annual by 2060 under the projection of documented Sangh Parivar growth from 1990s-2020s. Prosperity gospel: current ~$40B+ annual global, rises to ~$70-100B annual by 2040 + ~$120-200B annual by 2060 under the projection of documented prosperity-gospel expansion across Africa + Latin America + Asia.

**Projected cross-tradition work.** The contemporary architecture dossiered in Chapter 28: documented coordination across Christian-nationalist, Hindu-nationalist, Jewish-nationalist, Islamic-nationalist alignments including the documented coordination against LGBT populations, reproductive rights, secular governance. The extrapolation under the projection: the cross-tradition coordination architecture continues operating across the coming decades producing sustained transnational religious-political coordination across multiple sites of contestation. Under projection, the cross-tradition coordination architecture functions as the principal coordination layer between captured apparatuses across multiple traditions producing aggregate political effects beyond the per-tradition scales.

**Projected technocratic-religious work.** The contemporary architecture dossiered in Chapter 29: WEF religion-track participants, "AI and religion" forums, interfaith financial-political bodies operating as coordination layers, the documented contemporary AI-substrate use by named the named bodies. The extrapolation under the projection: the technocratic-religious interface continues developing across the coming decades producing sustained integration of the apparatuses with the broader technocratic governance architecture documented in Volume Two. Under projection, the technocratic-religious interface produces the architecture through which captured apparatuses become integrated into AI-mediated governance configurations across multiple jurisdictions including AI-substrate captured-apparatus vehicles + AI-mediated religious-content production + AI-mediated administrative work for the religious sector + broader configurations producing the loop back to the AI substrate of Volume One.

The summary of § II: the projected trajectory under continuation produces aggregate religious revenue of ~$1.6-2.0T annual by 2035 + ~$2.2-3.5T annual by 2050. Aggregate real-estate ~$40-50T by 2035 + ~$50-80T by 2050; demographic-political reach ~2.0+ billion by 2040 + ~2.5+ billion by 2060; scales projected for Catholic + US evangelical + Russian Orthodox + Saudi + Hindu nationalist + prosperity gospel; sustained cross-tradition coordination architecture; deepening technocratic-religious interface. The projected wars and consent architecture dossiered in § III constitutes the second principal extrapolation across the coming decades.

---


### III. Projected Wars and Projected Consent

The projected wars and projected consent extrapolate the documented contemporary patterns dossiered in Chapters 28-32 across the coming decades. The architecture has six principal nodes: (1) projected armed conflict; (2) projected religiously-framed conflict; (3) projected framing patterns; (4) the suppression; (5) projected cross-tradition coordination on consent; (6) effects on the believer population.

**Armed conflict.** The current armed-conflict bodies dossiered in Chapter 30: Gaza war from 7 October 2023 producing 1,200+ Israeli + 47,000+ Palestinian deaths through early 2026 + comprehensive infrastructure destruction; Russian-Ukrainian war from 24 February 2022 producing 700,000+ Russian + 400,000+ Ukrainian casualties + 10+ million displaced; Sudanese civil war from 15 April 2023 producing 150,000+ deaths + 13+ million displaced + comprehensive famine; broader theatre-level conflicts including Nigeria, Myanmar, DRC, Sahel, Ethiopia, broader. The extrapolation under the projection of documented trends: religiously-framed armed conflict continues across the coming decades. Per SIPRI Yearbook 2025 data documenting ~60 active armed conflicts globally in 2024 + continuation of the post-2014 trend of increasing global armed-conflict count, the trajectory of religiously-framed armed conflict under continuation produces sustained or expanding armed conflict across the coming decades. Religiously-framed armed conflict remains a leading current configuration of organised violence.

**Religiously-framed conflict.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 30: armed-conflict bodies with religious framing across Gaza (defense of holy land + holy sites + faith communities), Russian-Ukrainian (defense of Orthodox civilisation per Kirill 6 March 2022 + WRPC 27-28 March 2024 declaration), Sudanese (sectarian framings overlaid on resource conflict), Nigeria (Christian-Muslim sectarian framings), Myanmar (Buddhist-nationalist framings of operations against the Rohingya), broader. The extrapolation under the projection: religious framing of armed conflict continues producing continued operation of the wars-worn-as-faith pattern. The wars-worn-as-faith pattern produces continued institutional positioning of religious bodies as principal legitimators of armed conflict across multiple theatres.

**Consent framings.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 32 § IV: religious teaching producing populations consenting to extraction through four documented framings — suffering-as-spiritually-beneficial; obedience-as-spiritually-required; inequality-as-divinely-ordained; financial-extraction-as-spiritual. The extrapolation under the projection: the four-framing pattern continue operating across the coming decades, producing sustained mass populations responding to extraction not as imposed condition but as natural condition of life. The pattern: the framing architecture produces continued operation of belief as the surface through which the four-century mechanism produces population-level consent to extraction across multiple traditions.

**Suppression.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 33 § IV + Chapter 35 § IV: apparatus discipline of resistance configurations including Catholic discipline of liberation theology under John Paul II + Benedict XVI; ROC defrocking of Kuraev 2024 + dismissal of Uminsky January 2024; Iranian state suppression of Green Movement 2009 + 2022 Mahsa Amini protests; Indian state suppression including FCRA cancellations + Amnesty India 2020 + UAPA architecture; SBC institutional contestation of post-evangelical configurations; broader patterns. The extrapolation under the projection: institutional suppression of dissent within captured apparatuses continues producing sustained discipline of resistance configurations. The co-opted apparatus continues operating to suppress internal resistance through institutional discipline producing ongoing contestation between apparatus and exit infrastructure dossiered in Chapter 35.

**Cross-tradition coordination.** The contemporary architecture dossiered in Chapter 28: cross-tradition coordination against LGBT populations, against reproductive rights, against secular governance configurations producing transnational religious-political coordination. The extrapolation under the projection: cross-tradition coordination continues producing sustained transnational alignment between captured apparatuses on shared anti-secular, anti-LGBT, anti-reproductive-rights stances + broader stances. Cross-tradition coordination serves as the principal architecture through which the apparatuses align across traditions on shared political configurations.

**Effects on believer populations.** The contemporary scale: ~1.5+ billion individuals exposed to religious electoral guidance per 2020s electoral cycles. The extrapolation under the projection: across the coming decades, believer populations exposed to consent-framing architectures across the compromised apparatus continue at the documented scales + grow in absolute terms per Pew Research Center demographic projections (Christian ~3.1B by 2060, Muslim ~3.0B by 2060, Hindu ~1.4B by 2060, broader). The cumulative effect on those who believe under continuation is that ~3-4+ billion individuals will be exposed to consent-framing architectures across the co-opted apparatus during the projection period. The captured apparatus operates across the coming decades as the principal mass-mobilisation infrastructure for political configurations operating in service of the four-century mechanism documented in Volume One.

The summary of § III: the projected wars and projected consent under the projection produces sustained or expanding armed conflict with religious-institutional framing across the coming decades. Continued operation of the four-framing pattern producing population-level consent to extraction; continued institutional suppression of dissent within captured apparatuses; continued cross-tradition coordination on consent on shared anti-secular stances; cumulative effect on those who believe of ~3-4+ billion exposed to consent-framing architectures across the projection period. The accumulating cost dossiered in § IV constitutes the third extrapolation across the coming decades — what is taken from the believer across every tradition if the church continues.

---


### IV. The Accumulating Cost: What Continues to Be Taken from the Believer

The accumulating cost extrapolates the documented contemporary patterns dossiered in Chapter 34 § IV (the taking) across the coming decades. The architecture has eight principal nodes: (1) the accumulating financial cost; (2) the accumulating labour cost; (3) the accumulating consent cost; (4) the accumulating war cost; (5) the accumulating ecological cost; (6) the accumulating wealth-concentration cost; (7) the accumulating belief cost; (8) the closing of Chapter 36 — the architectural position of the projected trajectory.

**Financial cost.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 34 § IV: ~$1.2T+ annual global revenue routed through tithes, offerings, charity, "seed sowing," charitable giving as spiritual practice. The extrapolation under continuation: the cumulative financial extraction across the 2026-2050 period projects to ~$30-50T routed through the co-opted apparatus. The pattern: under continuation, ~$30-50T of the flows across the projection period are routed through vehicles operating in financial coordination with the four-century mechanism documented in Volume One. What the believer loses: the opportunity cost of ~$30-50T over the projection period — flows that could have funded education, healthcare, housing, broader believer-community needs but were instead routed through institutional vehicles whose financial integration with the four-century mechanism is documented across Chapters 14-27.

**Labour cost.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 34 § IV: religious-institutional labour extracted at low or no wages from monks, nuns, priests, religious-charitable workers, religious-institutional teachers, volunteer labour from those populations sustaining apparatus operations without wage cost. The extrapolation under continuation: cumulative labour extraction from believers across the 2026-2050 period continues at the documented contemporary scales sustaining the architecture funded by unpaid or low-paid believer labour. The believer loses the opportunity cost of believer-labour routed through the vehicles operating in coordination with the four-century mechanism.

**Consent cost.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 32 § IV: the teaching of populations into consent to extraction through the four documented framings. The extrapolation under continuation: ~3-4+ billion individuals across the projection period exposed to those architectures producing sustained population-level acceptance of extraction conditions as natural conditions of life. The believer loses the opportunity cost of believer political agency — agency channelled through the apparatus into legitimation of configurations operating in service of the four-century mechanism rather than against extraction.

**War cost.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 30: armed-conflict configurations operating with religious-institutional framing producing Gaza casualties + Russian-Ukrainian casualties + Sudanese casualties + broader theatre casualties through 2026. The extrapolation under continuation: cumulative armed-conflict casualties across the 2026-2050 period under religious-institutional framing project to multiple-million-scale deaths + tens-of-millions-scale displacement + sustained operation of military-industrial financial flows producing the leading current extraction vehicle alongside the flows. The believer loses the cost in believer lives — those across every tradition who have been killed, displaced, or otherwise harmed in armed-conflict configurations framed by the the captured religious-institutional apparatus as defense of holy land, holy sites, faith communities, religious civilisation.

**Ecological cost.** The contemporary pattern dossiered across multiple chapters: positioning across climate response producing varied institutional positioning including Francis *Laudato Si'* May 2015 (positioned for climate response), broader institutional positioning across multiple traditions (varied positioning), captured religious positioning across climate-denial configurations including US evangelical alignment with climate-denial policy positioning under current US political configurations. The IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report 2023 documents the cumulative trajectory of greenhouse-gas-driven climate change producing the leading current ecological cost framework. The extrapolation under continuation: religious positioning on climate continues across the coming decades producing varied contribution to climate response across captured apparatuses; alignment of the apparatus with extractive-industry political configurations produces sustained institutional positioning against climate response in particular national settings (US under contemporary Trump second-term Cabinet configuration; broader). The believer loses the cost of ecological-system degradation, which occurs in parallel with the alignment of the apparatus with extractive-industry political configurations.

**Wealth-concentration cost.** The contemporary pattern dossiered in Volume One + Volume Two: global wealth distribution producing ~50% of global wealth held by top 1% per Oxfam January 2024 *Inequality Inc.* report + UBS *Global Wealth Report 2024* + Credit Suisse *Global Wealth Report* (annual editions). The co-opted apparatus operates as a leading legitimator of wealth-concentration outcomes through the four consent framings (inequality-as-divinely-ordained); through the positioning of the figures providing political legitimation for wealth-concentration political configurations; through the governing positioning of real-estate and capital holdings within the concentrated-wealth architecture. The extrapolation under continuation: wealth concentration continues to grow across the coming decades + positioning continues to legitimate for concentrated-wealth political configurations. What the believer loses: the cost of wealth-concentration to the outcomes for those populations legitimated by the positioning across multiple traditions.

**Belief cost.** The volume GUIDANCE: *"It takes your belief, commodifies it, weaponises it."* The contemporary pattern dossiered in Chapter 34 § IV: the commodification of belief in captured-apparatus vehicles producing belief as institutional commodity operating to produce financial flows + labour + political legitimation + consent. The extrapolation under continuation: belief commodification continues to compound across the coming decades producing sustained operation of belief as the surface through which the four-century mechanism produces extraction. The believer loses the cost of belief being commodified and weaponised — what GUIDANCE names directly. The believer's belief is taken. It is commodified. It is weaponised. The four-century mechanism continues operating through the religious mask, the political mask, and the financial mask — three of the five masks dossiered across this five-volume series — and the cost compounds across the projection period.

**Closing of Chapter 36: the architectural position of the projected trajectory.** Chapter 36 has dossiered what happens if the church continues. The financial-political trajectory continues. The wars-worn-as-faith pattern continues. The consent framings continue. The cross-tradition coordination continues. The technocratic-religious interface continues. The accumulating cost compounds. The four-century mechanism continues extracting through the religious mask alongside the political mask documented in Volume Two and the financial mask documented in Volume One. The believer across every tradition continues to be the surface through which the apparatus extracts. The record has not predicted that this is what will happen. The record has dossiered what happens if continuation is what happens. The evidence does not advocate continuation. The record does not advocate the alternative. The evidence covers continuation as one of the two architectural possibilities. Chapter 37 dossiers the other possibility — what happens if the church is refused. The believer reads both chapters and decides what to do. The volume's authorial position remains documentation, not direction. Direction belongs to the faithful.

The summary of § IV: the accumulating cost under continuation of documented patterns across the coming decades produces cumulative financial extraction from believers of ~$30-50T routed through captured apparatus. Sustained labour extraction from believers; cumulative consent-architecture exposure of ~3-4+ billion individuals; cumulative armed-conflict cost in multiple-million-scale deaths + tens-of-millions-scale displacement; cumulative ecological cost under alignment of the apparatus with extractive-industry political configurations; cumulative wealth-concentration cost under religious-institutional legitimation; cumulative belief commodification per GUIDANCE direct quote. Chapter 36 closes by naming the architectural position of the projected trajectory — one of the two architectural possibilities, dossiered alongside the alternative dossiered in Chapter 37.

Chapter 36 closes here. The projected trajectory under continuation of documented patterns has been dossiered across four sections — the methodological frame (§ I), the projected financial-political trajectory (§ II), the projected wars and consent architecture (§ III), the accumulating cost (§ IV). Chapter 37 dossiers what happens if the church is refused — the configurations through which the compromised apparatus loses operational capacity through believer-level exit, reform-track institutional accountability, broader cultural shifts. Chapter 38 closes the volume by returning to the believer and the demon and naming the closing distinction.

---


## Chapter 37. If the Church Is Refused

Chapter 37 is the third chapter of Part VII. Chapter 35 dossiered the exit infrastructure already operating. Chapter 36 dossiered the projected trajectory of the apparatus under continuation of documented patterns. Chapter 37 dossiers the alternative — the configurations through which the captured apparatus loses operational capacity if exit happens at scale, if reform-track accountability succeeds at scale, if broader cultural shifts produce pressure on the apparatus sufficient to reach the threshold at which capacity is lost. The documentation covers refusal.

The argument is organised in four sections. Section I dossiers what "refused" means in the methodological frame of this chapter — same extrapolative methodology as Chapter 36, inverse trajectory; what it is and what it is not; the relationship between Chapter 37 and the architectural counter-claim. Section II dossiers it at the believer level — the documented configurations of believer-level exit and the documented sociology-of-religion projections of where those configurations lead under continuation. Section III dossiers it at the institutional and cultural level — reform-track accountability, cultural shift, and legal and political contestation operating against the apparatus. Section IV dossiers what it produces — the inverse of the cost in Chapter 36 § IV, the relationship between religious-mask refusal and the broader five-mask architecture, and the closing setup for the last chapter.

### I. What "Refused" Means: The Methodological Frame

The methodological frame of this chapter has four principal nodes: (1) same extrapolative methodology as Chapter 36, inverse trajectory; (2) what it is and what it is not; (3) the relationship between refusal of the apparatus and the underlying tradition; (4) the relationship between Chapter 37 and the architectural counter-claim established across Chapters 31-36.

**Same extrapolative methodology as Chapter 36, inverse trajectory.** The architecture: the chapter uses the same methodology as Chapter 36. The record does not predict. The analysis extrapolates. The extrapolation operates from documented configurations — the exit infrastructure dossiered in Chapter 35, the broader sociology-of-religion work of believer-level disaffiliation, the accountability outcomes from reform-track work, the cultural-shift configurations producing pressure on the apparatus. The extrapolation does not claim this will happen. It describes what happens if it does. Chapter 36 dossiered continuation. This chapter dossiers refusal. The two chapters operate as the architectural complement to each other.

**What it is and what it is not.** The architecture: the chapter establishes four characteristics of it as documented configuration. First characteristic: it operates at multiple scales — individual believer-level refusal, communal departure through breakaway movements + alternative congregations + intentional communities, broader cultural withdrawal through population-level secularisation and disaffiliation, institutional reform through reform-track accountability work + governance changes + leadership transitions, political-legal contest through litigation + tax-status contestation + property-rights contestation. Second characteristic: this is not refusal of the faith. It is refusal of the co-opted apparatus. The pastoral register of the faith — the prayer, the teaching, the pastoral care, the charity, the celebration, the mourning — does not require the seized structure. The faith continues. This is what is being refused. Third characteristic: this is not destruction. It is the withdrawal of belief, financial flows, labour, political legitimation, consent from captured apparatus. The apparatus does not have to be destroyed for refusal to operate. The captured apparatus loses operational capacity when the inputs that sustain it are withdrawn. Fourth characteristic: this is documented as already happening. The configurations dossiered in §§ II-III are not hypothetical configurations. The configurations are evidenced in sociology-of-religion data, in accountability outcomes, in cultural-shift markers, in legal precedents. It is dossiered as actual current configuration operating at documented scales.

**Refusal and the underlying tradition.** The architecture: the chapter operates from the architectural counter-claim established in Chapter 31 and named through Chapters 32-36. The believers across every tradition are not the demon. It is. The faith is not the demon. The pastoral register the apparatus speaks is the language of the faith — genuine, not corrupted, spoken by a structure operating in financial-political coordination with the four-century mechanism documented in Volume One. Those who refuse the apparatus do not abandon the underlying tradition. Those who refuse the compromised apparatus separate the tradition from what has been built on top of it. The tradition continues in personal practice, family practice, communal practice — across breakaway congregations, alternative communities, individual practice, the broader configurations through which the underlying tradition operates outside the co-opted institutional vehicles. Refusal is, in this framing, the act through which the believer reclaims that tradition.

**Chapter 37 and the architectural counter-claim.** The architecture: Chapter 37 is the practical demonstration of the architectural counter-claim. Chapter 31 named the believer is not the demon. Chapter 32 named the four-century mechanism for the third time in the series through the religious mask. Chapter 33 named the demons at operational scale. Chapter 34 named the mask itself. Chapter 35 dossiered the exit infrastructure already operating. Chapter 36 dossiered continuation. This chapter dossiers refusal — what happens when the architectural counter-claim is enacted at scale. The analysis completes the architectural counter-claim. The believer is not the demon. The apparatus is. The apparatus operates through the believer's belief, labour, financial flows, political legitimation, consent. It loses capacity when those inputs are withdrawn. The record covers what that refusal produces.

The summary of § I: the methodological frame of Chapter 37 operates through the same extrapolative methodology as Chapter 36 producing the inverse trajectory. Refusal is documented at multiple scales as current configuration. It is not refusal of the faith but of the apparatus. It is not destruction but the withdrawal of inputs that sustain the apparatus. Chapter 37 completes the counter-claim from Chapters 31-36. The believer-level refusal dossiered in § II constitutes the first principal node.

---


### II. Refusal at the Believer Level

The modes of believer-level refusal operate across multiple scales: individual departure, communal breakaway, generational secularisation, intra-tradition reform movements. The architecture has six nodes: (1) documented US departure trajectory; (2) European secularisation; (3) intra-tradition reform; (4) "spiritual but not religious" patterns; (5) projected refusal trajectory; (6) believer-level refusal across non-Western settings.

**Documented US departure trajectory.** The Pew Research Center 17 October 2019 report "In US, Decline of Christianity Continues at Rapid Pace" documented US Christian self-identification declining from 77% in 2009 to 65% in 2019; US religiously unaffiliated growing from 17% in 2009 to 26% in 2019. The Pew 24 January 2024 report "Religious 'Nones' in America" recorded ~28% US religiously unaffiliated, the largest single category in US religious-identification. The Pew 13 September 2022 report "Modeling the Future of Religion in America" projected four scenarios projecting US Christian self-identification through 2070: under continuation of current departure rates, US Christian self-identification projects to between 35% (steady-decline scenario) and 46% (slowed-decline scenario) by 2070 — declining from 64% in 2020 to a minority position within the projection period. PRRI's annual American Values Atlas records further contemporary breakdowns including Catholic departure patterns. The pattern: in the principal contemporary US religious-affiliation market, believer-level departure operates as mass-scale configuration throughout the post-2000 period producing measurable structural decline in apparatus's believer base.

**European secularisation.** The UK Office for National Statistics 2021 Census recorded first-time minority Christian identification in England and Wales (46.2% Christian, down from 59.3% in 2011, 71.7% in 2001); "no religion" at 37.2%, up from 25.2% in 2011, 14.8% in 2001.
