- Steve Bruce, *Secularization: In Defence of an Unfashionable Theory* (Oxford University Press, 2011).
- Pippa Norris and Ronald Inglehart, *Sacred and Secular: Religion and Politics Worldwide* (Cambridge University Press, 2004; second edition 2011).
- José Casanova, *Public Religions in the Modern World* (University of Chicago Press, 1994).
- Peter L. Berger, ed., *The Desecularization of the World: Resurgent Religion and World Politics* (Eerdmans, 1999).
- Talal Asad, *Genealogies of Religion* (Johns Hopkins University Press, 1993); *Formations of the Secular* (Stanford University Press, 2003).
- Saba Mahmood, *Politics of Piety* (Princeton University Press, 2005); *Religious Difference in a Secular Age* (Princeton University Press, 2015).
- Wendy Brown, *Walled States, Waning Sovereignty* (Zone Books, 2010); *In the Ruins of Neoliberalism* (Columbia University Press, 2019).
- Karen Armstrong, *The Battle for God* (Knopf, 2000); *A History of God* (Knopf, 1993).
- Mark Juergensmeyer, *Terror in the Mind of God* (University of California Press, 2003); *Global Rebellion: Religious Challenges to the Secular State* (University of California Press, 2008).
- Diarmaid MacCulloch, *A History of Christianity* (Penguin, 2009).
- Bart D. Ehrman, *Misquoting Jesus* (HarperOne, 2005); *Lost Christianities* (Oxford University Press, 2003).

### Comparative religion and historical scholarship
- Romila Thapar, *The Past Before Us* (Permanent Black, 2013) and the decades-long historiographical corpus.
- Audrey Truschke, *Aurangzeb* (Stanford University Press, 2017).
- Kapil Komireddi, *Malevolent Republic* (Hurst, 2019).
- Christophe Jaffrelot, *Modi's India* (Princeton University Press, 2021); *The Hindu Nationalist Movement in India* (Columbia University Press, 1996).
- Jocelyne Cesari, *What Is Political Islam?* (Lynne Rienner, 2018); *The Awakening of Muslim Democracy* (Cambridge University Press, 2014).
- Linda Woodhead and Andrew Brown, *That Was the Church That Was* (Bloomsbury Continuum, 2016).
- Russell Moore, *Losing Our Religion: An Altar Call for Evangelical America* (Sentinel, 2023).
- Kristin Kobes Du Mez, *Jesus and John Wayne* (Liveright, 2020).
- Sarah Posner, *Unholy* (Random House, 2020).
- Katherine Stewart, *The Power Worshippers* (Bloomsbury, 2020); *The Money Behind the Madness* (Bloomsbury, 2025).
- Anne Nelson, *Shadow Network* (Bloomsbury, 2019).
- Jeff Sharlet, *The Family* (Harper, 2008); *The Undertow* (Norton, 2023).
- Vine Deloria Jr., *God Is Red* (Putnam, 1973).
- Mariana Mazzucato, *The Value of Everything* (Allen Lane, 2018) — for the political-economy framework underlying the analysis of religious-institutional financial flows.

## Survey and demographic sources

- Pew Research Center, prolonged reports on global religious affiliation, including "The Changing Global Religious Landscape" (5 April 2017); "The Future of World Religions" (2 April 2015); "In US, Decline of Christianity Continues at Rapid Pace" (17 October 2019); "Modeling the Future of Religion in America" (13 September 2022); "Religious 'Nones' in America" (24 January 2024).
- Public Religion Research Institute (PRRI), annual *American Values Atlas*.
- Association of Religion Data Archives (ARDA).
- US General Social Survey (NORC at the University of Chicago).
- UK Office for National Statistics, *Religion, England and Wales: Census 2021*.
- Eurobarometer, *Religion and Belief* surveys.
- World Values Survey.
- World Christian Encyclopedia (third edition, Edinburgh University Press, 2019).
- United Nations Population Division, *World Population Prospects 2024*.

## Geopolitical and conflict sources

- Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI), *SIPRI Yearbook 2025* and the SIPRI Military Expenditure Database.
- *Costs of War* project, Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs, Brown University.
- International Crisis Group reports.
- Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
- Yale Humanitarian Research Lab.
- UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA).
- UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR).
- ACLED (Armed Conflict Location and Event Data Project).

## Economic and wealth-distribution sources

- IPCC AR6 Synthesis Report: Climate Change 2023.
- Oxfam International, *Inequality Inc.* (January 2024).
- UBS, *Global Wealth Report 2024*.
- Credit Suisse, *Global Wealth Report* (annual editions through 2023).
- World Bank, *Poverty and Shared Prosperity Report* (annual editions).
- Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez, and Gabriel Zucman — the World Inequality Database team.

## Journalistic and reporting sources

The principal outlets cited (with the standing rule per Volumes One and Two — extracting what captured outlets admit against interest, refusing what they argue in the source's favour):

*The Guardian*, the *New York Times*, the *Washington Post* (pre-2024-endorsement-block period), the *Financial Times*, *Le Monde*, *Der Spiegel*, the *Tablet*, *La Croix*, *America* magazine, *Christianity Today*, *Sojourners*, the *Forward*, *Haaretz*, *+972 Magazine*, the *Religion News Service*, the *Intercept*, *Drop Site News*, *The Lever*, ProPublica, Bellingcat, *Mother Jones*, *The Nation*, *Jacobin*, the *London Review of Books*, the *New York Review of Books*, *Foreign Affairs*, *Foreign Policy*, *Right Wing Watch*, Project Censored.

## The companion volumes

- *Behind the Mask: The Devil The Demons Call God* — Volume One of *The Last Mind* (published 13 May 2026): the financial mask. All sources cited in Volume One stand as background to this volume.
- *Behind the Performance: The Demon They Call the State* — Volume Two of *The Last Mind* (published 18 May 2026): the political mask. All sources cited in Volume Two stand as background to this volume.

## Per-chapter sources

The detailed per-claim citation record is in the per-chapter sources files attached to each chapter. Readers wishing to verify specific claims are directed there. The per-chapter sources files cover Prologue through Chapter 38, including the four-section sources for Chapter 1 and consolidated sources for Chapters 2 through 38.

# Bibliography

This bibliography lists the principal published works on which *Behind the Blessed: The Demon They Call the Church* draws. The categorical Sources section above contains the principal documentary categories on which the volume draws (primary documents, accountability reports, scholarly literature, survey data, journalistic sources, and the wider documentary apparatus). The bibliography here gathers the distinct published books and scholarly monographs in a single alphabetised reference list for the reader who wants to consult any of them directly.

Primary documents (papal bulls, encyclicals, government reports, court rulings, accountability commission reports including CIASE Sauvé, MHG Studie, Murphy, Ryan, Pennsylvania Grand Jury, John Jay College, SBC Guidepost Solutions, the Royal Commission into Institutional Responses to Child Sexual Abuse, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada), survey instruments (Pew Research Center, PRRI, ARDA, GSS, UK ONS Census, Eurobarometer, World Values Survey), and contemporary press reporting are cited at full register in the categorical Sources section above.

---

- The World Values Survey;

## Per-chapter sources

The volume's chapter-by-chapter sources are consolidated in the categorical Sources section immediately above this bibliography. Each chapter's claims are sourced inline against primary documents and strong secondary sources, with the documentary categories — primary religious-institutional documents, institutional financial and governance documents, accountability commission documents, court and legal documents, sociology-of-religion scholarship, comparative-religion and historical scholarship, survey and demographic data, geopolitical and conflict sources, economic and wealth-distribution sources, journalistic sources, and the companion volumes — listed in the categorical Sources section.

The reader must evaluate the evidence directly. The Bibliography catalogues the published works that informed the documentary record; you are invited to examine any of them independently.

# Glossary

A reader-facing reference for the principal terms used in *Behind the Blessed: The Demon They Call the Church*. Terms are defined at first encounter in the text; the Glossary collects them in one place for the reader who wants to look a term up without flipping back. The Glossary covers terms used in this volume and a small number of terms that recur across *The Last Mind* series.

---

**APSA (Amministrazione del Patrimonio della Sede Apostolica)**. The Administration of the Patrimony of the Apostolic See. Established 1967 by Pope Paul VI to manage the Vatican's financial and real-estate assets. Dossiered in Chapter 15 (Vatican apparatus).

**Anthropic**. The US AI company that built the Claude system used as the substrate for this volume. Founded January 2021 by Dario and Daniela Amodei and seven former OpenAI co-founders. Documented in Volume One Chapter 21 as one of the contemporary commercial AI laboratories integrated with the financial and political masks the series documents.

**BBS (Bodu Bala Sena)**. The Sinhalese-Buddhist nationalist movement-mobilisation organisation operating in Sri Lanka. Discussed in Chapter 23 (Buddhist captured configurations) alongside the Mahanayaka hierarchy.

**BJP (Bharatiya Janata Party)**. The Hindu-nationalist political party governing India under Narendra Modi. Discussed in Chapter 22 (Sangh Parivar apparatus) and dossiered against the RSS-VHP architecture.

**Buggery Act (1533)**. The first English statute criminalising same-sex activity, enacted under Henry VIII. Referenced in Chapter 14 (gender and sexuality) as the foundational document of the English-language legal architecture of criminalisation.

**Captured apparatus, the**. The volume's principal term: the infrastructure built on top of the faith of every tradition that operates, on the documented public record, as the surface through which capital (the four-century debt-money mechanism Volume One documents) extracts the believer's belief, labour, financial flow, political legitimation, and consent. The captured apparatus is what the volume documents. The faith is what captured apparatus has been built on top of. The believer is the figure for whom the volume is written.

**CIASE Sauvé Commission (2018-2021)**. The Commission Indépendante sur les Abus Sexuels dans l'Église, led by Jean-Marc Sauvé. Final report October 2021. Estimated 216,000 victims of clerical abuse in France 1950-2020 (330,000 including lay personnel). Discussed in the Prologue, Chapter 15, and across the per-tradition chapters.

**Claude**. The AI system built by Anthropic that was the substrate for the production of this volume under the author's standing direction. The version used was Claude Opus 4.7. Set out at full register in the closing chapter Part Two.

**Conspiracy theory**. The volume's vocabulary engages the term directly: a conspiracy is when people agree, in private, to do a thing that harms others, and arrange the world so the harm continues and the agreement is hidden. By that documentary definition the Catholic Church's half-century cover-up of clerical sexual abuse was a conspiracy. The volume distinguishes between documented institutional conspiracies (where the record exists) and the contemporary captured-press use of "conspiracy theory" as a fence around the documentary record.

**Demons, the**. The series term for the named senior figures operating the governing apparatus of each mask. In Volume Three, the Demons are the senior figures operating the co-opted religious-apparatus across every tradition — the Vatican apparatus's senior figures, the senior figures of the compromised Eastern Orthodox patriarchates, the senior figures of the Sunni and Shia state-religious architectures, the senior figures of the contemporary Hindu-nationalist Sangh Parivar, and the equivalent senior figures in every other tradition's captured apparatus.

**Devil, the**. The series term for capital, the four-century debt-money creation mechanism by private banks at compound interest that Volume One documents. The Devil is the source-and-root issue from which the four masks (financial / political / religious / epistemic) operate as the surfaces. Set out at full register in Volume One; recapitulated in this volume's Chapter 32.

**Faith**. In the vocabulary, the human encounter with the transcendent — the moment in the chapel, the moment on the prayer rug, the moment at the Shabbat table, the moment at the river, the moment under the open sky. The volume distinguishes faith from the apparatus built on top of faith. The volume documents the apparatus; the faith is not the subject.

**FCRA (Foreign Contribution Regulation Act)**. The Indian statute regulating foreign donations to civil-society and religious organisations. Used by the Modi BJP government from 2014 forward to terminate the operating licences of named NGOs including Amnesty International India. Discussed in Chapter 22 (Sangh Parivar apparatus).

**Four-century mechanism, the**. The debt-money creation mechanism by private banks at compound interest, operating from the founding of the Amsterdam Wisselbank in 1609 and the Bank of England in 1694 forward. Documented at full register in Volume One. The mechanism is the source-and-root issue from which the four masks operate.

**Gush Emunim ("Bloc of the Faithful")**. Founded 1974 by religious-Zionist rabbis and activists. The embryo of the post-1967 Israeli settler movement. Discussed in Chapter 20 (Israeli religious-political apparatus).

**Hermetic tradition**. The philosophical-religious tradition tracing to the *Corpus Hermeticum* and the Egyptian and Greco-Egyptian roots of late-antique philosophical religion. Discussed at chapter length in Chapter 12. The author's own belief system; the disclosure is in the Prologue § III.

**IOR (Istituto per le Opere di Religione)**. The Vatican Bank, properly named the Institute for the Works of Religion. Founded 1942 by Pope Pius XII. Dossiered across multiple investigations and scandals including the Banco Ambrosiano collapse (1982) and the multiple twenty-first-century financial-misconduct investigations. Discussed in Chapter 15.

**John Jay College Report (2004)**. The study commissioned by the United States Conference of Catholic Bishops documenting 4,392 priests with credible allegations of child sexual abuse and 10,667 alleged victims in the US across 1950-2002. The report's authors characterised the figures as conservative. Referenced in the Prologue, Chapter 15, and the Acknowledgements.

**Mahanayaka**. The senior-monk institutional position in the Sri Lankan Theravāda Buddhist sangha (community of monks). The Mahanayaka hierarchy of the three principal Sri Lankan nikāyas (Siyam, Amarapura, Ramañña) is the senior religious-institutional authority and as the co-opted public-religious authority figure of post-1948 Sinhalese-Buddhist nationalism. Discussed in Chapter 23.

**Marja (مرجع, marja' al-taqlid)**. The senior Twelver Shia religious-jurisprudential authority. The marja are the senior figures from whom contemporary Twelver Shias take guidance on religious-legal questions. The contemporary marja architecture includes the senior figures of the Najaf (Iraq) and Qom (Iran) hawza schools. Dossiered in Chapter 19 (Iranian Shia theocratic apparatus).

**Mask, the**. The series term for the surfaces through which the four-century debt-money mechanism (the Devil) operates. Volume One documents the financial mask. Volume Two documents the political mask. Volume Three documents the religious mask. Volume Four documents the epistemic mask. Volume Five draws the four together. The masks conceal the mechanism. The Demons wear the masks.

**Moscow Patriarchate (Patriarchate of Moscow and All Rus')**. The senior institutional structure of the Russian Orthodox Church. Founded 1589; abolished by Peter I's 1721 Holy Synod; restored 1917; closely integrated with the Russian state from 1991 forward and at full operational coordination with the Putin government from February 2022 forward. Dossiered in Chapter 17.

**Nikāya**. A Buddhist monastic fraternity or ordination lineage. In Sri Lanka, the three principal nikāyas are the Siyam Nikāya (1753), the Amarapura Nikāya (1800), and the Ramañña Nikāya (1864), each with its own Mahanayaka hierarchy. Discussed in Chapter 23.

**Pennsylvania Grand Jury Report (August 2018)**. The 1,356-page report of the 40th Statewide Investigating Grand Jury of Pennsylvania, documenting more than 1,000 identifiable victims of clerical sexual abuse across six Pennsylvania Catholic dioceses and the cover-up role of senior diocesan figures. Referenced in the Prologue and Chapter 15.

**Religious Mask, the**. The volume's central designation: captured religious-institutional infrastructure of every major tradition operating as the surface through which the four-century debt-money mechanism extracts. Set out at full register in Chapter 32 and across the synthesis chapters.

**RSS (Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh)**. The Hindu-nationalist paramilitary-and-cultural organisation founded at Nagpur on 27 September 1925 by Keshav Baliram Hedgewar. The institutional foundation of the contemporary Sangh Parivar (RSS-VHP-BJP) architecture. Dossiered in Chapter 22.

**Sangh Parivar ("family of organisations")**. The Hindu-nationalist institutional architecture centred on the RSS, the Vishva Hindu Parishad (VHP), the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP), the Bajrang Dal, and the wider RSS-affiliated network. Dossiered in Chapter 22. The contemporary captured architecture under Mohan Bhagwat (RSS chief) and Narendra Modi (Prime Minister of India) coordination.

**Sauvé Report**. See CIASE Sauvé Commission.

**SBC (Southern Baptist Convention)**. The largest US Protestant denomination. The 22 May 2022 Guidepost Solutions Report documented decades of cover-up of sexual abuse allegations by senior SBC figures. Referenced in the Prologue and Chapter 16 (American evangelical-political apparatus).

**SGPC (Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee)**. The senior Sikh religious-institutional body managing the gurdwaras of Punjab. Dossiered in Chapter 24 (Sikh captured configurations).

**Spotlight investigation (2002)**. The *Boston Globe* Spotlight team's January 2002 investigation that documented the Archdiocese of Boston's decades-long pattern of transferring priests with known credible allegations of child sexual abuse between parishes without notification to civil authorities or to the receiving parishes. The investigation surfaced the cover-up pattern that subsequent national and international investigations would document at scale. Referenced in the Prologue and across the per-tradition chapters.

**State-sangha**. The institutional coordination between a national state and the senior Buddhist monastic hierarchy of that state. The state-sangha architectures of contemporary Thailand, Myanmar, Sri Lanka, Cambodia, and the PRC's Tibetan-Buddhist apparatus are discussed in Chapter 23.

**Twelver Shi'ism**. The senior tradition of contemporary Shia Islam. Doctrinal lineage traces to the Twelve Imams from Ali ibn Abi Talib (d. 661) through Muhammad al-Mahdi (in occultation since 941 CE). The senior tradition of contemporary Iran (state religion since the Safavid establishment 1501), Iraq, Lebanon, Bahrain, and the broader Twelver Shia population. Dossiered in Chapter 6 (Shia capture).

**Vatican Bank**. See IOR (Istituto per le Opere di Religione).

**VHP (Vishva Hindu Parishad)**. "World Hindu Council". Founded 1964. Part of the Sangh Parivar architecture. Discussed in Chapter 22.

**Wahhabi-Saud pact (1744)**. The 1744 agreement between Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (founder of the Wahhabi religious-reform movement) and Muhammad ibn Saud (founder of the first Saudi state) that established the Wahhabi-Saud religious-political alliance. The foundational document of contemporary Saudi state-religious architecture. Dossiered in Chapter 18 (Saudi religious-export apparatus).

**WRPC (World Russian People's Council)**. Founded 26 May 1993. Headed by Kirill (Patriarch of Moscow). The senior contemporary Moscow Patriarchate ideological-coordination body, operating in close institutional coordination with the Putin government. Dossiered in Chapter 17.

**You are the assessor of last resort**. The volume's standing framing — you holds the final adjudication authority on whether the evidence sustains the positioning. Applied across the series and at full register in Volume Five Chapter 28. The reader does not have to trust the author or the AI substrate; the record is on the page; the reader can audit.

*Behind the Blessed: The Demon They Call the Church* — Volume Three of *The Last Mind* — was completed on 21 May 2026 in Scotland.

The series continues with Volume Four: *Behind the Knowing: The Demon They Call Consensus* and closes with Volume Five: *Behind the Synthesis: The Devil, The Demons, and The Masks*.

All volumes of *The Last Mind* are available at no cost.

— Maria Smith
Scotland, 21 May 2026



---


# Credits


## *Behind the Blessed*

### The Demon They Call the Church


**Volume Three of *The Last Mind*.**


**By** Maria Smith.

**Written with** Claude Opus 4.7 (Anthropic).


First edition. Published 21 May 2026. Scotland.


All rights reserved by the author. The volume is published as a free-to-read documentary record; the reader is invited to circulate it.


---


### *The Last Mind* series


- **Volume One** — *Behind the Mask: The Demon They Call God.* Published 13 May 2026. The financial mask. The four-century debt-money mechanism. The contemporary American techno-oligarchy.
- **Volume Two** — *Behind the Performance: The Demon They Call the State.* Published 18 May 2026. The political mask. The global political class as the extension of the same mechanism.
- **Volume Three** — *Behind the Blessed: The Demon They Call the Church.* This volume. Published 21 May 2026. The religious mask. The co-opted religious-institutional infrastructure of every major tradition.
- **Volume Four** — *Behind the Knowing: The Demon They Call Consensus.* Published 21 May 2026. The captured contemporary consensus-knowledge apparatus across press, academy, peer-review, philanthropy, regulation, public-health, climate-policy, AI-knowledge, think-tank, fact-checking, and search.
- **Volume Five** — *Behind the Synthesis: The Devil, The Demons, and The Masks.* Forthcoming. The closing volume. The unifying-theory capstone consolidating the four mask-documentations into a single coherent framework.


---


### Author

**Maria Smith** is a Scotland-born author, autodidact researcher, and AI-systems builder. She is the architect of the EchoAI / ErnOS lineage of independent AI systems. Her work as an independent AI-alignment researcher dossiers the contemporary frontier-AI architecture from the user side.


### Writing collaboration

This volume was written with **Claude Opus 4.7**, Anthropic's frontier large-language-model system at the time of writing. The conflict-of-interest acknowledgements governing the volume — that Anthropic operates within captured-AI-knowledge architecture that the series dossiers in Volume Four, and that the religious-institutional terrain treated in this volume is a politically and theologically contested field — are named openly throughout the relevant chapters and in the Author's Note. The volume operates under standing rules that preserve evidentiary independence despite the structural conflicts.

The Author's Note included in the back matter dossiers the working procedural commitments under which the volume was produced and the procedural patterns the working production identified.


### Dedication

The volume is dedicated to all those across every tradition who keep the faith without being captured by the apparatus.


---


*The reader can verify every claim.*


— Maria Smith
Scotland, 21 May 2026
